

## THE BOOK OF LUKE

The third biographical account of Jesus Christ in the New Testament was written by Paul\'s missionary companion Luke, ["the beloved physician"]{.class_4723} (Col. 4:14). From the time he joined the apostle in Acts 16:10--17, Luke accompanied Paul (2 Tim. 4:11) all the way to the time of his execution. For further discussion on the authorship of the book, we refer you to our comments under Acts 1:1 in that Commentary.

Luke's writing is that of a highly educated, very intelligent man. While we won't go into the fineries of Greek here, suffice it to say that the language of Luke is a very precise, correct, classical style not found in the writings of a commercial fisherman like John or a publican like Matthew.

Now you don't need to know Greek or Hebrew to see that the writers of Scripture have different styles and personalities. The truth of the matter is that although ["all scripture is given by inspiration of God"]{.class_4723} (2 Tim. 3:16), the Holy Spirit didn't override the ["holy men of God"]{.class_4723} He ["moved"]{.class_4723} to speak the words of the Scriptures (2 Pet. 1:21).

Like the living Word, the written word of God has two natures: a human one and a divine one. The divine nature was, of course, the Holy Ghost, and the human nature was the men who wrote it. The men who wrote down what the Holy Spirit gave them had individual personalities, and God spoke through those personalities without changing them.

Because of Luke's command of the Greek language, the teaching of modern apostate Fundamentalists and Conservatives is that Luke himself was a Gentile Greek. Of course, that proves nothing, for Paul could speak and write Greek quite well when he wanted (see Acts 21:37 and our remarks on the authorship of Hebrews in the introduction to that Commentary). Romans 3:4 says ["the oracles of God"]{.class_4723} were given to the [Jews,]{.class_4725} and the singular exception of Job (written 300 years before Moses penned the Pentateuch) does nothing to overthrow that divine rule. Scofield correctly points out that Luke was "of Jewish ancestry\...a Jew of the Dispersion," []probably from Antioch (!).

The Gospel of Luke contains 24 chapters, 1,151 verses, and 25,939 words. The exact time and place of its authorship isn't fixed. It was obviously written before the book of Acts (see Acts 1:1), which wasn't finished until after the events of Acts 28 (probably around A.D. 65). Because Luke wrote his Gospel from the eyewitness accounts of Christ's life (Luke 1:2), the most logical time for him to have interviewed the various disciples and folks who saw and heard Jesus would have been when Paul was imprisoned in Caesarea (Acts 24:27). The most probable time for the writing, then, would be between A.D. 58 and 60.

Luke is what is known as a "synoptic Gospel"; that is, it, along with Matthew and Mark, gives a synopsis of the life and ministry of the Lord Jesus Christ. Now there is nothing wrong with that label per se, but the Liberals use that to isolate and get rid of John's Gospel as a genuine historical account of the life of Jesus Christ.

You see, it is obvious to anyone that John had a [purpose]{.class_4725} in writing his Gospel.

["But these are written, that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God; and that believing ye might have life through his name"]{.class_4723} (John 20:31).

John wrote his Gospel to show you Jesus Christ was God in the flesh and to get you saved. Such things are ["verboten"]{.class_4725} to unsaved and apostate scholarship. Historians aren't supposed to write anything to prove any Biblical, theological [truth.]{.class_4725} After all, "all religions are the same," and "we are all working to get to the same place." Yeah, and you're gonna make it if you don't watch out.

The reason for the so-called "synoptic problem" is the amount of New Testament revelation each author had. Matthew and Mark were the earliest two Gospels, and neither one had anything the Apostle Paul wrote. Luke would have had 1 & 2 Thessalonians, Galatians, Romans, and 1 & 2 Corinthians. John, though, would have had the entire body of Pauline revelation available to him. When Luke wrote, the [destruction of Jerusalem]{.class_4725} had not yet taken place, but John wrote twenty years [after]{.class_4725} the Jews had ceased to be a nation. That explains the alleged "disparities" between the accounts of Matthew, Mark, and Luke and John's Gospel.

Each one of the Gospels has a special theme and these are given in the Old Testament prophecies on ["the Branch"]{.class_4723} (see our comments on Zech. 3:8 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Minor Prophets,]{.class_4725} Vol. II). As we pointed out above, John wrote about Jesus as [the Son of God;]{.class_4725} it has a universal appeal not found in the other three Gospels (e.g., John 1:10--12, 3:16, 4:42, 12:32, 21:25). John has Christ's genealogy going back all the way before Genesis 1:1 (see John 1:1--3). So John emphasizes Christ's Deity, and he writes to the world at large.

Matthew presents Jesus as the [King of the Jews.]{.class_4725} He traces Christ's genealogy back to Abraham, the father of the Jewish race (Matt. 1:1). Matthew is a Jew writing to Jews, so his emphasis is on Christ as the ["King of the Jews"]{.class_4723} because those Jews were looking for their Messiah to show up as a conquering King.

The Gospel of Mark is fast-paced with a lot of action. There are not a lot of doctrinal discourses in Mark like you find in the other Gospels. For that reason, the scholars say Mark writes to the Romans. Be that as it may, the emphasis in Mark is on Christ as the Servant of God. Because of that, you won't find any genealogy given by Mark. Nobody cares from where a servant comes.

The Gospel of Luke, though, traces Christ back to the very first man, Adam (Luke 3:38), so the theme of Luke is Jesus Christ as the [Son of man.]{.class_4725} Luke writes to the Greeks as a group, so he presents Jesus Christ as the perfect man. Luke puts the emphasis on Christ's human nature, which is why the Liberals prefer it over John's Gospel, because John emphasizes Christ's divine nature.

Because of his audience, you will notice a peculiar [Gentile]{.class_4725} slant to Luke's Gospel. It's all through the book. In the famous Christmas passage in Luke 2, the angel told the shepherds, ["behold, I bring you good tidings of great joy, which shall be to ALL PEOPLE"]{.class_4723} (Luke 2:10). At Christ's circumcision, Simeon said He would be ["A light to lighten THE GENTILES"]{.class_4723} (Luke 2:32). Luke changes the Parable of the Talents (Matt. 25:14--30), which was a [Jewish]{.class_4725} weight, to the Parable of the Pounds, a [Gentile]{.class_4725} weight.

While Luke is a "synoptic Gospel," still there are things peculiar to the book, which are found in no other Gospel account. Luke is the only Gospel to give you the Parable of the Good Samaritan, the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ72.xhtml_page_6}Parable of the Prodigal Son, and the story of the rich man and Lazarus. You won't find the annunciations of John the Baptist or Jesus Christ in Matthew, Mark, or John. Although Matthew will tell you what happens after the birth of Christ with the wise men, only in Luke will you read about the actual birth of the Lord Jesus Christ. And only Luke gives you the conversion of the dying thief on the cross. So without the Gospel of Luke, you would have an incomplete picture of the Lord Jesus Christ's earthly ministry. The idea that you can use the "synoptic problem" to get rid of one or more of the Gospel accounts is straight out of Hell. You can take the "Q document theory" and the "Jesus Seminar" and deposit them in "File 13" where they belong.

As in every book in the Bible Believer's Commentary Series, we will take a [believing]{.class_4725} approach to the work of Dr. Luke. We will accept it as an absolutely, 100% correct, historically accurate account of the events as they are recorded. We won't give a "Continental dollar" (I forbear to give the "original") for the opinions of apostate scholarship in the matter. Why would we take the conjectures and guesswork of a bunch of educated jackrabbits over the witness of people who lived, worked, and talked with Jesus Christ face to face? Our approach to what the Holy Spirit recorded in the Book He authored is that of the Thessalonian Christians.

["For this cause also thank we God without ceasing, because, when ye received the word of God which ye heard of us, ye received it not as the word of men, but as it is in truth, the word of God, which effectually worketh also in you that believe"]{.class_4723} (1 Thess. 2:13).

If that's not the approach of the other bunch, that's their problem; it's a free country. They can believe anything they want about the Book; just don't ask us who believe it to give them any more respect for their position or education than we would give to a two-year-old Ubangi. They will answer to their Creator just like I will answer to mine (Rom. 14:12). All my chips are on the Book.

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml}

# Chapter 1 {.heading_78-1}

1:1 Forasmuch as many have taken in hand to set forth in order a declaration of those things which are most surely believed among us,

2 Even as they delivered them unto us, which from the beginning were eyewitnesses, and ministers of the word;

3 It seemed good to me also, having had perfect understanding of all things from the very first, to write unto thee in order, most excellent Theophilus,

4 That thou mightest know the certainty of those things, wherein thou hast been instructed.

First of all, notice Luke's style. Verses 1--4 are all one sentence. That's Luke's style: long sentences and more grammatically correct than that of someone like Simon Peter. Some of Peter's grammar is terrible. In Greek grammar, that's known as [anacoluthon]{.class_4729-0}; that is, the writer doesn't have his genders, voices, and modes match correctly.

That's the Holy Spirit using the man as he is; He doesn't override the fellow's grammatical errors. The Fundamentalists and Conservatives who only see Greek grammar and syntax in the New Testament have this weird idea that the Bible has to follow the rules of perfect Greek. Perfect according to whom? Those are highly educated scholars exalting their own rules of Greek grammar over what the Holy Spirit inspired and preserved in His Book.

The Lord used all kinds of people to write the Scriptures. He not only used educated folks like Moses and Paul (see Acts 7:22, 22:3), He used ["unlearned and ignorant men"]{.class_4730-0} like Peter and John (Acts 4:13). Let that be an encouragement to you. God will use you as you are; you don't have to be like someone else. The degree to which God will use a man has nothing to do with his level of education; it has to do with the man's willingness and faithfulness in accord with the Lord's purpose for that man.

["Forasmuch as many have taken in hand to set forth in order a declaration of those things which are most surely believed among us"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 1). Now what follows verse 1 is a biography of the life of Jesus Christ, so the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_8 .class-1}["things\...most surely believed among us"]{.class_4730-0} have to do with ["all that Jesus began both to do and teach"]{.class_4730-0} (Acts 1:1). Luke's not giving a doctrinal dissertation like Paul does in Romans, Galatians, and Ephesians on New Testament salvation. He's setting forth a record about the life of Christ upon which the Holy Spirit and the apostles built the doctrines of the Church (Eph. 2:20). The Hyperdispensationalists teach that only the Pauline Epistles have any application to the Body of Christ (and some even get rid of Romans, Corinthians, and Thessalonians). Heretical nonsense!

Do you know what the "Apostle to the Gentiles" (Rom. 11:13)---the one you were commanded to follow as a New Testament Christian in the Body of Christ (1 Cor. 11:1)---told you? I mean, we do want to be "Pauline" in our ministry, don't we? The Apostle Paul told you to ["consent\...to wholesome words, EVEN THE WORDS OF OUR LORD JESUS CHRIST"]{.class_4730-0} (1 Tim. 6:3). He said if you don't, you aren't some sort of hyper-spiritual "Berean" who "searches the Scriptures" (Acts 17:11) to "rightly divide" ["the word of truth"]{.class_4730-0} (2 Tim. 2:15); you are ["proud, KNOWING NOTHING"]{.class_4730-0} (1 Tim. 6:4). Well, do you know where ["the words of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_4730-0} are found [mainly]{.class_4729-0}? They're found in Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John.

The Dry Cleaners have forgotten that the Scripture is not only ["profitable for doctrine,"]{.class_4730-0} but also ["for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness"]{.class_4730-0} (2 Tim. 3:16). There's plenty in the Gospels for a New Testament Christian.

Now look at verse 1 again: ["Forasmuch as MANY have taken in hand to set forth in order a declaration of those things which are most surely believed among us." ]{.class_4730-0}That means there were all kinds of "Gospels" circulating when Luke began writing his account; after all, Matthew and Mark hardly constitute ["many."]{.class_4730-0} (Luke wouldn't be including John's Gospel in that number because John didn't write until thirty years later.) And, of course, he was not talking about "The Gospel of Peter," "The Gospel of Thomas," "The Infancy Gospel of James the Less," "The Gospel of Judas," or any of the other so-called ["Lost Books of the Bible."]{.class_4729-0} All those were Gnostic forgeries written nearly a hundred years after John wrote Revelation. Verse 1 refers to accounts of the life of Christ, written by []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_9}First-Century Jewish believers, which have been lost to posterity.

This goes into matters of the [canonicity]{.class_4729-0} of the Scriptures. The word [canon]{.class_4729-0} is a Latin word that means "rule," as in a standard by which to judge something. The "canon of Scripture" are those books accepted into the Bible as inspired by God. We will discuss this more under our comments on 24:44.

In regards to verse 1, what you need to realize is that the Holy Spirit spoke much more than what is recorded in the 66 books of the Bible. Enoch prophesied way back in Genesis 5, but you only have two verses of anything he said, and they weren't written down as Scripture until over 2,500 years after he was raptured (Jude 14--15). If you think all Jonah prophesied was what is recorded in the book bearing his name, "you have another think coming" (2 King 14:25). The Apostle Paul wrote all kinds of letters that aren't in the Bible (cf. Col. 4:16).

What's going on in verse 1 is that there were believers in Judea, Samaria, and Galilee who wrote down their own versions of Christ's life in an attempt to win people to Jesus Christ. The Holy Spirit only chose two of those accounts (Matthew and Mark) as authoritative Scripture, and He dumped the rest.

What you have to keep in mind, brethren, is that it is not God's [spoken]{.class_4729-0} words that are the final authority; the final authority for the Christian is the [written]{.class_4729-0} words of the Scriptures (2 Pet. 1:17--20). Moreover, it is not the "inspired Word of God" that is the final authority for the believer like the Fundamentalists teach; it is[ "the holy scriptures"]{.class_4730-0} which God has preserved for us just as He wants us to have them (2 Tim. 3:15; Psa. 12:6--7). If you had the "original" of the book of Jeremiah, you would not have the Scriptures God wanted you to have (see Jer. 36:32). If you did find Paul's "Epistle to the Laodiceans," it would mean nothing one way or another because the Holy Spirit didn't put it into the canon of the Scripture.

People like R. L. Hymers, Bobbie Sumner, Curtis Hutson, Bob Jones III, and James White are always complaining about "Ruckmanism" and ["King James]{.class_4729-0} Onlyism." I have never taught that the [King James Version]{.class_4729-0} is the only Scripture that ["is given by inspiration."]{.class_4730-0} I recognize what God did with Luther in German, Valera in Spanish, Diodati in Italian, Olivetan in French, etc. I'm not "[King James]{.class_4729-0}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_10 .class-1} Only" when it comes to that sort of thing. But I do believe that [King James]{.class_4729-0} Bible is the Holy Scriptures in English, with the very words God wants you to have in English, and that ["ALL SCRIPTURE is given by inspiration of God."]{.class_4730-0} What these apostates can't stand is that I [have]{.class_4729-0} the Scriptures and they [don't]{.class_4729-0} because they believe the only Scriptures that were ["given by inspiration" ]{.class_4730-0}were the "original autographs." Their authority is a lost pile of papers that no one has ever seen or read for the last 1,900 years, while mine is [the Book I hold in my hand.]{.class_4729-0}

Verse 2: ["Even as they delivered them unto us, which from the beginning were eyewitnesses, and ministers of the word."]{.class_4730-0} That is what sets the Bible apart from amateurish works like the [Koran.]{.class_4729-0} The Bible is composed of the [eyewitness accounts]{.class_4729-0} of God's dealings with men. That's not only the four Gospels; that's the entire Bible, including the Old Testament (see the work of Robert Dick Wilson in David Otis Fuller's book [Which Bible?]{.class_4729-0} where Wilson proves from the language of the book of Genesis that Moses used the written accounts of the patriarchs to compose the historical record all the way back to the Garden of Eden). The Bible is not [Übergeschichte]{.class_4729-0} (suprahistory) composed of myths and fables that are supposed to teach some "truth." It is actual, factual [history.]{.class_4729-0} The Bible is the only 100% correct history book you will ever read.

Christ had people witness the entire three-and-a-half years of His earthly ministry. Outside of the original eleven apostles (Judas having hung himself), there were two other men that stuck with Jesus Christ from the time He was baptized until His ascension: Matthias and Barsabas (Acts 1:23). Over 5,000 people saw Him feed the multitude the first time, and over 4,000 saw Him do it the second time (Matt. 16:9--10). There were women who traveled with Christ and the apostles who saw everything He did (Luke 8:3); they even went with Him to the cross when all the apostles except John deserted Him (Matt. 27:55--56). Those women were witnesses of the Resurrection even before the apostles (Matt. 28:1--10). In fact, not only were those women and the apostles eyewitnesses of the Resurrection, Paul says over 500 people at one time saw Christ after His resurrection, and that most of them were still alive in A.D. 60, and that the Corinthians could go and talk to them and verify what []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_11}Paul preached about the death, burial, and resurrection (1 Cor. 15:6).

How is that for [proof]{.class_4729-0} that Jesus Christ was who He said He was? If you were accused of murder and could produce 500 eyewitnesses who saw you in another city when the crime occurred, do you reckon you would have any trouble proving your innocence? Five hundred witnesses, man! and that's besides the twelve apostles and the Apostle Paul. You talk about something being confirmed, that's it! That's what Luke calls ["many infallible proofs"]{.class_4730-0} in Acts 1:3. Of course, all the new "Bibles" change that so the truths will be "[convincing]{.class_4729-0}" without being ["infallible,"]{.class_4730-0} but in any court of law, your "alibi" would be [foolproof]{.class_4729-0} if you had 500 eyewitnesses on your side. The prosecution's case would just fall apart.

Now compare that with Mohammed. When Mohammed got his revelation from "the god" (that's what [Allah]{.class_4729-0} means), he would hear a ringing in his ears, his heart would start pounding, and he would turn beet red in the face. Then he would fall to the ground, pouring with sweat and foaming at the mouth. He would then pass out and snore like a camel. When he got up, he would start "prophesying" [without prophesying anything.]{.class_4729-0}

There are no [real,]{.class_4729-0} [historical]{.class_4729-0} prophecies in the [Koran.]{.class_4729-0} Of the four supposed prophecies that took place in history (the Last Judgment takes place after time is no more---Rev. 10:6, 11:18), not one of them demonstrates the supernatural character of Bible prophecy. One was about the outcome of a battle (Sura 54:45), which, as anyone knows, has a 50/50 chance: you will either win or you will lose. That wasn't a prophecy; it was a lucky (or a calculated) [guess.]{.class_4729-0} When we talk about Bible prophecy, we are talking about things being fulfilled that had a 1 in 10[157]{.class_4757} chance of taking place (Peter Stoner, [Science Speaks]{.class_4729-0}); there aren't that many electrons in three universes the size of ours.

The second one was written [after]{.class_4729-0} the battle was over (Sura 8:43; the [Koran]{.class_4729-0} is not written in any kind of chronological, or even logical, order), so it was not a prophecy of anything that would take place in the future.

Mohammed's third bungled attempt at prophecy was a vision about a pilgrimage which was [never fulfilled]{.class_4729-0} (Sura 48:27--28), which made him a [false]{.class_4729-0}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_12 .class-1} prophet (Deut. 18:20--22).

The last attempt by Mohammed at prophecy was that the Roman Empire would be defeated, but then be victorious "within a few years" (Sura 30:1--4). Of course, neither Mohammed, Gabriel, nor Allah would ever make any attempt at dating a specific year like Jehovah did in His Book (e.g., Dan. 9:24--27; Num. 14:33--34). But unfortunately for Mohammed and his nondescript "prophecy," Daniel beat him to it by over a thousand years. In Daniel 2:40--43, Daniel already told you that the Roman Empire (the iron legs of the image) would be replaced by the revived Roman Empire of the Antichrist (the ten toes made of iron and clay). So Mohammed's "prophecy" turns out to be nothing more than another plagiarism (see our remarks under Hag. 1:8 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Minor Prophets,]{.class_4729-0} Vol. II).

Calling Mohammed a prophet is just too funny for words. Surely a prophet would know where he's going when he dies. After all, Paul did (Phil. 1:23), Peter did (1 Pet. 1:3--4), John did (1 John 5:13), and [Jesus Christ did]{.class_4729-0} (John 16:10). Mohammed had no idea what "Allah" was going to do with him when he died (["Holy" Hadith,]{.class_4729-0} Bukhari, Vol. V, No. 266). If Mohammed was such a big boy as a prophet, how is it that he didn't know as much about his own future as the Prophet he replaced (Jesus Christ)? If you are saved and know it (1 John 5:13), you are more of a prophet than Mohammed ever thought about being (see Rev. 19:10).

Nobody could confirm anything the angel "Gabriel" spoke to Mohammed in his epileptic fits. Compare that to the cloud of God's glory that the children of Israel saw every single day for forty years over the Tabernacle (Exod. 40:38); that was nearly two million eyewitnesses to God's revelation to Moses. Nobody saw Gabriel say "nuthin'" to that old epileptic, fornicating, murderous, child-molesting raghead.

Peter, James, and John were eyewitnesses to God the Father speaking to His Son on the Mount of Transfiguration (2 Pet. 1:16). Peter said he was ["a witness of the sufferings of Christ, and also a partaker of the glories that shall be revealed"]{.class_4730-0} (1 Pet. 5:1).

The Apostle John said, ["That which was from the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_13}beginning, which we have HEARD, which we have SEEN WITH OUR EYES, which we have LOOKED UPON, and OUR HANDS HAVE HANDLED, of the Word of life"]{.class_4730-0} (1 John 1:1).

And you reckon some camel jockey who was born 500 years later is going to know more about the life of Jesus Christ than that? Mohammed said in the [Koran]{.class_4729-0} that Jesus Christ never died on the cross (Sura 4:157); somebody else was crucified in His place.

Yeah, Mohammed, you're the smart one, ain't 'cha? You're so smart that you haven't even got figured out what actually did happen to Him. You will find Imams who teach that Jesus Christ went to Kashmir, India and died there at 120 years old. Then you will get different Imams who say, "No, that's not what happened; he was raptured with His mother Mary up to Paradise" (a good Roman Catholic doctrine).

Which one was it? They can't both be right. He died in India, did He? Who saw it? He was caught up to Paradise with Mary, was He? Who witnessed it happen?

I've got the eyewitness testimonies of the people who were there at the cross with Jesus Christ and talked to Him as He died. I've got the records of the ones who buried Him and who [saw]{.class_4729-0} Him, [talked]{.class_4729-0} with Him, and [ate]{.class_4729-0} with Him after He came up from the dead. To reject the Bible, you have to pretend that you and the fools who taught you are smarter up here 2,000 years past the events than the people who saw them happen. You haven't got a brain in your head.

Listen, the Apostle John was closer to Jesus Christ than any infidel or skeptic after whom you've ever read. You talk about hearing, seeing, and touching Jesus Christ, do you know what John did? At the Last Supper, John lay there (in Bible times people ate reclining on couches, which always sounded to me like a pretty good way to eat; that way you don't get a cramped stomach like you do sitting vertically) with his head on Christ's chest (John 13:23). John had his ear three inches from the heartbeat of eternal life.

John was listening to that heart pumping that blood through the body of Jesus (He was the Son of [man]{.class_4729-0} as well as the Son of God). The Bible says, ["the life of all flesh is the blood thereof"]{.class_4730-0} (Lev. 17:14). Well, what kind of blood did Jesus Christ have flowing through His veins? It was [God's blood]{.class_4729-0} (Acts 20:28). So you know []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_14}what kind of life was in the blood of the Lord Jesus Christ? It would have to be eternal life because God inhabits eternity (Isa. 57:15).

["And this is the record, that God hath given to us ETERNAL LIFE, and this life is IN HIS SON"]{.class_4730-0} (1 John 5:11).

And you're going to take the word of some demon-possessed military dictator over a testimony like that?

["Even as they delivered them unto us, which from the beginning were\...ministers of the word."]{.class_4730-0} Notice that true "apostolic succession" has nothing to do with the Roman Catholic Church (or any other denomination, for that matter) or any Pope. It has to do with the transmission of New Testament doctrine and practice through the faithful teaching and preaching of the Scriptures.

["And the things that thou hast heard of me among many witnesses, the same commit thou to faithful men, who shall be able to teach others also"]{.class_4730-0} (2 Tim. 2:2). (For further study, we refer you to our remarks on the above passage in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Pastoral Epistles]{.class_4729-0} and to chapter one of our work on [The History of the New Testament Church,]{.class_4729-0} Vol. I.)

Now, as we pointed out in the Introduction, the Bible is a book with two natures: a human one and a divine one. Verse 2 gives you the human character of the book of Luke; verse 3 gives you the divine character. Luke wrote his account based on the eyewitness testimonies of people who walked, talked, and heard Jesus personally; but Luke had ["perfect understanding of all things from the very first"]{.class_4730-0} by [divine revelation.]{.class_4729-0}

["And we know that the Son of God is come, and hath given us an understanding, that we may know him that is true"]{.class_4730-0} (1 John 5:20).

["Then opened he their understanding, that they might understand the scriptures"]{.class_4730-0} (Luke 24:45).

You can know the facts of the Gospel and of the Scriptures, but unless the Holy Spirit of God opens your understanding to them, they are nothing but pitch black darkness (2 Cor. 4:4). The Apostle Paul wrote:

["Which things also we speak, not in the words which []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_15}man's wisdom teacheth, but which the Holy Ghost teacheth; comparing spiritual things with spiritual. But the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God: for they are foolishness unto him: neither can he know them, because they are spiritually discerned"]{.class_4730-0} (1 Cor. 2:13--14).

Verse 4:[ "That thou mightest know the certainty of those things, wherein thou hast been instructed."]{.class_4730-0} Luke is writing to Theophilus so he can know that the things he has been taught about Jesus Christ are [absolutely sure.]{.class_4729-0} ["Those things"]{.class_4730-0} are a reference to the things about Christ recorded in the [Scriptures.]{.class_4729-0}

When I was in my first year of school at Bob Jones University, the Lord put a verse on me that I have never forgotten. I had only been saved for six months, but I had already been through the Bible six times. I was reading my Bible and enjoying it, but nothing had really jumped out at me until I hit Proverbs 22 the sixth time.

The Lord will do that to you. If you read your Bible faithfully as you should, you'll be going through there, and suddenly the Holy Spirit will cause a verse to leap out of the text at you and sink its spiritual claws in you. And what the Holy Spirit puts on you from His word, He won't necessarily put on me.

Brethren, that Book creates a [rugged individualism.]{.class_4729-0} It doesn't create a uniform string of bologna sausages; it will create [individuals.]{.class_4729-0} That's why the Founding Fathers of this country were great [statesmen:]{.class_4729-0} they were [Bible readers.]{.class_4729-0} What you've got today are just [politicians:]{.class_4729-0} they are all cut out of the same cloth. All you'll get in the next election (regardless of when it is) are two left-wing international Socialists---Republican or Democrat. It won't make a difference; they're all cloned robots.

If you spend time in that Book, it will make you an individual. People tell the young men who are thinking about coming to the Pensacola Bible Institute: "Don't go down there to Pensacola; you'll turn out just like Ruckman." I don't know any student who graduated from this school, whom the Lord has used in the ministry, who turned out to be just like me. You take men like Art Martin, Rick DeMichele, Rick Sowell, Bob Joseph, and Nathan Bemis; they're not anything like "Ruckman" except for the fact that they [believe that []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_16}Book.]{.class_4729-0}

The Book creates individuals because the Book creates [convictions]{.class_4729-0} in a man. If you put Lester Roloff, Carl McIntyre, Bob Jones Sr., Maze Jackson, J. Harold Smith, Oliver B. Greene, Percy Crawford, J. Frank Norris, Charlie Fuller, Hugh Pyle, E. J. Daniels, et al., in a room and locked them in, there would be blood coming out from under the door in less than thirty minutes. Why is that? Because they all have convictions they got from the Book. One of the Baptist "distinctives" should be [division.]{.class_4729-0} Baptists split and then split the splits until they split into splinters and all that's left is sawdust. Why? Because Baptists are people of the Book, and the Book creates convictions about everything.

So the Lord emphasizes something from the Scriptures for you that He won't necessarily emphasize with me. That is what the Lord did for me in Proverbs 22:17--21.

["Bow down thine ear, and hear the W-O-R-D-S of the wise\..."]{.class_4730-0} (Prov. 22:17). He's talking about the WORDS of the Scriptures, and I don't mean the principles, the fundamentals, the message, or even the "word" (or the "Word"). It's those individual [words.]{.class_4729-0}

["\...and apply thine heart unto my knowledge"]{.class_4730-0} (Prov. 22:17). Well, I had applied my heart to [man's ]{.class_4729-0}knowledge for 27 years before I was saved. I had read the Hindu literature (the [Shastas, ]{.class_4729-0}the [Puranas,]{.class_4729-0} the [Sutras,]{.class_4729-0} and the [Vedas]{.class_4729-0}) and the Buddhist literature (the [Tripitaka]{.class_4729-0}). I had read the [Bhagavad-Gita,]{.class_4729-0} the [Zend Avesta,]{.class_4729-0} and the [Analects of Confucius.]{.class_4729-0} So now it was time to apply my heart to the knowledge of God as found in His Book.

["Have not I WRITTEN to thee excellent things in counsels and knowledge]{.class_4730-0} \[there's the Book\][, that I might make thee know the CERTAINTY OF THE WORDS OF TRUTH]{.class_4730-0} \[see Luke 1:4\][ to them that send unto thee?"]{.class_4730-0} (Prov. 22:20--21).

So for 48 years now the Lord has sent me young men so I can make them ["know the certainty of the words of truth."]{.class_4730-0} That's what God called me to do. In a day and age when the preachers and the professors and the Bible revisers are cutting up that Book, changing that Book, correcting that Book, and destroying the faith of Christians in that Book, God has called me to teach that Book so that []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_17}the faith of young men is restored in what God said.

There was a fellow who went through school here. He had grown up in a Christian home, and his parents sent him to the Christian school in the church they attended. As a child, he had always been taught that the [King James]{.class_4729-0} Bible was the inspired word of God. But when he got into tenth grade, the teacher in his Bible class (a graduate of Bob Jones University) proceeded on the [first day of class]{.class_4729-0} to tell those teenage kids who were struggling with the flesh, the world, drugs, rock music, etc., that the Book they had always been taught was God's infallible, inerrant word was just a translation of men filled with mistakes, and that only the "originals" were inspired. When that teacher got through, he asked those kids if they [now]{.class_4729-0} believed the [King James]{.class_4729-0} was the word of God. That teacher took a whole class of about 25 teenagers who believed the Book and reduced them down to two, one of whom was the young man who came to our school. But that teacher had managed to create doubts in that kid's mind as to whether that [King James]{.class_4729-0} Bible really was God's word or not.

Fortunately, that teenage boy had a father who gave him my books on [Bible Babel]{.class_4743} and [The Christian's Handbook of Manuscript Evidence,]{.class_4743} and that kid got his faith restored in the Book and is doing something for the Lord today. But that story shows you the two "ministries" of "Bible teachers": one is to cast doubt in your mind as to what God said (Gen. 3:1), and the other is to ["make thee know the certainty of the words of truth"]{.class_4730-0} so that your students can teach others to believe the Book.

5 There was in the days of Herod, the king of Judaea, a certain priest named Zacharias, of the course of Abia: and his wife was of the daughters of Aaron, and her name was Elisabeth.

6 And they were both righteous before God, walking in all the commandments and ordinances of the Lord blameless.

7 And they had no child, because that Elisabeth was barren, and they both were now well stricken in years.

8 And it came to pass, that while he executed the priest's []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_18}office before God in the order of his course,

9 According to the custom of the priest's office, his lot was to burn incense when he went into the temple of the Lord.

10 And the whole multitude of the people were praying without at the time of incense.

11 And there appeared unto him an angel of the Lord standing on the right side of the altar of incense.

12 And when Zacharias saw him, he was troubled, and fear fell upon him.

13 But the angel said unto him, Fear not, Zacharias: for thy prayer is heard; and thy wife Elisabeth shall bear thee a son, and thou shalt call his name John.

14 And thou shalt have joy and gladness; and many shall rejoice at his birth.

15 For he shall be great in the sight of the Lord, and shall drink neither wine nor strong drink; and he shall be filled with the Holy Ghost, even from his mother's womb.

16 And many of the children of Israel shall he turn to the Lord their God.

17 And he shall go before him in the spirit and power of Elias, to turn the hearts of the fathers to the children, and the disobedient to the wisdom of the just; to make ready a people prepared for the Lord.

18 And Zacharias said unto the angel, Whereby shall I know this? for I am an old man, and my wife well stricken in years.

19 And the angel answering said unto him, I am Gabriel, that stand in the presence of God; and am sent to speak unto thee, and to shew thee these glad tidings.

20 And, behold, thou shalt be dumb, and not able to speak, until the day that these things shall be performed, because thou believest not my words, which shall be fulfilled in their season.

21 And the people waited for Zacharias, and marvelled that he tarried so long in the temple.

22 And when he came out, he could not speak unto []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_19}them: and they perceived that he had seen a vision in the temple: for he beckoned unto them, and remained speechless.

23 And it came to pass, that, as soon as the days of his ministration were accomplished, he departed to his own house.

24 And after those days his wife Elisabeth conceived, and hid herself five months, saying,

25 Thus hath the Lord dealt with me in the days wherein he looked on me, to take away my reproach among men.

Verses 1--4 were Luke's introduction to his work; the real narrative of the book begins at the paragraph mark in verse 5.

["There was in the days of Herod, the king of Judaea, a certain priest named Zacharias, of the course of Abia"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 5). Notice how meticulous Luke is in dating the events of his work. He gives precise times and reigns of Kings and Governors (see 2:1--2, 3:1--2) so that there is no doubt in your mind that he's talking about real, historical events (see our comments on [Übergeshichte]{.class_4729-0} under vs. 2).

Now verse 5 is a key verse in determining the real birthday of the Lord Jesus Christ. The Roman Catholic myth that He was born on the day the sun god was resurrected (Dec. 25) is just so much pagan fiction. Verse 5 leaves no doubt as to the actual date of the First Advent. What is about to happen in verses 13--17 is the announcement of the birth of John the Baptist, and he is conceived shortly thereafter (vss. 23--41). Six months later, the Holy Spirit conceives Jesus Christ in Mary (vs. 26). So if you can figure out when John the Baptist was conceived, you can work out the date of Christ's birth. Verse 5 sets the date for the conception of John the Baptist.

["A certain priest named Zacharias, of the course of Abia."]{.class_4730-0} In 1 Chronicles 24, David divided all the priests into 24[ "divisions"]{.class_4730-0} or courses to serve in the Temple. A "course" would serve one week and then change. That way each course would serve twice a year, with all the priests officiating at the three main feasts (Passover, Pentecost, and Tabernacles---Exod. 23:14--17). ["The course of Abia"]{.class_4730-0}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_20 .class-1} is the eighth division in 1 Chronicles 24:10 (["Abijah"]{.class_4730-0} being the Old Testament spelling of ["Abia"]{.class_4730-0}; Abijah was one of the ["chief men"]{.class_4730-0} in the two households of the priests---1 Chron. 24:4).

Now we are not going into a long, detailed explanation here of how the times of service are calculated, but the men who have done that come out with the first time of service for ["the course of Abia"]{.class_4730-0} being December 6--12 and the second time of service being June 13--19. In the Hebrew calendar, that would be the 12th--18th of Chisleu and the 12th--18th of Sivan.

If you make verse 5 refer to the first ministration, then John the Baptist is conceived at the end of December, and nine months later in September, he is born around the Feast of Tabernacles. That would place the birth of Christ six months later around Passover.

But that won't do at all. You say, "Why not?" Look at John 1:14.

"And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us, (and we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father,) full of grace and truth."

That's a verse on the [incarnation]{.class_4729-0} of Jesus Christ. That's the Living Word taking on a body of flesh. Do you know what Simon Peter calls the body? He calls it a ["tabernacle"]{.class_4730-0} (2 Pet. 1:13--14); so does the Apostle Paul (2 Cor. 5:1). You see that word ["dwelt"]{.class_4730-0} there in John 1:14? That's translated from the Greek word [eskanosen,]{.class_4729-0} which means ["tabernacled."]{.class_4729-0} Jesus Christ wasn't born at Passover; He was born at the Feast of Tabernacles.

So ["the course of Abia"]{.class_4730-0} in verse 5 is the second ministration in June. That would make John the Baptist born at Passover, with Jesus Christ being born six months later at Tabernacles. It was the [conception]{.class_4729-0} of Jesus Christ that took place on December 25, [not His birth.]{.class_4729-0}

Back to verse 5: ["and his wife was of the daughters of Aaron, and her name was Elisabeth."]{.class_4730-0}[ ]{.class_4750}So Elizabeth was from the tribe of Levi, but if you look down at verse 36, you see that she was ["cousin"]{.class_4730-0} to Mary, who was from the tribe of Judah. The question is, how could this relationship be if the women were from two different tribes? The answer is found all the way back in Exodus 6:23. There Aaron marries the ["daughter of Amminadab,"]{.class_4730-0} and Amminadab []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_21}is from the tribe of Judah (1 Chron. 2:10). So the tribes of Levi and Judah are connected through marriage.

Now the typology should be obvious to the Christian who studies his Bible. Judah is the tribe of the King; Levi is the priestly tribe. Jesus Christ is not only the ["King of the Jews"]{.class_4730-0} and the ["King of kings"]{.class_4730-0} (1 Tim. 6:15), He's our great ["High Priest"]{.class_4730-0} (Heb. 3:1). The two came together in Aaron because of his marriage into the tribe of Judah.

Verse 6: ["And they were both righteous before God, walking in all the commandments and ordinances of the Lord blameless."]{.class_4730-0} Works! You're still in the Old Testament here in Luke 1.

["They were both righteous before God."]{.class_4730-0} Well, ["There is none righteous, no, not one"]{.class_4730-0} (Rom. 3:10). But you see, that is the difference between how the Old Testament uses the word [righteous]{.class_4729-0} and how the New Testament uses it.

The Fundamentalists who say that righteousness is the same under the Law as it is under grace know nothing about what they're talking. People in the Old Testament were considered righteous because they did the right thing, because they walked in the commandments.

["And it shall be OUR righteousness, if we observe to do all these commandments before the LORD our God, as he hath commanded us"]{.class_4730-0} (Deut. 6:25).

That's [not]{.class_4729-0} why [you]{.class_4729-0} are righteous. Paul says about the Jews of his day that were following the Old Testament Law and rejecting Christ:

["For they being ignorant of GOD'S righteousness\...have not submitted themselves unto the righteousness of GOD. For CHRIST is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth"]{.class_4730-0} (Rom. 10:3--4)

In the New Testament under grace, the rule is: ["NOT BY WORKS OF RIGHTEOUSNESS WHICH WE HAVE DONE, but according to his mercy he saved us, by the washing of regeneration, and renewing of the Holy Ghost"]{.class_4730-0} (Tit. 3:5).

[That's not the case in the Old Testament because there was no New Birth. In the Old Testament, salvation was a combination of personal faith in what God told a man to do and works that acted on that faith (see our remarks on Hab. 2:4 in the ]{.class_4728}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_22}[Bible Believer's Commentary on the Minor Prophets,]{.class_4729-1}[ Vol. II).]{.class_4728}

Didn't you ever notice back there before the Law that Job was called ["perfect and upright, and one that feared God, and eschewed ]{.class_4730-0}\[i.e., turned from\][ evil"]{.class_4730-0} (Job 1:1). That's the Holy Spirit commenting on Job's righteousness, and it's [works]{.class_4729-0} from start to finish. Paul will tell you real quick that before he trusted Christ, as a Jew under the Old Testament Law, he was counting on his [works]{.class_4729-0} to save him.

["Though I might also have confidence in the flesh, If any other man thinketh that he hath whereof he might trust in the flesh, I more"]{.class_4730-0} (Phil. 3:4); then in verses 5--6 of Philippians 3, he gives you his credentials. He ends that passage with: ["touching the righteousness which is in the law, blameless"]{.class_4730-0} (Phil. 3:6). That is, like Zacharias and Elizabeth, you couldn't legitimately accuse him of breaking any ordinance of the Law.

Old Testament salvation was faith and works. That's not New Testament salvation. New Testament salvation is ["the righteousness of God without the law"]{.class_4730-0} (Rom. 3:20); it's faith in Jesus Christ who did the work [for you]{.class_4729-0} (Rom. 3:21).

["But to him that worketh not, but believeth on him that justifieth the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness"]{.class_4730-0} (Rom. 4:5). [That's]{.class_4729-0} New Testament salvation.

There are two groups who don't get the difference between Old Testament and New Testament salvation: there are the ones who think you have to do something [right now]{.class_4729-0} for salvation, and then there are those who "get it" when it comes to salvation by grace through faith in the Church Age but won't rightly divide the Book when it comes to the Old Testament, the Tribulation, and the Millennium.

I have always had Campbellites who have followed up my radio program. They have no audience of their own, so they have to piggy-back on some other preacher's program to get a hearing. The one who currently comes on after me says, "These Baptists believe salvation is a free ticket; you have to earn it. There's something required for salvation; it is going to cost you something to be a []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_23}disciple."

Watch it, stupid! You're gonna cut your spiritual throat wide open from ear to ear. You started out talking about salvation. [Discipleship ain't salvation.]{.class_4729-0}

He goes on to say: "Salvation isn't free." [It is in the Bible.]{.class_4729-0}

["For the wages of sin is death; but the GIFT of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord"]{.class_4730-0} (Rom. 6:23).

["But not as the offence, so also is the FREE GIFT. For if through the offense of one many be dead, much more the grace of God, and the GIFT by grace, which is by one man, Jesus Christ, hath abounded unto many"]{.class_4730-0} (Rom. 5:15).

["And not as it was by one that sinned, so is the GIFT: for the judgment was by one to condemnation, but the FREE GIFT is of many offences unto justification"]{.class_4730-0} (Rom. 5:16).

["Therefore as by the offence of one judgment came upon all men to condemnation; even so by the righteousness of one the FREE GIFT came upon all men unto justification of life"]{.class_4730-0} (Rom. 5:18).

That's one side of the coin. The other side is everyone is saved the same way in the Old Testament and the New Testament: "the people in the Old Testament looked forward to the cross, and we look backward to the cross." Then don't you find it peculiar that the word ["faith"]{.class_4730-0} only appears twice in the Old Testament (Deut. 32:20; Hab. 2:4)? Nobody tells anybody to believe in the coming Messiah for salvation.

The rich young ruler comes up to Christ and says, ["Good Master, what good thing shall I do, that I may have eternal life?"]{.class_4730-0} (Matt. 19:16). What does Jesus tell him? "Well, just believe on me, and you'll have eternal life"; "Here's the free gift; help yourself"; ["For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God: Not of works, lest any man should boast"]{.class_4730-0} (Eph. 2:8--9)?

Not on your life, brother! He tells that young fellow: ["if thou wilt enter into life, KEEP THE COMMANDMENTS" ]{.class_4730-0}(Matt. 19:17). And when the young man told him what commandments he kept and how long he had kept them, Christ never contradicted him. He told him what to do to ["be perfect"]{.class_4730-0} and ["have treasure in heaven"]{.class_4730-0}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_24 .class-1} (Matt. 19:21) with no New Testament plan of salvation mentioned in the entire passage.

Now the [practical]{.class_4729-0} application of the passage to New Testament salvation is not lost to us. We certainly recognize the point Christ is trying to get across to the young man: ["Every man at his best state is altogether vanity"]{.class_4730-0} (Psa. 39:5). However, that does not change the [doctrine]{.class_4729-0} of the passage: Matthew 19 is still an Old Testament setting of [faith and works.]{.class_4729-0}

What changes everything between Luke 1; Matthew 19 and Romans 4; Philippians 3 is the vicarious atonement of Jesus Christ for our sins and the regenerating work of the Holy Spirit in the New Birth. The Old Testament had no sacrifice that could take away sin (Heb. 10:4), and no one was regenerated by the Holy Ghost. In the Old Testament, God accepted the good works of a man who believed Him until the man's sins were paid for by Jesus Christ on the cross (see our comments on Rom. 3:25 in that Commentary).

You had better study your Bible, folks. You've got to quit watching the idiot box (the boob tube, the glass toilet, etc.), and open up your Bible and READ IT! You'll never get the thing straight unless you spend time in that Book.

Back to Luke 1:[ "And they had no child, because that Elisabeth was barren, and they both were now well stricken in years"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 7). Zacharias and Elizabeth are in the same state as Abraham and Sarah (Rom. 4:19). That means they had to have divine intervention in order to have a child. That makes Elizabeth one of seven women in the Bible who experienced a miraculous birth, thus foreshadowing the virgin birth of Jesus Christ through Mary. They are: 1) [Sarah]{.class_4729-0} (Gen. 11:30), 2) [Rebekah]{.class_4729-0} (Gen. 25:21), 3) [Rachel]{.class_4729-0} (Gen. 30:1), 4) [Manoah's wife]{.class_4729-0} (Judg. 13:2), 5) [Hannah]{.class_4729-0} (1 Sam. 1:2), 6) [the great woman of Shunem]{.class_4729-0} (2 Kings 4:14), and 7) [Elizabeth]{.class_4729-0} (here). In each case, one of the children they bore (when they bore more than one) was a type of Jesus Christ. Sarah bore [Isaac,]{.class_4729-0} a type of Jesus Christ according to the Apostle Paul (Gal. 3:16; Heb. 11:17--19); Rebekah bore [Jacob,]{.class_4729-0} a type of Jesus Christ as ["the Prince"]{.class_4730-0} (Dan. 8:25, 9:25; Acts 3:15, 5:31); Rachel bore the greatest type of Jesus Christ in the Bible, [Joseph;]{.class_4729-0} Manoah's wife bore [Samson]{.class_4729-0} who killed more Philistines in his death than he did in his []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_25}lifetime, a picture of Christ's death that gives us victory over the Devil; Hannah gave birth to Samuel, and if you check out 1 Samuel 2, you will find Hannah using her son's birth to talk about the Second Advent of Jesus Christ like Mary does in her "Magnificat"; the great woman of Shunem's son got resurrected like Christ; and Elizabeth's son (John the Baptist) was so much like Jesus Christ in his ministry as a prophet (see Deut. 18:18--19) that people often mistook the two (John 1:21; Matt. 14:1--2, 16:13--14).

Verse 8 is a repeat of the information given in verse 5 about Zacharias working as a priest in his ["course."]{.class_4730-0} You can get the whole run-down in 1 Chronicles 24, and if there is any duller book than 1 Chronicles, I don't know what it is. Over three-quarters of the book is just a list of names: either genealogies or the Levites David appointed to serve in the Temple. It'll just about put you to sleep, man.

When I attended Bob Jones University, before each meal at the "dining common" (we called it a "mess hall" in the Army), each person at the table would have to quote a verse. The first year I was there, I was seated at the table with a character who quoted: ["At Parbar westward, four at the causeway, and two at Parbar."]{.class_4730-0} How about that? Did you get a spiritual blessing out of that? Did it bring revival to your soul? Of course, everybody at the table "ribbed" him about it and said it wasn't in the Bible, but it was. He was quoting 1 Chronicles 26:18.

I used to wonder about passages like that when I was a younger Christian. Why in the world would God put something like that in there when it had nothing to do with sin, salvation, Heaven, Hell, living right, or anything else of any importance? I think it took me thirty years to figure the thing out.

You see, that Bible isn't a "religious" book; it's a [history]{.class_4729-0} book. That's why the world doesn't like it: because it keeps running God in on history. Man wants his history separate from God so that God isn't running things.

The fact of the matter is that in history, God records something if it's connected with [His Son:]{.class_4729-0} that's what's important to God. God will let Kings and empires pass in history with little or no mention of them at all except where they cross the path of the people connected []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_26}with His Son. God will skip the whole line of Pharaohs and devote two pages of His Book to the names of Esau's and Jacob's descendants. Why is that? Why, Egypt was the world power of its time, but the line of Christ comes through Jacob, and Esau was Jacob's brother. God will ignore one of the most influential civilizations of its time (the Hittites) and talk about an Edomite finding some jackasses and feeding them: ["this was that Anah that found the mules in the wilderness, as he fed the asses of Zibeon his father"]{.class_4730-0} (Gen. 36:24). Glory, hallelujah! don't that make you want to shout and run the bases?! But that's a story about someone descended from a man who was the brother of someone who loved the Lord, so God stuck it in there. The practical lesson for us is that if you are interested in God's Son, God's interested in you, and if you're not, He won't give you the time of day (Matt. 7:23).

Now I must confess that when it came to reading through the Bible, I kind of balked at reading those long lists of names, like most of you do. I mean, they were just big lists of nobodies as far as I could tell, so I would skim those chapters, and I began to skip verses.

The Lord rebuked me for that and said, "Don't you go lying to those people and telling them you've read the Bible through eighty times when you're not reading all of it. Remember, ['Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God']{.class_4730-0} (Matt. 4:4). Aren't you always talking about those W-O-R-D-S? Well, slow down and READ THEM!"

So I slowed down, and I tried to tell myself that maybe I would get a blessing out of it. Well, at that time I was reading through 1 Chronicles 26, and just about the time I said to myself that maybe I would get a blessing, I ran into verse 5 in that chapter: ["Ammiel the sixth, Issachar the seventh, Peluthai the eighth: FOR GOD BLESSED HIM."]{.class_4730-0} God said to me, "You see there, if you had skipped that verse, you would have missed the blessing."

Brethren, you couldn't talk me out of believing that Book with a talking machine. I have seen it do stuff about which no Greek or Hebrew scholar knew anything; they had absolutely no idea what was going on.

Verse 9: ["According to the custom of the priest's office, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_27}his lot was to burn incense when he went into the temple of the Lord."]{.class_4730-0} There were two altars in the Tabernacle. One was the Brazen Altar out in the courtyard where the burnt offerings were made. That pictured the judgment of God on a sinner, so it's made of brass. A fire was to be burning in that altar continually, so it's a picture of Hell. Then in the first compartment of the Tabernacle, what was known as the Holy Place, there was a golden altar before the vail. That altar was more upright and looked about like a pulpit. It was called the "Altar of Incense." No burnt offerings were to be made on that altar. Instead, hot coals were brought from the Brazen Altar outside and used to light incense. Then the priest would take a golden censer (a curved plate held by three chains) that hung beside that altar and fill it with the incense and wave it around until the sweet smell of that smoke filled the inside of the Tabernacle. The Catholic Priests copy that in their church services because they think they've replaced the Old Testament priesthood.

The incense altar represents prayer (see vs. 10 here in Luke 1 and Rev. 8:3--4), and the picture is that God only accepts prayers that have been sanctified by the blood of the Lamb of God and His sacrifice for our sins on the cross. One time back there in the Old Testament, Aaron's two oldest boys, Nadad­­­­ and Abihu, offered ["strange fire"]{.class_4730-0} to the Lord (i.e., they used coals from somewhere besides the Brazen Altar to light the incense), and God killed both of them (Lev. 10:1--2). You can pray until you're blue in the face, and if you've never been saved through faith in the Lord Jesus Christ, you'll split Hell wide open (cf. Luke 18:10--14).

Now, as a priest served in his ["course,"]{.class_4730-0} he would be chosen by lot to perform different tasks; one of those was to offer incense on the golden altar in the Temple. ["The time of incense"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 10) was three times a day: morning, noon, and evening (Psa. 55:17; Dan. 6:10). The old fornicating raghead Mohammed tried to "one-up" David and Daniel by making his followers pray five times a day facing Mecca instead of Jerusalem. That was something else he stole from the Bible (see our remarks on Mohammed and plagiarism under vs. 2); all Mohammed could do was counterfeit other religions, mainly Judaism and the Bible (although you will find elements of Catholicism, Gnosticism, and Zoroastrianism in there as well; if []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_28}Mohammed ever had an original idea in his head, it died of loneliness). The Bible talks about Peter and John going to ["the temple at the hour of prayer, being the ninth hour"]{.class_4730-0} (Acts 3:1); that would be three o'clock in the afternoon, the time of the evening prayer.

So while Zacharias is there at the golden altar offering incense, he's praying, and according to what follows, he is praying for a child (vs. 13). God answers his prayer by sending an angel to announce the birth of John the Baptist (vs. 11). This is one of only two angels in the entire Scripture who are identified by name (Lucifer was not an angel as all the scholars mistakenly identify him; he was a cherub---Ezek. 28). In the Bible, there is [Michael;]{.class_4729-0} he is called ["the archangel"]{.class_4730-0} (Jude 9) because he is the chief of all the angels (Rev. 12:7). Michael is the angel who represents Israel before God (Dan. 12:1), so when the Church is called out at the Rapture and God resumes His dealings with Israel during the Tribulation, you have ["the voice of the archangel"]{.class_4730-0} as well as ["the trump of God"]{.class_4730-0} (see 1 Thess. 4:16 and comments on the verse in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on 1 & 2 Thessalonians and Philemon]{.class_4729-0}). The Roman Catholic teaching of seven archangels (with Lucifer and Gabriel being two of them) can be written off as more pagan mythology picked up and propagated as "Christian doctrine." The Bible only speaks of one archangel, and he is Michael.

The angel here identifies himself as ["Gabriel"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 19). That is the angel who spoke to Daniel in Daniel 8:16, 9:21 and gave him the revelation of the Seventy Weeks (Dan. 9:24--27). He is the one who appears to Mary to announce the birth of Christ in verse 26 here in Luke 1. (There is no indication from Scripture that he is the same angel who appeared to Joseph in Matt. 1, although that would certainly be a reasonable assumption given the two appearances here.)

And by sheer coincidence (don't you know), Gabriel just happens to be the angel who appears to Mohammed to recite the [Koran]{.class_4729-0} to him. Of course, Mohammed's Gabriel was quite a sight. According to the second most holy book in Islam, the [Hadith,]{.class_4729-0} wonder of wonders, the Gabriel that appeared to Mohammed had [600 wings]{.class_4729-0} (Bukhari, Vol. VII, No. 380).

[No angel in the Bible has wings.]{.class_4729-0}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_29 .class-1} You say, "But doesn't the Bible talk about angels flying (Dan. 9:21; Rev. 8:13)?" The comic books are full of men (and women) flying around without wings. Where do you think those two Jews got their idea for "Superman"? They got it out of a [King James]{.class_4729-0} Bible. In the [King James]{.class_4729-0} Bible, no angel has wings.

The Bible talks about ["some have entertained angels unawares"]{.class_4730-0} (Heb. 13:1). Well, if a man or woman showed up at your door with a ten to fifteen-foot wingspan, I think you would be "aware" of that, wouldn't you? Lot entertained a couple in Genesis 19 and had no idea who they were until they blinded the men of Sodom. He didn't recognize them as angels (and neither did the Sodomites) because they appeared as normal men with [no wings.]{.class_4729-0}

Mohammed's "Gabriel" was like Joseph Smith's [Moroni]{.class_4729-0} and Friedrich Nietzsche's [Thus Spake Zarathustra:]{.class_4729-0} it was Satan transforming himself ["into an angel of light"]{.class_4730-0} (2 Cor. 11:14) for the purpose of damning billions of suckers (Gal. 1:8). Paul warned you in Colossians 2:18 about men coming to you pretending to worship angels and putting stuff on you (which they supposedly got from the angels) about which they know nothing.

You want proof that the "Gabriel" of the [Koran]{.class_4729-0} was Satan masquerading as an angel? Okay, here we go. From the official commentary of the [Koran,]{.class_4729-0} the ["Holy" Hadith,]{.class_4729-0} from the most accepted version of the [Hadith]{.class_4729-0} (Bukhari, Vol. IV, No. 546): "Abdullah said, 'He \[i.e., Gabriel\], from amongst all the angels, is THE ENEMY OF THE JEWS."

Revelation 12: ["And the great dragon was cast out, that old serpent, called the DEVIL, AND SATAN\...And when the dragon saw that he was cast unto the earth, he persecuted the woman which brought forth the man child]{.class_4730-0} \[who's that woman? It's Israel, the Jews---see our comments on Rev. 12:1--2 in that Commentary\][\....And the dragon was wroth with the woman, and went to make war with the remnant of her seed"]{.class_4730-0} (Rev. 12:9, 13, 17).

In the Holy Bible, [Satan]{.class_4729-0} is the enemy of the Jews. That's why he shows up in Job 1--2 accusing Job (cf. Rev. 12:10), because Job pictures that Jew in the Tribulation (see the Introduction to the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Job]{.class_4729-0}). The "Gabriel" of Mohammed is []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_30}no more the Gabriel of the Bible than "Allah" ("the god"---see 2 Cor. 4:4; Dan. 11:38) is Jehovah. For further study, get our works on [The Holy Scriptures vs. the Holy Koran, Will the Real "Allah" Please Stand Up?, The Source of International Terrorism,]{.class_4729-0} and another work by a gentleman named Abdullah Aziz. It is a comic book entitled [Mohammed's Believe It or Else!. ]{.class_4729-0}Or else what? Or else you'll get your head cut off. Aziz will give you exactly what the [Koran]{.class_4729-0} and the [Hadith]{.class_4729-0} say (which if you don't believe it, you are to be killed by the Mohammedans under Sharia law): stuff like Adam being ninety feet tall, Jews being apes and pigs, Allah destroying a people because they wouldn't listen to the preaching of a giant camel the size of a mountain, Satan sleeping in your nose at night, Satan peeing in your ear if you fall asleep while praying, your eyes popping out of your head if you look up to Heaven while praying, and Mohammed's command to drink camel urine.

You are to be killed if you don't believe all that crap. That's what Obama is trying to set up here in the United States; it's already going on in Britain and France. That's not just a bunch of "radical terrorists" or "Moslem extremists" who believe that; it's the whole lot of crazy, cockeyed, murdering nuts---all one billion of them.

Back to Luke 1: ["And when Zacharias saw him, he was troubled, and fear fell upon him"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 12). That's the common reaction in the Bible to someone seeing an angel of the Lord show up. When the angel appears to the shepherds to announce the birth of Christ, the Bible says: ["they were sore afraid"]{.class_4730-0} (Luke 2:9). The women ["were afraid"]{.class_4730-0} when the angels appeared to them at the empty tomb (Luke 24:4--5).

["But the angel said unto him, Fear not, Zacharias"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 13). Someone counted up the "fear nots" in the Bible one time and supposedly came up with 365---one for each day of the year. Whether that is so or not, I don't know, but I do know that wisdom is knowing when to be afraid and when not to be afraid. ["The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom"]{.class_4730-0} (Prov. 9:10), but when the Lord has delivered you from a fear (2 Tim. 1:7; 1 John 4:18; Heb. 13:6), you don't have to live in terror of it anymore.

["Thy wife Elisabeth shall bear thee a son, and thou shalt call his name John."]{.class_4730-0} John is the most common name in the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_31}English language. In the Bible, you have John the Baptist, the Apostle John, John Mark, and one of the chief priests named John (Acts 4:6). Some famous Johns in church history were: John Chrysostom---"the Golden Mouth," John Huss, John Knox, John Wycliffe---"the Morningstar of the Reformation," John Wesley, John Calvin, John Paton---missionary to the New Hebrides islands in the South Pacific, John R. Rice---the evangelist, and John Rawlings---the former pastor of Landmark Baptist Temple in Cincinnati, Ohio. At least four Presidents of the United States were named John: John Adams, John Quincy Adams, John Tyler, and the fornicating Roman Catholic John Fitzgerald Kennedy. John is the first Gentile name in Jewish history. This was a warning that the Messiah was going to save and rule over the Gentiles just as He would His own people.

John the Baptist is one of six men in the Bible who were named before they were born. The others are: [Ishmael]{.class_4729-0} (Gen. 16:11), [Isaac]{.class_4729-0} (Gen. 17:19), [Josiah]{.class_4729-0} (1 Kings 13:2), [Cyrus]{.class_4729-0} (Isa. 44:28--45:1), and[ Jesus Christ Himself ]{.class_4729-0}(vs. 31; Matt. 1:21).

["He shall be great in the sight of the Lord"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 15). He certainly was. Jesus said of John the Baptist: ["Among them that are born of women there hath not risen a greater than John the Baptist"]{.class_4730-0} (Matt. 11:11).

["And shall drink neither wine nor strong drink."]{.class_4730-0} That was part of the oath of the Nazarite (Num. 6:3). John the Baptist was called to observe that from the womb just as Samson was (Judg. 13:4--5, 7, 14).

["He shall be filled with the Holy Ghost, even from his mother's womb."]{.class_4730-0} John the Baptist was the only man born like that, but like Jeremiah, he was called to be a prophet from the womb (Jer. 1:5). Because of that, the "pro-lifers" (the anti-abortionists) use Jeremiah, John the Baptist, and Jesus Christ to prove abortion is murder. But that is stretching things a bit. Jeremiah 1:5 was addressed to a grown man who was not aborted; Jesus Christ was the virgin-born Son of God; John the Baptist was the only man ever to be filled with the Holy Ghost from the womb. They are the exceptions, not the rule.

Verse 17 is a quote from Malachi 4:5--6. Notice that the ["him"]{.class_4730-0} of verse 17 is ["the LORD"]{.class_4730-0} of Malachi 4:5, proving that Jesus Christ is []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_32}the Jehovah of the Old Testament. This is the same thing Mark does in his Gospel. In Mark 1:2, he is quoting Malachi 3:1 where Jehovah says that John the Baptist will ["prepare the way BEFORE ME]{.class_4730-0} \[i.e., Jehovah\][."]{.class_4730-0} Mark changes the ["before me"]{.class_4730-0} to ["before THEE,"]{.class_4730-0} referring to Jesus Christ. The ["thee"]{.class_4730-0} of Mark 1:2 (Jesus) is the ["me"]{.class_4730-0} of Malachi 3:1 (Jehovah). Here in Luke 1:17, the antecedent of ["him"]{.class_4730-0} is ["the Lord their God"]{.class_4730-0} in verse 16, proving again that Jesus Christ is God Almighty in the flesh (John 1:14; 1 Tim. 3:16).

Now one of the things into which you are going to run in the Old Testament that just messes up all the scholars is known as "double application," and Luke 1:17 is a good example. You see, the passage Gabriel is giving Zacharias has nothing to do with the First Advent of a suffering Saviour at all. Look at Malachi 4:5.

"Behold, I send you Elijah the prophet before the coming of the great and dreadful DAY OF THE LORD."

There was nothing ["great and dreadful"]{.class_4730-0} about Christ's First Coming. He was ["meek and lowly in heart"]{.class_4730-0} (Matt. 11:29). The Bible says, ["A bruised reed shall he not break, and the smoking flax shall he not quench"]{.class_4730-0} (Isa. 42:3; Matt. 12:20). He was the suffering Saviour who came to atone for the sins of mankind (Isa. 53), not the conquering King coming to judge the nations. The ["day of the LORD"]{.class_4730-0} is a reference to the Second Coming, not the First.

But often in the Old Testament, the First and Second Advents are spoken of right together, sometimes separated by only a comma or a semicolon. We'll discuss this more in our comments on 4:18--19. Here, John the Baptist is the perfect example of the problem. Look at Isaiah 40:1--5.

"Comfort ye, comfort ye my people, saith your God. Speak ye comfortably to Jerusalem, and cry unto her, that her warfare is accomplished, that her iniquity is pardoned\...."

Well, not yet it isn't. It won't be until Jesus Christ returns (Rom. 11:26--27). Isaiah 40:2 wasn't fulfilled at Christ's First Coming, but look at verse 3 in that chapter.

["The voice of him that crieth in the wilderness, Prepare []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_33}ye the way of the LORD, make straight in the desert a highway for our God."]{.class_4730-0} That's John the Baptist (Mark 1:3--4; John 1:23). The Holy Spirit went from the Second Advent in verses 1--2 to the First Advent in verse 3 with no warning. He'll go back to the Second Advent in Isaiah 40:4--5.

["Every valley shall be exalted, and every mountain and hill shall be made low ]{.class_4730-0}\[never took place at the First Coming, but it will at the Second---Deut. 33:2 cf. Judg. 5:5\][: and the crooked shall be made straight, and the rough places plain: And the glory of the LORD shall be revealed, and all flesh shall see it together."]{.class_4730-0}

That doesn't take place until Jesus Christ shows up in power at the Second Advent (Rev. 1:7). That's a First Advent verse sandwiched right between two Second Advent passages. Those Old Testament authors like Isaiah would write something like that, and they couldn't figure out what was going on. Simon Peter wrote:

["Searching what, or what manner of time the Spirit of Christ which was in them did signify, when it testified beforehand the sufferings of Christ, and the glory that should follow"]{.class_4730-0} (1 Pet. 1:11).

All through the Gospels and up until Acts 7, God had things worked out so that the First Advent could have been the Second Advent IF (and get that little two-letter conditional word because it makes all the difference) the Jews had accepted Jesus Christ as their Messiah. Understand that He still would have had to die for our sins to fulfill the Scriptures, but the Jews would not have been guilty of His death (Matt. 27:25). [If]{.class_4729-0} that had been the case, there could have been a rapture in Acts 7 with the Tribulation following. The ["son of perdition"]{.class_4730-0} (John 17:12 cf. 2 Thess. 2:2) would have come up from the Bottomless Pit (Acts 1:25 cf. Rev. 9:11) and taken his place as Caesar in Rome, and away she would have gone, boy. But you see, that was conditioned upon what those Jews did with Jesus Christ.

That just throws a Calvinist for a loop. He figures God's foreknowledge is immutable and His decrees are eternal, and neither one of those suppositions is true. In 1 Samuel 23, David learned that Saul had heard he was at Keilah. David prayed to God and asked if Saul would come down to get him and if the men of Keilah would deliver him up to Saul. God told David [yes]{.class_4729-0} to both questions. []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_34 .class-1}[Neither thing took place,]{.class_4729-0} even though [God said it would.]{.class_4729-0} Why not? [David got out of there.]{.class_4729-0} He altered God's foreknowledge with an act of [free will.]{.class_4729-0} In other words, God not only knows what [will ]{.class_4729-0}happen, He knows what [could ]{.class_4729-0}happen given whatever decision you make. John Calvin's "God" was a weak, anemic Deity who couldn't handle contingencies like that.

Do you know what the "eternal decree" of God is for every sinner on the face of this earth? It's Hell: ["The wages of sin is death"]{.class_4730-0} (Rom. 6:23); ["The soul that sinneth, it shall die"]{.class_4730-0} (Ezek. 18:20). When it comes to Calvin's "decree of reprobation," every human being on earth is under that decree, because ["all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God"]{.class_4730-0} (Rom. 3:23).

You say, "But what about the decree of election?" The decree of election [as applied to you]{.class_4729-0} is not rooted in eternity; it's rooted in [time.]{.class_4729-0}

["According as he hath chosen us IN HIM before the foundation of the world"]{.class_4730-0} (Eph. 1:4). No one was [in Christ]{.class_4729-0} before Genesis 1:1. You are in Christ when the Holy Spirit puts you there [at salvation]{.class_4729-0} (Eph. 1:13 cf. 1 Cor. 12:13). Imagine being [in Christ]{.class_4729-0} before Genesis 1:1, being kicked out of Christ and put [in Adam]{.class_4729-0} in Genesis 3, being born ["dead in trespasses and sins"]{.class_4730-0} (Eph. 2:1) after having been in Christ ["before the foundation of the world,"]{.class_4730-0} and then being forced by "irresistible grace" to be [put back]{.class_4729-0} in Christ upon salvation. You talk about a tangled, fouled-up mess, boy, did Calvin ever make one of that!

What God chose ("elected") before Genesis 1:1 was the [means]{.class_4729-0} of our salvation: the vicarious sacrifice of Jesus Christ for our sins. God determined that anyone who came to Him through His Son would be "elected" and anyone who didn't, wouldn't. But as Sam Jones, the old-time Methodist evangelist, used to say, "You have to be [nominated]{.class_4729-0} before you can be elected"; that is, you have to be [in the running,]{.class_4729-0} you have to "throw your hat into the ring."

Do you know what God's [will]{.class_4729-0} is for every sinner on earth?

["The Lord is not slack concerning his promise, as some men count slackness; but is longsuffering to us-ward, NOT WILLING THAT ANY SHOULD PERISH, BUT THAT ALL SHOULD COME TO REPENTANCE"]{.class_4730-1}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_35}[ (2 Pet. 3:9).]{.class_4728}

["WHO WILL HAVE ALL MEN TO BE SAVED, and to come to the knowledge of the truth"]{.class_4730-0} (1 Tim. 2:4).

If God had His "druthers," no one would go to Hell. You say, "Then why do they?" Because God's holiness demands judgment on sin, and sinners turn down the ["free gift"]{.class_4730-0} of God (Rom. 6:23, 5:15--18; Eph. 2:8--9) that satisfied God's holiness.

Friend, do you know what I would do if I knew I was under the "decree of reprobation"? I would come to Jesus Christ just as quickly as I could (Matt. 11:28; John 7:37; Rev. 22:17), because He promised if I did, He wouldn't cast me out (John 6:37). I would [change]{.class_4729-0} God's "eternal decree" by receiving Christ (John 1:12). I did just that on March 14, 1949.

Now back to this double application on John the Baptist. The quotation in Malachi 4:5--6 says Elijah will be the forerunner of Christ at the Second Advent, but Gabriel says John the Baptist is the forerunner of Jesus Christ at the First Advent because he came ["in the spirit and power"]{.class_4730-0} of Elijah. In other words, John [could]{.class_4729-0} have been the fulfillment of Malachi 4:5--6 [if ]{.class_4729-0}the Jews had accepted his testimony about Christ.

Was John the Baptist Elijah? John himself said he wasn't: ["Art thou Elias? And he saith, I AM NOT"]{.class_4730-0} (John 1:21). But Christ said he was: ["And if ye will receive it, THIS IS ELIAS, which was for to come"]{.class_4730-0} (Matt. 11:14).

But notice the conditional clause on that last verse: ["IF ye will receive it."]{.class_4730-0} Did they? No: ["But I say unto you, that Elias is come already, and they knew him not, but have done unto him whatsoever they listed"]{.class_4730-0} (Matt. 17:12). So what happens? Everything's put off until the literal fulfillment of Malachi 4:5--6 at the Second Advent: ["Elias truly SHALL first come, and restore all things"]{.class_4730-0} (Matt. 17:11). God had the thing flexible enough so that He could have gone either way depending on what those Jews did. Prophecy could have been fulfilled either way, and God's integrity would have remained intact (Rom. 3:4).

That thing is right in Luke 1:17. Notice how Gabriel changes the prophecy of Malachi 4:6.

["And he shall go before him in the spirit and power of Elias, to turn the hearts of the fathers to the children\...."]{.class_4730-0}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ102.xhtml_page_36 .class-1} So far, so good. That's the Second Advent like Malachi gave it to you. But watch Gabriel change it. ["\...and the disobedient to the wisdom of the just; to make ready a people prepared for the Lord."]{.class_4730-0} There's the Church Age and the First Advent. They're just given backwards to mess up all the scholars who won't rightly divide the Book.

Now, Zacharias is having the same trouble Abraham had in Genesis 17:17, and that Sarah had in Genesis 18:11--12. Zacharias had a hard time believing what God said because he knew he and Elizabeth were too old to have children. God struck him dumb (i.e., unable to talk; not being stupid) because he didn't believe the W-O-R-D-S (vs. 20).

That has happened to some of the translators of the new versions. Kenneth Taylor ([The Living Bible]{.class_4729-0}) lost his voice; Philip Schaff, the chairman of the [ASV]{.class_4729-0} committee, lost his voice; B. F.
