The answer, of course, is [Australia.]{.class_4725} Australia didn't suddenly "become" the largest island simply because somebody found out about it; it always was whether anybody "discovered" it or not. The thing to remember about "Biblical scholarship" and "Biblical archaeology" is that the Bible wasn't "wrong" until scholarship and archaeology said it was "right"; [it was never wrong to begin with. ]{.class_4725}The [facts]{.class_4725} of the Bible were accurate all along; and scholarship, science, and archaeology just happened to find verification outside of the Scriptures that what the Bible said was [right all along.]{.class_4725}

One time, somebody wrote Stewart Custer, the head of the Bible department at Bob Jones University, and asked him if he had a copy of an infallible Bible. He had been telling his students for [years]{.class_4725} that [no translation]{.class_4725} of the Scriptures was perfect. (If he had been completely honest, he would have told them that no [copy]{.class_4725} or [text]{.class_4725} of [any]{.class_4725} Bible in Greek or Hebrew was perfect either. That's what he believes; if you don't believe me, write him and ask him.)

Stewart Custer wrote that fellow back and said, "As for having an infallible Bible, yes, I have one, and I read it every day. The Greek text I have in my hands, I will defend every word of it." Of course, he []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_72}never told you what that Greek text was (there are more than 26 of them), but if it was the text he taught in class, it was [Nestle's Greek text.]{.class_4725}

Well, it wasn't long after that that Nestle's Greek New Testament was [changed]{.class_4725} in something like 500 places; many of those changes [reinstated the King James readings]{.class_4725} into the text after having rejected them for 85 years. Suddenly, Stewart Custer's "infallible Greek text" that he would "defend every word of it" became [indefensible]{.class_4725} in 500 places. And not only that, but the text that he had been attacking in the classroom (the [AV]{.class_4725} 1611), the editors of Nestle's text now admitted was right all along in those 500 places. That is, an old grandmother in the hills of North Carolina, sitting in her rocking chair reading and believing her [KJV]{.class_4725} Blue-ribbon Hertel Bible, [had more sense]{.class_4725} than a professor at Bob Jones University who read his Nestle's Greek New Testament every day.

Notice, again, the prophetic typology in verse 2. Right after talking about a Roman dictator levying a worldwide tax, the Holy Spirit connects that to a Syrian Governor. Why is that?

Well, the first "Jew" (Jacob--Israel) was ["a Syrian"]{.class_4723} by nationality (Deut. 26:5). The ["little horn"]{.class_4723} of Daniel 7:8, 19--20 (the Antichrist) is pictured historically by Antiochus Epiphanes, the King of [Syria]{.class_4725} (see Scofield's notes on Dan. 8:9, 11:2). In the Gospels, the ["son of perdition"]{.class_4723} (John 17:12 cf. 2 Thess. 2:3) is from [Kerioth in Moab]{.class_4725} (Jer. 48:25); Judas was called "Iscariot," meaning "man of Kerioth." So verses 1--2 of Luke 2 is a warning that the upcoming Roman dictator will be a [Syrian Jew.]{.class_4725}

["And Joseph also went up from Galilee, out of the city of Nazareth, into Judaea, unto the city of David, which is called Bethlehem; (because he was of the house and lineage of David:)"]{.class_4723} (vs. 4). Now what you are seeing in verses 1--4 is a case of God and the Devil working together to accomplish different ends like you find it in the book of Job. In the book of Job, the Devil is trying to break Job and turn him against God and God against him, but it is God Himself who brings up Job and turns him over to Satan to expose Job's self-righteousness so it can be dealt with.

One of the great "contradictions" in the Scriptures is found in []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_73}comparing 2 Samuel 24:1 to 1 Chronicles 21:1. The passage in 2 Samuel 24 says the [Lord]{.class_4725} moved David to number Israel, while 1 Chronicles 21 says [Satan]{.class_4725} did it. Which one was it? It was [both:]{.class_4725} it was [God operating through the Devil]{.class_4725} to judge the sin of His people.

What is going on here in Luke 2 is that God is working things out to fulfill prophecy, while Satan is working to kill the baby before it's born, or at least try to have it born prematurely before it reaches Bethlehem. Nazareth is something like 100 miles from Bethlehem, and Mary is traveling that distance on a donkey or mule. She's only a few days away from giving birth. Don't you know that was a rough ride for her? The risks were either that she would miscarry or that she would go into labor early and the baby would be born somewhere else besides Bethlehem and break the prophecy of Christ's birth.

Micah 5:2, ["But thou, Bethlehem Ephratah, though thou be little among the thousands of Judah, yet out of thee shall he come forth unto me that is to be ruler in Israel; whose goings forth have been from of old, from everlasting."]{.class_4723}

That's the prophecy of Christ's birthplace made 750 years before it took place. The [Koran]{.class_4725} and the [Book of Mormon]{.class_4725} have nothing like it. That would be like me saying that in the year 2762 a man named Ted Butler will be born in Boise, Idaho. He will live to be 55½ years old and will be killed in downtown Chicago at Mulberry and 6th Streets at 4:30 p.m. while crossing the street. His funeral will be at Memorial Gardens on the east side of Chicago, and fifty people will be in attendance. What would be the odds of all that coming to pass?

Well, in the case of Jesus Christ, the Old Testament makes 48 detailed prophecies on His First Advent. A mathematician named Peter Stoner calculated the odds of all those prophecies being fulfilled. He came up with the statistic that for one man to fulfill all 48 prophecies would be one out of ten to the 157th power: that's 10 with 157 zeroes after it. There are not that many electrons in three universes the size of ours. Moreover, if the chances of a thing taking place are one out of ten to the 50th power (ten with fifty zeroes after it), that thing will [never]{.class_4725} take place. Einstein and Hawking couldn't pull off what Jesus Christ did at His First Advent.

An infidel sarcastically asked one of our young men who brought this up: "Would that be the Peter Stoner who taught at Wheaton College (a Christian institution)?" Our young man wisely answered, "That would be the Peter Stoner who did the calculations on the factual, historical material." The fact that Peter Stoner was a Christian who taught at a Christian College doesn't change the laws of statistical probability.

I'll never forget when I debated the Catholic lawyer Karl Keating. When I pointed out that statistical probability proved the infallibility of the Bible, he said, "I never thought I would hear an argument for the Bible that Carl Sagan uses to prove there's life on other planets." Problem, Karl: [no one has ever found life on other planets.]{.class_4725} Jesus Christ fulfilled 48 Old Testament prophecies made anywhere from 400 to 1,500 years before He was born; those are [historical facts.]{.class_4725} It's not anybody "supposing" anything.

A funny side note on the prophecy of Micah 5:2 was when Herod asked the Rabbis where the Christ child would be born, they changed the verse to make themselves look good. Micah said Bethlehem was ["little among the thousands of Juda."]{.class_4723} The Rabbis misquoted the verse to Herod and said Bethlehem was ["NOT the least among the princes of Juda"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 2:6). Bethlehem of Judah was so small that it is missing from the catalog of cities in Joshua, and it is not mentioned in Nehemiah 11:25 among the villages rebuilt after the Jews returned from captivity.

Bethlehem was not known because of its size (see our comments on Zech. 4:10 in that Commentary); it was known because it was the birthplace of King David (Ruth 4:11, 17; 1 Sam. 16:1--4). That is why it is called ["the city of David"]{.class_4723} in Luke 2 (cf. 1 Sam. 20:6). David is one of the greatest types of Jesus Christ in the Bible, second only to Joseph in Genesis. David is said to be ["as an angel of God"]{.class_4723} (1 Sam. 29:9; 2 Sam. 14:17, 20, 19:27), just as Jesus Christ (Acts 27:23; Gal. 4:4). He was picked out from among his brothers and anointed to be King (1 Sam. 16:13; Heb. 1:9). Like Christ, he was anointed to be King years before he took the throne. He kills Goliath with a stone to the head; Christ is the Smiting Stone of Daniel 2:44--45 (cf. Matt. 21:42--44), and at His Second Advent, He bruises the serpent's head in the person of the Antichrist (Gen. 3:15; Hab. 3:13). Like Christ, David []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_75}was a prophet (Acts 2:29--30), a priest (2 Sam. 24:25), and a King.

Over and over again in the Psalms, you see David speaking in the first person with no reference at all to anything that takes place in his life. What's he doing? He's speaking prophetically about Jesus Christ (see Acts 2:36). If you want a good example of that, read Psalm 22. The whole chapter is about the crucifixion of Jesus Christ, and David gives it in the first person as if it's happening to him.

Now, Christ is born in Bethlehem. Do you know what Bethlehem means? It means "the house of bread." Jesus said, ["I am the bread of life"]{.class_4723} (John 6:35). So when the Bread of Life came down from Heaven, He came down to the "house of bread"---Bethlehem.

Verse 7: ["And she brought forth her firstborn son."]{.class_4723} The cross-reference is Matthew 1:25, where the new "Bibles" remove the word ["firstborn."]{.class_4723} They couldn't get rid of the word here in Luke, but they got rid of it in Matthew. So the excuse they give you for attacking a doctrine of the word of God is that since you can find it [somewhere]{.class_4725} in the Scripture, it is all right to get rid of it in another part of the Scripture. Lester Roloff was really strong against that kind of thing. He likened those Bible corrupters to a fellow attacking his mother with a butcher knife. Well, would you like someone cutting off one of your mother's fingers with the excuse that she still has nine left? No? [Then keep your dirty, stinking, filthy hands off my Book!]{.class_4725}

What's going on in Matthew 1:25 is that the word ["firstborn"]{.class_4723} implies Mary had [other]{.class_4725} sons (they are listed by name in Mark 6:3). So to protect the Roman Catholic doctrine of the Perpetual Virginity of Mary (see our comments on 1:36), the modern translations get rid of the word ["firstborn"]{.class_4723} from the text; that is, the modern versions are [Roman Catholic Bibles.]{.class_4725} Way back in 1970, I wrote a book entitled [The Christian's Handbook of Manuscript Evidence]{.class_4725} which just threw all the professors and faculties of the Christian Colleges and Universities for a loop; they have never successfully answered the material I presented in that book. In chapter ten of that work, I argued that all the modern versions of the Bible were basically Roman Catholic translations, and I gave you 23 examples of passages where the new "Bibles" [matched]{.class_4725} the Jesuit [Rheims Version]{.class_4725} of 1582 and the Catholic [Confraternity Version]{.class_4725} of 1941.

["And she brought forth her firstborn son, and wrapped him in swaddling clothes."]{.class_4747}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_76 .class-1} To "swaddle" something is to wrap something tight like you would with a bandage. ["Swaddling clothes"]{.class_4747} were broad linen strips of cloth like bandages that were wrapped around a body to keep the child warm. But the way they were wrapped around the body was like a [mummy. This baby was born to die.]{.class_4746}

If you want to see how that baby would be wrapped when He died, look at Acts 5:6. He's not wrapped from head to foot longways like the Roman Catholic "Shroud of Turin."

"And the young men arose, WOUND HIM UP, and carried him out, and buried him."

That's the Holy Spirit killing Ananias for lying, and when Ananias dies, they wind him up like a mummy. That's what Nicodemus and Joseph of Arimathea did to Jesus when He died.

["Then took they the body of Jesus, and WOUND IT IN LINEN CLOTHES with the spices, as the manner of the Jews is to bury"]{.class_4723} (John 19:40).

So when this baby is born, He is wrapped in the same type of clothes and in the same manner as He was when He was buried.

["And she brought forth her firstborn son, and wrapped him in swaddling clothes, and laid him in a manger."]{.class_4723} Now the ["manger"]{.class_4723} itself is actually the feed trough for the livestock, but the tendency is to use the term for the stall where Jesus was born. The common expression is: "Close the door! Were you RAISED IN A BARN?" When Jesus Christ came down from the ivory palaces of Glory, the first place He was raised was [in a barn.]{.class_4725}

The Roman Catholics convert the ["manger"]{.class_4723} into a [cave;]{.class_4727} hence the Christmas carol "In a Cave" which you find in some hymn books. They get that godless mess from a Gnostic work called [The Gospel of the Infancy of Jesus Christ;]{.class_4727} they don't get it from the Bible.

["And she brought forth her firstborn son, and wrapped him in swaddling clothes, and laid him in a manger, because there was no room for them in the inn."]{.class_4723} The town's all filled up because of the taxation and the Feast of Tabernacles (Bethlehem's only about twelve miles from Jerusalem). "No room, lady; go back to the stable and have your baby."

Now isn't that something? But things haven't changed much in []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_77}2,000 years. There's no room for Jesus Christ in the holiday that's supposed to be celebrating His birth. Children can't sing Christmas carols anymore in the public schools. Why not? Because the carols are about [Jesus Christ.]{.class_4725} People don't mind getting the day off for CHRISTmas, but don't mention His name. They don't mind celebrating the "holy day" (that's what [holiday]{.class_4725} means---"holy day"), but don't you dare sing "Silent Night, HOLY NIGHT." The move now is not even to say "Merry Christmas."

Back in 1960, in one of those "Ivy League" Universities up in New England, the students were given a "response test" in one of their classes. In a response test, the student is given a word and is to write down the first thing that comes to his mind about that word. Bob Jones Sr. used to say: "You can judge a man by his response." That's true. You can generally judge whether a fellow is saved or not by how he responds to [Jesus Christ.]{.class_4725} Anyway, during that test, 45 students were given the word [Christmas. The name Jesus Christ didn't occur on one paper.]{.class_4725} No room in the inn.

"Have you any room for Jesus, He who bore your load of sin?" No, they don't. They have room for Santa Claus, Rudolph the Red-Nosed Reindeer, Frosty the Snowman, the Little Drummer Boy; [but]{.class_4725} [no room for Jesus Christ.]{.class_4725}

["And there were in the same country shepherds abiding in the field, keeping watch over their flock by night"]{.class_4723} (vs. 8). The Good Shepherd and the Lamb of God has been born, so the first ones to hear the news are shepherds keeping sheep. Some of the greatest types of Christ in the Bible were shepherds. [Abel]{.class_4725} was a shepherd; [Jacob and his sons]{.class_4725} were shepherds; [Moses]{.class_4725} was a shepherd; [David]{.class_4725} was a shepherd. Christ is a shepherd who "watches over His flock by night" (1 Pet. 5:4 cf. 2 Pet. 1:19).

["And, lo, the angel of the Lord came upon them"]{.class_4723} (vs. 9). The angel is unidentified here, but he is called ["the angel of the Lord."]{.class_4723} Throughout the Old Testament, that's a reference to Jesus Christ appearing to men in His pre-incarnate state. In theology, that's called a "Christophany" or a "theophany." The problem is that here in Luke 2, Jesus Christ is a baby in a manger. But look at Revelation chapters 4--5. In those chapters, you have God the Father [on]{.class_4727} the throne (Rev. 4:2--3), God the Holy Spirit [before]{.class_4727} the throne (Rev. 4:5), God the Son [coming to]{.class_4727}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_78} the throne (Rev. 5:6--7), and God the Holy Spirit [in Jesus Christ]{.class_4727} (Rev. 5:6). The Lord has no problem in being in two or more places at once (He's omnipresent, remember?) and appearing in more than one form. In Revelation 4--5, He takes the form of [lamps]{.class_4727} (Rev. 4:5), a [lion]{.class_4727} (Rev. 5:5), a [lamb]{.class_4727} (Rev. 5:6), [eyes]{.class_4727} (Rev. 5:6), and a [man]{.class_4727} (Rev. 5:7). So ["the angel of the Lord"]{.class_4723} here in verse 9 could be one particular angel sent by God, or he could be an angelic appearance of God or Jesus Christ as you read in the Old Testament.

["And the glory of the Lord shone around about them."]{.class_4723} This is what Moses and the children of Israel saw on Mt. Sinai (Exod. 24:16--17) and in the Tabernacle (Lev. 9:23); it is what the priests saw in the Temple (1 Kings 8:11; 2 Chron. 5:14). It's what appears at the Second Advent as a bright, shining light (Isa. 58:8, 60:1).

["And they were sore afraid"]{.class_4723} (see our comments on 1:12).

["And the angel said unto them, Fear not ]{.class_4723}\[see our comments on 1:13\][: for, behold, I bring you good tidings of great joy ]{.class_4723}\[the book of Luke opens with ["great joy"]{.class_4723} here, and it closes with ["great joy"]{.class_4723} in 24:52\][, which shall be to all people"]{.class_4723} (vs. 10). That's a warning to those Jews that the Messiah isn't going to be just for Israel. You'll see the same thing even clearer down in verse 32: ["A light to lighten the GENTILES."]{.class_4723} So from the birth of His Son, the Lord is telling you that His intention is to ["save"]{.class_4723}[ more]{.class_4725} than ["his people]{.class_4723} \[the [Jews]{.class_4725}\] [from their sins"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 1:21). Those Samaritans in Sychar understood that the Messiah was to be ["the Saviour of the WORLD"]{.class_4723} (John 4:42).

Notice in verse 11 that the baby is identified as the ["Saviour."]{.class_4723} The name [Jesus]{.class_4725} means "Jehovah Saves." He's the Saviour [first,]{.class_4725} then Christ the King. Hebrews 7:2 says, ["King of righteousness, and AFTER THAT\...King of peace."]{.class_4723}

Notice also the Trinitarian formula of verse 11:[ "a SAVIOUR ]{.class_4723}\[1\][, which is CHRIST ]{.class_4723}\[2\][ the LORD]{.class_4723} \[3\][."]{.class_4723} He's the [Saviour:]{.class_4725} that's the [Son]{.class_4725} who came to give you eternal life (John 3:16). He's the [Christ]{.class_4725},[ ]{.class_4725}the "anointed one"; He was anointed by the [Holy Ghost,]{.class_4725} He's the [Lord:]{.class_4725} He's Deity. That's [God the Father.]{.class_4725} Jesus said, ["I and my Father are one"]{.class_4723} (John 10:30), and He is called ["the true God"]{.class_4723} (1 John 5:20) just like His Father (John 17:3). That's Jesus Christ identified with all three members of the Trinity. The Bible says, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_79}["For in him dwelleth all the fulness of the Godhead bodily"]{.class_4723} (Col. 2:9).

Verse 12: ["And this shall be a sign unto you; Ye shall find the babe wrapped in swaddling clothes, lying in a manger."]{.class_4723} Now you can see why we put the emphasis we did on the ["swaddling clothes"]{.class_4723} and the ["manger."]{.class_4723} We weren't "allegorizing" anything. Those things were for ["a sign."]{.class_4723} A sign of what? [A sign of why He was born.]{.class_4725} He was born to [die]{.class_4725} as a [sacrifice]{.class_4725} for our sins, and if you partake of the Bread of Life (a manger is a [feed]{.class_4725} trough), you have eternal life (John 6).

Verses 13 and 14 are the so-called "angelic chorus" of the Christmas carols. You know, "Angels we have heard on high, Sweetly singing o'er the plains" and "Hark! the herald angels sing" and all that sort of thing. Of course the text doesn't say that. It says, ["praising God, and SAYING,"]{.class_4723} not singing; but you can understand how people get the idea. Psalm 149:1 says, ["Praise ye the LORD. Sing unto the LORD a new song, and his praise in the congregation of saints."]{.class_4723} Praise is connected with singing, and singing is connected with joy (see our work [God is Love]{.class_4725}).

Verse 14 is one of the most misquoted verses in the Bible. All the politicians talk about "peace on earth." All the Popes talk about "praying for world peace." For years, Sunbeam Bread put up billboards all over the country with a little girl praying, and the caption read: "Peace on earth."

[The verse said nothing of the sort.]{.class_4725} The verse said, ['GLORY TO GOD IN THE HIGHEST, and on earth peace, good will toward men."]{.class_4723} No [glory to God,]{.class_4725} no peace. It's just like Hebrews 7:2 (see our comments on vs. 11): no [righteousness,]{.class_4725} no peace. ["The wisdom that is from above is FIRST PURE, then peaceable"]{.class_4723} (James 3:17). No [purity,]{.class_4725} no peace. Do you know why this country is in a constant state of warfare? It's no longer concerned about [righteousness, purity,]{.class_4725} or [giving glory to God.]{.class_4725}

Luke 2:14 is a slap in the face to every Amillennial and Postmillennial Bible corrector trying to set up his own literal, visible, physical kingdom instead of God's. It is obvious to anyone who has any sense and knows history that the verse wasn't fulfilled at []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_80}the First Advent of Jesus Christ. You call the destruction of Jerusalem in A.D. 70, the barbarian invasions of Rome, the campaigns of Charlemagne and Ghenghis Khan, the Crusades, the Hundred Years War, the Thirty Years War, the Seven Years War, the French and Indian War, the American Revolution, the Napoleonic Wars, the War of 1812, the Civil War, the Boer War, the Boxer Rebellion, the Sino-Japanese War, the Spanish-American War, the Philippine Insurrection, World War I, World War II, the Korean War, the Vietnam War, two Gulf Wars, and Bush's "War on Terror" "peace on earth"?! Who are you trying to kid, man?

Besides that, do you know what Jesus Christ Himself said about why He came? Luke 12:51, ["Suppose ye that I am come to give peace on earth? ]{.class_4723}\[Well, sure, Lord; isn't that what the angel said you would do?\][ I tell you, NAY ]{.class_4723}\[NO!\][; but rather division."]{.class_4723} In Matthew 10:34, He said, ["Think NOT that I am come to send peace on earth: I came NOT to send peace, but a sword."]{.class_4723}

How's that for a contradiction in the Scriptures? The angel said Jesus came to send peace, and Jesus said He didn't. That thing shook up Origen so much that he changed the passage to ["peace on earth to men of good will";]{.class_4725} that way, only the ["men of good will"]{.class_4725} got the ["peace."]{.class_4725} The Catholic Church picked that up from Origen, and Jerome stuck it in the Latin [Vulgate.]{.class_4725} The revisers and translators of the modern versions picked up this corrupt reading from the same place Jerome got it---[Alexandria, Egypt]{.class_4725} ([Vaticanus]{.class_4725} and [Sinaiticus]{.class_4725}).

What you have in Luke 2:14 is another example of the First and Second Advents being right together in the Scriptures (see our comments on 1:17). You see, Christ [will]{.class_4725} bring ["peace on earth"]{.class_4725} at His Second Coming. The same prophet who predicted the birthplace of the Messiah said, ["And this man ]{.class_4723}\[the ["ruler in Israel"]{.class_4723} of Mic. 5:2\][ shall be THE PEACE, when the Assyrian shall come into your land"]{.class_4723} (Mic. 5:5). That's a reference to Jesus Christ coming back and destroying the Antichrist (["the Assyrian"]{.class_4723}---see Isa. 10:5--6). Notice that ["the peace"]{.class_4723} in Micah 5:5 is the ["man"]{.class_4723} [Himself;]{.class_4725} so there is no peace apart from the righteous rule of ["The Prince of Peace"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 9:6--7). So one more time with feeling: no [purity,]{.class_4725} no peace; no [righteousness,]{.class_4725} no peace; no [glory to God,]{.class_4725} no peace; and no JESUS, no peace. The old saying is: "[No]{.class_4725} Jesus, [no ]{.class_4725}peace; [know]{.class_4725} Jesus, [know]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_81} peace."

If you are going to make a [proper]{.class_4727} spiritual application of verse 14, you will have to take the [whole]{.class_4727} verse just as it is worded in a [King James Authorized Version]{.class_4727} of the Holy Bible. The passage is contrasting what's taking place ["in the highest"]{.class_4723} to what's taking place ["on earth." "In the highest,"]{.class_4723} God is getting the glory for what He has done in sending His Son to the earth. ["On earth,"]{.class_4723} God is offering ["peace"]{.class_4723} and ["good will"]{.class_4723} to ["men"]{.class_4723} in the person of His Son. In the Pauline Epistles, that is known as [reconciliation.]{.class_4727}

["Wherefore in all things it behoved him to be made like unto his brethren, that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 2:17).

["To wit, that God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them; and hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 5:19).

You see, because of our sinful state, we are all born into this world "at war" with God. In our natural condition, ["the wrath of God abideth"]{.class_4723} on us (John 3:36). God didn't want the war between Him and His creation to continue (2 Pet. 3:9; 1 Tim. 2:4), but at the same time, His holiness and justice had to be satisfied (Rom. 3:25---the word ["propitiation"]{.class_4723} means "to satisfy or appease"). So God sent down His Son as an act of ["good will toward men."]{.class_4723} Jesus Christ as 100% man lived a perfect life to satisfy God's holiness, and He suffered the penalty of our sin to satisfy God's justice. As 100% God, He rose from the dead and offers forgiveness of sins, God's righteousness, and eternal life to any sinner who will receive Him. When a sinner trusts Christ, at that moment his war with God is over; the two are "reconciled." Jesus Christ becomes his ["peace."]{.class_4723}

["Therefore being justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 5:1).

In Luke 2:14, it is [Jesus Christ Himself]{.class_4725} who is ["peace, good will toward men."]{.class_4723} Peace with God is not just offered to ["men of good will";]{.class_4725} it is offered to [all]{.class_4725} men, good and bad.

In verses 15--16, the angels return to Heaven, and the shepherds go to the manger and find the Christ child. Where are the ["wise men"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 2:1)? []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_82}[They aren't there.]{.class_4725}

It is perfectly apparent when you compare Luke 2 with Matthew 2 that some time has passed between the birth in the manger and the arrival of the wise men. In Luke 2:16, Jesus is ["the babe"]{.class_4723}; but in Matthew 2:8, 9, 11, He is called ["the young child,"]{.class_4723} indicating that He is no longer a newborn baby. How ["young"]{.class_4723} was He? Well, Herod had all the children in Bethlehem who were ["two years old and under"]{.class_4723} killed (Matt. 2:16) based on ["what time the star appeared"]{.class_4723} to the wise men (Matt. 2:7). Moreover, when the wise men found the Christ child, they didn't find Him in a manger; Joseph had moved his family into a ["house"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 2:11).

At this point, the Roman Catholic and Greek Orthodox Churches do have a certain amount of truth on the subject. They separate the birth of Christ at "Christmas" (Dec. 25) from the visit of the wise men at "Epiphany" on January 6, which is from where the idea of the "Twelve Days of Christmas" comes (although what partridges, French hens, gold rings, and leaping "lords" have to do with it, no one knows). But you don't get a caravan together and make the trek from Babylon and Persia to Jerusalem in only twelve days.

The wise men didn't say they followed the star to Jerusalem. They said they saw the ["star in the east"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 2:2), and then they made their way to the most logical place for a Jewish King to be born---in the capital city of Israel, Jerusalem. They weren't "following yonder star" until they left Herod to go to Bethlehem (Matt. 2:9).

The connection between Luke 2 and Matthew 2 is that ["star."]{.class_4723} It was no "supernova" or "conjunction of the planets" as you hear over and over again [ad nauseum]{.class_4725} every year at Christmas from Bible-rejecting astronomers. If you go back to the prophecy the wise men were following, that ["star"]{.class_4723} is a [person.]{.class_4725}

["I shall see him, but not now: I shall behold him, but not nigh: there shall come a Star out of Jacob ]{.class_4723}\[notice the capitalization, indicating it is the person of the Messiah Himself\][, and a Sceptre ]{.class_4723}\[a [King]{.class_4725}---Matt. 2:2\] [shall rise out of Israel"]{.class_4723} (Num. 24:17).

In the Bible, stars are [angels]{.class_4725} (Job 38:7; Rev. 1:20). What the wise men saw was ["the glory of the Lord"]{.class_4723} that ["shone round about" "the angel of the Lord."]{.class_4723} And if Numbers 24:17 is any indication, ["the angel of the Lord"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_83} in Luke 2 would indeed be an angelic appearance of Jesus Christ. The wise men were still ["in the east"]{.class_4723} when they saw the light of God's glory shining like a star as the angel announced Christ's birth to the shepherds. It took a period of somewhere between one and two years to gather the gifts, organize the caravan, and travel the distance to Jerusalem. The wise men were not at the manger, all the nativity scenes and Christmas pageants not withstanding.

The "Christmas story" ends with a contrast between the shepherds and Christ's mother. The shepherds leave the manger hooping and hollering, "Hallelujah! Praise God! The Messiah's finally come! Glory to God!" (vs. 20). They're telling everyone they meet what they saw until all the people in Bethlehem think they're a bunch of crazy fanatics (vss. 17--18).

Not Mary! The Bible says she ["kept all those things, and pondered them in her heart"]{.class_4723} (vs. 19). And who can blame her? After all, the only people who would believe her were the ones to whom the angel appeared and told them what was going on (Zacharias, Elizabeth, Joseph, and the shepherds). The rumors about the birth were already flying, and the natural conclusion was being reached (see John 8:41). Mary had no doubt about who her baby was after the events of Luke 1; her introspection now was over how to protect the integrity of her child (see her response in vs. 48 of this chapter).

21 And when eight days were accomplished for the circumcising of the child, his name was called JESUS, which was so named of the angel before he was conceived in the womb.

22 And when the days of her purification according to the law of Moses were accomplished, they brought him to Jerusalem, to present him to the Lord;

23 (As it is written in the law of the Lord, Every male that openeth the womb shall be called holy to the Lord;)

24 And to offer a sacrifice according to that which is said in the law of the Lord, A pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons.

25 And, behold, there was a man in Jerusalem, whose name was Simeon; and the same man was just and devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel: and the Holy Ghost was upon him.

26 And it was revealed unto him by the Holy Ghost, that he should not see death, before he had seen the Lord's Christ.

27 And he came by the Spirit into the temple: and when the parents brought in the child Jesus, to do for him after the custom of the law,

28 Then took he him up in his arms, and blessed God, and said,

29 Lord, now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace, according to thy word:

30 For mine eyes have seen thy salvation,

31 Which thou hast prepared before the face of all people;

32 A light to lighten the Gentiles, and the glory of thy people Israel.

33 And Joseph and his mother marvelled at those things which were spoken of him.

34 And Simeon blessed them, and said unto Mary his mother, Behold, this child is set for the fall and rising again of many in Israel; and for a sign which shall be spoken against;

35 (Yea, a sword shall pierce through thy own soul also,) that the thoughts of many hearts may be revealed.

36 And there was one Anna, a prophetess, the daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Aser: she was of a great age, and had lived with an husband seven years from her virginity;

37 And she was a widow of about fourscore and four years, which departed not from the temple, but served God with fastings and prayers night and day.

38 And she coming in that instant gave thanks likewise unto the Lord, and spake of him to all them that looked for redemption in Jerusalem.

39 And when they had performed all things according []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_85}to the law of the Lord, they returned into Galilee, to their own city Nazareth.

40 And the child grew, and waxed strong in spirit, filled with wisdom: and the grace of God was upon him.

The passage is on the circumcision of Jesus Christ according to the Law of Moses. Circumcision was originally given in Genesis 17:10--14 as ["a token of the covenant"]{.class_4723} that God made between Himself and Abraham's seed. Paul calls it a ["sign"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 4:11), which makes it strictly a Jewish matter (1 Cor. 1:22). It has nothing to do with any Gentile believer in the Body of Christ (Gal. 5:6). The covenant theologians (Reformed and Presbyterians) try to equate New Testament water baptism with Old Testament circumcision as a sign of the new covenant in Christ. So babies in a Christian family are to be baptized to "put them into the covenant."

There is no familial covenant in the New Testament. As someone has so aptly put it: "God has no grandchildren." You are either a child of God by faith in Jesus Christ, or you are not. Baptism is not a "sign" of anything; it is a [testimony]{.class_4727} of how the Holy Spirit has cut your new spirit away from your sinful flesh through the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ ([spiritual]{.class_4727} circumcision---see Col. 2:11--12; Rom. 6:3--4). The ["seal"]{.class_4723} of your righteousness by faith (see Rom. 4:11) is not baptism or any other outward thing; it is the Holy Spirit of God living inside your body (Eph. 1:13, 4:30).

Now it is true that there are definite medical benefits to physical circumcision. A child is circumcised at eight days in the Old Testament because that is the time when Vitamin K is at its peak in the child's body, allowing for natural clotting so the child doesn't bleed to death. How did Abraham and Moses know that, not having "modern scientific breakthroughs"? They didn't; that was the Creator, who made the circulatory system, revealing the exact time to them.

Additionally, male circumcision cuts down on cervical cancer in women. Doctors discovered that when they saw Jewish women were having far fewer incidents of cervical cancer than Gentile women. Of course, now, the big push by the news media is that circumcising baby boys is "mutilation." That comes from the fruity, fairy faggots []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_86}in Frisco. The best work you can get on the medical benefits of the Mosaic Law was done by a Seventh-day Adventist and is entitled [None of These Diseases.]{.class_4725} Of course, the author is trying to manipulate his readers into observing the Mosaic Law for spiritual salvation, which is bunk, but the[ physical]{.class_4725} benefits he documents are sound, scientific facts.

Verse 23 is from Exodus 13:2, 12, 15. The firstborn of the Israelites were redeemed and set apart to the Lord because God spared them from the death of the firstborn in Egypt by the sacrifice of the Passover lamb. Back in Exodus 4:22--23, God told Moses to tell Pharaoh:

"And thou shalt say unto Pharaoh, Thus saith the LORD, Israel is my son, even my firstborn: And I say unto thee, Let my son go, that he may serve me: and if thou refuse to let him go, behold, I will slay thy son, even thy firstborn."

Notice there that Israel is a [type of Jesus Christ]{.class_4727} (cf. Matt. 1:25). That's why the firstborn males were redeemed and sanctified.

["The law"]{.class_4723} in verse 24 is Leviticus 12:1--8. Mary had to be "purified" because she was a sinner with a sin nature (see our comments on Psa. 51:5 in that Commentary). The offering to be made was ["a sin offering"]{.class_4723} (Lev. 12:6), showing that Mary was a sinner in need of a Saviour (see our comments on 1:47).

The normal offering was a turtle dove and a lamb, or a pigeon and a lamb, but Mary was too poor to afford a lamb (the wise men had not yet arrived---see our comments on vss. 15--16), so she offered the alternative. Mary actually had the Lamb in her arms that could cleanse her from her sins (John 1:29, 36).

["And, behold, there was a man in Jerusalem, whose name was Simeon, and the same man was just and devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel: and the Holy Ghost was upon him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 25). Simeon was a man cut out of the same cloth as Zacharias (see our comments on 1:6). ["The consolation of Israel"]{.class_4723} is the ["redemption in Jerusalem"]{.class_4723} spoken of in verse 38; it is the physical deliverance of Israel from its enemies (see our comments on 1:68).

The Jews had a right to believe and expect that. Daniel 7:22 says, ["Until the Ancient of days came, and judgment was given []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_87}to the saints of the most High; and the time came that the saints possessed the kingdom."]{.class_4723} All the apostate expositors will tell you that that ["kingdom"]{.class_4723} is a [spiritual]{.class_4725} kingdom, but there's not a spiritual kingdom in the entire chapter. Look at Daniel 7: verse 4 is [Persia]{.class_4725} (England prophetically), a [physical]{.class_4725} kingdom; verse 5 is [Greece]{.class_4725} (Russia prophetically), a [physical]{.class_4725} kingdom; verse 6 is [Rome]{.class_4725} (the United States prophetically), a [physical]{.class_4725} kingdom; verse 7 is the kingdom of the [Antichrist,]{.class_4725} a [physical]{.class_4725} kingdom. So when Jesus Christ comes in power in verses 13--14 of Daniel 7, do you know what He gets? He gets a [physical]{.class_4725} kingdom that He rules from Jerusalem (Zech. 14).

["The consolation of Israel"]{.class_4723} is the [physical]{.class_4725} comfort brought to her when Jesus Christ comes to destroy her enemies. Look at Isaiah 40:1--2.

["Comfort ye, comfort ye my people, saith your God. Speak ye comfortably to Jerusalem, and cry unto her, that her warfare is accomplished]{.class_4723} \[Second Advent---Joel 3:16--17\][, that her iniquity is pardoned]{.class_4723} \[Second Advent---Rom. 11:25--26\][; for she hath received of the Lord's hand double for all her sins]{.class_4723} \[Second Advent---Isa. 61:6--7\][."]{.class_4723}

Back to Luke 2:[ "And it was revealed unto him by the Holy Ghost, that he should not see death, before he had seen THE LORD'S CHRIST"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26).[ ]{.class_4723}Now that last statement is very important; it's so important that I include it among the "salient" verses of the Bible---the ones absolutely necessary for understanding the Scriptures. You can infer from the words ["the Lord's Christ"]{.class_4723} that there is a "Christ" who is [not God's Christ.]{.class_4725} (The same author repeats this in Acts 4:26 when he writes, ["the rulers were gathered together against the LORD, and against HIS Christ."]{.class_4723})

You see, ["Christ"]{.class_4723} is not a [name;]{.class_4725} it's a [title.]{.class_4725} It means "anointed" or "anointed one." In Hebrew, it's [Mashiach;]{.class_4725} transliterated in your Bible as ["Messiah"]{.class_4723} (see Dan. 9:25--26; John 1:41). Not only does God have a "Messiah"---an anointed one---so does Satan.

["Thou art the ANOINTED CHERUB that covereth"]{.class_4723} (Ezek. 28:14). That's a reference to the Devil before his fall (Ezek. 28:15). Satan himself was a "Messiah," and he has a King who is a "Messiah." The Bible calls him []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_88}["antichrist.]{.class_4723}

["Little children, it is the last time: and as ye have heard that antichrist shall come, even now are there many antichrists; whereby we know that it is the last time"]{.class_4723} (1 John 2:18).

Jesus said that ["false Christs and false prophets shall rise"]{.class_4723} (Mark 13:22). So there are ["Christs"]{.class_4723} that are not ["the Lord's Christ."]{.class_4723} Satan has fake [apostles]{.class_4725} (2 Cor. 11:13; Rev. 2:2), he has fake [Holy Spirits]{.class_4725} (2 Cor. 11:4; 1 John 4:1--3), he has fake [gospels]{.class_4725} (2 Cor. 11:4; Gal. 1:6--8), and he has fake JESUSES.

["For if he that cometh preacheth ANOTHER JESUS, whom we have not preached, or if ye receive ANOTHER SPIRIT, which ye have not received, or ANOTHER GOSPEL, which ye have not accepted"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 11:4).

And just as sure as you live and breath, the Devil has a fake [church]{.class_4725} (Rev. 17) and a fake [Bible]{.class_4725} (2 Cor. 2:17). Why is all of that? Because the Devil's original desire was to ["be like the most High."]{.class_4723} So the Devil is a great [imitator,]{.class_4725} which explains why all the "New Age Bible versions" change 1 Corinthians 11:1 to have you be ["imitators of Christ"]{.class_4725} (see our comments on the verse in that Commentary). Satan is an "imitator" of Christ, but he is not a ["follower."]{.class_4725}

The Devil has a false King who is an "anointed one" like Jesus Christ. He is pictured in the Old Testament by King Saul, whom David called ["the LORD'S anointed"]{.class_4723} (1 Sam. 24:6, 10, 26:9, 11, 16, 23; 2 Sam. 1:14, 16). And while it is true that the Lord had Saul anointed to be King (1 Sam. 10:1)---like Lucifer himself was anointed by God (Ezek. 28:14)---the Lord [rejected]{.class_4725} him for David (1 Sam. 15:23, 28), one of the greatest types of Christ in the Bible.

Back in Luke 2, Simeon becomes a great picture of the New Testament child of God: ["he should not see death, before he had seen the Lord's Christ."]{.class_4723} If you've been saved, that's true of you [spiritually]{.class_4725} at [salvation]{.class_4725} and [physically]{.class_4725} at the [Rapture.]{.class_4725} If you are alive when the Rapture takes place, you will see the Lord literally without ever seeing death; if you have already died, it won't matter, because death has lost any power over you.

["Jesus said unto her, I am the resurrection, and the life: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_89}live: And whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die"]{.class_4723} (John 11:25--26).

["For the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trump of God: and the dead in Christ shall rise first: Then we which are alive and remain shall be caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air: and so shall we ever be with the Lord. Wherefore comfort one another with these words"]{.class_4723} (1 Thess. 4:16--18).

["Behold, I shew you a mystery; We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed, In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trump: for the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed. For this corruptible must put on incorruption, and this mortal must put on immortality. So when this corruptible shall have put on incorruption, and this mortal shall have put on immortality, then shall be brought to pass the saying that is written, Death is swallowed up in victory"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 15:51--54).

The Old Testament picture of that is Enoch. ["By faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death; and was not found, because God had translated him"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 11:5).

Now spiritually, aren't you glad that the Lord has revealed ["the Lord's Christ"]{.class_4723} to you before you died?

["But if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost: In whom the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them\....For God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness, hath shined in our hearts, to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 4:3--6).

Aren't you fortunate that you didn't die first before the Holy Spirit showed up to reveal the Saviour to you? When I was an unsaved young man, I was itching to go overseas and fight in World War II. I was up for being shipped out to the Hurtgen Forest in the Battle of the Bulge (or the battles of Leyte and Manila in the Pacific) []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_90}when I was reassigned to train infantry replacements in Ft. Jackson in Carolina and then in Camp Rucker in Alabama. When the war was over in Germany, I was on a Merchant Marine ship ready to go over to the Philippines for the invasion of the Japanese mainland when the Enola Gay dropped the "big one" on Hiroshima and ended the war. Everyone else was shooting off fireworks, jumping up and down, and cheering; and there was "Ruckman" sitting there dejected on the yardarm of that ship cursing because he wouldn't get to fire a shot. What if I had died there in Germany, the Philippines, or Japan? I would have been in Hell now 67 years. What a blessing for God to spare my life until I saw ["the Lord's Christ"]{.class_4723} on the 14th of March, 1949!

Notice in verse 29 of Luke 2 that Simeon was operating ["according to THY WORD."]{.class_4723} Isaiah wrote, ["To the law and to the testimony: if they speak not according to THIS WORD, it is because there is no light in them"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 8:20); that's a reference to the [written words of God]{.class_4725} as recorded in the Scriptures (Psa. 19:7--9). The Christian is commanded to ["speak as the oracles of God"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 4:11)---the written words of God in the [Scriptures]{.class_4725} (Rom. 3:2).

["For mine eyes have seen thy salvation"]{.class_4723} (vs. 30). Salvation is in a [person,]{.class_4725} not a [thing.]{.class_4725} The mark of a false religion is found in 1 Samuel 4:3---["When IT cometh among us, IT may save us."]{.class_4723} False religions count on [things]{.class_4725}---like good works, the Ten Commandments, "holy sacraments," tradition, the Mass, water baptism, "praying through," meditation, etc.---to save them. Lost sinners depend on [something]{.class_4725} to save them instead of [somebody;]{.class_4725} New Testament salvation is by a [Person]{.class_4725} (Matt. 1:21).

Of course, the ["salvation"]{.class_4723} of which Simeon spoke was the [physical]{.class_4725} salvation of Israel at the Second Advent (see our comments on 1:68--71). Psalm 98:3 says, ["He hath remembered his mercy and his truth toward the house of Israel: all the ends of the earth have SEEN THE SALVATION OF OUR GOD."]{.class_4723}

Note the all inclusive ministry of Jesus Christ in verses 31--32: ["all people" ]{.class_4723}and ["Gentiles,"]{.class_4723} as well as the Jews---["thy people Israel."]{.class_4723} John wrote, ["He came unto his own ]{.class_4723}\[the nation of Israel\][, and his own received him not. But as many as received him ]{.class_4723}\[Jew or Gentile\][]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_91}[, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name"]{.class_4723} (John 1:11--12).

Again, verse 32 could apply to either the First or Second Advents (see our comments on 1:17). We have already commented on the ["light"]{.class_4723} and how it can apply [spiritually]{.class_4725} to the [First Advent]{.class_4725} or [physically]{.class_4725} to the [Second Advent]{.class_4725} under our comments on 1:79 (which see).

Verse 33 is a famous attack on the doctrine of the Virgin Birth of Christ by all the corrupt modern translations. The new "Bibles" change ["JOSEPH and his mother" ]{.class_4723}to ["his FATHER and his mother";]{.class_4725} that would be the [ASV,]{.class_4725} the [NASV,]{.class_4725} the [RSV,]{.class_4725} the [NRSV,]{.class_4725} the [NIV,]{.class_4725} and the [ESV]{.class_4725} (the "Conservative" version of the [RSV,]{.class_4725} meaning the same old [Liberal crap]{.class_4725}). Jesus Christ Himself told you Joseph wasn't His father right in the same chapter (vs. 49). But apostate Conservative and Fundamentalists like John R. Rice support the change because [Mary]{.class_4725} called Joseph Christ's father in verse 48. [Jesus Christ corrected His mother in the next verse]{.class_4725} (see our comments on 1:48). It should be obvious to anyone reading the text that verse 48 is [Mary]{.class_4725} trying to protect her Son's integrity from accusations of illegitimacy, while verse 33 is the [Holy Spirit's]{.class_4725} evaluation of Christ's lineage (cf. Matt. 1:16; Luke 3:23).

The other argument for attacking Christ's Deity in verse 33 (if He wasn't virgin born, He wasn't God---see Rom. 1:3--4) is that the Holy Spirit calls Joseph and Mary ["his parents"]{.class_4723} in verse 41. However, a "parent" can be a [step-father]{.class_4725} or an [adoptive]{.class_4725} father without being a [literal]{.class_4725} father. The Bible is as careful about not calling Joseph Christ's father as Jesus was about not calling Mary His mother (see our comments on 1:28). The [King James]{.class_4725} Bible has the right reading in verse 33, and all the other so-called "Bibles" are [corrupt]{.class_4725} (2 Cor. 2:17).

Verse 34: ["And Simeon blessed them, and said unto Mary his mother, Behold, this child is set for the fall and rising again of many in Israel."]{.class_4723} A prophecy---the ["fall"]{.class_4723} is described in Isaiah 28:13, 16--17 and Matthew 21:44; and the ["rising again"]{.class_4723} is given in Isaiah 26:19--21 and Ezekiel 37.

["And for a sign which shall be spoken against."]{.class_4723} What ["sign"]{.class_4723} would that be? Well, when the Pharisees asked for []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_92}["a sign,"]{.class_4723} Jesus said, ["An evil and adulterous generation seeketh after a sign; and there shall no sign be given to it, but the sign of the prophet Jonas"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 12:38--39).

What was ["the sign of\...Jonas"]{.class_4723}? Matthew 12:40, ["For as Jonas was three days and three nights in the whale's belly; so shall the Son of man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth."]{.class_4723}

The ["sign which shall be spoken against"]{.class_4723} is the [Resurrection.]{.class_4725} It is ["spoken against"]{.class_4723} in its type: no book in the Bible has more criticism leveled against it than the book of Jonah; I gave you a whole list of the critics and their criticisms in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Matthew]{.class_4725} (see our remarks on Matt. 12:40). The Liberals and Modernists treat it as a myth or legend, and the Conservatives and Fundamentalists cast doubt on the details of the account (the fact that the ["great fish"]{.class_4723} was a [whale]{.class_4725} and that Jonah died and went to Hell like Christ).

The resurrection of Christ is ["spoken against."]{.class_4723} The Jews say the disciples stole the body (Matt. 28:13--5); the Liberals and Modernists say Christ "swooned" on the cross and was "revived by the coolness and freshness of the tomb" (never mind that no trauma patient is treated by [cooling them off]{.class_4725}); the cults make it a [spiritual]{.class_4725} resurrection (i.e., the disciples saw a [ghost]{.class_4725}---a ghost with ["flesh and bones"]{.class_4723} that could be touched and that [ate food]{.class_4725}---Luke 24:37--43). But the bodily resurrection is the acid test of the Gospel. Paul puts more emphasis on that than he does the blood atonement.

["And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures: And that he was seen of Cephas, then of the twelve: After that, he was seen of above five hundred brethren at once; of whom the greater part remain unto this present, but some are fallen asleep. After that, he was seen of James; then of all the apostles. And last of all he was seen of me also, as of one born out of due time"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 15:4--7).

There is no salvation without the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ.

["And if Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_93} (1 Cor. 15:17).

["That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that GOD HATH RAISED HIM FROM THE DEAD, thou shalt be saved"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 10:9).

Brethren, the best news we ever got, we got from a graveyard:[ "He is not here: for he is risen"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 28:6). Why do I say that? Because it means that [you're]{.class_4725} going to make it. Jesus said, ["because I live, ye shall live also"]{.class_4723} (John 14:19).

Verse 35: ["(Yea, a sword shall pierce through thy own soul also,) that the thoughts of many hearts may be revealed."]{.class_4723} Now the statement about the sword piercing Mary's soul is a parenthetical expression. The ["sign"]{.class_4723} that is ["spoken against"]{.class_4723} is to reveal ["the thoughts of many hearts,"]{.class_4723} which is why I said the Resurrection is the acid test. Most expositors and commentators make the ["sword"]{.class_4723}[ ]{.class_4737}that pierces Mary's soul the anguish of soul she felt at the sufferings and crucifixion of her Son, and that application can certainly be made. But if you take the verse as a whole, the cross-reference is to Hebrews 4:12--13.

"For the word of God is quick, and powerful, and sharper than any twoedged SWORD, PIERCING even to the dividing asunder of SOUL and spirit, and of the joints and marrow, and is a DISCERNER of the THOUGHTS and intents of the HEART. Neither is there any creature that is not MANIFEST in his sight: but all things are NAKED and OPENED unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do."

Notice again the prominent place God gives to a woman in verses 36--38 (see our remarks about women and Islam under 1:26). The first broadcaster of the good news of redemption was a [woman.]{.class_4725} It was [women]{.class_4725} who first preached the Resurrection. Women are given a prominence in the Bible that they aren't given in the [Koran,]{.class_4725} especially in the New Testament with Jesus Christ.

One of these stupid, liberal, left-wing television shows tried to compare the Bible to the [Koran]{.class_4725} and Islam by saying the Bible didn't allow women to speak (a perversion of 1 Tim. 2:11 and 1 Cor. 14:34). Listen, if you think that women not being allowed to teach men (1 Tim. 2:11--12) or to speak in tongues in the church (1 Cor. 14:34--35) []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_94}compares to how Islam treats women, you have a real twisted sense of morality, did you know that?

In Islam, a woman is not even allowed into a mosque. Women are not allowed to go outside their houses without their husbands, and when they do, they are required to wear the burka head dress (the strictest sects make women cover themselves from head to foot). Why? Because if they don't, any man can [rape them with impunity.]{.class_4725} If they are raped, they cannot testify in court on their own behalf, and it takes four men to confirm a rape. Imagine four grown men standing around watching a woman being raped without doing anything to stop it!

In Islam, women cannot drive a car. It takes two women to equal the testimony of one man in court. A wife may be divorced at any time for [any reason]{.class_4725} by the husband simply saying "I divorce thee" three times. If a woman is divorced by her husband, she has no custodial rights to her children and is required to nurse the child of her estranged husband for four months after the divorce if she has a baby.

Do you liberal, left-wing "women's libbers" honestly think that is comparable to how women are treated in a New Testament local church? A woman may not have been allowed to speak in tongues in the church, but she could prophesy (Acts 21:9). She may not be allowed to teach men, but she can teach women (Tit. 2:4) and children (2 Tim. 1:5 cf. 3:15). Women are commended in the New Testament for their service in the local church (Rom. 16:1, 3, 6), and they are protected in marriage by the command of God (Eph. 5:25--31; Col. 3:19; 1 Cor. 7:3; 1 Pet. 3:7).

There is no doubt about it, ladies; that Book is a macho, male-chauvinistic set-up. Every writer in the Book is [male]{.class_4725}---even the two books bearing the names of women (Esther and Ruth). The writer of the main body of New Testament doctrine and practice was a [male]{.class_4725}---Paul---and he was never even married. God the Father is [male;]{.class_4725} God the Son is [male;]{.class_4725} God the Holy Spirit is [male.]{.class_4725} There isn't a female apostle, elder, or deacon in that Book. Yet where the Book is honored, read, used, and obeyed, women have status and rights and privileges they don't have in any other country in the world. Where the Bible isn't observed, women are beasts of burden; they are []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_95}slaves; they are mere objects of sex and reproduction. What makes the difference between there and here? It's the influence of the Bible.

Verse 36 proves that none of the ten northern tribes were "lost" (Herbert W. Armstrong, Garner Ted Armstrong, and British Israelism). God knows exactly where each one is, and He has them numbered (Rev. 7:3--8).

The events of Matthew 2 take place after the first comma in verse 39, between ["law of the Lord"]{.class_4723} and ["they returned into Galilee."]{.class_4723} As we will see when we get to chapter 4, a punctuation mark in a [King James]{.class_4725} Bible can hide 2,000+ years of history, so what is the problem of just a couple of years in Egypt here?

41 Now his parents went to Jerusalem every year at the feast of the passover.

42 And when he was twelve years old, they went up to Jerusalem after the custom of the feast.

43 And when they had fulfilled the days, as they returned, the child Jesus tarried behind in Jerusalem; and Joseph and his mother knew not of it.

44 But they, supposing him to have been in the company, went a day's journey; and they sought him among their kinsfolk and acquaintance.

45 And when they found him not, they turned back again to Jerusalem, seeking him.

46 And it came to pass, that after three days they found him in the temple, sitting in the midst of the doctors, both hearing them, and asking them questions.

47 And all that heard him were astonished at his understanding and answers.

48 And when they saw him, they were amazed: and his mother said unto him, Son, why hast thou thus dealt with us? behold, thy father and I have sought thee sorrowing.

49 And he said unto them, How is it that ye sought me? wist ye not that I must be about my Father's business?

50 And they understood not the saying which he spake unto them.

51 And he went down with them, and came to Nazareth, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_96}and was subject unto them: but his mother kept all these sayings in her heart.

52 And Jesus increased in wisdom and stature, and in favour with God and man.

Verse 40 shows you that Jesus grew up as a normal human boy with the sole exception that He was sinless. There is nothing in the childhood of Jesus like you find in Roman Catholic and Mohammedan mythology. He never spoke in the crib, and He didn't bring clay pigeons to life in Egypt ("Infancy Gospel" and Sura 3:45--51). Except for one incident at the age of twelve (recorded here in vss. 41--50), the childhood of Jesus Christ remains an obscurity until His baptism by John.

We know that He lived in Nazareth and learned the trade of a carpenter from His step-father Joseph (Mark 6:3). We know that He was the oldest of seven children (Mark 6:3), and that His family was poor (see our remarks under vs. 24). Unlike Mohammed, Jesus learned to read and write, probably from the synagogue schools of His day. But there are no details given in the Scriptures of Christ's childhood until He is twelve years old.

Now why is that? Because as we are about to see, the record shows that that was the first time He had a consciousness of who and what He was. Before that, there is no indication that He knew He was God in the flesh at age two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten, or eleven. He didn't lay in the manger at Bethlehem with His full omniscience as God. As a human being, He had to [learn]{.class_4725} some things (see Heb. 5:8).

There are all sorts of things connected with this incident in Christ's life at age twelve. A lot of folks think that a child can't be saved before age twelve, but that's not so. A child is [safe]{.class_4725} ([not]{.class_4725} saved) until he has a knowledge of sin, and that knowledge of sin can come at any age (see our comments on Rom. 4:15 in that Commentary); there is no set age for the "Age of Accountability."

The Jews have a boy become a man at age thirteen; that's the Bar Mitzvah. Isn't it interesting that numbers change when you get past twelve? It's ten, eleven, twelve; and then it's thirteen ([three-]{.class_4725}teen), [four]{.class_4725}teen, fifteen ([five]{.class_4725}-teen), [six]{.class_4725}teen, [seven]{.class_4725}teen, etc. Well, why don't []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_97}you have one-teen and two-teen? It's because when it comes to His [Son]{.class_4725} (whether it is His national son Israel or His only begotten Son Jesus), God counts to [twelve]{.class_4725} and then starts over. The numbers change after the first time Jesus manifests any kind of self-consciousness about His work and calling. Folks, that Bible runs the world whether anyone believes it or not.

Verse 41: ["Now his parents ]{.class_4736}\[see our remarks about Joseph under vs. 33\][ went to Jerusalem every year at the feast of the passover."]{.class_4736}

There were three feasts that all Jewish males were required to attend at the Temple in Jerusalem: Passover, Pentecost, and Tabernacles (Deut. 16:16). Passover is connected with Christ's blood atonement on the cross (1 Cor. 5:7); Pentecost is connected with the birth of the Church (Acts 2); and Tabernacles is connected to the Second Advent (Zech. 14). Passover became a time of family worship and sacrifice (see 1 Sam. 1).

Verses 43--46 show you the difference between the historical Mary of the Scriptures and the "Mary" of Roman Catholic fiction. The Mary of the Bible was [not]{.class_4725} some kind of "mother of God," "immaculately conceived" without sin, who was a "co-redemptrix" with her Son (one order of Catholic monks publishes a "prayer card" with Mary seated in Heaven as the fourth member of the Trinity); she was a normal mother who lost her Son for [four days.]{.class_4725} And you're going to trust [Mary]{.class_4725} to keep you out of Purgatory, are you? Did a Cuban hijack your brain, or what?

Why would you go to Mary and ask her to save you from Purgatory or Hell when she couldn't even keep track of her own kid? Do you honestly think she's going to be able to keep track of you when she couldn't even keep track of Jesus Christ? There are over 900 million Catholics who think Mary hears them when they pray. How is she going to keep track of 900 million people when she can't even keep track of one twelve-year-old boy?

Now don't misunderstand me; I'm not getting on to Mary. What happened to her is nothing particularly strange. I understand what you mothers go through; I had to "mother" five children all by myself for twelve years. You can't keep track of the kids 24 hours a day. We've had families here at Bible Baptist Church with seven or []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_98}more kids, and nearly every one of them has left one of their children at church because they thought the kid was with the other parent or going over to some friend's house for dinner. That's typical; it's not abnormal.

So Mary is not some [big]{.class_4725} sinner; she's just a [normal]{.class_4725} sinner; but she is a [sinner.]{.class_4725} To make a god out of her and think she can help you is just a little too much. I would tell Mary to stick to her knitting. She's got her problems and I've got mine.

Now there is a great [spiritual]{.class_4725} truth here in the passage. A Christian can "lose" Jesus. Of course, by that I [don't]{.class_4725} mean a Christian can lose his [salvation.]{.class_4725} That's not about what I'm talking. I'm talking about a Christian [getting out of fellowship with his Lord.]{.class_4725}

Once you get saved, Jesus Christ won't leave you. The promise in Hebrews 13:5 is: ["I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee."]{.class_4723} The promise is that [nothing]{.class_4725} can ["separate us from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:38--39). You are ["sealed"]{.class_4723} by the Holy Spirit ["unto the day of redemption"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 4:30), and you can be ["confident"]{.class_4723} that God will finish the salvation He started in you all the way to Glory (Phil. 1:6). As a New Testament Christian in the Body of Christ, you are [predestinated]{.class_4725} ["to be conformed to the image of his Son"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:29), and you don't have to worry about any Old Testament prayer like ["take not thy holy spirit from me"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 51:11) because it ain't gonna happen.

But you can lose your fellowship with God (1 John 1:3--7). You can get to the place where the Lord becomes foreign to you in your personal walk from day to day. The terminology among preachers for that is [backsliding.]{.class_4725} It only occurs one time in the Old Testament in regards to an [individual:]{.class_4725} ["The backslider in heart shall be filled with his own ways"]{.class_4723} (Prov. 14:14). The picture is a guy trying to drive a cow up a hill, and she won't go forward when he prods her, but keeps kicking at the prod and sliding back down the hill (Hos. 4:16). There's nothing wrong with applying that analogy to a New Testament child of God, but the correct terminology is "out of fellowship with the Lord."

A perfect example of losing Christ in the sense of fellowship is Simon Peter. God the Father revealed to Peter the identity of His []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_99}Son, and Peter made that wonderful confession you read in Matthew 16:16; six verses later he's letting Satan control his tongue (Matt. 16:2--23). What's wrong? He's judging things by his fleshly nature and not by the words of Christ.

He gets in that upper room and won't let Jesus wash his feet. Why? Because he doesn't love Christ? Of course not! He's thinking of the Lord's dignity and says, ["Thou shalt never wash my feet"]{.class_4723} (John 13:8). When Jesus tells him, "Simon, if I don't wash your feet, that means you've got no part in me," Peter says, "Then give me a bath" (John 13:8--9).

There's no doubt about Peter's sincerity and love for the Lord. But the Devil trapped him in a mess when he wasn't ready (Luke 22:31). Peter got out there in the Garden of Gethsemane, and he was ready to fight for the Lord, and the Lord wouldn't let him fight (John 18:11). Peter got mad at the Lord, and that busted his fellowship. As a result, he denies Jesus three times. He never stopped loving Christ; he was just mad at Him for letting Himself go down without a fight.

Now I know you highly spiritual brethren out there have never gotten mad at God, but believe me, I've had my share of it. I've learned that when you get mad at the Lord about something He's allowed to happen, it can be dangerous to you. But I've also learned that if you get mad and are honest with God and yourself about it and still want to do right, the Lord will work things out right for you.

Jonah and Job were like that. Jonah's out there on the east side of Nineveh waiting for God to blast those Assyrians to a "fare-thee-well," and the only thing he gets for his trouble is a sirocco about to knock him over and the sun about to give him a heat stroke (Jon. 4:8). He gets mad at God and says, "Why don't you just let me die?" God asks him, ["Doest thou well to be angry?"]{.class_4723} (Jon. 4:9) Jonah replies, "You bet your life I do!"

But God's gentle with Jonah; He doesn't dump him like some preachers would lead you to believe. When Jesus Christ wants to compare Himself to an Old Testament prophet, do you know whom He picks? I mean, other people compared Jesus to Elijah and Jeremiah (Matt. 16:14), but when the Lord Himself made the comparison, He picked Jonah (Matt. 12:40). Jonah was the [only]{.class_4725} Old Testament prophet to whom Jesus personally compared Himself.

Job got mad at God for everything that happened in Job 1--2, and who could blame him? He tells the Lord one time, "Why don't you just let me alone and let me swallow down my own spit (Job 7:19)?" Job didn't mince any words with the Lord. But you know the story: the Lord comes down and straightens Job out and then gives him twice as much as he had before.

When Peter denied the Lord, he didn't lose his salvation, but he did lose his fellowship. That's why he had to be ["converted"]{.class_4723} (Luke 22:32); that is, he had to be brought back into fellowship with the Lord. When Jesus came up from the dead, the angels told the women who came to the tomb, ["But go your way, tell his disciples AND PETER"]{.class_4723} (Mark 16:7). Peter lost his discipleship. Jesus appeared to him personally and privately after the Resurrection and restored the fellowship (Luke 24:34; 1 Cor. 15:5), but to be restored as a disciple, Peter had to confess Christ publicly three times just as he had denied Him three times.

Now in that sense, let's apply our text here (vss. 43--46) for our exhortation and edification.

I. Mary lost Christ at a [religious meeting.]{.class_4725} She didn't lose Jesus Christ in a dance hall or a beer joint; she lost Him at the Temple. You can be in the middle of a church service or revival meeting and be out of fellowship with your Saviour.

II.[ She lost Him;]{.class_4725} He didn't lose her. He knew right where His parents were. The problem was [theirs,]{.class_4725} not His.

III\. She lost Him in [one day.]{.class_4725} You can do something in 24 hours that will mess you up for three days or three weeks or three months or three years. I have preached to guys in prison who are just as saved as you are that did something that messed them up for twenty to fifty years. You can do something in a day for which you will reap the consequences for years to come, like David did with Bathsheba.

IV\. She lost Him and [she didn't even know it.]{.class_4725} She thought He was with the kinfolk or off playing with His friends, and He was back in Jerusalem. You can lose fellowship with the Lord by you going one way while He goes another. I have seen that happen. The person hadn't a clue that he was out of fellowship with God; he just went right on in the same rut he had been in for years.

V. Losing her Son brought her [fear]{.class_4725} and [sorrow.]{.class_4725} Mary was a good []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_101}mother; there's no doubt about that. When she discovered Jesus was missing, she was worried to death and looked all over the place for Him. When a Christian gets out of fellowship with his Lord, he gets away from the ["power," "love,"]{.class_4723} and ["sound mind"]{.class_4723} the Lord gave him and puts himself back under ["the spirit of fear"]{.class_4723} he had before the Lord saved him (see 2 Tim. 1:7). That's why some Christians doubt their salvation: they get to living for the world, the flesh, and the Devil, and they know that's not how a Christian should live, and it causes them to doubt whether they were ever saved to begin with. A backslidden Christian is a miserable Christian because his new nature won't allow him to completely enjoy the things of the world and the flesh; it will always pine for fellowship with God.

["And it came to pass, that after three days they found him in the temple"]{.class_4723} (vs. 46). It took them three days because the Temple was the last place they thought of looking for a twelve-year-old boy. That just goes to show you that Jesus grew up as a normal human boy; He showed no abnormal piety that would have led anyone to believe He was the Messiah. He didn't walk around with a halo over His head like all the Roman Catholic painters in the Middle Ages pictured Him. He was a carpenter's son; what would He be doing in the Temple?

["Sitting in the midst of the doctors, both hearing them, and asking them questions."]{.class_4723} Notice again the parallel of the beginning of Luke to the end of Luke (see our comments on vs. 10). When He becomes aware of who He is as the God-man, He's in the Temple hearing the doctors of the Law, asking them questions they can't answer, and answering their questions in ways that astonished them; that's what He's doing just before He dies (see Luke 20). At twelve years old, He's dealing with His ["Father's business,"]{.class_4723} and the last words He spoke before He died dealt with His ["Father's business"]{.class_4723} (Luke 23:46 cf. John 19:30).

["And all that heard him were astonished at his understanding and answers"]{.class_4723} (vs. 47). That continues into His ministry.

["And the Jews marvelled, saying, How knoweth this man letters, having never learned?"]{.class_4723} (John 7:15).

["And they were astonished at his doctrine: for he taught []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_102}them as one that had authority, and not as the scribes"]{.class_4723} (Mark 1:22).

["The officers answered, Never man spake like this man"]{.class_4723} (John 7:46).

Now, to cover up what everyone perceived to be an illegitimate birth, Mary says, ["Son, why hast thou thus dealt with us? behold, THY FATHER and I have sought thee sorrowing"]{.class_4723} (vs. 48). Of course, you can understand why she did it. Everyone knew that Jesus was conceived out of wedlock; they told Him over in John 8:41, ["We be not born of fornication,"]{.class_4723} implying [He was.]{.class_4725} So Mary is just trying to protect her Son's birth.

But the fact of the matter is [she lied.]{.class_4725} Mary knew perfectly well Joseph wasn't Christ's father; no one knew better. Christ has to [correct]{.class_4725} her, and when He does, she [doesn't understand the rebuke.]{.class_4725} In other words, the "immaculately conceived" Mary of Roman Catholicism is a [phony]{.class_4725} who never existed in history.

Verse 49 is a remarkable verse, for it is the first recorded words of Jesus Christ in the Scriptures. Of course, we understand He talked before this.

There's an old joke about a little boy who never talked for the first five or six years of his life. His parents worried there was something wrong with him, but the doctors could find nothing. One day out of the clear blue, while they were all at the dinner table, the little boy said, "Please pass the salt." In astonishment the parents said, "You can talk!" "Sure I can talk," said the little boy. "Well why haven't you said anything before now?" asked his parents. "Well up till now, everything's been fine," said the boy.

Of course Jesus talked before Luke 2:49, but nothing He said was recorded by the Holy Ghost. The first recorded words of Jesus Christ are: ["How is it that ye sought me? wist ye not ]{.class_4723}\[archaic English for: "don't you know"\][ that I must be about my Father's business?"]{.class_4723}

Well, then, He wasn't talking about Joseph. Joseph was a carpenter, and if Jesus had been about Joseph's business, they would have found Him in a carpenter's shop, not the Temple. I heard a Liberal once in a Southern Baptist church up in Rocky Mount, North Carolina use verse 48 to prove that Jesus never claimed to be virgin born. But verse 48 is [Mary]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_103} speaking, not Christ. When Christ talks about His ["Father's business,"]{.class_4723} it's obviously in contrast to what Mary said in verse 48. Jesus never claimed Joseph as His father, but He did say time and time again that [God]{.class_4725} was His Father. You will find most of those times in the Gospel of John (see John 5--10), which is why the Liberals and Modernists want to get rid of the Gospel of John (see our remarks on the "synoptic problem" in the Introduction).

Verses 46--49 are the first time Jesus acknowledges His person and commission. I believe He did that through the [Scriptures.]{.class_4725} I think He goes into that Temple and opens a scroll sitting there on a reading desk, and the Holy Spirit says, "Look there." He looks down at the scroll and reads, ["Thou art my Son; this day have I begotten thee"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 2:7), and the Holy Spirit says, "[That's you."]{.class_4725} The Holy Spirit says, "Pick up the scroll of Isaiah," and the boy picks up the scroll and reads, ["For unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given\...and his name shall be called Wonderful, Counsellor, The mighty God, The everlasting Father, The Prince of Peace"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 9:6). He reads over there in Isaiah 53 how He'll be ["wounded for our transgressions"]{.class_4723} and ["bruised for our iniquities"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 53:5), and the consciousness of who He was and what He came to do, which had lain dormant for twelve years, suddenly awoke.

Amen, brother! I think that's how it happened. He says over there in Hebrews 10:7, ["Then said I, Lo, I come (in the volume of THE BOOK it is written of me,) to do thy will, O God."]{.class_4723} I believe God used THE BOOK to awaken that little boy to the will of His Father; after all, He was the living Word (John 1:1--3, 14).

["And he went down with them, and came to Nazareth, and was subject unto them"]{.class_4723} (vs. 51). What's so important about that statement? Well, He just got through admitting Joseph wasn't His father. Verse 49 is also Christ correcting His mother for the first time. That's an overt slap in the face to the Catholic doctrine of Mary as the "mother of God." When you point out to a Catholic that Jesus Christ never called Mary His mother in the Scriptures (see our remarks under 1:28), the standard comeback is: "Do you mean that Jesus disrespected His mother?" The obvious answer from verse 51 []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ501.xhtml_page_104}is: "No, He didn't." He obeyed both Joseph and Mary, and honored them according to the commandment (see Eph. 6:1--3). Nevertheless, the fact of the Scripture is that He refused to call Mary His mother so that no Catholic would ever think you had to come to [her]{.class_4725} to get to [Him.]{.class_4725}
