Some Christian with a wry sense of humor once said, "There are so many 'doctors' in the church these days, you would think God is sick." Lester Roloff turned down a number of honorary doctorates throughout his life because he said, "Giving a doctorate to me would be like putting whipped cream on an onion."

Now, the scene of verses 18--19 is a man who is bed-ridden with palsy being brought by four of his friends to Jesus. The ["bed"]{.class_4723} of verse 18 is called a ["couch"]{.class_4723} in verse 24. It is like the furniture on which people ate in the Middle East (see our comments on Esth. 7:8 in that Commentary). It is probably being carried by staves through rings on the two sides like the furniture of the Tabernacle was transported. We would call it a "cot" or "stretcher" today.

What happens is kind of wild when you think about it. When the palsied man's four buddies can't get anywhere near the door because of the crowd, they take a side stairs on the outside of the house to the rooftop. Rooftops in the Middle East in Bible times were often flat, and people used them like we would use a balcony or veranda today. They often relaxed on their rooftops on summer evenings trying to catch a cool breeze.

When the four men got the palsied man to the roof of the house, they proceeded to remove the tiles to create a hole big enough to lower the sick man down in front of Jesus. Imagine being in that packed room when all of a sudden some dust falls from the ceiling and sunlight begins to pour into the room. Jesus keeps on teaching while everyone around Him is staring up at the growing hole overhead. Suddenly, something blocks the light as the palsied man is lowered down into the room.

["And when he saw THEIR faith"]{.class_4723} (vs. 20). Whose faith? Why, the four men who brought the palsied man to Jesus. There is no indication from the text that the sick man had any faith at all to be healed. All Charismatic faith healers blame their failures on the lack of faith of the sick person when they aren't able to heal someone. In the New Testament, [it's not the sick person who has to have the faith.]{.class_4727}

["Is any sick among you? let him call for the elders of the church; and let THEM pray over him\...And the prayer of faith ]{.class_4723}\[whose ["prayer of faith"]{.class_4723}? It's the [elders]{.class_4725} who are praying, not the sick person\][ shall save the sick, and the Lord shall raise him up; and if he have committed sins, they shall be forgiven him"]{.class_4723} (James 5:14--15).

That's what happens here in verse 20. The palsied man is healed, and his sins are forgiven, based on the faith of his [buddies,]{.class_4725} not [his ]{.class_4725}faith.

I used to have a friend named John Hall. John Hall was crippled and confined to a wheelchair, but he loved the Lord and believed the Book. Back when I had more time in the ministry, I would go to those healing meetings to see what was going on. I took John Hall with me, and we both had a good laugh. We knew that faith healer was a fake and a phony. But after about three of those meetings, John didn't []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1117.xhtml_page_175}want to go back anymore.

"Why not?" I asked.

"I just can't take it anymore," he said. "I know they're fake; I know they're lying. But when they get to singing 'Only believe; only believe. All things are possible; only believe,' I want to get out of that wheelchair and walk so bad I just break out into a sweat."

Brethren, you've never met a more dirty, rotten, low-down, God-forsaken, demon-possessed scoundrel than one of these Charismatic fakirs who would take advantage of a crippled man like that. What kind of dirty dog would do a thing like that? Oh, Oral Roberts, Kathryn Kuhlman, Robert Tilton, Benny Hinn---folks like that.

Over at the Brownsville Assembly of God, when all that foolishness was going on back in the late 90s, some poor farmer from up around Molino or Andalusia or Atmore---some little farming community around the Florida--Alabama border---had a baby die. That evangelist who was carrying on the "Brownsville Revival" (Stephen Hill) had bragged to the media that God was raising the dead at the meetings, so that farmer took the little body of that dead baby and put it in a cooler and packed it in ice and brought it to the meeting for Stephen Hill to raise from the dead. When he got there, Hill and Kilpatrick (the pastor) couldn't do anything for the dead child and said, "Oh, we didn't mean God was [physically]{.class_4725} raising the dead; we meant [spiritually."]{.class_4725} Yeah, don't you know.

["Man, thy sins are forgiven thee."]{.class_4723} Those are the sins associated with the sickness, like you read in James 5:15. That means that some sickness is due to sin. That's why the disciples ask over there in John 9:2, ["Master, who did sin, this man, or his parents, that he was born blind?"]{.class_4723} Of course, that's not always the case, as you read in John 9:3, but sometimes it is, and it is the case here in Luke 5.

["And the scribes and the Pharisees began to reason, saying, Who is this which speaketh blasphemies? Who can forgive sins, but God alone?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 21). Again, the Pharisees knew exactly who Christ was claiming to be (see our remarks on the willful rejection by the Pharisees above). Over in John 10:33, they told Him, ["For a good work we stone thee not; but for blasphemy; and because that thou, being a man, MAKEST []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1117.xhtml_page_176}THYSELF GOD."]{.class_4723}

["Who can forgive sins, but God alone?"]{.class_4723} Good question. Mohammed can't; Buddha can't; Mary can't; the Pope can't; and neither can any Roman Catholic Priest. How do you know? Because when they die, [they stay down.]{.class_4725} Look at Acts 13:37--38.

"But he, WHOM GOD RAISED AGAIN, saw no corruption. Be it known unto you therefore, men and brethren, that through this man is preached unto you the forgiveness of sins."

In verse 22, Jesus exercises His Deity by reading the thoughts of His opponents. John records this about Jesus: ["And needed not that any should testify of man: FOR HE KNEW WHAT WAS IN MAN"]{.class_4723} (John 2:25).

The Holy Spirit had Ezekiel write to the ["house of Israel"]{.class_4723}: ["I know the things that come in your mind, every one of them"]{.class_4723} (Ezek. 11:5). Before he died, David charged Solomon and said, ["for the LORD searcheth all hearts, and understandeth all the imaginations of the thoughts"]{.class_4723} (1 Chron. 28:9).

The reason this country is in the state it is in today is the teachers have stopped telling the children that God knows everything they do. Wouldn't it make you a little nervous to think of God knowing every place you put your hand and everywhere your feet go and every word your tongue says and every thought in your mind? Don't you think that would put a damper on a lot of devilment?

That Bible says that ["God shall bring every work into judgment, WITH EVERY SECRET THING, whether it be good, or whether it be evil" ]{.class_4723}(Eccl. 12:14).

There are only three religions in the entire world that teach you will give account to God after death: Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. Jews teach that Jehovah will judge you by the Ten Commandments. Islam teaches that Allah will judge you not only by the [Koran]{.class_4727}, but by Mohammed as well, because the [Koran]{.class_4727} clearly states: "He who obeys [Allah's messenger]{.class_4727} (that's Mohammed playing coy while he's talking about [himself]{.class_4727}) obeys [Allah"]{.class_4727} (Sura 4:80). Bible-believing Christianity teaches that the standard is [Jesus Christ,]{.class_4727} and the proof of that is the [Resurrection.]{.class_4727}

["In the day when God shall judge the secrets of men BY JESUS CHRIST according to my gospel"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1117.xhtml_page_177} (Rom. 2:16).

["Because he hath appointed a day, in the which he will judge the world in righteousness by that man whom he hath ordained; whereof he hath given assurance unto all men, in that HE HATH RAISED HIM FROM THE DEAD"]{.class_4723} (Acts 17:31).

["Whether is easier, to say, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Rise up and walk?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 23). When it came to the Pharisees and scribes, [neither]{.class_4725} was easier: they couldn't heal the man, and they couldn't forgive his sins either. So Jesus does [both.]{.class_4725} To prove to them that He can forgive sins, he heals the palsied man.

The reaction of the palsied man here is like that of the lame man in Acts 3:8; he goes home ["glorifying God."]{.class_4723} The reaction of the crowd is not only glorifying God, they are ["amazed"]{.class_4723} and ["filled with fear"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26). They really don't know what to think yet: ["We have seen strange things today."]{.class_4723}

But that changes in just one chapter. In 6:10, Jesus heals the withered hand of a man. When He does, look at the response of the Pharisees there: ["And they were filled with MADNESS; and communed one with another what they might do to Jesus"]{.class_4723} (6:11).

Why the difference in responses? Because in chapter 6, Jesus heals the man with the withered hand [on the Sabbath]{.class_4725} (6:7--9). That's what happened in John 5; Jesus heals an impotent man and tells him to take up his bed and walk on the Sabbath day (John 5:8--11).

In that passage, rather than rejoicing that the impotent man was healed and giving glory to God, ["the Jews persecute Jesus, and sought to slay him"]{.class_4723} (John 5:16). They were more concerned with their religious rituals than they were with God working in the life of some needy sinner. It's like those Gadarenes over in Mark 5:1--17 being more concerned over the loss of a herd of pigs than over Jesus removing a threat to their community.

27 And after these things he went forth, and saw a publican, named Levi, sitting at the receipt of custom: and he said unto him, Follow me.

28 And he left all, rose up, and followed him.

29 And Levi made him a great feast in his own house: and there was a great company of publicans and of others that sat down with them.

30 But their scribes and Pharisees murmured against his disciples, saying, Why do ye eat and drink with publicans and sinners?

31 And Jesus answering said unto them, They that are whole need not a physician; but they that are sick.

32 I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.

33 And they said unto him, Why do the disciples of John fast often, and make prayers, and likewise the disciples of the Pharisees; but thine eat and drink?

34 And he said unto them, Can ye make the children of the bridechamber fast, while the bridegroom is with them?

35 But the days will come, when the bridegroom shall be taken away from them, and then shall they fast in those days.

36 And he spake also a parable unto them; No man putteth a piece of a new garment upon an old; if otherwise, then both the new maketh a rent, and the piece that was taken out of the new agreeth not with the old.

37 And no man putteth new wine into old bottles; else the new wine will burst the bottles, and be spilled, and the bottles shall perish.

38 But new wine must be put into new bottles; and both are preserved.

39 No man also having drunk old wine straightway desireth new: for he saith, The old is better.

Verses 27--28 record the calling of the Apostle Matthew as found in Matthew 9:9. The indication from Mark 2:14 and Luke 5:27 is that his original name was Levi and that he took the name of Matthew as a surname after he began following Jesus. The name [Matthew]{.class_4725} means "gift of God." Matthew received the gift of God through the Lord Jesus Christ (Rom. 6:23) and became a new creature in Christ (vss. 36--39 cf. 2 Cor. 5:17).

Levi is of that hated class of collaborators and extortioners known as the [publicans]{.class_4725} (see our comments on 3:12). ["The receipt of custom,"]{.class_4723} then, is the tax money. The publicans were associated with ["sinners"]{.class_4723} (vs. 30) because both groups were considered by the Jews to be extremely wicked people like thieves, murderers, and prostitutes. That is different from the Pauline doctrine of the depravity of man as given in Romans 3. The Old Testament makes a practical distinction between righteous people (see our comments on 1:6) and sinners that has nothing to do with the theological doctrines of human depravity and imputed righteousness.

["Follow me"]{.class_4723} (vs. 27) is the motto of the infantry school at Ft. Benning. That is the logo I put on the shield of the Pensacola Bible Institute, except I originally put it in German ([Folgen sie meir]{.class_4725}) because it was Martin Luther's German Bible that got the ball rolling and set things up for the [King James]{.class_4725} Bible. It wasn't till years later that I found out that "PBI" (Pensacola Bible Institute) was also an acronym for "Poor Bloody Infantry." The call to the Christian soldier (2 Tim. 2:3) from the Captain of his salvation (Heb. 2:10) is[ "Follow me,"]{.class_4723} and the picture Paul gives of the Christian soldier is that of a First-Century [infantryman]{.class_4725} (Eph. 6:10--17).

Verse 30: ["But their scribes and Pharisees murmured against his disciples, saying, Why do ye eat and drink with publicans and sinners?" ]{.class_4723}That was the complaint the scribes and Pharisees had against Jesus over in 15:2---["And the Pharisees and scribes murmured, saying, This man receiveth sinners and eateth with them." ]{.class_4723}This occurred so often that they called Jesus ["a gluttonous man, and a winebibber, A FRIEND of publicans and sinners"]{.class_4723} (7:34).

WELL GLORY! That's where I get in. Jesus Christ was [my friend]{.class_4754} when I was [His enemy]{.class_4754} (Rom. 5:8--10). I wasn't thinking of Him, and He sent His Holy Spirit to convict me of sin, righteousness, and judgment (John 16:7--12). I didn't care about God or His word, and yet He put it right in my hands where I could get it, read it, and believe it (Rom. 10:8, 17).

["They that are whole need not a physician; but they that are sick. I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance"]{.class_4723} (vss. 31--32). That is, Jesus didn't come to save good folks; He came to save [really bad folks.]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1117.xhtml_page_180} You won't get saved if you don't take your place with the pimps, hookers, "second-story men," drug dealers, wife beaters, serial killers, queers, pederasts, bank robbers, etc.

The Apostle Paul wrote, ["This is a faithful saying, and worthy of all acceptation, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners; OF WHOM I AM CHIEF"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 1:15). That came from a man who could honestly say of himself: ["touching the righteousness which is in the law, blameless"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 3:6). But when Paul trusted Christ's righteousness, he counted his own righteousness as ["loss"]{.class_4723} and ["dung"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 3:7--8; you won't find that last word "updated" in the new, modern translations "in the language of the common man").

["And they said unto him, Why do the disciples of John fast often, and make prayers"]{.class_4723} (vss. 33). In the Scriptures, fasting and praying go together (Psa. 35:13, 69:10--13; Dan. 9:3; Acts 10:30). Fasting should accompany prayer when there is a really serious matter to which you need an answer (Matt. 17:21; 1 Cor. 7:5). Nor is it restricted to the Old Testament or before the Crucifixion. The early church fasted and prayed over important decisions (Acts 13:2, 14:23); the Apostle Paul practiced it and recommended it as part of the New Testament ministry (2 Cor. 6:4--5). Fasting is different from not eating because you have no food or because you are on a diet (2 Cor. 11:27); it is a time when you purposefully go without eating in order to seek God's face in a matter.

Now the question posed to Jesus in verse 33 is why do the disciples of John the Baptist and the Pharisees fast and pray all the time and your disciples don't? The illustration Jesus gives in answer to the question is rather vailed and obtuse unless you understand Jewish wedding customs. This is the kind of material scholars like to make much of in regards to a layman understanding the Bible. If you picked up any standard work on Biblical hermeneutics (i.e., Biblical interpretation) from a Christian bookstore, you would get whole chapters on "archaeology," "customs in Bible lands," and "original languages" with the point being that you can't properly interpret the Bible unless you understand all those things.

That's a bunch of baloney! The Bible is a [universal]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1117.xhtml_page_181} Book: it's written so that it can be understood in [any culture,]{.class_4725} in [any time period,]{.class_4725} by [any class,]{.class_4725} regardless of [age]{.class_4725} or [education.]{.class_4725} And while there may be instances where understanding Jewish customs helps you get the "meat" of a passage, those instances are not as many as the scholars lead you to believe. What they do is magnify passages like the one we are about to examine completely out of proportion so that you will have to come to [them]{.class_4725} for understanding of what God said.

Listen, people, do you know what the original intent of the Bible being in your language was? It was given by William Tyndale nearly a hundred years before your [King James]{.class_4725} Bible was published. Tyndale told a Catholic Priest, "If God spare my life ere long, I will make it so that a boy who driveth the plow will know more of the Scriptures than thou dost." He said that to a fellow who could read and write Hebrew, Greek, and Latin. Evidently, knowing the "original languages" and "the original text" means nothing when it comes to understanding the Holy Scriptures; it's [reading]{.class_4725} and [believing]{.class_4725} what God gave you [in your own language.]{.class_4725}

In verses 34--35, ["the children of the bridechamber"]{.class_4723} are the attendants at a wedding; they would be the bridesmaids and the groomsmen today. ["The bridegroom"]{.class_4723} is obviously a reference to Jesus Christ (John 3:29). What's missing in the illustration? It's [the Bride.]{.class_4725} The Bride is the Church, the Body of Christ (Eph. 5:22--33), and there is no revelation yet of Christ's spiritual body. So to the Pharisees, what Jesus is saying is an enigma.

The matter is further complicated by the fact that ["the children of the bridechamber"]{.class_4723} are a reference to the [Jewish]{.class_4725} disciples. While Christ is with them, they rejoice and don't fast, but when Christ is taken from them into Heaven in Acts 1, they fast (see references in Acts and 2 Cor. above). But when they get into the book of Acts, they are no longer technically Jewish disciples; they are baptized by the Holy Spirit into the Body of Christ and become part of the Bride. They are no longer ["children of the bridechamber."]{.class_4723} But in Matthew 25, you have some ["children of the bridechamber"]{.class_4723}: they are ["virgins"]{.class_4723} who go out to ["meet the bridegroom"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 25:1).

In Bible times, after a man became engaged to a woman, he would go off to build a house for her (cf. John 14:2). He would then come to get his bride and bring her back to his house (cf. John 14:3) for the wedding feast. Along the way, they would be joined by attendants. That is what is going on in Matthew 25.

The Parable of the Wise and Foolish Virgins is a [Jewish]{.class_4725} parable, not a Church Age one. The Body of Christ is a singular ["virgin"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 11:3); it is the Jewish saints in the Tribulation who are ["virgins"]{.class_4723} plural (Rev. 14:4). The virgins in Matthew 25 go out to ["meet"]{.class_4723} the Bridegroom, not to marry him. The virgins of Matthew 25 can [lose the Holy Spirit]{.class_4725} (Matt. 25:8 cf. Psa. 51:11); a born-again believer in the Body of Christ [can't]{.class_4725} (Eph. 4:30).

So in Luke 5:34--35, [doctrinally speaking,]{.class_4725} the Lord skips 2,000+ years of church history. The people who fast in verse 35 are [Jewish disciples]{.class_4725} just as they were in verse 34, but [prophetically speaking,]{.class_4725} they are the Jewish disciples of Christ in the [Tribulation]{.class_4725} (Rev. 12:17, 14:12) fasting and praying for His return at the Second Advent. They are the ["virgins"]{.class_4723} who attend the Bridegroom and the Bride at the Marriage of the Lamb (Psa. 45:14; Song of Sol. 6:8--10).

As if things weren't confusing enough, in verses 36--39, immediately after sidestepping the New Testament Bride of Christ in verses 34--35, the Lord gives two illustrations of the New Birth and the indwelling Holy Spirit. In verse 36, the word ["parable"]{.class_4723} shows up for the first time in the book of Luke. A parable is a story given for the purpose of illustration which draws a parallel or comparison between something that is known to something that is unknown. It is a figure of speech, and there are all kinds of them in the Scriptures, like an ["allegory"]{.class_4723} (Gal. 4:24) and ["similitudes"]{.class_4723} (Hos. 12:10). They are all similar, but have fine distinctions of meaning which we will not go into here.

Notice that the two illustrations are actually [one]{.class_4725} (cf. Gen. 41:25): it is ["a parable"]{.class_4723} (singular), not "parables" (plural). This means that the ["new garment"]{.class_4723} of verse 38 will answer to the ["new bottles"]{.class_4723} of verse 38, and the old garment of verse 38 will answer to the ["old bottles"]{.class_4723} of verse 37. You don't sew a new piece of cloth onto an old worn-out garment because the old won't be able to handle the stress and strain that the new can. The ["bottles"]{.class_4723} of verses 37--38 are [cloth]{.class_4725} bottles---wine skins. The ["new wine"]{.class_4723} is grape juice found []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1117.xhtml_page_183}["in the cluster"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 65:8) that is squeezed into the cup (Gen. 40:11); it is [not]{.class_4725} fermented liquor (Deut. 32:14 cf. 32:32--33). The longer new wine sits in a wine skin, it begins to ferment and produces gas as a part of the fermentation process. The reason you don't put ["new wine into old bottles"]{.class_4723} is because the old wine skins have expanded all they are going to; if you fill the old wine skin with the new wine, when it begins to ferment, the gas will burst the wine skin because it won't expand any further.

To this last illustration, Christ gives this addendum: ["No man also having drunk old wine straightway desireth new: for he saith, The old is better"]{.class_4723} (vs. 39). In other words, he's getting a "kick" out of the alcoholic content of the old wine that he doesn't get out of the grape juice, so he's enjoying the liquor better.

The whole point of all three illustrations is that you can't mix the new life in Christ (2 Cor. 5:17) with the old Adamic nature; it won't work. The new man in Christ can't be tacked onto the old man in Adam (Eph. 4:22--24); it has to be cut loose from the corrupt flesh---spiritual circumcision (see our comments on Col. 2:11--12 in that Commentary).

Curtis Hutson didn't understand that Bible doctrine. In a [Sword of the Lord]{.class_4725} article entitled "The Ramblings of Ruckman," he said, "Ruckman teaches 'spiritual circumcision,' [whatever that is."]{.class_4725} "That" is what prevents you from ending up like Adam in Genesis chapter 3. Adam sinned in the flesh, and that affected his spirit so that it died (Eph. 2:1). That's because in the Old Testament, the soul and spirit are attached to the body and are affected by it (see our comments on Gen. 2:7, 12:13 in that Commentary). In the New Testament, when the Holy Spirit comes to live in the body of the believer upon salvation, He cuts the dead, sinful flesh away from the new man in Christ by the word of God (Heb. 4:12) so that no matter what the flesh does, it will never affect the spirit again (see our work on [Body, Soul, Spirit]{.class_4725}).

The ["new wine"]{.class_4723} is a type of the Holy Spirit (Acts 2:13; Eph. 5:18). Before the Holy Spirit comes to live in your body, He has to have a new vessel. That vessel is the spiritual body of the Lord Jesus Christ. So at salvation, the Holy Spirit baptizes you into the Body of Christ so He has a proper vessel to inhabit. He puts you []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1117.xhtml_page_184}["in Christ"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 12:13) so Christ can be ["in you"]{.class_4723} (Col. 1:27).

And from Christ's final remarks in verse 39, we gather that the old nation of Israel with its system of laws and regulations is not going to be enough to "fill the bill." It's going to take a [new people]{.class_4725} to be ["the people of God"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 2:9--10); it's going to take a people born again through the blood of Christ (1 Pet. 1:18--23), which is also what new wine pictures (Matt. 26:29; John 2:3--4).

The old nature always prefers its own righteousness to God's (Rom. 10:3); that way it can take the credit for its own success (Rom. 4:2). It is Cain's fruit stand religion (Gen. 4:3). The problem is [God won't accept it]{.class_4725} (Gen. 4:5); you have to have the [blood of the Lamb]{.class_4725} (Gen. 4:4).

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml}

# Chapter 6 {.heading_78-0}

6:1 And it came to pass on the second sabbath after the first, that he went through the corn fields; and his disciples plucked the ears of corn, and did eat, rubbing them in their hands.

2 And certain of the Pharisees said unto them, Why do ye that which is not lawful to do on the sabbath days?

3 And Jesus answering them said, Have ye not read so much as this, what David did, when himself was an hungred, and they which were with him;

4 How he went into the house of God, and did take and eat the shewbread, and gave also to them that were with him; which it is not lawful to eat but for the priests alone?

5 And he said unto them, That the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath.

All the scholars will swear on a stack of lexicons that the word ["corn"]{.class_4723} here should be "grain" because actual corn ("maize") was supposed to be unknown in Egypt or Palestine. Of course, this is a "red herring" to mislead the reader into [doubting his Bible]{.class_4725} and [trusting scholarship.]{.class_4725} The word [corn]{.class_4725} in an English dictionary can be used of the kernel of any cereal plant (wheat, barley, oats, etc.), including "corn on the cob." This is how the word is defined in a [King James]{.class_4725} Bible in John 12:24, where you read ["a corn OF WHEAT."]{.class_4723} You can have a corn of wheat, a corn of barley, a corn of oat, or a corn [of corn.]{.class_4725}

Genesis 41:5--7 and Mark 4:26--29, though, talk about ["ears of corn" ]{.class_4723}and ["corn in the ear,"]{.class_4723} which has nothing to do with the kernel at all, and in 2 Samuel 17:28 and Amos 8:5, the ["wheat"]{.class_4723} and the ["corn"]{.class_4723} are given as two separate things. So I teach that when the Bible says ["corn,"]{.class_4723} it certainly could refer to "corn on the cob."

According to Stewart Custer, the former head of the Bible department at Bob Jones University ([The Truth About the King James Version Controversy,]{.class_4725} 1981, pg. 32), that is supposed to be one of Ruckman's "odd doctrines." That is because Stewart Custer []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_186}himself can be rather Liberal in his theology from time to time (see [Custer's Last Stand,]{.class_4725} 1981, pp. 51--52). For years, the Liberals said that the Bible was historically inaccurate when it talked about the Hittites, Nineveh, and Belshazzar until someone went digging over in the Middle East and found them. The Liberals were wrong all along, and the Bible was right (see our comments on 2:2).

Now the fact about corn in the Bible is that "corn corn" has been found sealed in pottery in Egyptian tombs, but because the scholars approached such evidence from the prejudice that "New World corn" wasn't in the "Old World" until Columbus brought it over, the find was dismissed as a fraud planted by a Moslem guide. But in 2008, an archaeologist named Gunnar Thompson published his findings that "Indian corn" was well documented in Egyptian temples, tombs, and scrolls 3,000 years before Columbus sailed in 1492, and that its farming spread throughout the Mediterranean area ([Ancient Egyptian Maize,]{.class_4725} 2010). So "Ruckman" wasn't quite as "peculiar" in his teachings as Custer made him out to be, but that is because "Ruckman's" approach to the Scriptures is that the [Bible is right,]{.class_4725} and where scholarship disagrees with it, "scholarship can go plumb to the devil" (Billy Sunday, 1927).

Now what the disciples were doing in verse 1 is allowed under the Law.

["When thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour, then thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand; but thou shalt not move a sickle unto thy neighbour's standing corn" (]{.class_4723}Deut. 23:25).

The idea is that one is satisfying immediate hunger and the other is theft. The problem the Pharisees have in verse 2 is that the disciples did it [on the Sabbath day.]{.class_4725}

The Sabbath was a sign given to Israel that has nothing to do with the Gentiles or the Church (Ezek. 20:12, 20 cf. 1 Cor. 1:22). Contrary to the teachings of the Seventh-day Adventists, the commandment to keep the Sabbath is a [ceremonial]{.class_4725} law (not a [moral]{.class_4725} law) that has no binding on the New Testament Christian whatsoever (Col. 2:16 cf. Rom. 13:9).

Any Seventh-day Adventist who thinks he keeps the Sabbath should consider the restrictions God put on that Jew in the Old []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_187}Testament. If you are a Seventh-day Adventist reading this, let me ask you something: do you cook your meals on "the Sabbath"? Well, if you do, you've broken the commandment. That Jew wasn't even allowed to kindle a fire on the Sabbath (Exod. 35:3). So if you light the burner on your stove on the Sabbath, you've violated the Sabbath. If you start the ignition on your car to go to Sabbath services at the Seventh-day Adventist church, you've violated the Sabbath because you've lit a spark to fire up the internal combustion engine on your car. Do you attend a Seventh-day Adventist church more than a mile from your house? Then you've violated the Sabbath (Acts 1:12).

You see, people don't think. They figure they are doing the best they can do, so they're all right. Listen, the Lord had a fellow put to death over in Numbers 15:32--36 for [gathering sticks]{.class_4725} on the Sabbath. The Seventh-day Adventist response to this is that they are keeping the spirit of the Law and not just the letter (cf. 2 Cor. 3:6). Oh, I see; you mean you are keeping the parts you [want to keep]{.class_4725} and [letting the rest slide.]{.class_4725} That's the business.

The Pharisees figured that if God were so strict about the Sabbath that He wouldn't even let the children of Israel gather manna on that day (Exod. 16:5, 22--30), then what Jesus' disciples were doing must certainly have been a violation of the Law. Jesus' response to the Pharisees was: ["Have ye not read\...?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 3). As He did with Satan back in chapter 4, Jesus answers His accusers with [Scripture.]{.class_4725} This is known as the "proof-text method" of argumentation; that is, you appeal to a text of Scripture to justify what you are doing or teaching. It is used by Jesus [and ]{.class_4725}the Devil (see our comments on 4:10--11), so to this day heretics appeal to Scripture when they want to put something over on you that isn't so. Done correctly under the leadership of the Holy Spirit (see our comments on Rom. 9:25, 10:15 in that Commentary), it is ["Sola Scriptura"]{.class_4725}: interpreting the Scriptures with the Scriptures (1 Cor. 2:13). When done incorrectly, it is "wresting the Scriptures to your own destruction" (2 Pet. 3:16).

I was witnessing to a fellow one time at the gym. He was telling me that he didn't receive Christ as his Saviour because he didn't know whom to believe: the Catholics say one thing; the Jehovah's Witnesses say another; the Baptists say another. I asked that fellow, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_188}"You mean to tell me that you're a grown man with a College education, and you can't tell when a fellow is lying to you from Scripture?" If you want to figure out whether someone is telling you the truth about what the Scriptures say, open up the Scriptures and READ THEM. Read them in their [context.]{.class_4725} Make sure nothing is being [added to]{.class_4725} or [subtracted from]{.class_4725} them. [Study]{.class_4725} them; [rightly divide]{.class_4725} them (2 Tim. 2:15). Be like those Bereans over in Acts 17:11---search the Scriptures to see whether what you are being told is so.

The point Christ makes in verses 3--4 is that the Lord allows exceptions to the Law in the Scriptures. Over in Matthew 12:5, He says, ["Or have ye not read in the law, how that on the sabbath days the priests in the temple profane the sabbath and are blameless."]{.class_4723} You will find an example of this in Numbers 29:32. Sacrifices were still made by the priests on the Sabbath day; the showbread was baked fresh daily, even on the Sabbath. The Israelites were allowed to violate the Sabbath when they conquered Jericho (Josh. 6:3--4).

The example Jesus gives here is a bombshell. It is David in exile (1 Sam. 21). Like David, Jesus had already been anointed to be King (1 Sam. 16:13 cf. Matt. 3:16--17). And just as David could not take the throne until years later after the death of Saul, Christ's ascent to the throne of David got put off 2,000+ years because the Jews rejected Him and tried to kill Him as Saul tried to kill David. Right now, Jesus Christ is in "exile" and won't take the throne for which He has been anointed until "Saul" (the [Antichrist]{.class_4725}) is dead.

["And he said unto them, That the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath"]{.class_4723} (vs. 5). An obvious allusion to Christ's Deity. The reason He can make an exception to the Sabbath is because He's the one who instituted it; He's the God of the Old Testament.

Note the term ["the Son of man"]{.class_4723}; it is Christ's favorite reference to Himself in the Gospels. The emphasis is on His unique birth by a virgin (Isa. 7:14). He's not just "A son of man" as anyone else would be; He is ["THE Son of man,"]{.class_4723} the one chosen by God as the only mediator between Him and man (1 Tim. 2:5). In like fashion, He is not just "A son of God" (see our comments on 3:38); He is ["THE Son of God,"]{.class_4723} the only one begotten directly by God (Psa. 2:7).

6 And it came to pass also on another sabbath, that he entered into the synagogue and taught: and there was a man whose right hand was withered.

7 And the scribes and Pharisees watched him, whether he would heal on the sabbath day; that they might find an accusation against him.

8 But he knew their thoughts, and said to the man which had the withered hand, Rise up, and stand forth in the midst. And he arose and stood forth.

9 Then said Jesus unto them, I will ask you one thing; Is it lawful on the sabbath days to do good, or to do evil? to save life, or to destroy it?

10 And looking round about upon them all, he said unto the man, Stretch forth thy hand. And he did so: and his hand was restored whole as the other.

11 And they were filled with madness; and communed one with another what they might do to Jesus.

Here is another encounter Jesus has with the Pharisees over the Sabbath. The Pharisees aren't watching Christ to verify His claims of Deity and Messiahship; they are watching Him in order to accuse Him (vs. 7).

We have already commented on Jesus' ability to read the thoughts of men (vs. 8) under 5:22, which see. Two good cross-references to verse 9 are found in 13:15 and 14:5 here in Luke.

["The Lord then answered him, and said, Thou hypocrite, doth not each one of you on the sabbath loose his ox or his ass from the stall, and lead him away to watering?" ]{.class_4723}(Luke 13:15).

["And answered them, saying, Which of you shall have an ass or an ox fallen into a pit, and will not straightway pull him out on the sabbath day?"]{.class_4723} (Luke 14:5).

To those two verses, you can add Paul's comment: ["Doth God take care for oxen?"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 9:9).

In other words, those Jews recognized exceptional cases for breaking the Sabbath that were allowed by God. Why did they give more care to their [own beasts]{.class_4725} than they did to [God's people?]{.class_4725}

The Pharisees refuse to answer the question Christ posed to them []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_190}in verse 9 (Mark 3:4), so the Lord answers it Himself in Matthew 12:12---["Wherefore it is lawful to do well on the sabbath days."]{.class_4723}

To verse 10, Mark 3:5 adds: ["And when he had looked round about on them WITH ANGER, BEING GRIEVED FOR THE HARDNESS OF THEIR HEARTS."]{.class_4723} Jesus grew angry with those Jews from time to time (see John 2:15), and for good reason. So in Matthew 5:22, the new "Bibles" make Christ a sinner in danger of judgment by removing the phrase ["without a cause"]{.class_4723}: ["whosoever is angry with his brother WITHOUT A CAUSE shall be in danger of the judgment."]{.class_4723} Jesus [did get angry]{.class_4725} with folks, but He always [had a cause.]{.class_4725} As a matter of fact, Paul [commanded]{.class_4725} you to be angry at some things as long as you did not sin in doing it (Eph. 4:26). The corrupt, apostate translations create something not found in a [King James]{.class_4725} Bible: [contradictions in the Scriptures.]{.class_4725}

We have already commented on verse 11 under 5:26.

12 And it came to pass in those days, that he went out into a mountain to pray, and continued all night in prayer to God.

13 And when it was day, he called unto him his disciples: and of them he chose twelve, whom also he named apostles;

14 Simon, (whom he also named Peter,) and Andrew his brother, James and John, Philip and Bartholomew,

15 Matthew and Thomas, James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon called Zelotes,

16 And Judas the brother of James, and Judas Iscariot, which also was the traitor.

We have already commented on Christ's all-night prayer meetings under 5:16 (which see). Here He consults His Father as to which disciples He should choose as His apostles. At least three times in His high priestly prayer, Jesus says the Father gave Him the twelve apostles (John 17:11--12, 24). The only one of them that was lost was Judas Iscariot (John 17:12), but even he was purposefully []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_191}chosen by Jesus under the leadership of the Father (John 6:70--71).

The word ["apostles"]{.class_4723} means "sent ones." They are the disciples who were ["sent forth"]{.class_4723} by Jesus to preach ["to the lost sheep of the house of Israel"]{.class_4723} in Matthew 10:5--7.

["Of them he chose twelve"]{.class_4723}: that's found in Luke 6:[13.]{.class_4725} Twelve and one (Jesus) makes [thirteen.]{.class_4725} The [thirteenth]{.class_4725} man is ["Judas Iscariot\...the traitor"]{.class_4723}; he's the last man in every list of the apostles given in the Gospels. According to Jesus, the [thirteenth]{.class_4725} man was ["a devil"]{.class_4723} (John 6:70--71), so in the Bible, the number [thirteen]{.class_4725} has a bad meaning 85% of the time, which is where the idea comes of thirteen being an unlucky number. The word ["sinners"]{.class_4723} first shows up in your Bible in Genesis [13:13.]{.class_4725} Nimrod, a type of the Antichrist, is the [thirteenth]{.class_4725} from Adam (1 Chron. 1:1--10). Judas Iscariot has [thirteen letters]{.class_4725} in it, and he is the one referred to in John [13:13,]{.class_4725} which has [thirteen words]{.class_4725} in it (if you don't count the italicized word added by the [AV]{.class_4725} translators), with [39 letters]{.class_4725} in those words (13x3). (For further study, get the work [Bible Numerics,]{.class_4725} Bible Baptist Bookstore, 2002.)

The choice of [twelve]{.class_4725} apostles reinforces the "Jewishness" of Christ's earthly ministry. The "Twelve" are to be set over ["the twelve tribes of Israel"]{.class_4723} in the Millennium (Matt. 19:28); they are the New Testament counterparts of the twelve patriarchs in the Old Testament (Rev. 21:12--14 cf. Exod. 1:1--5). They are sent to Israel both before and after the Resurrection (Matt. 10:5--6; Gal. 2:7--9).

The twelve apostles are listed three times in the Gospels (here in vss. 14--16; Matt. 10:2--4; Mark 3:16--19), and once in Acts 1:13 (minus Judas Iscariot, of course). In every list, the first name given is Simon Peter's. That is one of the reasons the Catholics call him the "Prince of Apostles" and make him the first Pope. Unfortunately for them, Peter never did claim to be the "Vicar of Christ." Peter called himself ["AN apostle"]{.class_4723} in both of his epistles (1 Pet. 1:1; 2 Pet. 1:1), not "the Prince of apostles." All Peter ever claimed to be was ["an elder"]{.class_4723} among other elders (1 Pet. 5:1).

Simon Peter wouldn't think of calling himself "Holy Father" (cf. John 17:11) or letting anybody bow down to him and kiss his ring (Acts 10:25--26). Peter called himself ["a servant"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 1:1) and said the Lord Jesus Christ was the Bishop over all other Bishops (1 []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_192}Pet. 2:25, 5:4). Simon Peter was a cursing, swearing commercial fisherman who, from time to time, rejected sound doctrine (Gal. 2:11--14) and let Satan control him (Matt. 16:22--23); that's some "Holy Father" you've got there.

The name ["Simon"]{.class_4723} means "shifting sand." When he met Jesus, the Lord changed his name to ["Peter,"]{.class_4723} meaning ["a stone"]{.class_4723} (John 1:42) or "a rock." So there are [two stones]{.class_4725} (1 Pet. 2:4--6; John 1:42) and [two rocks]{.class_4725} (Matt. 16:18; 1 Pet. 2:8); the Catholics just picked the [wrong one]{.class_4725} (Deut. 32:31). For further comments, get the [Bible Believer's Commentary on John]{.class_4725} and the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Matthew,]{.class_4725} and study John 1:42 and Matthew 16:18.

["And Andrew his brother"]{.class_4723} (vs. 14). Andrew is the personal worker of the twelve. He is the one who brought his brother Simon Peter to Jesus (John 1:40--42); he is the one who brought the little boy with the five loaves and two fishes to Jesus at the feeding of the 5,000 (John 6:8--9).

["James and John."]{.class_4723} Those are the sons of Zebedee, another set of brothers (there's a possible third set with Matthew who was called ["the son of Alphaeus"]{.class_4723} \[Mark 2:14\] and ["James the son of Alphaeus"]{.class_4723} in vs. 15). They were Simon Peter's business partners in the fishing industry (5:10). Jesus calls them ["Boanerges,"]{.class_4723} meaning ["The sons of thunder"]{.class_4723} (Mark 3:17), because when they first start out with Jesus, both of them have quite a temper (see our comments on 9:54--56).

["Philip"]{.class_4723} is the one who brought Nathanael to Christ in John 1:45--46 (with the speculation being that the ["Nathanael"]{.class_4723} of John is the ["Bartholomew"]{.class_4723} in the apostolic lists \[vs. 14\]). He is the one who calculates the amount of money needed to feed the 5,000 in John 6:7, and who asks Jesus to show them the Father at the Last Supper (John 14:8--9).

We have already commented on ["Matthew"]{.class_4723} (vs. 15) under 4:27. ["Thomas"]{.class_4723} is the famous "doubting Thomas" of John 20:24--29 (see our remarks on the passage in that Commentary). He was ["called Didymus"]{.class_4723} (John 11:16, 20:24), which is the Greek word for "twin."

["James the son of Alphaeus"]{.class_4723} is one of three Jameses listed in this passage and Acts 1:13. There is James the brother of John and the son of Zebedee (vs. 14), there is []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_193}["James the son of Alphaeus"]{.class_4723} (vs. 15), and there is a James who is the brother of another Judas (vs. 16). In addition to those Jameses, there are also ["James the less,"]{.class_4723} the son of Cleophas and a different Mary than the mother of Jesus (Mark 15:40 cf. John 19:25), and ["James the Lord's brother"]{.class_4723} mentioned by Paul in Galatians 1:19 and 1 Corinthians 15:7.

The tendency of the scholars who accept Catholic tradition over the Bible in these matters is to make ["James the Lord's brother"]{.class_4723} a [cousin]{.class_4725} instead of a half-brother as you find it in Mark 6:3. They get this by making the ["Mary"]{.class_4723} of Mark 6:3 ["the wife of Cleophas"]{.class_4723} (John 19:25), since her first two children's names were ["James, and Joses"]{.class_4723} (Mark 6:3 cf. Mark 15:40).

Now since we have already covered these matters on brothers, sisters, and cousins thoroughly under our remarks on 1:36 (which see), we will not cover the same ground again here. Suffice it to say that names such as Mary (Miriam), James (Jacob), and Joses (Joseph) were just as common names among First Century Jews as John, Bob, and Smith are in America today. There are at least six "Marys" in the New Testament (see our comments on Rom. 16:6 in that Commentary). In comparing Scripture with Scripture, I take it that there are three main Jameses in the Scriptures.

1\. James, the brother of John and the son of Zebedee. I take this James to be the author of the epistle bearing his name, and you can see my remarks in the introduction to that Commentary for my reasons why.

2\. James the half-brother (NOT cousin) of the Lord Jesus Christ. He was converted after the Resurrection and became an apostle outside of the twelve and the pastor of the church at Jerusalem (Acts 15:13, 21:18; Gal. 1:19, 2:9, 12).

3\. ["James the son of Alphaeus." "Cleophas"]{.class_4723} (John 19:25) is supposed to be the Hebrew form of ["Alphaeus,"]{.class_4723} so this James [may]{.class_4725} have been the ["James the less"]{.class_4723} of Mark 15:40. If not, then ["James the less"]{.class_4723} would be a fourth James. This James may or may not have been an actual cousin of the Lord Jesus, but whether he was or whether he wasn't, he certainly was [not]{.class_4725} ["James the Lord's brother"]{.class_4723} or the author of the epistle of James. I take him to be the brother of the Apostle Judas ([not]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_194} Judas Iscariot) in verse 15 here in Luke 6.

Back to Luke 6, verse 15: ["Simon called Zelotes."]{.class_4723} This would be ["Simon the Canaanite"]{.class_4723} of Matthew 10:4. He was not a ["Canaanite"]{.class_4723} by nationality, but by political party. The Zealots were Jews who wanted the land of Canaan back under Jewish control (the name [Palestine]{.class_4725} is a Roman name for the land of the [Philistines;]{.class_4725} the Jews would naturally use the name God gave the Promised Land back in the Old Testament. There never has been any such thing as a "Palestinian State" in 6,000 years of world history). Like the "Stern Gang" of the 1920s (see [Israel: A Deadly Piece of Dirt,]{.class_4725} 2001, pg. 287), they didn't hesitate to use guerilla warfare and counter-terrorism tactics to try to free the land of Canaan from Roman occupation.

["And Judas the brother of James"]{.class_4723} (vs. 16). This is the ["Judas\...not Iscariot"]{.class_4723} of John 14:22. If you compare Scripture with Scripture, this would be the ["Lebbaeus"]{.class_4723} of Matthew 10:4 who was surnamed ["Thaddaeus."]{.class_4723} There is nothing odd or unusual for someone to have three names in or out of the Scriptures. One of the two men who were considered to take Judas Iscariot's place among the twelve apostles had three names (Acts 1:23---["Joseph called Barsabas, who was surnamed Justus"]{.class_4723}); I have three names ("Peter Sturges Ruckman"); and I dare say most people reading this page have three names (first, middle, and last). The idea that the various apostolic lists contradict each other because different names are given for the same people is just a little too ridiculous for words.

17 And he came down with them, and stood in the plain, and the company of his disciples, and a great multitude of people out of all Judaea and Jerusalem, and from the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon, which came to hear him, and to be healed of their diseases;

18 And they that were vexed with unclean spirits: and they were healed.

19 And the whole multitude sought to touch him: for there went virtue out of him, and healed them all.

Now this is not the "Sermon on the Mount." This sermon is given after the calling of the twelve apostles in Matthew 10, and Christ doesn't go ["up into a mountain"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:1) to preach it. Here He comes down from the mountain with the twelve ["and stood in the plain"]{.class_4723} (vs. 17). So this is the "Sermon on the Plain," but much of the same material that Jesus gave in the Sermon on the Mount is covered here, with some variation. They are not identical, though, because they are not the exact same message.

Now, anyone in the ministry is familiar with this situation. Anything worth saying is worth saying more than once. I have over 300 messages prepared. Some I preach every three years because of the students who go through the three-year program here at the Bible Institute; over half the congregation is new each time I preach those sermons. Any members who have been with me for 25 to thirty years (or more) have heard those sermons at least eight times. Because of the importance of the material covered in some of the other sermons (e.g., "Seven Mysteries," "Seven Baptisms," "Four Judgments"), I preach those more often (every one to two years). But each time I deliver one of those messages, it is a little different than the time before. So anyone who finds a "contradiction" between Luke 6 and Matthew 5--7 is sorely lacking experience in the ministry.

["And a great multitude of people out of all Judaea and Jerusalem, and from the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon, which came to hear him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 17). Now Tyre and Sidon are outside of the Promised Land, but because of what took place during the intertestamental period, they're connected with Israel as part of the province of Syria (see our comments on 2:2). That's why that Gentile woman in Mark 7:26 is called a ["Syrophenician"]{.class_4723}: she came from the coasts of ["Tyre and Sidon"]{.class_4723} (Mark 7:24), which were two Phoenician cities in the Greek province of Syria. Acts 12:20 says that ["Tyre and Sidon"]{.class_4723} were ["nourished"]{.class_4723} by Herod, King of Galilee, so there was another connection.

For the last part of verse 17 and for verse 18, we refer you to our remarks under 4:40--41.

["And the whole multitude sought to touch him: for there went virtue out of him, and healed them all"]{.class_4723} (vs. 19). This is where the Charismatics get their nonsense about a "point of []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_196}contact" (see our remarks on that under 4:39). Those sick folks in verses 17--18 heard how Jesus healed people by laying His hands on them (see 4:40), and they figured that the healing was in the touch. Over in the book of Acts, they are trying to get healed by Peter's shadow passing over them (Acts 5:15), and another bunch is being healed by aprons and handkerchiefs that had touched the body of Paul (Acts 19:12). But as we already saw in Luke 4:39, physical contact, direct or indirect, is not necessary for divine healing. The healing is in the Lord Jesus Christ Himself and those to whom He gives the gift and power to heal.

What is going on here is that these sick folk are coming to Jesus Christ in faith for healing, and the Lord is honoring their faith (see our remarks on the woman with the issue of blood under 8:43--48). When we get to the next chapter, we are going to run into a Gentile who has more faith than the Jews here in verse 19; he has faith to believe that Jesus can heal his servant [without]{.class_4725} a "point of contact" (7:6--10).

The word ["virtue"]{.class_4723} in verse 19 has two meanings in the Scripture. One is moral character, as in Proverbs 31:10---["who can find a virtuous woman?"]{.class_4723} The other is divine power, and that is the meaning here.

20 And he lifted up his eyes on his disciples, and said, Blessed be ye poor: for yours is the kingdom of God.

21 Blessed are ye that hunger now: for ye shall be filled. Blessed are ye that weep now: for ye shall laugh.

22 Blessed are ye, when men shall hate you, and when they shall separate you from their company, and shall reproach you, and cast out your name as evil, for the Son of man's sake.

23 Rejoice ye in that day, and leap for joy: for, behold, your reward is great in heaven: for in the like manner did their fathers unto the prophets.

24 But woe unto you that are rich! for ye have received your consolation.

25 Woe unto you that are full! for ye shall hunger. Woe unto you that laugh now! for ye shall mourn and weep.

26 Woe unto you, when all men shall speak well of you! for so did their fathers to the false prophets.

Verses 20--23 are an abbreviated version of the "Beatitudes" found in Matthew 5:2--12. But notice the addition of "woes" in verses 24--26 that are not found in Matthew's version. This contrast between blessings and woes calls to mind the blessings and cursings given to the children of Israel in Deuteronomy 27--28 before they entered the Promised Land. This places what you are about to read in a completely [Jewish]{.class_4727} setting. There are no "Christians" here in Luke 6, and the doctrinal application has nothing to do with any born-again believer in the Body of Christ (Jew or Gentile). Notice the ["fathers"]{.class_4723} in verses 23 and 26; those are the [Jewish]{.class_4727} fathers you read about in 1 & 2 Kings, 2 Chronicles, and Jeremiah. This is the [Jewish]{.class_4727} Messiah talking to His [Jewish]{.class_4727} disciples.

["Blessed be ye poor: for yours is the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (vs. 20). Right off the bat, there are marked differences between Matthew's "Beatitudes" and Luke's. Note first that the "reward" for the poor is ["the kingdom of God,"]{.class_4723} [not]{.class_4725} ["the kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:3).

Now, if you went to any major Christian College, University, or Seminary in the United States or Europe, or if you read after the main expositors or commentators accepted by Christian scholars, you would be told that the Kingdom of God and the Kingdom of Heaven are the same.[ They aren't the same anywhere in the Book.]{.class_4725} The words [God]{.class_4725} and [Heaven]{.class_4725} aren't even the same, or synonymous for that matter. Heaven is a [place created by God]{.class_4725} (Gen. 1:1). The Kingdom of Heaven is a [physical]{.class_4725} kingdom that can be taken ["by force"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 11:12). The Kingdom of God is a [spiritual]{.class_4725} kingdom that, [right now,]{.class_4725} has nothing to do with anything physical.

["For the kingdom of God is not meat and drink, but righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 14:17).

The reason the dumb quacks in "institutions of higher learning" can't get it is because when Jesus Christ was here on earth, [both kingdoms were present]{.class_4725} because He is the King of [both kingdoms.]{.class_4725} When the Jews reject their King, the physical kingdom is taken from []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_198}them until His return in power. Right now, the Kingdom of God is an [inward]{.class_4725} kingdom (Luke 17:21) that is entered at the New Birth (John 3:5). No "kingdom" is being "spread" or "built" now except the Kingdom of Death and Hell (Isa. 28:18). [Right now,]{.class_4725} sinners are invited to leave that kingdom and enter an invisible, spiritual one which they will one day ["see"]{.class_4723} when the King comes back to rule and reign (John 3:3). The reason you will be able to [see]{.class_4725} it then (if you are saved) is because you will have a glorified body just like His (Phil. 3:20--21; 1 John 3:2). When the King returns in power, both kingdoms---the physical Kingdom of Heaven and the spiritual Kingdom of God---will be right there visible. Men will comply inwardly and outwardly, or they will get it in the neck (Matt. 5; Zech. 14).

Secondly, note the [physical]{.class_4727} nature of the blessings in verses 20--21. They are not ["Blessed are the poor IN SPIRIT" ]{.class_4723}or ["Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst AFTER RIGHTEOUSNESS"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:3, 6). The blessing here in Luke is for those who are poor and hungry---[period.]{.class_4727} How do you know that is so? Look at the contrast in verses 24--25.

"But woe unto you that are RICH! for ye have received your consolation. Woe unto you that are full! for ye shall hunger."

That's [physical]{.class_4725} prosperity. Why would God be cursing someone who is "rich in spirit" or "full in righteousness"? Don't you recognize what you are reading? It's Luke 16:25---the rich man and Lazarus.

"But Abraham said, Son, remember that thou in thy lifetime receivedst thy good things, and likewise Lazarus evil things: but now he is comforted, and thou art tormented."

That's a rich man suffering in Hell just for being rich. Then it has nothing to do with right now in the Church Age. A sinner can go to Hell just as easily from the Bowery as he can from the Hamptons; salvation in this present dispensation has nothing to do with one's financial status.[ But it will in the Tribulation.]{.class_4725}

In the Tribulation, just before the return of Christ, a rich man is cursed and a poor man is blessed because the only way you can get any money in the Tribulation is to take the Mark of the Beast (Rev. []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_199}13:17). That's why the rich man in James 5:1--8 is cursed, because the context is just before ["the coming of the Lord"]{.class_4723} (James 5:7--8).

The emphasis in Matthew is if that Jew is to gain a physical kingdom (["the kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4723}), he has to have an inward change (["poor in spirit," "meek," "hunger and thirst after righteousness," "merciful," "pure in heart," "peacemakers"]{.class_4723}---Matt. 5:3--9). The emphasis in Luke is if that Jew is ever to gain a spiritual kingdom, he has to endure a time of physical hardship at the hands of his enemies (Matt. 24:13). And while there is certainly spiritual and practical application to be made to the New Testament believer, [neither of those apply doctrinally now.]{.class_4725}

The born-again child of God enters the Kingdom of God at his New Birth when he places his faith and trust in Christ for salvation, and he will see that kingdom when his Saviour comes for him at the Rapture and changes him from corruptible to incorruptible and from mortal to immortal (1 Cor. 15:51--54). His status "in Christ" guarantees that he will one day rule and reign with his Lord in the Kingdom of Heaven as a King and priest (Rev. 1:5--6, 5:9--10), and the extent of that reign depends on faithful service to Him in this life (Luke 19:12--19; 2 Tim. 2:12).

Now those are the Kingdom of Heaven and the Kingdom of God. They are not the same, but when the King is here, they are both present at the same time. That's why you will read similar statements about both in the Gospels. Dogs and cats both have four legs, they both have tails, and they both have fur. [But they are not the same.]{.class_4727} Yet, they can sometimes be [found together.]{.class_4727}

["Blessed are ye that weep now: for ye shall laugh"]{.class_4723} (vs. 21). A direct reference to the restoration of [Israel]{.class_4725} at the Second Advent.

["To appoint unto them that mourn in Zion, to give unto them beauty for ashes, the oil of joy for mourning, the garment of praise for the spirit of heaviness; that they might be called trees of RIGHTEOUSNESS]{.class_4723} \[there's that spiritual kingdom of vs. 20---cf. Rom. 14:17; Matt. 6:33\][, the planting of the LORD, that he might be glorified"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 61:3).

Now, verses 20--21 have strict doctrinal application to Jewish believers in the Tribulation, but when you get to verses 22--23, you have a blessing that's good for anybody at any time. Notice in verse 23 that the ["reward"]{.class_4723} is ["in heaven,"]{.class_4723} not in "the kingdom" or on earth. So verses 22--23 can be claimed by the Christian in the Body of Christ.

["Blessed are ye, when men shall hate you\..."]{.class_4723} (vs. 22). That's a strange thing; I haven't heard that preached lately on any "family hour" or Christian FM station. Modern Christianity is more concerned with being accepted and loved by everybody than bringing on the hatred and reproach of a lost, sinful world.

["\...and when they shall separate you from their company\...." ]{.class_4723}If you are "accepted" by the world and in good standing with them, you are in the [wrong company.]{.class_4725} When Peter warmed himself by the fire with the crowd that arrested Jesus, he got in trouble. He should have separated from them. If he had spoken up for Jesus as he should have, [they would have separated from him.]{.class_4725}

When I first got saved, one of the first problems I had was how to get rid of my buddies from the unsaved days. There was one in particular, a radio announcer at WEAR, who always used to go out for a beer with me after our shift was up. I talked to an evangelist named James McGinlay about it.

"How am I going to get rid of this fellow I used to drink beer with?" I asked him.

"You won't have to get rid of him," he said, "he'll get rid of you."

"Oh no," I told him, "we've been buddies for a long time."

"Are you afraid to confess Christ publicly to him?" Bro. McGinlay asked.

"No," I replied.

"Try it," he said, "and he'll get rid of you."

So the next day at the radio station when I finished my shift, my drinking buddy came by and said, "Let's go have a beer, Ruckman."

"O.K.," I said, "just a minute." I took him back in the record room and shut the door behind us so we could have some privacy. Then I asked him, "Have you ever been saved?"

He turned beet red and said, "What's the matter with you, Ruckman? Are you getting religion?"

"No, I'm not getting religion," I told him. "I just got saved; I received Jesus Christ as my Saviour. Have you ever been born again?"

He shot out the door of that record room, and we never had another beer together ever again. Do you want to get rid of the old crowd? Start speaking up for Jesus Christ, and they'll scatter.

["\...and shall reproach you, and cast out your name as evil, for the Son of man's sake."]{.class_4723} That's good for a Christian. The Apostle Peter says, ["If ye be reproached for the name of Christ, happy are ye; for the spirit of glory and of God resteth upon you: on their part he is evil spoken of, but on your part he is glorified"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 4:14).

Now the key in both passages is to suffer persecution for [Jesus' name,]{.class_4725} not [your own stupidity.]{.class_4725} We had a fellow come here to school one time who was obsessed with being persecuted. The problem was he would always do something stupid to bring on the persecution; he wasn't being persecuted for Christ's sake at all. One time, he took six of our students and got on some church's property here in town and started street preaching. When that church called the law out on him for trespassing, instead of moving to a public sidewalk across the street, he provoked the officers until they arrested him and the six young men with him and hauled them all off to jail. At that time Lester Roloff was having a big rally out in Texas against the HEW, and he wanted me to come out and stand with him. He said, "Bro. Ruckman, some of our girls from the home are in jail." I had to tell him, "I can't come, Bro. Roloff, because some of my young men here are in jail."

Later on, that same fellow was invited by a native pastor over in central Africa to go minister in that country. The African pastor told him, "We have a place for you and your family to stay, and have taken care of all your paperwork. Just don't bring any guns or a large amount of cash into the country." So with everything set up for him to go as a missionary to that country, do you know what that fool did? He showed up by himself without his family and with a duffle bag full of weapons and cash. He got himself and that native pastor thrown in prison and wrote letters back to the States like he was the Apostle Paul writing "prison epistles." He ruined that African pastor's ministry by being an [idiot,]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_202} not for anything that had to do with ["the name of Christ."]{.class_4723}

In that passage in 1 Peter 4, Peter goes on to say, ["But let none of you suffer as a murderer, or as a thief, or as an evildoer, or as a busybody in other men's matters"]{.class_4736} (1 Pet. 4:15), implying that a believer can indeed suffer for all those things. But if you don't want to be ashamed of why you suffer, you are to ["suffer as a Christian"]{.class_4736} (1 Pet. 4:16); that is, you are to suffer for living as a Christian [ought]{.class_4733} to live.

Back to Luke 6: ["Rejoice ye in that day]{.class_4723} \[i.e., the day you suffer reproach for Christ's sake\][, and leap for joy\..."]{.class_4723} (vs. 23). What a way to respond to persecution! Peter says, ["But and if ye suffer for righteousness' sake, HAPPY ARE YE"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 3:14). The early church rejoiced ["that they were counted worthy to suffer shame"]{.class_4723} for the name of Christ (Acts 5:41).

["\...for, behold, your reward is great in heaven ]{.class_4723}\[for the New Testament believer in the Body of Christ, at the Judgment Seat of Christ---1 Cor. 3:9--14; for the Tribulation saint, at the White Throne Judgment---Rev. 11:18\][: for in like manner did their fathers unto the prophets."]{.class_4723} The prophets here are to be distinguished from ["the false prophets"]{.class_4723} of verse 26. The false prophets in the Old Testament were marked by their [positive ]{.class_4725}message and by their [changing what God said]{.class_4725} (Jer. 23:13--38).

["Woe unto you, when all men shall speak well of you!"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26). So when you are a "prophet with honor" like Billy Graham (see our comments on 4:24), you are [cursed by God.]{.class_4725} If everyone speaks ["well of you"]{.class_4723} like they do Joel Osteen and Rick Warren, you are a [false prophet,]{.class_4725} not a "man of God" or a Bible-believing preacher. Jesus said, ["that which is highly esteemed among men is abomination in the sight of God"]{.class_4723} (16:15).

27 But I say unto you which hear, Love your enemies, do good to them which hate you,

28 Bless them that curse you, and pray for them which despitefully use you.

29 And unto him that smiteth thee on the one cheek offer also the other; and him that taketh away thy cloke []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_203}forbid not to take thy coat also.

30 Give to every man that asketh of thee; and of him that taketh away thy goods ask them not again.

31 And as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise.

32 For if ye love them which love you, what thank have ye? for sinners also love those that love them.

33 And if ye do good to them which do good to you, what thank have ye? for sinners also do even the same.

34 And if ye lend to them of whom ye hope to receive, what thank have ye? for sinners also lend to sinners, to receive as much again.

35 But love ye your enemies, and do good, and lend, hoping for nothing again; and your reward shall be great, and ye shall be the children of the Highest: for he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil.

36 Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful.

Verse 27 (along with verse 35) shows you that Islam, Mohammed, and the [Koran]{.class_4725} are completely, 100% [Satanic.]{.class_4725} The New Testament command is to ["overcome evil with good"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 12:21). The teaching of Mohammed, according to the Ulamas, Hafizes, and Imams, is that Jesus and Paul didn't know about what they were talking: overcoming ["evil with good"]{.class_4723} and loving your enemies [don't work.]{.class_4725} The teaching of Islam on how to treat one's enemies is to [retaliate]{.class_4725} (blood for blood if necessary---Sura 2:178), [avenge]{.class_4725} yourself (Sura 2:194), and do it according to [your own depraved imagination]{.class_4725} (Sura 23:96, note 1727---"the best way you [think]{.class_4725} is the way to do it"). And of course, Mohammed himself had no qualms in having people murdered in the most horrific fashions possible just for [making fun of him]{.class_4725} (see the work [Will the Real "Allah" Please Stand Up?,]{.class_4725} 2010, pp. 82--83, 113); that was the best way [he]{.class_4725} thought was the way to do it.

That is [not ]{.class_4725}the way of Jesus Christ. He says in verse 28: ["Bless them that curse you, and pray for them which despitefully use you."]{.class_4723} That command is repeated by the Apostle Paul for the New Testament Church in Romans 12:14---[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_204}["Bless them which persecute you: bless and curse not."]{.class_4723}

Jesus prayed for those who crucified Him, even as He was dying (Luke 23:34); so did Stephen (Acts 7:60). When Peter tried to defend the Lord with a sword, Jesus told him to put it up because that wasn't the way Jesus wanted things done (John 18:11). ["The weapons of our warfare are not carnal"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 10:4); they're [spiritual.]{.class_4725} They're [truth, righteousness,]{.class_4725} the [preaching of the Gospel, faith, salvation,]{.class_4725} the [word of God, ]{.class_4725}and [prayer]{.class_4725} (Eph. 6:14--18). They're not guns, bombs, rockets, anthrax, missiles, and nuclear weapons.

Islam and Biblical Christianity are [not the same;]{.class_4725} there is no such thing as "Christlam." And it's not merely a matter of one being right and the other being wrong. Obviously one is [terribly right]{.class_4725} and the other is [terribly wrong,]{.class_4725} and the one that is absolutely right is [faith in the Lord Jesus Christ.]{.class_4725}

Listen, folks, if you've got to defend or spread your religion with bloodshed, [you've got the wrong religion.]{.class_4725} If you can't defend and propagate your religion [logically, rightly, spiritually, factually, ]{.class_4725}and[ rationally]{.class_4725} without killing someone or maiming them or beating them, [you ain't got nothin']{.class_4725} when it comes to a religion.

I'm not worried about what I believe. Those Jews call Jesus Christ a bastard born of a whore, and all the Rabbis teach that (John 8:41). You say, "What do you think about that, Ruckman?" I don't think anything about it on a personal level. I think God takes care of fools and drunkards. I think God is merciful and longsuffering with a lot of people. It's my job to pray for them and witness to them and to try to win them to Christ. I've got no business going around and saying, "How dare you say a thing like that! That offends me and insults 'Allah'! Away with him! Kill him!"

Aw, BALONEY! My God is big enough to take care of Himself (see Judg. 6:31). If my God wants you dead, He'll drop you like a hot rock; He'll drop you like a bad habit. I don't have to do my God's "dirty work" for Him.

That's why a Mohammedan has to kill: he can't defend his religion in any other way. The reason he can't defend his religion is because it is the work of an [insane man:]{.class_4725} it's the work of a paranoid, fornicating, epileptic killer. They can't defend it on any kind of rational or []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_205}spiritual level, so they kill anyone who gets in the way. That's all there is to that.

["And unto him that smiteth thee on the one cheek offer also the other"]{.class_4723} (vs. 29). Now the question arises in verses 29--30, do these things apply to a New Testament Christian [doctrinally? ]{.class_4725}This first thing with turning the other cheek is a good test case. One of the officers of the high priest struck Jesus in the face at His trial. Christ's response was not to turn the other cheek. It is true that ["when he was reviled"]{.class_4723} He ["reviled not again,"]{.class_4723} and ["when he suffered, he threatened not"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 2:23). But He did "call the hand" of the one who struck Him.

["Jesus answered him, If I have spoken evil, bear witness of the evil: but if well, why smitest thou me?"]{.class_4723} (John 18:23).

Not being a sinless man like his Saviour, Paul did not fare quite as well in a similar situation. When the high priest ordered him to be struck, Paul lost his temper and said, ["God shall smite thee, thou whited wall: for sittest thou to judge me after the law, and commandest me to be smitten contrary to the law?"]{.class_4723} (Acts 23:3).

Both Jesus and Paul appeal to the Law when "smitten" (see Deut. 25:1--2); they don't "turn the other cheek." The ones who always throw the command to turn the other cheek in the face of a Christian do so because they want [you]{.class_4725} to obey it in order for them to run over you in any way they want; they have no intention of keeping it themselves.

Once Sam Jones, the old-time Methodist evangelist, was preaching in a city and stirring up a lot of trouble with the liquor crowd, as he always did with his preaching. A couple of men got together and laid a trap for him in a post office. The first fellow hit Sam with an umbrella while the other stood back and watched the whole thing so he could accuse Sam if he did anything wrong. When the guy hit Sam Jones with the umbrella, Sam grabbed the umbrella out of the man's hands and knocked him down with it. The witness said, "Now, Sam, shouldn't you turn the other cheek?" (Religious hypocrites are always very pious---see John 18:28.) Sam replied, "Well, I'd turn the other cheek, but it doesn't say what to do after that. Besides, I've got to take care of Mrs. Jones' husband."

When it comes to this business of turning the other cheek, I have practiced it. I had a Christian slap me across the face one time. I turned the opposite cheek to that person and said, "You want to try the other one? I've got more grace in one cheek than you've got in both of yours." That Christian's response to that was: "Damn you! Damn you! Damn you!" I pointed my finger at that person and said, "In the name of Jesus, come out of him you unclean spirit." And down he went like a sack of potatoes. So I am not just dismissing the verse outright without having put it into practice. Have you?

Now what about these matters of turning the other cheek and someone taking your cloak from you (vs. 29)? Well, the Matthew account is much clearer in these matters than the account in Luke. Look at the same command in Matthew to turn the other cheek and note the context.

["Ye have heard that it hath been said, An eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth: But I say unto you, That ye resist not evil: but whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:38--39).

Look at that ["eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth" ]{.class_4723}thing in verse 38. That has nothing to do with matters of personal vengeance as Mohammed and all his followers teach. That's a quotation from Deuteronomy 19:21 under the Law. It's a command for a judge ruling from the bench in a civil dispute (Deut. 19:18). So the smiting and the taking of the cloak have to do with a [law suit]{.class_4725} under [Jewish Law]{.class_4725} (Matt. 5:25, 39--40). It has nothing to do with personal wrongs done to a believer under the New Testament.

Come on, folks, do you really think these churches like the Southern Baptist Convention and the United Methodist Conference and mission boards like BIMI and Baptist Mid-Missions follow Luke 6:29? Are you kidding?! When a church tries to go independent from one of those organizations, they take that church to court to get back the buildings and the property. They don't turn everything over to the pastor and congregation and give them some extra money besides. That has happened over and over and over again here in the States and on the mission field; it has happened to some of our own graduates. Those groups don't practice that verse.

What you have in verses 29--30 is [Jewish Law]{.class_4725} as applied to the [Millennial kingdom.]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_207} The "Sermon on the Mount" (in this case, the "Sermon on the Plain") is the constitution of the Kingdom as given by the King. The commands for the Christian in these matters are given by the Apostle Paul in Romans 12:17--20, and they constitute a much higher standard than being forced by a judge to turn the other cheek or turn over your cloak to the one who is suing you.

["Give to every man that asketh of thee"]{.class_4723} (vs. 30). Try that one down at the bank: "I want to borrow some money." "Do you have any collateral or securities?" "I'm a Baptist preacher." Why, they'd laugh you out of the bank.

Did you know that up until about World War I in this country, a man could borrow money just on his church membership alone? If a fellow was a member in good standing in a Methodist church, the bank would lend him the money based on his good character. That's because those Methodists were known for keeping their word: if they said they would pay back a loan, they would pay it back no matter what, even if they went bankrupt. That went for other churches like Baptists and Presbyterians in different parts of the country as well. Not any more, boy. There have been Fundamental Baptist preachers in good standing with works like [The Sword of the Lord]{.class_4725} and Tennessee Temple who have absconded with \$300,000 worth of bearer bonds. There have been churches that have defaulted on a four million dollar bond program. There have been TV evangelists who have gone to prison for embezzling millions of dollars from their ministry.

Again, what you have in verse 29 is a point of conduct for a Jew under the Law (see Deut. 15:7--10). It has nothing to do with a born-again child of God in the Body of Christ. Of course, personal application can be made from time to time which the Lord will bless.

John Wesley was out on horseback one time returning from a meeting. It was twilight when suddenly a highwayman appeared from the side of the road. He put a musket on Wesley and demanded his money. John gave the robber what cash he had, and the bandit rode off into the dark. Later on, that man was caught. When Wesley was called to testify against him, he told the magistrate, "No, he didn't rob me; that money was a gift from me to him." After the trial, that man came to Wesley wanting to know why he hadn't condemned []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_208}him, and that gave John a chance to witness to the fellow. That man ended up getting saved and becoming a Methodist preacher. But you see, that was an individual case applied by Wesley to himself personally because he had the faith to do so.
