If you've been robbed, you haven't sinned if you try to protect your property or call the police on the burglars.

Verse 31 is the famous "Golden Rule," found also in Matthew 7:12. It is loved so much by Liberals because it is salvation by [works.]{.class_4725} There is no blood atonement or salvation by grace through faith in it. In the Matthew account, it is the summation of the Law and the Prophets, which ended with John the Baptist according to Jesus (Luke 16:16). It is a statement of righteousness under the Old Testament and, as such, has nothing to do with New Testament salvation at all. As a practical standard for getting along with others, it may be fine; as a doctrinal gauge for getting you to Heaven, it needs to be left back in the Old Testament where it has been blotted out and taken out of the way at Calvary (Col. 2:14).

Verses 32--34 is sound reasoning, about which a demon-possessed, paranoid epileptic like Mohammed knew nothing at all. Any fool loves those who love him and treats people right who treat him right. There's nothing particularly righteous or good in that; even a dog or cat will do that. If all you do is love those who love you, what makes you different than a brute beast in that regard? No Mohammedan is any better than a drunk on skid row when it comes to "love."

Of course, you have to keep the passage in perspective.

1\) Obedience to the [second]{.class_4725} great commandment should never supersede obedience to the [first]{.class_4725} great commandment (Matt. 22:37--39).

2\) The two great commandments (loving God first and neighbor second) are the total summation of the Law and the Prophets (Matt. 22:40), and ["by the deeds of the law there shall no flesh be justified in his sight: for by the law is the knowledge of sin"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 3:20). ["The law was our schoolmaster to bring us unto Christ, that we might be justified BY FAITH"]{.class_4723} (Gal. 3:24).

3\) True Christian love does not violate the truth of the Scriptures (1 Cor. 13:6 cf. Dan. 10:21; John 17:17). That's particularly true []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_209}when applying the "Golden Rule." You don't violate a clear command of Scripture in order to do something for someone because you'd like them to do it for you. If you have a weakness for liquor, you don't violate Habakkuk 2:15 just because you'd like someone to give you a drink.

Back to Luke 6:[ "And if ye lend to them of whom ye hope to receive, what thank have ye?\...lend, hoping for nothing again; and your reward shall be great"]{.class_4723} (vss. 34--35). Give without expecting anything in return. Over in chapter 14, verses 12--14, Jesus told those Pharisees that every once in a while they needed to hold a feast and invite a bunch of poor folks who couldn't do anything for them or pay them back, and if they did that, [God]{.class_4727} would pay them back at the judgment. Hebrews 6:10 says, ["For God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labour of love."]{.class_4723}

["And ye shall be the children of the Highest."]{.class_4723} It is passages like this one that mess up the lost Liberal trying to work his way to Heaven by his own self-righteousness. There is nothing about faith in the passage, and there is nothing about the shed blood of Christ for sinners or imputed righteousness. It is [works]{.class_4725} from start to finish.

You have to watch out in getting doctrine for the New Testament believer from the Gospels. There is some there, but you can't apply everything to you doctrinally. Nine-tenths of all four books is before the Crucifixion or Resurrection. No one is born again, and there is no indwelling Holy Spirit in the believers. There is no spiritual Body of Christ with both Jews and Gentiles in it, and there is no Pauline revelation to the churches. In the Gospels, they are still operating under an Old Testament system of [faith and works.]{.class_4725}

Now when it comes to this business of being a child of God, what you have to understand is that in the Old Testament, there are no individual sons of God outside of the [created]{.class_4725} sons of God (the angels and Adam---see our comments on 3:38). Israel is a [corporate ]{.class_4725}son of God in the [national]{.class_4725} sense (Exod. 4:22--23). As part of the nation of Israel (and everyone in Luke 6 to whom Christ is speaking is a [Jew]{.class_4725}), those Jews could be recognized as ["children of the Highest"]{.class_4723} if they lived up to the covenant God made with the fathers (Rom. 2:25--29)---[works.]{.class_4725} That is a [practical]{.class_4725} sense, and Paul uses it that way in 2 Corinthians 6:17--18, but that is not your case [doctrinally]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_210} as a Christian.

["But as many as RECEIVED HIM, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that BELIEVE ON HIS NAME" ]{.class_4723}(John 1:12).

["For as many as are led by the Spirit of God, they are the sons of God. For ye have not received the spirit of bondage again to fear; but ye have received the Spirit of adoption, whereby we cry, Abba, Father. The Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit, that we are the children of God"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:14--16).

["For ye are all the children of God BY FAITH IN CHRIST JESUS"]{.class_4723} (Gal. 3:26).

[That]{.class_4725} is your case as a New Testament believer. You become a child of God by faith in Christ's atoning sacrifice and by the indwelling Holy Spirit of God, [not]{.class_4725} by treating your neighbor or your enemies or anyone else good or like you want to be treated.

["For he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil. Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful"]{.class_4723} (vss. 35--36). Absolutely true. Every Bible-rejecting, Christ-denying atheist, humanist, agnostic, Moslem, Hindu, Buddhist, Satanist, pedophile, queer, drunkard, murderer, thief, liar, blasphemer, pornographer, dope-head, and fornicator on the planet is beholden to the kindness and mercy of God whether he acknowledges it or not. How do you suppose a serial killer lives to be forty or fifty years old when some of his victims don't even make it into their thirties? That's the mercy and longsuffering of God.

When God is merciful to one man, it is another man's destruction. Haven't you ever read Psalm 136 where every verse ends with: ["for his mercy endureth for ever"]{.class_4723}? In verse 10 of that chapter it says, ["To him that SMOTE EGYPT IN THEIR FIRSTBORN: for his mercy endureth for ever."]{.class_4723} Then again in verse 15, same chapter: ["But OVERTHREW PHARAOH AND HIS HOST in the Red sea: for his mercy endureth for ever."]{.class_4723} That's God being merciful to those Jews by [killing the Egyptians.]{.class_4725} Well, He sure wasn't merciful to Pharaoh, was He? At least not then.

God's mercy to you may cost someone else his life. There's a []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_211}story about a troop ship in World War II traveling along in a convoy in the Pacific. A Japanese sub attacked that convoy, and the men on that troop ship saw a torpedo coming right toward their bow. Some Christian young men on that ship began to pray, and at the last second, that torpedo missed that ship by about a foot off the bow. Those Christians began to shout and praise God that the torpedo missed their ship. Do you know what happened to that torpedo? [It got the ship next to them.]{.class_4725} One man's meat is another man's poison.

["For he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil."]{.class_4723} In theology, this is known as "common grace." It is the common, everyday blessings God bestows on all men whether they are saved or not. Jesus said, ["for he maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:45). Paul and Barnabas told those pagan idolaters in Lystra, ["Nevertheless he left not himself without witness, in that he did good, and gave us rain from heaven, and fruitful seasons, filling our hearts with food and gladness"]{.class_4723} (Acts 14:17). The reason God is good and kind to the wicked is to lead them to repentance (Rom. 2:4). A man becomes an atheist when he dwells on the inequities and injustices in the world brought on by sin; if he focused on God's goodness and blessings, he'd repent of his devilment and believe God and live right.

["Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful."]{.class_4723} The companion passage in Matthew says, ["Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:48). So Luke defines what Jesus means by ["perfect"]{.class_4723} in that passage: Jesus is talking about perfection in [mercy,]{.class_4725} not [sinless perfection.]{.class_4725} Jesus said,[ "But go ye and learn what that meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 9:13).

37 Judge not, and ye shall not be judged: condemn not, and ye shall not be condemned: forgive, and ye shall be forgiven:

38 Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom. For with the same []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_212}measure that ye mete withal it shall be measured to you again.

["Judge not, and ye shall not be judged"]{.class_4723} (vs. 37). This verse (as its counterpart in Matt. 7:1) has now taken the place of John 3:16 as the most quoted verse in the Bible, and is most often [misquoted]{.class_4725} as: "Judge not lest ye be judged." It has been used by Liberals to shut the mouths first of Protestant and then of Fundamental ministers from [preaching against sin.]{.class_4725} It is now used to justify Socialism, riots, suicide bombings, sodomy, drunkenness, pedophilia, the drug traffic and drug abuse, adultery, filthy music, cross-dressing, cussing, bestiality, abortion, and [removing the Bible from every facet of life---including the pulpit.]{.class_4725} It is called "the new tolerance," meaning that you don't just put up with somebody's wickedness, you have to "accept" it and "embrace" it and [promote]{.class_4725} it. That's what all the queers want: they want you to [approve]{.class_4725} their sex perversion and [foot the bill]{.class_4725} for them to practice it. If you try to put any restrictions on it at all, you are a judgmental, intolerant BIGOT.

The same One who commanded you to ["Judge not" ]{.class_4723}also commanded you to ["judge righteous judgment"]{.class_4723} (John 7:24). The spiritual man ["judgeth all things"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 2:15). So Christ's words of verse 37 have nothing to do with not declaring the whole ["counsel of God"]{.class_4723} (Acts 20:27) in regards to sin, righteousness, and judgment to come.

The context dictates the meaning of the command. It is a [personal]{.class_4725} matter that has nothing to do with social or legal moral codes. Judgment certainly does need to be made against lawbreakers. The command is that in personal matters, a believer is not to judge without love, mercy, and forgiveness. The verse cannot be divorced from the rest of Scripture. Where the Bible clearly condemns a practice or teaching, the truth of the Scripture is to be proclaimed. Where judgment must be made, the goal is always to be the restoration of the offending brother (Gal. 6:1; 2 Cor. 2:1--7). Forgiveness, though, by no means is approval of sin; Jesus told the woman taken in adultery, ["Go, and SIN NO MORE"]{.class_4723} (John 8:11). He forgave her sin [without]{.class_4725} accepting or promoting it.

Again, you need to be careful in trying to apply verse 37 [doctrinally]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_213} to the New Testament Christian. Judy Garland said, "I hope when I meet God that He will be as merciful to me as I have been to others." Later on, she died of alcohol and drugs. She was counting on making it through the judgment by being kind and merciful and forgiving and tolerant of others. Where did she get that? She got it from verse 37.

[If that's what Judy Garland was relying on to get her into Heaven, then she split Hell wide open.]{.class_4725} So will [you]{.class_4725} if you depend on that sort of thing to get you in good standing with the Lord. Freedom from condemnation and forgiveness of sins does not come through tolerance or forgiveness of others; it comes through [Jesus Christ.]{.class_4725}

["For God sent not his Son into the world to condemn the world; but that the world THROUGH HIM might be saved. He that BELIEVETH ON HIM is not condemned: but he that believeth not is CONDEMNED ALREADY, because he hath not believed in the name of the only begotten Son of God"]{.class_4723} (John 3:17--18).

["There is therefore now no condemnation to them which are IN CHRIST JESUS, who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit" ]{.class_4723}(Rom. 8:1).

["Be it known unto you therefore, men and brethren, that THROUGH THIS MAN is preached unto you the forgiveness of sins: And BY HIM all that believe are justified from all things, from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses"]{.class_4723} (Acts 13:38--39).

You can be the most sweet, kind, loving, forgiving, tolerant human being on the face of this earth; and without Jesus Christ, [you'll go to Hell and fry like a greaseball.]{.class_4725} Luke 6:37 is not a definitive statement on the New Testament doctrine of salvation. The doctrinal setting is given in Matthew 18:23--35. It is a Jew under the Old Testament Law---as they are in the Tribulation (Rev. 12:17, 14:12)---giving account to his King when He comes in His physical kingdom (Matt. 18:23--35).

Now there is no disputing that a Christian is to be forgiving, but it has nothing to do with getting his own sins forgiven. Paul said, ["And be ye kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another\..."]{.class_4723} (Eph. 5:32). Why? So God will forgive you? No! [Because you've already been forgiven.]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_214}[ "\...EVEN AS GOD FOR CHRIST'S SAKE HATH FORGIVEN YOU."]{.class_4723} That's the difference between New Testament salvation in the Pauline Epistles and Old Testament salvation in the Gospels. [You have got to rightly divide that Book, people.]{.class_4725}

And then a great verse on giving: ["Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom. For with the same measure that ye mete withal it shall be measured to you again"]{.class_4723} (vs. 38). The promise is that if you give, God's going to cause men to give to you with the same measure you gave, and if you give generously, what comes back is going to be packed down and shaken to remove the empty spaces until your bushel basket is overflowing.

Now that's a valid promise for the New Testament believer because it is repeated by the Apostle Paul in 2 Corinthians 9:6.

"But this I say, He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully."

Paul then adds that it's not just the [amount]{.class_4725} of what you give but the [attitude]{.class_4725} with which you give it: ["not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 9:7).

That's ["a CHEERFUL giver,"]{.class_4723} not a [greedy]{.class_4725} or [covetous]{.class_4725} one. This is not giving for the sake of [getting:]{.class_4725} that's the modern, Charismatic, TV-evangelist approach. "Plant your seed faith," you know.

The promise is [not]{.class_4725} that you'll be rich, have a big bank account, drive a new car every year, dress in \$500 suits, own two or three ranch-style homes, etc. The promise is that God will give you whatever you [need]{.class_4725} to [accomplish]{.class_4725} whatever He's called you to do.

["But my God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by Christ Jesus"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 4:19).

The one who wrote that knew not only abundance, he knew want as well (Phil. 4:11--12). When the Apostle Paul died, he died without a home, without any money in the bank, without any property, and with only the clothes on his back. But he never lacked the provision needed to preach the Gospel wherever he went. The promise is this:

["And God is able to make all grace abound toward you; that ye having all SUFFICIENCY\...."]{.class_4723} That's ["sufficiency,"]{.class_4723} [not a surplus. ]{.class_4725}["\...in all things, may ABOUND TO EVERY GOOD WORK"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 9:8).

If you're wasting what God gives you on your own carnal lusts and greed, the promise is not to you. That doesn't mean you can't have nice things, but if you are giving just so God will give back to you in order to ["consume it upon your lusts"]{.class_4723} (James 4:3), you can write off what you gave as a loss.

39 And he spake a parable unto them, Can the blind lead the blind? shall they not both fall into the ditch?

40 The disciple is not above his master: but every one that is perfect shall be as his master.

41 And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother's eye, but perceivest not the beam that is in thine own eye?

42 Either how canst thou say to thy brother, Brother, let me pull out the mote that is in thine eye, when thou thyself beholdest not the beam that is in thine own eye? Thou hypocrite, cast out first the beam out of thine own eye, and then shalt thou see clearly to pull out the mote that is in thy brother's eye.

Verse 39 is the second ["parable"]{.class_4723} that shows up in the book of Luke (see our comments on 5:36). It is called a ["parable"]{.class_4723} here because it is drawing a parallel between [physical]{.class_4725} blindness and [spiritual]{.class_4725} blindness. It is spoken of the Pharisees in Matthew 15:12--14 (see our comments on 5:17 in this Commentary).

Verse 40: ["The disciple is not above his master: but every one that is perfect shall be as his master."]{.class_4723} The obvious application is that the Jews to whom He is speaking are the disciples and He is the Master. Over in Malachi 1:6, the Lord tells those Jews: ["A son honoureth his father, and a servant his master: if I then be a father, where is mine honour? AND IF I BE A MASTER, where is my fear?"]{.class_4723}

Now there is nothing wrong with applying that to you [spiritually]{.class_4725} []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_216}if you are a Christian. Are you a son of God (John 1:12)? Then you should honor your heavenly Father. Are you God's servant? Paul said he was (Rom. 1:1); Peter said he was (2 Pet. 1:1). Then you should fear your Master (Heb. 12:28).

The application Jesus puts on verse 40 is threefold. You are to be as your Master in helping the brethren to stay clean by the ministering of the word.

["For I have given you an example, that ye should do as I have done to you. Verily, verily, I say unto you, The servant is not greater than his lord; neither he that is sent greater than he that sent him"]{.class_4723} (John 13:15--16 and comments in that Commentary).

Furthermore, you are to be as your Master in suffering persecution.

["Remember the word that I said unto you, The servant is not greater than his lord. If they have persecuted me, they will also persecute you; if they have kept my saying, they will keep yours also"]{.class_4723} (John 14:20).

Finally, you will not be above your Master in false accusations.

["The disciple is not above his master, nor the servant above his lord. It is enough for the disciple that he be as his master, and the servant as his lord. If they have called the master of the house Beelzebub, how much more shall they call them of his household?"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 10:24--25).

Verse 41: ["And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother's eye, but perceivest not the beam that is in thine own eye?"]{.class_4723} A ["mote"]{.class_4723} is a word like [mite]{.class_4725} (as in "the widow's mite"---21:2). It is a small particle of something; in this case, a small piece of wood. It is contrasted with a ["beam"]{.class_4723} here in the verse.

What Christ is saying here is: "Why are you worried about a splinter in your brother's eye when there's a two-by-four in yours?" The idea is take care of your own sin and problems first before you start criticizing your brother (vs. 42). Don't be guilty of "nitpicking" and "gnat straining" (Matt. 23:24); I mean, we all have our problems.

The Pharisees were guilty of that. They would complain about Jesus healing on the Sabbath day while they were plotting to kill Him (see our remarks under vss. 7--11). They would complain about His []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_217}disciples not washing their hands before they ate and then allow a fellow to curse his parents if he made a donation (\$\$\$) to the Temple (Matt. 15:1--6). That's straining at a gnat and swallowing the camel nose, hump, hooves, tail, and all in one gulp. That's walking around with a railroad tie in your own eye while complaining about the wood chip in the other guy's eye.

I was up in Middletown, Ohio one time in a meeting with Bro. Howard Sears. I was eating breakfast one morning, and a Holiness girl (Church of God) about 22 or 23 years old was waiting on the table. I asked that young lady, "Are you saved?"

She said, "Well, no one can know that till they die."

I told her, "Well, the Bible says you can know."

"Well, you Baptists are different than us," she said. "We don't do what you people do. We don't allow laughing in church, and our preacher doesn't yell or talk loud when he preaches."

Not to be sidetracked or put off, I asked her again, "Do you know you're saved?"

"Nobody can know that till they die," she said again, "but I do the best I can. In our church, the ladies have to wear three-quarter-length sleeves and wear their hair down to their back."

"But you don't know where you're going when you die?" I said.

She replied, "No, but we don't believe in wearing jewelry."

I said, "Lady, you sure believe a lot for somebody who doesn't believe enough to know where she's going when she dies."

You don't believe in laughing in church, do you? What do you believe in doing---sleeping?

I'll give you another example of this nitpicking? I mean, I've experienced this all my life. I was in one home where we were eating fried chicken, and the little girl of the family kept staring at me. Finally, the mother told her, "Quit staring at Bro. Ruckman like that; it's not polite." That girl kept on staring, and that mother asked, "Honey, why do you keep on staring at Bro. Ruckman?"

That little girl replied, "Momma, when is he going to eat the bones?"

It had gotten around that I eat chicken bones. Well, sure I eat chicken bones; I've eaten them since I was ten years old. I found out when I was seventy that they're good for arthritis.

I was at a farmhouse one time having ice tea, and there was nothing to break the ice into small enough pieces to go in a glass, so that farmer cracked the ice on the table. Well, after that, I always cracked my ice on the table, and I never heard the end of that.

But the perfect example of the passage before us is when I was eating breakfast one time with a pastor down in Key West. The whole family was there: the wife and his three little children. Well, we had a good meeting with souls saved, but he never invited me back. That didn't bother me, but it did make me curious, because most pastors invited me back and wanted a permanent, regular engagement every year.

Well, I let it go and didn't think anything more of it. About five years later, I was talking with another fellow over the phone. He asked, "Do you know Pastor So-and-so down in Key West?"

I said, "Yeah, I held a meeting for him about five years ago."

"Did he ever invite you back?" he asked.

"No," I replied.

"Do you know why?" he said.

"No," I said, "but I've always wondered about it."

Do you know what that fellow told me? That pastor's wife didn't want him to have me back in because I used my fingers to eat my eggs at breakfast. Down South, it's a common thing to take a piece of toast and sop up the yolk of a fried egg, and I do that. That woman was offended at me doing that.

What I found out later was that woman left her husband, and when she did, she informed him that two of those kids weren't his. She had been shacking up with the pastor's cousin the whole time. And she was worried about "Ruckman" dipping his toast in his egg yolks.

You see that stuff? That's your old Pharisee. He's worried about some piddly thing someone else is doing while he's living like hell.

43 For a good tree bringeth not forth corrupt fruit; neither doth a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit.

44 For every tree is known by his own fruit. For of thorns men do not gather figs, nor of a bramble bush gather they grapes.

45 A good man out of the good treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is good; and an evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is evil: for of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaketh.

Verses 43--44 hearken back to the proper interpretation of verse 37 (see our remarks under that passage). Obviously, verse 37 can't be talking about all forms of judging or condemning because how are you to tell whether a tree and its fruit are good or bad if you don't make some kind of judgment on them?

The answer is, [you can't.]{.class_4725} You have to pass some judgment on a thing, and to ["judge righteous judgment"]{.class_4723} (John 7:24), you have to have some kind of accurate criteria for making the judgment. If you are going to be ["spiritual"]{.class_4723} and judge all things properly (1 Cor. 2:15), you have to judge things by the one infallible guide God gave you: Scripture with Scripture (1 Cor. 2:13 cf. 2 Pet. 1:19).

Now that ["corrupt tree"]{.class_4723} that brings forth ["corrupt fruit"]{.class_4723} in verse 43 is [burned in Hell.]{.class_4725} Go back to 3:9 and look at what John the Baptist said there.

["And now also the axe is laid unto the root of the trees: every tree therefore which BRINGETH NOT FORTH GOOD FRUIT]{.class_4723} \[like you just read in 6:43\][ is hewn down, AND CAST INTO THE FIRE."]{.class_4723}

The ["fire"]{.class_4723} in that verse is HELL (see 3:17 and our comments on that verse). There's a passage over in Hebrews 6:1--9 that all the Holiness folks use to prove you can lose your salvation [right now]{.class_4725} and end up in Hell. That passage ends with: ["But that which beareth thorns and briers is rejected, and is nigh unto cursing; whose end is to be BURNED"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 6:8).

Why, that's just what you read in Luke 6:43--44 and 3:9, 17. There's no doubt the reference is to Hell, but what do you do with verses 4, 5, and 6 of Hebrews 6 where someone was ["made partakers of the Holy Ghost"]{.class_4723} and ["tasted the good word of God,"]{.class_4723} but ["fall away"]{.class_4723} and ["it is impossible\...to renew them again unto repentance"]{.class_4723}?

Well, we had a fellow named Tabb come through here one time. He attended our school for a year, and then figured he knew more []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_220}than his teacher, so he went over to Fort Walton Beach (about an hour away) and set up a school there to compete with ours here in Pensacola. Tabb teaches that the "burning" in Hebrews 6:8 is the fire at the Judgment Seat of Christ that burns up a Christian's works without burning up the Christian himself (see 1 Cor. 3:13--15). He got that by believing salvation is the same in every dispensation (which is theological BALONEY), so if a Christian can't lose his salvation (and he can't---Rom. 8:29, 38--39; 1 Cor. 1:8; Eph. 4:30; Phil. 1:6, etc.), then there is no way that God is going to burn up the people of Hebrews 6:4--6.

Tabb didn't like the fact that I teach the book of Hebrews is aimed [doctrinally]{.class_4725} at [Hebrews]{.class_4725} in the [Tribulation]{.class_4725} just before the return of Christ. If you take the passage as it stands, with the [proper]{.class_4725} cross-references, the Holiness are right---someone [can]{.class_4725} lose his salvation and go to Hell. [It's just not you or anyone else in the Body of Christ.]{.class_4725}

If you give that to the typical Baptist (like Tabb), he'll just about blow a gasket. But why do you think that nearly every branch of Christianity outside of the Presbyterians and the Baptists teach that you can lose your salvation? It's because there are about a hundred verses in that Bible that say that [someone]{.class_4725} can, and I can show you where they are. Well, if it's not anyone in the Body of Christ, then who is it? It's somebody under a faith-and-works system like those Jews will be in the Tribulation (Rev. 12:17, 14:12) when the Body of Christ is taken away from this earth.

Those ["thorns and briers"]{.class_4723} in Hebrews 6:8, and the corrupt trees and bramble bushes in Luke 6:43--44 and 3:9, 17 aren't anyone's "works"; they're [people.]{.class_4725} Don't believe me? Then look up the following references: 2 Samuel 23:6--7; Jeremiah 5:14; Isaiah 33:12; Ezekiel 15:6--7, 20:47--48; Matthew 13:30, 40--42. Look them up and READ THEM. In every one of those passages, trees and thorns and tares are likened to [people]{.class_4725} who are [burned in the fire]{.class_4725} at the Second Advent of Christ, and that fire is [Hell]{.class_4725} (note especially the unquenchable fire in Ezek. 20 and the ["wailing and gnashing of teeth"]{.class_4723} in Matt. 13). Tabb didn't know about what he was talking.

Folks, you don't make the Bible fit your theology; you make your [theology fit the Bible.]{.class_4725} The only way you are going to do that is if you rightly divide that Book as you were commanded to do (2 Tim. 2:15---don't look for it in the [NASV, NIV, ESV,]{.class_4725} or [RSV;]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_221} the only place you'll find it is in a [King James]{.class_4725} 1611 [Authorized Version]{.class_4725}---["King James ]{.class_4725}ONLYISM").

Verse 45: ["A good man out of the good treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is good; and an evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is evil."]{.class_4723} In other words, what's on the inside will come out sooner or later. The modern expression of the "computer age" is GIGO: "Garbage In, Garbage Out."

The ["good man"]{.class_4723} and the ["evil man"]{.class_4723} are used in the Old Testament sense (see our remarks on 1:6 and 5:30) and not the doctrinal sense of the Pauline Epistles, since ["there is none that doeth good, no, not one"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 3:12). Nevertheless, there is practical application for the born-again Christian, for what you put in is what you get out. If you put in TV, radio, magazines, rock music, filthy words and pictures, that's what's going to come out; if you put in the word of God, prayer, hymns, faith, etc. (see Phil. 4:8), that's what's going to come out.

The ["good treasure"]{.class_4723} in the Scriptures is ["the fear of the LORD"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 33:6), [the word of God itself]{.class_4725} (Matt. 13:52), and the [affections set]{.class_4725} ["on things above"]{.class_4723} (Col. 3:2 cf. Matt. 6:21). The ["evil treasure"]{.class_4723} is [covetousness]{.class_4725} (Luke 12:21) and [hardness and impenitance]{.class_4725} (Rom. 2:5).

["For of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaketh."]{.class_4723} The heart and the mouth are connected: you'll talk about what you love.

["Death and life are in the power of the tongue]{.class_4723} \[there's the mouth\][: and they that love it]{.class_4723} \[and there's the heart\][ shall eat the fruit thereof"]{.class_4723} (Prov. 18:21).

["Be not rash with thy MOUTH, and let not thine HEART be hasty to utter any thing before God"]{.class_4723} (Eccl. 5:2).

So the heart and the mouth are connected. They are even connected in salvation.

["That if thou shalt confess with thy MOUTH the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine HEART that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved. For with the HEART man believeth unto righteousness; and with the MOUTH confession is made unto salvation"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_222} (Rom. 10:9--10).

The question, then, is how can a Christian, whose body is ["the temple of the Holy Ghost"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 6:19) and who has Christ in him as ["the hope of glory"]{.class_4723} (Col. 1:27), fail to speak up for Jesus Christ? If the Holy Spirit is in a believer as ["a well of water springing up into everlasting life"]{.class_4723} (John 4:14), you would think a Christian would be talking about his Saviour all the time since ["of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaketh."]{.class_4723} But the fact of the matter is that most believers keep their mouths shut about the Lord and the Book.

Down South when I bring this up to some "good old boy," he will say, "Well, preacher, I don't believe in talking it; I believe in living it." Mark it down, when you hear someone talking like that, it's because he's afraid if he starts talking about it, those around him are going to start paying more attention to the way he lives it, and he knows the way he's "living it" [can't stand the scrutiny.]{.class_4725} If you want to ["let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:16), I'll tell you how to do it and get people's attention really quickly. Get you a handful of tracts and go knock on every door in your neighborhood and say, "Hello, I'm your Christian neighbor that lives down the street. I want to tell you how you can make it to Heaven some day," and then give them what the Scriptures say about sin, Hell, judgment, and salvation through Jesus Christ alone. I'll guarantee you they'll watch you like a hawk.

I'll tell you something else about these folks who don't believe in talking about it and just living it: they don't have any problem at all with talking about something they like or like to do. They'll talk to you about being in the deer stand and getting a shot at a ten-point buck, or being out there in the lily pads and getting a strike off pork rind or a "yellow-tailed sally," or about how their favorite team made a touchdown or goal or basket. They don't believe in just "living it" when it comes to hunting, fishing, and sports; they believe in talking about it, and they'll talk your ear off about it. The problem is not that they don't believe in talking about Jesus Christ, God, and the Bible; the problem is that they don't have enough love for their Saviour or the Book to talk about it. ["Of the abundance of the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_223}heart his mouth speaketh."]{.class_4723}

The truth is, the reason most Christians don't witness is because they are [ashamed of Jesus Christ]{.class_4725} and they are [ashamed of the Book.]{.class_4725} They are afraid that someone will find out what they really believe about a sinner going to Hell or no man's righteousness being able to save him or Jesus Christ being the only way to Heaven. They want to be "politically correct" and "accepted" by the world---the same world that [crucified their Saviour.]{.class_4725} Why would any Christian want that? It's a [love]{.class_4725} problem (1 John 2:15; 2 Tim. 4:10).

You say, "Bro. Ruckman, it's not that I'm ashamed of Jesus Christ; it's just that I'm scared." That's no excuse. I don't care how shy you are, you can do [something]{.class_4725} to speak up for Jesus Christ.

I know a man up in Tennessee named Bob Price. Bob Price is so shy that he will stay in his apartment all the time to avoid being around people and talking to them; the only time he will go out is to buy groceries, and even then he avoids contact as much as possible. That fellow asked me one time how he could witness when he was so shy. "Why don't you mail tracts out to folks," I suggested; so he did.

That fellow started out by mailing tracts, and then he started getting the addresses of celebrities and was sending them my teaching tapes on Revelation. He sent a set to Billy Graham and Elvis Presley, among others. In fact, he got a letter back from Elvis Presley, before he doped himself to death, thanking Price for the tapes and saying he was listening to them. Well, that shy fellow did something I never did. I preached to Billy Graham one time when he was down in Clearwater, Florida, but I never preached to Elvis Presley. Price did.

When we came out with our booklet tract on [Millions Disappear,]{.class_4725} Price would get those things by the case load from the Bookstore and mail them out with a decision card to return to him if anyone got saved reading it. This was a guy who would get physically sick---I mean, literally---if he had to look someone in the face and talk to him about [anything.]{.class_4725} Do you know how many decision cards Price got back? It was [500:]{.class_4725} that's 500 people who got saved from a fellow who was as shy and as timid as a person can get. If he can do it, you sure have no excuse. ["For of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaketh."]{.class_4723}

46 And why call ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say?

47 Whosoever cometh to me, and heareth my sayings, and doeth them, I will shew you to whom he is like:

48 He is like a man which built an house, and digged deep, and laid the foundation on a rock: and when the flood arose, the stream beat vehemently upon that house, and could not shake it: for it was founded upon a rock.

49 But he that heareth, and doeth not, is like a man that without a foundation built an house upon the earth; against which the stream did beat vehemently, and immediately it fell; and the ruin of that house was great.

Verse 46 is one of the greatest questions ever asked in the Scriptures. It should be memorized by every Christian. ["And why call ye me Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say?"]{.class_4723}

Jesus said, ["If ye love me, keep my commandments"]{.class_4723} (John 14:15).

James wrote, ["But be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only"]{.class_4723} (James 1:22).

Now what follows in verses 48--49 is an illustration of the principle in verse 46. It is [works]{.class_4725} from start to finish and has [nothing]{.class_4725} to do with New Testament salvation. Of course, [practical]{.class_4725} application can certainly be made for a Christian to "build his life on the Lord Jesus Christ," like the song the Sunday school children sing, and that is backed up by the Apostle Paul in 1 Corinthians 3:11--12 and Ephesians 2:20--22. But notice in both of those passages that no one "digs deep" (vs. 48) to lay his foundation. The foundation has already been laid by the Holy Spirit through the preaching of the Gospel (1 Cor. 3:5--10). If you applied the "Parable of the Wise Man and the Foolish Man" to the New Testament Christian, you would have to apply it to [service,]{.class_4725} not [salvation.]{.class_4725} The ["ruin"]{.class_4723} (vs. 49) would be the loss of rewards at the Judgment Seat of Christ (1 Cor. 3:15) or the destruction of the body here on earth (1 Cor. 3:16--17). It certainly would have nothing to do with salvation, because in either case above (loss of rewards or destruction of the flesh), the believer [remains saved]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1280.xhtml_page_225} (read 1 Cor. 3:15, 5:5).

[Doctrinally speaking,]{.class_4725} the ["house"]{.class_4723} can only be one thing---[the house of Israel.]{.class_4725} There is a clear example of this in Jeremiah 35. In that chapter, the Lord contrasts two "houses." One is ["the house of the Rechabites"]{.class_4723} (Jer. 35:18) who [obeyed]{.class_4725} the voice of their father in not drinking wine (Jer. 35:14), and because of that they were given a promise that their house would ["stand"]{.class_4723} before God ["for ever"]{.class_4723} (Jer. 35:19). The other was the house of Judah which [disobeyed]{.class_4725} God and was destroyed by Nebuchadnezzar (Jer. 35:15--17). If you want to see how ["great"]{.class_4723} the ["ruin"]{.class_4723} of that house was (Luke 6:49), read the book of Lamentations.

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml}

# Chapter 7 {.heading_78-0}

7:1 Now when he had ended all his sayings in the audience of the people, he entered into Capernaum.

2 And a certain centurion's servant, who was dear unto him, was sick, and ready to die.

3 And when he heard of Jesus, he sent unto him the elders of the Jews, beseeching him that he would come and heal his servant.

4 And when they came to Jesus, they besought him instantly, saying, That he was worthy for whom he should do this:

5 For he loveth our nation, and he hath built us a synagogue.

6 Then Jesus went with them. And when he was now not far from the house, the centurion sent friends to him, saying unto him, Lord, trouble not thyself: for I am not worthy that thou shouldest enter under my roof:

7 Wherefore neither thought I myself worthy to come unto thee: but say in a word, and my servant shall be healed.

8 For I also am a man set under authority, having under me soldiers, and I say unto one, Go, and he goeth; and to another, Come, and he cometh; and to my servant, Do this, and he doeth it.

9 When Jesus heard these things, he marvelled at him, and turned him about, and said unto the people that followed him, I say unto you, I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel.

10 And they that were sent, returning to the house, found the servant whole that had been sick.

This is the account of the healing of the centurion's servant, which you will also find in Matthew 8:5--13. A centurion was a captain in the Roman legions. Your words are [century]{.class_4725}---100 years, and [centipede]{.class_4725}---a 100-legged "creepy crawly." So a [centurion]{.class_4725} is a captain over [100 men.]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_227} Most centurions in the New Testament are good. The centurion at Christ's crucifixion bore witness to the Lord's righteousness and His Deity (Luke 23:47; Matt. 27:54). Cornelius in Acts 10 was a centurion; he was the first Gentile to be saved as a Gentile. And a centurion saved Paul from scourging in Acts 22:24--26.

The centurion here in Luke 7 has a servant who is so sick that he is about to die, and the centurion hears that Jesus is in town and wants the Lord to heal his servant. Now Luke doesn't tell you what was wrong with the servant, but Matthew does.

["And saying, Lord, my servant lieth at home sick of the PALSY, grievously tormented"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 8:6).

Palsy is a paralysis of a part of the body where a person loses control of his muscular motor functions. It usually involves loss of sensation and uncontrollable shaking as well. Most people are familiar with cerebral palsy and Bell's palsy. Neither one of those is necessarily life-threatening, but there is a form of palsy which is like Lou Gehrig's Disease in which you lose control of your muscles until finally you can't swallow or breathe, and you die.

["And when he heard of Jesus, he sent unto him the elders of the Jews, beseeching him that he would come and heal his servant"]{.class_4723} (vs. 3). Luke says the centurion asked the Jewish elders of the city to approach Jesus with his request; Matthew says the centurion himself came out to Christ (Matt. 8:5). Which is right? Well, you have to understand how the centurion is approaching Christ. The centurion is coming to Christ [through those Jews]{.class_4725} just like he does [through his friends]{.class_4725} down in verse 6 (cf. Matt. 8:8). So you follow a basic rule of Biblical interpretation: always interpret an unclear passage (the account in Matthew) by a clear passage (the account in Luke).

["And when they came to Jesus, they besought him instantly, saying, That he was worthy for whom he should do this: For he loveth our nation"]{.class_4723} (vss. 4--5). That's the passage in the story that's going to destroy all 191 Gentile nations in the UN; those are the verses that are going to be the final nails in the coffin of the United States.

That Bible says, ["Pray for the peace of Jerusalem: THEY SHALL PROSPER THAT LOVE THEE" ]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_228}(Psa. 122:6). Any blessings that America has had in 236 years of its history (1776--2012) are due to two things: 1) honoring, believing, and obeying the Book, and 2) taking care of that Jew. The United States dumped the Book in 1901, and they are in the process of dumping Israel. This country has put two holes in the bottom of the "Ship of State" that no one is going to be able to plug up; you're headed to the bottom, folks.

The promise to those Jews is: ["I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 12:3). The standard rebuttal to that by the anti-Semites is that that promise was just to one man---Abraham. Not in Genesis 27:29 it's not. That promise is passed from Abraham to [Isaac]{.class_4725} (not [Ishmael]{.class_4725}), from Isaac to [Jacob]{.class_4725} (not [Esau]{.class_4725}), and from Jacob to the [twelve tribes of Israel]{.class_4725} (Num. 24:9) (not the [Moslems]{.class_4725} or the ["Palestinians"]{.class_4725}).

Listen, people, you aren't going to tell me anything about that Jew that I don't already know. Texe Marrs is just now getting around to saying that the Antichrist is going to be a Jew. I've taught that for about forty years (Dan. 11:37; John 5:43); Clarence Larkin and Sir Robert Anderson had Marrs beat by 90--100 years.

The Antichrist will be a Jew (or part Jewish); sure he will. He will rule the world from Jerusalem (Ezek. 21:25; Zech. 11:16--17), and when he does, it will be ["Sodom and Egypt"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 11:8). Right now, a lot of devilment in this world is due to the Jews: Karl Marx was a Jew (Communism), Albert Einstein was a Jew (relative truth), Sigmund Freud was a Jew (sex-obsessed psychoanalysis). Tell me all about it.

Texe Marrs is against that Jew; the syndicated columnist Charlie Reese is against that Jew; some of my friends in the ministry have turned against the Jews. I have been exposed to all the anti-Semitic literature these guys have and more, and I came out 100% [for]{.class_4725} the Jews while they came out 100% [against]{.class_4725} the Jews. I settled that way back in 1952. How do you account for that difference in opinion?

Well, here is the Apostle Paul, the greatest Christian who ever lived. From the time he got saved to just a few years before his death when he was shipped off to Rome, he was lied about by the Jews, hounded from town to town by the Jews, beaten by the Jews, and had his life threatened by the Jews (2 Cor. 11:21--33). Do you know []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_229}what he said about his own people?

["For I could wish that myself were accursed from Christ for my brethren, my kinsmen according to the flesh ]{.class_4723}\[have you heard any of these anti-Semites wish they could to go Hell for those Jews?\][: Who are Israelites; to whom pertaineth ]{.class_4723}\[present tense\][ the adoption, and the glory, and the covenants, and the giving of the law, and the service of God, and the promises; Whose are the fathers, and of whom as concerning the flesh Christ came"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 9:3--5).

Everything God promised the Jews back in the Old Testament is still valid and binding. People like Texe Marrs think that because God has temporarily set aside the nation because of their rejection of Christ that He has dumped them altogether (see the [Amplified Version]{.class_4725} in 1 Thess. 2:16). Paul said if you think that way that you are stupid and conceited (Rom. 11:25). When Jesus Christ comes back, He saves those people as a nation (Rom. 11:26--27) and fulfills all the promises of the Old Testament to them.

Until then, ["they are beloved for the fathers' sakes,"]{.class_4723} even though they are enemies of the Gospel (Rom. 11:28). I love those Jews because [that is what God told me to do.]{.class_4725} I don't have to love everything they do; I don't have to love all their politicians or their policies as a government. I don't even have to like some of them as individuals, but I do have to love them and be a blessing to them as a people and nation if I don't want a curse on me as an individual or the country corporately (USA). Here is what God said to the United States, the Arab League, NATO, the EU, and the UN; every verse I am going to give you is just as applicable and fitting as John 3:16.

["Israel was holiness unto the LORD, and the firstfruits of his increase: all that devour him shall offend; evil shall come upon them, saith the LORD"]{.class_4723} (Jer. 2:3).

["Rejoice, O ye nations, with his people: for he will avenge the blood of his servants, and will render vengeance to his adversaries, and will be merciful unto his land, and to his people"]{.class_4723} (Deut. 32:43).

["Behold, they shall surely gather together, but not by me: whosoever shall gather together against thee shall fall for thy sake\....No weapon that is formed against thee shall []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_230}prosper; and every tongue that shall rise against thee in judgment thou shalt condemn. This is the heritage of the servants of the LORD, and their righteousness is of me, saith the LORD"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 54:15, 17).

["Behold, all they that were incensed against thee shall be ashamed and confounded: they shall be as nothing; and they that strive against thee shall perish. Thou shalt seek them, and shalt not find them, even them that contended with thee: they that war against thee shall be as nothing, and as a thing of nought"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 41:11--12).

So if I were you, I would love that nation and help it out like the centurion did.

Back to Luke 7: ["And when he was now not far from the house, the centurion sent friends to him, saying unto him, Lord, trouble not thyself: for I am not worthy that thou shouldest enter under my roof"]{.class_4723} (vs. 6). Now to understand what the centurion is saying in verses 6--8, you have to understand the military way of thinking. As a soldier and an officer, the centurion understands the military chain of command: he knows how to take orders and how to give them (vs. 8). He sees Jesus in a light that Christ's own kinsmen don't: as the Supreme Commander, the Commander-in-Chief. He knows that ["befel ist befel"]{.class_4725} ("an order is an order"). He realizes that all Christ has to do is give the command, and it will be followed. There was no need for the Lord to waste His time with a lowly "Buck Private."

["I am not worthy\...neither thought I myself worthy"]{.class_4723} (vss. 7--8). In verses 3--5 and 6--7, you see a wonderful contrast, in type, between Gentile salvation [now]{.class_4725} and Gentile salvation in the [Tribulation.]{.class_4725} At the return of Jesus Christ, those Gentile nations are hauled before Jesus Christ at the Judgment of the Nations, and the individuals in those nations are judged on [how they treated the Jews]{.class_4725} in the Tribulation (Matt. 25:35--46). Check it out: go to Matthew 25 and read verses 31--46 and see if you see any salvation "by grace through faith" in it. Why, it's [works]{.class_4725} from start to finish. In the Millennium, the Gentiles come to Christ [through the Jews]{.class_4725} (see Zech. 8:21--23 and our comments on the passage in [The Bible Believer's Commentary on the Minor Prophets,]{.class_4725} Vol. II).

That's not the case [now.]{.class_4725} No one is ["worthy"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_231} of salvation (cf. Rev. 5:2--4). ["All have sinned"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 3:23); ["There is none righteous"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 3:10). Right now, it is: ["Not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy he saved us"]{.class_4723} (Tit. 3:5). Right now, you approach God through ["one mediator"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 2:5), and you approach that Mediator [through faith]{.class_4725} (Eph. 2:8--9; Rom. 5:1).

["I say unto you, I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel"]{.class_4723} (vs. 9). We have already commented on faith, healing, and the "point of contact" in 4:39--40 and 6:19, which see. Here we want to note what follows the Lord's comments in verse 9. To do that, you have to go over to Matthew 8 and see what follows the statement of Luke 7:9 (cf. Matt. 8:10). The "P.S." to Christ's commendation of the centurion's faith is found in Matthew 8:11--12.

["And I say unto you, that many shall come from the east ]{.class_4723}\[China, Japan, India\][ and west ]{.class_4723}\[Spain, Italy, Germany, England, the Americas\][, and shall sit down with Abraham]{.class_4723} \[a Jew\][, and Isaac]{.class_4723} \[a Jew\][, and Jacob]{.class_4723} \[a Jew\][, in the kingdom of heaven ]{.class_4723}\[a literal, visible, physical kingdom on earth in the Millennium\][. But the children of the kingdom ]{.class_4723}\[Jews\][ shall be cast out into outer darkness: there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth."]{.class_4723}

Those are individual Jews who miss the Millennial kingdom and end up [in Hell]{.class_4725} for rejecting their Messiah. A Christ-rejecting Jew goes to Hell just like a Christ-rejecting Gentile (1 Thess. 2:14--16). So when Paul writes ["And so ALL ISRAEL shall be saved"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 11:26), he's not referring to [every single Jew who ever lived]{.class_4725} as Fred Potter (Bristol, TN) and some of the brethren teach in order to get out of witnessing to the Jews. He's talking about the Jews who are alive at Christ's return (see our comments on Zech. 12:7--10 in that Commentary). Brethren, if the Devil can't shove you one way (Texe Marrs), he'll shove you another (Fred Potter). You had better [pray]{.class_4725} for those Jews, try to [win them to Christ,]{.class_4725} and [support their right to the land.]{.class_4725} You had better help them out, both individually and nationally, whenever you can.

Verse 10 is self-explanatory.

11 And it came to pass the day after, that he went into a city called Nain; and many of his disciples went with him, and much people.

12 Now when he came nigh to the gate of the city, behold, there was a dead man carried out, the only son of his mother, and she was a widow: and much people of the city was with her.

13 And when the Lord saw her, he had compassion on her, and said unto her, Weep not.

14 And he came and touched the bier: and they that bare him stood still. And he said, Young man, I say unto thee, Arise.

15 And he that was dead sat up, and began to speak. And he delivered him to his mother.

16 And there came a fear on all: and they glorified God, saying, That a great prophet is risen up among us; and, That God hath visited his people.

17 And this rumour of him went forth throughout all Judaea, and throughout all the region round about.

The Lord goes from Capernaum on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee to the village of Nain, located south and east of Nazareth and situated on the northern slope leading down into the Valley of Megiddo. Nain is about 25 miles from Capernaum, which is quite a trip by foot---about seven hours---for no apparent reason. But the Lord is headed there on purpose. In John 4:4, you're told, ["And he must needs go through Samaria."]{.class_4723} Why? There was a woman at a well who needed to be saved. In Acts 8:26, the Holy Spirit pulls Philip out of a successful city-wide evangelistic campaign and sends him into a desert. Why? Because there was an Ethiopian eunuch there who needed to hear about Jesus Christ. Jesus walks 25 miles to go to Nain. Why? He's going there to break up a funeral.

Now, there are three instances recorded in the Gospels of Christ raising someone from the dead: there is the son of the widow of Nain here in chapter 7, there is Jairus' daughter in the next chapter, and there is Lazarus in John 11. Jairus' daughter had only been dead for less than an hour, the young man in Nain had been dead much longer []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_233}and was being carried out of the town for burial, and Lazarus had been dead and buried for four days when Jesus raised him. There are a couple of lessons we can learn from these three resurrections.

The first is that when the Lord comes back, no matter how long you've been dead, you're [going up.]{.class_4725} It doesn't matter whether you just died or whether the undertaker has embalmed you or whether your body has rotted to dust, ["this corruption must put on incorruption"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 15:53). If a Christian dies at sea and the fish have eaten him, or even if he has been cremated and his ashes scattered, he doesn't have to worry; the Lord knows where every atom is when it comes time to reassemble them into a glorified body. If the Lord can make a man out of dust (Gen. 2:7), He won't have any trouble getting D. L. Moody, M. R. DeHaan, John Bunyan, Martin Luther, John Wycliffe, Constantia Bellione ([History of the New Testament Church,]{.class_4725} Vol. I, 2000, pg. 391), Columba, Patrick, Ignatius, Polycarp, Paul, John, or YOU out of the grave and back together in a body just like His Son's (Phil. 3:21; 1 John 3:2).

A little boy asked his mother one time, "Momma, is it true that God made Adam out of dust like we learned in Sunday school?" "Yes," replied his mother. "And is it true that when we die we go back to dust like God told Adam?" he asked. "Yes," she said, "Why do you ask?" "Well," said the little boy, "you'd better come to my room quick. There's someone under my bed either [coming or going."]{.class_4725}

The second thing we learn from these three resurrections is related to salvation: it's a [spiritual]{.class_4725} lesson. Some sinners get saved young, like the little girl in Luke 8. When they do, they don't have to recover from a wicked life. Jairus' daughter got up right away and got something to eat. The widow of Nain's son sat up right away and began talking (vs. 15), but he had to have help getting down off the bier. When you get saved as a teenager or young adult, you have a little bit more baggage of sin for which to account, so it takes a little longer to recuperate from it. But if you've lived like the devil for thirty, forty, or fifty years, you are like Lazarus: you are going to need some help getting out of your grave clothes (John 11:44).

["And there came a fear on all: and they glorified God, saying, That a great prophet is risen up among us"]{.class_4723} (vs. 16). He's the Prophet like unto Moses (Acts 3:22--26); Mohammed []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_234}doesn't win, place, or show as any kind of a prophet, much less THE Prophet. Mohammed couldn't even prophesy his own death; Jesus Christ could (Matt. 16:21).

["And, that God hath visited his people"]{.class_4723} (see our comments on 1:68). His Messianic name, as given by Isaiah, was ["Emmanuel"]{.class_4723}---["God with us"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 1:23). In all three passages (vs. 16, 1:68; Matt. 1:21--23), God's people are the [Jews,]{.class_4725} [not Christians.]{.class_4725} Now thank God we got included (Eph. 2:12--13: Acts 15:14), but that was [after ]{.class_4725}Israel rejected her Messiah (Rom. 11:11). What you have to keep in mind in ["rightly dividing the word of truth"]{.class_4723} (2 Tim. 2:15) is that the Church is a "parenthesis" in redemptive history. God is dealing with the [Jews]{.class_4725} in the Gospels, and He will deal with them again after the Rapture of the Church. You can't read Jewish doctrine into the Church Age, and you can't read Church doctrine into the Gospels and the Tribulation.

Verse 17 is a repeat of what we have already seen in 4:37 and 5:15.

18 And the disciples of John shewed him of all these things.

19 And John calling unto him two of his disciples sent them to Jesus, saying, Art thou he that should come? or look we for another?

20 When the men were come unto him, they said, John Baptist hath sent us unto thee, saying, Art thou he that should come? or look we for another?

21 And in that same hour he cured many of their infirmities and plagues, and of evil spirits; and unto many that were blind he gave sight.

22 Then Jesus answering said unto them, Go your way, and tell John what things ye have seen and heard; how that the blind see, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, to the poor the gospel is preached.

23 And blessed is he, whosoever shall not be offended in me.

John the Baptist is the man who said he saw the Holy Spirit coming down from Heaven like a dove and abiding on Christ (John 1:33). He's the one who said, ["Behold the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world"]{.class_4723} (John 1:29). He was the one who said, ["He must increase, but I must decrease"]{.class_4723} (John 3:30). Well, now that he's "decreasing," what's he doing doubting Christ's mission?

Well, what Luke doesn't tell you, that Matthew does (Matt. 11:2), is that John is in prison at this time. You don't understand John's doubts unless you've actually been through what John was going through, or at least have familiarized yourself with the testimonies of those who have been. If you want to understand why John said what he did, get the biographies of Haralan Popov ([Tortured for his Faith]{.class_4725}) and Richard Wurmbrand ([Tortured for Christ]{.class_4725}). At one point, Richard Wurmbrand spent three years in solitary confinement. From time to time, he would lose his mind, and the Communist guards would have to put him in a straitjacket until he regained his sanity. He would get mad at God and preach at Him. He had a series of sermons on "God's Unjust Laws." They would run something like this:

"Lord, you told us, ['Thou shalt not steal.']{.class_4723} You know that's not right. We've got to steal to stay alive; if we didn't steal, we'd starve to death. And you know we have to lie. If we told the truth, hundreds of people would be arrested and tortured like us. We have to lie to protect them."

Now Wurmbrand didn't mean any of that. He was just mad at God after being tortured to the point of insanity. His fellow prisoners and he developed a method of communicating with each other by tapping out Morse code on the walls of their cells. After one torture session, the guy next to Wurmbrand tapped out, "I had to forgive God again tonight." Those guys had to do that, or else they would get bitter at God like Job.

That's what John the Baptist is going through. He was supposed to be a prophet ["in the spirit and power of Elias"]{.class_4723} preparing the way for the Messiah, and here he is in prison. Like the rest of Israel, he expected Jesus to fulfil all the prophecies of setting up His kingdom in power and destroying the Gentile nations [right then,]{.class_4725} and it wasn't happening. That's what shook him up and made Him []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_236}question who Jesus was.

Now, Jesus doesn't answer the disciples John sent right off the bat. He ignores them and performs a bunch of [miracles]{.class_4725} in front of them (vs. 21). When the Lord finally does answer them, He doesn't tell them: "Tell John I'm the one." Instead He tells them: ["tell John what things ye have seen and heard"]{.class_4723} (vs. 22)---the [miracles.]{.class_4725} Christ told those Jews in the Gospel of John:

["But I have greater witness than that of John: for the works which the Father hath given me to finish, the same works that I do, bear witness of me, that the Father hath sent me"]{.class_4723} (John 5:36).

["Jesus answered them, I told you, and ye believed not: the works that I do in my Father's name, they bear witness of me\....If I do not the works of my Father, believe me not. But if I do, though ye believe not me, believe the works: that ye may know, and believe, that the Father is in me, and I in him"]{.class_4723} (John 10:25, 37--38).

Peter called Jesus: ["a man approved of God among you]{.class_4723} \[i.e., the Jews\] [by miracles and wonders and signs"]{.class_4723} (Acts 2:22). John is a [Jew,]{.class_4725} and ["the Jews require a sign"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 1:22), so Jesus gives John the signs of His Messiahship (see our comments on 4:40--41).

["And blessed is he, whosoever shall not be offended in me"]{.class_4723} (vs. 23). Jesus Christ is a ["rock of offence"]{.class_4723} to every sinner who won't believe on Him and obey the Gospel (Rom. 9:33; 1 Pet. 2:8). The preaching of the cross is an offence to every self-righteous sinner trying to work his way to Heaven (Gal. 5:11 cf. 1 Cor. 1:18).

24 And when the messengers of John were departed, he began to speak unto the people concerning John, What went ye out into the wilderness for to see? A reed shaken with the wind?

25 But what went ye out for to see? A man clothed in soft raiment? Behold, they which are gorgeously apparelled, and live delicately, are in kings' courts.

26 But what went ye out for to see? A prophet? Yea, I say unto you, and much more than a prophet.

27 This is he, of whom it is written, Behold, I send my messenger before thy face, which shall prepare thy way before thee.

28 For I say unto you, Among those that are born of women there is not a greater prophet than John the Baptist: but he that is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he.

29 And all the people that heard him, and the publicans, justified God, being baptized with the baptism of John.

30 But the Pharisees and lawyers rejected the counsel of God against themselves, being not baptized of him.

Verses 24--28 are the Lord Jesus bragging about John the Baptist, but notice in verse 24 that He doesn't do it until the disciples of John are gone. That's typical of the Lord: to your face He's pretty rough with you, but behind your back, He speaks up for you. That's the way He did with Job. When Satan accuses Job, the Lord brags about how righteous he is (Job 1:8--11), but when the Lord confronts Job at the end of the book, He says, ["Who is this that darkeneth counsel by words without knowledge"]{.class_4723} (Job 38:2). He does the same thing with Israel in the wilderness: He kills them right and left from Numbers 11--21, but when Balaam tries to curse them, God says He didn't see any iniquity in them at all (Num. 23:21). That's the Lord whipping His children. Hebrews 12:6--8 says:

"For whom the Lord loveth he chaseneth, and scourgeth every son whom he receiveth. If ye endure chastening, God dealeth with you as with sons; for what son is he whom the father chasteneth not? But if ye be without chastisement, whereof all are partakers, then are ye bastards, and not sons."

God spanks you because you're His kid. I'm responsible for whipping my children, not you; God's the same way. God will take the "strap" to you if you are one of His children; but when the Devil shows up before the throne to accuse you (Rev. 12:10), the Lord says, "I don't see anything wrong with him." That's the difference between [standing]{.class_4725} and [state.]{.class_4725} Your standing before God is in Christ's righteousness; your state is however you are living at the moment. The Lord deals with you according to your [state;]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_238} He defends you before the Devil according to your [standing in Christ]{.class_4725} (1 John 2:1). So here is Jesus Christ sticking up for John the Baptist before his enemies.

["What went ye out into the wilderness for to see? A reed shaken with the wind?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 24). ["A reed"]{.class_4723} speaks of weakness (2 Kings 18:21; Isa. 36:6, 42:3; Ezek. 29:6); ["shaken with the wind"]{.class_4723} speaks of doubt and a lack of faith (James 1:6; Heb. 10:23). To John's enemies, Jesus said John had a backbone of steel and never had any doubts about what He preached. Well, you just read where John [did]{.class_4725} express doubts; that's the Lord covering up for one of His saints. Haven't you ever noticed how the Lord skips the sins of the Old Testament saints over and over again in the New Testament? There's no mention of Noah's drunkenness or Abraham's lying or Moses' anger or Joshua's prayerlessness or David's adultery. That's [grace.]{.class_4725}

["But what went ye out for to see? A man clothed in soft raiment?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 25). Not John! John would "walk a mile for a camel" because that's where he got his suit of clothes (Mark 1:6). John the Baptist was a type of Elijah at the Second Coming (1:17), so he dressed like Elijah (see our comments on Zech. 13:4 in that Commentary).

["But what went ye out for to see? A prophet? Yea, I say unto you, and MUCH MORE than a prophet"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26). And there goes Mohammed out the window. If Mohammed were "Allah's Prophet" (and God knows he never prophesied anything a day in his life---see our remarks under 1:2), then he was a [second-rate]{.class_4725} prophet to John the Baptist, according to Jesus Christ (and God knows Mohammed couldn't even measure up to a prophet like Obadiah).

Verse 27 is a quote from Malachi 3:1, which is also quoted in Mark's Gospel (Mark 1:2). You can see our remarks on both passages under 1:17 and 3:15. The only thing further I will add here is that the term [messenger]{.class_4725} is one of the three titles of the Mohammedans for their "god in the flesh" (Sura 4:80): they call Mohammed Allah's "Prophet" or "Apostle" or "Messenger," depending on to whom they're trying to give the shaft (Jews, Christians, or others). According to Malachi, Mark, Luke, and Jesus Christ (as well as Matthew---Matt. 11:10), Mohammed and all his followers are [liars.]{.class_4725}

Verse 28: []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_239}["For I say unto you, Among those that are born of women there is not a greater prophet than John the Baptist: but he that is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he."]{.class_4723} Note the contrast here is between ["John the Baptist"]{.class_4723} and ["the kingdom of God,"]{.class_4723} not ["the kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4723} as it is given in the parallel passage in Matthew 11:11. You are dealing with a [spiritual]{.class_4725} kingdom here, not a [physical]{.class_4725} one (see our comments on 6:20).

The passage that explains verse 28 is Luke 16:16---["The law and the prophets were until John: since that time the kingdom of God is preached."]{.class_4723} John the Baptist is the last in a long line of Old Testament prophets; he is the greatest of all those prophets because he is the one who heralds the Messiah (vs. 27). The theme of the Old Testament is a visible, physical, literal kingdom on earth with David and his descendants on the throne (2 Sam. 7; Psa. 2; Jer. 33; etc.). When Jesus Christ shows up, He offers that kingdom (the Kingdom of Heaven) to its legitimate heirs, the Jews, but when they reject the King, that kingdom is postponed 2,000+ years until the King returns in power as is prophesied over and over again in the Old Testament (Psa. 18, 110; Isa. 63; Zech. 12, 14; etc.). Until then, another kingdom, a [spiritual]{.class_4725} one (Rom. 14:17; Luke 17:21; Matt. 6:33), is offered to a new ["nation"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 21:43), a nation of people who are not ["born of women"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 2:9 cf. 1 Pet. 1:23). They have a New Birth, a [spiritual]{.class_4725} birth (John 3:3, 5).

The reason the ["least in the kingdom of God is greater than"]{.class_4723} John the Baptist is because John the Baptist had no New Birth, being an Old Testament prophet under the Old Testament Law (16:16). John the Baptist was never baptized by the Holy Spirit into Christ's spiritual body (3:16) and was not part of Christ's Bride (John 3:29). John the Baptist didn't have Christ in him, ["the hope of glory"]{.class_4723} (Col. 1:27). If you are saved, you have all those things, whether you are a "Christian celebrity" or not.

Notice the peculiar expression in verse 29:[ "justified God."]{.class_4723} That's not God justifying the sinner; that's the [sinner justifying God]{.class_4725} (see the same thing in vs. 35, where Jesus Christ is personified as wisdom). The basic meaning of [justify]{.class_4725} is "to declare right." The publicans were declaring God to be right. How? By being baptized with John's ["baptism of repentance"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_240} (see our comments on 3:3). That is, they were saying that God was right by admitting they were sinners who deserved to die.

Baptism is a picture of death, burial, and resurrection (Rom. 6:3--4). When you go down under that water, you are telling the whole world that you are such a sinner that you deserve to die and be buried (Rom. 6:23), and when you come up, you are saying you have trusted Jesus Christ and He is raising you up from the death you deserve.

These publicans and harlots who came to John to be baptized were saying, "God, you're right. We are such bad sinners that we need to repent, so we'll go down under that water and admit that to the whole world." The Pharisees and the Sadducees, though, didn't think they had to repent of anything; that's why they didn't obey John and get baptized (vs. 30 cf. Matt. 21:32). They didn't justify God; they [justified themselves]{.class_4725} (see the Parable of the Pharisee and the Publican in 17:9--14).

31 And the Lord said, Whereunto then shall I liken the men of this generation? and to what are they like?

32 They are like unto children sitting in the marketplace, and calling one to another, and saying, We have piped unto you, and ye have not danced; we have mourned to you, and ye have not wept.

33 For John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine; and ye say, He hath a devil.

34 The Son of man is come eating and drinking; and ye say, Behold a gluttonous man, and a winebibber, a friend of publicans and sinners!

35 But wisdom is justified of all her children.

Notice the words ["liken"]{.class_4723} and ["like"]{.class_4723} in verses 31--32. When the Lord wants to teach you something, that's how He does it: with the words ["like" ]{.class_4723}and ["as."]{.class_4723} He says over in Hosea 12:10, ["I have multiplied visions, and USED SIMILITUDES, by the ministry of the prophets."]{.class_4723} In other words, He teaches you about the things you [can't see]{.class_4725} by pointing out the similarities to those things you [can see.]{.class_4725} That's why the Bible says: []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_241}["For the INVISIBLE THINGS of him from the creation of the world are CLEARLY SEEN"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 1:20). That's a contradiction of terms, unless you understand that God is using the visible things to teach you about ["the invisible things."]{.class_4723}

Christ preaches about Hell. A fellow says, "What do you mean by 'Hell'? I don't see any Hell." Jesus says, "Look at gehenna, the city dump. You see that place there with the dead bodies, the dung, the garbage, the fire, the worms, and the ashes? Hell's [like]{.class_4725} that."

So Christ is going to show ["the Pharisees and lawyers"]{.class_4723} what their ["generation"]{.class_4723}---[the generation that rejected John the Baptist and Jesus Christ---]{.class_4725}is ["like."]{.class_4723} Jesus says they are like children taunting the people walking by ["in the market place"]{.class_4723} because they can't get any reaction out of them. ["We have piped unto you, and ye have not danced; we have mourned to you, and ye have not wept"]{.class_4723} (vs. 32).

Now what is that a picture of? ["For John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine"]{.class_4723} (vs. 33). He was the one who wouldn't dance when the ["children" "piped"]{.class_4723} (see 5:33); Jesus Christ was the one who wouldn't weep when they ["mourned"]{.class_4723} (5:34). The Lord tried to appease them with an austere prophet, and they turned him down and said he was demon-possessed. So the Lord sent along a popular preacher, and they turned Him down too because He was ["a friend of publicans and sinners"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34). They couldn't be satisfied no matter how the Lord tried to reach them.

["The Son of man is come eating and drinking; and ye say, Behold a gluttonous man, and a winebibber"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34). Professing Christians who believe in "social drinking" (Lutherans, Episcopalians, Catholics, etc.) use the verse to prove that since Jesus Christ drank intoxicating liquor, you can too. This totally overlooks the fact that the accusation was a [lie]{.class_4725} spread by Christ's [enemies.]{.class_4725} Jesus Christ wasn't a glutton or drunk just because He didn't fast, and because He ate and drank ["with publicans and sinners"]{.class_4723} (5:29--30). Jesus Christ would no more drink [Catholic hootch]{.class_4725} than He would get fifty to sixty people drunk on fermented wine at a wedding (see our extensive comments on the subject under John 2:6--10 in []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_242}that Commentary; see also our comments on Hab. 2:5, 15 in that Commentary).

["But wisdom is justified of all her children"]{.class_4723} (vs. 35; see comments on vs. 29). In comparing verse 35 with verse 29, you can see that God is typified, here, by the trait of ["wisdom."]{.class_4723} In fact, there's a whole chapter on it in the Old Testament---Proverbs 8. In that chapter, ["wisdom"]{.class_4723} is spoken of in the feminine gender (["she"]{.class_4723}---vss. 2--3; ["her"]{.class_4723}---vs. 1), just as Christ did of Himself in 13:34. That thing is so strong on the deity of wisdom, that the ancient Greeks worshipped it as a goddess ([Sophia]{.class_4725}). But if there is any doubt that Proverbs 8 is speaking of Jesus Christ as wisdom incarnate, look at verses 23--30 in that chapter.

["I was set up from everlasting, from the beginning, or ever the earth was ]{.class_4723}\[before Gen. 1:1\][. When there were no depths, I was brought forth ]{.class_4723}\[before Gen. 1:2\][\...before the hills was I brought forth: While as yet he had not made the earth\...When he prepared the heavens, I was there\...when he appointed the foundations of the earth; Then I was by him, as one brought up with him: and I was daily his delight, rejoicing always before him."]{.class_4723}

That's Jesus Christ in John 1:1--2. ["In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. The same was in the beginning with God."]{.class_4723} Jesus Christ's ["goings forth have been\...from everlasting"]{.class_4723} (Mic. 5:2), just as you read it in Proverbs 8:23.

But if all that weren't enough, look at Proverbs 8:35--36.

["For whoso findeth me findeth life ]{.class_4723}\[1 John 5:12---["He that hath the Son hath life"]{.class_4723}\][, and shall obtain favour of the LORD]{.class_4723} \[I would say so; see John 1:12; Rom. 8:29; Eph. 4:30; Col. 1:27; etc.\][. But he that sinneth against me wrongeth his own soul ]{.class_4723}\[see Matt. 16:25--26\][: all they that hate me love death ]{.class_4723}\[John 3:36---["he that believeth not the Son shall not see life; but the wrath of God abideth on him"]{.class_4723}\][."]{.class_4723}

Of course, Proverbs 8 is a type and a metaphor (which is where Augustine, Calvin, and the Reformers blew it; see our remarks under Prov. 8:22--36 in that Commentary), but the passage is still speaking of Jesus Christ as wisdom incarnate. The New Testament says, ["But []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_243}of him are ye in Christ Jesus, who of God is made unto us WISDOM"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 1:30); and ["In whom are hid all the treasures of WISDOM and knowledge"]{.class_4723} (Col. 2:3).
