The job of a child of God in any age is to justify God by declaring [He is right]{.class_4725} and [you are wrong,]{.class_4725} and to do what He tells you to do to get right (Psa. 51:4; Rom. 3:4, 10:3; Rev. 15:3, 16:5). The New Testament is clear that [in this age]{.class_4725} you are made right before God through [faith in His Son]{.class_4725} (Rom. 4:5, 5:1; Eph. 2:8--9).

36 And one of the Pharisees desired him that he would eat with him. And he went into the Pharisee's house, and sat down to meat.

37 And, behold, a woman in the city, which was a sinner, when she knew that Jesus sat at meat in the Pharisee's house, brought an alabaster box of ointment,

38 And stood at his feet behind him weeping, and began to wash his feet with tears, and did wipe them with the hairs of her head, and kissed his feet, and anointed them with the ointment.

39 Now when the Pharisee which had bidden him saw it, he spake within himself, saying, This man, if he were a prophet, would have known who and what manner of woman this is that toucheth him: for she is a sinner.

40 And Jesus answering said unto him, Simon, I have somewhat to say unto thee. And he saith, Master, say on.

41 There was a certain creditor which had two debtors: the one owed five hundred pence, and the other fifty.

42 And when they had nothing to pay, he frankly forgave them both. Tell me therefore, which of them will love him most?

43 Simon answered and said, I suppose that he, to whom he forgave most. And he said unto him, Thou hast rightly judged.

44 And he turned to the woman, and said unto Simon, Seest thou this woman? I entered into thine house, thou gavest me no water for my feet: but she hath washed my feet with tears, and wiped them with the hairs of her head.

45 Thou gavest me no kiss: but this woman since the time I came in hath not ceased to kiss my feet.

46 My head with oil thou didst not anoint: but this woman hath anointed my feet with ointment.

47 Wherefore I say unto thee, Her sins, which are many, are forgiven; for she loved much: but to whom little is forgiven, the same loveth little.

48 And he said unto her, Thy sins are forgiven.

49 And they that sat at meat with him began to say within themselves, Who is this that forgiveth sins also?

50 And he said to the woman, Thy faith hath saved thee; go in peace.

The passage logically follows up what you just read in verse 34: ["The Son of man is come eating and drinking; and ye say, Behold a gluttonous man, and a winebibber, a friend of publicans and sinners."]{.class_4723} That's a direct reference to Jesus going to the feast at Matthew's house back in 5:29--30. So at least one Pharisee (named ["Simon"]{.class_4723}---vs. 40) figures that since "you can draw more flies with honey than with vinegar," that he'll try to make friends with this preacher who's been lambasting him and his buddies. He invites Him to a feast of his own. As you are about to see, it doesn't do him any good, but he tries it anyway.

Notice how the word ["meat"]{.class_4723} is used in verse 36. It is an "archaic" usage, but it is still valid. If you take the "m" off the word, you get the word [eat,]{.class_4725} and that is how the word ["meat"]{.class_4723} is used in a [King James]{.class_4725} Bible. ["Meat"]{.class_4723} in the [AV]{.class_4725} covers [anything]{.class_4725} you put in your mouth, like the herbs and fruits in Genesis 1:29, and the ["fine flour"]{.class_4723} used in the ["meat offering"]{.class_4723} in Leviticus 2.

Now, what you read in verses 37--38 is the anointing of Jesus' feet by a woman. This takes place [twice]{.class_4725} in Christ's ministry, exactly like the cleansing of the Temple and the "Sermon on the Mount" (see our comments on 6:17). It takes place once at the [beginning]{.class_4725} of Christ's ministry (vss. 37--38) and again at the [end]{.class_4725} of Christ's ministry (John 12:1--3; Mark 14:3). The anointing in John 12 and Mark 14 is by Mary, the sister of Martha and Lazarus. The one here is by a wicked woman who is seeking forgiveness from Christ (see our comments on the Old Testament usage of the word ["sinner"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_245} under 1:6 and 5:30, 32); the implication is that the woman here in verses 37--38 is a prostitute.

The Pharisee figures that if Jesus were really a prophet, He would know the character of the woman touching Him. Jesus not only knew about the woman, He knew about the Pharisee and what he was thinking (see our remarks under 5:22). How would you like to be seated at the dinner table with someone who knew everything you were thinking? That's what's going on here. When Jesus answers Simon (vs. 40), He's answering what Simon was thinking, not any question Simon asked Him out loud.

What follows in verses 41--42 is obviously a parable, although the word [parable ]{.class_4725}is not used to describe the story. What sets verses 41--42 apart from the account of the rich man and Lazarus in chapter 16 (which is also not called a parable) is that no proper names are used of any of the characters in the story (the naming of Lazarus in Luke 16 shows you the Lord is giving you the historical account of an actual man who died), and application of the story is made by Christ in verses 44--47. There is no parabolic application made of the story of the rich man and Lazarus.

The application of the parable is obvious. The ["creditor" ]{.class_4723}is Jesus Christ (another assertion of His Deity); the debtor who owed fifty pence is the Pharisee; the one who owed the ["five hundred pence"]{.class_4723} is the wicked woman. The question Jesus puts to Simon is: ["which of them]{.class_4723} \[i.e., the debtors\][ will love him]{.class_4723} \[the creditor who forgave them\][ the most?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 42). The Pharisee gives the correct answer: the one ["to whom he forgave most"]{.class_4723} (vs. 43).

["Her sins, which are many, are forgiven"]{.class_4723} (vs. 47). Again, Christ is asserting His Deity (see our comments on 5:21--24). Brethren, if Jesus Christ were not ["God\...in the flesh,"]{.class_4723} then He was an egotistical hypocrite. Do you know what He said at His second anointing? Someone complained about the waste of the expensive perfume that could have been sold to feed the poor, and Christ told him, "Don't worry about the poor; pay attention to me" (read John 12:8). You talk about egotistical, boy, that takes the cake. That is, unless Jesus Christ was whom He claimed to be---God Almighty come down in human form. The Lord is the only one who has a right to be []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1554.xhtml_page_246}egotistical.

["For she loved much: but to whom little is forgiven, the same loveth little."]{.class_4736} Now, the best way to go through life is to get saved at an early age and stay clean. A lot of times, though, kids who grow up in a Christian home and attend church all the time get in trouble when they hit adolescence. They make such a mess of things that they think they are lost, so they "get saved" again in their twenties. That happens all the time. But if you can stay clean, stay clean.

If you get saved young, you have all the advantages except one. You take an old dog like me who got dragged up through the sewer, I've got one big advantage that some of these pink-cheeked, fresh-faced Christian College students don't have. You see, when I got saved, a big devil went out of me. These kids who get saved at five, six, or seven, yes, they were sinners who needed a Saviour; but when they got saved, only a little devil made an exit. We guys who got saved out of the sewer appreciate our salvation in a way that the kids who got raised right and saved early never can. Why? We were [forgiven much.]{.class_4725}

When Jesus forgave that fallen woman, He got the same reaction from those Pharisees at the table as He did when he healed the palsied man who was let down into the house by his four friends (vs. 49 cf. 5:21--24 and comments on that passage). When Jesus said, ["Thy faith hath saved thee"]{.class_4723} (vs. 50), He was not just talking about faith in general. Faith is no better than the object in which it is placed. The faith that saved that wicked woman was [faith in Jesus Christ.]{.class_4725}

Jesus Christ told the apostles that ["repentance and remission of sins should be preached IN HIS NAME"]{.class_4723} (24:47). Paul said, ["that THROUGH THIS MAN]{.class_4723} \[i.e., Jesus Christ\][ is preached unto you the forgiveness of sins: And BY HIM all that believe are justified from all things, from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses"]{.class_4723} (Acts 13:38--39).

["Thy faith hath saved thee; go in peace."]{.class_4723} The Apostle Paul wrote, ["Therefore being justified BY FAITH, we have PEACE with God through our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 5:1).

I experienced what Christ told that woman when I got saved. The night I professed Christ at the Brent Baptist Church in Pensacola, I had to hitchhike back downtown to where I was staying because the buses stopped running after six p.m. As I walked along the highway in the moonlight, the Devil started working me over: "Fine bunch of Christians they are! Wouldn't even give you a ride home."

About that time, a car pulled up in front of me, and the driver offered me a ride. It was a man and his wife from the church. As we drove down the road, the man said, "I was in church tonight and saw you get saved. You'll never regret doing that!"

"Nah, I guess I won't," I said as I stared out the window. But that old nature in me was thinking, "You gotta lotta room to talk. You've got a car, and your wife is with you. I'm on foot, and my family is all busted up, and my wife is about to divorce me."

That fellow had no idea what I was thinking. He just kept on talking and said, "I never had a momma or daddy; I was raised in an orphan's home. I got saved when I was nine years old, and ever since then, I've been just as happy as a dead pig in the sun!"

I said to myself, "All right, Lord, I'll shut up." But I didn't "shut up." I got back to the "flophouse" with a thousand conflicting, disturbing thoughts running through my head. "What if you lose it? What if you never got it? How do you know it's real? You're losing your mind like your brother did." And on and on it went.

I got down on my knees beside my bed, and the Holy Spirit said to me, "It's all right now, Pete. It's all right now." "But what if\...," I said. And the answer came back: "It's all right now."

Here I am 62 years later. I have been through two divorces, three law suits, two church splits, family members turned against me, church members turned against me, friends turned against me, back problems, eye problems, and all kinds of other problems; and do you know what the Holy Spirit still tells me? "It's all right now, Pete; it's all right now."

["And the peace of God, which passeth all understanding, shall keep your hearts and minds through Christ Jesus"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 4:7).

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml}

# Chapter 8 {.heading_78-0}

8:1 And it came to pass afterward, that he went throughout every city and village, preaching and shewing the glad tidings of the kingdom of God: and the twelve were with him,

2 And certain women, which had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities, Mary called Magdalene, out of whom went seven devils,

3 And Joanna the wife of Chuza Herod's steward, and Susanna, and many others, which ministered unto him of their substance.

Notice again the prominent place given to women in the passage (see our remarks under 1:26, 2:36--38). Even though Christ was never married, women knew instinctively that He had their best interest at heart. The women of verses 2--3 follow Jesus all the way to the cross (23:49) and the tomb (23:55), and they are the first ones to proclaim the good news of the Resurrection (24:10).

["Mary called Magdalene, out of whom went seven devils"]{.class_4723} (vs. 2). This is the Mary Magdalene who was the first person to see Jesus after His resurrection (John 20:11--17). Recently, an author named Dan Brown wrote a book entitled [The DaVinci Code]{.class_4725} in which he had Jesus shacking up with Mary Magdalene. He got that from the Gnostic "Gospel of Philip" (c. A.D. 200), and the Gnostics got it from an unsaved Greek philosopher named Celsus (d. A.D. 180). The Mormons teach that some of these women who traveled with Jesus were His wives (i.e., Jesus Christ was a fornicating polygamist like Mohammed and Joseph Smith). Of course, none of this has anything to do with the actual eyewitness accounts of the Lord Jesus (see our comments on 1:2). They are simply the depraved imaginations of a bunch of sex-obsessed neurotics.

We have already discussed what the Bible says about ["devils"]{.class_4723} under 3:22 and 4:33 (which see). Verse 2 shows you that "demons" are [not]{.class_4725} fallen angels. Everywhere an angel shows up in that Bible, he is a [man ]{.class_4725}with [no wings]{.class_4725} (Gen. 19:1, 5; Judg. 13:3, 6, 8, 9, 10, 11; Rev. []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_249}21:7). Well, it would be pretty hard to fit seven full-grown men into the body of a woman, now, wouldn't it? But if a devil were the size of a bird (vss. 5, 12) or a fly (11:15), anywhere from seven to a thousand (Matt. 8:28 cf. Mark 5:13) could inhabit a body.

4 And when much people were gathered together, and were come to him out of every city, he spake by a parable:

5 A sower went out to sow his seed: and as he sowed, some fell by the way side; and it was trodden down, and the fowls of the air devoured it.

6 And some fell upon a rock; and as soon as it was sprung up, it withered away, because it lacked moisture.

7 And some fell among thorns; and the thorns sprang up with it, and choked it.

8 And other fell on good ground, and sprang up, and bare fruit an hundredfold. And when he had said these things, he cried, He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.

9 And his disciples asked him, saying, What might this parable be?

10 And he said, Unto you it is given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of God: but to others in parables; that seeing they might not see, and hearing they might not understand.

11 Now the parable is this: The seed is the word of God.

12 Those by the way side are they that hear; then cometh the devil, and taketh away the word out of their hearts, lest they should believe and be saved.

13 They on the rock are they, which, when they hear, receive the word with joy; and these have no root, which for a while believe, and in time of temptation fall away.

14 And that which fell among thorns are they, which, when they have heard, go forth, and are choked with cares and riches and pleasures of this life, and bring no fruit to perfection.

15 But that on the good ground are they, which in an honest and good heart, having heard the word, keep it, and bring forth fruit with patience.

This is the Parable of the Sower, which is also given in Matthew 13:1--23 and Mark 4:1--20. It is one of only a handful of parables found in three of the four Gospels. Here in Luke, it is the only parable in the book where the interpretation is given right in the context. Unlike Matthew's version, though, the parable here is on ["the mysteries of the kingdom of GOD,"]{.class_4723} not ["the kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4723} (vs. 10 cf. Matt. 13:11). One of the biggest differences between the two versions of the parable is that the seed in Matthew 13 is ["the word of the kingdom"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 13:19), which in the context would be the literal, visible, physical Kingdom of Heaven (Matt. 13:11), with Jesus Christ as a literal, physical, visible King on the throne of David ruling from Jerusalem (Zech. 14:9--21; Jer. 33:15--17). The seed in Luke, though, is ["the word of God"]{.class_4723} (vs. 11), given to produce a New Birth (1 Pet. 1:23) into a spiritual kingdom (Rom. 14:17)---the Kingdom of God (John 3:3, 5).

Notice that the purpose of the parable is to [hide]{.class_4725} truth from those who do not want it (vs. 10). The disciples get the interpretation because they come to Christ without criticism or "hardness of heart" trying to catch Him in His words (see 20:20). They have received and believed the light they have been given and are given more; but when light is rejected, it becomes [lightning]{.class_4725} (for further study, see our comments on Matt. 13:10--13 in that Commentary).

Verse 5: ["A sower went out to sow his seed."]{.class_4723} The expression ["HIS seed"]{.class_4723} identifies the ["sower"]{.class_4723} as the Lord Jesus Christ Himself, since the seed is defined as ["the word OF GOD"]{.class_4723} (vs. 11). This picture of the word of God as seed is taken from the Old Testament (Isa. 55:10--11) and is applied to every believer in Ecclesiastes 11:1--6. You are to sow the word of God regardless of circumstances (Eccl. 11:4), you are to sow bountifully (Eccl. 11:2), and you are to sow at all ages of life (Eccl. 11:6). The promise is that God won't allow His word to return void (Isa. 55:11), and that, eventually, you will see the results (Eccl. 11:1). Your job is to sow the word and leave the results to God.

A good example of this was a deacon out of the Bible Baptist Church here in Pensacola, Florida, who is now at home with the Lord. His name was Malcolm Dickman. He wanted to get the word of God out, so he started making these plastic, corrugated signs with []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_251}verses of Scripture on them. You can be driving on some back road in Alabama or Pennsylvania, and there will be one of Bro. Dickman's Scripture signs out in front of someone's yard. I have been in Germany and seen them there. We have had missionaries come through our church and show their slides, and there will be one of Bro. Dickman's signs sitting out there in front of some church in the Philippines, Papua New Guinea, or Africa. Bro. Dickman got out the word, boy. His signs are in all fifty States and in several foreign countries. He obeyed the command to sow the word. Have you?

Now, sowing the word produces four kinds of results in this parable. The first two don't produce salvation. The second two produce salvation, but one is superior to the other. And if you preach or do personal work, these are the kinds of results you will get. The four soils are the different hearts of those who hear the word of God and react to it four different ways.

[Case 1:]{.class_4725} The [hard]{.class_4725} heart (vss. 5, 12). This is the person who hears the word of God and goes out the door without doing anything about it. Why? Because ["the devil"]{.class_4723} comes along and takes the word away. So Satan's main job in this world is not pornography or the drug traffic or murder or crooked politicians, etc. Satan's main work has to do with the [Bible.]{.class_4725} He will do anything he can to keep you from hearing the words in that Book. That's why it's forbidden in so many countries: the leaders are afraid their people will read the Book and follow it instead of them.

The first thing the Devil will do is to try to keep you from [hearing]{.class_4725} what God says in His Book. If Satan can't stop you from hearing it, then he will try to prevent you from [receiving]{.class_4725} it (cf. 1 Thess. 2:13); he'll try to get you to push it off. If he can't get you to push it off so you don't receive it, then he'll work so that you don't [understand]{.class_4725} what you read or hear. If you understand what you read, he'll work on getting you to [doubt it's true.]{.class_4725} Suppose you hear it and receive it and understand it so that you end up [believing]{.class_4725} it; do you think he quits? No! he'll try to get you to [forget]{.class_4725} it. If he can't stop you from hearing it, receiving it, understanding it, believing it, and remembering it, then he'll try to get you to [disobey]{.class_4725} it.

[That's]{.class_4725} the Devil's work. When you deal with abortions and condoms and queers and faggots and Lesbians and fruitcakes and serial killers and transvestites, etc., you're dealing with the [flesh.]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_252} The attack on that Book is [spiritual,]{.class_4725} and if the Devil can finish off his attack on the Book, the rest of the stuff will multiply like weeds.

If you want to find the Devil, don't go to a discotheque or an X-rated movie theater or a crack house. If you want to find the Devil, I know where to find him: in a [Bible translation committee.]{.class_4725} When James White wrote his work [The King James Only Controversy,]{.class_4725} the silly, naive child thought that the modern Bible versions were only the work of "scholars"---some good; some bad---and that Satan had nothing to do with the [RV, ASV, RSV, NASV, NRSV, NIV, NKJV, ESV,]{.class_4725} etc. To the contrary, Satan was right there on those committees guiding the translators and revisers as to their choice of words. If you want the definitive work that proves that is so, get Gail Riplinger's book [New Age Bible Versions.]{.class_4725} She proves beyond the shadow of a doubt (and I would add WAY beyond the shadow of a doubt) that the wording of the new translations matches the words and doctrines of the New Age and the occult so that you end up with a "Bible" that promotes a one-world religion under the control of [Satan.]{.class_4725}

So this first group of hearers dismiss the word and end up lost (vs. 12---["lest they should believe and be saved"]{.class_4723}). Those are the ones who roll up the window when you're out street preaching, or crumple up the tract and throw it on the ground when you hand it to them. They do that because the Devil and his evil spirits are working on them to prevent them from hearing the word.

[Case 2:]{.class_4725} The [shallow]{.class_4725} heart (vss. 6, 13). This second group hears the word, and they get all excited about it ("Hallelujah!" "Glory to God!", etc.). The problem is they ["have no root"]{.class_4723} (vs. 13). What's the ["root"]{.class_4723}?

["I JESUS have sent mine angel to testify unto you these things in the churches. I AM THE ROOT and the offspring of David, and the bright and morning star"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 22:16).

["And again, Esaias saith, There shall be A ROOT of Jesse, and he that shall rise to reign over the Gentiles; IN HIM SHALL THE GENTILES TRUST" ]{.class_4723}(Rom. 15:12).

Take one more in Isaiah 53. The whole chapter is a prophecy on the blood atonement of Jesus Christ. Look at verses 1--2.

"Who hath BELIEVED our report? and to whom is the arm of the LORD revealed? For he shall grow up before him as a tender plant, and as a ROOT out of a dry ground."

So the ["root"]{.class_4723} in the parable is Christ, and the fellow with the shallow heart is someone who makes a [profession]{.class_4725} but who does not actually receive Christ's blood atonement for himself. As a result, when ["temptation"]{.class_4723} (vs. 13), [trouble ]{.class_4725}(Matt. 13:21), or ["persecution"]{.class_4723} (Mark 4:17) come ["for the word's sake"]{.class_4723} (Mark 4:17), they get ["offended"]{.class_4723} (Mark 4:17; cf. our comments on 7:23 and 6:45) and drop out.

[Case 3:]{.class_4725} The [choked]{.class_4725} heart (vss. 7, 14). The teaching of the Fundamentalists and Conservatives is that the first three "hearts" are [lost]{.class_4725} and the last "heart" is the only one that is saved. That comes from the Calvinistic teaching that if you were "really" saved, you wouldn't do this or that, meaning the Calvinist isn't committing the sins you're committing, so he can glory in his own self-righteousness (see Luke 18:11). That's only a hair's breadth of difference from the Arminian teaching that if you commit this or that sin, you [lose]{.class_4725} your salvation. A hyper-Calvinism always leads to a hyper-Arminianism.

The truth is that a Christian can commit any sin a lost person can (see 1 Cor. 5:1--5). Demas was just as saved as Mark; the difference between the two is that Mark repented of his desertion while Demas didn't (see our comments Phile. 24 and 2 Tim. 4:10 in their respective Commentaries).

That the third "heart" is saved is obvious from the fact that, unlike the shallow heart, this one [does have root;]{.class_4725} ["the word"]{.class_4723} is [in]{.class_4725} him (note the wording in Mark 4:19). Moreover, he has the [possibility]{.class_4725} of bearing fruit, for ["it BECOMETH unfruitful"]{.class_4723} (Mark 4:19). It is part of the vine (John 15:1--2), and the fruit of the Spirit is there (see Gal. 5:19--23 and remarks in that Commentary); it just can't bring that fruit ["to perfection"]{.class_4723} (vs. 14).

Now, what's the problem? The shallow hearts ["are choked with CARES and RICHES and PLEASURES of this life"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 14). Mark describes them in more detail: ["the cares OF THIS WORLD, and THE DECEITFULNESS of riches, and THE LUST OF OTHER THINGS"]{.class_4730-0} (Mark 4:19). This is a case where a fellow genuinely gets saved and starts out really well (["go forth"]{.class_4730-0}---vs. 14), []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_254}but as he goes along in his Christian life, he gets distracted by these three things, and his Christian growth is strangled.

You take that first thing: ["the cares of this world."]{.class_4723} The kids are sick, you had a fight with your spouse, you got laid off down at the plant, the car won't start, the washing machine breaks down, and on and on. The more "civilized" you are, the more ["cares"]{.class_4723} with which you have to contend. The saved folk in the Philippines, Mexico, and Africa don't have as much to bother them in this regard as Christians in the United States, Canada, England, Germany, etc. Their day is spent just trying to get enough to eat to stay alive.

What's the solution for ["cares"]{.class_4723}? It's 1 Peter 5:7---["Casting all your care UPON HIM; for he careth for you."]{.class_4723} You're to turn everything over to the Lord and let Him worry about it. The Apostle Paul said, ["Be careful]{.class_4723} \[the word means "full of care"\][ for nothing; but in every thing by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known unto God"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 4:6).

The second thing is ["the deceitfulness of riches."]{.class_4723} How do riches deceive? They get you to trust [them]{.class_4725} instead of God (1 Tim. 6:17; Prov. 11:20). They cause you to err ["from the faith"]{.class_4723} and pierce yourself ["through with many sorrows"]{.class_4723} trying to gain them (1 Tim. 6:10). They promise you comfort and security, but they trap you and drown you ["in destruction and perdition"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:9). The proverb says they ["make themselves wings"]{.class_4723} and ["fly away"]{.class_4723} (Prov. 23:5), and they do you no good in the day of judgment (Prov. 11:4).

The modern teaching of the health-and-wealth prosperity gospel is that God wants every believer rich. Izzat so? How do you account, then, for the Apostle Paul? That's the one who said, ["Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 11:1). Well, what kind of example did he leave you to follow? Oh, he died with no house, no land, no school, no car, no bank account, no Social Security, no Medicare, no wife, no children, no family; and he was buried in an unmarked grave. That's the greatest Christian who ever lived. He knew not only ["how to abound,"]{.class_4723} he knew ["how to be abased"]{.class_4723} and how ["to suffer need"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 4:12).

Now maybe you will get rich; I don't know. God gave R. G. []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_255}LeTourneau a lot of money; He gave J. C. Penny a lot of money. They were both good Christians who loved the Lord and gave a lot of what God gave them back to the work of the Lord. There's nothing sinful about being a millionaire, but those are the [exceptions,]{.class_4725} not the [rule.]{.class_4725} The old saying is: "You can tell what God thinks of money by the ones He gave it to."

The last thing is ["the lust of other things,"]{.class_4723} what Luke calls the ["the pleasures of this life."]{.class_4723} Any kind of want or desire is a lust. Paul said that ["in the last days"]{.class_4723} men would be ["lovers of pleasures more than lovers of God"]{.class_4723} (2 Tim. 3:1--4). So ["the lust of other things"]{.class_4723} is a love of the pleasures of this world: love of sports, love of fame, love of sex, love of power, love of possessions, love of comfort, etc. In philosophy, it's called hedonism and Epicureanism---a total abandonment to the satisfaction of the flesh. Such things kill your love for God.

[Case 4:]{.class_4725} The [fruitful]{.class_4725} heart (vss. 8, 15). This heart is described by Jesus as ["honest and good"]{.class_4723} (vs. 15). No one who knows what the Scriptures say about the human heart can fail to note the contrast between Christ's words in verse 15 and Jeremiah 17:9---["The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked; who can know it?"]{.class_4723} What makes the heart of verse 15 ["honest and good"]{.class_4723} has nothing to do with any innate goodness in man (see Rom. 3:10--18, 7:18).

The heart of verse 15 has to do with the preparation of the heart by the Holy Spirit. Note the phrase ["good ground."]{.class_4723} No ground, no matter how fertile it may be, is naturally good for bearing fruit until it has been disked and plowed and the stumps and rocks removed. The people in verse 15 have come under the reproving work of the Holy Spirit (John 16:7--11) and have responded correctly to it. They not only ["hear the word"]{.class_4723} (Mark 4:20 cf. James 1:22), they ["receive it"]{.class_4723} (Mark 4:20) and [understand it]{.class_4725} (Matt. 13:23).

The Apostle Paul told the Christians at Thessalonica: ["For this cause also thank we God without ceasing, because, when ye received the word of God, which ye heard of us, ye received it not as the word of men, but as it is in truth, the word of God, which effectually worketh also in you that believe"]{.class_4723} (1 Thess. 2:13).

The ["honest and good heart"]{.class_4723} [believes]{.class_4725} and [receives]{.class_4725} []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_256}["the word of God"]{.class_4723} as ["God's WORDS"]{.class_4723} (John 8:47), and they ["keep"]{.class_4723} those words (Rev. 3:8). They don't [change]{.class_4725} them, [correct]{.class_4725} them, [laugh at]{.class_4725} them (like they do in the Bible classes at Bob Jones University), [revise]{.class_4725} them, [retranslate]{.class_4725} them, or [cast doubt on]{.class_4725} them.

["And bring forth fruit with patience."]{.class_4723} Although the analogy of the Parable of the Sower is different from that of Christ's discourse on the True Vine (John 15:1--8)---the parable deals with grain; the discourse with grapes---the principles of fruit-bearing in both are the same. ["With patience"]{.class_4723} speaks to what I call "the unhappy work of the Holy Spirit." That is, to get a child of God to bear fruit, the Holy Spirit will "prune off" those things in the believer's life that keeps him from bearing the fruit of the Holy Spirit (John 15:2 cf. Gal. 5:22--23). If the Christian patiently endures that cutting and trimming (Heb. 12:7), the more fruit he is able to bear.

Although the classes of fruit-bearing are not brought out in the Luke account, they are in Mark's Gospel: ["and bring forth fruit, some thirtyfold, some sixty, and some an hundred"]{.class_4723} (Mark 4:20). In John 15, they are given as: ["fruit"]{.class_4723} (John 15:2), ["more fruit"]{.class_4723} (John 15:2), and ["much fruit"]{.class_4723} (John 15:5). They match the three stages of Christian growth as given in Revelation 17:14---["called, and chosen, and faithful."]{.class_4723} The three levels of Christian experience in the fight against sin and the Devil are: ["they overcame him ]{.class_4723}\[1\] [by the blood of the Lamb, and ]{.class_4723}\[2\][ by the word of their testimony; and ]{.class_4723}\[3\][ they loved not their lives unto the death"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 12:11). Among the apostles, there were three circles of fellowship with Christ: there were the [twelve;]{.class_4725} then there was Christ's "inner circle"---[Peter, James, and John;]{.class_4725} and finally there was the "beloved disciple,"[ John.]{.class_4725} So there are [apprentice]{.class_4725} Christians, [journeyman]{.class_4725} Christians, and [master]{.class_4725} Christians.

16 No man, when he hath lighted a candle, covereth it with a vessel, or putteth it under a bed; but setteth it on a candlestick, that they which enter in may see the light.

17 For nothing is secret, that shall not be made manifest; neither any thing hid, that shall not be known and come abroad.

18 Take heed therefore how ye hear: for whosoever hath, to him shall be given; and whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken even that which he seemeth to have.

This is the Parable of the Candle. The children's song is: "This little light of mine, I'm gonna to let it shine!" and "Hide it under a bushel \[see 11:33\], No! I'm gonna to let it shine!"

Now what is the "light"? Jesus said, ["I am the light of the world: he that followeth me shall not walk in darkness, but shall have the light of life"]{.class_4723}[ ]{.class_4737}(John 8:12). John wrote that Jesus ["was the true Light, which lighteth every man that cometh into the world"]{.class_4723} (John 1:9); he said in his first epistle, ["God is light, and in him is no darkness at all"]{.class_4723} (1 John 1:5).

A Christian has [Christ in him,]{.class_4725} ["the hope of glory"]{.class_4723} (Col. 1:27), so [he too]{.class_4725} is ["the light of the world"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:14). Paul said, ["Ye are all the children of light"]{.class_4723} (1 Thess. 5:5). John said, ["But if we walk in the light, as he is in the light, we have fellowship one with another, and the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin"]{.class_4723} (1 John 1:7). The sinning Christian is commanded to awake from sleep and rise from death and let Christ give him light (Eph. 5:14).

So the light is Christ. In and of himself, the Christian is nothing: he is a blackout. But just as the moon reflects the light of the sun, the Christian is to reflect the light of his Saviour (see our remarks under Song of Sol. 6:10 in that Commentary). You are not to let your [works]{.class_4725} shine before men so they can see Jesus Christ; you are to ["let your LIGHT so shine before men, that they may see your good works"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:16). If you speak up for Jesus Christ, men will pay attention to your works (see our remarks under 6:45).

Verse 17: ["For nothing is secret, that shall not be made manifest; neither any thing hid, that shall not be known and come abroad."]{.class_4723} Jesus said, ["every idle word that men shall speak, they shall give account thereof in the day of judgment"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 12:36). Solomon said, ["For God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether it be good, or whether it be evil"]{.class_4723} (Eccl. 12:14). For the believer, that will be at the Judgment Seat of Christ; for the lost man, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_258}it will be at the White Throne Judgment.

Romans 2:16, ["In the day when God shall judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ according to my gospel."]{.class_4723} That is the unsaved facing Jesus Christ as Judge at the White Throne Judgment.

For the Christian at the Judgment Seat of Christ, look at 1 Corinthians 4:5. ["Therefore judge nothing before the time, until the Lord come, who both will bring to light the hidden things of darkness, and will make manifest the counsels of the hearts: and then shall every man have praise of God."]{.class_4723}

Verse 18:[ "Take heed therefore HOW ye hear."]{.class_4723} The account in Mark says, ["Take heed WHAT ye hear"]{.class_4723} (Mark 4:24). You have to watch out as to WHAT you are listening and HOW you are listening to it. In Mark, Jesus adds this exhortation: ["If any man have ears to hear, let him hear" ]{.class_4723}(Mark 4:23). In the context, He is talking about ["the word of God"]{.class_4723} (vs. 11).

Now, Paul said, ["faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 10:17), but obviously that depends on how you receive that word (1 Thess. 2:13). So look at what Jesus goes on to say: ["for whosoever hath, to him shall be given, and whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken even that which he seemeth to have."]{.class_4723} You read a similar thing in both the Parable of the Talents (Matt. 25:29) and the Parable of the Pounds (Luke 19:26).

The idea is that if you don't believe the words of God and act on them, you'll lose what you were given; if you believe and obey the Scriptures, you will be given more. Back in the Parable of the Sower, the first three hearts lost the word of God and its effects because they didn't receive those words in faith and keep them. The last heart did, and as a result, it had ["abundance"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 13:12). The old song goes: "Trust and obey, for there's no other way to be happy in Jesus, but to trust and obey."

19 Then came to him his mother and his brethren, and could not come at him for the press.

20 And it was told him by certain which said, Thy []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_259}mother and thy brethren stand without, desiring to see thee.

21 And he answered and said unto them, My mother and my brethren are these which hear the word of God, and do it.

We have already commented extensively on Mary under 1:28, 48 (which see), so we will not go into it again here. But notice, again, how "impolite" and "unkind" Jesus Christ could be sometimes (see our remarks on Stewart Custer under 3:8). If Jesus had been "decent" and "nice" to His mother, He would have said, "Tell them I'm busy right now, but I'll be there in a moment," or "Just give me a couple of minutes, and I'll be able to see them." But that's not what He said. He said, "She ain't my momma, and they ain't my family."

Brethren, if Jesus Christ spoke at your Sunday morning service, you wouldn't even recognize Him. One time He's sitting at the dinner table, and the Pharisees ask, "Why didn't your disciples wash their hands before they ate?" Jesus says, "Why didn't you wash your hearts? Please pass the salt" (see Mark 7:5--6). Some Gentile woman comes to Him wanting Him to cast the devil out of her daughter, and He calls her a female dog (Matt. 15:22--26). You call that "polite" or "decent"? A Pharisee comes to Him trying to show Him some respect and give Him the benefit of the doubt concerning His ministry, and Jesus doesn't thank him or engage in any small talk or return the compliment at all. He tells the man, "If you don't get born again, you won't even see the Kingdom of God" (John 3:2--3). Do you see the difference between the Jesus of that Bible and the "Jesus" of the average American Christian---Fundamental or Liberal?

Now there's a reason Jesus is treating His mother the way He does. When I debated the Catholic lawyer Karl Keating out in California, I pointed out that Jesus never called Mary "mother" and would not even acknowledge her as His mother, and an Hispanic man stood up and asked me if Christ disrespected His mother. The answer is no, He did not disrespect His mother. He kept the fifth commandment just as He kept all the commandments. But Jesus was thinking way beyond His own earthly life or the life of Mary. He purposefully [deemphasizes]{.class_4725} Mary's role so you would never elevate []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_260}her to the position of "Queen of Heaven" (see Jer. 44) or "co-redemptrix" with Jesus Christ as Popes John Paul II and Benedict XVI and 900 million Catholics do. In other words, if you honor Mary as the "Mother of God," you have to [step over the words of the Lord Jesus Christ to do it.]{.class_4725}

Second, notice the continued theme of hearing and obeying God's word: ["My mother and my brethren are these which hear the word of God, and do it"]{.class_4723} (vs. 21). Doing the will of God (Mark 3:35) is hearing God's word and doing it. ["The word of God"]{.class_4723} is made up of [words]{.class_4725} (John 8:47). Those words are recorded in a [Book]{.class_4725} where you can [get them;]{.class_4725} they aren't in some lost scraps of paper that no one has ever seen since A.D. 100 (see our remarks under 4:17).

22 Now it came to pass on a certain day, that he went into a ship with his disciples: and he said unto them, Let us go over unto the other side of the lake. And they launched forth.

23 But as they sailed he fell asleep: and there came down a storm of wind on the lake; and they were filled with water, and were in jeopardy.

24 And they came to him, and awoke him, saying, Master, master, we perish. Then he arose, and rebuked the wind and the raging of the water: and they ceased, and there was a calm.

25 And he said unto them, Where is your faith? And they being afraid wondered, saying one to another, What manner of man is this! for he commandeth even the winds and water, and they obey him.

This is the account of Jesus calming the storm, which you can also read in Matthew 8:23--27 and Mark 4:35--41. There are several applications which can be made from the story. The first is obviously that Jesus Christ is God. As the Creator (John 1:1--3), Jesus exercises His authority over the elements. No man can do that. When a hurricane comes in at 120 m.p.h., you can hunker down and ride it out, you can cuss, you can pray, you can get drunk or high to get []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_261}your mind off it, or you can get out of town. But one thing you aren't going to do is stand out there on the beach with a 25-foot storm surge coming in and stop it by speaking to it. There are three things that when they get loose you can't do anything about them but let them run their course: those are wind, water, and fire. But as the One by whom ["all things consist"]{.class_4723} (Col. 1:17), Jesus could take a raging storm and calm it to a breeze with a word.

Next, there is a spiritual application that can be made to the Christian life. That's what the hymn "Master, the Tempest is Raging" is all about. That storm is the troubles we all face in our Christian lives. When the troubles start, what you have to remember is that Jesus Christ is right there in the "boat" with you. If He allows you to go through trouble, it's to get you through it to the other side. Jesus told the disciples, ["Let us go over unto the other side"]{.class_4723} (vs. 22), not "let's go out into the middle of the lake and drown." Remember that when you're in a panic over your trouble, the Lord isn't: Jesus was in the back of that ship fast asleep. And finally, the only one who is going to deliver you out of your trouble is the Lord.

Now look at verse 24: ["And they came to him, and AWOKE him, saying, Master, master, we perish. Then he AROSE."]{.class_4723} Those two words ["awoke"]{.class_4723} and ["arose"]{.class_4723} set the story as a type of Christ's return to rescue the Jews at the Second Advent. Over and over again in the book of Psalms, you read about that Jew in the Tribulation praying for the Lord to wake up and get up, and come down and get him out of ["Jacob's trouble"]{.class_4723} (Jer. 30:7). Here is a small sampling of about two dozen passages:

["Arise, O LORD; save me, O my God: for thou hast smitten all mine enemies upon the cheek bone; thou hast broken the teeth of the ungodly"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 3:7).

["Arise, O LORD, in thine anger, lift up thyself because of the rage of mine enemies: and awake for me to the judgment that thou hast commanded"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 7:6).

["Arise for our help, and redeem us for thy mercies' sake"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 44:26).

["Arise, O God, plead thine own cause: remember how the foolish man reproacheth thee daily. Forget not the voice of thine enemies: the tumult of those that rise up against thee increaseth continually"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_262} (Psa. 74:22--23).

["Then the Lord awaked as one out of sleep, and like a mighty man that shouteth by reason of wine. And he smote his enemies in the hinder parts: he put them to a perpetual reproach" ]{.class_4723}(Psa. 78:65).

["Arise, O God, judge the earth: for thou shalt inherit all nations"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 82:8).

Back to Luke 8:[ "And he said unto them, Where is your faith?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 25). I have to admit, I don't like that. That's the Lord getting upset with the disciples for not having faith. Faith to do what?! Faith to stop a hurricane! They are to believe that is no big deal. It's one thing to be sitting at home in your living room, or in a padded pew at church, reading this, and it's something else to be in the boat with the water coming over the sides faster than you can bail and believe that. ["Where is your faith?"]{.class_4723} I don't know; it went out the window, I guess.

["And they being afraid wondered, saying one to another, What manner of man is this!"]{.class_4723} That's not a question; it's an exclamation. He's a man like no other. He's the God-man, and if He weren't, He was the biggest demon-possessed liar you ever saw.

You take a lying hypocrite like Mohammed: after saying that no man will get to Paradise if he has "the weight of a mustard seed of pride" (or "an atom of pride" or "a grain of pride." Take your pick; those are all official Arabic translations of the ["Holy" Hadith,]{.class_4725} Sahih Muslim, Book 1, Nos. 164--166), the puffed-up egomaniac declared that to obey [him]{.class_4725} was to obey [God]{.class_4725} (Sura 4:80), and that the earth was made for [him]{.class_4725} and God ([Hadith,]{.class_4725} Bukhari, Vol. IV, No. 392). Why, I don't recall Mohammed ever calming a storm or walking on water. Jesus Christ could because He's the one who holds this world together (Col. 1:17).

The psychotic, megalomaniacal nut said, "Allah created the human race and divided it into two groups \[not in the Holy Bible, He didn't; He divided it first into [three]{.class_4725} groups---Shem, Ham, and Japheth---and then into [twelve]{.class_4725}---Deut. 32:8\]. He placed ME \[that's Mohammed speaking of [himself]{.class_4725}\] in the [better]{.class_4725} of the two groups---the Arabs \[the Arabs were [rejected]{.class_4725} in both Testaments---Gen. 21:10; Gal. 4:30\]. Then he made them tribes, and he placed ME among the best---the Quraysh tribe \[the Bible said it was the tribe of [Judah]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_263}---Gen. 48:8--10; 1 Chron. 5:2; Rev. 5:5\]. Then Allah made them houses, and he placed ME in the noblest house and made ME the [best person"]{.class_4725} (Dr. Said Al-Buti, [Fiqh Al-Sira,]{.class_4725} 7th ed. pg. 50; Dr. Bint Al-Shati; [Daughters of the Prophet,]{.class_4725} pg. 14).

That's Mohammed professing to be the best man who ever lived. Nice humble fellow, wasn't he? That's the one of whom the Moslems say, "Peace be upon him." [He's burning in Hell.]{.class_4725} How's that for "peace be upon him"? You say, "According to whom?" [According to his own profession:]{.class_4725} "No man will get into Paradise if he has an atom of pride." Well, what do you call what you just read above? If that isn't pride, then what is it?

When Jesus Christ made a statement like ["I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me"]{.class_4723} (John 14:6), it wasn't bragging; it was a [statement of fact.]{.class_4725} How do you know? [He had the credentials to back it up]{.class_4725} (read John 5). Mohammed "never did nuthin'."

26 And they arrived at the country of the Gadarenes, which is over against Galilee.

27 And when he went forth to land, there met him out of the city a certain man, which had devils long time, and ware no clothes, neither abode in any house, but in the tombs.

28 When he saw Jesus, he cried out, and fell down before him, and with a loud voice said, What have I to do with thee, Jesus, thou Son of God most high? I beseech thee, torment me not.

29 (For he had commanded the unclean spirit to come out of the man. For oftentimes it had caught him: and he was kept bound with chains and in fetters; and he brake the bands, and was driven of the devil into the wilderness.)

30 And Jesus asked him, saying, What is thy name? And he said, Legion: because many devils were entered into him.

31 And they besought him that he would not command them to go out into the deep.

32 And there was there an herd of many swine feeding on the mountain: and they besought him that he would suffer them to enter into them. And he suffered them.

33 Then went the devils out of the man, and entered into the swine: and the herd ran violently down a steep place into the lake, and were choked.

34 When they that fed them saw what was done, they fled, and went and told it in the city and in the country.

35 Then they went out to see what was done; and came to Jesus, and found the man, out of whom the devils were departed, sitting at the feet of Jesus, clothed, and in his right mind: and they were afraid.

36 They also which saw it told them by what means he that was possessed of the devils was healed.

37 Then the whole multitude of the country of the Gadarenes round about besought him to depart from them; for they were taken with great fear: and he went up into the ship, and returned back again.

38 Now the man out of whom the devils were departed besought him that he might be with him: but Jesus sent him away, saying,

39 Return to thine own house, and shew how great things God hath done unto thee. And he went his way, and published throughout the whole city how great things Jesus had done unto him.

40 And it came to pass, that, when Jesus was returned, the people gladly received him: for they were all waiting for him.

This is the story of the maniac of Gadara. If you read the same account in Matthew 8:28, you find out there were [two]{.class_4725} of them; Mark and Luke go into detail on how Jesus dealt with [one]{.class_4725} of them.

This is the textbook case on demonology in the Scriptures; that is, it has all the basic essentials you need to know about "demons" (see our comments under 3:22, 4:33). The [King James]{.class_4725} word is ["devils"]{.class_4723} to show you that all "demons" are part of [Satan]{.class_4725} (see vss. 5, 12). The old Fundamentalist saying is: "There is only one Devil but []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_265}many demons." Listen, there is one Lord but many lords; there is one God but many gods (1 Cor. 8:5--6). There is one Son of God but many sons of God (John 1:12); there is one Angel of the Lord (Acts 27:23) but many angels of the Lord (Luke 2:13). Given all that, what makes you think there would only be one devil (see John 6:70 and comments in that Commentary)?

Now, the account gives all the characteristics of demon possession. Look at verse 27: ["And when he went forth to land, there met him out of the city a certain man, which had devils long time, and ware no clothes."]{.class_4723} Demon possession is connected with [nakedness.]{.class_4725} You know, like Pensacola Beach (or Malibu or Daytona, etc.) in the Summer with everyone running around taking their clothes off. You know what I mean, jelly bean? The first thing God did for Adam and Eve when they sinned was to put clothes on them (Gen. 3:21). When the maniac got right, do you know what he did? He put some clothes on (vs. 35). When you die as a Christian, do you know what God does for you? He puts some clothes on you (2 Cor. 5:2--3).

When Noah got drunk and was violated by his son, he got naked (Gen. 9:21--24). When the children of Israel got to dancing around the golden calf, they got naked (Exod. 32:25). Nakedness is connected to sexual perversion (Lev. 18). So nakedness is a sign of Satanic control over the flesh.

["Neither abode in any house, but in the tombs."]{.class_4723} He lived in a graveyard; he had a preoccupation with death. The young people today are hung up on [vampires;]{.class_4725} they're in the movies, in the paperback novels, and on the TV. It's not like Dracula anymore; the vampires are young, handsome, and romantic. That's Satan making death look desirable. That's why you have teenagers going around dressed in black with their faces painted white and their lips painted red ("Goths"). Back in Proverbs 8 where Jesus Christ is likened to wisdom (see our remarks under 7:35), the Bible says, ["But he that sinneth against me wrongeth his own soul: all they that hate me LOVE DEATH"]{.class_4723} (Prov. 8:36).

Why hang around dead people? ["Why seek ye the living among the dead?"]{.class_4723} (24:5). Young people go by a pool hall or dance hall or a rock concert or a bar and think, "Boy, that's living!" No, that's [dying;]{.class_4725} those places are full of [dead people]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_266} (9:60). A lover of the pleasures of this world is a [zombie:]{.class_4725} he or she is a [dead]{.class_4725} person walking around like he or she is [living]{.class_4725} (1 Tim. 5:6).

We have already commented on how all devils are Fundamentalists (vs. 28) under 4:34 (which see). Belief in one God (James 2:19) or the Deity of Christ does not make you a Christian; it could just mean you're full of the devil.

["For oftentimes it had caught him: and he was kept bound with chains and in fetters; and he brake the bands"]{.class_4723} (vs. 29). [Supernatural strength]{.class_4725} is a mark of demon possession. Now I know there are all kinds of things that can give a person extraordinary strength. We're not talking about normal bodybuilding here like the Christian weightlifter Paul Anderson. When you see people like that lift an elephant on a platform or pull a train engine with their teeth, they have worked out and built up their bodies and trained themselves to do that. That's not demon possession. Insane people in the asylum can display great strength, and that's closer to what's going on here, but not all insanity is due to, or connected with, demon possession. When you get a kid hyped up on a drug like "angel dust" (PCP) having to have four or five men to hold him down, that's really close to demon possession because the drugs open a person up to spiritual influence (see our remarks on [sorcery]{.class_4725} under Rev. 9:21 in that Commentary).

Mark adds a couple of details that Luke leaves out of his account. Mark 5:5 says first of all that ["he was in the mountains."]{.class_4723} In the Old Testament, those are called ["high places"]{.class_4723} (1 Kings 3:2; 2 Chron. 33:17). They start out on Mt. Ararat when Noah comes out of the ark and sacrifices to God (Gen. 8:20--21). Abraham built an altar to the Lord on a mountain between Bethel and Ai (Gen. 12:8). Of course, that is before the Law, under grace, by the patriarchs. Among the heathen, the ["high places"]{.class_4723} became connected with idolatry (Num. 22:41, 33:52; Jer. 48:35), so under the Law, the Lord commanded the Jews to destroy all the high places ["upon the high mountains, and upon the hills"]{.class_4723} and to sacrifice in only one place which He would choose (Deut. 12:1--6). That's a picture of Jesus Christ and His one effectual sacrifice.

Demon-possessed people have an affinity for high places because Satan himself wanted to ["ascend into heaven"]{.class_4723} and []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_267}["sit also upon the mount of the congregation"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 14:13). Satan is called ["the prince of the power of the air"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 2:2), and the Christian fights ["against spiritual wickedness in high places"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 6:12).

Next, Mark says this demon-possessed man was ["in the tombs, crying, and cutting himself with stones"]{.class_4723} (Mark 5:5). There are two more marks of demon possession: excessive crying and self-mutilation. In the Old Testament, the priests of Baal ["cried aloud, and cut themselves AFTER THEIR MANNER with knives and lancets till the blood gushed out upon them"]{.class_4723} (1 Kings 18:25). Those are religious leaders who are full of the devil. You can see the same thing in the Philippines and Spain around Easter. In Madrid and Barcelona, the Catholics dress in burlap sacks and crawl up steps on their knees until they are scratched and bruised and bleeding in order to atone for their sins; in Manila, they reenact the Crucifixion by whipping themselves down the street and being tied (or nailed!) to crosses. What is that? That's [Satanic;]{.class_4725} it's not "Christian." That's a bunch of Catholics who are just crawling with demons.

Listen, people, your blood ain't worth a dime. You die because your blood is no good. That's why your daddy, your granddaddy, your great granddaddy, and your ancestors all the way back to Adam died: their blood was no good. If the Lord tarries, that's why your children will die also. You could shed every drop of blood in your body to pay for your sins, and you'll still wind up in Hell. You need a blood transfusion from someone who didn't get His blood from Adam (Acts 20:28; 1 John 1:7).

["And Jesus asked him, saying, What is thy name? And he said, Legion: because many devils were entered into him"]{.class_4736} (vs. 30). A Roman legion had an average of 6,000 men in it. This guy doesn't have 6,000 demons in him, but he sure has a mess of them. The demons in this man and his buddy (Matt. 8:28) end up possessing 2,000 pigs (Mark 5:13); that would be at least 1,000 demons in each man.

Now, we have already discussed that unclean spirits are like winged creatures under 3:22 (which see). In someone like Mary []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_268}Magdalene who had seven in her (vs. 2), they would be the size of birds (Rev. 18:2), but for someone like the maniac, who had a thousand in him, they would have to be much smaller, the size of flies or mosquitoes. Do you know what Satan is called in Luke 11:15? He's ["Beelzebub."]{.class_4723} Beelzebub means "lord of FLIES," or "lord of [filth."]{.class_4725}[ ]{.class_4723}A fly is an [unclean flying creature]{.class_4725} picturing [a devil.]{.class_4725}

["And they besought him that he would not command them to go out into the deep"]{.class_4723} (vs. 31). The deep there has nothing to do with the Sea of Galilee or the Mediterranean or the Atlantic or any other body of water on this earth. How do you know that is so? Because in the next two verses the demons leave the man and go into a herd of hogs, and they all run down into the sea. This ["deep"]{.class_4723} first shows up in Genesis 1:2, and it is separated from the waters on earth by [outer space]{.class_4725} (Gen. 1:6--8, 14--16). Look at Psalm 148:3--4.

["Praise ye him, SUN and MOON: praise him, all ye STARS of light. Praise him, ye HEAVENS OF HEAVENS]{.class_4723} \[where the sun, moon, and stars are\][, and YE WATERS THAT BE ABOVE THE HEAVENS."]{.class_4723}

Then it has nothing to do with clouds or water vapor in the atmosphere. The deep is a body of water [above]{.class_4725} the sun, moon, and stars. Do you know what John sees when he gets caught up to Heaven? He sees ["a SEA of glass"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 4:6). It is described as ["glass"]{.class_4723} because ["the face of the deep is frozen"]{.class_4723} (Job 38:30).

The devils don't want to go to the frozen deep because they have an affinity for [warm, wet]{.class_4725} places. The ["dumb spirit"]{.class_4723} in Mark 9:17--22 cast the boy he possessed in the [fire]{.class_4725} and in the [water]{.class_4725} (Mark 9:22). When a demon leaves a man, Jesus said it wanders in ["dry places"]{.class_4723} and then tries to return to a human body (11:24). Why? Because a human body is [warm]{.class_4725} (97.6 degrees) and [wet]{.class_4725} (75% of the human body is [water]{.class_4725}). The final place the devils wind up is in a [lake]{.class_4725} of [fire.]{.class_4725} So when the demons leave the maniac and inhabit the pigs, they head for a [wet ]{.class_4725}place: ["the herd ran violently down a steep place into the lake and were choked"]{.class_4723} (vs. 33).

The keepers of the hogs fled into the city to tell everyone what had happened, and when the people came out to Jesus, they found the maniac sitting at Jesus' feet ["clothed, and in his right mind"]{.class_4723} []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_269}(vss. 34--35). You would think the reaction would be: "Praise God! look at that. We don't have to worry about him tearing around the countryside anymore screaming, yelling, throwing rocks, and cutting himself. We can walk by the cemetery and not worry about being attacked. We don't have to worry about this fellow grabbing our kids and doing something to them. Isn't it wonderful that guy got converted!"

But that's not the reaction. They just lost 2,000 head of hog, and with pork at \$3.00 a pound, they were out a fortune! They're scared stiff that Jesus Christ is going to take a lot more from them (see Matt. 19:21--22).

I remember one time preaching with Lester Roloff in a meeting over in Jacksonville. One woman came out of the church saying, "This is terrible; this is just terrible!" "What's terrible?" I asked. "Well," she said, "Lester Roloff has taken our beer, our cigarettes, our TVs, and our red meat from us; and you've taken our other versions of the Bible from us. We haven't got anything left!" "It sure beats going to Hell, don't it?" I replied. [Yeah, it sure does.]{.class_4725}

The whole region of Gadara (vs. 37) would rather have two demon-possessed maniacs running around terrorizing the community than have Jesus Christ saving folks. Why? Because He threatened their livelihood (\$\$\$).

That's why America isn't about to have a revival or anything close to it. For fifty years every Southern Baptist church in this country held "simultaneous revivals." All the churches would pray for revival and hold revival services, and no revival ever came on a national scale. Billy Graham has held evangelistic campaigns all over the country, and a lot of folks made professions of faith, yet no revival broke out in communities like you read of in the days of D. L. Moody or Sam Jones or Billy Sunday. Why not? Because every time the churches prayed "Lord, send a revival; send a great revival to my soul, etc.," the Holy Spirit would say, "All right, but if I do, then this has to go and that has to go." The Christians look at those things and say, "I just can't live without them," and revival passes them by.

["In his right mind."]{.class_4723} Paul wrote, ["For God hath not given us the spirit of fear; but of power, and of love, and of a SOUND MIND"]{.class_4723} (2 Tim. 1:2). Before I was saved, I was smoking, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_270}drinking, cussing, and fornicating. Then I got saved, and the Holy Spirit took away my cigars, my beer, my dirty women, and (eventually) cleaned up my speech. I went to witness to my momma and daddy up in Delaware, and they thought I had gone crazy like my brother. That has happened to a lot of folks who have gotten saved. Friends and relatives think you have lost your mind because you start talking about God, Jesus Christ, and the Bible. That's when you started getting some sense, man. ["The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom: and the knowledge of the holy is understanding"]{.class_4723} (Prov. 9:10).

The delivered maniac wanted to go with Jesus when He left (vs. 38), but Jesus told him, ["Return to thine own house, and shew how great things God hath done unto thee"]{.class_4723} (vs. 39). Witness, witness, witness! When a Catholic gets saved, the first thing that occurs to him is: "I was a Catholic, and I was deceived and on my way to Hell. If I was deceived all those years, momma and daddy could be to. I need to tell them." That's how I felt when I got saved.

You should start out witnessing to your family (vs. 39), then your friends (Mark 5:19). After that, you should follow the pattern laid down for you in Acts 1:8. You should witness to your hometown (your ["Jerusalem"]{.class_4723}), then move on to your region or State (your ["Judaea"]{.class_4723}), then neighboring regions (your ["Samaria"]{.class_4723}), and finally ["the uttermost part of the earth."]{.class_4723} You keep going until the Lord stops you, and don't worry, He will stop you where He wants you. You let the Lord worry about the place; you just start witnessing and keep going until He tells you to stop. The problem is that some of you haven't [started]{.class_4725} yet.

["And he went his way, and published throughout the whole city how great things JESUS had done unto him."]{.class_4723} Notice the substitution of ["Jesus"]{.class_4723} for ["God"]{.class_4723} back in the first part of the verse---an obvious reference to the Deity of the Lord Jesus Christ. Mark adds, ["and all men did marvel."]{.class_4723} I imagine they did.

Can you imagine that man coming home to his wife and kids? They see that fellow coming down the walkway, and they lock all the doors so he can't get in. Then the wife peeks out the window shades and sees her husband fully clothed and calm as can be. She opens the door just a crack and says, "Is that you? What happened to you?" []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_271}That guy stands there with his hands clasped in front of him and the tears streaming down his face, and he says, "I got saved, honey." The door flies open, and she's in his arms, and both are weeping and laughing at the same time. All the kids come out and dance around momma and daddy and smother them in a group hug.

I saw that one time up in Spartanburg, South Carolina. I had just finished preaching on the street when suddenly a rough-looking character in his fifties ran up to me. He took a half-filled bottle of whiskey out of his coat pocket and threw it on the ground. It didn't bounce twice before some other "sneaky Pete" picked it up and ran off with it.

That fellow stood two feet from my face and said, "I'm saved now, right?"

"I don't know. Are you?" I replied.

"You said if I would trust the shed blood of Jesus Christ to pay for my sins He would save me."

"Sure, I said that, and I meant every word of it."

"Well I just did that," he said. "I asked Jesus to save me. So am I saved?"

"If you meant it, that will do it," I replied. "Jesus said, ['him that cometh unto me I will in no wise cast out']{.class_4723} (John 6:37)."

That fellow had just gotten out of prison where he had served fifteen years for second degree murder. His wife had divorced him, and he was headed to where she lived with her daughter and son-in-law to kill her. He hauled out a .38 revolver and unloaded all five bullets and gave them to me. Now that he was saved, he wanted me to drive him to his wife so he could tell her.

When we arrived at the house, I stayed in the car while he went up and knocked on the door. The wife wasn't there, but the daughter and her husband were. They wouldn't open the screen door; he had to stand there and talk to them through it. He stood there bawling, wiping his face with his sleeve, and pretty soon the screen door opened. After a while longer, the couple stepped out on the porch with him. Then his daughter was embracing him, and when I left, all three of them were kneeling on the porch praying.

A week later, I came back to Spartanburg to preach, and there was my new "babe in Christ" on the street corner. He had a big Sears []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_272}and Roebuck pulpit Bible laying open on his left arm while he had some poor sinner up against a wall preaching the Hell fire out of him. Like they say up in the hills of North Carolina: "I wouldn't take a purty for that one."

Verse 40 is Jesus returning to Capernaum (Matt. 9:1). Note the contrast between the reception Christ received here and the one in verse 37 in Gadara. But as we will see in 10:15, this is the superficial reception of a celebrity. It is like the stony ground of the Parable of the Sower (vss. 6, 13): a lot of emotion but no real repentance and faith.

41 And, behold, there came a man named Jairus, and he was a ruler of the synagogue: and he fell down at Jesus' feet, and besought him that he would come into his house:

42 For he had one only daughter, about twelve years of age, and she lay a dying. But as he went the people thronged him.

43 And a woman having an issue of blood twelve years, which had spent all her living upon physicians, neither could be healed of any,

44 Came behind him, and touched the border of his garment: and immediately her issue of blood stanched.

45 And Jesus said, Who touched me? When all denied, Peter and they that were with him said, Master, the multitude throng thee and press thee, and sayest thou, Who touched me?

46 And Jesus said, Somebody hath touched me: for I perceive that virtue is gone out of me.

47 And when the woman saw that she was not hid, she came trembling, and falling down before him, she declared unto him before all the people for what cause she had touched him, and how she was healed immediately.

48 And he said unto her, Daughter, be of good comfort: thy faith hath made thee whole; go in peace.

Among the crowd of verse 40, two people stand out. The first is ["a man named Jairus\...a ruler of the synagogue"]{.class_4723} (vs. 41). He []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_273}comes to Jesus because he has a twelve-year-old daughter who is near death, and he wants Christ to heal her before it's too late. The other is ["a woman having an issue of blood twelve years"]{.class_4723} (vs. 43). That means the whole time that little girl was alive, this woman was sick with this infirmity. The number [twelve]{.class_4725} undoubtedly marks these ladies out as prophetic types of Israel, although I am not sure as to the exact application.

Note how Jairus ["fell down at Jesus' feet"]{.class_4723} (vs. 40); Matthew says he ["worshipped him"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 9:18). So one of the acts of worship in the Bible is to bow in submission and honor to someone or something (see Mark 15:19). That explains why the three Hebrew children wouldn't bow to Nebuchadnezzar's image (Dan. 3:6); it also explains why Mordecai wouldn't bow to Haman (Esth. 3:2). Those were cases where men were seeking the honor and reverence that belonged to God alone. Jesus Christ did not hesitate to accept worship (Matt. 8:12, 14:33, 15:25; John 9:38). So if Jesus were not God in the flesh, then the priests and Rabbis of His day were right: He was a blasphemer who deserved to die (Matt. 26:65--66).

["And a woman having an issue of blood twelve years, which had spent all her living upon physicians, neither could be healed of any"]{.class_4723} (vs. 43). A practitioner of "alternative medicine" once told me that all most doctors know how to do is [cut]{.class_4725} you, [burn]{.class_4725} you, or [poison]{.class_4725} you. Nevertheless, not all doctors are bad (take Luke for example---Col. 4:14), and Jesus recommended them (Matt. 9:12). What you have to keep in mind is that you shouldn't rely on them [alone]{.class_4725} (2 Chron. 16:12). When you're sick, you [trust God first]{.class_4725} and then see a doctor if you need to.

For verse 44, we refer you to our remarks on 6:19. The word ["stanched"]{.class_4723} in the verse means the blood stopped flowing.

["And Jesus said, Who touched me?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 45). The "touch" is much more than merely touching, for Peter makes the obvious observation: ["the multitude throng thee and press thee, and sayest thou, Who touched me?"]{.class_4723} In other words, everybody is "touching" you; what do you mean who's touching you? The woman's touch is one of [purposeful intent:]{.class_4725} she wants to be healed. It is one of [faith:]{.class_4725} she believes that if she touches Him she can be healed (Matt. 9:21). So her "touch" was different than anyone else's in the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_274}crowd, and Jesus can tell that.

Verses 45--46 is one of those instances in the Gospels where Jesus does not exercise His full omniscience. There is no doubt that Jesus could have picked the woman out of the crowd if He had wanted to do so (see John 1:48). But the Lord is giving the woman the opportunity to come forward on her own so He can deal with her personally. This affords her the chance to testify openly as to what Jesus had done for her.

In verse 48, Jesus tells the woman in other terms basically what He told the wicked woman who anointed Him back in 7:50---["thy faith hath made thee whole; go in peace."]{.class_4723}

The "serious student of the Bible" (to borrow a hackneyed cliché from the apostate Fundamentalists) should mark the difference between faith in the Old Testament and the Gospels from faith in the Pauline Epistles. In the Gospels it is ["THY faith"]{.class_4723} (here and 7:50); in the Old Testament it is ["HIS faith"]{.class_4723} (Hab. 2:4). But when you get to the Pauline Epistles, that ["his" ]{.class_4723}is dropped (see our comments on Rom. 1:17 and Hab. 2:4 in those respective Commentaries). Why is that? Because the[ "faith"]{.class_4723} is no longer yours; it is a gift from God.

["For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that ]{.class_4723}\[the antecedent is ["faith"]{.class_4723}\] [NOT OF YOURSELVES]{.class_4723} \[the faith is [not yours. ]{.class_4725}Well, if it isn't your faith, whose faith is it?\][: it ]{.class_4723}\[the antecedent is still ["faith"]{.class_4723}\] [is the GIFT OF GOD"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 2:8).

In the Old Testament, the faith is [personal;]{.class_4725} that is, the Old Testament saints believed what God told them personally to do and DID it---[faith and works.]{.class_4725} That's what you read all through Hebrews 11. Nobody in that chapter trusted in the finished work of Jesus Christ for salvation, not even when they were types of Christ's sacrifice like Abel.

In the New Testament, the faith is in what was DONE FOR YOU. That's why the New Testament Gospel in 1 Corinthians 15:1--4 is [not]{.class_4725} the ["gospel of the kingdom"]{.class_4723} like it is in the Gospels (Matt. 4:23, 9:35, 24:14; Mark 1:14); it is ["the gospel of the grace of God"]{.class_4723} (Acts 20:24) and ["the gospel of Christ"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 1:16, 15:19, 29; 1 Cor. 9:12, 19; 2 Cor. 4:4, 9:13, 10:14; Gal. 1:7; etc.). It's not "the King is coming"; it's [the Saviour died and rose again.]{.class_4725}

God gives you the faith to believe in Christ's atonement when you []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_275}hear the Gospel preached from the word of God (Rom. 10:14--17). Of course, it's up to you what you do with the gift (Rom. 10:9--13)---you can either take it or leave it---but you have been given the gift nonetheless. It's right there in your heart and mouth waiting for you to do something about it (Rom. 10:8). If you don't get saved, you have no one to blame but [yourself,]{.class_4725} because God has given you enough faith to believe the Gospel.

49 While he yet spake, there cometh one from the ruler of the synagogue's house, saying to him, Thy daughter is dead; trouble not the Master.

50 But when Jesus heard it, he answered him, saying, Fear not: believe only, and she shall be made whole.

51 And when he came into the house, he suffered no man to go in, save Peter, and James, and John, and the father and the mother of the maiden.

52 And all wept, and bewailed her: but he said, Weep not; she is not dead, but sleepeth.

53 And they laughed him to scorn, knowing that she was dead.

54 And he put them all out, and took her by the hand, and called, saying, Maid, arise.

55 And her spirit came again, and she arose straightway: and he commanded to give her meat.

56 And her parents were astonished: but he charged them that they should tell no man what was done.

Now in verse 42, the girl was dying, but she wasn't dead yet; in verse 49, Jairus received word that his daughter was now dead. This seems to contradict what Matthew wrote in chapter 9, verse 18 of his Gospel, which reads: ["My daughter is EVEN NOW dead: but come and lay thy hand upon her, and she shall live."]{.class_4723} But as we have seen in the account of the maniac of Gadara (and later with the healing of blind Bartimaeus), Matthew gives a synopsis of an event while Mark and Luke often go into much greater detail.

The message of the daughter's death in Mark sheds some light on what Matthew wrote: ["Thy daughter is dead: why troublest thou the Master ANY FURTHER"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_276} (Mark 5:35). One may infer from those last two words that Jairus continued to beg Jesus to come and raise his daughter from the dead, which is why Matthew gave the account as he did.

The Lord's statement in verse 50 is quite a thing to accept if it were [your]{.class_4725} daughter who had just died. Of course, Jairus knew from the Old Testament that it was [possible,]{.class_4725} (see 1 Kings 17:17--22; 2 Kings 4:32--37), but it was quite something else for him to have to apply the passages to himself.

It is Christ's "inner circle" (Peter, James, and John) who accompany Jesus into the house (vs. 51), and this represents one of the three levels of fruit-bearing which we learned about in the Parable of the Sower (see our comments on vs. 15).

The word ["suffered"]{.class_4723} has an archaic usage in the verse which means "allowed." The "solution" to this "problem" is to revise or retranslate the Bible so it can be "understood in plain language." Of course it doesn't do any good. You have more Biblical illiteracy with the advent of the [RSV, NASV, NIV, NKJV, CEV, ESV,]{.class_4725} etc., than you previously had in 350 years of an "archaic" Book from 1611. But more importantly, you pay a terrible price for getting rid of the "archaic" words. You remove the Deity of Christ from 1 Timothy 3:16; you get rid of the Trinity from 1 John 5:7; you dump the Virgin Birth from Luke 2:33; etc. I won't pay the price; I leave the text alone. When we came out with the [Ruckman Reference Bible,]{.class_4725} I gave the definitions of archaic words in the margin where they belong and left what the Holy Spirit put in the text alone.

Verse 52: ["And all wept, and bewailed her."]{.class_4723} These are professional mourners and musicians who have come in for the funeral (Matt. 9:23). Mark describes what those people are doing as a ["tumult"]{.class_4723} (Mark 9:38); it is the mark of a lost [pagan]{.class_4725} who has no idea where he is going when he dies. If you want to see the scene played out before your eyes, find any newscast or documentary on a Moslem funeral. When the Ayatollah Khomeini died in Iran many years back, the mourners not only screamed and cried hysterically, they [tore the body apart]{.class_4725} trying to get some piece of it as a "relic." Paul says Christians are not to be sorrowful in the same manner as ["others which have no hope"]{.class_4723} (1 Thess. 4:13).

["But he said, Weep not; she is not dead, but sleepeth."]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_277} Of course, the mourners ["laughed him to scorn, knowing that she was dead"]{.class_4723} (vs. 53). That is, they laughed at Jesus in mockery and ridicule because He said the girl was asleep. Of course, you have to look at things from the Lord's point of view. From the Lord's perspective, death is a sleep from which you will someday be awakened.
