["And many of them that sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt"]{.class_4723} (Dan. 12:1).

Every Jew in the Gospels believed in a general resurrection at the "Last Day" (John 11:24 cf. Job 19:25--26). What Jesus told Martha when He was about to raise Lazarus from the dead was that if you believed in Him you were going to come up [before]{.class_4725} the Last Day, and if you were alive when He came back, you would ["never die"]{.class_4723} (John 11:25--26). So if you ["sleep IN JESUS,"]{.class_4723} you get caught up with the living saints at the Rapture of the Church (1 Thess. 4:14--17). This is a ["comfort"]{.class_4723} for the Christian at the time of death (1 Thess. 4:18) and a ["blessed hope"]{.class_4723} during life (Tit. 2:13).

The Lord puts out all the unbelieving mourners in verse 54 and keeps Peter, James, and John, and the father and mother (Mark 5:40). Mark also adds a detail not found here in Luke: he tells you the language Jesus is speaking when He raises the girl. ["Talitha cumi"]{.class_4723} is Aramaic for ["Maid]{.class_4723} \[or ["Damsel"]{.class_4723}---Mark 5:41\][, arise"]{.class_4723} (vs. 54).

This has led the Catholics and the higher critics to invent some non-existent "Q document" in Aramaic from which Matthew, Mark, and Luke were all supposed to have copied. The Catholics hold to the theory so they can make Peter the ["rock"]{.class_4723} on which the Church (i.e., the [Catholic]{.class_4725} Church) is built (see our comments on Matt. 16:18 in that Commentary); the Liberals love the theory because it does away with the divine inspiration of the Gospels.

Of course, the theory has no more substance than Darwin's evolution or Hort's "Lucian recension" or Santa Claus coming down the chimney. Why insert Aramaisms into your Greek text when you are supposed to be [translating]{.class_4725} an Aramaic document? As we saw back in 1:2, the Gospel writers are giving eyewitness accounts of Jesus' life and ministry, so from time to time, they insert the actual []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_278}words in the language in which they were originally spoken to authenticate for their audience that what is written is actual history and not some myth or legend like the stories of the Greek and Roman gods.

We have already commented on verse 55 in our remarks on 7:11--17. Here we will only add one further reference for you to consider.

["But if the Spirit of him that raised up Jesus from the dead dwell in you, he that raised up Christ from the dead shall also quicken your mortal bodies by his Spirit that dwelleth in you"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:11).

Do you know what causes a balloon to rise instead of fall to the ground like a rock? [It has something in it to make it go up.]{.class_4725} When the Lord comes back to take all those who ["sleep in Jesus" ]{.class_4723}home to Heaven, do you know what gets them out of the grave? [They have something ]{.class_4725}(i.e., [Someone]{.class_4725}) [in them that makes them go up just like it made Jesus Christ go up.]{.class_4725}

Verse 56: ["And her parents were astonished: but he charged them that they should tell no man what was done."]{.class_4723} This is similar to what Jesus told the leper back in 5:14 (see our comments on the verse), and it is for the same reason. Well, you know how long that lasted (see 5:15). I dare say with the Phoenicians from Tyre and Sidon there (see 6:17) that the story probably made it to Spain (and possibly England) before Christ died.

You sit here up in the Twenty-first Century reading the story, and for the most part it's just dead history. But if you put yourself in the shoes of someone who lived in that day, what do you think you would have done if you saw a man get out of the grave after four days (John 11)? Why, the news spread like wildfire, man, and those Phoenician ships went all over the Mediterranean all the way to the Straits of Gibraltar and up the coast of France. I guarantee you there was plenty of scuttlebutt among those sailors about the miracle worker from Galilee.

Paul told Festus when he made his case before Agrippa that ["this thing was not done in a corner"]{.class_4723} (Acts 26:26). In Acts 2, the news of Christ's resurrection went from Rome in the west all the way to Persia in the east, and all over northern Africa and Asia Minor []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1700.xhtml_page_279}(Acts 2:9--11). The problem is not with the people in the Gospels Christ told not to tell but did anyway; it's the ones He has told to tell today and who keep their mouths shut (see our comments on 6:45).

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml}

# Chapter 9 {.heading_78-0}

9:1 Then he called his twelve disciples together, and gave them power and authority over all devils, and to cure diseases.

2 And he sent them to preach the kingdom of God, and to heal the sick.

3 And he said unto them, Take nothing for your journey, neither staves, nor scrip, neither bread, neither money; neither have two coats apiece.

4 And whatsoever house ye enter into, there abide, and thence depart.

5 And whosoever will not receive you, when ye go out of that city, shake off the very dust from your feet for a testimony against them.

6 And they departed, and went through the towns, preaching the gospel, and healing every where.

This is the commissioning of the disciples to the apostolic ministry (see Matt. 10). Note the two things Christ gives them in verse 1: ["POWER and AUTHORITY."]{.class_4723} The reason this is so important is that the Greek word for ["authority"]{.class_4723} here is εξουσια, which was translated in John 1:12 as ["power"]{.class_4723} by the [King James]{.class_4725} translators---["to them gave he POWER]{.class_4723} \[εξουσια\] [to become the sons of God."]{.class_4723}

At this point, all the apostate Fundamentalists and Conservatives come unglued. "The [King James]{.class_4725} is inconsistent in its translating; we need uniformity in translating. Blah, blah, blah, blah, blah." You will even hear Bob Jones Sr. make the change on his radio program. "Old man Bob" was a [King James]{.class_4725} man from the crown of his head to the soles of his feet, but once in a while he would consult one of his faculty members about a verse (in this case either Brokenshire or Brunner), and he would give him some "help" from the original language. That's what happened in John 1:12. Of course, if you listen to that broadcast all the way through, you will hear where he "backslides" and quotes the [King James.]{.class_4725}

The truth is that [no translation]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_281} consistently translates a word the same way every time. You take the [New American Standard Version:]{.class_4725} after changing ["power"]{.class_4723} to ["authority"]{.class_4725} in John 1:12 to have "uniformity in translating," it translates εξουσι[α]{.class_4752} as ["power"]{.class_4725} nine times in the New Testament (Luke 22:53; Eph. 2:2, 6:12; Rev. 9:3, 10, 11:6, 14:18, 16:9, 20:6. The first five are a reference to [the Devil]{.class_4725}---see [New Age Bible Versions]{.class_4725}). You never met a bigger bunch of lying hypocrites outside of Islam and the Catholic Church; alongside them the Mafia are fine folks.

Now, outside of the fact that the word [power]{.class_4725} can mean "political or governmental authority" (as in a "POWER-mad dictator"; see how the word is used in Rom. 13:1--4) is the fact that becoming a child of God has nothing to do with any "right" or "authority." If "all men are [endowed]{.class_4725} by their Creator with certain inalienable rights" (Declaration of Independence), then why don't all men [have]{.class_4725} those rights? It's because they don't have the [power]{.class_4725} to exercise the "rights" or "authority" they supposedly have been given. Some men have power with no authority to back it up, and some men have authority with no power to enforce it.

You can be given all the legal authority you want to become a son of God, and it won't do you any good if you don't have the [power]{.class_4725} to actually accomplish it. It takes the power of God that raised Jesus Christ from the dead to pass you from death to life (Acts 26:18 cf. John 5:24) and give you the New Birth (John 3:3--7); only that resurrection power can make you a son of God like Jesus Christ (Eph. 3:19--20; 1 John 3:1--2; Phil. 3:20--21; Rom. 8:11--29).

Back to Luke 9: ["And he sent them to preach the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (vs. 2). The difference between the apostolic commission of Matthew 10 and the one here in Luke 9 is due to the [message preached.]{.class_4725} The apostles are commanded in Matthew 10:9 to preach: ["The kingdom of heaven is at hand."]{.class_4723} That is, they are to preach a literal, visible, physical kingdom (see our remarks under 6:20). As a consequence, they are to preach only ["to the lost sheep of the house of Israel"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 10:6). The message here in Luke 9 is ["the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (a spiritual kingdom); hence, the restrictions against preaching to the Gentiles and Samaritans (Matt. 10:5) are missing from the commission in Luke.

Verse 3 is another good example of how trying to apply material in the Gospels [doctrinally]{.class_4725} to the New Testament Church doesn't work. The death of Christ [changed]{.class_4725} the instructions to the apostles (see 22:35--36). Some of the brethren forget that when dealing with Christ's words in Matthew 10:8---["freely ye have received, freely give"]{.class_4723}; the idea being that since you got salvation as a free gift (Rom. 5:18), that it is a sin to sell a Bible, or to make a profit at a Christian bookstore so you will have money to pay your bills, or to copyright your material. All that is just an excuse to [steal]{.class_4725} other people's work because they are too lazy to put in the study or they want to claim it as their own so they will get the credit for it. None of them practice anything else in Matthew 10:5, 6, 7, 8, 9, or 10.

What Jesus is saying in verses 3--4 is that the apostles are to let those who believe their preaching provide for their daily needs: money, food, clothing, and shelter. The New Testament principle is found in 1 Corinthians 9: note especially verses 11 and 14.

"If we have sown unto you spiritual things, is it a great thing if we shall reap your carnal things?\...Even so hath the Lord ordained that they which preach the gospel should live of the gospel."

The command to shake off the dust of a city that rejects Christ (vs. 5) is nowhere repeated in the Pauline Epistles, but Paul and Barnabas practice it in Acts 13:51. Of course, those are two [Jewish]{.class_4725} apostles doing it against the [Jews]{.class_4725} in a city who oppose the preaching of the Gospel to Gentiles who received it (Acts 13:48--50), so it is a peculiarly [Jewish]{.class_4725} practice.

There is an interesting story from early American history that is connected with verse 5. Back in the early 1800s, there was an itinerant Methodist evangelist named Lorenzo Dow. Dow was quite a character. Once he was preaching in Jacksonborough, Georgia, and some rowdies from the local saloon came to disrupt the meeting. They heckled him and pelted him with rotten eggs. Dow followed them back to the saloon and began giving them thunder and lightning stewed down to a fine poison; he even busted open a keg of beer. That did it! The whole town turned against him except one man, a fellow named Seavorn Goodall. A mob formed and ran him out of town. At the edge of town, Dow stopped, took off his shoes, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_283}and beat the dust off them. He then cursed that town for rejecting the Gospel. Over the next thirty years, every building in town was destroyed by mysterious fires and gusts of wind, and by General Sherman's army when they came through Georgia. The only building left standing in the entire town was the place where Lorenzo Dow had stayed: the Goodall house. That's one of those instances where the Lord honored a fellow for believing His word even though the passage of Scripture he claimed may not have been aimed directly at him doctrinally.

["Shake off the very dust from your feet for a TESTIMONY AGAINST THEM"]{.class_4723} (vs. 5). ["A testimony against them"]{.class_4723} when? ["In the day of judgment"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 10:15); Jesus said, ["It shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom and Gomorrha\...than for that city."]{.class_4723} The cities that get singled out in the next chapter are ["Chorazin," "Bethsaida,"]{.class_4723} and ["Capernaum"]{.class_4723} (10:12--15). Why? Because they had light and advantages that Sodom and Gomorrah didn't have.

The same thing can be said about the United States. When the axe falls in America, it's going to fall hard. Bob Jones Sr. said, "God won't put up with this country what He puts up with from other countries." Why not? Because America started out right. She started out with God, Jesus Christ, and the Bible. America has had more Gospel preached in it and more churches in it than any other country on earth, and now she has traded it in for a mess of humanistic, sentimental, Socialistic, hedonistic [slop.]{.class_4725}

["And they departed, and went through the towns, preaching THE GOSPEL"]{.class_4723} (vs. 6). The word [gospel]{.class_4725} means "good news," and there are ten "gospels" in the New Testament. There are the Gospels of Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John. Those are four separate Gospels that don't match each other; each one views Jesus Christ from a different perspective (see the Introduction).

There is ["the gospel of the grace of God"]{.class_4723} (Acts 20:24); that's five. That's the Gospel Paul called ["my gospel"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 2:16) because it was revealed to him personally by Jesus Christ (Gal. 1:11--12). It is the Gospel as defined in 1 Corinthians 15:1--4; it is the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ FOR SINS (don't forget [that]{.class_4725} part).

Number six is the good news that was preached to Abraham in Genesis 18:18 that all the [nations]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_284} would be blessed through his seed (Gal. 3:8). Now you know [who]{.class_4725} that blessing is: it's Jesus Christ. But notice the lack of detail. That blessing could not only apply to salvation by grace through faith preached to the Gentiles as Paul applies it in Galatians 3, it could also apply to the blessing of the Gentiles during Christ's Millennial reign (Isa. 2:2--4). So it's [not]{.class_4725} the same "gospel" as number five.

The seventh "gospel" is the good news that Joshua and Caleb preached to the other ten spies and the congregation of Israel at Kadesh-barnea in Numbers 13 (Heb. 4:2)---that the Israelites could kill the giants and take the Promised Land. That has nothing to do with the blood atonement of Jesus Christ at all; it is applied prophetically to the Millennial restoration of Israel (Heb. 4:9).

Number eight is ["the gospel"]{.class_4723} Christ preached ["to them that are dead"]{.class_4723} when He went down to Paradise and Abraham's Bosom after His crucifixion (1 Pet. 4:6). He preached the good news that He did what the Old Testament sacrifices could not do (Exod. 34:6): He completely took away the sins of the Old Testament saints so they could finally go to Heaven to be in God's presence (Rom. 3:25; Heb. 9:15; Eph. 4:8--10).

Number nine is ["the gospel of the kingdom"]{.class_4723} preached by Jesus in Matthew 4:23. It can either be the gospel of the Kingdom of Heaven (Matt. 4:17--23) or ["the gospel of the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (Mark 1:14). It is the good news that the King has come and is offering both the physical kingdom of David and a spiritual kingdom of the heart to the nation of Israel if they will just accept Him as their Messiah. That is the gospel that will be preached in the Tribulation (Matt. 24:14): the King is coming back to overthrow the Antichrist and set up His kingdom on earth; you'd better get ready.

The final gospel in the New Testament is found in Revelation 14:6--7 and is called ["the everlasting gospel."]{.class_4723} It is preached by an angel, and it has nothing to do with any "gospel" preached by Paul at all. It is preached by an angel who would be [cursed by God]{.class_4725} if he preached it to you right now (Gal. 1:8). It is the Lord reaching out one last time to the inhabitants of the earth during the Tribulation with the good news that if they will just renounce Darwin, Hawking, Dawkins, John Paul II,[ National Geographic,]{.class_4725} and the American public school system ([evolution]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_285}), and fear and glorify God as the Creator, they will be saved at Christ's return.

Now those are the ten gospels, and they are not the same. You hear the Campbellite talking about "the Gospel of Christ." Well, do they mean the Gospel [about Christ]{.class_4725} or the gospel [Christ preached?]{.class_4725} Jesus Christ never preached the Gospel Paul preached a day in His life. He got close. I mean, He talked about His death, burial, and resurrection (Matt. 16:21); He said He came to save the lost (Luke 19:10); He said He came ["to give his life a ransom for many"]{.class_4723} (Mark 10:45); the angel prophesied He would ["save his people from their sins"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 1:21); but the connection is never made between His death and salvation from sins. Peter doesn't even make it in Acts 2 after the Resurrection. The first time anyone ever preaches the blood atonement of Jesus Christ for sinners is when Philip preaches to the Ethiopian eunuch from Isaiah 53 (Acts 8:30--35); Isaiah 53:10 talks about making Jesus Christ ["an offering for sin."]{.class_4723} Before that, the death, burial, and resurrection are preached but are not connected with any atonement for sin.

So you have to watch out for that word ["gospel"]{.class_4723} in Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John; especially [before]{.class_4725} the Resurrection. What the disciples are preaching in verse 6 here in Luke 9 is that the King of the Jews has come and will establish an inner kingdom of righteousness in their hearts if they will just repent of their sins and receive Him as the Messiah. No death, no burial, no resurrection, no blood atonement for sin; just repent (Mark 6:12) and [receive the King.]{.class_4725}

7 Now Herod the tetrarch heard of all that was done by him: and he was perplexed, because that it was said of some, that John was risen from the dead;

8 And of some, that Elias had appeared; and of others, that one of the old prophets was risen again.

9 And Herod said, John have I beheaded: but who is this, of whom I hear such things? And he desired to see him.

John's actual death is not recorded in Luke, but it is given in []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_286}Matthew 14:3--12 and Mark 6:17--29. Those passages record the death of Herod's conscience, which is why when Jesus finally appears before Herod in Luke 23:8--12, the Lord has nothing to say to him. Herod had already rejected the light God had given him through John the Baptist. For further study on this passage, we refer you to our comments on Matthew 14:1--12 in that Commentary.

10 And the apostles, when they were returned, told him all that they had done. And he took them, and went aside privately into a desert place belonging to the city called Bethsaida.

11 And the people, when they knew it, followed him: and he received them, and spake unto them of the kingdom of God, and healed them that had need of healing.

12 And when the day began to wear away, then came the twelve, and said unto him, Send the multitude away, that they may go into the towns and country round about, and lodge, and get victuals: for we are here in a desert place.

13 But he said unto them, Give ye them to eat. And they said, We have no more but five loaves and two fishes; except we should go and buy meat for all this people.

14 For they were about five thousand men. And he said to his disciples, Make them sit down by fifties in a company.

15 And they did so, and made them all sit down.

16 Then he took the five loaves and the two fishes, and looking up to heaven, he blessed them, and brake, and gave to the disciples to set before the multitude.

17 And they did eat, and were all filled: and there was taken up of fragments that remained to them twelve baskets.

The passage relates one of the most remarkable events in Christ's earthly ministry. It is the feeding of the five thousand, and the reason it is so notable is that it is one of the few things in the life of Christ that is mentioned in all four Gospels (Matt. 13:13--21; Mark 6:30--44; Luke 9:10--17; John 6:1--14). The virgin birth is only found []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_287}in two Gospels (Matthew and Luke), so whatever is going on here is more important in God's eyes than even the birth of His own Son.

The ["desert place"]{.class_4723} of verses 10 and 13 is not a desert like the Gobi or the Sahara, a place of sand nearly devoid of normal plant life. This is obvious from the fact that there was ["grass"]{.class_4723} there (Matt. 14:19), it was ["green grass"]{.class_4723} (Mark 6:39), and there was ["much grass"]{.class_4723} (John 6:10). We would say today that the place was [deserted;]{.class_4725} that is, it was apart from people and houses and the city.

When you compare verse 10 with Mark 6:45, 53, you discover that there are [two]{.class_4725} Bethsaidas. There is one on the [northwest]{.class_4725} side of the Sea of Galilee in Gennesaret (Mark 6:53), which was the hometown of Philip, Andrew, and Peter (John 1:44); and there was another Bethsaida across the northern projection of the lake on the east side, which is the Bethsaida of Luke 9:10. You can get all the historical material on that Bethsaida in [Smith's Bible Dictionary,]{.class_4725} so we will not go into it here.

Although this was supposed to be a private retreat for the disciples after the preaching junket of verse 6, when the multitudes followed Jesus from the cities, the Bible says He was ["moved with compassion toward them, because they were as sheep not having a shepherd"]{.class_4723} (Mark 6:34). His compassion led Him not only to teach them the truth, but to heal the sick and feed the hungry. Jesus made real, tangible displays of His compassion.

Why is this so significant? Because in the [Koran,]{.class_4725} every "Sura" (chapter) opens with: "In the Name of Allah, the COMPASSIONATE, the Merciful." You will search the [Koran]{.class_4725} and the [Hadith]{.class_4725} in vain for one act of compassion by "Allah" toward his creation. If Allah were "compassionate," he certainly never showed it. The Jehovah God of the Bible displayed His compassion for all men by humbling Himself and becoming a man in order to take on Himself the punishment for the sins of His creation so they could have eternal life as a [free gift]{.class_4725} (Phil. 2:5--8; 1 John 4:10; Rom. 5:7--8, 15--21). That's [in addition]{.class_4725} to the particular acts of compassion He showered on individuals throughout the Scriptures. Men like Noah, Abraham, Job, Moses, David, Jonah, Daniel, and Esther experienced God's compassion in ways Mohammed couldn't discuss because Allah never showed any compassion on anyone (including Mohammed himself) that []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_288}Mohammed ever saw.

Verse 12: ["And when the day began to wear away."]{.class_4723} Mark says, ["the day was now far spent"]{.class_4723} (Mark 6:35); Matthew says, ["it was evening"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 14:15). So Jesus has ministered through suppertime, and it is somewhere between five and seven o'clock in the evening, but still daylight as it would be in the summer. By the time the multitude has been fed, it would have been dark; hence the suggestion of lodging made by the disciples in verse 12 because it would have been too late to travel by foot back to their homes.

The word ["victuals"]{.class_4723} in verse 12 (cf. Matt. 14:15) means "prepared food"; country folks pronounce it "vittles." The word ["bread"]{.class_4723} is used in Mark 6:36 and John 6:5, and although the word ["meat"]{.class_4723} in verse 13 can be used for any type of food (see our comments on 7:36), the two ["victuals"]{.class_4723} which turn up are [bread]{.class_4725} and [meat]{.class_4725} (fish).

As we compare the parallel accounts, we find different details given. Christ's question to Philip in John 6:5 (["Whence shall we buy bread, that these may eat?"]{.class_4723}) seems to follow the Lord telling the disciples ["Give ye them to eat"]{.class_4723} here in Luke 9:13. It is John's account that reveals that this is a test for the disciples (John 6:6), and it is also John's Gospel which tells you from where the disciples got the ["five loaves and two fishes"]{.class_4723} (vs. 13 cf. John 6:9). The proposal of the ["two hundred pennyworth of bread"]{.class_4723} is found in Mark (Mark 6:37) and John (John 6:7), but not in Matthew or Luke. Mark and Luke mention the fact that the "5,000" are seated in ranks and companies of fifties and hundreds (vs. 14 cf. Mark 6:40).

Now we call this miracle "the feeding of the 5,000," but there are many more besides 5,000. Luke says, ["they were about five thousand MEN"]{.class_4723} (vs. 14), but Matthew adds ["beside women and children"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 14:21). So you are looking at easily between 10,000 and 15,000 people total. Philip had said, ["Two hundred pennyworth of bread is not sufficient for them that every one of them may take a little"]{.class_4723} (John 6:7), and all they end up with is ["FIVE barley loaves, and two SMALL fishes"]{.class_4723} (John 6:9).

The humanistic slop put out by the Liberals is that when the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_289}crowd saw the generosity of the little boy giving his lunch to Jesus, that they all took out the food they had been hiding and shared it with one another. Nonsense! If they had had the food on them, they would have hauled it out and started eating it long before the last of the ranks of fifty were seated. This is a genuine miracle on the same order as that of the manna in the wilderness (Exod. 16), but we will get into that in a moment.

["And looking up to heaven, he blessed them, and BRAKE"]{.class_4723} (vs. 16). The [spiritual]{.class_4725} lesson here is that if God is ever going to use you, He is going to have to [break]{.class_4725} you. The roof had to be [broken]{.class_4725} up to get the palsied man to Jesus (Mark 2:4), the alabaster box had to be [broken]{.class_4725} for the aroma to come out (Mark 14:3), Gideon and his men had to [break]{.class_4725} their pitchers in order for their lights to shine (Judg. 7:19--20). It's not until Jesus [breaks]{.class_4725} the bread that He is able to feed the 5,000. If you are ever going to be of any use to the Lord, He has to send something in your life that will totally and permanently ruin this world for you so that you ["set your affection on things above, not on things on the earth"]{.class_4723} (Col. 3:2).

["And gave to the disciples to set before the multitude."]{.class_4723} Notice that the Lord didn't personally feed the multitude; He let the disciples do it. He didn't call bread down from Heaven to feed them (cf. John 6:31); He let a little boy give Him his lunch. The [spiritual]{.class_4725} lesson should be obvious: God wants to give the multitudes of this world the Bread of Life [through you.]{.class_4725}

["For we are labourers together with God"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 3:9).

["Now then we are ambassadors for Christ, as though God did beseech you by us: we pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 5:20).

Back to Luke 9:[ "And they did eat, and were all filled"]{.class_4723} (vs. 17). Now put yourselves in the shoes of the disciples. Christ breaks the bread and gives each disciple a little less than half a loaf each; each disciple gets one-sixth of a piece of ["small"]{.class_4723} fish (John 6:9), which would not be much. Each disciple is responsible for feeding anywhere from 417 to 1,000 people (depending on how many ["women and children"]{.class_4723} there were---Matt. 14:21). What each disciple had in his basket shouldn't have been enough to feed even []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_290}the first row of the first rank of fifty, but no matter how much was given out, there was never any less in the basket. Each person not only got some food; they all were ["filled."]{.class_4723} One is reminded of the miracle of the oil and meal performed by Elijah for the widow of Zarephath in 1 Kings 17:14 and the miracle of the oil performed by Elisha for the Jewish widow in 2 Kings 4:2--7.

["And there was taken up of fragments that remained to them twelve baskets."]{.class_4723} There was not only enough for the multitude to eat until they were full, there was enough left over to fill each of the baskets of the apostles. The apostles took their meal from those ["fragments"]{.class_4723} (see Matt. 16:6).

Now as I said earlier, this miracle is so amazing that it appears in all four Gospels. Evidently, it is more important than the virgin birth or the ascension. And if that were not enough, Jesus [repeats]{.class_4725} the miracle with the feeding of the 4,000 (Matt. 15:32--38; Mark 8:1--9). The first temptation with which Christ is faced is turning stones to bread (see our comments on 4:3). So what makes this miracle so very important?

Well, the main theme of the Bible is [not]{.class_4729-0} Christ's First Coming; it is His [Second Coming.]{.class_4729-0} And as we observed in 1:17, when Jesus came the first time, His First and Second Advents are often mentioned right together because the Jews don't finally reject their King until the Crucifixion. Everything Jesus does in His earthly ministry [could]{.class_4729-0} have had application to His Second Coming. As we pointed out in our comments on 4:3, the Lord is going to feed those Jews in the wilderness during the Tribulation exactly as He did back there in Exodus and Numbers. That's why He called those events a ["parable"]{.class_4730-0} in Psalm 78:2; they are an [historical parallel]{.class_4729-0} of what will happen in the future during the Tribulation and Second Advent (note particularly Psa. 78:24--25 in regard to our discussion here and compare it with Mic. 7:14--15).

18 And it came to pass, as he was alone praying, his disciples were with him: and he asked them, saying, Whom say the people that I am?

19 They answering said, John the Baptist; but some say, Elias; and others say, that one of the old prophets is risen []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_291}again.

20 He said unto them, But whom say ye that I am? Peter answering said, The Christ of God.

21 And he straitly charged them, and commanded them to tell no man that thing;

22 Saying, The Son of man must suffer many things, and be rejected of the elders and chief priests and scribes, and be slain, and be raised the third day.

In verse 18, we once more see Christ alone praying (see our comments on 5:16). Matthew and Mark say He was up north around ["Caesarea Philippi"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 16:13; Mark 8:27), which is near the source of the Jordan River around Mt. Hermon.

["His disciples were with him: and he asked them, saying, Whom say the people that I am?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 18). What a question! How you answer that question determines where you will spend Eternity (see Acts 8:37, 9:5--6; Luke 23:42, which are all changed in the new "Bibles" right where someone is getting saved).

To Dan Brown ([The DaVinci Code]{.class_4725}), He's a fornicator shacking up with Mary Magdalene. To the Mormons, He's the brother of Lucifer and the product of "Elohim" shacking up with Mary His mother. To the National Endowment for the Arts in Washington, D.C., He's a drug addict shooting heroin in His veins. To the Liberation theologians, He's a Communist revolutionary. To Mohammed, He was a Moslem who never claimed to be the Son of God because "Allah has no Son."

There are all kinds of "Jesuses" (2 Cor. 11:4). There's the "Jesus" of "Jesus Christ, Superstar": a poor, confused teenager on dope. There's the "Jesus" of the Liberals and Modernists (now called "emergents") who was a good man and a great teacher, but not God in the flesh or the virgin-born, only begotten Son of God. I mean, you can get anything for a "Christ" these days.

["Whom say the people that I am?"]{.class_4723} The answer to that question in verse 19 shows you that everything you have heard or seen about Jesus Christ from the movies and television, and from about 90% of the preachers for that matter, is absolutely [false.]{.class_4725} The people who[ saw]{.class_4725} Him and [knew]{.class_4725} Him (see our comments on 1:2) []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_292}didn't make the same mistake this modern bunch makes.

The first two answers that come to mind are ["John the Baptist"]{.class_4723} and ["Elias"]{.class_4723} (vs. 19): two rough-looking, rude, irritating, aggravating [street preachers]{.class_4725} (see our comments on 3:8 and 7:25). Matthew adds Jeremiah to the list (Matt. 16:14); that's the fellow who was commanded to preach ["violence and spoil"]{.class_4723} and could not stop because God's word was like ["a burning fire"]{.class_4723} in his heart (Jer. 20:8--9). In other words, no one who actually [heard]{.class_4725} and [saw]{.class_4725} Jesus Christ would ever mistake Him for the smooth-talking, slick, "nice," effeminate one you're getting over the radio and television nowadays.

For verse 20, see our comments on ["the Lord's Christ"]{.class_4723} in 2:26.

The reason Jesus charged the disciples not to tell anyone that He was the Christ in verse 21 was to avoid any charges of insurrection against Rome as was reported taking place in Acts 5:36--37. The natural assumption by the Jews was that Messiah would ["fight against those nations, as when he fought in the day of battle"]{.class_4723} (see Zech. 14:3 and comments in that Commentary). That wasn't going to happen before the Crucifixion, so when some Jews try to force Him to be King, He abandons them (John 6:15) and then gives them an excuse to abandon Him (John 6:60--66). The Lord wasn't about to be rushed into anything against God's timetable.

Verse 22 marks Jesus Christ as a true prophet, unlike a rank amateur "wannabe" like Mohammed. In the verse, Jesus gives [seven]{.class_4725} details of His death that all come true right on the money.

1\. He would ["suffer many things."]{.class_4723}

2\. He would ["be rejected of the elders."]{.class_4723}

3\. He would be rejected by the ["chief priests."]{.class_4723}

4.[ ]{.class_4723}He would be rejected by the[ "scribes."]{.class_4723}

5\. He would ["be slain."]{.class_4723}

6\. He would ["be raised."]{.class_4723}

7\. He would be raised again ["the third day."]{.class_4723}

Matthew 20:18--19 adds six more for a total of thirteen.

8\. He would ["be betrayed."]{.class_4723}

9\. The chief priests and scribes would ["condemn him to death."]{.class_4723}

10\. They would []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_293}["deliver him to the Gentiles."]{.class_4723}

11\. The Gentiles would ["mock"]{.class_4723} Him.

12\. The Gentiles would ["scourge"]{.class_4723} Him.

13\. The Gentiles would ["crucify him."]{.class_4723}

Mohammed didn't know [when]{.class_4725} he was going to die or [how]{.class_4725} he was going to die; he was a perfect blank. I suppose he wanted to die fighting for Allah so he could be guaranteed a place in Paradise (since he [didn't know]{.class_4725} what was going to happen to him when he died---[Hadith,]{.class_4725} Bukhari, Vol. V, no. 266), but that's not what happened. He died in his bed from a high fever and severe headache which he attributed to a Jewish woman poisoning him two years earlier after he had murdered her family when he invaded her town for the booty (Mohammed was not even enough of a prophet to tell when a woman was trying to poison him). He didn't foresee his death; he couldn't predict the circumstances of his death; he made no preparation for his death; he made no arrangements for a successor in the event of his death; and his death took him so unawares that he didn't even have the time to gather his "revelations" from Allah into one authoritative book.

People, you've got a Book so far ahead of Mohammed and the [Koran]{.class_4725} that they're not even in the running; they never even made it to the starting line. That Book is able to give you 48 details of a man's life 400--1,500 years before He was born. Mohammed, Allah, and the [Koran]{.class_4725} don't even attempt it. That same man who fulfilled those 48 prophecies was able to give you thirteen specific details about the last week of His earthly life before they ever happened. Allah never showed Mohammed "nuthin'."

23 And he said to them all, If any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross daily, and follow me.

24 For whosoever will save his life shall lose it: but whosoever will lose his life for my sake, the same shall save it.

25 For what is a man advantaged, if he gain the whole world, and lose himself, or be cast away?

26 For whosoever shall be ashamed of me and of my []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_294}words, of him shall the Son of man be ashamed, when he shall come in his own glory, and in his Father's, and of the holy angels.

Verse 23 logically follows what the Lord said in verse 22 about His death and resurrection. Notice the allusion to the method of His execution here in verse 23: ["take up his CROSS."]{.class_4723} So when Jesus says ["If any man will come after me"]{.class_4723} (vs. 23), He's not talking about someone [following]{.class_4725} after Him (see Matt. 10:38); He's talking about the disciples who show up after the Resurrection. The Pauline commentary on the verse for the Body of Christ is found in 1 Corinthians 15:31; Colossians 3:5; and Romans 6:11. The Christian life is to be one of [self-denial, not self-love]{.class_4725} (see 2 Tim. 3:2) [or self-esteem]{.class_4725} (see Phil. 2:3).

Verse 24 reiterates the great principle of truth which is completely contradictory to the carnal, worldly mind: the way up is down (Luke 18:14; 1 Pet. 5:6); the way to gain your life is to lose it (Matt. 10:39; John 12:25). Notice again the conditional phrase which we ran into in 6:22 (which see)---["for my sake."]{.class_4723} A man or woman can lay down his or her life for all kinds of causes, but unless it is for [Christ's sake,]{.class_4725} the sacrifice is in vain.

The wording in verse 25 should be contrasted with the wording of Mark 8:36.

Verse 25---["For what is a man advantaged, if he gain the whole world, and LOSE HIMSELF, OR BE CAST AWAY?"]{.class_4723}

Mark 8:36, ["For what shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and LOSE HIS OWN SOUL?"]{.class_4723}

In Mark's version, the one who gains the world [goes to Hell;]{.class_4725} not so here in Luke. A Christian can be ["a castaway"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 9:27). The emphasis in Mark is on [salvation]{.class_4725} (notice the gospel showing up in Mark 9:35); the emphasis in Luke is on [discipleship.]{.class_4725}

Again, verse 26 deals with [discipleship,]{.class_4725} not salvation. The shame that Christ shows to the disciple who is ashamed of Him does not take place at death; it takes place at His return in glory---the Second Advent. The disciple who is ashamed of Jesus Christ doesn't go to Hell; he loses a Millennial inheritance. Look at what Paul says in 2 Timothy 2:11--13.

["It is a faithful saying: For if we be dead with him, we shall also live with him: If we suffer,]{.class_4723} \[watch it!\][ WE SHALL ALSO REIGN WITH HIM]{.class_4723} \[that's the Christian reigning with his Saviour in the Millennium\][: if we deny him, he also will deny us ]{.class_4723}\[["deny us"]{.class_4723} what? A place in Heaven? No! Look at the next verse\][: If we believe not, YET HE ABIDETH FAITHFUL: HE CANNOT DENY HIMSELF."]{.class_4723}

If you are [in Christ,]{.class_4725} you won't be cast [out of Christ]{.class_4725} no matter what happens (Rom. 8:38--39; Eph. 4:30), but you can lose your inheritance in the Kingdom of God at Christ's return (1 Cor. 6:9--10; Gal. 5:19--21).

Note once again the emphasis Christ places on His ["WORDS"]{.class_4723} (see our remarks under 4:4), and I don't mean the "WORD" or the "word." I'm talking about those W-O-R-D-S of God. It's those ["words"]{.class_4723} that are purified and preserved (Psa. 12:6--7), not the message or the fundamentals or the principles.

The job of every major Christian school in this country is to take those ["words"]{.class_4723} away from you and exchange them for [something else]{.class_4725}---preferably faith in the school itself. The modern "gaff" is to make you believe you have an infallible Bible [that is not HOLY SCRIPTURE]{.class_4725} because ["ALL SCRIPTURE is given by inspiration of God"]{.class_4723} (2 Tim. 3:16), and people like Jack Schaap (the man who took over Hyles' work) don't believe the [King James]{.class_4725} ["is given by inspiration of God."]{.class_4723} That would associate them with that heretic down in Pensacola, Florida, and they don't want to catch "leprosy."

Let's just face it, folks, you either [have]{.class_4725} God's words or you [don't.]{.class_4725} These Fundamentalist apostates don't have them in Greek, Hebrew, English, or any other language. They don't believe anything they have ["is given by inspiration of God."]{.class_4723} They believe it [was]{.class_4725} way back there between 1800 B.C.--A.D. 96, and what you have now has mistakes in it. "But for all intents and purposes you can call the [King James]{.class_4725} the Word of God." Yeah, don't you know, baby!

Now if they don't have God's words in the Holy Scriptures which are ["given by inspiration of God,"]{.class_4723} [I do.]{.class_4725} You say, "In what language?" In [English.]{.class_4725} I have the [words]{.class_4725} God wants me to have in the Book He has given me---an [Authorized King James Version]{.class_4725} from 1611. I believe [that Book]{.class_4725} is the Holy Scriptures in English. I'm not ashamed of the words in [that Book,]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_296} which is why I don't mess with them. If those other guys do, it's their own funeral. Me, I'm not going to change a word in there.

You say, "What if you're wrong, Ruckman?" Then I'll take my chances at the Judgment Seat of Christ and answer for what I've taught about the Book. But if they're wrong, [so will they.]{.class_4725} Which do you think will come out better---[believing]{.class_4725} the words in that Book ([AV]{.class_4725} 1611) or [being ashamed]{.class_4725} of them and [casting doubt]{.class_4725} on them and [changing]{.class_4725} them?

27 But I tell you of a truth, there be some standing here, which shall not taste of death, till they see the kingdom of God.

28 And it came to pass about an eight days after these sayings, he took Peter and John and James, and went up into a mountain to pray.

29 And as he prayed, the fashion of his countenance was altered, and his raiment was white and glistering.

30 And, behold, there talked with him two men, which were Moses and Elias:

31 Who appeared in glory, and spake of his decease which he should accomplish at Jerusalem.

32 But Peter and they that were with him were heavy with sleep: and when they were awake, they saw his glory, and the two men that stood with him.

33 And it came to pass, as they departed from him, Peter said unto Jesus, Master, it is good for us to be here: and let us make three tabernacles; one for thee, and one for Moses, and one for Elias: not knowing what he said.

34 While he thus spake, there came a cloud, and overshadowed them: and they feared as they entered into the cloud.

35 And there came a voice out of the cloud, saying, This is my beloved Son: hear him.

36 And when the voice was past, Jesus was found alone. And they kept it close, and told no man in those days any of those things which they had seen.

Verse 26 ends with Christ's Second Advent, and immediately the Lord follows it up with verse 27: ["there be some standing here, which shall not taste of death, till they see the kingdom of God."]{.class_4723} The implication is that the Kingdom of God shows up when Christ comes in power, so all the commentators and expositors make the Kingdom of God and the Kingdom of Heaven the same. Of course that ignores the clear statements of Scripture in Romans 14:17; Luke 17:21; and Matthew 11:12 (see our remarks on the two kingdoms under 6:20).

The fact of the matter is that one day (the Day of the Lord---the Second Advent), if you are saved, you will [see]{.class_4725} the spiritual Kingdom of God (John 3:3). You will be with the King of both kingdoms at His return, in a body just like that of the King's (2 Thess. 1:7--10).

Now, what is about to take place in verses 28--35 is the Transfiguration of the Lord Jesus Christ. According to what Christ said in verses 26--27, the Transfiguration is a picture of the Second Advent. How do you know that for sure? Look at what Peter says about it years later.

["For we have not followed cunningly devised fables, when we made known unto you the POWER AND COMING OF OUR LORD JESUS CHRIST, but were eyewitnesses of HIS MAJESTY"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 1:16).

That's Peter saying he saw the Second Coming of Jesus Christ in power to set up His kingdom. When did that take place? On the Mount of Transfiguration (2 Pet. 1:17--18). So Peter, James, and John experience the Second Advent in [type]{.class_4725} here in verses 28--35.

["And it came to pass about an eight days after these sayings, he took Peter and John and James, and went up into a mountain to pray"]{.class_4723} (vs. 28). Matthew says it was ["after six days"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 17:1). Well, it doesn't take a genius to figure out that ["after six"]{.class_4723} and ["about\...eight"]{.class_4723} is [seven.]{.class_4725} So the Second Advent takes place on the "seventh day."

What do we mean by that? Well, in Leviticus 23 and 25, you have a system of "sevens" laid out by the Lord. You go six days, and then you have a Sabbath. You go six weeks from Firstfruits, and on the seventh week you have Pentecost. The seventh month on a Jewish calendar contains three of the most important feasts Israel was to []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_298}observe (Trumpets, Atonement, and Tabernacles. The Feast of Tabernacles in the [seventh]{.class_4725} month, by the way, is connected to the Second Advent---see our comments on Zech. 14:16 in that Commentary). You go seven years and let the land rest. You go seven groups of seven years, and on the fiftieth year is Jubilee. So you count 6,000 years from the creation (4,000 from Adam to Christ, and 2,000 from Christ to the present), and in the 7,000th year, the Lord comes back and sets up His Millennial kingdom (see our comments on 2 Pet. 3:8 in that Commentary).

Now I have taught this for 62 years (as of 2012), and I did not change my teaching once the year 2000 came and went. You say, "What went wrong, Ruckman? I thought you said the Lord was coming back in 2000." Well, I have never set a firm date without making a couple of [conditions.]{.class_4725} One is that [our calendar may not be right;]{.class_4725} the other is that there may be certain years that the Lord does not count, just as He did not count certain years in Israel's history (1 Kings 6:1 cf. Job 3:6). One thing is for certain: when Christ returns, the year will be 2000 on God's calendar no matter what it says on ours.

The ["mountain"]{.class_4723} in verse 28 is supposed to be Mt. Hermon between Israel and Lebanon according to [tradition.]{.class_4725} But if you compare Scripture with Scripture---keeping in mind that the Transfiguration is a type of the Second Advent---you would have to make the mountain [Sinai]{.class_4725} (see our remarks under Hab. 3:3 in that Commentary). In just two verses, Moses and Elijah are going to show up; there was only one mountain both had in common: Mt. Sinai. The Lord spoke to both Moses and Elijah on Mt. Sinai (Exod. 3:1--4; 1 Kings 19:9--12). Peter said the Transfiguration took place ["in the holy mount"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 1:18); the only two "holy mounts" in the Scripture are Mt. Zion (Isa. 27:13, 66:20; Dan. 9:16;) and Mt. Sinai (Psa. 68:17; Exod. 3:5). There are four names at the end of the Old Testament in connection with the Second Advent: Moses, Elijah, the Lord, and [Horeb]{.class_4725} (Mt. Sinai---see Mal. 4:4--5). So the seven days between verses 27 and 28 were spent in transit to Mt. Sinai.

["And as he prayed, the fashion of his countenance was altered"]{.class_4723} (vs. 29). Matthew and Mark say Jesus ["was transfigured before them"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 17:2; Mark 9:2). So once again the [KJV]{.class_4725} defines []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_299}its own terms apart from any Greek or Hebrew. The Transfiguration was an [alteration,]{.class_4725} a [change]{.class_4725} in the [figure]{.class_4725} of Christ from one form to another.

["And his raiment was white and glistering."]{.class_4723} Mark says, ["his raiment became shining, exceeding white as snow; so as no fuller on earth can white them"]{.class_4723} (Mark 9:3). That's because the ["white"]{.class_4723} was ["as the light"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 17:2). That ["light"]{.class_4723} is the original light of creation before the sun showed up (Psa. 74:16 cf. Gen. 1:3), because ["God is light, and in him is no darkness at all"]{.class_4723} (1 John 1:5). When Jesus appeared to Saul of Taursus on the road to Damascus, that light was said to be ["above the brightness of the sun"]{.class_4723} (Acts 26:13).

Matthew describes the ["shining"]{.class_4723} of Mark 9:3 (the ["glistering"]{.class_4723} of Luke 9:29) as that of ["the sun"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 17:2). Once more the reference is to the Second Advent (Mal. 4:12; 2 Pet. 1:19; Matt. 13:43). Moses and Elijah show up in verse 30 because they show up at the Second Advent (Mal. 4:4--5; Rev. 11:3--6). They appear ["in glory"]{.class_4723} because that is how Christ is said to come at His return (Matt. 25:31; 2 Thess. 1:7--10).

["And behold, there talked with him two men, which were Moses and Elias\...and spake of his decease which he should accomplish at Jerusalem"]{.class_4723} (vss. 30--31). Luke's is the only account which gives you the topic they were discussing. Moses represents the Law, and Elijah represents the Prophets. So after the Resurrection, when Jesus gives those disciples a Bible lesson on His death, burial, and resurrection, He does it from ["Moses and all the prophets"]{.class_4723} (24:25--27).

["But Peter and they that were with him were heavy with sleep: and when they were awake, they saw his glory"]{.class_4723} (vs. 32). The disciples had a bad problem with sleeping while the Lord was praying (see 22:45--46). As Peter, James, and John wake up out of a heavy sleep, they see Jesus with Moses and Elijah right as they are finishing up their conversation and as Moses and Elijah are returning to Heaven. Without even thinking about what he is saying (see the end of vs. 33), Peter makes one of his usual outbursts for which he is so well known (see Matt. 16:22; Mark 14:29; John 21:21): ["Master, it is good for us to be here: and let us make three []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_300}tabernacles; one for thee, and one for Moses, and one for Elias"]{.class_4723} (vs. 33).

Problem: Jesus is not just another "religious leader" like Moses and Elijah. He is the grand subject of the Law and the Prophets (John 1:45) and their culmination (Heb. 1:1--2). He is not merely the human founder of a world religion like Moses, Confucius, Zoroaster, Buddha, or Mohammed; He is the Creator in human flesh (John 1:1--3, 14). He is not one of many saints like Patrick, Francis, or Nicholas; He is the ["one mediator between God and men"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 2:5) and the only one who can give you access to the Father (John 14:6).

Verse 33 sets the timeframe of the Second Advent as the Feast of Tabernacles. The Feast of Tabernacles marks Christ's birth (see our comments on 1:5) and the beginning of His earthly ministry (see our comments on 3:23). It also marks the day of creation, for during the Feast of Tabernacles in September (Sept. 20--23), the sun is four days off center because the sun was created on the fourth day. So if the sun is a picture of God (and it is---see our remarks on the sun under Psa. 19:1--6 and ["the Sun of righteousness"]{.class_4723} under Mal. 4:2 in their respective Commentaries), then Jesus Christ shows up the first time on the "fourth day" after creation at the Feast of Tabernacles, and He shows up the second time at the Feast of Tabernacles (see our comments on Zech. 14:16 in that Commentary) as well.

["While he thus spake, there came a cloud, and overshadowed them: and they feared as they entered into the cloud"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34). That's not any cumulus, stratus, or nimbus cloud. That's the cloud of God's glory that came down on the Tabernacle so that Moses wasn't even able to go into the tent (Exod. 40:35). When the Temple was dedicated, that cloud came in there so that the priests couldn't even minister in the house (2 Chron. 5:14). When Jesus ascended to Heaven, ["a cloud received him"]{.class_4723} (Acts 1:9), and the angels told His disciples that ["this same Jesus\...shall so come in like manner as ye have seen him go into heaven"]{.class_4723} (Acts 1:11). So when the Lord Jesus returns, He comes back the same way He went: in the cloud of the glory of God (1 Thess. 4:17; Mark 14:62; Rev. 1:7). So what you just read in verse 34 is yet another picture of Christ's Second Coming; it's all the way through []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_301}there.

["And there came a voice out of the cloud, saying, This is my beloved Son: hear him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 35). This is the second time God has audibly identified Jesus Christ as His [Son]{.class_4725} (see 3:22 and our comments on the latter part of the verse). Whoever "Allah" was, he sure wasn't the God of the Bible, because "the God" of the [Koran]{.class_4725} never had a son (Sura 3:58, 9:30, 17:39, 2:116, 72:3, 6:102, 37:152). The God of the Bible claimed to be ["the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 11:31; Eph. 1:3; 1 Pet. 1:3). (For further study, see [Will the Real "Allah" Please Stand Up?]{.class_4725}.)

["Hear HIM!"]{.class_4723} Allah and Mohammed are [out!]{.class_4725} Mary, John Paul II, and Benedict XVI are [out!]{.class_4725} Buddha, Confucius, the Dalai Lama, Joseph Smith, and Charles Taze Russell have nothing to contribute whatsoever. ["Hear HIM"]{.class_4723} (Acts 3:22, 7:37; John 5:24).

["And when the voice was past, Jesus was found alone"]{.class_4723} (vs. 36). With Moses and Elijah returned to Heaven and the voice of God silent, the disciples are left with only the earthly Jesus, the one in whom there was ["no beauty that we should desire him"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 53:2). He no longer appears as the King coming in glory; He is just the humble servant prepared to die for sinners (Phil. 2:7--8).

["And they kept it close, and told no man in those days any of those things which they had seen."]{.class_4723} This is done at the command of Jesus who told them, ["Tell the vision to no man, until the Son of man be risen again from the dead"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 17:9). This is done for the reasons covered in verse 21 here in Luke 9.

37 And it came to pass, that on the next day, when they were come down from the hill, much people met him.

38 And, behold, a man of the company cried out, saying, Master, I beseech thee, look upon my son: for he is mine only child.

39 And, lo, a spirit taketh him, and he suddenly crieth out; and it teareth him that he foameth again, and bruising him hardly departeth from him.

40 And I besought thy disciples to cast him out; and they could not.

41 And Jesus answering said, O faithless and perverse generation, how long shall I be with you, and suffer you? Bring thy son hither.

42 And as he was yet a coming, the devil threw him down, and tare him. And Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, and healed the child, and delivered him again to his father.

The passage is the account of Jesus delivering a demon-possessed boy. The companion passage in Matthew 17:15 says the boy was ["lunatick,"]{.class_4723} literally meaning "moonstruck." We would say that he was mad or crazy. This means that [some]{.class_4725} mental problems have a [spiritual]{.class_4725} cause that cannot successfully be treated by drugs or therapy. The view of the Catholic Church in the Dark Ages was that all mental problems were caused by demons; the modern view is that all mental problems are physical or psychological in nature. The fact is that neither one is true.

Matthew 4:24 distinguishes between lunatics who are demon-possessed and those who are merely lunatic (["those which were possessed with devils, and those which were lunatick"]{.class_4723}). Some mental illness is the result of physical ailments, chemical imbalances in the brain, traumatic experiences, etc., while others are the results of [sin]{.class_4725} where a door has been opened in a person's mind for Satan to have free course. You treat a [physical]{.class_4725} problem [physically,]{.class_4725} and you treat a [spiritual]{.class_4725} problem [spiritually.]{.class_4725} The secret is finding out which is which.

The new "Bibles" change the word ["lunatick"]{.class_4723} in Matthew 17:15 to ["epileptic"]{.class_4725} to drive you to one of the two extreme viewpoints above. That would be the [RSV, Good News for Modern Man,]{.class_4725} the [NIV,]{.class_4725} the [ESV,]{.class_4725} and even the so-called ["New" King James Version]{.class_4725} (which is nothing but the [RSV]{.class_4725} readings smuggled into the [KJV]{.class_4725} text, as evidenced here). Surprisingly this time, the [NASV]{.class_4725} reads [with]{.class_4725} the [King James.]{.class_4725} Epilepsy is [not]{.class_4725} demon-possession.

Verse 39: ["And, lo, a spirit taketh him, and he suddenly crieth out."]{.class_4723} The ["spirit"]{.class_4723} of the passage is called ["the devil"]{.class_4723} in verse 42, meaning one particular devil, not Satan himself (see our remarks on one Devil and many demons under 8:2). It is called an ["unclean spirit"]{.class_4723} (vs. 42) or ["foul spirit"]{.class_4723} (Mark 9:25) to distinguish it from the Holy Spirit. Mark reveals that it is a []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_303}["dumb and deaf spirit"]{.class_4723} (Mark 9:25), meaning that it wouldn't let the child hear and wouldn't let the child speak unless he cried out in pain.

["And it teareth him that he foameth again, and bruising him hardly departeth from him."]{.class_4723} The symptoms are like epilepsy, but [deafness and dumbness aren't.]{.class_4725} If that were not all, the spirit threw the boy into fire and water (see our comments on 8:31) with the intent ["to destroy him"]{.class_4723} (Mark 9:22); that's not epilepsy. The Liberals and the Catholics don't know about what they are talking.

Verse 41: ["And Jesus answering said, O faithless and perverse generation."]{.class_4723} He's talking to the [disciples]{.class_4725} (vs. 40)! Jesus is continually rebuking them for their lack of faith (Matt. 6:30, 8:26, 14:31, 16:8). Well, if He rebukes a bunch of [healing evangelists]{.class_4725} (Matt. 10:1) for their lack of faith, where does that leave [us]{.class_4725}?

Mark adds two things to the story that Luke leaves out. First is the confession of the father. Right after rebuking the disciples for their faithlessness, Jesus tells the father, ["If thou canst believe, all things are possible to him that believeth"]{.class_4723} (Mark 9:23). The father makes a confession and a petition at the same time: ["Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief"]{.class_4723} (Mark 9:24). The amazing thing is that Jesus honors what [little]{.class_4725} bit of faith the father has but rebukes the disciples for their [unbelief.]{.class_4725}

["Then came the disciples to Jesus apart, and said, Why could not we cast him out? And Jesus said unto them, Because of your unbelief: for verily I say unto you, If ye have faith as a grain of mustard seed, ye shall say unto this mountain, Remove hence to yonder place; and it shall remove; and nothing shall be impossible unto you"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 17:19--20).

Evidently the disciples didn't have the faith of ["a grain of mustard seed."]{.class_4723} We shouldn't be too hard on them, though, for the possession here is of a particularly difficult nature. Both Mark and Matthew add a second detail left out by Luke.

["This kind can come forth by nothing, but by prayer and fasting"]{.class_4723} (Mark 9:29). The disciples didn't exercise their faith to that point. To guarantee you won't either, apostate corruptions of the word of God like the [NIV]{.class_4725} leave the word ["fasting"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_304} out of Mark 9:29; it drops the companion passage in Matthew 17:21 slam out of the text altogether. To compound the hypocrisy, the [NIV]{.class_4725} retains the [KJV]{.class_4725}'s verse numbering system (after telling you it is not "inspired") with [no verse 21]{.class_4725}; it runs verse 19, verse 20, verse 22---just like that. What's the matter, boys? Forgot how to count? You never met a bigger bunch of apostate hypocrites in your life unless it was a Catholic Pope, Cardinal, or Bishop.

43 And they were all amazed at the mighty power of God. But while they wondered every one at all things which Jesus did, he said unto his disciples,

44 Let these sayings sink down into your ears: for the Son of man shall be delivered into the hands of men.

45 But they understood not this saying, and it was hid from them, that they perceived it not: and they feared to ask him of that saying.

While the crowd is ["amazed at the mighty power of God,"]{.class_4723} Christ ignores them altogether and turns His attention to the disciples (vs. 43). ["Let these sayings sink down into your ears"]{.class_4723} (vs. 44). What ["sayings"]{.class_4723}? The colon indicates it is what is to follow: ["for the Son of man shall be delivered into the hands of men."]{.class_4723} It's exactly what He told them back in verse 22 and about what He was talking with Moses and Elijah on the Mount of Transfiguration (vs. 31): it is His death on Calvary. He knows exactly when and how it will take place (13:32--33; see also our comments on vs. 22).

Verse 45: ["But they understood not this saying, and it was hid from them, that they perceived it not."]{.class_4723} No matter how many times He tells them, the disciples just don't "get it." In fact, they don't "get it" until [after]{.class_4725} the Resurrection when the Lord ["opened\...their understanding, that they might understand the scriptures"]{.class_4723} (24:45).

Now isn't that amazing? Do you know what that shows you? That shows you that there are things in that Book that you will never get unless the Holy Spirit [reveals]{.class_4725} them to you (see John 16:13).

Recently the Bookstore put out the second volume of my Commentary on the Minor Prophets. I put that thing off for [33 years.]{.class_4725} You say, "Why, Ruckman?" Because there were things in those last five books (especially in Zechariah) about which I had [no clue]{.class_4725}---even after reading through the Book more than 152 times. I had no more light on certain passages than the first time I picked up the Book. Why is that? [Because the Holy Spirit didn't show me anything about them.]{.class_4725} If some of my people hadn't persuaded me to put down on paper [what I did know]{.class_4725} about those books (Habakkuk--Malachi), you never would have gotten Volume II of Minor Prophets.

Listen, brethren, I haven't gotten everything in that Book nailed down. If I did, I would know that someone wrote it who didn't have any more sense than I do (to quote Sam Jones). Do you know why no one has gotten the date of the Rapture figured out yet? I mean, Paul said you know ["the times and the seasons"]{.class_4723} of the Lord's return ["perfectly"]{.class_4723} (1 Thess. 5:1--2). Well, if that's the case, why hasn't anyone gotten it right yet? It's because there are certain things about the timing of the Rapture that are hidden away, and until the Holy Spirit reveals where, no one's going to get it any more than the apostles got what Jesus was saying about His death, burial, and resurrection.

["And they feared to ask him of that saying."]{.class_4723} Maybe if they had done like they did when Jesus gave them the Parable of the Sower (see 8:9), Jesus would have shown them right then about what He was saying; I don't know. But the last time Jesus had talked to the whole group about His death, Peter shot off his mouth without thinking (see our remarks under vs. 33), and Jesus had to rebuke the "first Pope" for teaching false doctrine (see our remarks on Matt. 16:21--23 in that Commentary). So the disciples are rather skiddish about asking the Lord what He meant.

46 Then there arose a reasoning among them, which of them should be greatest.

47 And Jesus, perceiving the thought of their heart, took a child, and set him by him,

48 And said unto them, Whosoever shall receive this child in my name receiveth me: and whosoever shall []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_306}receive me receiveth him that sent me: for he that is least among you all, the same shall be great.

This argument about who is going to be the greatest in the kingdom comes up about three times during Christ's ministry: here in verse 46 (see also Matt. 18:1; Mark 9:33--34), in Matthew 20:20--28 with the mother of James and John, and again at the Last Supper (Luke 22:24--27 cf. John 13:4--15). Each time the Lord tells them that the way up in the kingdom is [down.]{.class_4725}

We have already commented on Jesus being able to read men's thoughts (vs. 47) under 5:22 (which see).

There are two instances of Jesus using a little child as an example of getting into the kingdom: here (vss. 47--48 cf. Mark 9:36--37; Matt. 18:3--5) and 18:16--17 (cf. Mark 10:14--15; Matt. 19:14). In the Matthew account, Jesus makes a statement that precedes what He says here in Luke 9:48.

["And said, Verily I say unto you, Except ye be CONVERTED, and become as little children, ye shall not enter into the kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 18:3).

That conversion is to a [person,]{.class_4725} not an [organization]{.class_4725} through a [ritual.]{.class_4725} It is the conversion to an [inner kingdom]{.class_4725}[ ]{.class_4737}(18:19) through [Jesus Christ Himself.]{.class_4725}

["Whosoever shall receive this child in my name RECEIVETH ME: and whosoever shall RECEIVE ME receiveth him that sent me"]{.class_4723} (vs. 48).

The [practical]{.class_4725} application is that you receive Jesus Christ (John 1:12) with the simple faith of a child. A child is trusting; he will believe what an adult tells him, even when that faith and trust are misplaced. Faith in Jesus Christ and the Bible is [never misplaced]{.class_4725} (Rom. 10:8--17); it will do the job every time.

But there is a deeper, [doctrinal]{.class_4725} application that goes beyond the obvious [spiritual]{.class_4725} application. Notice in the passage here (vs. 48) that to [receive the child]{.class_4725} in Christ's name is to [receive Christ.]{.class_4725} That's not New Testament salvation. But look at Matthew 25:40.

"And the King shall answer and say unto them, Verily I say unto you, Inasmuch as ye have done it unto the LEAST OF THESE MY BRETHREN, ye have done it UNTO ME."

That text is from the Judgment of the Nations (Matt. 25:31--46). If you read the passage, you will see that there isn't one reference to trusting Christ by grace through faith anywhere; the whole thing is [works.]{.class_4725} The works are what the Gentiles do with the Jews in the Tribulation. In Matthew 25:40, the child of Luke 9:48 is [Israel.]{.class_4725}

49 And John answered and said, Master, we saw one casting out devils in thy name; and we forbad him, because he followeth not with us.

50 And Jesus said unto him, Forbid him not: for he that is not against us is for us.

This is John, "the beloved apostle," being jealous of someone else who is ["casting out devils"]{.class_4723} in Christ's name, and the Lord doesn't stop the fellow. When seven ["vagabond Jews"]{.class_4723} attempt it in Acts 19:13--14, they get their clothes torn off by the devil and end up a bloody mess (Acts 19:16). What was the difference between the guy here in Luke 9:49--50 and those seven Jews in Acts 19?

Well, that devil told those seven Jews, ["Jesus I know, and Paul I know; but who are ye?"]{.class_4723} (Acts 19:15). The devils know that Jesus Christ is the one, true God (see our comments on 4:34); they also know who belongs to Christ and who doesn't. The fellow here in Luke 9 [believed on Christ]{.class_4725}; those seven Jewish exorcists in Acts 19 [didn't.]{.class_4725}

["Forbid him not: for he that is not against us is for us"]{.class_4723} (vs. 50). Of course, that is not without some conditions. First, you have to know Christ, and Christ has to know you (Matt. 7:23). Second, you have to be preaching the Jesus of the [Bible,]{.class_4725} not ["another Jesus"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 11:4).

A bunch of Christian "celebrities" are now accepting [Mormons]{.class_4725} (like Glen Beck and Mitt Romney) as "Christians" because they all "believe in Jesus." Not the Jesus of Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John, they don't; they accept the "Jesus" of the [Book of Mormon,]{.class_4725} who was the spirit brother of Lucifer and was not conceived by the Holy Ghost (1:35; Matt. 1:18, 20) but by "Elohim" shacking up with Mary. They preach a "Jesus" who became a god by [works.]{.class_4725} That's [not]{.class_4725} the Jesus of the Holy Bible.

Now don't misunderstand me. I agree with Bob Jones Sr.: "You can go down the [right road]{.class_4725} with anybody." If a Catholic Priest wants to pass around a petition to shut down an X-rated movie theater, I can put my name right beside his. I don't have to compromise the Gospel or the Scriptures one bit to do that. If a Mormon wants to pass a law against faggots getting married so they can get access to your tax money to finance their sex perversion, I can vote right along with him. But I will not "accept" any Mormon as a Christian to get his votes or his money, because [they are not Christians.]{.class_4725}

The Pauline commentary on verse 50 is found in Philippians 1:14--18. In that passage, there are two groups, and [both]{.class_4725} of them are ["brethren."]{.class_4723} That is, they both are [saved children of God]{.class_4725} on their way to Heaven. The first group is encouraged by Paul's testimony to preach the Gospel with boldness in sincerity and love for how Paul is enduring persecution. The other is also preaching the Gospel, but their motive is to prove Paul wrong and ["add affliction"]{.class_4723} in his ["bonds"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 1:16). What was Paul's response to that second group?

["What then? notwithstanding, every way, whether in pretence, or in truth, Christ is preached; and I therein do rejoice, yea, and will rejoice"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 1:18).

I never have cared one way or another whether someone likes me or not. If they love God and believe the Book and are trying to win souls, I am for them no matter what they think of me. They don't have to follow me to be right with God.

One time Joshua came to Moses and said, "There are a couple of guys in the camp named Eldad and Medad, and they are prophesying. You need to stop them because you're God's prophet" (read Num. 11:27--28). Do you know what Moses told Joshua? He said, ["would God ALL the LORD'S people were prophets"]{.class_4723} (Num. 11:29).

I wish there were more churches that preached on the street. Amen! It wouldn't bother me a bit. Every church should be out there saying, "No hope in the Pope! Repent or perish! Turn or burn!" I wish more churches believed the [King James]{.class_4725} Bible was God's perfect word and the Holy Scriptures; I wouldn't care if they were Episcopalians, Lutherans, Presbyterians, Methodists, or Baptists. Do []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_309}you realize, brethren, that at one time in this country, every major Protestant denomination in this country believed that Book no matter what they believed from it? Yeah, and this country was a lot better off when they did.

I don't care if Pensacola Christian College and Bob Jones University both hate my guts. I thank God for them. Sure! I think they are clean places to get a College education, and that they are both doing something for God. Of course, if you are going there to [learn the Bible,]{.class_4725} you are blowing your money down a rat hole. But they are good places to go if you want to learn how to be a Christian teacher or a Christian nurse, etc.

God has called them to do what they are doing, and God has called me to do what I am doing. I don't tell them how to do what God called them to do, and [they shouldn't tell me how to do what God has called me to do.]{.class_4725} I never told John R. Rice how to publish a Christian newspaper; I never told Bob Jones Jr. or III how to run a Christian University; and I never told Jack Hyles how to build a big Sunday school. Bless God! They don't need to tell me how to ground young men in [belief in that Book]{.class_4725}

So if someone is magnifying Jesus Christ and trying to get people saved, I say let'er rip! I have no argument with anybody over anything unless they [mess with that Book.]{.class_4725}

51 And it came to pass, when the time was come that he should be received up, he stedfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem,

52 And sent messengers before his face: and they went, and entered into a village of the Samaritans, to make ready for him.

53 And they did not receive him, because his face was as though he would go to Jerusalem.

54 And when his disciples James and John saw this, they said, Lord, wilt thou that we command fire to come down from heaven, and consume them, even as Elias did?

55 But he turned, and rebuked them, and said, Ye know not what manner of spirit ye are of.

56 For the Son of man is not come to destroy men's []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_310}lives, but to save them. And they went to another village.

Luke has dispensed with the three years of Christ's earthly ministry pretty quickly; verse 51 is already getting near Calvary [chronologically.]{.class_4725} You still have thirteen chapters to go to get to Calvary. You see this kind of "imbalance" all through the Scriptures. Genesis 1--11 covers about 2,000 years of history; the rest of Genesis only covers 231 years. In John, you have five chapters (13--17) that cover only three hours of time. So what you have to remember when making a "harmony of the Gospels" (trying to line up all the events in the life of Christ as recorded in Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John) is that not all events are given in chronological order, and some events are two separate occurrences (see our remarks on the Sermon on the Mount under 6:17).

["He stedfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem"]{.class_4723} (vs. 51). This is a fulfillment of Isaiah 50:7 where Isaiah, speaking prophetically in the first person as a type of Christ (see Isa. 50:6), says, ["I set my face like a flint."]{.class_4723} You see that thing over and over again in the Old Testament. Psalm 22 is a whole chapter on the Crucifixion (note vss. 1, 16, 18). The whole psalm is written by David in the first person as if it's happening to him, and nothing in the chapter is happening to him.

That's the thing that messes up those Jews. God gives material on the blood atonement of Christ in the past tense (see Isa. 53) and in the first person of the prophets (Psa. 22). The Jews look at those passages and say, "Well, it can't be referring to Jesus." [But it is]{.class_4725} (see how Peter handles Psa. 16:8--11 in Acts 2:25--31).

Now, Jesus passes through Samaria on His way to Jerusalem (vs. 52) as He did going from Jerusalem to Galilee in John 4:3--4, but He doesn't receive the same reception in verse 52 as He did in John 4 (see John 4:39--42). The reason is that in John 4, He deals personally with the Samaritan woman one on one, and in turn, she goes and tells the men of the city (John 4:28--30). It's one of their own testifying about Christ. Here in verse 52, Jesus makes an official offer to the Samaritans of this village (vs. 52), and He is rejected (vs. 53) for the reasons the Samaritan woman gave in John 4:9, 20.

Verse 54 shows you why Jesus called James and John ["The sons of thunder"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_311} (Mark 3:17). Before the Resurrection, both of them had quite a tempter. A man should have a temper; it just should not control him. James and John wanted to call fire down from heaven and burn the whole village up as Elijah did to those soldiers Ahaziah sent to bring him in (read 1 Kings 1:9--12).

The Lord rebukes them in verse 55 because this is His [First Coming,]{.class_4725} not the Second Coming. He brings the fire at His [Second Advent]{.class_4725} (see comments on 3:17; also see our comments on Hab. 3:3; Zeph. 3:8, and Mal. 4:1 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Minor Prophets,]{.class_4725} Vol. II).

["And to you who are troubled rest with us, when the Lord Jesus shall be revealed from heaven with his mighty angels]{.class_4723} \[Second Coming\][, IN FLAMING FIRE taking vengeance on them that know not God, and that obey not the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723} (2 Thess. 1:7--8).

James and John have the right idea but the wrong time. Moses and Elijah [will]{.class_4725} call down fire (Rev. 11:5), but [now]{.class_4725} it's: ["the Son of man is come TO SEEK AND TO SAVE that which was lost"]{.class_4723} (19:10). We don't go around trying to kill those who disagree with our religion like a Moslem (see our comments on 6:27--28).

["And they went to another village"]{.class_4723} (vs. 56). The implication is that Jesus and the disciples ["went to another village"]{.class_4723} [of the Samaritans ]{.class_4725}(vs. 52), and that this time, they were received. The old saying is: "You can't win 'em all." Not even Jesus could. If you can't win them in one place, move on to someplace where you can.

57 And it came to pass, that, as they went in the way, a certain man said unto him, Lord, I will follow thee whithersoever thou goest.

58 And Jesus said unto him, Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests; but the Son of man hath not where to lay his head.

59 And he said unto another, Follow me. But he said, Lord, suffer me first to go and bury my father.

60 Jesus said unto him, Let the dead bury their dead: but go thou and preach the kingdom of God.

61 And another also said, Lord, I will follow thee; but let []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_312}me first go bid them farewell, which are at home at my house.

62 And Jesus said unto him, No man, having put his hand to the plough, and looking back, is fit for the kingdom of God.

The passage deals with [discipleship,]{.class_4725} not salvation; the two are not the same.
