There is many a sinner who truly trusts Christ for salvation but never successfully follows his Lord in the Christian life. The doctrine of "Lordship Salvation" ("If Christ is not Lord of all, He is not Lord at all") is a Calvinistic [heresy]{.class_4725} where [works]{.class_4725} are the sole measure of one's salvation. As I have stated on many an occasion: "a hyper-Calvinism always leads to a hyper-Arminianism."

Salvation hinges on the answer to [one question:]{.class_4725} Have you personally received Jesus Christ as your Saviour (John 1:12) by trusting only in His finished blood atonement for your sins (Acts 16:13 cf. Rom. 10:9--10)? Discipleship, however, is an entirely different matter. Discipleship is actively following the Lord Jesus Christ in your daily walk as a believer---works. So here in Luke 9:57--62, there are three tests of discipleship.

1)[ "Lord, I will follow thee whithersoever thou goest" ]{.class_4730-0}(vs. 57). The fellow had good intentions, but he didn't consider the consequences of his decision. If He followed Christ, he might not have a house or a bed in which to sleep (vs. 58). The Apostle Paul told those Christians in Corinth, ["Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ"]{.class_4730-0} (1 Cor. 11:1). Well, if you want to find out what all that may entail, just read 2 Corinthians 11:23--28. Are you ready to go through the kinds of things through which Paul had to go to follow Christ? You had better count the cost (see 14:27--28).

["Foxes have holes"]{.class_4723} (vs. 58); hence the expression "foxhole" in the military. There are a lot of popular expressions like that which are from the Bible. If you went down to the nearest bar and interviewed the first twenty drunks about from where the word [beer]{.class_4725} comes, I dare say not a one of them could tell you. It comes from a [King James]{.class_4725} Bible in Numbers 21:16. The word [highway]{.class_4725} comes from Numbers 20:17. The fact that the average American no longer believes the Bible doesn't stop him from quoting it at least ten times []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_313}a day, every day of his life, before he hits the judgment.

2\) Unlike the first guy in verse 57 and the last guy in verse 61, Jesus commands this second fellow to follow him (vs. 59), just as He did to Levi back in 5:27 (which see). This guy, though, doesn't get up and leave all to follow Christ. Instead he asks Jesus: ["Lord, suffer me ]{.class_4723}\[i.e., "allow me" or "permit me"\][ first to go and bury my father"]{.class_4723} (vs. 59).

Now doesn't that seem like a reasonable request? What could possibly be wrong with that? But look at what that man gets back from Jesus: ["Let the dead bury their dead"]{.class_4723} (vs. 60). "Lord, I'll follow you, but I have a funeral for my daddy I have to attend this afternoon first." "You let that bunch of dead folks bury their own dead, and you follow me." I mean, how rude and uncouth can one get?! Some people are going to be in for a real shock at the Judgment Seat of Christ.

Now about what is the Lord talking when He says, ["Let the dead bury their dead"]{.class_4723}? He's obviously talking about people who are ["dead in trespasses and sins"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 2:1). That would include everyone to whom Christ was talking at the time, for there was no New Birth before the Resurrection (John 7:39 cf. Eph. 2:5). The point Jesus is making here is that [He]{.class_4725} is more important than your father and mother, your spouse and children, and your brothers and sisters (see 14:26). Over in John 12:8, He even put Himself before helping poor folks. It's [Jesus first, others second,]{.class_4725} and [you last.]{.class_4725} J-O-Y: [J]{.class_4725}esus, [O]{.class_4725}thers, [Y]{.class_4725}ou.

["But go thou and preach the kingdom of God."]{.class_4723} The call to preach is more important than momma and daddy. Now if you have a saved momma and daddy who support your decision to enter the ministry as a Bible-believing preacher, that's great, but some of us had to step over an unsaved momma or an unsaved daddy to do what God called us to do. When I got saved, my dad figured it was just another "phase" through which I was going---like the Buddhism, Taoism, and Confucianism into which I had gotten when I was stationed in Japan; or like the Catholicism into which I had gotten just before I got saved. But after watching me for a year he told my mom he thought I was serious about my faith, so he tried to get me into an Episcopalian College in Sewanee, Tennessee to train to be an []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_314}Episcopalian Bishop. Can you imagine me as an Episcopalian Rector in a robe with my collar on backwards?! But I had a choice to make. It was either my daddy or my Saviour. My daddy was a lost man who didn't believe the Book and spent ten years in College trying to disprove Genesis. And I was supposed to take his advice? I went with my Saviour and discarded my father. He was very upset with me for not going to that Episcopalian College. Too bad; tough apples.

3\) Like the first guy, the third fellow volunteers to follow Christ, but he has an excuse for putting it off like the second guy. This fellow's excuse is: ["let me first go bid them farewell, which are at home at my house"]{.class_4723} (vs. 61). ["Let me first\...."]{.class_4723} Let me get married first; let me finish school first; let me pay off my debts first. It's not you first; [He comes first.]{.class_4725} Some people who think they're following Christ are so far behind Him that they don't know which way He's going.

Now what's the problem? The fellow's looking back to his old life and wishing he could still be there even while he's trying to follow Christ. Jesus said, ["No man, having put his hand to the plough, and LOOKING BACK, is fit for the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (vs. 62). You can't plow a straight row looking back over your shoulder; you have to keep your eyes straight ahead on a mark at the end of the row (see Paul's commentary on this in Phil. 3:13--14).

There's an account in the Old Testament that is set in contrast to this last fellow. It is the calling of Elisha in 1 Kings 19:19--21. Elisha is out in the field plowing with twelve yoke of oxen when Elijah comes by and casts his mantle on him. The idea is that God had chosen Elisha to be the successor to Elijah. Look at 1 Kings 19:20 and note what Elisha says and how Elijah answers him.

["And he]{.class_4723} \[i.e., Elisha\][ left the oxen, and ran after Elijah, and said, Let me, I pray thee, kiss my father and my mother, and THEN I will follow thee. And he said unto him, Go back again: for what have I done to thee?"]{.class_4723}

Elisha asked Elijah for the same thing the guy here in vs. 61 asked Christ: to go tell his family goodbye before he began his discipleship. Elijah's answer was basically: "Go do whatever you want to do; I'm not the one putting any demands on you." The difference between Elisha and the fellow here in Luke 9 is that Elisha wasn't looking back []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ1895.xhtml_page_315}desiring the old life; he was going home to cut off all contact with it. Look at what he did in 1 Kings 19:21.

"And he returned back from him, and took a yoke of oxen, and slew them, and boiled their flesh with the instruments of the oxen, and gave unto the people, and they did eat. Then he arose, and went after Elijah, and ministered unto him."

Elisha made sure he [couldn't]{.class_4725} go back to the old life by killing the oxen with which he had been plowing and burning their instruments. We would say he "burned his bridges behind him." He made sure he couldn't "look back." The problem with a lot of Christians is, they keep the path to the old life clear in case they don't like following Jesus Christ.

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml}

# Chapter 10 {.heading_78-0}

10:1 After these things the Lord appointed other seventy also, and sent them two and two before his face into every city and place, whither he himself would come.

2 Therefore said he unto them, The harvest truly is great, but the labourers are few: pray ye therefore the Lord of the harvest, that he would send forth labourers into his harvest.

3 Go your ways: behold, I send you forth as lambs among wolves.

4 Carry neither purse, nor scrip, nor shoes: and salute no man by the way.

5 And into whatsoever house ye enter, first say, Peace be to this house.

6 And if the son of peace be there, your peace shall rest upon it: if not, it shall turn to you again.

7 And in the same house remain, eating and drinking such things as they give: for the labourer is worthy of his hire. Go not from house to house.

8 And into whatsoever city ye enter, and they receive you, eat such things as are set before you:

9 And heal the sick that are therein, and say unto them, The kingdom of God is come nigh unto you.

10 But into whatsoever city ye enter, and they receive you not, go your ways out into the streets of the same, and say,

11 Even the very dust of your city, which cleaveth on us, we do wipe off against you: notwithstanding be ye sure of this, that the kingdom of God is come nigh unto you.

This is the commissioning of the ["seventy"]{.class_4723} (vs. 1). Back in 9:1--6, Jesus picked out twelve of His disciples and sent them out to preach. They were called ["apostles"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 10:2) because the word [apostle]{.class_4725} means "sent one." Here in chapter 10, Jesus picks out seventy more disciples and sends them out with the same []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_317}commission, so they too could rightly be called "apostles," although you never read of the title being applied to this second group. What you do read about is a couple of "dark horses" named ["Andronicus and Junia"]{.class_4723} who were Jewish apostles ["in Christ"]{.class_4723} before the Apostle Paul was saved (Rom. 16:7). There's a good chance those two men were part of this group sent out here in Luke 10.

Like the number [twelve,]{.class_4725} the number [seventy]{.class_4725} is connected with Israel. Seventy members of Jacob's family went down into Egypt to join Joseph (Gen. 46:27). There were seventy elders who represented the nation of Israel (Exod. 24:1, 9; Num. 11:25; Ezek. 8:11). So the twelve apostles match the twelve patriarchs, and the seventy match the seventy elders.

While much of what you read in verses 1--11 matches the original commission given to the twelve in Matthew 10:1--16, there are some significant [differences.]{.class_4725} The big difference is the [message.]{.class_4725} The seventy are sent out to preach the [Kingdom of God]{.class_4725} (vss. 9, 11), not the Kingdom of Heaven (Matt. 10:7). The seventy are to preach an inward, spiritual kingdom of righteousness; [not]{.class_4725} a visible, literal, physical Jewish kingdom with the Son of David on the throne in Jerusalem (see our comments on 6:20). Because of that, there is no restriction placed on the seventy to preach only in [Jewish]{.class_4725} cities as was placed on the twelve in Matthew 10:5--6.

The ["harvest"]{.class_4723} of verse 2 pops up three times in Christ's ministry: once at the beginning (John 4:35--36), once in the middle (Matt. 9:38), and once at the end (vs. 2 cf. 9:51). This matches the three stages of harvest about which you read in the Scriptures: firstfruits, harvest, and gleanings. Once the firstfruits come in, the harvest begins (John 4:35).

The Christian is to sow the word (see our remarks under 8:5) and reap the harvest (win souls). Jesus said that the one who sows and the one who reaps ["rejoice together"]{.class_4723} (John 4:36). Paul adds the task of watering the seed (1 Cor. 3:6--7) and says, ["he that planteth and he that watereth are one"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 3:8). Why? Because it is ["God that giveth the increase"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 3:7).

If you are involved in ministry or personal work, there will be times you will win someone to the Lord who had the seed planted by another soulwinner or pastor. You weren't the first one to give that []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_318}person the Gospel, but the Lord let you reap the harvest. By the same token, there will be times you witness to someone who doesn't get saved, but later on the Lord lets another Christian lead that person to Christ. Sometimes you will [plant,]{.class_4725} sometimes you will [plow,]{.class_4725} sometimes you will [water,]{.class_4725} and sometimes you will [reap.]{.class_4725} But never forget that it is the [Lord]{.class_4725} who saves the soul, [not you.]{.class_4725} You just be faithful to labor in the field where He puts you.

["The harvest truly is great"]{.class_4723} (vs. 2). It starts out with the firstfruits in the Gospels and gets into full swing in the book of Acts. It reached its peak in the Nineteenth Century with the Philadelphia church period where the Lord set before them ["an open door"]{.class_4723} that no one could shut (Rev. 3:8). That was the great age of revival and missions. The Gospel was making such great strides that people like Darwin thought everything was getting better and better. You are living now in Laodicea where you are only picking up the gleanings of the harvest. Yes, there are still pockets of activity here and there around the globe (e.g., Africa, India, the Philippines, and China), but you are no longer seeing the great numbers come to Christ like they did before World War I. The big churches these days aren't being built on soulwinning, Bible teaching, or the preaching of the Gospel. They're being built on compromise with the world, positive messages pandering to middle class suburbanites, rock music, self-help, and "Christian" psychology. A lost person can come to a church and be told he's O.K.; he just needs to fix up a few things in his life to be acceptable to God. [That's not the harvest.]{.class_4725}

The harvest these days is in the prisons. That's because you don't have to convince the guys in the slammer that they're sinners. Most of the lost people who come to church think they're basically good and there's nothing wrong with them. As a result, they see no need to accept Christ. Those prisoners [know]{.class_4725} they're sinners, so when you present the Gospel that Jesus Christ will save sinners just for the asking, a lot of them will respond.

Every Christian church should be occupied with getting the Gospel out on the streets, in the jails and prisons, and in the hospitals and nursing homes. The emphasis should be on [missions]{.class_4725}---getting the Gospel overseas to those who've never heard it. That's a church operating at 100%. Most churches never get beyond 25%; []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_319}their mission giving is less than a fifth of their income.

["But the labourers are few."]{.class_4723} You're not kidding they are few. When Jesus said that, there were only 82 preachers for a world population of approximately 180 million. That was one preacher for every 2,200,000 people. Things are better off now than they were then. According to one source ([Operation World,]{.class_4725} 2001), there are now 201,928 missionaries to the entire world (including the United States and Canada); that's one missionary for every 30,000 people. But that number includes all the Liberals, all the "water dogs" ([baptismal regeneration]{.class_4725}), all the tongues-blabbers (Charismatics and Pentecostals), and all the other Protestant groups that add works to salvation. If you count only the missionaries preaching the Biblical Gospel and Biblical salvation as found in 1 Corinthians 15:1--4; Acts 16:31; Romans 10:9--13; Ephesians 2:8--9; Titus 3:5; etc., the ratio would be far greater. It would be like only one preacher having to reach the entire population of Pensacola all by himself. ["The labourers are few"]{.class_4723}; they are so few it is pitiful.

["Pray ye therefore the Lord of the harvest, that he would send forth labourers into his harvest."]{.class_4723} The command to ["pray ye therefore"]{.class_4723} is followed up by the command to ["go ye therefore"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 28:19). The reason most Christians never pray for the Lord to send laborers into the harvest is because they are afraid He might call [them.]{.class_4725}

Verse 3:[ "Go your ways: behold, I send you forth as lambs among wolves."]{.class_4723} Dangerous company; the Lord certainly doesn't paint a rosy picture for this second group of evangelists. They're not just ["sheep,"]{.class_4723} as the Lord told the twelve in Matthew 10:16; they're ["lambs."]{.class_4723} The ["wolves"]{.class_4723} are right in ["the flock"]{.class_4723} (Acts 20:29), [disguised as sheep]{.class_4725} (Matt. 7:15). Fortunately, they have a Good Shepherd who will lay down His life for them and keep them safe when the wolves attack (John 10:11--15, 27--29).

We have covered much of the material in verses 4--11 in our comments on 9:1--5 (which see). Here we will pick up a few "loose ends" not covered there.

Verse 4: ["And salute no man by the way."]{.class_4723} Due to the importance of the task, they weren't to stand around "shooting the bull" with folks they met. That's what Elisha commanded Gehazi to []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_320}do in 2 Kings 4:29 when he sent him ahead to raise the great woman of Shunem's son. John Wesley made it a point never to carry on a conversation longer than fifteen minutes.

Verses 5--6: ["And into whatsoever house ye enter, first say, Peace be to this house. And if the son of peace be there, your peace shall rest upon it: if not, it shall turn to you again."]{.class_4723} The standard Jewish greeting is ["shalom aleichem"]{.class_4725}---"peace be unto you." The greeting in verse 5 is much more than a casual salutation; it is connected to the preaching of the Kingdom of God. The seventy are preaching about ["The Prince of Peace"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 9:6). The implication of the greeting is if you believe that Jesus is the ["Shiloh"]{.class_4723} prophesied in the Old Testament (Gen. 49:10), then you're a ["son of peace"]{.class_4723} (an inward condition of the heart---Rom. 14:17) and the peace of God rests upon you (see Phil. 4:7; Col. 3:15).

Notice that peace has sons (see our remarks on [reconciliation]{.class_4725} under 2:14) just as wisdom has children (see our comments on 7:35). Jesus Christ is peace personified (Eph. 2:14), just as He is wisdom personified (1 Cor. 1:30).

The peace turning back to the evangelist is an old Oriental belief. The idea is if you don't accept an insult, it returns upon the person who made it. Buddha had a fellow spit on him one time, and Buddha told him, "I do not receive it." In other words, the offense was on the guy who spit because Buddha didn't accept it.

["Go not from house to house"]{.class_4723} (vs. 7). This has nothing to do with the "door-to-door visitation" mentioned in Acts 20:20. In the context, Jesus is telling the seventy not to beg for food ["from house to house."]{.class_4723} They are to go to one house and greet it (in Matt. 10:11, Jesus told the twelve to ask around to find out who was ["worthy"]{.class_4723}; i.e., who was suitable to receive their message). If they were received with peace, then they were to stay there and eat whatever was set before them. If they weren't received, they were to shake off the dust of the city and move on to the next town.

Back to verse 7: ["the labourer is worthy of his hire."]{.class_4723} This is a principle repeated by Paul for the New Testament ministry (1 Tim. 5:17--18; see also 1 Cor. 9:7--14). Bob Jones Sr. used to say that a man called to the ministry by God is entitled to anything he gets in line with God's purpose for his life, and that he is entitled to nothing []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_321}he gets that is not in line with that purpose. That means if you are doing what God wants you to do in the ministry the way He wants it done, then you can accept any good thing that comes your way. If someone wants to treat you right because they appreciate your ministry, let them; if they want to give you a gift or buy you something, take it. But you are not to go out of your way or do wrong to get it. If you've got to lie or politick or cut corners to get what you want, you are no longer entitled to it.

12 But I say unto you, that it shall be more tolerable in that day for Sodom, than for that city.

13 Woe unto thee, Chorazin! woe unto thee, Bethsaida! for if the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon, which have been done in you, they had a great while ago repented, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.

14 But it shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon at the judgment, than for you.

15 And thou, Capernaum, which art exalted to heaven, shalt be thrust down to hell.

16 He that heareth you heareth me; and he that despiseth you despiseth me; and he that despiseth me despiseth him that sent me.

["That day"]{.class_4723} in verse 12 is defined as ["the day of judgment"]{.class_4723} in Matthew 11:24 and Mark 6:11. While it can be a reference to a particular day when God judges a city as He did to Sodom (see the wording at the end of Matt. 11:23), ["that day"]{.class_4723} in the Old Testament Prophets is a reference to ["the day of the LORD"]{.class_4723} (see Isa. 2:11--12)---the Second Advent. It can encompass everything from the beginning of the Tribulation (see our comments on 2 Thess. 2:2--3 in that Commentary) to the White Throne Judgment at the end of the Millennium (see our comments on 2 Pet. 3:10 in that Commentary). Verse 14 clearly sets ["that day"]{.class_4723} here in this passage as the White Throne Judgment.

Now, Jesus is "bawling out" the main cities in Galilee in which He ministered: Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum. Jesus is saying that if the miracles which He had done in those cities had been done []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_322}in Sodom and Gomorrha, or Tyre and Sidon, that the inhabitants of those cities would have repented in ["sackcloth and ashes"]{.class_4723} and avoided the judgment. The city of Nineveh repented at far less.

["The men of Nineveh shall rise in judgment with this generation, and shall condemn it: because they repented at the preaching of Jonas; and behold, a greater than Jonas is here"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 12:41).

Jonah didn't heal anyone or raise anyone from the dead or stop a thunderstorm; he just went through Nineveh preaching, and those Assyrians got right. Jesus had ministered in those Jewish cities for three years, and they hadn't gotten right yet. Such passages are the death knell for the United States, for God has done more for this nation and given it more light than any other nation on earth. My, my, my, what a time Americans are going to have when they hit the judgment.

Verses 12 and 14 are quite a statement to make. Christ-rejection is a worse sin than either pagan idolatry or sex perversion.

The word ["hell"]{.class_4723} in verse 15 means exactly what it says. Refusing to translate the word [hades]{.class_4725} doesn't do anything for the text as far as accuracy or clarity. Notice the comparison is to Sodom and Tyre. Sodom wasn't just burned to the ground by the Lord; it suffers ["the vengeance of ETERNAL FIRE"]{.class_4723} (Jude 7). Tyre is brought down to the pit just like the Antichrist (Ezek. 26:20 cf. Isa. 14:15). That Pit is called Hell (Isa. 14:15) and is said to be ["a lake of fire burning with brimstone"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 19:20). What versions change the text so "all is well, there is no Hell"? The [Revised Standard Version]{.class_4725} of the Liberal National Council of Churches, the [New American Standard Version]{.class_4725} of the "godly [Fundamentalists"]{.class_4725} in the Lockman Foundation, the [English Standard Version]{.class_4725} (the supposedly ["Conservative"]{.class_4725}[ ]{.class_4737}version of the [RSV]{.class_4725}), and the [NEW KING JAMES VERSION]{.class_4725} put out by Thomas Nelson and Sons ([the original publishers of the RSV]{.class_4725}) and promoted by Jerry Falwell before he died. Folks, mark it down and get it: the [NKJV]{.class_4725} is NOT a [King James Version;]{.class_4725} it is a warmed-over [RSV.]{.class_4725}

Verse 16: ["He that heareth you heareth me."]{.class_4723} Paul said, ["Now then we are AMBASSADORS FOR CHRIST, as though God did beseech you BY US: we pray you IN CHRIST'S STEAD, be ye reconciled to God"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_323} (2 Cor. 5:20). He told those Corinthians: ["To whom ye forgive any thing, I forgive also\...for your sakes forgave I it IN THE PERSON OF CHRIST"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 2:10). The Christian represents his Lord; in fact, the word [Christian]{.class_4725} means "little Christ."

["He that despiseth you despiseth me."]{.class_4723} Jesus told the disciples, ["If the world hate you, ye know that it hated me before it hated you"]{.class_4723} (John 15:18). When the Lord knocked Saul of Taursus to the ground on his way to Damascus, He said, ["Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?]{.class_4723} (Acts 9:4); and ["I am Jesus whom thou persecutest"]{.class_4723} (Acts 9:5). Well, Saul couldn't persecute Jesus personally; the Lord had returned to Heaven (Acts 1:9). But he had been persecuting ["the disciples of the Lord"]{.class_4723} (Acts 9:1), and in so doing, he had been persecuting Jesus by proxy.

["He that despiseth me despiseth him that sent me."]{.class_4723} Jesus said, ["He that honoureth not the Son honoureth not the Father which hath sent him"]{.class_4723} (John 5:22). The born-again Christian is one with his Saviour and is sent out in the same way his Saviour was sent by the Father (John 17:23, 20:21). When a lost man rejects the Gospel witness of a Christian, he is rejecting the person of Jesus Christ in the believer (1 Thess. 4:8 cf. Col. 1:27), the Father who sent forth His Son to save the sinner, and the child of God who presented the message of salvation.

By despising Jesus Christ as the Son of God, Mohammed sinned against God, after claiming it was "the God" who gave him the revelation of the [Koran.]{.class_4725} The one, true God never called Mohammed, He never spoke to Mohammed, He never revealed anything to Mohammed, and He never sent Mohammed to do anything. The God who created the universe sent His only begotten Son down to this earth to die for sinners (Rom. 5:8; Phil. 2:8). [Mohammed despised that God.]{.class_4725}

17 And the seventy returned again with joy, saying, Lord, even the devils are subject unto us through thy name.

18 And he said unto them, I beheld Satan as lightning fall from heaven.

19 Behold, I give unto you power to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy: and nothing shall by any means hurt you.

20 Notwithstanding in this rejoice not, that the spirits are subject unto you; but rather rejoice, because your names are written in heaven.

The seventy return from their evangelistic junket excited over the fact that the devils did what they told them to do in the name of Jesus Christ. The Lord completely ignores what they said and replies, ["I beheld Satan as lightning fall from heaven"]{.class_4723} (vs. 18).

Because the statement is given in the past tense, all the scholars fall into the bear trap of making this a reference to the fall of Satan in Isaiah 14 and Ezekiel 28. There are [five]{.class_4725} times Satan is cast down in the Scriptures (five being the number of [death]{.class_4725}, with the Devil being given ["the power of death"]{.class_4723} by God---Heb. 2:14. By sheer coincidence---of course, of course--- the words [Satan]{.class_4725} and [Devil]{.class_4725} both have [five letters]{.class_4725}). They are as follows:

1\. From his position in the third heaven as ["the anointed cherub"]{.class_4723} that covered the throne of God down to the second heaven before Genesis 1:2 (see Ezek. 28:14--16).

2\. From the second heaven to the first heaven (see Eph. 2:21) at some point before Luke 10:18.

3\. From the first heaven down to earth during the Tribulation (Rev. 12:7--9, 12).

4\. From the earth to the Bottomless Pit at the Second Advent (Rev. 20:1--3).

5\. From the Bottomless Pit to the Lake of Fire at the White Throne Judgment (Rev. 20:10).

Notice the connection of ["Satan"]{.class_4723} to ["lightning."]{.class_4723} That's [electricity;]{.class_4725} that's the [electromagnetic forcefield]{.class_4725} surrounding the earth. So in Daniel 11:38, ["the prince of the POWER OF THE AIR"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 2:2---lightning comes down through [air]{.class_4725}) and ["the god of this world"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 4:4) is called ["the God of forces."]{.class_4723} Or as they say in "Star Wars": "May the FORCE be with you." During the Tribulation, he's able to call the lightning down from heaven to []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_325}deceive men so they will worship him in the person of the Antichrist (Rev. 13:13--14).

Back to verse 19. While the ["serpents and scorpions"]{.class_4723} could have reference to the literal creatures (see Mark 16:18; Acts 28:3--6), it is obvious from the context that the reference is to the Devil and his devils. The word [Satan]{.class_4725} means "enemy" or "adversary." The ["scorpions"]{.class_4723} are men who rebel against God and speak against His words (Ezek. 2:6).

While a spiritual application certainly can be made to the apostles in the book of Acts, the doctrinal meaning, as usual, is [prophetic.]{.class_4725} During the Tribulation, there are a group of Jewish evangelists (Rev. 7:3--8) who preach to ["the cities of Israel"]{.class_4723} before the Lord comes back (see our comments on Matt. 10:23 in that Commentary). They face those serpents and scorpions literally (Rev. 12:9--17, 9:3--10) and are given power by Jesus Christ to tread them under feet (Psa. 91:13).

In verse 20, Jesus tells them that if they are going to be happy about something (vs. 17), they should take joy in the fact that their ["names are written in heaven."]{.class_4723} "There's a new name written down in Glory, and it's [mine,]{.class_4725} O yes, it's [mine]{.class_4725}!" WELL GLORY! It doesn't matter whether or not I have my name in "Who's Who"; what matters is that my name is in the Lamb's Book of Life (Rev. 21:27).

21 In that hour Jesus rejoiced in spirit, and said, I thank thee, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes: even so, Father; for so it seemed good in thy sight.

22 All things are delivered to me of my Father: and no man knoweth who the Son is, but the Father; and who the Father is, but the Son, and he to whom the Son will reveal him.

23 And he turned him unto his disciples, and said privately, Blessed are the eyes which see the things that ye see:

24 For I tell you, that many prophets and kings have desired to see those things which ye see, and have not seen []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_326}them; and to hear those things which ye hear, and have not heard them.

Verse 21 is the only record in the life of Christ where Jesus rejoiced over something. What caused the Son of God to rejoice while here on earth? He rejoiced over the fact that His Father made some smart folks [stupid]{.class_4725} and some simpletons [smart.]{.class_4725}

["I thank thee, O Father\..."]{.class_4723} (vs. 21). Jesus just didn't rejoice over it; He thanked God that He did it! ["I thank thee, O Father\...that thou hast HID these things from the wise and prudent."]{.class_4723} It wasn't just the fact that the smart boys didn't get it; the Lord [purposefully hid it from them]{.class_4725}. Why? Because they didn't want it when He offered it to them.

["The wise and prudent"]{.class_4723} are a reference to the chief priests, scribes, and Pharisees. They rejected the witness of John the Baptist, and they rejected Jesus as the Messiah, so God takes any further revelation from them and [hides]{.class_4725} it. In the meantime, He shows things to a bunch of publicans, harlots, and fishermen because they receive and believe the words of God (see our comments on 8:15).

The principle laid down by Christ in verse 21 is so strong in the Scriptures that it should be counted as one of the "fundamentals of the faith." It is mentioned more times than the Virgin Birth.

["Thus saith the LORD, Let not the wise man glory in his wisdom"]{.class_4723} (Jer. 9:23).

["Whom shall he teach knowledge? and whom shall he make to understand doctrine? them that are weaned from the milk, and drawn from the breasts"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 28:9).

["For the LORD hath poured out upon you the spirit of deep sleep, and hath closed your eyes: the prophets and your rulers, the seers hath he covered. And the vision of all is become unto you as the words of a book that is sealed, which men deliver to one that is learned, saying, Read this, I pray thee: and he saith, I cannot; for it is sealed\...Wherefore the Lord said, Forasmuch as this people draw near me with their mouth, and with their lips do honour me, but have removed their heart far from me, and their fear toward me is taught by the precept of men\...for []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_327}the wisdom of their wise men shall perish, and the understanding of their prudent shall be hid"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 29:10--14).

This thing is all through the Bible. God thinks more of it than He does the Virgin Birth or the New Birth. When I got saved, my mother-in-law up in Alabama sent me a [Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_4725} with Proverbs 3:5--6 written in the front. That old redneck farm woman couldn't have picked out a better passage for someone like me who fancied himself an "intellectual."

"Trust in the LORD with all thine heart; and LEAN NOT UNTO THINE OWN UNDERSTANDING. In all thy ways acknowledge him, and he shall direct thy paths."

Parents in America make a big deal about their kids getting a College education. Well, if all you want is to get a College education just to have a College education, then maybe you should spend your money on something worthwhile. A College education can be a real hindrance sometimes. A lot of College graduates these days are out of work because no employers need or want a "liberal arts" graduate trained in the "humanities"; they want someone with some kind of practical skill. And when it comes to salvation and spirituality, a College education more often than not will [damn you to Hell.]{.class_4727}

["For the preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness; but unto us which are saved it is the power of God. For it is written, I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and will bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent. Where is the wise? Where is the scribe? where is the disputer of this world? hath not God made foolish the wisdom of this world? For after that in the wisdom of God the world by wisdom knew not God, it pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 1:18--21).

["But God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise; and God hath chosen the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty; And base things of the world, and things which are despised, hath God chosen, yea, and things which are not, to bring to nought things that are"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 1:27--28).

That Bible says, ["Men of HIGH DEGREE are a lie"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 62:9). []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_328}I guess all the Ph.D.s and Litt.D.s and D.D.s, etc., missed that one, or at least pretended it wasn't there when they read it. Paul's advice to such men is: ["If any man among you seemeth to be wise in this world, let him become a fool, that he may be wise"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 3:18). Why? Because ["the wisdom of this world ]{.class_4723}\[like a College or University education\][ is foolishness with God. For it is written, He taketh the wise in their own craftiness. And again, The Lord knoweth the thoughts of the wise, that they are vain"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 3:19--20).

Back to Luke 10. Verse 22 lays down another principle abhorrent to the modern movement to integrate all religions into one mongrel mass. Jesus said no one can know the Father except through the Son, and no one can know the Son except through the Father (John 5:19--38, 14:6; 1 John 2:22--23, 5:10--12). That means Mohammed never [knew]{.class_4725} God, never [saw]{.class_4725} God, never [heard]{.class_4725} God, never [communed with]{.class_4725} God, and never [received any revelation from]{.class_4725} God. Why is that? Because Mohammed said "the God" (Allah) [had no Son]{.class_4725} (Sura 3:58, 9:30, 17:39, 2:116, 72:3, 6:102, 37:152---LOOK THEM UP AND READ THEM! Don't just take my word for it; get it from the camel's mouth).

Moreover, the only way either the Father or Son can be revealed to any man is by the WORDS in the Holy Scriptures (John 8:47, 3:34; Deut. 18:18--19). No matter what you do, you aren't going to get around the WORDS (and I don't mean the "Word" or fundamentals or principles) in that Book (John 6:68; 2 Tim. 3:15; 1 Pet. 1:23--25).

Verses 23--24 reveal that the disciples had a special blessing that the Old Testament saints did not have: they got to [see]{.class_4725} the fulfillment of the Old Testament prophecies regarding the First Coming of the Messiah (see our comments on 1:2). Now I must admit, I would like to have seen all this stuff about which I read. I don't like living by faith any more than you do. I mean, if you love Jesus Christ, you want to see Him, don't you? Sure you do. But the Lord has given you a blessing that He didn't give the disciples.

He told Thomas, ["because thou hast seen me, thou hast believed: blessed are they that have not seen, and yet have believed"]{.class_4723} (John 20:29). Well, that includes [me]{.class_4725}; it includes [you]{.class_4725} if you've trusted Christ. Peter said, ["Whom having not seen, ye love; in whom, though now ye see him not, yet believing, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_329}ye rejoice with joy unspeakable and full of glory: Receiving the end of your faith, even the salvation of your souls"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 1:8--9).

One of these days, I'll see the One about whom I've been preaching for 62 years. When I do, it won't be the earthly Christ the disciples saw for three-and-a-half years (2 Cor. 5:18); it will be the Lord of Glory who appeared to John on the isle of Patmos (Rev. 1:9--18). I'll ["see him as he is,"]{.class_4723} and when I do, I'll be ["like him"]{.class_4723} (1 John 3:2) because I'll be [conformed to His image]{.class_4725} (Rom. 8:29). That's the One whom I'm waiting to see (1 Thess. 1:10; Tit. 2:13).

25 And, behold, a certain lawyer stood up, and tempted him, saying, Master, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?

26 He said unto him, What is written in the law? how readest thou?

27 And he answering said, Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy strength, and with all thy mind; and thy neighbour as thyself.

28 And he said unto him, Thou hast answered right: this do, and thou shalt live.

29 But he, willing to justify himself, said unto Jesus, And who is my neighbour?

Now, we are about to get into the Parable of the Good Samaritan, but it is prefaced by what takes place in verses 25--29. ["A certain lawyer"]{.class_4723} comes to Jesus (vs. 25). He's not a lawyer as we understand the term today; that is, he's not an advocate in a court case. He's someone who is well versed in the Law of Moses.

Christ says over in chapter 11, verse 46: ["Woe unto you also, ye lawyers!"]{.class_4723} He didn't think much of them. Paul talks about a good lawyer named ["Zenas"]{.class_4723} (Tit. 3:13), so they're not all crooked. But the one here in verse 25 and the one in Matthew 22:35 try to trip up Jesus in His words.

["Master, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?"]{.class_4723} The lawyer asks Jesus the same question the rich young ruler does in 18:18, and he gets basically the same answer: [keep the commandments]{.class_4725} (18:20)---[works.]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_330} There's not one word here or in Luke 18 about faith in Christ; it's ["the law"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26) and ["the commandments"]{.class_4723} (18:20). This is Old Testament salvation under the Law; it is faith [plus works.]{.class_4725} (Note Christ's words in vs. 28---["this DO, and thou shalt live."]{.class_4723})

When Jesus asks the lawyer ["What is written in the law"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26), the lawyer correctly gives the two great commandments which are the summary of all the Law and the Prophets (Matt. 22:35--40): love God [first]{.class_4725} and love neighbor [second.]{.class_4725} The Modernists, Liberals, and "emergents" put the second commandment before the first, or worse yet, make the keeping of the second great commandment synonymous with the keeping of the first---[humanism.]{.class_4725} This is a road that ends up in the Marxist Socialism and Communism of Lenin, Stalin, and Mao Tse-Tung. The only way it can exist is by eliminating all those who put God first before their "fellow man." Its culmination is a one-world government and religion under the control of Satan (Rev. 13).

One should note that the two great commandments are a summation of [the Law]{.class_4725} (Deut. 6:5; Lev. 19:18), [not the Gospel.]{.class_4725} If you are counting on loving God with all your heart, soul, strength, and mind to get you to Heaven, you are headed for a rude awakening in the Lake of Fire. No one, outside of the Son of God Himself, loves God completely; everyone falls short somewhere (Rom. 3:23).

The first step to loving God in the New Testament is admitting that [you don't.]{.class_4725}

["Herein is love, NOT THAT WE LOVED GOD, but that HE LOVED US, and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins"]{.class_4723} (1 John 4:10).

["But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 5:8).

No man in his natural state loves God as he ought, no matter what he does. He is an enemy of God who requires reconciliation (Rom. 5:10). ["The love of God"]{.class_4723}---whether it's God loving you or you loving God (1 John 4:19)---is found ["in Christ Jesus our Lord"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:39), and until you receive Him by faith (Rom. 5:1), you will never truly love God (Rom. 5:5). And until that happens, you can never love your neighbor properly; the command to love your neighbor in the Pauline Epistles (Rom. 13:8--10) comes []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_331}[after]{.class_4725} Paul has dealt with the fact that [the Law cannot save you]{.class_4725} (Rom. 8:3--4).

Verse 29:[ "But he, willing to JUSTIFY HIMSELF."]{.class_4723} Now the false motive of the lawyer comes out. This lawyer is not like the scribe of Mark 12:28--34 who asked Jesus an honest question and was commended by the Lord for his response. The lawyer here in Luke 10 knows perfectly well that he doesn't keep the first great commandment, so he deals with the second.[ "And who is my neighbour?"]{.class_4723}

He's trying to find the "loophole" in the Law; in that sense, he's a typical lawyer. That's the deviousness of the human mind. "What do you mean by 'born again' (John 3:4)?" "What do you mean by 'Christ' (Matt. 22:41--46 cf. 26:63--65)?" "What do you mean by 'neighbor' (here)?" "What do you mean by 'what do you mean'?" He's trying to find a way out of loving his neighbor while still appearing to do so.

30 And Jesus answering said, A certain man went down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and fell among thieves, which stripped him of his raiment, and wounded him, and departed, leaving him half dead.

31 And by chance there came down a certain priest that way: and when he saw him, he passed by on the other side.

32 And likewise a Levite, when he was at the place, came and looked on him, and passed by on the other side.

33 But a certain Samaritan, as he journeyed, came where he was: and when he saw him, he had compassion on him,

34 And went to him, and bound up his wounds, pouring in oil and wine, and set him on his own beast, and brought him to an inn, and took care of him.

35 And on the morrow when he departed, he took out two pence, and gave them to the host, and said unto him, Take care of him; and whatsoever thou spendest more, when I come again, I will repay thee.

36 Which now of these three, thinkest thou, was neighbour unto him that fell among the thieves?

37 And he said, He that shewed mercy on him. Then said Jesus unto him, Go, and do thou likewise.

This is the Parable of the Good Samaritan. Jesus tells it in answer to the lawyer's question in verse 29:[ "And who is my neighbour?"]{.class_4723}

Now if you went to any major Christian College, University, or Seminary in the country, you would be taught: "A parable only presents a central truth and cannot be used to teach doctrine." That is a lie which flies straight in the face of Scripture.

Jesus only interprets two parables in the Gospels (the Parable of the Sower and the Parable of the Wheat and the Tares), and in both of them He assigns doctrinal meaning to every detail of the parable. The Scriptures are first and foremost given ["for doctrine"]{.class_4755} (2 Tim. 3:16), and parables are not exempt from that purpose. So aside from the "central truth" of ["Go, and do thou likewise"]{.class_4755} (vs. 37), we may seek doctrinal truth from the typology presented by Christ in the parable.

["A certain man went down from Jerusalem to Jericho"]{.class_4723} (vs. 30). Jerusalem is ["the holy city"]{.class_4723} (Neh. 11:1); Jericho is a [cursed]{.class_4725} city (Josh. 6:26). In type, you have a man going from the blessing of God to the curse of God, so when Jesus said the ["man went down,"]{.class_4723} it is much more than going from the mountainous city of Jerusalem to the plain city of Jericho. It's a picture of a man descending into sin and destruction.

Along the way the man ["fell among thieves, which stripped him of his raiment, and wounded him, and departed, leaving him half dead."]{.class_4723} There is no doubt whom the ["thieves"]{.class_4723} picture. Jesus said, ["The thief cometh not, but for to steal, and to kill, and to destroy"]{.class_4723} (John 10:10). It's a picture of the Devil who steals the word of God from a sinner's heart with the intent of damning him to Hell (8:12). The sinner is stripped of any goodness (Rom. 3:10--18) and left in the filthy rags of his own self-righteousness (Isa. 64:6). He is ["wounded"]{.class_4723} to the point where he cannot help himself. Bob Jones Sr. used to say, "Sin never leaves a man better than it finds him."

Notice that the man is only ["half dead," ]{.class_4723}[not completely dead.]{.class_4725} In Calvinism, the sinner is completely dead spiritually and is []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_333}[unable]{.class_4725} to respond to the Gospel. "Regeneration" must precede faith in Calvin's ["Ordo Salutis"]{.class_4725}; that is, a sinner has to be "born again" before he is born again. The Holy Spirit has to regenerate a sinner's dead spirit before he can receive Christ. If you are under the decree of election, the Lord will regenerate you and draw you by Irresistible Grace to receive Christ. If you are under the decree of reprobation, the Holy Spirit leaves you alone, and you can't receive Christ because in Calvinism "Total Depravity" means [total inability]{.class_4725} to respond to God in faith.

BUNK! The only part of you that is ["dead in trespasses and sins"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 2:1) is your [spirit.]{.class_4725} You have a ["living soul"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 2:7), and if you didn't, you wouldn't be breathing. The will is part of the [soul,]{.class_4725} which is evident by the fact that [no one]{.class_4725} in the Old Testament was born again---they were all ["dead in trespasses and sins"]{.class_4723}---yet they all had a freewill that could choose to serve God (Ezra 1:4, 6, 7:13; Psa. 119:108; Exod. 35:21--22; Gen. 24:58). The sinner is only half dead: he has a dead spirit but a live soul with a live will.

There's a famous tale that comes to us from medieval times. A mischievous boy decided to trick an old man who was purported to be very wise. The boy caught a sparrow and held it behind his back. He then asked the old man whether he had a live sparrow or a dead sparrow. He figured if the old man said live, he would crush the sparrow and produce a dead bird, but if the answer were dead, he would release the live sparrow into the old man's face. The boy asked his question, and the old man wisely answered, "That depends entirely upon you."

Now look, you've got a soul in your hands. Is it going to Heaven, or is it going to Hell? Well, that depends entirely on you. It depends on what you do with Jesus Christ that determines which way you will go. If you accept Him as your Saviour, you will go to Heaven; if you reject Him, you'll go to Hell. But [you]{.class_4725} make that decision; God doesn't make it for you. He made the payment for your sins, He makes sure you get the Gospel, He convicts you of your sin, but [you]{.class_4725} have to receive Christ and believe the Gospel [yourself.]{.class_4725}

So the parable is a picture of a lost man who can't save himself, and three people come by who can help him. The first is a [priest]{.class_4725} (vs. []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_334}31). There's your Catholic: the Pope, "Hail Mary," tic-tac-toe three in a row, twiddling the beads, etc. He passes the wounded fellow by ["when he saw him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 31)---he doesn't want to get involved.

The second one to come by is ["a Levite"]{.class_4723}---a religious leader. He actually comes up and looks at the wounded fellow before passing by.

There's a famous preacher's illustration about a guy who falls into a twenty-foot pit with no food, no water, and no way of getting out. He's down there in that pit yelling for help when an Episcopalian Rector sticks his head over the side of the pit and says, "You want out of there?" "Yeah," says the guy down in the pit. "Well, just keep the Ten Commandments and the Golden Rule, and you'll get out."

Of course, that does no good. After a long while someone else stops by the pit; this time it's a Buddhist monk. "Help me out," cries the man in the pit. "I can't help you out, my son; only you can help yourself out. Sit cross-legged and meditate a while until you become one with the pit."

The guy tries it, but he's still in the pit. A Catholic Priest comes by and tells him, "Find the church Christ founded, join it, take the sacraments, and die in a state of grace, and you'll make it out of the pit." "But I want out now!" "Oh well, I tried," says the Priest and walks away.

The fellow's huddled up down there in the pit, trying to stay warm, when one last person comes by. "Can you get me out?" cries the man in the pit. "You really want out of there?" says the fellow at the top. "Yes, I do; if I don't get out of here soon, I'm going to die." So that man reaches down into the pit and tells that fellow down there, "Take my hand, and I'll get you out." The man takes his rescuer's hand and gets hauled out of that pit.

[I was the man in that pit.]{.class_4725} I'll give you one guess who the one was who got me out (Psa. 40:1--2). Jesus Christ didn't just tell me how to make it out of the pit; He came down and [got me out Himself.]{.class_4725} That's what sets Jesus Christ apart from a blasphemous imposter like Mohammed. My Prophet actually [did something for me;]{.class_4725} He didn't just say, "Do this and do that, and you [might]{.class_4725} make it."

So there's no doubt who the "Samaritan" is in the story (vs. 33). One time those Christ-rejecting Jews tell Jesus, ["Say we not well that thou art a SAMARITAN, and hast a devil?"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_335} (John 8:48). Samaritans were half Jew and half Gentile. Do you know what the Apostle Paul says about the spiritual Body of Christ in this age?

["For by one Spirit are we all baptized into ONE BODY, whether we be JEWS OR GENTILES"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 12:13).

["And that he might reconcile BOTH ]{.class_4723}\[Jews and Gentiles---vss. 11--12\] [unto God in ONE BODY by the cross"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 2:16).

The Samaritan doesn't pass by; he stops and helps that wounded Jew. ["He had compassion on him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 33), like Jesus had on the multitudes (Matt. 9:36). ["And went to him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34), like Christ came to us (Phil. 2:5--8). ["And bound up his wounds, pouring in oil and wine"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34), types of the Holy Spirit (4:18 cf. Heb. 1:9; Matt. 26:29 cf. Acts 2:13; Eph. 5:18).

["And set him on his own beast"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34). In the parable it would be a mule or a jackass. It's a picture of a converted sinner (see our comments on Matt. 21:2 in that Commentary). When a fellow gets saved, he should be discipled by a more mature Christian.

["And brought him to an inn"]{.class_4723}---a place where the wounded man could rest and recover with people to take care of him (like a hospital). A new convert should get into a good church where he can get sound preaching and teaching from the word of God and the right kind of fellowship to help him recover from the old life.

Verse 35: ["And on the morrow when he departed, he took out two pence, and gave them to the host, and said unto him, Take care of him; and whatsoever thou spendest more, when I come again, I will repay thee."]{.class_4723} The promise is that God will supply anything you need to do what He's called you to do (2 Cor. 9:8--10), and when Christ returns for you at the Rapture, you'll be paid for whatever you did for Him (1 Cor. 3:11--14; 2 Cor. 5:10).

["Which now of these three, thinkest thou was neighbour unto him that fell among the thieves?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 36). Now we are back to the "central truth" of the parable. Here was a Jew left for dead, and his own people (the priest and the Levite) passed him by. The one who helped him was a despised Samaritan who normally wouldn't have any dealings with a Jew (see John 4:9 and our comments in that Commentary). The point is not lost on the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_336}lawyer (vs. 37) and is a pointed rebuke to his hypocrisy (vs. 29). If someone like a Samaritan could be a good neighbor to a Jew by showing mercy, then a Jew who supposedly knew the Law certainly had no excuse. The command of Jesus is to love and pray even for your enemies (see our comments on 6:27--28).

People think when they read my stuff that I'm full of "hate" and am using "hate speech." Listen, when it comes to telling the truth, I will "tell it like it is" (as they used to say in the 60s and 70s), but I have nothing personal against anyone. I speak out strongly against Islam and Moslems because of their [false doctrine,]{.class_4725} but I don't have anything [personally]{.class_4725} against any Moslem. I actually feel sorry for them. They've been deceived by a sex-obsessed, demon-possessed military dictator and are headed for Hell.

My way of "fighting" against them is not by cutting off their heads or forcing them at sword point to convert (as they do); it is witnessing to them, praying for them, and giving them the Gospel. The problem is that they don't believe that. The [Koran]{.class_4725} commands them to kill Christians and Jews unless they convert to Islam (Sura 9:29--30, 474, 9:123, 2:193), so you have to defend yourself and your country. But when it comes to trying to "set up a democracy," like Bush tried to do, I wouldn't even attempt it. All you will end up with after losing thousands of lives and wasting billions of dollars is what you've got in Afghanistan: one group of Moslems ruling by "Sharia" (Koranic law) instead of another group of Moslems ruling by Sharia. That's not a "democracy" or a "republic"; it's a militant Church-State ruled by religious demagoguery.

38 Now it came to pass, as they went, that he entered into a certain village: and a certain woman named Martha received him into her house.

39 And she had a sister called Mary, which also sat at Jesus' feet, and heard his word.

40 But Martha was cumbered about much serving, and came to him, and said, Lord, dost thou not care that my sister hath left me to serve alone? bid her therefore that she help me.

41 And Jesus answered and said unto her, Martha, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_337}Martha, thou art careful and troubled about many things:

42 But one thing is needful: and Mary hath chosen that good part, which shall not be taken away from her.

This is the Mary and Martha of John 11--12. In John, you learn they have a brother named Lazarus. The ["certain village"]{.class_4723} in which they live is ["Bethany"]{.class_4723} (John 11:1), which is located a mile and a half east of Jerusalem on the Mount of Olives (John 11:18; Luke 19:29). Jesus counted this family of siblings as close personal friends (John 11:5).

Verses 39--40 show you the spiritual character of the sisters. Mary is the [learner;]{.class_4725} Martha is the [worker.]{.class_4725} Martha serves (John 12:2) while Mary anoints the feet of Jesus for His burial (John 12:3, 7). You continually find Mary at the feet of Jesus: she sits at His feet (vs. 39), she falls at His feet (John 11:32), and she anoints His feet (John 12:3).

Now there's nothing wrong with a Martha. You need both Marthas and Marys in a church. Somebody has to clean the building, put fresh toilet paper in the commode stalls, change the burnt out light bulbs, watch the kids in the nursery, etc. But that's not what's going to last forever. It's the spiritual stuff---the teaching and preaching of the word of God---that ["shall not be taken away"]{.class_4723} (vs. 42).

Martha's problem wasn't that she was serving; her problem was that she ["was cumbered]{.class_4723} \[we would say "encumbered" today; i.e., she was [preoccupied]{.class_4725} with it, she was [loaded up]{.class_4725} with it, she was [distracted]{.class_4725} and [troubled]{.class_4725} by it\][ about much serving"]{.class_4723} (vs. 40). Jesus was more concerned with teaching the word (vs. 39) than He was with supper being served (see what He said in John 4:31--34). Martha needed to take a break and sit down and listen to Jesus along with her sister.

["Mary hath chosen that good part"]{.class_4723} (vs. 42); that is, sitting and listening to the word of God being taught (vs. 39). That being the case, it must be a real privilege to sit and hear the word of God. One preacher said of his congregation, "I see no glad glow, no anticipation. I see dull faces and lead in the ears. You go with sparkle and anticipation in your eyes to a concert or a comedian or a []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2127.xhtml_page_338}football game, but when you sit and hear the word of God, the anticipation suddenly leaves." When a movie or a TV show or a sporting event is more exciting to you than hearing the word of God preached and taught, you need a spiritual revival in your soul.

["One thing is needful"]{.class_4723} (vs. 42). It's not money or education or power or political connections. Above anything else you need [God's words.]{.class_4725} Anything a man needs to be successful in this life in the eyes of God is in that Book (Josh. 1:8).

What do you need to know? I mean, what is really important that you need to know? Do you need to know about life? [It's in the Book.]{.class_4727} Do you need to know about death? [It's in the Book.]{.class_4727} Do you need to know about marriage and relationships? [They're in the Book.]{.class_4727} Do you need to know about sin and evil? [They're in the Book.]{.class_4727} Do you need to know about good and how to live right? [They're in the Book.]{.class_4727} Do you need to know about work and money?[ ]{.class_4723}[They're in the Book.]{.class_4727}[ ]{.class_4723}Do you need to know about rest and leisure?[ They're in the Book.]{.class_4727} Do you want to know about the future? [It's in]{.class_4727} [the Book.]{.class_4727} Whatever you need to make it through this life is [in that Book.]{.class_4727}

["All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness: That the man of God may be PERFECT, THROUGHLY FURNISHED unto all good works"]{.class_4723} (2 Tim. 3:16--17).

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2278.xhtml}

# Chapter 11 {.heading_78-0}

11:1 And it came to pass, that, as he was praying in a certain place, when he ceased, one of his disciples said unto him, Lord, teach us to pray, as John also taught his disciples.

2 And he said unto them, When ye pray, say, Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done, as in heaven, so in earth.

3 Give us day by day our daily bread.

4 And forgive us our sins; for we also forgive every one that is indebted to us. And lead us not into temptation; but deliver us from evil.

This is what most people call "the Lord's Prayer." It's not the [Lord's]{.class_4727} Prayer at all; it's the [Disciples' ]{.class_4727}Prayer. Look at verse 1: ["one of his DISCIPLES said unto him, Lord, teach US to pray, as John also taught his DISCIPLES."]{.class_4723}

So this is the Lord teaching His disciples how to pray; it's not any prayer He would ever pray Himself. How do you know that? Look at verse 2: ["When YE pray, say."]{.class_4723} Jesus is not praying this prayer Himself; He's telling the disciples what to say when they pray.

Further, look at verse 4: ["And forgive us OUR SINS."]{.class_4723} You reckon Jesus had to pray that prayer for Himself? I don't think so!

["For such an high priest became us, who is HOLY, harmless, UNDEFILED, SEPARATE FROM SINNERS"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 7:26).

["Forasmuch as ye know that ye were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold\...But with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb WITHOUT BLEMISH and WITHOUT SPOT"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 1:18--19).

["For he]{.class_4723} \[God---vs. 20\][ hath made him ]{.class_4723}\[Christ---vs. 20\][ to be sin for us, WHO KNEW NO SIN; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 5:21).

So this is NOT the Lord's Prayer. If you want to read the actual "Lord's Prayer," go to John 17 and read the whole chapter. That's not the [corporate]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2278.xhtml_page_340} prayer of a group of disciples or the nation of Israel (["Our Father"]{.class_4723}---vs. 2); that's the[ personal]{.class_4725} prayer of the Son to the Father (see John 17:1, 5, 11, 21, 22, 25). The stupid Catholics screwed this up just like they screwed up Mary only having one child and being a perpetual virgin. When it comes to the Catholic Church, [there are no deep students of the Scriptures.]{.class_4725} Any Catholic who gets into the Scriptures in earnest [gets out of the Catholic Church.]{.class_4725} When it comes to what the Scriptures say, you can count on the Catholic Church to make a mess of things every time.

Another thing to notice about this passage is that it [differs]{.class_4725} from the Disciples' Prayer as given in Matthew 6:9--13. The Disciples' Prayer of Matthew 6 is part of the "Sermon on the Mount"; this is [not]{.class_4725} (see our comments on 6:17). Because of Luke's emphasis to the Gentiles, the whole part of the prayer that deals with ["the kingdom"]{.class_4723} (see Matt. 6:13) is left off. Because of that, the ending to the prayer is removed from the Matthew version by all the new "Bibles," even though [every Greek manuscript but ten have the reading,]{.class_4725} and it is quoted by two church fathers as ancient as [Vaticanus]{.class_4725} and [Sinaiticus]{.class_4725} (see our remarks under Matt. 6:13).

That's not all. Not being content to attack the prayer in one place, the Devil attacks it in [both occurrences.]{.class_4725} The new versions ([NIV, NASV, ]{.class_4725}etc.) drop [twenty words]{.class_4725} from the text here in Luke 11: ["Our" "which art in heaven," "Thy will be done, as in heaven, so in earth," "but deliver us from evil."]{.class_4723} This mutilated version of the Disciples' Prayer came from the pen of ["Marcion the heretic"]{.class_4725}[ ]{.class_4723}(d. A.D. 160) who merged early Christianity with Gnosticism (Dean John Burgon, [The Revision Revised,]{.class_4725} pp. 34--35; Elgin Moyer, [Who Was Who in Church History,]{.class_4725} pg. 270). Origen picked up this perverted version and stuck it into the manuscripts which Eusebius copied out as [Vaticanus]{.class_4725} and [Sinaiticus.]{.class_4725} From there, two [esoteric spiritualists]{.class_4725} (Westcott and Hort) put it into their revised text of the Greek New Testament, which became the basis for all the modern perversions put out since 1880. It is this corrupted version of the prayer (found in the [NIV, NASV, NRSV,]{.class_4725} etc.) which was adopted by [Madame Blavatsky]{.class_4725} (Theosophy) and the [New Agers]{.class_4725} as a prayer to [Satan]{.class_4725} because it came from a Second-Century [heretic]{.class_4725} and [removed all references to the God of the Bible.]{.class_4725} (For all the documented evidence, see the third chapter of Gail Riplinger's []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2278.xhtml_page_341}[New Age Bible Versions.]{.class_4725}) That is, the modern translations are [Satanic "Bibles."]{.class_4725}

The Disciples' Prayer only occurs in two places in the Gospels (Matthew 6 and Luke 11), and the new versions pervert the text in [both places.]{.class_4725} Fourteen words are removed from Matthew 6, and twenty words are removed from Luke 11. So with that much attention paid to the prayer by the Devil, there must be something very important in it.

Verse 2:[ "And he said unto them, When ye pray, say, Our Father which art in heaven."]{.class_4723} This is the famous "Our Father" ([Pater noster]{.class_4725}) prayed by Catholics in the rosary (see our remarks on the Catholic "Hail Mary" under 1:28). Matthew makes it plain that what Jesus gives the disciples is a [pattern]{.class_4725} for prayer (["after this manner\...pray ye"]{.class_4723}---Matt. 6:9) and not some "vain repetition" said from rote memory (see our comments on Matt. 6:7 in that Commentary).

Note that both the ["ye"]{.class_4723} and the ["Our"]{.class_4723} are [plural.]{.class_4725} Christ never addressed His Father that way, and neither do [you]{.class_4725} (see Rom. 8:15). Sometimes you will hear a preacher address God as "our Father," like Bob Jones Sr. did many a time on his radio program, but that is a [public]{.class_4725} prayer for a [congregation.]{.class_4725} When you pray as a Christian, you approach God one on one; there's no "we" or "our" to it.

So the first thing you need to get about the so-called "Lord's Prayer" is that it is not a [Christian]{.class_4725} prayer. The ["ye"]{.class_4723} is a reference to [Jewish]{.class_4725} disciples under the Law, before the Resurrection. It is an [Old Testament]{.class_4725} prayer.

David prayed, ["O LORD OUR God"]{.class_4723} in 1 Chronicles 24:16. Why? Because Israel [as a nation]{.class_4725} (not individually) was a son (Exod. 4:22) which God begat and to which He gave birth (Isa. 63:16, 64:8; Deut. 32:5--6, 18). These are [Jewish]{.class_4725} disciples addressing God as part of the [Jewish]{.class_4725} nation. There is not yet any New Birth making any individual a son of God (John 1:12).

["Hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done, as in heaven, so in earth."]{.class_4723} The pattern of prayer is God [first,]{.class_4725} you [next.]{.class_4725} Praise to God is offered before personal requests are made.

The word ["Hallowed"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2278.xhtml_page_342} means "to make holy." You are to treat God's name as holy and not take it in vain (Exod. 20:7).

Notice that the prayer is for God's kingdom to ["come."]{.class_4723} This is [not]{.class_4725} a spiritual kingdom that a believer enters at the New Birth (John 3:5) and which is [within]{.class_4725} a man (Luke 17:21). This is a kingdom which every Jew expected to show up [when the King showed up]{.class_4725} (Zech. 14). There is no command in either Testament for anybody to "bring in the kingdom." Rat control bills, civil rights acts, the UN, "Joel's Army," "manifest sons of God," "gay marriage bills," women's lib, "the City of God," wars on poverty, the "social gospel," and any other "do-gooder" attempts by Liberals, Modernists, Catholics, Amillennial and Postmillennial Protestants, and Socialistic "Liberationists" to "bring in the kingdom" have nothing to do with either the Kingdom of God or the Kingdom of Heaven as found in the Holy Bible.

["Give us day by day our daily bread"]{.class_4723} (vs. 3). Now there's nothing wrong with applying that verse to you spiritually, especially if you're out of work and don't know from where your next meal is coming. If some Christian over in Africa, Mexico, India, or the Philippines prayed that prayer, I wouldn't fault him at all. Some of the people in those places spend all day long trying to get something to eat. In some cities in India, workers go around every morning collecting the dead bodies of people who starved to death. So I am not unreasonable when it comes to a [practical]{.class_4725} application of this verse, but that is [not]{.class_4725} its [doctrinal]{.class_4725} meaning.

Do you know of any place in Scripture where God gave those Jews bread on a daily basis? Why, sure: the manna in Exodus 16. As we discussed in our comments on 4:3 and remarks under the feeding of the 5,000 in 9:10--17 (which see), this is a type of the Jews being fed in the wilderness during the Tribulation (Psa. 78:2, 24--25 cf. Rev. 12:14; Mic. 7:14--15). So what you are reading here is the prayer the Jews will pray in the Tribulation when they're running from the Antichrist (see Matt. 24:15--21).

Like I've told you before, brethren, the main theme of that Bible is [not God saving you;]{.class_4725} it's [the exaltation of His Son to the throne He was promised]{.class_4725} in Isaiah 9:6--7 and Luke 1:32--33. Now thank God salvation is in there; that's how I got in on the "goodies." But the Bible spends more time on what happens [after]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2278.xhtml_page_343} 2012 than it does on what takes place before it.

["And forgive us our sins; for we also forgive every one that is indebted to us"]{.class_4723} (vs. 4). Matthew says, ["And forgive us our debts AS we forgive our debtors"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 6:12). So the word ["for"]{.class_4723} here means "because" just like it did back in 3:3 (see our comments on the verse). In other words, forgiveness of sins here depends on how you forgive others (see Matt. 18:21--35). Is that your case as a born-again believer? Of course not! You get your sins forgiven when you trust Jesus Christ as your Saviour.

["In whom we have redemption, through his blood, even the forgiveness of sins"]{.class_4723} (Col. 1:14).

["Be it known unto you, therefore, men and brethren, that through this man is preached unto you the forgiveness of sins: And by him all that believe are justified from all things, from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses" ]{.class_4723}(Acts 13:38--39).

So the [doctrine]{.class_4725} of the verse is not aimed at you. Now, practically speaking, your [fellowship with the Lord ]{.class_4725}depends on you forgiving others: ["And be ye kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God for Christ's sake hath forgiven you"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 4:32). But that's not a condition of your sins being forgiven; that's a condition of living right [after]{.class_4725} your sins have been forgiven. The Lord forgives your sins on the basis of [Christ dying in your place.]{.class_4725} You have to rightly divide the Scriptures when it comes to [doctrinal]{.class_4725} application[ ]{.class_4725}and [practical]{.class_4725} application.

["And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil."]{.class_4723} Now that's a legitimate prayer for a Christian. There's nothing wrong with a Christian praying for the way God has provided for him to escape temptation (1 Cor. 10:13). But even then, you have to watch out. That ["temptation,"]{.class_4723} when it comes to that Jew, is a specific [time period]{.class_4725} of temptation (Heb. 3:8; Rev. 3:10), and it is [one particular]{.class_4725} temptation (see our remarks on the ["sin"]{.class_4723} of 1 John 3:9 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the General Epistles,]{.class_4725} Vol. II). During the Tribulation, that Jew is tempted to sell out the Lord and worship the Antichrist and his image (Rev. 13). The ["evil"]{.class_4723} from which the Jews are praying for deliverance is the Devil coming after []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2278.xhtml_page_344}any of them who won't "swear fealty" to him (Rev. 12).

That's what takes place in the book of Daniel. The temptation over and over again in the book of Daniel is for Daniel and his three buddies to forsake the Law of God and give in to idolatry: eat meat offered to idols (Dan. 1), worship Nebuchadnezzar's image (Dan. 2), pray to Darius (Dan. 6). In each case, there are dire consequences if they don't. The two most notable are the fiery furnace and the lion's den, and in both cases, God "delivers them from evil." [That's]{.class_4725} for what Jesus is commanding Jewish disciples to pray.

That's what's such a shock to those Jews, especially the orthodox ones. They read those Old Testament stories about the Lord delivering His chosen people, and they try to apply those Scriptures to themselves, and it doesn't work. One old boy in the Middle Ages (Moses Botarel) told the Roman Catholic King of Spain to go ahead and burn him because nothing would happen to him; he ended up a "crispy critter." Another told the Mohammedan King of Yemen that if the Moslems cut off his (the Jew's) head, it wouldn't kill him: he ended up "beside himself," deader than a doornail. The Rabbis in the concentration camps in Germany during World War II prayed all those prayers for deliverance written by David in the Psalms, and the Lord didn't answer a one of them: they went to the ovens anyway.

There is no miraculous deliverance for that Jew [now]{.class_4725} because he has rejected his Messiah, and God has [temporarily]{.class_4725} set him aside (1 Thess. 2:16). There will be no miraculous deliverance until the Law and the Prophets return (Moses and Elijah---Rev. 11) and convert a remnant of those people back to their Messiah. The Jews who receive miraculous deliverance from Satan and the Beast in the Tribulation are not only those ["which keep the commandments of God,"]{.class_4723} but ["have the testimony of Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 12:17).

5 And he said unto them, Which of you shall have a friend, and shall go unto him at midnight, and say unto him, Friend, lend me three loaves;

6 For a friend of mine in his journey is come to me, and I have nothing to set before him?

7 And he from within shall answer and say, Trouble me not: the door is now shut, and my children are with me in bed; I cannot rise and give thee.

8 I say unto you, Though he will not rise and give him, because he is his friend, yet because of his importunity he will rise and give him as many as he needeth.

Jesus continues His teachings on prayer here and on into verses 9--13. Verses 5--8 are what is known as "the Parable of the Importunate Friend," the word [importunate]{.class_4733} coming from the word ["importunity"]{.class_4736} in the text (vs. 8). [Importunity]{.class_4733} means an "urgent request made with troublesome frequency."

In the parable here, the man keeps knocking at his buddy's door in the middle of the night until the guy gets out of bed and gives him what he wants just to shut him up. The matchmeet to this parable is found in Luke 18:1--7, in the Parable of the Unjust Judge. In that parable, a wicked judge grants a widow woman justice in a matter just to get her to stop bothering him day in and day out.

That's how the Lord wants you to approach Him in prayer: with dogged persistence. When Paul tells you to ["Pray without ceasing"]{.class_4723} (1 Thess. 5:17), that's about what he's talking. He's not telling you to pray 24 hours a day; he's saying to keep on praying about a request until you get a definite answer one way or another. Paul prayed three times about his ["thorn in the flesh"]{.class_4723} until the Lord gave him a definite, emphatic NO (2 Cor. 12:7--9). When that Syrophenician woman came to Christ and the disciples about her demon-possessed daughter, she didn't go away simply because she was snubbed by the disciples and then insulted by Jesus; she just took another approach until she got her prayer answered (Mark 7:24--30). Sometimes the Lord just wants to see how serious you are in your prayers.

There's an illustration about a pirate ship that went down at sea. All the crew that could made it into the one lifeboat until it was full, and the rest had to fend for themselves in the water. The cook was in the water and swam over to the lifeboat and grabbed onto the side with his right hand. The captain took out his cutlass and cut off the cook's hand. The cook reached up and grabbed the lifeboat with his []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2278.xhtml_page_346}left hand, and the captain cut that one off too. Kicking up with his feet and struggling up with his now handless arms, the cook managed to grab the side of the lifeboat with his teeth. The captain ordered the men to pull him aboard. That guy was [serious]{.class_4725} about his request.

I wouldn't take no as an answer from the Lord the first time I prayed about a thing. I wouldn't take no for an answer the second time. I have been in situations where I didn't take no for an answer [fifteen]{.class_4725} times. I kept on praying until it was perfectly evident that I wasn't going to get what I prayed for. When the answer is no, all you can do is claim Romans 8:28.

Paul knew what it was like to take no as an answer from the Lord. He had ["a thorn in the flesh"]{.class_4723} that he prayed three times for God to take away, and the Lord wouldn't do it (2 Cor. 12:7--9). Paul would pray, "Lord, please take away this thorn. You know I could have a better ministry and get more souls saved if I didn't have to contend with this thing. It just interferes with things, and if you would take it away, I could get more done for you. So please, Lord, for your glory, please take this thorn out of my flesh."

Paul prayed for that three times, and each time God would reach down there and push that thorn in another quarter of an inch. He told Paul, ["My grace is sufficient for thee"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 12:9). That's a tough one; you won't hear much about that on James Dobson's "family hour."

I would recommend that until you get an answer like that to a prayer request, you keep on praying about the matter. Some Christians have prayed for [years]{.class_4725} about a thing and had God answer it. George Mueller kept a list of people he prayed for God to save. When Mueller died, I think it was all but three people on that list had received Christ as Saviour. Of those three, two of them got saved at his funeral, and the other got saved later on. So until you get a definite no from the Lord, keep on praying.

9 And I say unto you, Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you.
