Now I'll grant you that the final rejection of Christ is unpardonable, but that's [not]{.class_4725} blasphemy against the Holy Ghost.

The exact definition of blasphemy against the Holy Ghost is given in the companion passage in Mark 3:28--30.

["Verily I say unto you, All sins shall be forgiven unto the sons of men, and blasphemies wherewith soever they shall blaspheme ]{.class_4723}\[so it is possible to be forgiven of rejecting and blaspheming Christ; Paul said that before he was saved, he was ["a blasphemer,"]{.class_4723} 1 Tim. 1:13\][: But he that shall blaspheme against the Holy Ghost hath never forgiveness, but is in danger of eternal damnation: Because they said ]{.class_4723}\[it's something spoken with the mouth; it's not a decision you make\][, He ]{.class_4723}\[referring to Jesus Christ\][ hath an unclean spirit."]{.class_4723}

So the blasphemy against the Holy Ghost is not cursing the Holy Spirit per se. There is a new movement by the infidels and the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_382}atheists to get Christian teens who want to rebel against momma and daddy and their Christian upbringing to go on camera and curse the Holy Spirit. The idea is that they can never be forgiven of that sin, so there is no turning back. They have burned all their bridges behind them, so they can now go out and live like hell and not worry about getting right.

I have bad news for you, kid: ["the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from ALL sin"]{.class_4723} (1 John 1:7). You couldn't commit the "unpardonable sin" right now if you wanted to. Go back and read Mark 3:30.

["Because THEY\...."]{.class_4723} Then it's NOT YOU; it's someone [before]{.class_4725} the Crucifixion. ["\...said, He hath an unclean spirit."]{.class_4723} Any of you ever been tempted to say that? If you were, what would it mean? Jesus Christ isn't right here now in the flesh, performing miracles as signs to the nation of Israel.

The Charismatic faith healers say, "Well, it's attributing the works of the Holy Spirit to Satan." Ah, get off it. They say that so you won't read them the riot act like those Ephesians did in Revelation 2:2 (read it). You are commanded to ["try the spirits, whether they are of God"]{.class_4723} (1 John 4:1). I've tried 'em, and I don't hesitate for one second to say that people like Oral Roberts, Benny Hinn, Kathryn Kuhlman, T. T. Osborne, Stephen Hill, John Kilpatrick, et al., are OF THE DEVIL. You think I'm worried about committing the "unpardonable sin" when I say that? It doesn't bother me enough to take a baby aspirin. I have gone to these Charismatic sideshows and just laughed myself silly. They thought I had the "holy laugh." But I wasn't laughing [with]{.class_4725} them; I was laughing [at]{.class_4725} them.

The unpardonable sin was committed by certain Jews before Calvary who saw Christ's miracles and claimed He had a devil to be able to do them (see 11:15). When they crossed that line, they fixed themselves so that they don't get saved after the Resurrection in the book of Acts. You have to rightly divide that Book, people (2 Tim. 2:15), or you will get yourself in one flat-footed mess.

11 And when they bring you unto the synagogues, and unto magistrates, and powers, take ye no thought how or what thing ye shall answer, or what ye shall say:

12 For the Holy Ghost shall teach you in the same hour what ye ought to say.

Verse 12 is one of those verses used by the Holiness crowd and some of the hillbilly preachers to prove you shouldn't preach from notes. They think if a man is called to preach, he doesn't need to do any study or preparation. All he needs to do is pray, and God will give him the words to say when he gets up to preach. When a preacher believes that all he has to do is open his mouth wide for God to fill it (see Psa. 81:10), a lot of times what God fills it with is [hot air.]{.class_4725}

Early in my ministry, I used to preach up in the hills of North Carolina. I learned pretty quickly that if I wanted to have a good meeting, I had better not carry anything that looked like sermon notes into the pulpit. So what I did was get a black notebook that looked like a Bible on the outside, with blank pages on the inside. I would write my outline on the right-hand page, and on the left side, I would paste my Scripture texts that I had cut out of an old Bible. That way it looked to those mountaineers as if I was preaching with nothing but a Bible before me.

Of course the passage has nothing to do with that sort of thing at all. Jesus is talking about when the apostles and disciples are hauled before the civil and religious authorities for the sake of the Gospel (as they were in Acts 4, 7, 18, 23, 24, 26). They are not to worry ahead of time how they will answer their accusers, but let the Holy Spirit give them what to say when the time comes.

When it comes to preaching, I don't believe in any extracanonical inspiration that contradicts Scripture (like [The Book of Mormon,]{.class_4725} Mary Baker Eddy's [Key to the Scriptures,]{.class_4725} the [Koran,]{.class_4725} etc.). But I do believe that the Lord has taken many a fellow and spoken through him. There was plenty that ["holy men of God spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 1:21) that was not recorded or put into the canon of Scripture (see our remarks on the canon under 1:1). When a fellow gets preaching, he will say things in his message that aren't in his notes and that he never intended to say. Later on, he will find out there was a reason for what he said about which he didn't even know. I have seen that []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_384}happen several times. Well, that's the Holy Ghost speaking through that preacher, accomplishing what He wants with what is said.

13 And one of the company said unto him, Master, speak to my brother, that he divide the inheritance with me.

14 And he said unto him, Man, who made me a judge or a divider over you?

15 And he said unto them, Take heed, and beware of covetousness: for a man's life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth.

16 And he spake a parable unto them, saying, The ground of a certain rich man brought forth plentifully:

17 And he thought within himself, saying, What shall I do, because I have no room where to bestow my fruits?

18 And he said, This will I do: I will pull down my barns, and build greater; and there will I bestow all my fruits and my goods.

19 And I will say to my soul, Soul, thou hast much goods laid up for many years; take thine ease, eat, drink, and be merry.

20 But God said unto him, Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee: then whose shall those things be, which thou hast provided?

21 So is he that layeth up treasure for himself, and is not rich toward God.

What you have here in verses 16--21 is the Parable of the Rich Fool; verses 13--15 are what lead up to Jesus giving the parable. The whole thing was precipitated by a fellow who thought he wasn't getting his fair share of the inheritance left by his daddy when he died. The guy wanted Jesus to say something to his brother because Jesus' words obviously carried weight and authority as a prophet from God.

But Jesus would have none of it. Jesus didn't come to be a judge at His First Coming (John 3:17, 12:47); that is what He will do at His Second Advent (Acts 17:31; John 12:48). He certainly didn't come to get involved in arguments over money.

What Jesus does do is cut to the heart of the matter (see how the Lord deals with sinners in His personal work in John 3:2--3, 4:9--24). He tells the crowd who watched how He dealt with that man, ["Take heed, and beware of covetousness"]{.class_4723} (vs. 15).

Covetousness was the problem with that fellow who wanted his part of the inheritance, it was the problem with the rich young ruler (Matt. 19:21--22), and it was the problem with the Pharisees (Luke 16:14). Paul said, ["covetousness\...is idolatry"]{.class_4723} (Col. 3:5). Covetousness is wanting something you don't have and which God has not given you. It is ["the love of money"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:10), and it plagues both rich and poor alike.

["For a man's life consisteth not in the abundance of the THINGS which he possesseth."]{.class_4723} Someone put out a bumpersticker one time that said: "He who dies with the most toys wins." A Christian came back with a bumpersticker that read: "He who dies with the most toys STILL DIES."

A rich man died one time, and someone asked, "What did he leave?" The answer came back: "He left it [all."]{.class_4725} Those Egyptian Pharaohs were buried with boats and servants and gold and food, all to provide for them in the afterlife. It all just stayed in a hole in the ground till grave robbers and archaeologists came and took it all away.

There was a rich fellow in Texas who died one time who wanted to be buried in a gold Cadillac. As that gold Cadillac was being lowered into the ground, two colored guys were standing there watching that, and one said to the other, "Mmm, mmm, dat sho' am livin'." His dark buddy replied, "Man, dat ain't livin'; dat's a [dyin'."]{.class_4725} You can't take it with you no matter how hard you try (1 Tim. 6:7).

[Money can't bring happiness, although most people want the chance to prove that statement wrong. Some of the most miserable people on the face of this earth are]{.class_4728} rich folks. Their families get busted up, and their kids turn out wrong, because of greed. They have very few real friends; most of the people who hang around them are there because of their money.

In the early 1900s, one of those rich oil barons was sitting in a restaurant with his personal male secretary. He looked over at a young fellow eating a steak dinner. That tycoon had developed a bad []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_386}ulcer from worrying over financial decisions, the stock market, etc., so all he could eat was something like dried toast and milk. He said to his assistant, "I'd give all the money I've got if I could just enjoy a meal like that young man." All of that man's wealth couldn't buy him a decent meal to enjoy.[ "A man's life consisteth not in the abundance of things he possesseth."]{.class_4723}

Then Christ gives the Parable of the Rich Fool. Here's a fellow who is already rich; he has the houses and the lands already. In addition, his farm brings forth a bumper crop. He says to himself, "What am I going to do with all this stuff; I don't have anywhere else to put it." That's why there are all these "storage facilities" nowadays. People run out of room in their house to store all their junk; so instead of getting rid of it, they rent another place to keep it, and they just keep accumulating more junk.

This fellow's solution to his problem is to tear down all of his present barns and build bigger ones. He's going to get all he can and then sit on the can. He plans on keeping everything for himself, which is a dumb, stupid thing to do. He can't eat all the grain himself. Why not give some of it to some poor folks who are hungry. I mean, if all your bills are paid, help out someone else who can't pay their bills. Don't wait for the government, the doctors, and the lawyers to get it; do something with it worthwhile for those in need.

When you're gone, are your kids going to get your money? What are they going to do with it? Fight over it like the man in verse 13? Sam Jones asked a rich man he knew if he were going to leave his kids a lot of money when he died. "No, I'm not," said the man. "Why not?" asked Sam. "Well," said that rich fellow, "if they are like they ought to be, they won't need it, and if they're the wrong kind of people, it would just damn them." Money won't do you any good if your heart is not right.

Verse 19:[ "And I will say to my soul, Soul, thou hast much goods laid up for many years."]{.class_4723} One of the reasons God calls this man a fool is because he confuses the soul for the body. Your soul can't feed on physical things. Your body will die; your soul won't.

Second, the man was a fool because he presumed on the will and the grace of God. He thought he had a long time to live; he had less []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_387}than eighteen hours. James wrote:

["Whereas ye know not what shall be on the morrow. For what is your life? It is even a vapour, that appeareth for a little time, and then vanisheth away. For that ye ought to say, If the Lord will, we shall live, and do this, or that"]{.class_4723} (James 4:14--15).

["Take thine ease, eat, drink, and be merry."]{.class_4723} Here is the third reason this fellow was a fool: he wasted all his wealth on [himself.]{.class_4725} Back in the Old Testament, when those Jews should have been repenting in sackcloth and ashes, they were partying and saying, ["let us eat and drink; for to morrow we shall die"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 22:13).

That's called "living for the moment" in modern parlance. It isn't relying on the Lord for day-to-day provision like in Matthew 6:25. It's satiating the flesh right now with no thought of eternity.

There is an old preacher's illustration that has been illustrated by Jack Chick in his tract "The Fool." As the story goes, a King had a "fool" (a court jester) who made him laugh like no one else could. One day the King called the fool in for an audience. He handed him a golden scepter and said, "Fool, you are the greatest fool I know. But here is a golden scepter. I want you to go and search the length and breadth of my kingdom, and if you find a greater fool than yourself, present him with this golden scepter."

The fool was gone for some time searching the land for a greater fool than himself, but he found none. Upon returning to the palace, he learned that his master the King was dying. He went to see the King on his death bed.

"Hello, my friend," said the King. "Did you find a greater fool than yourself?"

"No, my Lord," said the fool. "What is wrong, O King?"

"Well," said the King, "I am about to depart on a long journey."

"Where are you going?" asked the fool.

"I don't know," replied the King.

"Have you made any preparations for this trip?" asked the fool.

The King said, "No."

"Then, your Majesty," said the fool, "let me present you with this golden scepter, for you are the greatest fool I know."

People, a man is a [fool]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_388} who makes no preparation for eternity. Jesus said, ["For what shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul"]{.class_4723} (Mark 8:36). You are a fool to trade your eternal soul for a buck. One day this world is going to go up in flames (2 Pet. 3:10). Don't you go up with it (Rev. 20:6, 14).

["But God said unto him, Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee"]{.class_4723} (vs. 20). He was about to be "reaped" before he could harvest. One of these days, the One who gave you your soul will require it of you. You had better ["prepare to meet thy God"]{.class_4723} (see Amos 4:12 and our comments in that Commentary).

["Then whose shall those things be, which thou hast provided?"]{.class_4723} Well, he'll leave it behind for someone, but it won't be his (see our remarks under vs. 15). He would have done just as well to have given it away while he had a chance. One preacher said, "Do your giving while you're living, then you'll be knowing where it's going." If he had given his excess away, that would have given him something to show before God when he met Him. Second Corinthians 9:9 says, ["he hath given to the poor: his righteousness remaineth for ever."]{.class_4723}

Verse 21 should be contrasted with Christ's words in Matthew 6:19--21, which see. When a Christian supposes ["that gain is godliness,"]{.class_4723} he is ["destitute of the truth"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:5). The mark of the Laodicean church age is Christians who believe themselves to be ["rich, and increased with goods, and have need of nothing"]{.class_4723} when they are actually ["wretched, and miserable, and poor, and blind, and naked" ]{.class_4723}(Rev. 3:17).

The Apostle Paul wrote his protégé in the ministry, ["But they that will be rich fall into temptation and a snare, and into many foolish and hurtful lusts, which drown men in destruction and perdition"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:9). When you love money and covet after money, you err ["from the faith"]{.class_4723} and pierce yourself ["through with many sorrows"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:10).

If you have a choice between being ["rich in this world"]{.class_4723} or being ["rich in good works"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:17--18), you had better choose the latter, because you will be storing up the things that give you ["a good foundation against the time to come"]{.class_4723} (see 1 Tim. 6:19 and our comments in that Commentary). One of the best things []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_389}you can do with your money, after the support of your local church, is to give it to missionaries that are trying to win the lost overseas, especially in third world countries where those poor folks don't have a chance of hearing the Gospel without your help. That's treasure laid up in Heaven. You will see that money again in the form of saved souls in eternity. You will get more returns on such an investment than you possibly could on Wall Street stocks, bonds, and securities.

["Set your affection on THINGS ABOVE, not on things on the earth"]{.class_4723} (Col. 3:2). Put your money into things you'll see up [there,]{.class_4725} not down here. Wouldn't you like somebody to come up to you in Glory and put his arms around you and say, "I'm here because you gave to that missionary or that ministry. Your gift made it possible for me to hear the Gospel, and I got saved. Thank you; thank you for giving that money to the Lord and His work instead of spending it on yourself and something you wanted"? That'll be something, boy. You won't be thinking about the new TV set or the new "iPad" or that new car, etc., then.

22 And he said unto his disciples, Therefore I say unto you, Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat; neither for the body, what ye shall put on.

23 The life is more than meat, and the body is more than raiment.

24 Consider the ravens: for they neither sow nor reap; which neither have storehouse nor barn; and God feedeth them: how much more are ye better than the fowls?

25 And which of you with taking thought can add to his stature one cubit?

26 If ye then be not able to do that thing which is least, why take ye thought for the rest?

27 Consider the lilies how they grow: they toil not, they spin not; and yet I say unto you, that Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.

28 If then God so clothe the grass, which is to day in the field, and to morrow is cast into the oven; how much more will he clothe you, O ye of little faith?

29 And seek not ye what ye shall eat, or what ye shall []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_390}drink, neither be ye of doubtful mind.

30 For all these things do the nations of the world seek after: and your Father knoweth that ye have need of these things.

31 But rather seek ye the kingdom of God; and all these things shall be added unto you.

32 Fear not, little flock; for it is your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom.

33 Sell that ye have, and give alms; provide yourselves bags which wax not old, a treasure in the heavens that faileth not, where no thief approacheth, neither moth corrupteth.

34 For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.

Here is another passage from the Sermon on the Mount (cf. Matt. 6:25--33; see our comments on 6:17), but the material naturally follows His comments on materialism and covetousness in verses 13--21.

["Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat; neither for the body, what ye shall put on. The life is more than meat, and the body is more than raiment"]{.class_4723} (vss. 22--23). Paul said, ["And having food and raiment let us be therewith content"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:8). Obviously, your life and your body are more important than what you stick in your mouth or what you wear. The rule of Scripture is that if you've got enough to eat to keep you alive and are clothed enough to protect you from the elements that that should be enough. Of course, for Americans, it isn't. They want a house, a car, a television set, a refrigerator/freezer, a washer/dryer, two weeks paid vacation, etc.

["Consider the ravens"]{.class_4723} (vs. 24). Jesus is much more specific than He was in Matthew 6:25; there He just said, ["Behold the fowls of the air."]{.class_4723} Here He gives you one specific kind of bird: a [raven.]{.class_4725} The raven is an unclean bird (Lev. 11:13--15; Deut. 14:12--14).

The Lord asked Job, ["Who provideth for the raven his food?"]{.class_4723} (Job 38:41). The answer is here in Luke 12:24---["God feedeth them."]{.class_4723} The idea is that if God takes care of an unclean []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_391}bird, how much more is He going to take care of His own children.

["And which of you with taking thought can add to his stature one cubit?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 25). A cubit is the distance between the tip of the middle finger and the bottom of the elbow: it is about eighteen inches. You can't grow one inch taller by worrying about it, much less eighteen inches. Worrying won't accomplish anything (vs. 26). The old saying is that there are two things you should never worry about: what you can do and what you can't do. If you can do something about a thing, do it and stop worrying about it; if you can't, let it be and don't worry about it.

For the Christian, worry is especially bad because it shows a lack of faith in his heavenly Father. That Bible says, ["Be careful for nothing]{.class_4723} \[i.e., stop worrying\][; but in every thing by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known unto God"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 4:6). The little chorus goes: "Why worry when you can pray?" To see how most Christian act, you would think it said: "Why pray when you can worry?"

Peter says, ["Casting all your care upon him; for he careth for you"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 5:7). Turn your worries over to the Lord and let Him worry about them for a change.

["Consider the lilies"]{.class_4723} (vs. 27). Jesus has been talking about food and clothes (vss. 22--23). The raven is to be considered for how God feeds it, and the lily is to be considered for how God clothes it. Lilies don't work to produce any thread or cloth, yet their beauty exceeds that of the finest clothes Solomon ever wore.

That's good for a Christian. Christians, especially women and girls, are too concerned about the fashions of this world. If Hollywood says take up the skirt, up it comes; if Hollywood says lower it, down it goes. For the Christian woman, the ["adorning"]{.class_4723} should ["be the hidden man of the heart\...even the ornament of a meek and quiet spirit, which is in the sight of God of great price"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 3:3--4). The Lord is more concerned with how you look on the inside, not how you look on the outside (1 Sam. 16:7).

["If then God so clothe the grass, which is to day in the field, and to morrow is cast into the oven"]{.class_4723} (vs. 28). ["The grass,"]{.class_4723} here, is obviously associated with ["the lilies"]{.class_4723} of verse 27, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_392}so these are probably not the traditional "Easter lilies" that are always pictured. This is obviously a species of grass bearing tiny flowers belonging to the lily family. Peter said, ["For all flesh is as grass, and all the glory of man as the FLOWER OF GRASS. The grass withereth, and the FLOWER THEREOF falleth away"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 1:24).

So the lilies, here, are pictures of unsaved men. Unsaved men either wither and die (1 Pet. 1:24), or they are gathered and burned at the Second Advent (Mal. 4:1; Matt. 13:30, 40--42). Yet during their brief time on earth, God feeds and clothes them.

In systematic theology, this is known as "common grace." It is the common goodness God extends to all of His creation. Jesus said that God ["maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:45). Paul said this was a ["witness"]{.class_4723} to all men, whether they had any Bible or not (Acts 14:17), and that such ["goodness"]{.class_4723} should lead a man ["to repentance"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 2:4). The point Jesus is making is: "If God takes care of unsaved men like that, don't you think He's going to take care of His children too?"

Of course, we have been making spiritual and practical application here to the born-again Christian in the Body of Christ, but look at the peculiar [Jewish]{.class_4725} nature of Christ's words: ["For all these things do the NATIONS OF THE WORLD seek after: and YOUR Father\..."]{.class_4723} (vs. 30). [Whose]{.class_4725} "Father"? [The Father of the Jewish nation]{.class_4725} (see our remarks under 11:2). He's not talking about the Father of the individual Christian, although we can certainly make a spiritual application that way (Rom. 8:15). The contrast here, though, is between ["the nations of the world"]{.class_4723} and His audience. His audience is bearded, Temple-worshipping, pork-abstaining Sabbath-observing [Jews,]{.class_4725} and ["the nations of the world"]{.class_4723} are ["Gentiles"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 6:32). There is no third group (["the church of God"]{.class_4723}---1 Cor. 10:32) until Calvary (Eph. 2:16).

Now that's the kind of thing that drives the Gentiles mad with envy. The Greeks said, "Whom the gods would destroy, they first make mad," and one of the best ways to drive a person crazy is with [jealousy.]{.class_4725} The Gentile nations can't stand the fact that God [discriminated]{.class_4725} and picked out [one people]{.class_4725} as His "son" (Exod. 4:22). God is the Gentiles' Creator, but He's [not]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_393} their Father (Acts 17:28--29). There is no such thing as "the Fatherhood of God and the brotherhood of man." If a Gentile wants God as his Father, He has to come [through that Jew]{.class_4725} (John 4:22).

Verse 31 should be compared with its companion passage in Matthew 6:33. What is so peculiar about Matthew 6:33 is that it is the first occurrence of ["the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} in that Gospel. Up until that point, Jesus has been preaching the physical Kingdom of Heaven (Matt. 4:17, 5:3, 10, 19--20). But when He gets to talking about not worrying about what you eat, drink, and wear, He brings up the Kingdom of God. Why is that? Because ["the kingdom of God is NOT meat and drink"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 14:17). ["Meat and drink"]{.class_4723} are [physical,]{.class_4725} and that's not what the Kingdom of God is all about (Luke 17:21).

Those Jews were commanded to seek the [spiritual]{.class_4725} kingdom ["first"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 6:33), and then, after that, they would get the physical one. They didn't do it, so they lost their shirt for the next 2,000 years.

The Apostle Paul wrote, ["For they ]{.class_4723}\[the Jews\][ being ignorant of GOD'S RIGHTEOUSNESS, and going about to establish THEIR OWN RIGHTEOUSNESS, have not submitted themselves unto THE RIGHTEOUSNESS OF GOD. For CHRIST is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 10:3--4).

Did you catch that? Those Jews weren't just commanded to ["seek\...the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (vs. 31); they were commanded to ["seek\...the kingdom of God, AND HIS RIGHTEOUSNESS"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 6:33). God's righteousness wasn't in the Mosaic Law; it was in [Jesus Christ.]{.class_4725} As a nation, they rejected Christ; so they lost God's righteousness, they lost the Kingdom of God (Matt. 21:43), and as a result, they lost the physical Kingdom of Heaven as well until they receive their Messiah at His return (Zech. 12, 14).

Verse 32: ["Fear not, LITTLE FLOCK; for it is your Father's good pleasure to give YOU the kingdom."]{.class_4723} So Christ is no longer talking to the nation as a whole; He is talking to a [small remnant]{.class_4725} out of that nation (see Rom. 11:2--5). What's the problem? The problem is that God rejects the physical seed of Abraham who reject His righteousness (see Rom. 9:6--8). He takes that []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_394}["little flock"]{.class_4723} and uses it to form a new nation---a [spiritual]{.class_4725} nation.

["But ye are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, AN HOLY NATION, a peculiar people\...which in time past WERE NOT a people, but are NOW the people of God"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 2:9--10).

Those Jews, as a people, turn down their Messiah, so God tells them, ["ye are not my people, and I will not be your God"]{.class_4723} (Hos. 1:9). Then the Lord takes the small remnant of the Jews who do believe in Christ and makes them one with Gentiles who believe in Christ (Rom. 9:24; Eph. 2:11--19) and says, ["I will call them my people WHICH WERE NOT MY PEOPLE; and her beloved, WHICH WAS NOT BELOVED ]{.class_4723}\[see Eph. 1:6\][. And it shall come to pass, that in the place where it was said unto them, Ye are not my people; there shall they be called the children of the living God"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 9:25--26).

Those Jews ["stumbled at that stumblingstone"]{.class_4723} \[Jesus Christ---1 Pet. 2:6--8\] and rejected the righteousness of God by faith (Rom. 9:31--32), so God took the Kingdom of God away from them and gave it to another nation (Matt. 21:43). That ["nation bringing forth the fruits thereof"]{.class_4723} is not any Vatican State, Roman Catholic Church, or "Islamic Nation"; it is His spiritual Body made up of Jews and Gentiles in Christ (see above).

Now one day, Israel will get [both kingdoms,]{.class_4725} but that's at the return of her King. When Jesus Christ comes back, He takes away their sins (Rom. 11:26--27) and fills every single Jew on earth with the Holy Spirit (Acts 2:16--18 cf. Zech. 12:10)---the Kingdom of God. Then He will be ["king over all the earth"]{.class_4723} (Zech. 14:9) with Jerusalem as His capital (Zech. 14:16--21), and if a Gentile wants to see Christ, he will have to have a Jew bring him (Zech. 8:23)---the Kingdom of Heaven. But right now, the nation of Israel has been [temporarily]{.class_4725} set aside (Rom. 11) ["until the times of restitution of all things"]{.class_4723} (Acts 3:21).

Now let that be a warning to you if you are an American Christian. [If]{.class_4725} America is a "Christian nation" (and it certainly did start out as a bunch of professing Christians forming a new country based on Biblical principles), and if God has been good to America (and He []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_395}certainly has), then don't get puffed up about being "the people of God" in this dispensation. If this nation took the place of Israel in this dispensation (which it didn't, though many Christians think it did), then you can expect the same treatment that He gave in the Old Testament to the people He chose and to whom He gave the Scriptures (Rom. 3:2). You can expect famines so bad till the women eat their children; you can expect lying face down in the dirt with your face covered with dung; you can expect foreign invaders taking your children away so that you never see them again. You say, "Why?" Because that's what God did to His people when they lived the way Americans live now (read the book of Lamentations).

America is a drop in the bucket just like Babylon, Persia, Greece, and Rome (Isa. 40:15). Its destiny is total destruction (Jer. 30:11) and the unsheathed wrath of God (Jer. 25:17--28). I am a red-blooded, true-blue American; I am not one of these neo-Marxist, "global citizen" Americans. But as much as I have loved this country and appreciated the freedom it has afforded me, I have no illusions about it. The Christian's home is not down here; [it's up there]{.class_4725} (Col. 3:2). "This world is not my home; I'm just a passin' through. My treasures are laid up somewhere beyond the blue." ["For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34).

35 Let your loins be girded about, and your lights burning;

36 And ye yourselves like unto men that wait for their lord, when he will return from the wedding; that when he cometh and knocketh, they may open unto him immediately.

37 Blessed are those servants, whom the lord when he cometh shall find watching: verily I say unto you, that he shall gird himself, and make them to sit down to meat, and will come forth and serve them.

38 And if he shall come in the second watch, or come in the third watch, and find them so, blessed are those servants.

39 And this know, that if the goodman of the house had known what hour the thief would come, he would have []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_396}watched, and not have suffered his house to be broken through.

40 Be ye therefore ready also: for the Son of man cometh at an hour when ye think not.

Verses 35--36 are a very abbreviated version of the Parable of the Virgins in Matthew 25:1--13, with some important variations. Like the virgins in Matthew 25, the disciples here are to be ready for the coming of the Bridegroom: ["your loins be girded about, and your lights burning"]{.class_4723} (vs. 35). But unlike the Matthew account, the Lord is not going in to the marriage (as in Matt. 25:10), He is returning from it: ["And ye yourselves like unto men that wait for their lord, when he will RETURN from the wedding"]{.class_4723} (vs. 36). That's the Advent, not the Rapture of the Church. The Bride of Christ has to be caught out first before there can be a wedding from which the Lord returns. So [doctrinally,]{.class_4725} the passage here in Luke 12 has nothing to do with the return of Christ for His Church.

Here is the order of events in Revelation, and there are several different groups involved. In Revelation 4--5, there is a group caught up to Heaven represented by the Apostle John (Rev. 4:1). These people have been "redeemed by the blood of the Lamb" (Rev. 5:9), so they are the Church, the Body of Christ (Col. 1:14--18). In Revelation 6, the Tribulation starts. One of the reasons I believe in a pre-Tribulation Rapture of the Church is very deep and highly theological, but I will give it to you anyway. It is: [Revelation 4 and 5 comes before Revelation 6.]{.class_4725} I know that is so technical it is hard for some of you to grasp, especially if you are educated beyond the twelfth grade, but there it is.

During the Tribulation, 144,000 Jews (12,000 from each tribe) are called out by God (Rev. 7:3--8), and they are said to be ["virgins"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 14:4 cf. Matt. 25:1). These ["virgins"]{.class_4723} are caught up to Heaven (Rev. 14:1) at some point toward the end of the Tribulation before the Second Advent (Rev. 14:14--20). Before the Advent, up in Heaven, there is a marriage that takes place between the Lamb (Jesus Christ---John 1:29) and His Bride (the Church---Eph. 5:21--32)---see Revelation 19:1--7. So these Jewish virgins go ["to meet the bridegroom"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 25:1) and go ["in with him to the marriage"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_397} (Matt. 25:10).

Meanwhile, back on earth, there is a group of Jewish saints, saved by keeping ["the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 14:12), who did not go up in the post-Tribulation rapture, but are left to ["endure to the end"]{.class_4723} to be saved (Matt. 24:13). They are represented by those Jewish servants in the Parable of the Talents (Matt. 25:14--30). They wait here on earth for their Lord to return from His wedding and reckon with them over their service. They open to their Lord (vs. 36); He doesn't open to them (Matt. 25:10--11).

Verse 37 indicates that there may be [two]{.class_4725} "Marriage Suppers of the Lamb": one up in Heaven before the Advent (Rev. 19:7--9) with the Bride and the virgins (Psa. 45:10--15) and one down here on earth with all the rest of the Jews alive at the Second Advent. This would explain how someone could show up at the feast without a wedding garment and be thrown out into Hell (Matt. 22:11--13); that would be the feast on earth, not in Heaven.

Now verse 37 has a wonderful spiritual application for the Christian. If you serve Christ now, He'll serve you later. When He was here on earth, He girded Himself and washed His disciples' feet (John 13:4--5); at His wedding, He'll gird Himself again, but this time as a waiter. "Can I take your order, sir? And what would you like to drink with that? Very good; I'll have it out to you shortly." Can you imagine that? Your Lord and Saviour waiting on [you?!]{.class_4725} But that's one of the ways He honors you for serving Him now.

Brethren, most Christians want their "dessert" before they eat their "spinach," but that's not how things work. It's the cross before the crown. It's suffering with Him [first,]{.class_4725} and [then]{.class_4725} reigning with Him. If you don't want to be stuck over in a corner somewhere at the Marriage Supper of the Lamb with nothing but a hunk of bread and a glass of water, you had better get busy serving Jesus Christ while you have a chance.

In verse 38, Jesus likens His Second Coming to a master returning to his house during one of the night watches. He mentions ["the second watch"]{.class_4723} and ["the third watch"]{.class_4723} here, but if you want the complete picture, you have to go back and pick up Mark 13:35.

"Watch ye therefore: for ye know not when the master []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_398}of the house cometh, at even, or at midnight, or at the cockcrowing, or in the morning."

With a Jewish day being divided into two twelve hour segments (cf. John 11:9), a night of four "watches" would be: 1) 6 p.m.--9 p.m., 2) 9 p.m.--12 a.m., 3) 12 a.m.--3 a.m., 4) 3 a.m.--6 a.m. The "night" is defined in the New Testament as the [Church Age,]{.class_4725} the time between Acts 2 and the Rapture (John 9:4--5 cf. 1 Thess. 5:1--10). Given that, we don't have to wonder at which "watch" the Lord returns. Both Testaments describe the Second Advent as the sun coming up in the [morning]{.class_4725}.

["But unto you that fear my name shall the SUN OF RIGHTEOUSNESS ARISE with healing in his wings; and ye shall go forth, and grow up as calves of the stall"]{.class_4723} (Mal. 4:2). The whole chapter is about the Second Advent.

["We have also a more sure word of prophecy; whereunto ye do well that ye take heed, as unto a light that shineth in a dark place, until the DAY DAWN, and the DAY STAR ARISE in your hearts"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 1:19). What's the context? ["The power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723}---2 Peter 1:16.

["Then shall the righteous shine forth AS THE SUN in the kingdom of their Father"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 13:43), talking about the coming of ["the Son of man"]{.class_4723} at ["the end of this world"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 13:40--41).

So there's no doubt about when Jesus returns at His Advent. It's not the second or third watch; it's the [fourth,]{.class_4725} the [morning watch.]{.class_4725} There's a marvelous picture of that over in Matthew 14:22--25 when Jesus walks on the water. Matthew 14:25 says, ["And in the FOURTH WATCH OF THE NIGHT Jesus went unto them, walking on the sea."]{.class_4723} When it comes to ["the sea"]{.class_4723} at Christ's Advent, that turns out to be a literal body of water between the second and third heavens that would make the combined oceans and seas of this world look like a single drop of water in the Pacific Ocean (see our comments on Hab. 3:8, 10 in that Commentary).

The illustration of Christ's return in verse 39 is quite ironic, for ["the goodman of the house"]{.class_4723} is not "good" at all. That's a reference to the Devil as ["the god of this world"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 4:4). How do you know that to be so? Because Jesus Christ Himself is []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_399}["the thief"]{.class_4723} (1 Thess. 5:2, 2 Pet. 3:10; Rev. 3:3, 16:15). When He is arrested in the Garden of Gethsemane, He asked the ones who had come to take Him, ["Be ye come out, as against a thief\...?"]{.class_4723} (22:52). When He was crucified, He was crucified between two thieves (Mark 15:27), and before dying, He saved one of those thieves (23:43). So Christ has quite a rapport with thieves, and this is why.

When Jesus Christ comes back, whether it be at the Rapture or the Advent, He comes back to spoil the Devil's house and rob it of the most valuable things in it. Do you know what those are? Well, in the case of the Rapture, it's [you,]{.class_4725} if you are saved.

Do you know why Satan fights you so hard if you are a soulwinner? It's because you are trying to [kidnap his children.]{.class_4725} Do you think you would be kindly disposed to someone breaking into your house and stealing your baby from the crib? I don't think so. Well, when you lead a lost person to Jesus Christ, you are taking him out of the family of the Devil and putting him into the family of God, and his "daddy" (John 8:44) doesn't like it.

Now the context of verse 40 is the Second Advent, which is reinforced by the use of the term ["Son of man."]{.class_4723} No Christian is waiting for the return of the Son of man; he's waiting for the return of the Son of God (1 Thess. 1:10). Nevertheless, you can certainly make spiritual application to the Rapture, and the Scriptures do. Look at Revelation 3:3.

"If therefore thou shalt not watch, I will come on thee as a thief, and thou shalt not know what hour I will come upon thee."

Without a doubt, the reference is to the Tribulation [doctrinally]{.class_4725} (cf. Rev. 16:15), but if you apply it to a New Testament local church (which you certainly can in the context), then you can definitely apply it [spiritually.]{.class_4725} The New Testament believer is not in darkness in these matters because he has the advanced revelation of completed Scripture (1 Thess. 5:4), but he can go to sleep spiritually and be taken by surprise at the return of his Lord. So the New Testament believer in the Body of Christ is commanded:

["Therefore let us not sleep, as do others; but let us watch and be sober"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_400} (1 Thess. 5:6).

The Christian is to be ["looking for that blessed hope, and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723} (Tit. 2:13).

41 Then Peter said unto him, Lord, speakest thou this parable unto us, or even to all?

42 And the Lord said, Who then is that faithful and wise steward, whom his lord shall make ruler over his household, to give them their portion of meat in due season?

43 Blessed is that servant, whom his lord when he cometh shall find so doing.

44 Of a truth I say unto you, that he will make him ruler over all that he hath.

45 But and if that servant say in his heart, My lord delayeth his coming; and shall begin to beat the menservants and maidens, and to eat and drink, and to be drunken;

46 The lord of that servant will come in a day when he looketh not for him, and at an hour when he is not aware, and will cut him in sunder, and will appoint him his portion with the unbelievers.

47 And that servant, which knew his lord's will, and prepared not himself, neither did according to his will, shall be beaten with many stripes.

48 But he that knew not, and did commit things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few stripes. For unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required: and to whom men have committed much, of him they will ask the more.

Now Jesus has just gotten through talking about His servants (vss. 36--37), and He has addressed His remarks to His ["little flock"]{.class_4723} (vs. 32). Naturally, Peter has a question as to the scope of Christ's words, for in Isaiah 44:1, [all]{.class_4725} of Israel is God's servant. Christ doesn't even answer directly; He leaves the application open. So we will []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_401}make application to the New Testament believer where appropriate, and where we can't, we'll put it where it belongs.

["Who then is that faithful and wise steward"]{.class_4723} (vs. 42). Well, that could be applied to the New Testament. Paul said, ["Let a man so account of us, as of the ministers of Christ, and stewards of the mysteries of God. Moreover it is required in stewards, that a man be found faithful"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 4:1--2).

The faithful steward is promised a Millennial rule with Christ in verse 44, and that can be applied to the Christian as well (2 Tim. 2:12). But look at verse 46. If the steward becomes unfaithful in his task and abuses his position (vs. 45), the Lord ["will appoint him his portion with the unbelievers."]{.class_4723} That's not you, if you are saved. Even a believer guilty of the most heinous of sins still has his spirit ["saved in the day of the Lord Jesus"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 5:5). [Nothing]{.class_4725} can separate you ["from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:38--39). You ["are sealed unto the day of redemption,"]{.class_4723} even when you grieve the Holy Spirit who lives in your body (Eph. 4:30).

But there are servants who not only lose their Millennial inheritance (which you can as a Christian---1 Cor. 6:9--10), but wind up in Hell (Matt. 25:30). As we will see when we get to Luke 19, that is the difference between the Gentile Parable of the Pounds and the Jewish Parable of the Talents. In the Gentile Parable of the Pounds, the wicked servant doesn't go to Hell, and he is not counted as one of the Lord's enemies (19:24--27).

Verses 47--48 are sometimes used to prove "degrees of punishment" in Hell, and there is certainly merit to the argument. God is just and will not punish some sinner in the jungles of the Amazon or Papua New Guinea, who never heard the Gospel and had no Bible or New Testament, to the same degree He will an American who had every opportunity to get saved over a period of sixty to seventy years. "The heathen who've never heard" go to Hell because they are [sinners,]{.class_4725} but they won't suffer the same torments as a fellow who, when confronted with the offer of free salvation through the Lord Jesus Christ, turned it down flat, and on more than one occasion.

Verse 47 shows you that there are sins of [omission]{.class_4725} as well as [commission.]{.class_4725} James 4:17 says, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_402}["Therefore to him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin."]{.class_4723} You may not outright break one of the commandments, but if you don't do what you know to be right, it's a sin.

Verse 48 reinforces the old adage "ignorance of the law is no excuse." The servant was still responsible for his master's orders whether he knew them or not. It was his responsibility to find out what the master said. He could have asked around and found out from his fellow servants. He could have acted on the last order he received.

When it comes to salvation, God has given "the heathen who've never heard" His word in nature (Psa. 19:1--3) and the conscience (Rom. 5:15). When any of them respond positively to the light God has given them, God obligates Himself to give them more light until they get the Gospel (see our remarks under Rom. 2:5--11).

["For unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required: and to whom men have committed much, of him they will ask the more"]{.class_4723} (vs. 48). The more you know and the more you have, the more responsible you are for it. That's why living in America is so much worse for a sinner than living some place like the Chad or the Amazon jungle or Saudi Arabia. The people in those countries may have little to no chance in a lifetime of hearing the Gospel, but someone living in America can get it any time he wants it. There are churches all over the place, tracts all over the place, Bibles all over the place. There's hardly a town anywhere where you can't pick up some Christian radio program; there are even Christian television stations where you can get [some]{.class_4725} truth. You have no excuse at all if you go to Hell from America. You [could]{.class_4725} have gotten the truth if you really wanted it.

49 I am come to send fire on the earth; and what will I, if it be already kindled?

50 But I have a baptism to be baptized with; and how am I straitened till it be accomplished!

51 Suppose ye that I am come to give peace on earth? I tell you, Nay; but rather division:

52 For from henceforth there shall be five in one house []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_403}divided, three against two, and two against three.

53 The father shall be divided against the son, and the son against the father; the mother against the daughter, and the daughter against the mother; the mother in law against her daughter in law, and the daughter in law against her mother in law.

["I am come to send fire on the earth"]{.class_4723} (vs. 49). Over in Matthew 10:34, He said, ["Think not that I am come to send peace on earth: I come not to send peace, but a sword."]{.class_4723} He said, "I've come to [cut]{.class_4725} you (Matthew) and [burn]{.class_4725} you (Luke)." He said that after the angels said, ["on earth peace, good will toward men"]{.class_4723} (Luke 2:14). How's [that]{.class_4725} for a "contradiction" in the Bible.

That's the kind of stuff that just drives folks crazy. They try to fix it with "the Greek," and when they get through, they have a bigger mess than when they started. You aren't going to get things straightened out by running to "the originals." You've got to get the texts in their [right place.]{.class_4725} He doesn't come to bring "peace on earth" at His First Advent; He doesn't even bring "peace on earth" at His Second Advent until He has cut and burned over 200 million UN troops (Hab. 3; Rev. 19; Psa. 110; Zech. 14).

["And what will I, if it be already kindled?"]{.class_4723} At the Second Advent, the ["fire"]{.class_4723} is [literal]{.class_4725} (Mal. 4); here it is [figurative,]{.class_4725} for He is about to talk about [dividing]{.class_4725} whole families over what each member does with [Him.]{.class_4725} But even then, you have to watch out. All throughout the era of the Roman Empire and into the Catholic Dark Ages, Christians were [burned at the stake]{.class_4725} because of the division caused by Jesus Christ and that Book. Even today, Moslems are burning down the houses and churches of Christians because of the division over Jesus Christ and the Book. So even though the immediate application may be figurative, the end result is still [literal fire.]{.class_4725}

The ["baptism"]{.class_4723} of verse 50 is obviously not [water]{.class_4725} baptism, for Jesus was baptized in water back in chapter 3. When the Lord brings up this baptism in Matthew 20:22, the context is His coming death on the cross (Matt. 20:18--19), so this baptism is one of [suffering.]{.class_4725}

["And how am I straitened till it be accomplished"]{.class_4723} (vs. 50). We already saw how Christ ["stedfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_404} (see Luke 9:51 and our comments on the verse). The word ["straitened"]{.class_4723} here is from the word [strait,]{.class_4725} meaning a narrow, tight passage. We would say "everything's coming down to the wire" for the cross.

You won't hear verse 51 preached by any major Christian leader in America today. That's because it says that Christ came to [divide]{.class_4725} folks, not bring them together. The Lord Jesus Christ didn't come to start any ecumenical movement or integrate "nuthin'." Americans won't accept that; it's too negative. They just want everyone to "get along."

Verses 52--53 say that whole families will split up over Jesus Christ. I have known young men who got saved, and their parents disinherited them. That's really bad in Catholic and Jewish families. Over in Jerusalem, the Rabbis put out tracts telling the Jews not to listen to Christian missionaries because it would break up the Jewish homes---AND THEY'RE RIGHT.

Over in the companion passage in Matthew 10:36, Jesus told His disciples that ["a man's foes shall be they of his own household."]{.class_4723} When we get to 14:26 here in Luke, He will tell them that if they don't hate father, mother, wife, children, brothers, and sisters, they can't even be His disciples; that is, when it comes to a choice between loving momma, daddy, et al., and doing what they say, and loving God and doing what He says, you had better let family go.

When I got saved, the rip between my family and me was absolutely complete. My father, a vestryman in the Episcopal Church, didn't approve of me getting saved. My mother, a chronic alcoholic and bridge player, didn't approve of it. My brother, a "section eight" in World War II who spent most of his years in mental institutions, didn't bother to handle it one way or another. I had an unsaved uncle and an unsaved aunt who were both drunkards who never listened to me. Now I had a saved mother-in-law praying for me, but when it came to her daughter, my first wife, I'll never know till I get home to Glory whether she was ever saved or not.

Brethren, especially you husbands and fathers, when it comes to this business of following Christ or following family, you had better take the lead and follow Christ and not worry about what family []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_405}does. Joshua had been a slave laborer in Egypt; he had spent forty years wandering in the wilderness and twenty years in combat. His wife went through all of that with him. When he came to the end of his life, he said, ["but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD"]{.class_4723} (Josh. 24:15). He didn't consult his wife one time before saying that. Now if that had been an American woman, she would have said, "We'll just see about that." But Joshua made up his mind for his whole family, and if any of them didn't like it, they could [get out.]{.class_4725}

We have had young men come through PBI whose wives left them when they decided to study for the ministry. All I can say is, if she wants to leave, let her leave. Don't you quit just because she does. When John Wesley's wife left him because of the ministry, he said, "I didn't send her away, and I don't recall her." That doesn't mean you should neglect your wife for the ministry, but it does mean that if she is not willing to follow you in what God has called you to do, you had better not give up God's calling to keep peace in the house.

54 And he said also to the people, When ye see a cloud rise out of the west, straightway ye say, There cometh a shower; and so it is.

55 And when ye see the south wind blow, ye say, There will be heat; and it cometh to pass.

56 Ye hypocrites, ye can discern the face of the sky and of the earth; but how is it that ye do not discern this time?

Here Jesus is rebuking the Jews because they can predict the weather, but they can't understand the times when it comes to Bible prophecy (see Matt. 16:3). People have all kinds of ways of predicting the weather. If you go into these little mom-and-pop tourist-trap stores along the side of the highway, they have rocks there in a cedar frame that are called "old Indian weather rocks." If it's white, it's snowing; if it's wet, it's raining; if it's dry, the sun is shining---"old Indian weather rock never wrong." Well, that's a joke, see, but it's better than some of the weathermen on TV. They've gotten the weather wrong so many times that now they don't even try to predict it for sure; they just say 20%, 30%, 40%, etc., []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_406}[chance]{.class_4725} of rain, snow, or whatever.

Those ancient peoples had all kinds of ways of predicting the weather without barometers or hydrometers or satellites, and Jesus said they were [right:]{.class_4725} ["and so it is"]{.class_4723} (vs. 54); ["and it cometh to pass"]{.class_4723} (vs. 55). The illustration He gives in Matthew 16:2--3 is from an old sailor's rhyme: "Red in the morning, sailor take warning; red at night, sailor delight." That's talking about whether the sky is red when the sun rises and sets: if it's red at sunrise, you are in for some rough weather; if it's red at sunset, the next day will be fine weather-wise. Well, if they have that much wisdom to predict the weather, why can't they discern what's going on around them with Jesus Christ (vs. 56).

Now the ["times"]{.class_4723} are marked by ["signs"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 16:3), and it is ["the Jews"]{.class_4723} who ["require a sign" ]{.class_4723}(1 Cor. 1:22), not the Church. But that doesn't mean you can't read those signs given to Israel. The signs are for the Second Advent, but since the Rapture comes before the Advent, any sign showing you the Advent is getting close means the Rapture is that much closer.

Evangelist Jim White used to put it like this: when he would be driving up I-75 to his home in Milford, Ohio, he would see signs telling him so many miles to Cincinnati. Now he wasn't going to Cincinnati; he was going to Milford. But Milford isn't very far from Cincinnati, so any sign for Cincinnati was a sign telling him he was getting closer to home.

So when you see a sign for Christ's Second Coming being fulfilled (and there are plenty of them), let that be an encouragement to you as a Christian. No, the signs aren't for you doctrinally, but you can still draw hope from them. He's coming to take the Christian out [before]{.class_4725} He comes down at the Advent.

57 Yea, and why even of yourselves judge ye not what is right?

58 When thou goest with thine adversary to the magistrate, as thou art in the way, give diligence that thou mayest be delivered from him; lest he hale thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2484.xhtml_page_407}officer cast thee into prison.

59 I tell thee, thou shalt not depart thence, till thou hast paid the very last mite.

Jesus doesn't just want them to discern what is going on around them in regards to Him; He wants them to [do]{.class_4725} something about it: ["why even of yourselves judge ye not what is RIGHT"]{.class_4723} (vs. 57). It's a [moral]{.class_4725} problem, not merely an intellectual or theological one.

The illustration Jesus gives is a law suit. He's warning those Jews that if they are in the wrong, they had better make things right before they wind up in debtors' prison. Back then, when you went broke, you didn't declare bankruptcy and default on your bills and get to keep your car, your house, and your property. Everything you had was taken from you and sold and applied to your debt. If that wasn't enough, you were sent off to a work prison and were paid a pittance for your labor; anything you earned was sent to your creditor until your bill was paid off. And of course, at the low wages you received, that might be years.

The companion passage to these verses is found in Matthew 18:28--35 in the Parable of the Unforgiving Servant. Neither the passage here in Luke nor the one in Matthew have anything to do with the Roman Catholic doctrine of Purgatory. The word [fire]{.class_4725} doesn't show up in either passage.

The teaching is that if you want the Lord to be forgiving and lenient in His day-to-day dealings with you, you had better be forgiving and lenient with others. Of course, if you are a Christian, all your sins were forgiven at Calvary (Heb. 1:3, 10:8--12), but you can still reap what you sow in the flesh (Gal. 6:7--8). And part of the law of harvest is that you always reap [more]{.class_4725} than you sow.

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml}

# Chapter 13 {.heading_78-0}

13:1 There were present at that season some that told him of the Galilaeans, whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.

2 And Jesus answering said unto them, Suppose ye that these Galilaeans were sinners above all the Galilaeans, because they suffered such things?

3 I tell you, Nay: but, except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish.

4 Or those eighteen, upon whom the tower in Siloam fell, and slew them, think ye that they were sinners above all men that dwelt in Jerusalem?

5 I tell you, Nay: but, except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish.

According to the historical records, one of the reasons Pontius Pilate was in such a quandary at the trial of Jesus was that he had already stirred up unrest among the Jewish people on two previous occasions, and Rome didn't look favorably on its [Pax Romana]{.class_4725} being disturbed by an incompetent Governor. Verse 1 is one of those occasions. During one of the feasts, when the Roman Governor resided in Jerusalem to preserve order, Pilate had his "peace-keeping forces" go into the outer court of the Temple and kill some Jews from Galilee while they were making their sacrifices. The natural assumption is the same as that made by Job's three friends: God wouldn't have allowed that to happen if they were not guilty of some great sin.

Jesus' answer to that is ["Nay"]{.class_4723} (vs. 3)---[no,]{.class_4725} they [weren't.]{.class_4725} There was nothing particularly more wicked about them than anybody else in Galilee. If you think it is only really bad people who get murdered, to say you are naive would be an understatement. The first person to be murdered and the last person to be murdered in a Jewish Old Testament were both [prophets]{.class_4725} (see 11:51).

When the Flood came in Noah's day, it probably drowned about 300 million people at a minimum. Well, do you think everyone was []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_409}so wicked in thought and deed that they deserved that? You say, "Yeah" (see Gen. 6:6). What about the babies? What about the infants? What about the toddlers? What did they do that was so bad that God had to drown them along with the adults?

You take Sodom? God sends the fire down and burns everyone up. Do you think everyone in Sodom was a queer and a faggot? Not everyone, no (see Gen. 19:14). I don't doubt the majority of them were (say maybe 70%), but not all. But down comes the fire and burns up the children, the cripples hobbling around on crutches, and the ones laid up in the hospital.

Brethren, just as God sends His goodness on the just and unjust (see our remarks under 12:27), He also allows trouble to come on them indiscriminately as well. Living a good life won't exempt you from trouble; it didn't the Apostle Paul (2 Cor. 11:23--28). You ought to do right whether it pays off in this life or not. When trouble comes on you along with everyone else (the hurricane hits, the tornado comes through, the flood washes everything away, you get cancer, etc.), just figure that God has a purpose and knows what He's doing, but don't expect trouble to bypass you.

["Nay: but, except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish."]{.class_4723} The point Christ is making is that they are [all]{.class_4725} sinners (Rom. 3:10, 23). You know that to be so. Just go back in your mind and review everything about which you've thought and planned to do since you were fifteen years old; then tell me what a fine, outstanding person you are.

["Ye shall all likewise perish."]{.class_4723} You live in a day and age when people take the love of God without accepting the fact that they'll ["perish"]{.class_4723} if they don't trust the only means God provided to get them out of perishing (John 3:16). They think the love of God will cause Him to overlook everything wrong they've done and let them into Heaven on "general principles."

[No, it won't.]{.class_4725} ["He that believeth not the Son shall not see life; BUT THE WRATH OF GOD ABIDETH ON HIM"]{.class_4723} (John 3:36). You say, "About whom is he talking, Bro. Ruckman?" Oh, the ones He loved enough to send His Son to die for. If you reject His Son, He'll send you to Hell like a bullet, and He won't think twice about it.

You see, people mistake the mercy, kindness, and longsuffering of God for His love. You can be the recipient of all three and still split Hell wide open. If you want God's love, there's only one place you can access it, and it's at Calvary.

["Herein is love, not that we loved God, but that he loved us, and SENT HIS SON TO BE THE PROPITIATION FOR OUR SINS"]{.class_4723} (1 John 4:10).

["But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, CHRIST DIED FOR US"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 5:8).

The only place where you are going to experience ["the love of God" "is in Christ Jesus our Lord"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:39). If you don't go there, you are under His wrath, not His love.

Verse 4:[ "Or those eighteen, upon whom the tower in Siloam fell, and slew them, think ye that they were sinners above all men that dwelt in Jerusalem?"]{.class_4723} Here are a couple of towers that get bombed in New York and fall and kill 3,000 people. Did all those people who died "get what was coming to them" like some of the liberals believe? Not according to Jesus Christ (vs. 5). There were probably some Christians who died in that tragedy; they paid the price right along with the unsaved crowd. And I bet some of those unsaved folks who died there were decent, respectable people.

Listen, folks, I labor under no delusions when it comes to these matters. I teach that America is under [God's judgment]{.class_4725} for the way she's been living. You can't have a population drunk and doped up, killing a million babies a year through abortion, with Lesbians and faggots running around all over the place, without God being mad enough to destroy the country with drought, famine, financial collapse, loss of freedoms, hurricanes, floods, tornadoes, earthquakes, fires, endless wars, etc.

You say, "Well, you're going to get it in the neck too, Ruckman." Yeah, I probably will; but that doesn't change God's attitude toward a country that kicks His Book out of its schools and public buildings, teaches its citizens that God lied, and passes laws in defiance of what God said. When the axe falls, I expect to get it in the neck along with everyone else. The difference between me and the unsaved crowd is that when my body perishes, that's the worst that can happen to me (see 12:4--5 and our comments on that passage). My sins were []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_411}already judged at Calvary, and I received forgiveness of them when I came to Christ on the fourteenth of March, 1949. When this old body of mine perishes, "I've got a home in Gloryland that outshines the sun." The lost go from the judgment of God here to the judgment of God in Hell.

["Except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish"]{.class_4723} (vs. 5). Unsaved friend, if you wind up in Hell, it's nobody's fault but your own. If God had His "druthers," you would repent and receive Christ right now. ["The Lord is\...not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 3:9). The problem is not with the Lord; it's with [you]{.class_4725}---["And ye will not come to me, that ye might have life"]{.class_4723} (John 5:40).

6 He spake also this parable; A certain man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard; and he came and sought fruit thereon, and found none.

7 Then said he unto the dresser of his vineyard, Behold, these three years I come seeking fruit on this fig tree, and find none: cut it down; why cumbereth it the ground?

8 And he answering said unto him, Lord, let it alone this year also, till I shall dig about it, and dung it:

9 And if it bear fruit, well: and if not, then after that thou shalt cut it down.

The parable has obvious connections to the cursing of the fig tree by Christ in Matthew 21:18--19. In both instances, the fig tree is a picture of self-righteous Israel (see Jer. 24:2, 5, 8; Amos 8:2; Hos. 9:10), a nation with [profession]{.class_4725} of fruit but [no possession.]{.class_4725} The ["certain man"]{.class_4723} (vs. 6) is the Lord, and the ["vineyard"]{.class_4723}[ ]{.class_4737}is the land promised to the nation of Israel---Palestine (Matt. 21:33--39; Isa. 5:1--7).

Just as a ["day"]{.class_4723} in the Scriptures can represent a prophetic time period of ["a thousand years"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 3:8), here, each year stands for 1,000 years. For 3,000 years (Adam--Solomon in 1000 B.C.), the Lord came seeking fruit from Israel and found none. In 1000 B.C., the Lord's ready to cut the fig tree down when ["the dresser"]{.class_4723} (probably a picture of the Lord Jesus Christ in His pre-incarnate state) talks Him into letting it go another thousand years to []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_412}["dig about it, and dung it"]{.class_4723} (vs. 8). In the next thousand years, from the dedication of the Temple to the First Advent of Jesus Christ, the digging comes when Shalmaneser takes the ten northern tribes into captivity (2 Kings 17:3), and the dunging comes when Nebuchadnezzar hauls the two southern tribes into captivity. When Christ shows up, He still finds no fruit, so the Lord cuts the nation down in A.D. 70 when Titus destroys Jerusalem. If it were not for ["the everlasting covenant"]{.class_4723} the Lord made with Abraham (Gen. 17:7, 13, 19), Jacob (1 Chron. 16:17--18; Psa. 105:10--11), and David (2 Sam. 23:5; Isa. 55:3; Jer. 33:19--21), there wouldn't be a Jew left alive on the face of this earth. God raises that nation from the dead (Ezek. 37), plants it back in the land (Isa. 11:11), and causes it to grow and prosper again (Matt. 24:32).

10 And he was teaching in one of the synagogues on the sabbath.

11 And, behold, there was a woman which had a spirit of infirmity eighteen years, and was bowed together, and could in no wise lift up herself.

12 And when Jesus saw her, he called her to him, and said unto her, Woman, thou art loosed from thine infirmity.

13 And he laid his hands on her: and immediately she was made straight, and glorified God.

14 And the ruler of the synagogue answered with indignation, because that Jesus had healed on the sabbath day, and said unto the people, There are six days in which men ought to work: in them therefore come and be healed, and not on the sabbath day.

15 The Lord then answered him, and said, Thou hypocrite, doth not each one of you on the sabbath loose his ox or his ass from the stall, and lead him away to watering?

16 And ought not this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan hath bound, lo, these eighteen years, be loosed from this bond on the sabbath day?

17 And when he had said these things, all his adversaries were ashamed: and all the people rejoiced for all the glorious things that were done by him.

Once more Christ is healing on the Sabbath, and this infuriates the religious rulers (see our remarks under 6:6--11). This time, the Lord heals a woman with a chronic ailment: she had been bent over for eighteen years and couldn't straighten up. The moment she stands up straight, she glorifies God (vs. 13).

["The ruler of the synagogue"]{.class_4723} does not give God the glory, however. He is indignant that Christ healed on the Sabbath day. We have already covered the material on the Sabbath in our remarks on 6:1--5, 6--11 (which see), so we will not go into it further here. Note, though, the evident hypocrisy in the ruler's words: ["There are six days in which MEN ought to work"]{.class_4723} (vs. 14). The healing wasn't the work of [man;]{.class_4725} it was the work of [God]{.class_4725} (John 5:16--17)[.]{.class_4725} God was working His purpose on His day (vs. 16).

["In them therefore come and be healed, and not on the sabbath day."]{.class_4723} The woman didn't come to the synagogue to be healed; she came to a place that [hadn't done a thing for her in eighteen years]{.class_4725}---on the Sabbath or any other day of the week. As far as the text goes, Jesus hadn't come to the synagogue to heal; He had come to teach (vs. 10). The healing was a natural response of compassion to the woman's predicament. When Jesus points out the lack of compassion on the part of the ruler, His enemies are shamed into silence (vs. 17). They no more cared what happened to that woman than the man in the moon.

The contrast between the religious rulers and the common people is sharp. While the rulers were indignant and then ashamed, the ordinary Jew in the "pew" was glorifying God (vs. 13) and rejoicing in what Jesus did (vs. 17). Mark 12:37 says, ["And the common people heard him gladly,"]{.class_4723} implying that the rulers [did not.]{.class_4725}

Now there are some wonderful lessons we can learn from the typology in the passage. As in nearly all of the recorded healings in the Gospels, what you have here is a picture of [salvation.]{.class_4725}

First of all, this woman was [bent.]{.class_4725} Every person who is born into []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_414}this world comes in bent and twisted spiritually. They all have a sinful, Adamic nature, and no amount of religion or church attendance can straighten it up.

Next, she was [bowed.]{.class_4725} Her eyes were downward on this sin-cursed earth. That's how animals are: animals are built so that they naturally look down. God made man so he could look up to [Heaven,]{.class_4725} to the [Lord]{.class_4725}. The problem is, sin weighs the sinner down to where he is only comfortable looking in one direction: [down.]{.class_4725}

In the original edition of [Pilgrim's Progress,]{.class_4725} there is an excellent illustration. It is a picture of a [muckraker,]{.class_4725} a man bent over a rake raking up muck and garbage. Behind him stands the Lord offering him a crown, but the muckraker won't look up. When I draw the picture in my sermon "The Four Essentials," I have a fellow digging through a garbage can for his next meal, and the Lord is standing behind him with a clean robe of righteousness, ready to take him to the Marriage Supper of the Lamb, if the guy will just get his head out of the trash. But that is the old, Adamic nature: it is ["of the earth, EARTHY"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 15:47). It takes the New Birth to change the sinner's outlook and get him to look up (John 3:5--7).

But not only is this woman bent and bowed, she is [bound]{.class_4725} (vs. 16). Satan has her tied hand and foot. That Bible says, ["His own iniquities shall take the wicked himself, and he shall be holden with the cords of his sins"]{.class_4723} (Prov. 5:22).

There are three things that if they ever get a hold of you, you will be bound good and tight. One is [liquor,]{.class_4725} one is [drugs,]{.class_4725} and one is [pornography.]{.class_4725} You'll have a hard time getting away from those things if they ever wrap their slimy tentacles around you. Some other good ones are [cigarettes, money, power,]{.class_4725} and [rock music.]{.class_4725}

Now, in type, the woman is [born again.]{.class_4725} The Lord straightens her up and gives her a new life free from her ailment. She's loosed from her bonds by Jesus Christ (vs. 16). Christian, if you've passed ["from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan unto God"]{.class_4723} (Acts 26:17), and you've become ["a new creature" "in Christ"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 5:17), then there ought to be a moral change in the way you live. I know a sinner is saved by grace through faith and not by works (Eph. 2:8--9), but when you get saved, you now have the Holy Spirit living in you to work in you toward good works (Eph. 2:10).

Wouldn't it have been strange if, after eighteen years of walking around bent over and then being miraculously healed and straightened up, that the woman went back to walking around bent over? But that's what Christians do when they yield their ["members as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 6:13).

When the New Birth came to this woman (in type), she was [blessed.]{.class_4725} She ["glorified God."]{.class_4723} That lame man who got healed in Acts 3:7--8 was ["walking, and leaping, and praising God."]{.class_4723} Now I know that people get saved out of all different circumstances and backgrounds. Not everyone got saved out of a profligate lifestyle like I did. But I'll tell you what, if you got saved out of the sewer I did, you would appreciate what a blessing salvation truly is.

And finally, this woman was [believed.]{.class_4725} Everybody could see that a change had taken place, and they ["rejoiced for all the glorious things that were done by him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 17). When blind Bartimaeus got his sight, the people around him saw what Jesus had done for him and ["gave praise unto God"]{.class_4723} (see 18:43). The purpose of a changed life as a Christian is not to brag or boast of your own self-righteousness; it's to point others to the saving grace of the Lord Jesus Christ in your life.

I remember one time down in Scotty Drake's church in Tampa, Florida, a man and his little girl got up to sing a special. Before that fellow had gotten saved, he had been a drunk and abusive to his family. Then the Lord saved him and took away the liquor, and he stood there as a good Christian daddy.

Well, the daddy sang a song about a saloon keeper who got saved, sort of like his own testimony. That guy would sing the verse about how bad the saloon keeper was before he got saved, and then his little girl would come in on the chorus in her little high-pitched voice: "Something happened to daddy\...." That whole church just went to pieces, boy. You know why? Because something happened to daddy, and everyone knew it and could see it. Everyone in that church was shouting and carrying on for what the Lord had done in the life of that little girl's daddy.

18 Then said he, Unto what is the kingdom of God like? []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_416}and whereunto shall I resemble it?

19 It is like a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and cast into his garden; and it grew, and waxed a great tree; and the fowls of the air lodged in the branches of it.

20 And again he said, Whereunto shall I liken the kingdom of God?

21 It is like leaven, which a woman took and hid in three measures of meal, till the whole was leavened.

Now here are two parables on ["the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (vss. 18, 20). You will find these same two parables in Matthew 13, except there they are parables on ["the kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 13:31, 33). That is the kind of thing that just screws up Christian scholarship. They read companion passages like these and say, "Well, Matthew says the Kingdom of Heaven, and Luke says the Kingdom of God, so they must be the same." They completely ignore the [definitive references]{.class_4725} on both kingdoms which I gave you in the comments on 6:20.

What is going on in Matthew 13 is that you have seven parables on ["the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven."]{.class_4723} Out of those seven parables, only [three]{.class_4725} match those of ["the mysteries of the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (Mark 4:11; Luke 8:10). They are the Parable of the Sower (which we covered in chap. 8) and the Parables of the Mustard Seed and Leaven (here). All that shows you is that there are [some similarities]{.class_4725} between the two kingdoms; similarities do not make them the same. Like I said back under 6:20, dogs and cats may have similar features, but they are not the same creatures.
