What you need to keep in mind in dealing with passages like this one is that there are things about the literal, physical kingdom in this age that affect the formation and progress of the spiritual kingdom. That's because the Devil has a church (represented by the woman in vs. 21) which seeks to be the head over [both kingdoms.]{.class_4725} It's a church (the spiritual Kingdom of God) which is a political State (the physical Kingdom of Heaven), and what that apostate organization does affects the body of true believers on this earth both spiritually and physically.

So first of all, the Kingdom of God ["is like a grain of mustard seed, which a man took and cast INTO HIS GARDEN"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_417} (vs. 19). Right away we see a difference between this parable on the Kingdom of God and the parable in Matthew on the Kingdom of Heaven. In Matthew, the mustard seed isn't cast in a ["garden"]{.class_4723}; it's sown in a ["field"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 13:31). Well, ["the field is the world"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 13:38). A garden is a smaller, enclosed, more tended plot of ground than a field, so this is something special. This is the Lord's bunch.

Now what is that ["mustard seed"]{.class_4723}? Well, in 17:6 here in Luke, it is associated with [faith]{.class_4727} (see also Matt. 17:20). But the "faith" in this parable is a perverted monstrosity. The mustard plant is an [herb]{.class_4727} (Matt. 13:32), not a tree. What you have here is [abnormal]{.class_4727} growth. This ["mustard seed"]{.class_4723} not only grows into a tree; it grows into ["a GREAT tree."]{.class_4723}

["And the fowls of the air lodged in the branches of it."]{.class_4723} There is no doubt what these ["fowls of the air"]{.class_4723} are. We ran into them back in 8:5; they are evil spirits (8:2) associated with the Devil (8:12). So this ["great tree"]{.class_4723} has nothing to do with the Gospel growing until it fills the whole earth and everyone comes under its shelter and protection. That is an Amillennial and Postmillennial pipedream. In Daniel 5 and Ezekiel 31, this tree is [the kingdom of the Antichrist.]{.class_4725} It is represented by a [woman]{.class_4725} that is a [city]{.class_4725} (Rev. 17:18) which is full of unclean spirits (Rev. 18:2).

That brings us to the second parable. The Kingdom of God ["is like leaven"]{.class_4723} (vs. 21)---[false doctrine, hypocrisy, sin]{.class_4725} (see our comments on 12:1). So this is [not]{.class_4725} the Gospel; it is [corruption.]{.class_4725} The spiritual kingdom is being [corrupted.]{.class_4725}

["It is like leaven, which a woman took\...."]{.class_4723} This woman is NOT the Church, the Body of Christ. The Body of Christ is never called ["a woman"]{.class_4723} in the New Testament. She is called ["a bride"]{.class_4723} in Revelation 21:2, ["a chaste virgin"]{.class_4723} in 2 Corinthians 11:2, and a[ wife]{.class_4725} in Ephesians 5:23--32; but never ["a woman."]{.class_4723} Now there is someone in the Bible who is called ["a woman"]{.class_4723} who is connected with sin and corruption. Look at Revelation 17.

["And there came one of the seven angels which had the seven vials, and talked with me, saying unto me, Come hither; I will shew unto thee the judgment of the great []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_418}whore that sitteth upon many waters\...So he carried me away in the spirit into the wilderness: and I saw A WOMAN sit upon a scarlet coloured beast, full of names of blasphemy, having seven heads and ten horns. And the WOMAN was arrayed in purple and scarlet colour, and decked with gold and precious stones and pearls, having a golden cup in her hand full of abominations and filthiness of her fornication"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 17:1--4).

That woman isn't ["a chaste virgin"]{.class_4723} like the Bride of Christ. She's a [whore]{.class_4725} who is spreading her filthy trade, not the Gospel. Jesus said she took that leaven and hid it ["in three measures of meal, till the whole was leavened."]{.class_4723} The ["meal,"]{.class_4723} of course, is the ground-up grain from which the dough (and eventually the loaf of bread) is made. The Church isn't commanded to [add]{.class_4725} leaven; it's commanded to [purge it out.]{.class_4725}

["Purge out therefore the old leaven, that ye may be a new lump, as ye are UNLEAVENED. For even Christ our passover is sacrificed for us: Therefore let us keep the feast, not with old leaven, neither with the leaven of malice and wickedness; but with the UNLEAVENED BREAD of sincerity and truth"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 5:7--8).

So this woman isn't doing the Church any favors. She's corrupting all three branches of professing Christianity (Roman Catholic, Greek Orthodox, and Protestant). Brethren, the future of the Body of Christ on this earth before the Rapture is [not]{.class_4725} revival or reformation or reconstructionism or dominionism; it's complete and utter [apostasy]{.class_4725} (Rev. 3:15--16; 2 Tim. 3:1--7; 1 Tim. 4:1--3). That's where you are [today.]{.class_4725} Everything is being set up right now for the Kingdom of Death and Hell to show up. The only hope you have, Christian, is for your Saviour to come back and get you out of here (Tit. 2:13).

22 And he went through the cities and villages, teaching, and journeying toward Jerusalem.

23 Then said one unto him, Lord, are there few that be saved? And he said unto them,

24 Strive to enter in at the strait gate: for many, I say []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_419}unto you, will seek to enter in, and shall not be able.

25 When once the master of the house is risen up, and hath shut to the door, and ye begin to stand without, and to knock at the door, saying, Lord, Lord, open unto us; and he shall answer and say unto you, I know you not whence ye are:

26 Then shall ye begin to say, We have eaten and drunk in thy presence, and thou hast taught in our streets.

27 But he shall say, I tell you, I know you not whence ye are; depart from me, all ye workers of iniquity.

28 There shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth, when ye shall see Abraham, and Isaac, and Jacob, and all the prophets, in the kingdom of God, and you yourselves thrust out.

29 And they shall come from the east, and from the west, and from the north, and from the south, and shall sit down in the kingdom of God.

30 And, behold, there are last which shall be first, and there are first which shall be last.

Christ has been slowly headed toward Jerusalem ever since 9:51 (see our comments on the verse). The reason the going has been so slow is because, along the way, He's teaching and preaching in all ["the cities and villages"]{.class_4723} (vs. 22). He spends a day at one place and moves on, a couple of days at another place, a week at another, and so on.

At one of these stops, someone asked Him, ["Lord, are there few that be saved?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 23). You'll remember that, back in 12:32, He called His followers a ["little flock,"]{.class_4723} implying that the whole nation wasn't going to be saved at His First Advent like it will at His Second Advent (see Zech. 12:8--10; Rom. 11:26). Christ's answer reveals not only the stubborn, self-righteous nature of that Jew (Rom. 10:1--3), but of all men (Prov. 14:12, 16:25).

["Strive to enter in at the strait gate"]{.class_4723} (vs. 24). The ["gate"]{.class_4723} is ["strait"]{.class_4723} (i.e., narrow) in that it only allows entry at one particular point: the Lord Jesus Christ.

["Jesus saith unto him, I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_420} (John 14:6).

["Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that entereth not by the door into the sheepfold, but climbeth up some other way, the same is a thief and a robber\....I am the door: by me if any man enter in, he shall be saved, and shall go in and out, and find pasture"]{.class_4723} (John 10:1, 9).

Back to verse 24:[ "for many\...will seek to enter in, and shall not be able."]{.class_4723} Why? Because they're going the [wrong way.]{.class_4725}

Matthew 7:13---["Enter ye in at the strait gate: for wide is the gate, and broad is the way, that leadeth to destruction, and many there be which go in thereat."]{.class_4723}

Christ is telling the inquirer, "Yes, few will be saved." Saved people have never been a majority anywhere at any time since Jesus died on the cross. Of course, "[MANY\...will seek to enter in"]{.class_4723}; boy, will they seek to make it. You can tell people [anything]{.class_4725} to do to get saved in their own efforts to make it to Heaven, and they'll try it. If I told people to shinny up a pine tree naked upside down to be saved, I would get a following. Yeah man! and I could take up a collection from them and make a killing on it too.

Now do you know why that is so? Because man wants [his own efforts]{.class_4725} to be part of his salvation. That's why Cain set up his fruit stand: it was [his]{.class_4725} work he was presenting to God. The ego of man insists that if he does the best he can do, God should accept it and let him into Heaven.

People, the best a man can do will wind him up in [Hell]{.class_4725} (Isa. 64:6; Tit. 3:5). The reason a man goes to Hell is not because of murder, adultery, idolatry, etc. It's because he takes [his own way]{.class_4725} instead of [God's.]{.class_4725}

["All we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned every one to his own way; and the LORD hath laid on him the iniquity of us all"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 53:6).

The thing that damns a man is doing things his own way. When it comes right down to it, the difference between [Biblical ]{.class_4725}Christianity (get that! I didn't say Catholic, Protestant, or Orthodox. I said BIBLICAL Christianity) and [any]{.class_4725} other religion in the world---I don't care which one it is; take your pick. One will damn you just as []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_421}quickly as the others---is this: every other religion in the world (that would be Hinduism, Buddhism, animism, Islam, Catholicism, Judaism, Zoroastrianism, Mormonism, etc.) says [do]{.class_4725} this and [don't]{.class_4725} do that and you[ might]{.class_4725} make it. In Biblical Christianity, you "don't do nuthin'." In Biblical Christianity, you're shot; you're ruined; and no matter what you do, it won't amount to a hill of beans. In Biblical Christianity, you have to [accept what Someone else did for you.]{.class_4725}

"Could my tears forever flow, Could my zeal no languor know, These for sin COULD NOT ATONE---THOU must save, and THOU ALONE." That's the business.

Verse 25:[ "When once the master of the house is risen up, and hath shut to the door\...." "Shut to the door"]{.class_4723} is an archaic English expression, but you can still hear it down South. That's because after the Civil War, all the Southerners had was God and the Bible. The Yankees went home to their industry, their factories, and their money. The Southerners lost the war, and when they got home, all they had was burnt up crops and burnt out buildings. They had to rely on God to survive, so as a result, they got into the Book and picked up the language of that [King James]{.class_4725} Bible (like ["yonder"]{.class_4723} and ["reckon"]{.class_4723} and ["you all"]{.class_4723}).

Of course, now that the churches have dumped the [AV]{.class_4725} for the [RSV, NRSV, NIV, NKJV,]{.class_4725} etc., those Biblical expressions are passing out of the language; and they're not being replaced with anything from the new versions. That's because the language of the modern versions isn't easy to retain; it's wooden and hollow. The "poetic cadence" of the [KJV]{.class_4725} has always made it easier to memorize.

Another reason is because Christians aren't reading the modern junk. A survey was taken during the 400th anniversary of the [King James]{.class_4725} Bible (2011) which showed that 85% of people who read their Bibles on a daily basis, read from the [King James Version,]{.class_4725} and the other 15% read from all the other junk. That means a lot of those Christians who get the newer versions because "the [King James]{.class_4725} is so out of date and hard to understand" [aren't reading]{.class_4725} their "clearer," "more accurate" translations.

["And ye begin to stand without, and to knock at the door, saying, Lord, Lord, open unto us; and he shall answer and say unto you, I know you not whence ye are."]{.class_4723} Why, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_422}that's what happened to those five foolish virgins in Matthew 25:10--12. So you are not dealing with a Church Age setting here (see our comments on 12:35--36). This is the post-Tribulation rapture of Jewish saints.

The Holiness crowd uses this passage and the Parable of the Ten Virgins in Matthew 25:1--13 to teach a split Rapture. That is, if you are not watching for the Lord to come back (Heb. 9:28) and living right when He does, you get left behind to go through the Tribulation. A few of them will even teach that if you get left behind that it will be impossible for you to repent and be saved (Heb. 6:4--6). Of course, no passage to which they run to teach that doctrine is aimed at any born-again child of God in the Church Age. Hebrews was written to [Hebrews,]{.class_4725} not any church or Christian.

Listen, if you have been born again by faith in the Lord Jesus Christ, you will never hear your Saviour say, ["I know you not whence ye are,"]{.class_4723} or ["I never knew you"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 7:23). The Apostle Paul told those Galatians,[ "But now, after that ye have known God, or rather ARE KNOWN OF GOD, how turn ye again to the weak and beggarly elements, whereunto ye desire again to be in bondage?"]{.class_4723} (Gal. 4:9).

If you have been saved, the Lord knows you as a son, and He can't say that He ["NEVER knew you."]{.class_4723} He doesn't forget you no matter how badly you "screw up" as a Christian. The promise is that He ["will never leave thee, nor forsake thee"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 13:5). The promise is that you ["are sealed unto the day of redemption"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 4:30). The promise is that nothing can separate you ["from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:38--39). So the passage here in Luke 13 has no doctrinal application to the child of God in the Body of Christ. Look at the next verse.

["Then shall ye begin to say, We have eaten and drunk in thy presence"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26). Any of you reading this page done that lately? You've invited Jesus Christ over for supper sometime, have you? Obviously He's talking about those Pharisees in 7:36 and 11:37, and some of those curiosity seekers in the crowds when He fed the 5,000 and then the 4,000. Those were Jews who were in Christ's physical presence and could honestly say they had eaten with Him.

["Thou hast taught in our streets."]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_423} Then He's not talking to you. Nobody in the last 1,900 years has heard Jesus Christ down on the corner street-preaching. He's talking to the Jews asking Him the question right then. He's talking to Jews standing right in front of Him, some of whom professed to be His disciples and weren't (John 6:60--66). They rejected Him during His earthly ministry, and He's telling them what's going to happen if they don't give up their own way and come through[ "the strait gate."]{.class_4723}

Now certainly you can make spiritual application to the companion passage in Matthew 7:22--23. There you have a bunch of Charismatic fakirs trying to get into Heaven, but again, it's a matter of profession without possession. None of them were trusting Jesus Christ for salvation; every one of them was depending on his [works]{.class_4725} to get him by the judgment. Is that you? Not if you've received Christ, no.

He's talking to [Jews]{.class_4725} (vs. 28), not any Gentile in the Body of Christ (vs. 29). The companion passage in Matthew 8:11--12 is talking about those Jews not making it into the physical Kingdom of Heaven (Matt. 25:11--12, 30) when there are Gentiles who do (Matt. 25:31--40). Those are Jews and Gentiles who enter into the Millennial kingdom based on a system of [faith and works]{.class_4725} (Rev. 12:17, 14:12). Not so here.

Luke 13:28--29 deals with a spiritual kingdom of the inward man (Luke 17:21) that is entered in at the New Birth (John 3:5). In that kingdom, ["the middle wall of partition"]{.class_4723} separating Jews from Gentiles spiritually has been ["broken down"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 2:14), and any Gentile who comes to Christ is no longer a stranger and a foreigner to ["the commonwealth of Israel"]{.class_4723} and ["the covenants of promise" ]{.class_4723}(Eph. 2:12), but a citizen of Heaven and a member of God's family (Eph. 2:19).

When Jesus Christ comes back, all those born-again Gentiles from the Church Age come back with Him (Rev. 19:14; 2 Thess. 1:7), and they get to ["see the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (John 3:3) because they're right there with the King in glorified bodies just like His (1 John 3:1--2; Phil. 3:21; Rom. 8:29). Hence the expression ["sit down in the kingdom of God."]{.class_4723} When the King shows back up, He comes to set up the literal, visible, physical Kingdom of Heaven; and He []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_424}brings the spiritual Kingdom of God with Him that was built up during the Church Age.

Don't you know there'll be some screaming and crying from those Christ-rejecting Jews then. Right before they are cast into the Lake of Fire, they will look behind the Messiah they rejected and see a bunch of Gentiles from Russia and Germany and England and the United States and South America and Africa (see 1 Cor. 6:2) who [made it]{.class_4725} because they accepted the Saviour that God's chosen people turned down. You talk about a bitter pill to swallow, that will be it.

The application of verse 30 is different than its companion passages in Matthew 19:30, 20:16. In Matthew, the application is to [service;]{.class_4725} here in Luke, it is to [salvation.]{.class_4725} The ["last which shall be first"]{.class_4723} are obviously the Gentiles who make it into the Kingdom of God in verse 30, and ["the first which shall be last"]{.class_4723} are the Jews who don't make it in verse 29.

Paul told those Christ-rejecting Jews in Antioch of Pisidia, ["It was necessary that the word of God should first have been spoken to you: but seeing ye put it from you, and judge yourselves unworthy of everlasting life, lo, we turn to the Gentiles"]{.class_4723} (Acts 13:46).

31 The same day there came certain of the Pharisees, saying unto him, Get thee out, and depart hence: for Herod will kill thee.

32 And he said unto them, Go ye, and tell that fox, Behold, I cast out devils, and I do cures to day and to morrow, and the third day I shall be perfected.

33 Nevertheless I must walk to day, and to morrow, and the day following: for it cannot be that a prophet perish out of Jerusalem.

34 O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, which killest the prophets, and stonest them that are sent unto thee; how often would I have gathered thy children together, as a hen doth gather her brood under her wings, and ye would not!

35 Behold, your house is left unto you desolate: and verily I say unto you, Ye shall not see me, until the time come when ye shall say, Blessed is he that cometh in the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_425}name of the Lord.

Now Jesus is headed toward Jerusalem; and what Luke gives you in chapters 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, and 19 are different incidents that take place during that journey. Here in this passage, He is obviously close enough to Jerusalem that the Pharisees are trying to frighten Him from going into the city at His Triumphal Entry. Later on, in chapter 17, Luke will tell you about something that took place back up around the border of Samaria and Galilee during that same journey. So all the accounts are not given in chronological order.

You know what takes place is just before the Triumphal Entry because the Pharisees warn Him that Herod is in Jerusalem and will try to kill Him like he killed John the Baptist. Well, Herod was King of Galilee, not Judea (3:1), but he was in Jerusalem for the Passover (23:6--7).

But Christ wasn't intimidated so easily. He tells those Pharisees, ["Go ye, and tell that FOX"]{.class_4723} (vs. 32). You talk about rude, boy; you talk about name-calling! Why, where is the nice, sweet, kind, gentle Jesus who never had "a particle of bitterness or unkindness" in His words (see our remarks on Stewart Custer under 3:8)? ["Go\...tell THAT FOX"]{.class_4723}---what do you call [that?]{.class_4725}

["Behold, I cast out devils, and I do cures TO DAY and TO MORROW, and the THIRD DAY I shall be perfected"]{.class_4723} (vs. 32). He's talking about the last three days of His earthly ministry: Sunday, Monday, and Tuesday of Passover week. The last day of His public ministry was Tuesday. He was arrested Tuesday night in the garden and crucified the next day on Wednesday.

Look at it again in verse 33: ["I must walk TO DAY ]{.class_4723}\[Sunday\][, and TO MORROW]{.class_4723} \[Monday\][, and THE DAY FOLLOWING]{.class_4723} \[Tuesday\][: for it cannot be that a prophet perish out of Jerusalem ]{.class_4723}\[the Crucifixion on Wednesday\][."]{.class_4723}

["For it cannot be that a prophet perish OUT OF JERUSALEM."]{.class_4723} Well, technically, He does ["perish"]{.class_4723} outside of the city: Calvary is ["without the camp"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 13:13). But He was condemned to death [inside]{.class_4725} the city, and the city is responsible for His death.

Notice again the blood guilt attributed to the Jews who crucified []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_426}Christ. Lots of prophets perished outside of Jerusalem (e.g., Abel, Micaiah, all the prophets Jezebel slew), but Jesus had told them ["that the blood of all the prophets, which was shed from the foundation of the world"]{.class_4723} would be required of the generation that crucified Him (see 11:50 and our comments on that verse).

And then a statement that just blows John Calvin's system to smithereens: ["how often would I have gathered thy children together, as a hen doth gather her brood under her wings, AND YE WOULD NOT"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34). The "I" in Calvin's TULIP stands for "Irresistible Grace." Since no one can receive Christ of his own free will under Calvin's system because of "Total Depravity" (even the will of man is dead in sin), when God "Unconditionally Elects" a man to salvation, the Holy Spirit has to draw the sinner by "Irresistible Grace" to get him saved. Calvin taught that the grace of God that saves a man is "irresistible": [it can't be resisted.]{.class_4725} The Holy Spirit draws a man against his will, and there's nothing the sinner can do about it.

After that you can write B-A-L-O-N-E-Y. You never got a bigger slice of baloney from the deep freeze than that. Christ said He would have saved that nation a thousand times if they would have let Him (["how often would I ]{.class_4723}\
[\...and ye would not"]{.class_4723}).

No sinner was ever put under any "decree of reprobation." The Scripture says, ["The Lord is\...not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 3:9). It says, ["Who will have all men to be saved, and to come unto the knowledge of the truth"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 2:4). If God had His way, no man, woman, boy, or girl would go to Hell. You say, "Then why do they go?" Because they ["would not"]{.class_4723}; they won't let the Lord save them because they reject His Son. If you go to Hell, you go against the [directive will of God ]{.class_4725}for your life.

"Irresistible Grace"---what nonsense! Stephen told those priests and elders of the Sanhedrin, ["Ye do ALWAYS RESIST the Holy Ghost"]{.class_4723} (Acts 7:51). God the Father wanted to save them, God the Son wanted to save them, God the Holy Spirit wanted to save them, and they would have none of it: ["AND YE WOULD NOT."]{.class_4723}

Listen, if you go to Hell, don't you blame God. He did everything He could to keep you out. He sent His Son to die for you; He gave you a Book to tell you about it; He sent the Holy Spirit to convict you of your sins; He sent witnesses to give you the Gospel. Don't you sit back on your dead can and expect to be overcome by "Irresistible Grace." Do what you know to be right and RECEIVE CHRIST (John 1:12). If you miss Heaven, you have no one to blame but YOURSELF.

Verse 35: ["Behold, your house is left unto you desolate."]{.class_4723} About what ["house"]{.class_4723} is He talking? He's talking about the ["house"]{.class_4723} where they claimed to be worshipping God: He's talking about the Temple. The Temple was supposed to be ["the house of God"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 7:5, 12, 16). He called it ["my Father's house"]{.class_4723} (John 2:16). He called it ["my house"]{.class_4723} (19:46)---a claim to His own Deity. But when He finally leaves it for the last time in Matthew 23:39, He calls it ["your house."]{.class_4723} The Lord has deserted it because those Jews rejected His Son; Deity incarnate walks out and doesn't come back until He returns at the Second Advent (see Hag. 2:9; Mal. 3:1 and our comments on those verses in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Minor Prophets,]{.class_4725} Vol. II).

["The house of God"]{.class_4723} is only the house of God [as long as God is in it.]{.class_4725} When He leaves, it's not His house anymore. That should be an important lesson to American Christians. I often think about that when I hear a preacher say, "It's good to be in the Lord's house today." It might not be "the Lord's house" at all; it might be just another building. You reckon some church where the pulpit has been removed, the rock band is playing, the "worship team" is doing the bumps and the grinds, and the pastor is "rapping" with his [RSV, ESV, NIV]{.class_4725} (or whatever) hanging out is "the Lord's house"? Are you mad?!

Listen, people, a church building is only "the house of God" when a bunch of temples of the Holy Ghost (see 1 Cor. 6:19) are gathered in it to worship God in spirit and truth (John 4:24). The rest of the time it's just a building. When a church building houses an unregenerate membership, fleshy worship, and a corrupted message, it's not the house of God at all. It may be a "chapel" or a "campus" or a "worship center," but the house of God it is not.

You say, "It was dedicated to God." So was Bethel (Gen. 28:16--[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2702.xhtml_page_428}22); so was Shiloh (Jer. 7:12); so was the Temple (1 Kings 8:63). Look at what God did to those places when His people stopped serving Him. The word [Bethel]{.class_4727} means "the house of God," and it ended up being the chapel of an idolatrous King where the preaching of the word of God wasn't even welcomed anymore (Amos 7:13). God destroyed that place and took the "pastor's wife" and turned her into a street walker (Amos 7:17). God took the Temple where He said He would put His name forever (1 Kings 9:3), and He had Nebuchadnezzar burn it to the ground. He does it again in A.D. 70 under Titus, after telling those Pharisees in Luke 13: ["your house is left unto you desolate."]{.class_4723}

["Ye shall not see me\...."]{.class_4723} Well, they do; He is brought before the chief priests and the council at His trial, and they see Him there. They see Him on the cross and mock Him. Obviously He is talking about seeing Him in a certain way. They won't see Him as the Messiah offering them the kingdom: that's what He's telling them.

["Ye shall not see me, until the time come when ye shall say, Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord."]{.class_4723} That's what they say at His Triumphal Entry (Matt. 21:9), and three days later they are yelling, "Crucify Him!" But the next time they say that, they won't be calling for His execution; they'll be in mourning over the realization that the One who comes to rescue them from the Antichrist is the One they had crucified 2,000 years earlier (Zech. 12:10).

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml}

# Chapter 14 {.heading_78-0}

14:1 And it came to pass, as he went into the house of one of the chief Pharisees to eat bread on the sabbath day, that they watched him.

2 And, behold, there was a certain man before him which had the dropsy.

3 And Jesus answering spake unto the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath day?

4 And they held their peace. And he took him, and healed him, and let him go;

5 And answered them, saying, Which of you shall have an ass or an ox fallen into a pit, and will not straightway pull him out on the sabbath day?

6 And they could not answer him again to these things.

This is the third time so far in the book of Luke where a Pharisee has invited Jesus to dinner (see 7:36, 11:37), and this one fares just as poorly as the last two. This is also the third recorded time Jesus heals on the Sabbath (see 6:7--11, 13:10--17). "Three strikes and you're out." Since we have commented extensively on the material in verse 1 under the above passages (see also our remarks under 11:54), we refer you to them.

["Dropsy"]{.class_4723} (vs. 2) is not a condition where you are continually dropping something. It is "the swelling of soft tissue due to accumulation of excess water." A person with dropsy will have his legs and feet swell up really big if he's on his feet any length of time, because all the fluid in the body drains down with nothing to stop it.

That happened to Harlan Popov one time. Popov was a pastor over in Bulgaria during the years when the Communists were in charge. The authorities hauled him in one time to interrogate him and made him stand upright on his feet for several days straight; he wasn't allowed to sit down or to lie down to go to sleep. By the time that thing was over, Popov's feet were swollen to the size of elephant's feet.

Notice the reaction of the Pharisees in verse 4 to Christ's question ["Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath day?"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_430} (vs. 3). They don't say "yes" or "no"; they plead the Fifth Amendment and keep their mouths shut. They do something similar in 20:4--5 when Jesus asks them about John's baptism (which see).

That's obfuscation that avoids giving a direct answer to a direct question. You see it all the time in personal work. You ask a person, "Are you saved?" The answer comes back: "I was baptized," "I belong to such-and-such a church," "My grandfather was a Presbyterian minister," "I'm a Catholic." That's not what I asked. I asked, "ARE YOU SAVED?"

I asked a fellow one time if he was saved, and he showed me his [ring.]{.class_4725} He didn't say a word; he just stuck his ring in my face. I didn't know what it was at the time, but it was a Masonic ring. The idea was: "Look, I'm a Mason; see my ring. If I'm a Mason, I must be saved." Most Masons aren't trusting Jesus Christ at all for their salvation; they're trusting their own good works: their [philanthropy.]{.class_4725}

I was out witnessing in Greenville, South Carolina one time and came across a group of young men. I asked, "You fellows saved?" Without saying a word, they all pointed to the beanies they were wearing that bore the letter "F." The "F" stood for Furman University, a big Southern Baptist school in town. The idea was: "We go to Furman; see the 'F' on our beanies. If we go to a Southern Baptist University, we must be saved." The "F" might not mean those fellows were saved at all; it might just have meant they were all [fools.]{.class_4725}

Getting back to our text, undoubtedly the reason those Pharisees ["held their peace"]{.class_4723} was so they wouldn't be ashamed before the people when Jesus healed the man, like Christ's enemies were back in 13:17. Jesus makes the same point here that he made in 13:15---those self-righteous Pharisees cared more for their beasts than they did for God's people. Of course, the Pharisees had no answer in the matter, because Jesus was right and they knew it.

7 And he put forth a parable to those which were bidden, when he marked how they chose out the chief rooms; saying unto them,

8 When thou art bidden of any man to a wedding, sit not down in the highest room; lest a more honourable man []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_431}than thou be bidden of him;

9 And he that bade thee and him come and say to thee, Give this man place; and thou begin with shame to take the lowest room.

10 But when thou art bidden, go and sit down in the lowest room; that when he that bade thee cometh, he may say unto thee, Friend, go up higher: then shalt thou have worship in the presence of them that sit at meat with thee.

11 For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted.

Now Jesus was at a dinner party being held by one of the high muckety-mucks among the Pharisees (vs. 1), and at these affairs, the guests were seated according to rank and status in different rooms on different levels. The more important you were, the higher the tier you occupied at the party. I don't know if they do anything similar in these Hollywood parties or at these political banquets, but I do know they have "VIP tables" where the big shots sit.

The Pharisees were so full of their own self-importance that they would go straight to ["the highest room"]{.class_4723} (vs. 8). In His tirade against the Pharisees in Matthew 23, Jesus said they loved ["the uppermost rooms at feasts, and the chief seats in the synagogues"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 23:6). It's kind of like Haman going before Ahasuerus and thinking to himself, ["To whom would the king delight to do honour more than to myself"]{.class_4723} (Esth. 6:6). He was setting himself up for a big disappointment (Esth. 6:10--12).

Jesus tells those egotistical stuff-shirts that when they get invited to a party, instead of strutting up to the top room and getting booted down if someone more important comes in, just automatically take the lowest room. That way they would have honor before the other guests when they saw the host come by and say, "Hey, what are you doing all the way down here? Why don't you come up and eat with me?" The ["worship"]{.class_4723} in verse 10 is not reverence given to Deity; it's honor such as would be shown to a King or dignitary (1 Chron. 29:20).

The point of the parable is in verse 11: ["For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted."]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_432} Jesus applies it to salvation in the Parable of the Pharisee and the Publican (18:14). Peter applies it to the New Testament ministry in 1 Peter 5:6. In the Kingdom of God, the way up is down. If you want God's blessings, you had better humble yourself and not wait for Him to humble you.

Now, all of this can be applied to the Christian and the Judgment Seat of Christ. Look at the ["wedding"]{.class_4723} in verse 8. At the ["marriage of the Lamb"]{.class_4723} in Revelation 19:7, the Bible says, ["his wife hath made herself ready."]{.class_4723} Paul said that the Lord wants to present His Bride to Himself ["not having spot, or wrinkle, or any such thing; but that it should be holy and without blemish"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 5:27).

The Judgment Seat of Christ is going to be a purification process to get you ready for the Marriage of the Lamb. The bad works are burned up, and the good works remain, and you receive a reward for them (1 Cor. 3:12--15). That Bride is ["arrayed in fine linen, clean and white,"]{.class_4723} and that ["fine linen"]{.class_4723} is ["the righteousness of saints"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 19:8): it's what you did for the Lord while you were down here on earth.

If you apply the parable to the Judgment Seat of Christ, let me give you some sound advice. When you come before the Lord at the Judgment Seat of Christ, [get down]{.class_4725} and [stay down]{.class_4725} and [don't look up]{.class_4725} until He tells you to. There won't be any "strutting" at the Judgment Seat of Christ. There won't be any of this: "I had a fleet of 100 buses and ran 10,000 in Sunday School with 150 saved every week." Christian, anything you accomplish in the ministry and any successes you have in life or in your family are strictly the [grace of God.]{.class_4725} You won't be boasting about such things at the Judgment Seat of Christ.

Do you know what you're going to see at the Judgment Seat of Christ? You are going to see some of these Christian "celebrities" taking the back seat to some dear saints of God who were laid up in old folks homes and all they could do was pray; their prayers accomplished more spiritually than any pastor could with his promotional programs and motivational techniques.

How do you think some of the big boys are going to stack up against a missionary like Nate Saint? Here's these "mega-church" []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_433}fellows saying, "Well, we ran 12,000 in Sunday school last year by offering folks a two-block-long hot dog or a three-acre hamburger (or whatever it was) and got eighty souls saved every week and fifty baptized." The Lord turns to Nate Saint and asks, "And what did you do?" Saint says, "Nothing. I went down to Ecuador to reach the Auca Indians, and they killed me before I had a chance to do anything."

"Well," says the Lord, ["if there be first a willing mind, it is accepted according to that a man hath, and not according to that he hath not"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 8:12). Nate Saint was willing to die for Christ. How do you think he's going to fare against some of the Christian "big shots" bragging about their buses and attendance when they're [afraid to preach on the street?]{.class_4727}

12 Then said he also to him that bade him, When thou makest a dinner or a supper, call not thy friends, nor thy brethren, neither thy kinsmen, nor thy rich neighbours; lest they also bid thee again, and a recompence be made thee.

13 But when thou makest a feast, call the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind:

14 And thou shalt be blessed; for they cannot recompense thee: for thou shalt be recompensed at the resurrection of the just.

Notice the distinction made in verse 12 between ["a dinner"]{.class_4723} and ["a supper."]{.class_4723} Up North and out West, the three meals are breakfast, lunch, and dinner; but down South, they're breakfast, dinner, and supper. The big meal is the noon meal: dinner. Supper is the lighter meal in the evening.

In the context of the ["wedding"]{.class_4723} in verse 8, the implication is once more to two parts of the Marriage Supper of the Lamb (see our remarks under 12:36--37): a big ["dinner"]{.class_4723} up in Heaven and a smaller ["supper"]{.class_4723} down on earth. Now if you think that's stretching things a bit too far, look at what Christ says in verse 14: ["thou shalt be recompensed at the resurrection of the just."]{.class_4723} That's ["the first resurrection"]{.class_4723} in Revelation 20:4--5 at the beginning of the Millennium. It involves Millennial rewards. The next passage here in Luke 14 is about ["a great supper"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_434} in the Kingdom of God (vss. 15--16). So Christ is talking about the Marriage Supper of the Lamb.

Of course, the practical application is to show kindness to the needy and those who can't pay you back. That's New Testament. Paul wrote to the Corinthians, ["As it is written, He hath dispersed abroad; he hath given to the poor: his righteousness remaineth for ever"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 9:9). The thing to remember about that, though, is that that ["righteousness"]{.class_4723} is performed by a believer who has already had the righteousness of God imputed to him by faith in the Lord Jesus Christ. It is not a good work done to gain salvation.

15 And when one of them that sat at meat with him heard these things, he said unto him, Blessed is he that shall eat bread in the kingdom of God.

16 Then said he unto him, A certain man made a great supper, and bade many:

17 And sent his servant at supper time to say to them that were bidden, Come; for all things are now ready.

18 And they all with one consent began to make excuse. The first said unto him, I have bought a piece of ground, and I must needs go and see it: I pray thee have me excused.

19 And another said, I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to prove them: I pray thee have me excused.

20 And another said, I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come.

21 So that servant came, and shewed his lord these things. Then the master of the house being angry said to his servant, Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in hither the poor, and the maimed, and the halt, and the blind.

22 And the servant said, Lord, it is done as thou hast commanded, and yet there is room.

23 And the lord said unto the servant, Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in, that my house may be filled.

24 For I say unto you, That none of those men which were bidden shall taste of my supper.

As we have told you before, even though ["the kingdom of God is not meat and drink; but righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 14:17), when the King comes back, He brings both kingdoms with Him. Hence the comment by the dinner guest in verse 15: ["Blessed is he that shall eat bread in the kingdom of God."]{.class_4723} The spiritual kingdom is right there in the Millennium with the physical kingdom; that's how you can ["eat bread"]{.class_4723} in a kingdom that has nothing to do with what you put in your mouth.

Now the parable Jesus gives in verses 16--24 is given as a wedding feast in Matthew 22:1--14, and we have already noted the reference to ["a wedding"]{.class_4723} in verse 8. But notice the conspicuous [absence]{.class_4725} of the words [wedding]{.class_4725} and [marriage]{.class_4725} in the passage. While there is no doubt as to the prophetic application to the Marriage Supper of the Lamb, Jesus has purposefully left the language open so as to make application to the [Gospel invitation in this age.]{.class_4725} This is the interpretation given by Isaiah.

["Ho, every one that thirsteth, come ye to the waters, and he that hath no money; come ye, buy, and eat; yea, come, buy wine and milk without money and without price. Wherefore do ye spend money for that which is not bread? and your labour for that which satisfieth not? hearken diligently unto me, and eat ye that which is good, and let your soul delight itself in fatness. Incline your ear, and come unto me: hear, and your soul shall live; and I will make an everlasting covenant with you, even the sure mercies of David"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 55:1--3).

That's a reference to New Testament salvation as the free gift of God (Rom. 5:18). ["The sure mercies of David"]{.class_4723} are applied by the Apostle Paul to justification by faith in Christ apart from the Law (Acts 13:34--39).

That's the difference between the parable here in Luke 14 and the one in Matthew 22. In Matthew 22, the Bride and Bridegroom are already married, and guests are invited to the wedding feast. Here, guests are being invited to ["a great supper"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_436} (vs. 17) with no mention of any Bride, Bridegroom, or wedding. Of course, we understand that if you as an individual want to make it to the Marriage Supper of the Lamb someday, you have to accept the Gospel invitation extended to you now. So in that sense, the parable could be applied generally to the Marriage Supper of the Lamb.

["And they all with one consent began to make excuse"]{.class_4723} (vs. 18). If you have done any amount of personal work at all, you know the truth of that statement. "I just don't have the right feeling." "Do you believe in the baptism of the Holy Ghost?" "I was slain in the Spirit." "I can't live it." "I'm not one of the elect." "What about the heathen who've never heard?" That's what you get into if you are actively engaged in trying to win sinners to Jesus Christ.

The Lord invites men to ["a great supper"]{.class_4723} absolutely free, that won't cost them a dime. The just men of all ages will be there. They get to sit down and ["eat bread in the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. D. L. Moody will be there; so will be Billy Sunday, Sam Jones, Charles Finney, John Wesley, Martin Luther, Moses, David, Paul, Peter, James, John, the cherubim, the seraphim, ["an innumerable company of angels"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 12:22), and the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Isn't it strange that with all of that waiting for them, men will say, "I think if a fellow does the best he can, he'll be all right"; "It depends on how you look at it; some folks look at it one way and some look at it another"; "I don't think anyone can know for sure"; etc.? Just one excuse after another for turning down the Lord's invitation.

Now if you will examine the excuses given in the passage before us, you will see that each of them is as threadbare as the ones above. Take that first one, for instance: ["I have bought a piece of ground, and I must needs go and see it: I pray thee have me excused"]{.class_4723} (vs. 18). What?! You mean you bought some land sight unseen? The old flim-flam artists used to sell "waterfront property" in Florida to the Yankees, and when those Yankees came down here to inspect their property, they found out it was [swampland]{.class_4725} about four feet [under water.]{.class_4725}

Here's the second excuse: ["I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to prove them: I pray thee have me excused"]{.class_4723} (vs. []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_437}19). Why would you wait until after you bought the oxen to prove them? Would you buy a used car without test driving it first? A fellow would be a fool to do that.

You see, these excuses make no sense. No man in his right mind would buy land without looking it over first. No one would buy beasts with which to plow without making sure they were up to the job. But these excuses make just as much sense as a sinner turning down God's free gift of eternal life.

Third excuse: ["I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come"]{.class_4723} (vs. 20). We're not talking about going off to war here or being ["charged with any business"]{.class_4723} (Deut. 24:5). We're talking about a free banquet where she doesn't have to cook or clean up afterwards. Don't you reckon that would "cheer her up" (Deut. 24:5)? What woman wouldn't like for you to take her out to a nice restaurant, especially if you didn't have to pay for the meal?

Do you see how lame these excuses are? They're just as lame as the excuses for turning down Christ.

"I can't live it." Nobody asked you to "live it." In fact, you can't live it; that's why Christ had to die.

"I don't think anybody can know." Well, how do you know you can't if you don't know what God told you to do? God says you can know (1 John 5:13). Are you calling Him a liar?

"I've got my religion and you've got yours." So what? The Devil has his religion too; what of it?

A fellow told me one time, "I'm satisfied with my life." "Is [God]{.class_4725} satisfied with it?" I asked.

"I don't have the right feeling." He didn't tell you you had to have a feeling. Why are you worried about your feelings when feelings aren't even involved? Excuses, excuses!

I was dealing with a Campbellite one time (Church of Christ) who had come to me with his wife for "counseling." It became apparent to me after a few minutes of talking with him that he was as lost as a goose in a horse race, so I switched gears and began to deal with him about salvation. That "water dog" brought up every excuse in the book: "Where did Cain get his wife?"; "How did Noah get all those animals aboard the ark?"; "Do you believe in healing?"; "Why don't Baptists speak in tongues?"; and on and on. After ninety minutes of []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_438}me answering all of his questions with Scripture, he sat there quietly a minute just staring at me; then he began to laugh. He said, "Preacher, I'm just stallin', ain't I?" I said, "That's exactly what you've been doing." And he dropped to his knees and received Christ as his Saviour. Would to God that more sinners would come to the realization that that Campbellite did.

Back to Luke 14: ["So the servant came, and shewed his lord these things"]{.class_4723} (vs. 21). The servant told his master the excuses given by the "high society" folks for turning down his invitation. In the Matthew version of the parable, the ["certain man"]{.class_4723} of verse 16 is the King (Matt. 22:2). The reason the master becomes angry is that the King's invitation demands obedience.

Someone asked Emily Post (the etiquette lady) one time how to turn down an invitation to dinner at the White House if you had a previous engagement. Her reply was: "You don't. An invitation from a head of State supersedes all previous engagements." In other words, an invitation from the King trumped inspecting your land, proving your oxen, or pacifying your spouse---even if those excuses had been legitimate. Turning down the King's invitation was a personal affront.

["Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in hither the poor, and the maimed, and the halt, and the blind."]{.class_4723} Those are the ones Jesus told the Pharisees to invite to their feasts back in verse 13 so they would ["be recompensed at the resurrection of the just"]{.class_4723} (vs. 14). Applying the typology to salvation, Jesus is contrasting the Pharisees who rejected Him to the publicans and harlots who believed on Him (Matt. 21:32). He told those Pharisees, ["the publicans and the harlots go into the kingdom of God before you"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 21:31).

Verses 22--23: ["And the servant said, Lord, it is done as thou hast commanded, and yet there is room. And the lord said unto the servant, Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in]{.class_4723} \[hence the expression "beat the bushes"\][, that my house may be filled."]{.class_4723} The song goes: "He's filling up Heaven with SINNERS, who've been washed in the blood of the Lamb."

In Matthew, the servants go out with white linen wedding garments and offer them to anyone who wants to come to the wedding feast. The typology should be apparent: the offer is the perfect righteousness of God's Son in the place of the sinner's filthy rags of self-righteousness (Isa. 64:6). In fact, in the Matthew account, a fellow manages to make it in somehow in the rags of a beggar, and he won't accept the wedding garment offered him personally by the King, so he's pitched out on his ear.

One of those servants goes out on the streets and finds a young lady and says, "Would you like to come to a big wedding party up at the King's palace?"

She says, "Why, yes, that would be wonderful. Who's getting married?"

"The King's Son," replies the servant.

"I'd love to go," says the young woman. "How much does it cost? Do you have to have a special invitation?"

"No," says the servant, "all you have to do is put on this wedding garment."

The girl looks with disdain at that clean, white linen garment and says, "That old thing?!"

The servant says, "This 'old thing' cost the King's Son His life."

"Well, it's not very becoming; it's not stylish at all. I think I'll just wear my own clothes if you don't mind."

The servant says, "Lady, you have to have this on to get in."

She says, "No thank you." And she stays outside.

The servant walks down the street and looks down an alley and sees an old snaggly-toothed bum sitting up against a wall. He's got about three-days growth of beard, and breath that would put a blister on a brick wall. His clothes are filthy, and he smells like he hasn't bathed in a month.

The servant says, "Hey, buddy, want to go to a wedding?"

"Now who would want me at their wedding?" says the bum.

"The King," says the servant. "He sent me to invite anyone who'll come to His Son's marriage."

That old bum lets out a gravelly laugh and says, "You must be pulling my leg. Who are you trying to kid? The King doesn't want someone like me at His Son's wedding."

"I'm serious," said the servant. "All you've got to do is put on this wedding garment and go in. They'll have a spread there for you like you've never seen down at the rescue mission. Go ahead; take it and put it on."

The servant holds out the garment to the old bum. The fellows stares at it a moment; then he reaches out with one of his gnarled, filthy hands with dirty finger nails and grabs it and says, "I'll take it."

He puts on that wedding garment and goes up to the gate of the palace. The porter takes one look at that wedding garment the fellow's wearing and swings open the gate. "Come on in," he says, and that bum goes on in and sits down at the table.

Now, do you want to go to the Marriage Supper of the Lamb? [Then take the wedding garment.]{.class_4725}

["And be found in him, not having mine own righteousness, which is of the law, but that which is through the faith of Christ, the righteousness which is of God by faith"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 3:9).

25 And there went great multitudes with him: and he turned, and said unto them,

26 If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brethren, and sisters, yea, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple.

27 And whosoever doth not bear his cross, and come after me, cannot be my disciple.

28 For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost, whether he have sufficient to finish it?

29 Lest haply, after he hath laid the foundation, and is not able to finish it, all that behold it begin to mock him,

30 Saying, This man began to build, and was not able to finish.

31 Or what king, going to make war against another king, sitteth not down first, and consulteth whether he be able with ten thousand to meet him that cometh against him with twenty thousand?

32 Or else, while the other is yet a great way off, he []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_441}sendeth an ambassage, and desireth conditions of peace.

33 So likewise, whosoever he be of you that forsaketh not all that he hath, he cannot be my disciple.

Now Jesus is about to give His followers the true test of a [disciple,]{.class_4725} not salvation. One should never confuse discipleship with [salvation.]{.class_4725} Judas was a [disciple]{.class_4725} and he was [lost.]{.class_4725} Discipleship without salvation will put you in [Hell.]{.class_4725}

From what you read in the passage before you, discipleship is [expensive;]{.class_4725} salvation is a ["free gift"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 5:15--19). The trouble you get into these days is preachers making discipleship a means of salvation. Discipleship is [works.]{.class_4725} Taking up the cross, denying yourself, obeying the Lord---[that's]{.class_4725} discipleship. It comes from the word [discipline.]{.class_4725} That's [not]{.class_4725} salvation. The most disciplined people in the world were the ones who [murdered Jesus Christ.]{.class_4725}

Salvation is not by any work or discipline. ["But to him that WORKETH NOT, but believeth on him that justifieth the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 4:5). The old saying is: "No Christian works to [get saved;]{.class_4725} no Christian works to [stay saved.]{.class_4725} A Christian works because [he is saved.]{.class_4725}" Yes, a Christian should do good works (Eph. 2:10), but works have nothing to do with his salvation (Eph. 2:8--9). You had better get the difference.

To get these matters straight in the passage, you have to keep in mind that what you are reading in Luke 14 is [Old Testament.]{.class_4725} You say, "But it's in the New Testament." That's true, but what you are reading is the account of how the New Testament came to be. No one in Matthew, Mark, Luke, or John had a New Testament. The only thing they had was Genesis through Malachi. The New Testament wasn't even instituted until the Last Supper (22:20), and it wasn't in force until the Crucifixion (Heb. 9:15--17). And if that weren't enough, the doctrines of New Testament salvation weren't even revealed until the Apostle Paul showed up (Gal. 1:11--12).

Now that doesn't mean you can't apply some of what's in the Gospels to you, but you have to be careful. You say, "Well, how do I tell what's for me and what's not?" If what you are reading matches the doctrinal revelation given to Paul for the New Testament Church, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_442}you can apply it to you, and if it doesn't, it's for somebody else. The problem is people like John MacArthur and L. R. Shelton and the rest of the TULIP sniffers ("Lordship salvation") try to force Paul to match what Jesus taught in the Gospels (and vice versa), and it [doesn't match.]{.class_4725}

Listen, everything in that Bible is the word of God. I believe every word in that Bible from cover to cover, including the cover ("Holy Bible"). But brethren, there are some things in that Bible that are for the Jews; there are some things in that Bible that are for the Gentiles; and there are some things in that Bible that are for the Body of Christ (cf. 1 Cor. 10:32). Christians in America are continually taking everything in that Book and trying to make it fit the Body of Christ when it [doesn't.]{.class_4725} You have to rightly divide that Book (2 Tim. 2:15), and you have to read the Pauline Epistles to get the rule for dividing it.

Now look at what Jesus says and see if it matches what Paul said: ["If any man come to me, and HATE NOT his father, and mother\..."]{.class_4723} (vs. 26). Is that what Paul told you to do? No, it isn't. He told you to obey and honor them (Eph. 6:1--2).

["\...and wife\...."]{.class_4723} Paul didn't say hate your wife; he said to love her: ["Husbands, love your wives, even as Christ also loved the church, and gave himself for it"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 5:25).

["\...and children\...."]{.class_4723} In the Pauline Epistles, the women were to be taught ["to love their children"]{.class_4723} (Tit. 2:4).

So what's Jesus saying in verse 26? Well, to begin with, you are dealing with an Old Testament theocracy under the Law. In the national theocracy of Israel, when a family member committed a sin worthy of death under the Law (like idolatry), you were to be the first one to cast a stone to kill him or her. You were to show no mercy or pity or love to that person at all (Deut. 13:6--10).

That's what the tribe of Levi did the very first time the Ten Commandments were broken (Exod. 32:26--28). Those Levites went throughout that camp and didn't spare father, mother, brothers, sisters, or children if they were worshipping that golden calf (Deut. 33:8--9). That's where Mohammed [stole]{.class_4725} his idea for "honor killing." But it's not given to some fornicating Arab camel-trader who formed his own theocracy by stealing and killing; it's given to Moses for the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_443}nation of Israel.

Now, even though Luke 14:26 is under the Old Testament, it is given by Jesus Christ and not Moses. ["For the law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723} (John 1:17). Jesus Christ didn't come at His First Advent ["to condemn the world, but to save it"]{.class_4723} (John 3:17).

The Pharisees brought a woman to Jesus one time who had been caught in adultery and said, "The Law says stone her. What do you say?" (John 8:4--5). Jesus Christ forgave that adulteress and told her, ["Neither do I condemn thee: go, and sin no more"]{.class_4723} (John 8:11). So Jesus is not telling His disciples to kill father, mother, brothers, sisters, et al., if they get in the way of following Him.

The word ["hate,"]{.class_4723} here, is used in the sense of not following the desires of someone else (Judg. 14:16). It is also used in a comparative sense. Did you ever read back there in Genesis where ["Leah was hated"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 29:31)? Well, for hating Leah, Jacob certainly had enough children by her, didn't he? But look at the verse right above it: ["he loved Rachel MORE than Leah"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 29:30). Jacob ["hated"]{.class_4723} Leah in the sense that he loved her less than he loved Rachel.

That's what Jesus is saying here. He's not telling you not to love your parents, your wife, your children, and the rest of the family because He told you in the Pauline Epistles that you [are]{.class_4725} to love them. He's telling you that when it comes to a choice between loving Him and doing what He says, and loving your family and doing what they say, you had better love Jesus Christ over your family.

He says in Matthew 10:37, ["He that loveth father or mother MORE THAN ME is not worthy of me: and he that loveth son or daughter MORE THAN ME is not worthy of me."]{.class_4723} You are to love God and Christ [first]{.class_4725}, and everybody else takes second place. The acronym for JOY is: Jesus first, Others next, and You last.

["\...and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple."]{.class_4723} This has nothing to do with [sonship.]{.class_4725} This would apply to someone who is saved who wants to follow Christ now that he is saved. Technically speaking, a Christian is not just someone who accepts Jesus Christ as his Saviour. In Acts 11:26, it was ["the DISCIPLES"]{.class_4723} who ["were called Christians first in Antioch."]{.class_4723} A Christian is a saved sinner who is faithfully trying to follow Christ as a []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_444}[disciple.]{.class_4725}

["And his own life also."]{.class_4723} If a Christian is going to follow Christ, he has to hate [himself.]{.class_4725} That's a reference to that old nature in the Christian that loves sin: you are to [hate]{.class_4725} it (Rom. 7:15--18).

This has particular application to the Jews who follow Christ in the Tribulation. The Bible says, ["and they loved not their lives unto the death"]{.class_4736} (Rev. 12:11). In the Tribulation, that Jew will have a choice: love his own life and take the Mark of the Beast, or hate his own life and refuse the mark and risk getting his head chopped off or starving to death (Rev. 20:4, 13:17). If he takes the first choice, he makes out pretty good for a while, but then the judgment of God falls (Rev. 16), and he's damned forever (Rev. 14:9--11). If he chooses the second, the Antichrist makes war on him (Rev. 13:7), but if he can just hold out till the end (Matt. 24:13), he winds up with a Millennial inheritance, ruling and reigning with Christ (Rev. 20:4, 14:12--13).

That's what the Lord told the apostles in Matthew 19:28--29. He said that if they forsook father, mother, brother, sister, wife, and child for His name's sake, that they would be paid back 100% interest and ["inherit everlasting life"]{.class_4723} by ruling with Him over ["the twelve tribes of Israel"]{.class_4723} ["in the regeneration"]{.class_4723} (the Millennium---see Isa. 11).

Back to Luke 14: ["And whosoever doth not bear his cross, and come after me, cannot be my disciple"]{.class_4723} (vs. 27). That has nothing to do with getting saved. Paul never told you that you had to bear a cross to be saved. But if you want to be more than a son, if you want to be a [disciple,]{.class_4725} you had better take up your cross and follow Him.

Now what is the ["cross"]{.class_4723}? The cross is where your will crosses His: that's the cross. The cross is [death.]{.class_4725} So when your will crosses His will, you take your will and nail it to the cross and [kill it.]{.class_4725}

Paul says you are to ["mortify]{.class_4723} \[you are to put to death\][ therefore your members which are upon the earth"]{.class_4723} (Col. 3:5). What ["members"]{.class_4723}? Why, they're ["the works of the flesh"]{.class_4723} that he lists over in Galatians 5 (Col. 3:5 cf. Gal. 5:19--21). Paul says you are to take those fleshy works and desires and [crucify them]{.class_4725} (Gal. 5:24).

That's why Paul said in 1 Corinthians 15:31, ["I die daily."]{.class_4723} Every []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_445}day when Paul woke up, he had to nail that flesh of his to the cross and say, ["You're dead; stay dead."]{.class_4725} That's called "dead reckoning": you ["reckon\...yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God through Jesus Christ our Lord"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 6:11). That's the work of [discipleship]{.class_4725} that takes place [after]{.class_4725} salvation.

Now when it comes to this business of discipleship, Christ gives you two illustrations: a contractor (vss. 28--30) and a King going to war (vss. 31--32). The illustrations are to get you to consider the cost of discipleship before you decide to start.

The first illustration is that of a contractor, a builder. Every Christian is a builder. You've been given a good foundation (1 Cor. 3:11), and you are to be careful what you build on it. You can build with ["gold, silver, precious stones"]{.class_4723} that will survive the fire at the Judgment Seat of Christ, or you can build with ["wood, hay, stubble"]{.class_4723} that will go up in smoke (1 Cor. 3:12--15). Notice in 1 Corinthians 3:15 that it is a man's [works]{.class_4725} that burn up, not the man; the man is [saved.]{.class_4725} That's the difference between discipleship and salvation.

In Christ's illustration here in Luke 14, the foundation is already laid (vs. 29): that's salvation. The contractor is building a tower on the foundation: that's discipleship. The admonition is, before you start building, you had better sit down and make a realistic estimate of what it's going to cost you to finish the job. You don't want to get halfway finished and have a monument to your own incompetence.

Christ is talking about building a life here, building character, doing something for the Lord. Bob Jones Sr. used to say, "Any fool can be saved in less than a minute, but it takes a lifetime of struggle and prayer and self-denial to build a Christian character that will stand the blows of time." Getting saved is the easiest thing on this earth because all you have to do is [receive it as a gift]{.class_4725} (John 1:12; Rom. 6:23). Christian character, though, is something else; you don't get that when you get saved.

Sometimes you will hear this statement: "If you get a child saved, you save a whole life. If you get an adult saved, you just save what's left." That can be generally true, but not always. If you lead a child to Christ at age five and he gets to messing around with the world in his teenage years, he can be in the same mess by the time he's 25 as the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_446}one who gets saved as an adult. That child needs character instilled in him; salvation doesn't automatically give that.

Brethren, the way to develop character is to make yourself do what you don't want to do and make yourself not do what you do want to do. I wish there was some other way, but there isn't. You will never develop character any other way. I didn't say you're lost; you just don't have any character.

The sad fact is, some unsaved men have more character than some saved men. That's the problem you run into in witnessing. Every community is plagued with Christians who don't have the character that the unsaved do, and that is a testimony against the Lord. That says to the lost that getting saved isn't going to do them any good.

"Old man so-and-so pays his debts, and he isn't a Christian. Why, I know folks who go to that church who are behind three months on their payments." "I saw this Christian messing around with another man's wife. You mean to tell me that's what it means to be saved?" That's the kind of stuff you run into.

That doesn't mean those people are lost. David wasn't (he committed adultery); Peter wasn't (he cursed). But you talk about a shot testimony, boy. They both gave ["great occasion to the enemies of the Lord to blaspheme"]{.class_4723} (2 Sam. 12:14).

Listen, if you had run into Lot there in that cave in Genesis 19, you would have sworn he was lost; after all, he's in there drunk, shacking up with his two girls. But he was saved. Peter says he was a ["just"]{.class_4723} man and a ["righteous man"]{.class_4723} who vexed his soul ["with the filthy conversation of the wicked"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 2:7--8). What was the problem? [No character.]{.class_4725} He lost everything, but he didn't lose his [soul.]{.class_4725}

As a Christian, you will never lose your soul; but you can lose your money, your wife, your kids, your testimony, your joy, your assurance, your inheritance, your rewards, your health, and your [life.]{.class_4725} Isn't that enough to lose? I think so. You had better not only count the cost of following Christ; you had better count the cost of [not following]{.class_4725} Him too.

["Lest haply ]{.class_4723}\[we would say "perhaps" today\][, after he hath laid the foundation, and is not able to finish it, all that behold it begin to mock him"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_447} (vs. 29). It's a case where a fellow gets saved, and people expect something of him. He gets baptized and joins the church. He cleans up his life and starts witnessing. Then something happens. He gets mad at the preacher or just becomes negligent in his duties. He quits witnessing. He stops coming to prayer meeting. Then he drops the Sunday night service. (It's perfectly uniform; I've seen it happen a hundred times.)

The next step is skipping Sunday morning services, and then dropping out altogether. He gets around the wrong crowd, and they offer him an easy job involving drugs (not taking them, no; just putting them in the right spot where someone can get them). The "friend" will offer him more money than he can earn in ten years at a normal job. That thing progresses step by step, until the pastor has to go visit him in jail on a drug charge.

The world looks at that sort of thing, and they mock it and make fun of it (like they did Jim Bakker and Jimmy Swaggart). They mock it because the guy had no character. He was saved; he didn't "lose it." But when it came to character, he wasn't worth shooting.

Now I'm no sports fanatic, and I am far less patriotic now than I was as a young man. But if you want to learn character, learn to play a sport competitively or join the military. Both of those require discipline. The coach and the instructor make you go till you drop. That's good for a young man. If he can take what he learns in that regard and apply it to the Christian life, he can really do something for the Lord.

The second illustration is along the same line as the first. Before you go into battle, you have to assess the enemy's strength and figure out if you can take him with the men and equipment you have. Rule number one in warfare: [never underestimate the enemy's strength.]{.class_4725}

That was America's problem in Korea and Vietnam and Iraq. We figured we were just fighting some little two-bit countries that we could whip with no problem. We didn't figure on the Chinese troops coming into Korea and Vietnam and outnumbering our boys four to one. We didn't count on Iran blowing up our boys right and left after we supposedly "won the war" against Saddam Hussein.

Now warfare is a part of Christian discipleship; sure it is. You are to ["endure hardness as a good soldier of Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_448} (2 Tim. 2:3). You are commanded in the New Testament to ["fight the good fight"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:12) and ["war a good warfare"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 1:18). You are told to ["put on the whole armour of God, that ye may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 6:11).

So the illustration Christ gives is for the New Testament believer in the Body of Christ. But if you are going to obey the commands of Christian warfare in the Pauline Epistles, you had better count the cost and be committed. There are no "weekend warriors" in the Lord's army. Paul told Timothy, ["No man that warreth entangleth himself with the affairs of this life; that he may please him who hath chosen him to be a soldier"]{.class_4723} (2 Tim. 2:4).

Verse 33 gives the application of what Jesus has been teaching in verses 26--32: ["So likewise, whosoever he be of you that forsaketh not all that he hath, he cannot be my disciple."]{.class_4723} Literally true of the twelve apostles (see Matt. 19:27); prophetically true of the Jewish disciples in the Tribulation who flee from the Antichrist (Matt. 24:15--18). Of course, this condition of discipleship is never given in the Pauline Epistles for the New Testament Christian, but Paul does say this:

["But godliness with contentment is great gain. For we brought nothing into this world, and it is certain we can carry nothing out. And having food and raiment let us be therewith content"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:6--8).

The Christian should hold the things of this world loosely in his hands, for at any moment his Lord may call on him to give them up. He may not call on you to give up your fortune or your inheritance to serve Him like He did William Borden (heir to the Borden dairy fortune who died in Egypt preparing to minister to Moslems in China) or C. T. Studd (a student at Cambridge University who came from a wealthy family and gave up his money to become a missionary in China, India, and Africa), but if He does, don't be surprised. That's the price of discipleship.

34 Salt is good: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be seasoned?

35 It is neither fit for the land, nor yet for the dunghill; but men cast it out. He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.

The passage is another excerpt from the Sermon on the Mount (Matt. 5:13). In that passage, the salt is [people:]{.class_4725} those who believe in Christ (Jewish disciples in the Gospels; born-again children of God in the Church Age).

In the ancient world, salt was a valuable commodity. The old expression "he's worth his salt" comes from the ancient practice of paying a man for his goods or services with salt. The idea of the expression is that the fellow is worth his weight in salt: whatever his weight is, he's worth the monetary value of the same weight of salt (which in the ancient world would be quite a bit).

The reason salt was so valuable was not only because of its flavor, but because it was the main way of preserving food. So when Christ says, ["Ye are the salt of the earth"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:13), He is saying that His disciples are valuable to this sin-cursed world (see our remarks under 12:39) because they are the only reason God hasn't rained down judgment on it a long time ago. The Lord wouldn't destroy Sodom and Gomorrah as long as Lot was in it (Gen. 19:22). In the New Testament, the Lord saved 276 people aboard a boat because of one man---the Apostle Paul (Acts 27:23--24, 37). As bad as things are now, you can't imagine the shape this world will be in after the Christians are taken out at the Rapture. ("When we nuts are gone, you squirrels will be up a tree.")

["Salt is good: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be seasoned"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34). If rock salt sits for a good while, it loses its flavor. The question Christ poses is: "How can you season salt once it has lost its flavor?" The answer is: "[You can't."]{.class_4725} Over in Matthew 5:13, He says, ["it is thenceforth GOOD FOR NOTHING"]{.class_4723} (another common expression taken from the [King James ]{.class_4725}Bible).

Here Jesus says, ["It is neither fit for the land, nor yet for the dunghill"]{.class_4723} (vs. 35). You can't put it on the roads to melt the snow and ice, and you can't put it in the sewage to take care of the refuse (that's what salt does in the ocean; it eats up and dissolves the wastes dumped in the water).

["But men cast it out."]{.class_4723} God uses [men]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_450} to judge His children who become ["good for nothing"]{.class_4723} in their service to the Lord. That's what Jesus says in John 15:6.

"If a man abide not in me, he is cast forth as a branch, and is withered; and MEN gather them, and cast them into the fire, and they are burned."

That's not the angels gathering tares that never were wheat and throwing them into Hell at the Second Advent (Matt. 13:40--42). The branches from the vine that are ["cast forth"]{.class_4723} aren't Christians who lose their salvation and go to Hell. This is God using [men]{.class_4725} to destroy a Christian (cf. Rom. 8:13; 1 Cor. 3:17, 10:10) who does not bear fruit.

["He that hath ears to hear, let him hear."]{.class_4723} That's a warning from the Lord that you had better pay attention to what He says about salt, so we will.

The first mention of salt is in Genesis 14:3 (["the salt sea"]{.class_4723}), where it is connected with the place where God rained down fire and brimstone on Sodom and Gomorrah and sent those cities into ["eternal fire"]{.class_4723} (Jude 7). So the first mention of salt is in connection with [Hell.]{.class_4725}

When God overthrew those cities, Lot's wife looked back and was turned into ["a pillar of salt"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 19:26). That means she was white all over. Do you know what the Law said about a leper when the disease had progressed to the point where his body was white all over? It said, ["he is clean"]{.class_4723} (Lev. 13:13). He still had the leprosy, and eventually it would kill him, but ["he is clean"]{.class_4723} as far as the Law was concerned.

Unsaved people think they are going to enjoy their sin in Hell just like they enjoy it here. They are in for a rude awakening: [there is no sin in Hell.]{.class_4725} The fire and the salt clean it out. The problem is, the people in Hell don't get the benefits of having their sins cleansed like those of us do whose sins are cleansed by the blood of Christ (1 John 1:7). You will either get the "salt" now, or you will get it in Hell. Look at Mark 9:49--50. After talking about Hell for six verses, Jesus says:

["For every one\...."]{.class_4723} About whom is He talking? He's talking about the people who went to Hell in Mark 9:43--48. ["For every one shall be salted with fire, and every sacrifice shall be salted with salt]{.class_4723} \[see Lev. 2:13; Ezra 7:22\][\...Have salt in []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ2836.xhtml_page_451}yourselves."]{.class_4723}

I don't have to ["be salted with fire"]{.class_4723} in Hell because I got my salt through the sacrifice of Jesus Christ. He was ["salted with fire"]{.class_4723} for me on the cross. He's in me (Col. 1:27), so I have His ["salt"]{.class_4723} in me.

Since you are commanded as a Christian to ["speak as the oracles of God"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 4:11), and since ["out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 12:34), if you have Christ living in you and His salt is in you, [" your speech"]{.class_4723} should ["be alway with grace, SEASONED WITH SALT" ]{.class_4723}(Col. 4:6). That means you should talk like He talked; not only the [positive]{.class_4725} (["grace"]{.class_4723}) but the [negative]{.class_4725} as well (["salt"]{.class_4723}). A Christian whose preaching or personal witness doesn't [sting]{.class_4725} the sinner (salt stings when it is put on an open wound) isn't speaking like His Saviour or His Saviour's Book.
