He's talking like Stewart Custer's "Jesus" (see our remarks under 3:8), with ["good words and fair speeches"]{.class_4723} to ["deceive the hearts of the simple"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 16:18).

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml}

# Chapter 15 {.heading_78-0}

15:1 Then drew near unto him all the publicans and sinners for to hear him.

2 And the Pharisees and scribes murmured, saying, This man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them.

3 And he spake this parable unto them, saying,

4 What man of you, having an hundred sheep, if he lose one of them, doth not leave the ninety and nine in the wilderness, and go after that which is lost, until he find it?

5 And when he hath found it, he layeth it on his shoulders, rejoicing.

6 And when he cometh home, he calleth together his friends and neighbours, saying unto them, Rejoice with me; for I have found my sheep which was lost.

7 I say unto you, that likewise joy shall be in heaven over one sinner that repenteth, more than over ninety and nine just persons, which need no repentance.

We have already commented on the use of the word ["sinners"]{.class_4723} in the Gospels under 1:6, 5:30, 32, and 7:37--38 (which see). The word is a reference to very wicked, bad people (thieves, extortioners, prostitutes, murderers, etc.). But I get a real blessing applying verse 2 to [me]{.class_4727} as a sinner (see Rom. 3:10--23): ["This man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them."]{.class_4723}

["This man receiveth sinners."]{.class_4723} I sure am glad about that, because [I'm a sinner.]{.class_4725} If you're not a sinner, He doesn't receive you. The old hymn goes: "Christ receiveth sinful men, even [me]{.class_4725} in all my sin." If you're not a sinner, then Christ didn't die for you (see Rom. 5:8).

["This man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them."]{.class_4723} One day a little girl came home from her first vacation Bible school, and her mother asked, "How did you like vacation Bible school, Edith?"

"It was fun!" exclaimed Edith.

"What was your lesson about today?" asked the mother.

"It was about [me,"]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_453} said the girl.

"It couldn't be about you, sweetheart; there's no Edith in the Bible," said the mother.

"Yes, there is," said the girl, "We learned the verse that said Jesus ['receiveth sinners, and Edith with them.'"]{.class_4725}

I can say "Amen!" to that. ["He receiveth sinners, and Pete Ruckman with them."]{.class_4725}

["And he spake this parable unto them, saying"]{.class_4723} (vs. 3). He's about to give them three parables, but the verse says ["parable"]{.class_4723} [singular]{.class_4725} because all three are basically the same parable. In each parable, you have something [lost]{.class_4725} that is [found.]{.class_4725} In verses 4--7, there is a lost [sheep;]{.class_4725} in verses 8--10, there is a lost [coin;]{.class_4725} and in verses 11--32, there is a lost [son.]{.class_4725} The sheep gets lost because it [wanders off,]{.class_4725} the coin is lost by [happenstance,]{.class_4725} and the son is lost because of his [disobedience.]{.class_4725}

Now we all understand the spiritual, inspirational application of the stories, and I have preached on them many times. Jesus Christ is the Good Shepherd who gives His life for the sheep (John 10:11). He came to seek you as a sheep who had ["gone astray"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 53:6) and to save you (Luke 19:10). The Holy Spirit was sent to seek you and draw you to Christ. The Father waits to receive you if you will come to Him in repentance and faith. I believe all of that and I preach it. But if you are going to apply those parables [doctrinally,]{.class_4725} that is something else.

Take, for example, the parable of the lost sheep. Make the sheep a saved man, like you find it in John 10:26--28. If that's the case, what's the Good Shepherd doing losing one of His sheep? He said, ["I give unto them eternal life; and they shall NEVER PERISH, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand"]{.class_4723} (John 10:28).

Now make the sheep a lost man. The Good Shepherd leaves 99 saved sheep ["in the wilderness"]{.class_4723} (vs. 4) to go find one lost sheep. Well, what's He doing leaving [any]{.class_4725} of His sheep when He said, ["I will NEVER LEAVE THEE, nor forsake thee"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 13:5)? How is it that only one sheep went ["astray"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 18:12) when the Bible says, ["ALL we like sheep have gone astray"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 53:6)? Do you see the problems involved?

Well, if Jesus is not talking about saved men and lost men [doctrinally,]{.class_4725} then about whom is He talking? Look at Psalm 100:3.

"Know ye that the LORD he is God: it is he that hath made us, and not we ourselves: WE ARE HIS PEOPLE, AND THE SHEEP OF HIS PASTURE."

God's people in the Old Testament were [Israel]{.class_4725} (2 Chron. 7:14). [They]{.class_4725} are His sheep, and He calls them ["my flock"]{.class_4723} throughout Ezekiel 34. The parable is given in response to one group of Jews (["the Pharisees and scribes,"]{.class_4723} vs. 2) complaining about another group of Jews (["the publicans and sinners,"]{.class_4723} vs. 1) coming to Christ. Jesus is explaining to the Pharisees and scribes His purpose in regards ["to the lost sheep of the house of Israel"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 10:6).

Now like I said, I don't hesitate to use the parable to preach salvation. One of the best sermons I have is a chalk talk I do for the kids in vacation Bible school called "Snowflake." It's about a little lamb who wanders off from the flock, and the shepherd goes out to find him. It's told from the lamb's point of view. It makes great preaching.

But brethren, good preaching is not always good doctrine, and good doctrine is not always good preaching. Preaching is to motivate you to do something about what you hear, and doctrine is to inform you about what is so and what is not so in the Scriptures. You can get so particular in your doctrine, like the Hyperdispensationalists do, that you become useless when it comes to leading a soul to Christ. I use verses like Romans 6:23 and Revelation 3:20 all the time when dealing with a sinner about receiving Christ, and neither one was written to a lost man doctrinally. But they certainly can be applied by the soulwinner to motivate the sinner to accept Christ as His Saviour, so I use them for that purpose.

You take the parable before us. You certainly can preach it as Jesus Christ coming to find a lost sinner to save him, because in a sense, it's true. The sheep is lost; the sinner is lost. The lost sheep isn't in the flock; the lost sinner is not in God's flock. The sheep is out on the mountainside about ready to die; the lost man is about ready to die and go to Hell. The sheep can't save himself; the lost man can't []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_455}save himself. The shepherd had to save the sheep; Jesus Christ has to save the sinner. You see, salvation is all through there; you might as well preach it. The Book is not only ["profitable for doctrine,"]{.class_4723} but ["for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness"]{.class_4723} as well (2 Tim. 3:16).

Verse 7: ["I say unto you, that likewise joy shall be in heaven over one sinner that repenteth, more than over ninety and nine just persons, which need no repentance."]{.class_4723} Again, the ["sinner"]{.class_4723} of verse 7 is one of those bad people up in verses 1--2; the ["just persons"]{.class_4723} would be the scribes and Pharisees who thought they kept the Law so that they didn't need to repent of anything. That's the immediate application. But you can apply that ["sinner"]{.class_4723} there to [any]{.class_4725} sinner who gets saved. Look at what Paul says in 1 Thessalonians 2:19.

"For what is our hope, or JOY, or crown of rejoicing? Are not even YE in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at his coming?"

That's the ["crown of rejoicing"]{.class_4723}; it's the soulwinner's crown. It's the souls you lead to Christ. The old song goes: "Will there be any stars, any stars in my crown, when at evening the sun goeth down?" That Bible says ["they that turn many to righteousness"]{.class_4723} shall be ["as the stars for ever and ever"]{.class_4723} (Dan. 12:3).

The soulwinner's crown is called the ["crown of rejoicing"]{.class_4723} because when you win a soul to Christ, it causes joy "upstairs." If you're worldly minded, you don't understand that. When one country starts slinging missiles and bombs and bullets at another country, the newspapers run big block headlines that say: "WAR!" Up in Heaven, all the angels yawn and say, "Here we go again." That's because they've seen a war once every nine months since Cain knocked Abel's brains out. That's nothing new to them.

Do you know what gets the presses running in Heaven? A sinner comes down the aisle during an invitation and accepts Christ as His personal Saviour, and up in Heaven they're yelling, "Extra! Extra! Read all about it! Another sinner passes from death to life!" And down here, do you know what you get from the magazines and the "dailies"? Zero! You can't be a citizen of Heaven and think like they do in Heaven and at the same time be a citizen of the world and think []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_456}like the world; it's impossible. You are going to have to choose one or the other.

Verse 7 is a great verse on eternal security when you really think about it, for a person who loses his salvation and repents and gets it back would cause more joy in Heaven than the guy who gets saved and stays saved. That would mean that all those crazy Charismatic nuts in the "Brownsville Revival" who lost it and got it back five or six times would cause more rejoicing in Heaven than someone who got saved and never lost it. Do you see how stupid those Charismatics can be?

8 Either what woman having ten pieces of silver, if she lose one piece, doth not light a candle, and sweep the house, and seek diligently till she find it?

9 And when she hath found it, she calleth her friends and her neighbours together, saying, Rejoice with me; for I have found the piece which I had lost.

10 Likewise, I say unto you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner that repenteth.

After giving you a clear picture of ["the lost sheep of the house of Israel,"]{.class_4723} up pops ["ten pieces of silver"]{.class_4723} (vs. 8). Ten is a [Gentile]{.class_4725} number. Noah was the [tenth]{.class_4725} from Adam, and he is the Father of the [Gentiles.]{.class_4725} The table of the [Gentile]{.class_4725} nations is given in Genesis [10.]{.class_4725} In Genesis [10:10,]{.class_4725} you have the first [Gentile]{.class_4725} kingdom. In Acts [10,]{.class_4725} the door of the Gospel is opened to the [Gentiles,]{.class_4725} and in Romans [10,]{.class_4725} you have the missionary call to the [Gentiles.]{.class_4725} So sandwiched between two parables on lost Jews is one on lost [Gentiles]{.class_4725} (see our comments on 13:28 and on John 1:11--12 in that Commentary).

The silver is the price of [redemption]{.class_4725} (see Num. 3:47--49; Lev. 5:15; Exod. 30:13--15). Some of the expositors make the ["ten pieces of silver"]{.class_4723} in verse 8 the dowry necklace worn by this woman. In the Middle East, when a couple got married, they didn't exchange rings like couples do today. Instead, a dowry would be paid for the bride (sometimes by her father to the husband and sometimes by the husband to her father). The wife would take some of the coins from the dowry and make a necklace out of them which []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_457}she wore as a symbol of her marriage to her husband. It meant she was bought and paid for to be the man's wife. So the symbolism is a Gentile Bride redeemed by the blood of Christ (1 Pet. 1:18).

Of course, you can't press the type too far. What happened? Did the woman lose her redemption? Nevertheless, the picture, spiritually speaking, is of the Lord seeking a lost Gentile to redeem to be part of His Bride.

Now in the Parable of the Lost Sheep, you have the Good Shepherd (God the Son) seeking the lost sheep. In the Parable of the Lost Son, you have the Father (God the Father) waiting for His lost son to repent and return to Him. So in this parable, you have a picture of the Holy Spirit using the light of the word of God (["light a candle,"]{.class_4723} vs. 8 cf. Psa. 119:105, 130) to expose the lost Gentile's sin and bring him under conviction (John 16:7--11) so he can be found.

11 And he said, A certain man had two sons:

12 And the younger of them said to his father, Father, give me the portion of goods that falleth to me. And he divided unto them his living.

13 And not many days after the younger son gathered all together, and took his journey into a far country, and there wasted his substance with riotous living.

14 And when he had spent all, there arose a mighty famine in that land; and he began to be in want.

15 And he went and joined himself to a citizen of that country; and he sent him into his fields to feed swine.

16 And he would fain have filled his belly with the husks that the swine did eat: and no man gave unto him.

17 And when he came to himself, he said, How many hired servants of my father's have bread enough and to spare, and I perish with hunger!

18 I will arise and go to my father, and will say unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and before thee,

19 And am no more worthy to be called thy son: make me as one of thy hired servants.

20 And he arose, and came to his father. But when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_458}compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him.

21 And the son said unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in thy sight, and am no more worthy to be called thy son.

22 But the father said to his servants, Bring forth the best robe, and put it on him; and put a ring on his hand, and shoes on his feet:

23 And bring hither the fatted calf, and kill it; and let us eat, and be merry:

24 For this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found. And they began to be merry.

25 Now his elder son was in the field: and as he came and drew nigh to the house, he heard musick and dancing.

26 And he called one of the servants, and asked what these things meant.

27 And he said unto him, Thy brother is come; and thy father hath killed the fatted calf, because he hath received him safe and sound.

28 And he was angry, and would not go in: therefore came his father out, and intreated him.

29 And he answering said to his father, Lo, these many years do I serve thee, neither transgressed I at any time thy commandment: and yet thou never gavest me a kid, that I might make merry with my friends:

30 But as soon as this thy son was come, which hath devoured thy living with harlots, thou hast killed for him the fatted calf.

31 And he said unto him, Son, thou art ever with me, and all that I have is thine.

32 It was meet that we should make merry, and be glad: for this thy brother was dead, and is alive again; and was lost, and is found.

Now in the context of Luke 15, this story would be the Parable of the [Lost]{.class_4725} Son, but it has come to be known as the Parable of the [Prodigal]{.class_4725} Son because of what the young man does in the narrative in verse 13. A [prodigal]{.class_4725} is a "spendthrift," someone who wastes what he []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_459}has and throws it away.

The story, of course, is a great picture of salvation. It is also a great picture of a backslider. The doctrine, though, is something else.

For instance, make the father God and make the son a sinner. Well, was the ["son"]{.class_4723} a [son]{.class_4725} or not? He was a ["son"]{.class_4723} before he ran off to the ["far country,"]{.class_4723} but his father said, ["this my son was DEAD, and is alive again"]{.class_4723} (vs. 24). That's not your case as a child of God. You weren't a son of God who sinned and became ["dead in trespasses and sins"]{.class_4723} the moment you sinned (Eph. 2:1). You were a child of disobedience and a child of wrath before you got saved (Eph. 2:2--3), not a child of God.

["He was lost, and is found"]{.class_4723} (vs. 24)? No way, José. That's not your case as a Christian. You can't be ["lost"]{.class_4723} after being in the Father's family (Eph. 2:19).

Now when I preach this parable, of course I use it to preach salvation, but that's not the doctrine. This is another doctrinal picture of Israel as God's ["son"]{.class_4723} (Exod. 4:22). Israel was dead and is brought back to life in Ezekiel 37.

As in the Parable of the Lost Sheep, you have a contrast between the publicans and the sinners, and the Pharisees and the scribes. The lost son is the publicans and sinners who run off to the far country away from God the Father. Though they are a "son" in the sense that they are part of Israel, they are still dead spiritually. The older brother in the parable is the Pharisees and scribes who keep the Father's commandment but are envious of the attention shown to the publicans and sinners when they repent and get right.

Of course, the parable is rich in sermonic material. I don't know how many times I have done my chalk talk on "The Prodigal Son," but it was one of about ten sermons the Lord gave me in the first couple of years of my ministry. I have preached those ten sermons so many times that I no longer need notes for them, and "The Prodigal Son" is one of them. I have preached that sermon, I would say, an average of three times a year for over sixty years. So let's approach the parable from a spiritual aspect of [preaching.]{.class_4725}

["A certain man had two sons: And the younger of them said to his father"]{.class_4723} (vss. 11--12). You might have known it would be the [younger]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_460} son who runs off in rebellion. There is something that happens to a boy when he gets to be about sixteen, and a girl when she gets to be around thirteen, that they go to pieces. It's called "puberty" and "adolescence," but it's more the Devil than anything else. Teenagers are smarter than at any other time in their lives: the reason you can't tell them anything is because they know everything and have all the answers.

["And the younger of them said."]{.class_4723} I wouldn't want to go back to fourteen, fifteen, and sixteen for anything. Young people can be some of the cruelest people in the world. They have no experience and make some of the dumbest mistakes you ever saw.

I feel sorry for teenagers in today's world. They have all the odds stacked against them. When it comes to [getting the truth]{.class_4725} and [living right,]{.class_4725} the government's against them, the media's against them, the schools and teachers are against them, and sometimes their parents and family are against them.

["The younger of them said\...give me"]{.class_4723} (vs. 12). When this country started out, Patrick Henry watched a Baptist beaten to death by the British for preaching on the street without a license, and he went into the Virginia House of Burgesses and said, "Give me liberty or give me death." One hundred-fifty years later, the citizenry said, "Give me liberty." Today, they say, "Give me." They want a hand-out; they want the government to take care of them. That's the whole motivation behind the "Occupy" movement under Obama: they want the government to give them free education; they want the government to give them free birth control; they want the government to give them free health insurance; they want the government to give them free rein to commit any act of sexual deviancy they want to commit. "Give me; give me; give me; give me."

["Give ME the portion of goods that falleth to ME."]{.class_4723} It's [me first.]{.class_4725} He's thinking of no one but [himself.]{.class_4725} He isn't concerned with how his actions affect his brother or his father as long as he gets what's coming to [him.]{.class_4725}

["And not many days after the younger son gathered all together, and took his journey into a far country, and there wasted his substance with riotous living"]{.class_4723} (vs. 13). The boy had already moved to the ["far country"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_461} in his heart long before he got his money and went there. That Bible says, ["My son, give me thine heart"]{.class_4723} (Prov. 23:26). Why? Because ["out of it]{.class_4723} \[the heart\][ are the issues of life"]{.class_4723} (Prov. 4:23). Never mind your brain; your brain wouldn't fill a good size garbage can. The Lord wants your [heart]{.class_4725} because if He's got your heart, He's got [you.]{.class_4725}

Now, you don't have to [go]{.class_4725} to the ["far country"]{.class_4723} to be [in]{.class_4725} it. You can be in the ["far country"]{.class_4723} sitting in the church pew on Sunday morning. If your [heart]{.class_4725} is in the ["far country,"]{.class_4723} you are there already. ["For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also"]{.class_4723} (12:34). That's why you are commanded to ["set your affection on things above, not on things on the earth"]{.class_4723} (Col. 3:2).

Let me ask you something: do you love anybody else more than you love Jesus Christ? Is there any book (or magazine or movie or TV program, etc.) that is more precious to you than the Bible? Is there any place you would rather be than in Heaven or the New Jerusalem? If the answer is "yes" to any of those questions, you have already started for the ["far country."]{.class_4723}

What is the ["far country"]{.class_4723}? Well, it can be a good place: ["As cold waters to a thirsty soul, so is good news from a far country"]{.class_4723} (Prov. 25:25). That's a picture of Heaven. But many times in Scripture, the ["far country"]{.class_4723} is [Babylon]{.class_4725} (Isa. 39:3; 2 Kings 20:14): a picture of the kingdom of the Antichrist (Rev. 17--18). When this young fellow takes ["his journey into a far country,"]{.class_4723} he isn't headed to Heaven or anywhere good; he's headed into ruin in the [world.]{.class_4725}

When I was a boy coming up, every town had a railroad track running down the middle of the town, and the good, respectable folks were on one side of the tracks while the bad, trashy people were on the other side of the tracks. If you wanted to go to a bad place, all you had to do was go to "the other side of the tracks."

Of course, there was also the difference between the country and the city. I grew up in Topeka, Kansas, where the country and city were right together. If you wanted to get into really bad trouble, you had to go to a big city like New Orleans or New York or Los Angeles. You had to [move]{.class_4725} to get to the ["far country."]{.class_4723}

Someone told me once to "go to hell," and the nearest place I could find to it was New Orleans. I was down there by myself in Club Bali and the Court of the Two Sisters playing drums in a dance band before I was eighteen years old. I was living in a hotel room down there trying to sell my paintings in Pirates Alley. I know the French Quarter; I was there back in the 1930s. That's why when Katrina went through there and tore the place up, I thought that was a great hurricane: it put a hold on a lot of devilment going on there.

I've been to several "far countries" like that. "Pier 5" in Manila is like that. "Hell's Half Acre" in Honolulu is like that. I've been to "Hell's Half Acre" in the daytime after I got saved, and I got scared just looking out the window. I used to go down there at night before I got saved with my trench knife in my boot and my money in my socks just in case I got jumped (they'll look in your shoes but not in your socks). I [know]{.class_4725} what's in the ["far country."]{.class_4723}

Back in the old days, there was a distinction between home and the ["far country."]{.class_4723} Not any more, boy! There is stuff going on tonight in little towns like Milton and Jay, Florida for which God burnt Sodom to the ground. In Vermont, way back yonder in the hills with the country folk, the law broke up a child pornography ring where the leader was [fourteen years old.]{.class_4725} He was taking pictures of [children fornicating with animals.]{.class_4725} That wasn't some street punk from Detroit or Chicago; that was a [country]{.class_4725} kid. Do you know what that means? That means there is no more "country."

The cause of that is [television.]{.class_4725} Through television, you have stuff going on in your living room that you had to go to the brothels, bordellos, bars, and strip joints to see when I was growing up. You've got the women in their underwear doing the bumps and grinds right there in your living room. You can see the couples fornicating in bed---stuff that was considered pornographic when I was young.

When I was a boy, there was a song that said: "I want a girl, just like the girl that married dear old dad. She was a pearl and THE ONLY GIRL that daddy ever had. Good old-fashioned girl with heart so true, one who loved NOBODY ELSE BUT YOU." Where would you find a girl like that these days? Why, the average high school girl has been slobbered all over by everyone in the class, including the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_463}janitor, by the time she graduates. The kids in "middle school" (junior high) are now mating around like a pack of animals, and the government wants to push that kind of behavior all the way down to kindergarten by teaching the little kiddies about "safe sex," condoms, sodomy, and bestiality.

Back in the old days when a guy courted a girl, he would sit with her in the parlor (with momma and daddy in the next room, usually). There would be an old grandfather clock there going tick-tock, tick-tock, tick-tock. It was telling that young fellow, "Take your time; take your time, take your time." Today, the couple ends up in the back seat of a car with wrist watches going, tick, tick, tick, tick, tick. It's "hurry up, hurry up, hurry up, hurry up, hurry up."

You don't have to go to Shanghai, Cairo, Istanbul, or Paris anymore to go to Hell. You've got stuff going on in small towns with populations of less than 2,000 for which God burned Sodom and Gomorrah, and there is going to be a repeat performance of it too.

["And there wasted his substance with riotous living."]{.class_4723} Liquor, drugs, prostitutes, gambling, the whole works. A young man as eager to get away from daddy's rules as this young man was wasn't going to invest his money wisely or start a business or get an education and make something of himself. No, he was going to "live for the moment" and "go for the gusto." "A fool and his money are soon parted." After a while, he no longer had his inheritance; it belonged to the bookies, the bartenders, the drug dealers, the pushers, the hookers and their pimps, and a whole cadré of people who latched onto him like leeches for his money.

["And when he had spent all, there arose a mighty famine in that land, and he began to be in want"]{.class_4723} (vs. 14). When the money ran out, the "friends" ran off. You'll find out really quickly who your real friends are when your money runs out.

He went to some bimbo with whom he had painted the town red and asked, "Suzie, can I sleep on your back porch tonight? I've been sleeping in the park, but it's getting kind of cold." She says, "Get out of here, you bum, before I call a cop."

He went to some woman with whom he had been running around and said, "Lucy, can you give me something to eat? I haven't eaten for three days." She said, "You'd better get on before my husband []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_464}gets home."

He tried hitchhiking, and no one would pick him up. Some guy thought about stopping for him, and the wife said, "Don't you pick up that tramp; he's liable to take the jack handle and knock you over the head with it."

["And he went and joined himself to a citizen of that country"]{.class_4723} (vs. 15): a picture of the world and the Devil. ["And he sent him into his fields to feed swine."]{.class_4723} Some job for a Jewish boy, huh? Feeding [pigs]{.class_4725}---an unclean animal he wasn't allowed to eat.

Now young man, young lady, I will give you some good advice, if you are willing to take it. Of course, you may not. It is easy to give advice and hard to take it. But I've been around a long, long time, and I know some things.

Some men live faster than others. Some men are old before they reach thirty. I have known at least five complete lives, among which are the life of an [author,]{.class_4725} the life of an [artist,]{.class_4725} and the life of a [military officer.]{.class_4725} There are some other things at which I have worked but not experienced in full, like the life of a [farmer,]{.class_4725} but I have seen and done many things in my ninety-plus years. I am going to tell you young people something: if you get fooling around with the world, you'll wind up [tending their pigs.]{.class_4725}

Christian young person, when the world applauds you, the blood of Jesus Christ splashes off its hands. The world nailed your Saviour to the cross, and you're going to try to get along with it, are you? When it gets through with you, it will throw you out there to its hogs to serve them.

["And he would fain have filled his belly with the husks that the swine did eat"]{.class_4723} (vs. 16). He's living on starvation rations. He's working and barely getting enough to stay alive. He is so hungry that he's ready to eat the slop given to the pigs (in this case, corn husks).

["And no man gave unto him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 16). He had thrown away his money like there was no tomorrow, and now it's tomorrow. It's easy to say, "Eat, drink, and be merry; for tomorrow we die," but what will happen to you tomorrow if you don't die? There was no one around when he was in need to show him any kindness. None of his so-called "friends" helped him out; his new boss wouldn't help him []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_465}out; the government wouldn't help him out; no one would help him out. ["No man gave unto him."]{.class_4723}

["And when he came to himself"]{.class_4723} (vs. 17). That's a great statement. The young man was out of his mind and just regained his sanity. The world had told him, "Man, you're living." Now he realized he was [dying]{.class_4725}. ["He came to himself,"]{.class_4723} like a boxer would after he has been knocked out. The world knocked the poor kid for a loop, and he was just now waking up from it.

When the boy comes to himself, he thinks about [going home]{.class_4725} (vss. 17--18). When that young man ["came to himself,"]{.class_4723} he realized that everything he wanted was in the [father's house.]{.class_4725} What did he want? He wanted love---it was in the father's house (vs. 20). He wanted nice clothes---they were in the father's house (vs. 22). He wanted money---it was in the father's house (vs. 22). He wanted happiness---it was in the father's house (vs. 23). He wanted a good meal---it was in the father's house (vs. 23). Everything that boy wanted was back home with daddy, the one he had [left.]{.class_4725} And sinner, backslidden Christian, everything you want and need, you will find in [the Father's house.]{.class_4725}

["I will arise and go to my father, and will say unto him, Father, I have sinned AGAINST HEAVEN, and before thee"]{.class_4723} (vs. 18). Note that he said he had ["sinned against heaven and before thee."]{.class_4723} He had sinned ["before"]{.class_4723} his father, but he hadn't sinned ["against"]{.class_4723} his father. His sin was ["against heaven"]{.class_4723}; that is, [against God.]{.class_4725} David said when he repented of committing adultery with Bathsheba and having Uriah killed, ["Against THEE, THEE ONLY, have I sinned, and done this evil in thy sight"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 51:4). To whom was he talking? He was talking to [God]{.class_4725} (vs. 1).

Mohammed was so stupid that he said [no man]{.class_4725} can sin against God; he just sins against [himself]{.class_4725} (Sura 4:110). That's the ravings of a demon-possessed crackpot. Mohammed had no more idea of what he was talking than the Three Stooges. When Potiphar's wife tried to seduce Joseph, he told that old sister, ["how then can I do this great wickedness and sin AGAINST GOD?"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 39:9). [Sin is against God.]{.class_4725}

["I have sinned."]{.class_4723} Several people say that in the Scriptures, and they wind up [dead and in Hell: Pharaoh]{.class_4725} said that (Exod. 9:27, 10:16); [Balaam]{.class_4725} said that (Num. 22:34); [Achan]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_466} said that (Josh. 7:20); [Saul]{.class_4725} said that (1 Sam. 15:24, 30, 26:21); [Judas]{.class_4725} said that (Matt. 27:4). So confession of sin is not enough.

Repentance isn't sorrow for your sins because you [got caught]{.class_4725} or because of the [consequence of those sins.]{.class_4725} Evangelical repentance isn't sorrow for what you [do;]{.class_4725} it's sorrow for what you [are.]{.class_4725} Isaiah said, ["I am a man of unclean lips"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 6:5): that's repentance. Job said, ["I abhor myself"]{.class_4723} (Job 42:6): that's repentance. Peter said, ["Depart from me; for I am a sinful man, O Lord"]{.class_4723} (Luke 5:8): that's repentance.

When you get saved, you have to admit that you are absolutely [no good.]{.class_4725} That's why you have to get born again: your first birth was no good. You have to come to God admitting that the best you have to offer Him will put you in Hell: [that's]{.class_4725} evangelical repentance.

["And am no more worthy to be called thy son"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 19). It's a good picture of a backslidden Christian. I will tell you something else I have learned by experience: ["the way of transgressors is hard"]{.class_4730-0} (Prov. 13:15). Christian young person, a sheep can't be happy in a hog wallow. Once you have tasted the bread of life, it is hard to be satisfied with the slop of this world.

["Make me as one of thy hired servants."]{.class_4723} One of the marks of repentance is [humility.]{.class_4725} The young man was willing to return home as one of the farm hands. He knew he was disgraced as a son and wasn't going to try to demand his "rights" in that regard like he did when he left.

["And he arose, and came to his father"]{.class_4723} (vs. 20). Hollywood once made a picture about the prodigal son. I never saw it and don't want to see it, but I just bet you that it made a hero out of that young man. The hero of the story is [not]{.class_4725} the prodigal; it's the [father.]{.class_4725}

I'm not going to make a hero out of the young man, but I will say one thing for him. He was a cussing, whoremongering, disobedient, rebellious, stupid young man; but I will say this for him: [you could count on him when he said something.]{.class_4725} He said, "I'm leaving," and he left. He said, "I'm going to live like the devil," and he did. He said, "I'm going home to daddy," and [he got up and went.]{.class_4725}

The trouble with Southerners is that they never get around to doing what they know they ought to do. Southerners are bad []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_467}procrastinators. There are "good, old boys" down South who've heard the Gospel twice a year in revival meetings every year for ten, twenty, thirty years; and they don't make a move to get saved. They say, "Well, I'm aiming to, preacher." Stop aiming and SHOOT!

I've had old goats up in their seventies and eighties tell me, "Well, preacher, when I get to where I can live it, I'll get saved." Man, at that age, you ain't gonna "live" nothin'. You're going to sit on the stump till you die. Thank God the young man in this story got right before he was 25 or thirty. Otherwise he would have gotten to seventy or eighty with one foot in the grave and the other raised to kick the bucket and still be saying, "I'm going to get up [someday]{.class_4725} and go home." ["Behold NOW is the accepted time; behold NOW is the day of salvation"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 6:2).

["But when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him."]{.class_4723} Now you see why the father is the hero of the story? Even though his boy had sinned before him, he was anxiously waiting to receive him back if he returned home. Daddy was out there every day, looking down that road for his boy. The old man was expecting him: he had been fattening a calf for his return (vs. 27). You don't fatten a calf in a couple of days.

The Lord is more anxious for you to get right than you are. If there were an altar call and you started down the aisle, God would beat you to the altar even if He were at the farthest confines of the universe creating new galaxies. The Lord is more concerned about you getting right than you are. The fact that most don't come to the altar shows they aren't particularly worried about it. But if the Lord saw you start, He would be there waiting for you before you took two steps.

In my mind's eye, I imagine the scene before us like the old Southern mansions you see in central Alabama and Georgia, with the high pillars and a balustrade at the top. The old man is up there on that balcony at sundown looking out across the plantation to the road leading up to the front porch. One of the colored servants comes up to him and says, "Massah, you betta come to de table. The Mrs. says suppa's ready."

The old man says, "I'll be there in just a minute." About five []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_468}minutes later, the colored boy comes back out there and finds the old man still staring down the road.

"Boss," he says, "you betta come on. The Mrs. is kinda upset wits you not comin' in to suppa."

"I'm sorry," says the old man, "I'm just watching the sun go down."

"Boss, the sun ain't goin' down over deah; it goin' down over yonder," says the servant.

The old man says, "I know, I know. I was just looking at how the sunlight was hitting those poplars there down the lane."

And as he looks down that road one last time before going in, he thinks he sees something. It looks like the figure of a man. It stumbles a few steps and then falls down; it gets up and stumbles a few more steps and falls down again. That old man lets out a war whoop like a Comanche Indian. "Yonder he comes," he yells, and down he runs to meet him. He meets that boy before he's halfway down the lane and throws his arms around him in a bear hug that likes to squeeze the life out of the kid.

Now like I said earlier, you could count on this young fellow to do what he said he was going to do. He had said he was going to tell his father he had sinned, and while daddy was hugging him, the boy started into his speech just like he said he was going to do. ["Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in thy sight, and am no more worthy to be called thy son"]{.class_4723} (vs. 21).

But before he could get to the part about being made a hired servant, the daddy was already barking out orders to the servants who had followed him down the lane, and everything the daddy says is a wonderful picture of salvation. ["Bring forth the best robe"]{.class_4723} (vs. 22): the sinner is stripped of the filthy rags of his self-righteousness and clothed in the righteousness of Jesus Christ.[ "Put a ring on his hand"]{.class_4723}: the ring is a birthstone signifying the restoration of sonship. ["And shoes on his feet"]{.class_4723}: signifying a new walk (Col. 2:6). ["And bring hither the fatted calf ]{.class_4723}\
[\...and let us eat"]{.class_4723} (vs. 23): he's not having to feed on the slop of the world anymore. ["And be merry":]{.class_4723} there's that joy and rejoicing again over a sinner repenting about which Jesus talked in verses 7 and 9--10.

["For this my son was dead, and is alive again"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_469} (vs. 29). Do you see why you can't press the parable doctrinally to you as a Christian? When you get saved, you do pass ["from death unto life"]{.class_4723} (John 5:24), but you don't become ["alive AGAIN"]{.class_4723} because you were never ["alive"]{.class_4723} spiritually to start with (Eph. 2:1).

["He was lost, and is found."]{.class_4723} The operative word throughout this chapter has been ["lost."]{.class_4723} Jesus defines the word ["found"]{.class_4723} in 19:10---["For the Son of man is come to seek and to SAVE that which was lost."]{.class_4723} When the Lord [finds]{.class_4725} you, He [saves]{.class_4725} you.

After the Titanic went down, there were two lists of passengers' names posted. One was a list of those who were [saved,]{.class_4725} and the other was a list of those who were [lost.]{.class_4725} It is a tragedy when a man loses his mind, his wealth, his health, his property, and his family. It is even a greater tragedy when a man loses his life, as those on the Titanic. But the greatest tragedy of all is when a man loses his [soul]{.class_4725} (Mark 8:37), for then he has lost [hope]{.class_4725} for all eternity.

["And they BEGAN to be merry."]{.class_4723} They ["began"]{.class_4723}; it never says they stopped. It's a picture of Heaven. One of the reasons you are going to need a glorified body in Heaven is because you couldn't stand the [joy ]{.class_4725}you will experience in the body you have now. Here on earth, the joy grows, it peaks, and it subsides; and it does that constantly. But up in Glory, it just keeps growing and growing and growing with no let up. If you didn't have a glorified body, you would literally burst with joy; you wouldn't be able to take it because there's no end to it.

Now in verse 25, the older brother returns home from working in the field. He was busy doing what he was supposed to be doing, but he obviously had not been praying for his younger brother to get right and come home like his daddy had been. It never occurred to him when he heard the music and dancing that it might have been over his brother being back.

["He heard musick and dancing"]{.class_4723} (vs. 25). It is verses like this one that the Charismatics use for bringing "the bumps and the grinds" into the worship service. When one of these fakirs came down to the Bayfront Auditorium here in Pensacola (Rodney Howard Brown, the inventor of the "holy laugh"), a group of our young men went down to see the sideshow. As they entered the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_470}building, the whole crowd was dancing to a rock band ("Christian," naturally) just like you would see in any "club" or discotheque in town. One of the young men got "in the flesh" and began doing some disco moves from his unsaved days, and some stupid Charismatic woman looked at that mess and said, "Oh look, he's got it." By "it" she meant the [Holy Ghost.]{.class_4725} That's the shape "Christianity" is in these days.

Now the first time dancing shows up in the Bible is in Exodus 15:20 where Miriam comes out with the women ["with timbrels and with dances"]{.class_4723} over Israel being delivered at the Red Sea---a type of salvation just like you find it here in verse 25. So technically, there is nothing wrong with dancing as long as it is done correctly and for the right reason. Scriptural dancing (e.g., David in 2 Sam. 6:16) is more akin to what the lame man did when he was healed in Acts 3:8---["walking, and leaping, and praising God."]{.class_4723} It is never men with women, and it never has any sexual connotation (like queers). That's the kind of dancing that went on at the golden calf (Exod. 32:19).

The kind of dancing that is going on here is a Jewish "hora." Other dances that could be considered legitimate would be an Irish jig, an old fashioned square dance, a highland fling, and some polkas. The less body contact there is, the less showing of the flesh, the less seduction of the opposite sex that goes on, the more legitimate the dance.

Now compared to today's dances, a waltz is tame. But you see, with a waltz, you have started the intimate contact of the sexes. The waltz leads to the tango, and when you get to that point, you are where you ought not to be.

Did you know that when Johann Strauss introduced his beautiful waltzes, there were riots in Vienna because the dances were considered to be "unChristian"---and that was by a bunch of lost Austrian Catholics! They were considered to be too sensuous, and when you see how low cut the women's dresses were, you can understand why they said that.

Of course, when you get into the tango and the rumba, the bodies of the couple are right together. From there, you get into the jitterbug, the bunny hop, the turkey trot, the grizzly bear, etc., where the animals are mating in the jungle. Those [aren't]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_471} Bible dances.

Back to Luke 15: ["And he called one of the servants, and asked what these things meant"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26). Now up to this point, you might be tempted to give the older brother a break. He comes in from a hard day's work in the fields and hears a party going on and can't figure out what the occasion is. All of that's perfectly normal under the circumstances. But look at how he reacts when the servant tells him that the celebration is over the return of his brother:[ "And he was angry, and would not go in"]{.class_4723} (vs. 28).

The older son wasn't looking or praying for his brother to return. Moreover, he wasn't so much angry with his brother as he was with his daddy. He was bitter and resentful of the attention his father was showing to his wayward son (vss. 29--30).

["Lo, these many years do I serve thee"]{.class_4723} (vs. 29). Well, that wasn't quite true. You see, he had gotten his inheritance at the same time his younger brother had gotten his (vs. 12). He was now the sole owner of his father's inheritance, so he was serving [himself]{.class_4725} as much as he was serving his father.

["Neither transgressed I at any time thy commandment."]{.class_4723} That wasn't true either. He had just transgressed his father's commandment [right then.]{.class_4725} His father had begged him to come in to the party (vs. 28), and he refused to go.

["Yet thou never gavest me a kid, that I might make merry with my friends: But as soon as this thy son was come, which hath devoured thy living with harlots, thou hast killed for him the fatted calf"]{.class_4723} (vss. 29--30). The older son is complaining because he believes the father is showing favoritism to the sinning brother. The idea is: "You wouldn't give me even a goat to have a party with my friends, but you'll slaughter a cow and have a big barbecue when this whoremonger comes back."

Again, everything the father had left belonged to the elder son (vs. 31). If he wanted to kill a kid and have a party with his friends, he could have done it at any time. Second, the younger son hadn't wasted his father's living; he had wasted his own. The father had given the boy his lawful inheritance, and the boy had squandered it. The father's ["living"]{.class_4723} hadn't been touched at all.

I heard a colored preacher really "shell the corn" on this passage one time. He said, "When dat prodigal son came home, his older brother wouldn't call him 'brother.' He called him ['thy son.']{.class_4723} Why didn't he say 'my brother'? He should'a called him brother cuz dey's both in de same family. \[Amen.\] He said, 'Why didn't you make a dinnah fo' me and my friends?' Well, in de fust place, maybe he had de kinda friends dat wouldn't appreciate a meal in de father's house. \[AMEN!\] De trouble is, you can pick yo' friends, but you can't pick yo' family. \[Amen!\] You may not like me, and maybe we will never get along, but deah's one thing dat if you don't like it, dat's too bad: [we both in de same family." ]{.class_4725}I got more out of that black man in ten minutes than I could ever get out of Osteen, Warren, or Schuller in a lifetime.

Now you see, that's good, [practical]{.class_4727} preaching. You can't beat it. But that's not the doctrine. The whole passage is to [Jews.]{.class_4727} That elder brother is those Jews back there in the Old Testament who kept the Law to the best of their ability and made the correct sacrifices for atonement where they blew it. That younger brother is a Jew who broke the Law right and left with no regard for what it said. You can read the whole thing laid out in Ezekiel 18. In that chapter, the whole thing is [works;]{.class_4727} there's no faith mentioned anywhere in the chapter. It's if you [do]{.class_4727} this, you'll live, and if you [do]{.class_4727} that, you'll die. But in the middle of all that, the Lord tells that sinning Israelite:

["But if the wicked will turn from all his sins that he hath committed, and keep all my statutes, and do that which is lawful and right, he shall surely live, he shall not die. All his transgressions that he hath committed, they shall not be mentioned unto him: in his righteousness that he hath done he shall live"]{.class_4723} (Ezek. 18:21--22).

That sinning Jew was given a chance to repent and get right (Ezek. 18:30--32), and that self-righteous Jew was warned that if he ever turned from the Law, he would die (Ezek. 18:26). That's the doctrine behind the Parable of the Prodigal Son.

Right now, those Jews, as a people, are lost and ["dead in trespasses and sins"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 2:1), and they're headed to Hell (see 13:28--29 and our comments on the passage). Of course, Billy Graham and the Pope won't tell you that, but it's so. An unsaved Jew []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3003.xhtml_page_473}goes to Hell the same as an unsaved Gentile. Being "God's chosen people" doesn't change that one way or another.

Right now, the state of the Jews is that they're guilty as a nation for killing the Lord Jesus Christ "and their own prophets" and rejecting the Gospel and trying to prevent their own people from accepting Christ (1 Thess. 2:14--15). Every one of them who rejects Jesus Christ is under the wrath of God (1 Thess. 2:16). They'll stay that way until Moses and Elijah show up in the Tribulation to straighten them out (Mal. 4:4--6). Any of them that are still alive when Jesus Christ shows back up at the Second Advent will repent of murdering their Messiah (Zech. 12:10), and they will all be saved (Rom. 11:25--27).

Until then, you are to love them and treat them right and try to get them saved even though they are your enemies when it comes to Jesus Christ and the Gospel (Rom. 11:28). Sure they're against Christians, and they're responsible for getting prayer and Bible reading kicked out of the public schools, and a lot of them are liberals and Socialists, and a few of them are international bankers, and all that stuff. But ["they are beloved for the fathers' sakes"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 11:28), and you aren't to go around killing them or persecuting them.

Jesus said, ["Woe unto the world because of offences! for it must needs be that offences come, but woe to that man by whom the offence cometh"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 18:7).

The Lord said He was going to use those Gentile nations to judge His people for their sins (Deut. 28), but in turn, God is going to judge the nations that persecute those Jews (Psa. 137:7--9). The offences have to come, but they don't have to come through [you.]{.class_4725} If they do, you're going to get it in the neck regardless of the fact that they have to come, so don't [you]{.class_4725} be the one bringing the offences.

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml}

# Chapter 16 {.heading_78-0}

16:1 And he said also unto his disciples, There was a certain rich man, which had a steward; and the same was accused unto him that he had wasted his goods.

2 And he called him, and said unto him, How is it that I hear this of thee? give an account of thy stewardship; for thou mayest be no longer steward.

3 Then the steward said within himself, What shall I do? for my lord taketh away from me the stewardship: I cannot dig; to beg I am ashamed.

4 I am resolved what to do, that, when I am put out of the stewardship, they may receive me into their houses.

5 So he called every one of his lord's debtors unto him, and said unto the first, How much owest thou unto my lord?

6 And he said, An hundred measures of oil. And he said unto him, Take thy bill, and sit down quickly, and write fifty.

7 Then said he to another, And how much owest thou? And he said, An hundred measures of wheat. And he said unto him, Take thy bill, and write fourscore.

8 And the lord commended the unjust steward, because he had done wisely: for the children of this world are in their generation wiser than the children of light.

9 And I say unto you, Make to yourselves friends of the mammon of unrighteousness; that, when ye fail, they may receive you into everlasting habitations.

10 He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much: and he that is unjust in the least is unjust also in much.

11 If therefore ye have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will commit to your trust the true riches?

12 And if ye have not been faithful in that which is another man's, who shall give you that which is your own?

This is the Parable of the Unjust Steward. The unjust steward stands in contrast to the ["faithful and wise steward"]{.class_4723} of 12:42 (see our remarks on that verse). A steward was a servant entrusted with the goods of his master; he managed the household. The term was adopted on the big ocean liners for the officer in charge of protecting the passengers' valuables. Later on, it was applied to stewardesses on airplanes who saw to the passengers' needs.

In the New Testament, a bishop is called ["the steward of God"]{.class_4723} (Tit. 1:7) because he has been given the responsibility of overseeing God's household here on earth (1 Tim. 3:15). He has been entrusted with ["the mysteries of God"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 4:1--2) and ["the manifold grace of God"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 4:10). When it comes to ["the mysteries of God,"]{.class_4723} there are seven mysteries that have been entrusted to the Body of Christ, and you can get a full study of those mysteries in our sermon booklet [Seven Mysteries.]{.class_4725} If every preacher since Pentecost had been a faithful steward in proclaiming those mysteries as the Bible lays them out, you wouldn't have half the troubles in the world that you have today.

Take, for instance, ["the mystery of godliness"]{.class_4723} in 1 Timothy 3:16---the [Incarnation.]{.class_4725} That would do away with 900 million Mohammedans just like that. Bush's "War on Terror" would be over before it started. But it's taken out of the [NIV, NASV, NRSV, ESV,]{.class_4725} etc. Somebody wasn't a faithful steward.

Here's another good one: the mystery of the restoration of Israel (Rom. 11:25). If every preacher had been faithful in proclaiming that truth, there would go all the trouble in the Middle East. The trouble in the Middle East is over whether Israel has a right to the land God gave it (Zech. 12:1--3). If all the preachers for the last 2,000 years had preached what God said about the matter, you wouldn't have the USA, the UN, the EU, NATO, the Arab League, and the Muslim Brotherhood getting ready to jump headlong into Zephaniah 3:8.

Now, in this parable, the steward was accused of wasting his master's goods (vs. 1). The master "calls him on the carpet" and says, ["give an account of thy stewardship; for thou mayest be no longer steward"]{.class_4723} (vs. 2).

That's a great statement. That's what [every]{.class_4725} Christian will hear from his Lord one day: ["Give an account of thy stewardship."]{.class_4723}

Paul said, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_476}["so then every one of us shall give account of himself to God"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 14:12). He said, ["For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ; that every one may receive the things done in his body\...whether it be good or bad"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 5:10).

"Ruckman, I gave you a wife, ten children, sixteen grandchildren, and two great grandchildren; what did you do with them? I gave you 600 church members; what did you do with them? I gave you 150 students; what did you do with them? I gave you a Bookstore there with all kinds of tapes and books; what did you do with them? I gave you the ability to draw; what did you do with it?"

Someday I'm going to hit the Judgment Seat of Christ and give account for everything God has given me. I won't be able to dump the blame on anybody else; it will be aimed right at [me.]{.class_4725}

One time, the pastor of a small church was talking to his buddy who pastored one of these big churches with a couple of thousand members, and he said, "I wish I had a church like yours. You have 2,000 in Sunday school, and I'm only running fifty." The other preacher, who had a great deal of experience in the ministry, said, "Don't worry about that, my brother. At the Judgment Seat of Christ I think you'll find you have plenty to give account for with the fifty you've got." That's true. The more a man is given, the more that is required of him (see 12:48).

["Thou mayest be no longer steward."]{.class_4723} Why? Because you'll be [dead.]{.class_4725} I don't care who you are, one day you are going to be called on to lay down your stewardship. If the Lord tarries ([and I hope He doesn't]{.class_4725}), one of these days I'm going to hear Him say, "Time's up, Ruckman. Stop what you're doing and come over here and lie down in a hole in the ground."

Have you got a house? You won't have to worry about paying the mortgage on it anymore or maintaining it. Have you got a wife and kids? Someone else can take care of them now. Have you got a church? They can get another preacher; you're done. ["Thou mayest be no longer steward."]{.class_4723}

I sat down at the table, not too long back, and wrote out the names of all the preachers who were preaching when I got in the ministry and all the ones who have gone on home to Heaven since []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_477}I've been in the ministry. Of all the preachers with whom I started out, 63 of them are now dead, and of those 63, I knew 35 of them personally. The man who led me to the Lord, Hugh Pyle, is on that list; the Lord took him home a couple of years ago. Glenn Schunk, the fellow who got me into the ministry, passed away many years ago. My old buddy Edmund Dinant from up in Flint, Michigan, is gone. I preached a message one time on "The Vacant Chair" up in Beauchamp Vick's church in Detroit and told the people, "One day you're going to look up here on the platform, and Bro. Vick's chair is going to be empty." Within a year of when I said that, Beauchamp Vick died. And like I said, one day if the Lord tarries, you will see Peter S. Ruckman's name on that list too. ["Thou mayest be no longer steward."]{.class_4723}

Now when the steward loses his stewardship, here is his predicament: ["What shall I do?\...I cannot dig; to beg I am ashamed"]{.class_4723} (vs. 3). Maybe he was physically unable to dig ditches---bad back, weak constitution, etc.---the text doesn't say. But look at the next statement: ["to beg I am ashamed."]{.class_4723} The real problem was pride: too good to do manual labor, too proud to beg.

You have about three generations now who don't know what a pick axe, a hoe, a shovel, a sledge hammer, or a double-bladed axe is. That's because they've never done a lick of work with any of those tools.

When John Rawlings' boys were about ten to twelve years old, they were playing basketball out in the driveway and got the ball stuck up in the hoop. They went inside to Mrs. Rawlings and said, "Momma, we can't get our basketball out of the hoop; it's stuck." She said, "Why don't you get a hoe handle and poke it out?" Those boys said, "A what?" Those boys didn't know what a hoe was.

A mother said to her daughter one time, "Your hair looks like a mop." That daughter said, "What's a mop?" If you don't know what a hoe and a mop are, there is something greatly missing in your education.

The plan with which this unjust steward comes up is to cut the debts of his master's creditors so they'll be indebted to him and take him in after he gets the boot from the stewardship. The accounting hadn't been finished yet, so he still had the authority as the steward to manage the master's money. But he had to work ["quickly"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_478} (vs. 6) before he got caught.

Of course what he did was cheat his master (which was why he was losing the stewardship to begin with), but his master commended him for being smart in the way he handled the situation. He [didn't]{.class_4725} commend him for being [honest]{.class_4725} or [faithful.]{.class_4725} So when Jesus applies the parable to His disciples (vs. 1), He's not telling them to be crooked like the unjust steward. He's telling them that sometimes unsaved men are smarter than saved men (vs. 8), and that the disciples should learn a lesson from that.

Some of those guys who worked for the Mafia were geniuses at what they did. Maier Lanski was the treasurer for five Mafia families. He didn't handle anything less than five billion dollars a year. That fellow never kept any books on the transactions; he kept account of everything [in his head.]{.class_4725} The Feds tried him three times for income tax evasion and couldn't pin anything on him. That old boy died of old age and never spent any time in prison. He was a Russian Jew, so he couldn't be a "capo" (an officer in the Mafia); but he was brilliant, boy, and the Mafia used him to handle the money from drug trafficking all around the world.

And here's the part that just messes everyone up: ["And I say unto you, Make to yourselves friends of the mammon of unrighteousness; that, when ye fail, they may receive you into everlasting habitations"]{.class_4723} (vs. 9). Most people read that statement as you are to be friendly with money. It's that little two-letter word ["of" ]{.class_4723}that messes everyone up.

Let me ask you something, the love of God---is that you loving God or is that God loving you? Well, it depends on whether you make God the object or the subject of the love; same thing with the fear of God. The reason the Campbellites splash water around in Titus 3:5 is because they make ["the washing of regeneration"]{.class_4723} the sinner being regenerated by [washing.]{.class_4725} Washing isn't the [subject;]{.class_4725} it's the [object.]{.class_4725} It's the ["regeneration"]{.class_4723} that does the ["washing."]{.class_4723} How do you know that to be so? Because the ["renewing of the Holy Ghost"]{.class_4723} isn't the Holy Ghost being renewed; it's the Holy Ghost doing the renewing. Some people can't comprehend sixth grade English.

Jesus isn't telling His disciples to make friends with money. He told them back in 12:15 to ["beware of covetousness."]{.class_4723} Bishops and deacons are not to be ["greedy of filthy lucre"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 3:3, 8 cf. Tit. 1:7; 1 Pet. 5:2): that's [money.]{.class_4725} Paul tells you not to ["trust in uncertain riches, but in the living God"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:17), and ["the love of money is the root of all evil"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:10).

Here in verse 9, Jesus is saying to make friends by using money to help others out financially. You can't buy true friendship, but if you've been generous with your money while you had it, the people you've helped will, generally speaking, try to help you out when you're "down on your luck." The ["everlasting habitations"]{.class_4723} have nothing to do with everlasting life in Heaven or everlasting death in Hell. It's another way of saying "until the end of your life." It's like the expression ["everlasting hills"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 49:26). The ["hills"]{.class_4723} aren't ["everlasting"]{.class_4723} in the sense that they will never end, because they do (2 Pet. 3:10). They're everlasting in the sense that as far as mortal men are concerned, the hills have always been around since the creation, and they will always be around until the Lord blows the whole thing up. So Jesus is telling you to use your money to make friends who will take care of you for the rest of your life.

When it comes to the [morality]{.class_4725} of the unjust steward, Jesus isn't telling you to emulate what he did at all. Look at verse 10. The Lord contrasts being ["faithful"]{.class_4723} and being ["unjust."]{.class_4723} The ["least"]{.class_4723} in the verse is [money]{.class_4725} (vs. 11--["unrighteous mammon"]{.class_4723}). If you can't be faithful with a little thing like money (and the unjust steward wasn't), the Lord isn't going to trust you with ["true riches"]{.class_4723} (which have nothing to do with money). The Lord never does glorify money. He calls it ["mammon"]{.class_4723}; the word [mammon]{.class_4725} is the name of a Persian [false god.]{.class_4725}

["He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much"]{.class_4723} (vs. 10). The Lord's going to test you on the little things before He'll trust you with the big stuff. He'll give you little tasks to do, like making ends meet and memorizing Greek verbs and doing the laundry and preaching on the street where folks make fun of you. He'll see if you're faithful to visitation, trying to find the house numbers in the dark. The Lord watches you to see how you handle those little things.

Here I am up past ninety somewhere, living in my fifth house on a big piece of land with a pond. The church is all paid for; the school is all paid for; the gym is all paid for; the Bookstore is all paid for. There's over 120 of my books in print, not to mention all the Bible studies on MP3 and the sermons on DVD and the artwork going out. I've got tracts in at least five different languages that I know of. But before you get envious or jealous of all that and start thinking that's the ministry, just remember [that's not how I started out.]{.class_4725}

I started out in a 25-foot trailer with an ice box---[not a refrigerator.]{.class_4725} It was a box where you had to haul the blocks of ice to it with a pair of tongs and put them in a tray at the bottom. I had no hot running water; I had to boil the water on a little heater. It didn't have a bathroom in it; there was a community bathroom down at the end of the trailer court. I lived there with a wife and (eventually) three children for four years, going to school at Bob Jones University year round.

Going through that, I only had two suits, four shirts, and two pairs of shoes. At one point (when I stopped tithing---see the full account in our remarks on Mal. 3:8 in that Commentary), I like to have starved to death. I had to send the wife and kids back to the farm in Alabama because there was no food in the trailer. I was going to school on the G.I. Bill at \$35.00 a week.

Through all of that, I was out on the streets every weekend preaching and winning souls. When the G.I. Bill was up and I graduated from Bob Jones, I had no job and no prospects. I was doing small-time evangelism, taking little meetings here and there, but that was it.

The Lord was watching me go through all of that, and do you know what He did? He gave me a revival in a church in Bay Minette, Alabama that shook that whole town to pieces. That opened up meetings for me in the big churches like Rawlings' and Vick's. The Christians in that church in Bay Minette, in gratitude for what the Lord had done through me in that meeting, built me a ranch-style house. That was the first house in which I lived since I left home to join the Army.

I have seen that kind of thing over and over again. If you are doing what God told you to do, the way He told you to do it, He's []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_481}watching you and checking you. He keeps records, and when the time comes for your need to be met, He'll meet the need. He more than met mine. He watched me to see if I would do the little things, and as I did, He gradually gave me a little bit better.

You take all those books in the Bookstore. Do you know how that started out? I had to type my first three books ([The Mark of the Beast, The Kingdom of God vs. the Kingdom of Heaven, ]{.class_4725}and [The Christian's Handbook of Manuscript Evidence]{.class_4725}) on a stationary, manual (not electric) Underwood typewriter. I had no lithographer to set the type, so I set the type myself on a machine called a Verityper. They don't even make those anymore. You had to count the increments per line and lay out the type yourself while taking into account the spaces between words. I got a burden to produce tracts and got a little mimeograph machine with a drum, roller, and purple ink. It made a lousy looking tract, but the Lord knew I wanted to get the Gospel out. So if you go over to that Bookstore today, you will find computers to lay out the print on a screen with a keyboard, a machine to transfer the manuscript to film for printing so you have a professional-looking job, a Heidelberg two-color press, and 25-foot shelves of my books.

["He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much."]{.class_4723} I don't know if God will treat you like He's treated me, but I do know that if you're faithful in the small things He gives you to do, that He'll give you something bigger until He's got you doing exactly what He wants you to do. And if you're not, well, He knows that too and won't trust you with anything bigger than what you started out with because ["he that is unjust in the least is unjust also in much."]{.class_4723}

Verse 12:[ "And if ye have not been faithful in that which is another man's, who shall give you that which is your own."]{.class_4723} That's a great verse for the Christian on the Judgment Seat of Christ. In this case, the "other man" is [God.]{.class_4725} He has made you a steward and given you some things of His to take care of here on earth. As you serve Him faithfully, you are earning rewards up in Heaven. At the Judgment Seat of Christ, you'll get ["that which is your own."]{.class_4723}

13 No servant can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon.

14 And the Pharisees also, who were covetous, heard all these things: and they derided him.

15 And he said unto them, Ye are they which justify yourselves before men; but God knoweth your hearts: for that which is highly esteemed among men is abomination in the sight of God.

16 The law and the prophets were until John: since that time the kingdom of God is preached, and every man presseth into it.

17 And it is easier for heaven and earth to pass, than one tittle of the law to fail.

18 Whosoever putteth away his wife, and marrieth another, committeth adultery: and whosoever marrieth her that is put away from her husband committeth adultery.

["No servant can serve two masters"]{.class_4723} (vs. 13). That's a verse against having more than one wife (just kidding, ladies). Seriously, though, the verse shows you that you cannot have [two authorities.]{.class_4725} You can only have [one authority.]{.class_4725} You can't follow both a [King James]{.class_4725} Bible and an [ASV]{.class_4725} (or [NIV,]{.class_4725} [NASV]{.class_4725}, [NRSV]{.class_4725}, [NKJV]{.class_4725}, [ESV, ]{.class_4725}etc.). You can act like you do, but you aren't going to get away with it. In a pinch, you are going to go one way or the other.

Now the application Christ makes in verse 13 is to what He just got through teaching back up in verses 10--12. ["Ye cannot serve God and mammon."]{.class_4723} Some Christians think you can but you [can't]{.class_4725}.[ ]{.class_4725}You can't love God and money at the same time (1 Tim. 6:9--11). If you love God, you won't love money, and if you love money, you won't love God. You might pretend that you do, but when God and money part ways, the one you follow is the one you love and serve.

Here are two men walking down the street, and behind them is a dog following them. You say, "To whom does the dog belong?" I'll tell you one sure way to find out: you let one fellow go one way and the other guy go another way, and []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_483}[the dog will follow its master.]{.class_4725}

["And the Pharisees also, WHO WERE COVETOUS\...derided him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 14). There's the problem---[covetousness.]{.class_4725} Jesus wasn't talking to the Pharisees at all; He was talking to His disciples (vs. 1). But there were a bunch of Pharisees standing there listening to Him, and they got the message loud and clear. If you throw a rock into a pack of dogs, the [hit dog]{.class_4725} will always yelp.

If you are a preacher, you are familiar with the scene of verse 14. "That preacher was preaching right at me." No, he was just preaching what God told him to preach, and you just happened to be in the line of fire.

["And he said unto them, Ye are they which justify yourselves before men"]{.class_4723} (vs. 15). The reason those Pharisees lived right was ["to be seen of men"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 23:5). John wrote that ["they loved the praise of men more than the praise of God"]{.class_4723} (John 12:43).

["But God knoweth your hearts."]{.class_4723} That Bible says, ["the Lord seeth not as man seeth, for man looketh on the outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart"]{.class_4723} (1 Sam. 16:7). Solomon said, ["thou only knowest the hearts of the children of men"]{.class_4723} (2 Chron. 6:30). You may be able to fool men, but you won't fool God.

And then a great statement which ties the whole world up in one knot and pitches it out the window (which is why you will never hear it preached in the average church): ["for that which is highly esteemed among men is abomination in the sight of God."]{.class_4723} That is the [absolute standard]{.class_4725} for judging everything in this world: all art, music, literature, education, lifestyles, news media, fashion, science, philosophy, events, religions, and people. If the world thinks highly of it, God thinks it [stinks.]{.class_4725}

And the opposite is true too. If God thinks highly of something, the world thinks it stinks. That's why the world wants nothing to do with your Saviour or that Book: God has exalted both above everything it holds dear (Phil. 2:9; Psa. 138:2). The world wants your [love]{.class_4725} (2 Tim. 4:10; 1 John 2:15) and your [conformity]{.class_4725} (Rom. 12:1), and if you are ["a friend of the world,"]{.class_4723} you are ["the enemy of God"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_484} (James 4:4). And if you are the friend of God, you are the enemy of this world.

["That which is highly esteemed among men is abomination in the sight of God."]{.class_4723} Does the world think highly of you? Pick up a newspaper or magazine; look at a TV set: about whom are they bragging? Billy Graham, the Pope, Elvis Presley, "Princess Di," "Slick Willie" Clinton, JFK, Martin Luther King Jr., Mahatma Gandhi? I don't care who it is---a good fellow or a bad fellow, a preacher or a junkie---if the media lifts someone up till he doesn't have an enemy in the world, [he's a dirty, rotten rascal.]{.class_4725}

["Woe unto you, when all men shall speak well of you!"]{.class_4723} (6:26). Bob Jones Sr. used to say, "The amount of publicity any man gets is directly in proportion to the extent to which he can be [controlled."]{.class_4725} If the news media is "puffing" you, Christian (like it did Billy Graham), that means you are a dirty compromiser. You are a stench in God's nostrils; He would just as soon spit you out as look at you (Rev. 3:16).

Verse 16:[ "The law and the prophets were until John."]{.class_4723} That's Christ laying down a dispensational boundary in the Scriptures. ["The law and the prophets"]{.class_4723}---that's the Old Testament. So the Old Testament goes up to the death of John the Baptist. John was beheaded at some point just before chapter 9 here in Luke (see our remarks under 9:7--9).

["Since that time the kingdom of God is preached."]{.class_4723} When John the Baptist and Jesus Christ start out their ministries, they're both preaching the Kingdom of Heaven (Matt. 3:2, 4:17)---a [physical]{.class_4725} kingdom. But now that John is gone, the Kingdom of Heaven assumes a mystery form (see chap. 7 of [The Sure Word of Prophecy]{.class_4725}), and it is the [spiritual]{.class_4725} kingdom---the Kingdom of God---that Jesus Christ and the disciples preach. So [technically]{.class_4725} speaking, the Old Testament ends at the end of chapter 8 here in Luke.

Problem: the New Testament doesn't begin until Luke 23 with ["the death of the testator"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 9:16--17). So from Luke 9--23 you have an intermediate period where there is an overlap of the Old and New Testaments. The only Scriptures anyone has are the Old Testament, and that is by what everyone is operating, but elements of the New Testament keep popping up here and there in preparation []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_485}for the time when it is in full force. So the interim between the end of the Old Testament with John's death and the beginning of the New Testament with the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ is a dangerous place to get your doctrine.

["It is easier for heaven and earth to pass, than one tittle of the law to fail"]{.class_4723} (vs. 17). The ["tittle"]{.class_4723} is the small projection on Hebrew letters like [Beth]{.class_4725} and [Daleth.]{.class_4725} It is connected with the smallest letter of the Hebrew alphabet, [Yod]{.class_4725} (see the header to Psa. 119:7 in most [King James]{.class_4725} Bibles)---called a ["jot"]{.class_4723} in Matthew 5:18. The importance of verse 17 and Matthew 5:18 is that not all of the Old Testament was fulfilled at Christ's First Advent (a misinterpretation of Matt. 5:17 by the Amillennialists and Preterists). See that passing of ["heaven and earth" ]{.class_4723}in both passages? That's 2 Peter 3:10--12; that's Revelation 20:11. Old Testament prophecy runs all the way to the creation of the New Heavens and New Earth (Isa. 66). All the prophecies about a Jewish Messiah sitting on the throne of David with Jerusalem as the capital of a literal, visible, physical kingdom await fulfillment. That comes to about 500 prophecies that have yet to be fulfilled, and Jesus said they're all going to come through right on the money.

That's not all. You talk about a radical promise of the preservation of the Scriptures, verse 17 is it. The promise isn't just that the WORDS of Scripture won't pass away (Matt. 24:35); it's that the tiny projections that distinguish one Hebrew letter from another won't pass away. Now if the Holy Spirit can do that, do you think He's going to have any problem giving you the words He wants you to have in other languages? He gave them in about fourteen languages at Pentecost (Acts 2:9--11). Why would He have a problem giving you the words He wants you to have in [English,]{.class_4725} especially when it's the universal language of the last days? If you've got an [Authorized Version,]{.class_4725} you've got the words the Holy Spirit wants you to have in English; and if you've got an [NIV, NASV, NKJV, RSV, NRSV, ESV, CEV,]{.class_4725} or anything else, you [haven't]{.class_4725} because they leave out anywhere from 500--64,000 words of what God said.
