Now, verse 18 is used by the Catholics, the Fundamentalists, and the Holiness to prove that if you get divorced and remarried that you are committing adultery because you have two living wives or []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_486}three living husbands or whatever. This is the perfect example of the principle of Biblical hermeneutics that you never interpret a [complete]{.class_4725} statement by an [incomplete]{.class_4725} statement. Verse 18 is [not]{.class_4725} the complete statement on divorce and remarriage.

To get the [complete]{.class_4725} statement on the subject from the mouth of Jesus Christ, you have to pick up Matthew 19:9---["Whosoever shall put away his wife, EXCEPT IT BE FOR FORNICATION, and shall marry another committeth adultery,"]{.class_4723} etc. So there [is]{.class_4725} an exception, according to the Lord Jesus Christ, where you can get a divorce and get remarried and NOT commit adultery. I'll give you another one. Look at 1 Corinthians 7:27--28.

["Art thou bound unto a wife? seek not to be loosed ]{.class_4723}\[don't get a divorce if you don't have to\][. Art thou loosed from a wife ]{.class_4723}\[a divorce\][? seek not a wife ]{.class_4723}\[stay single if you can\][. But and if thou marry ]{.class_4723}\[a remarriage after the divorce of vs. 27\][, THOU HAST NOT SINNED."]{.class_4723}

What's the context? The [desertion of an unbelieving spouse.]{.class_4725} First Corinthians 7:15 says, ["if the unbelieving depart, let him depart. A brother or a sister IS NOT UNDER BONDAGE in such cases."]{.class_4723} If a spouse deserts you, you aren't married to that person anymore, so you can't have "two living wives." And if you get remarried, ["THOU HAST NOT SINNED."]{.class_4723} Is that clear?

You say, "Ruckman, are you saying it's OK to get divorced?" [No, I'm not.]{.class_4725} If you can work things out, [stay married.]{.class_4725} But if there's any form of fornication involved (like adultery or sex perversion), or if your spouse leaves you and won't come back, you have a perfect right, [according to the Lord Jesus and the Apostle Paul,]{.class_4725} to get a divorce and to remarry as long as you remarry someone ["in the Lord"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 7:39). And if you get remarried, you are not an adulterer (or an adulteress), and you don't have more than one spouse.

19 There was a certain rich man, which was clothed in purple and fine linen, and fared sumptuously every day:

20 And there was a certain beggar named Lazarus, which was laid at his gate, full of sores,

21 And desiring to be fed with the crumbs which fell []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_487}from the rich man's table: moreover the dogs came and licked his sores.

22 And it came to pass, that the beggar died, and was carried by the angels into Abraham's bosom: the rich man also died, and was buried;

23 And in hell he lift up his eyes, being in torments, and seeth Abraham afar off, and Lazarus in his bosom.

24 And he cried and said, Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and cool my tongue; for I am tormented in this flame.

25 But Abraham said, Son, remember that thou in thy lifetime receivedst thy good things, and likewise Lazarus evil things: but now he is comforted, and thou art tormented.

26 And beside all this, between us and you there is a great gulf fixed: so that they which would pass from hence to you cannot; neither can they pass to us, that would come from thence.

27 Then he said, I pray thee therefore, father, that thou wouldest send him to my father's house:

28 For I have five brethren; that he may testify unto them, lest they also come into this place of torment.

29 Abraham saith unto him, They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them.

30 And he said, Nay, father Abraham: but if one went unto them from the dead, they will repent.

31 And he said unto him, If they hear not Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead.

This is the famous account of the Rich Man and Lazarus. A lot of expositors identify it as a parable, and it's [not]{.class_4725} a parable. The Lord never calls it a parable.

You say, "Well, Ruckman, He doesn't say the story of the Unjust Steward is a parable either." That's very true, but look at verse 1: ["And he said ALSO unto his disciples."]{.class_4723} The story of the Unjust []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_488}Steward is a continuation of what Jesus was saying in chapter 15, and those [were]{.class_4725} parables (see 15:3). Besides that, there are things that mark out the Unjust Steward as a parable and the Rich Man and Lazarus as an actual event that took place.

1\. There is clear typology in the Unjust Steward that is not found in the Rich Man and Lazarus. You might make Abraham a type of God the Father, but that is about it. The rich man doesn't "represent" anything but a rich man who died lost and went to Hell. Lazarus isn't a picture of anything but an Old Testament saint who went to Paradise. Hell fire is [Hell fire;]{.class_4725} it's not a "type" of anything. The surviving brothers of the rich man are actual Jews who reject the Old Testament.

2\. Jesus draws doctrinal and spiritual applications from the Unjust Steward as He does in all of His parables that He interprets (see our comments on the Parable of the Sower in chap. 8). The story of the Rich Man and Lazarus is presented as a matter of fact.

3\. The story of the Rich Man and Lazarus contains the [proper names of people]{.class_4725} (Lazarus, Abraham, Moses), which no parable does.

It was this passage of Scripture that got me studying Greek and Hebrew so I could find "the pig in the poke" when it came to the Bible correctors. I was in Dr. Charles Brokenshire's class at Bob Jones University. "Brokey" could speak, read, and write about eight different languages; and when it came to expositing the Holy Scriptures, he didn't have the sense God gave a brass monkey. He was a baby-sprinkling, five-point TULIP, Calvinistic Presbyterian (BJU has never been a Baptist school; it was and [is]{.class_4725} interdenominational).

One day in class, Dr. Brokenshire said, "Now in the Parable of Dives and Lazarus," and I raised my hand. "Who's 'Dives'?" I asked, "I don't see any 'Dives' in the passage."

"Well," said Dr. Brokenshire, "Dives is the Latin name for the rich man."

"Oh," I said, and he started in again. "Now in the Parable of Dives and Lazarus," and I raised my hand.

"Doctor, why do you keep calling it a parable when it's not?" I asked.

"What makes you think it's not a parable?" he asked in return.

"Well," I said, "Jesus doesn't say it's a parable. Every time He gives a parable, He says, ['he spake this parable' ]{.class_4723}or ['he spake a parable.']{.class_4723} Luke 16:19 isn't prefaced with those words."

"Brokey" coughed nervously and rearranged some papers on his desk. "Well, it is a parable," he said.

"Then why does Ezekiel say that people will call the truth about Hell just a 'parable'?" I asked.

Brokenshire stamped his feet underneath his desk like a two-year-old brat and cried, "What do you mean by THAT, Mr. Ruckman?"

"Well," I said, "Ezekiel said that when he got to preaching about Hell (Ezek. 20:47--48) that the people would say, ['Ah Lord GOD!\...Doth he not speak PARABLES?']{.class_4723} (Ezek. 20:49)."

And "Brokey" threw a little temper tantrum again and stomped his feet and screamed in a high-pitched voice, "Mr. Ruckman! have you studied Greek?"

"No, sir," I said.

"Have you studied Hebrew?"

"No, sir."

"Then I suggest you keep quiet until you know what you're talking about."

"Yes, sir."

And all of the class, who had been staring at me through that whole exchange, smiled knowingly and looked back to their textbooks and said to themselves, "That takes care of that character."

But it takes a lot more than some Bible-perverting little child throwing a "hissy fit" to shut up Ruckman. I sat back and said to myself, "So that's how the snow drifts, does it? You have to know Greek and Hebrew to spot falsehood, do you? Well, then, I'll just take Hebrew and Greek, and we'll continue our little discussion."

And that's what I did. It was a fleshy decision, I'll admit, but God used it. I found "the pig in the poke," and I haven't let go in sixty years.

So here we go with the text. Verse 19: ["There was a certain rich man, which was clothed in purple and fine linen, and fared sumptuously every day."]{.class_4723} Obviously the ["purple"]{.class_4723} is []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_490}referring to purple clothing such as was put on Christ at His crucifixion (John 19:2, 5), but it's given as the color because it was a dye taken from shell fish. It was produced in Greece (Ezek. 27:7) and Asia Minor (Acts 16:14). It was a sign of royalty and great wealth.

["Fared sumptuously"]{.class_4723} ---it's a reference to what he ate. In fancy restaurants, the menu is called the "bill of fare." This fellow lived "high on the hog and low on the chicken," so to speak.

The ["rich man"]{.class_4723} of verse 19 is contrasted with the ["certain beggar"]{.class_4723} in verse 20. Now in the story, the rich man goes to Hell and the beggar goes to Paradise, but the text never says that the rich man was bad or that the beggar was good. This sets the [doctrinal]{.class_4725} interpretation in the future during the Tribulation when the only people who have the money are those who take the Mark of the Beast and are damned for it (Rev. 14:9--11). In the Tribulation, the poor folks are the ones who don't take the mark and can't ["buy or sell"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 13:16--17). We have commented on this already in our remarks under 6:20--21 (which see).

Verse 22:[ "And it came to pass, that the beggar died, and was carried by the angels into Abraham's bosom."]{.class_4723} Now I don't know if you realize what you're reading or not, but here you have the first clear picture in the Bible of what happens to a soul after death. All throughout the Old Testament you have veiled hints at what goes on. The Old Testament talks about the patriarchs being ["gathered to their people"]{.class_4723} at death (Gen. 35:29, 49:33; Num. 20:24, 26, 27:13, 31:2; Deut. 32:50). The wisest man in the world could give you no definitive statement on the afterlife, for as far as the natural revelation he had ["under the sun,"]{.class_4723} when you died, they put you to bed with a shovel (Eccl. 9:10) and your spirit returns to God (Eccl. 3:21, 12:7) and that's it (Eccl. 9:5); there's no mention of the soul because in the Old Testament, the soul was equated with physical life (see our comments on Gen. 12:13 in that Commentary).

No-Hellers, like the Jehovah's Witnesses and the Seventh-day Adventists, like books like Genesis and Ecclesiastes because they give an [incomplete]{.class_4725} revelation of life after death. There is no [Christian doctrine]{.class_4725} on the afterlife in those books. When the [AV]{.class_4725} translates the Hebrew word [sheol]{.class_4725} as ["the grave"]{.class_4723} (see Gen. 42:38, 44:29, 31; 1 Sam. 2:6), it's not a reference to a hole in the ground; it's the place of [the dead]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_491}---whether they were saved dead or lost dead (in an Old Testament sense). It is not until you get to Luke 16 that you find out all that is involved in ["sheol."]{.class_4725} It is the Lord Jesus Christ Himself who lifts back the vail of death and reveals what happened to the dead in the Old Testament.

All right, ["the beggar died, and was carried by the angels into Abraham's bosom."]{.class_4723} As I said, this is an Old Testament setting; there is no indication in the Pauline Epistles that angels carry the soul of the dead Christian to Heaven. The New Testament statement is: ["absent from the body\...present with the Lord"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 5:8), but how you get from one place to the other is never elaborated on.

However, I will give you a testimony, for whatever it is worth. I don't teach this as doctrine, but I believe there is definitely something to it. I have gone to the hospital to visit a saved guy there who was dying. He invited me and my visitation partner to come in but not to bother the young man over by the window. [There was no man over by the window.]{.class_4725} We asked that dying Christian if the young man he saw had wings, and he said [no.]{.class_4725} I have had situations like that happen on more than one occasion.

["The beggar died, and was carried by the angels into Abraham's bosom."]{.class_4723} This is Old Testament, so Lazarus doesn't go to Heaven; he goes to Abraham's Bosom. Now where's Abraham's Bosom? Well, it's the place where Abraham was. We capitalize Abraham's Bosom because we think of it as a place, but the picture is Lazarus laying his head on the chest of Abraham like John did with Christ at the Last Supper (John 13:23).

Abraham was the father of the Jewish people (Luke 3:8; John 8:39), so in the Old Testament, when a saved Jew died, he went where his father was. And it was not just saved Jews, it was saved Gentiles as well, because Abraham received the imputed righteousness of God when he was an uncircumcised Gentile. Paul says he is ["the father of all them that believe, though they be not circumcised"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 4:11).

As the "father of the faithful," Abraham was given the promise to ["be the heir of the world"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 4:13). The promise to Abraham was not just Palestine, the Golan Heights, the Sinaitic Peninsula, and []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_492}all the land from the Jordan to the Euphrates (Gen. 15:18); Abraham and his seed eventually get the [whole earth.]{.class_4725}

Mohammed said that the earth was made for him and Allah ([Holy Hadith,]{.class_4733} Bukhari, Vol. IV, no. 392). [What a conceited ass!]{.class_4733} Jehovah said it belonged to [Him]{.class_4733} (Psa. 24:1) and His [Son]{.class_4733} (Zech. 14:9 cf. Isa. 9:6--7), and He gives it to the [Jews;]{.class_4733} not any Arab, Moslem, or demon-possessed, epileptic fornicator.

So Abraham's Bosom is downstairs in ["the heart of the earth"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 12:40). It is where Christ's soul went when He was buried to bring up the Old Testament saints at His Resurrection (Acts 2:31; Eph. 4:8--10; Matt. 27:52--53). Jesus called it ["paradise"]{.class_4723} before His death (24:43), and today, ["paradise"]{.class_4723} is "upstairs" (2 Cor. 12:4; Rev. 2:7).

["The rich man also died, and was buried; And in hell he lift up his eyes, being in torments"]{.class_4723} (vss. 22--23). So Hell is not the grave; that is, unless people are able to open their eyes in the grave and look around and see things. Nobody is ["in torments"]{.class_4723} in the grave.

One of our young men was witnessing to a Jehovah's Witness, and the JW told our student that Hell was just the grave. "I wish I had known that yesterday," said our young man. "Why is that?" asked the JW. "Because my sister died yesterday, and if I had known the grave was Hell, I would have hung her body from a tree," he replied. (His sister hadn't really died; he was just answering ["a fool according to his folly,"]{.class_4723} Prov. 26:5.)

Of course, the new "Bibles" omit the word ["hell"]{.class_4723} so as not to upset their readers. Instead, they transliterate the Greek word [hades.]{.class_4725} Having done that, what did they accomplish? The scholars say, "Well, it's more accurate. [Gehenna]{.class_4725} is Hell (Mark 9:47). [Hades]{.class_4725} is just a temporary holding place."

Really? [Gehenna]{.class_4725} is a place of [fire]{.class_4725} (Mark 9:47); [hades]{.class_4725} is a place of [fire]{.class_4725} (vs. 24). [Gehenna]{.class_4725} is a place of [torments]{.class_4725} (Rev. 14:10--11); [hades]{.class_4725} is a place of [torments]{.class_4725} (vss. 23--25).[ Gehenna]{.class_4725} is a place of [eternal punishment]{.class_4725} (Mark 9:48); [hades]{.class_4725} is a place of [eternal punishment]{.class_4725} (Matt. 25:41). The Lake of Fire is called Hell in the Gospels because in Revelation 20:14, ["death and hell were cast INTO the lake of fire"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 20:14). In Eternity, Hell is IN the Lake of Fire. Nobody's []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_493}worried about going to "Hades"; what they don't like is HELL.

Hell is not just "separation from God," like Billy Graham and the Pope teach. If all Hell is is separation from God, then every unsaved man is in Hell right now while he is alive on earth, for the unsaved are without hope and without God in this world (Eph. 2:12). Do you know how you can tell your Hell is not on earth? You can go down to the river (or reservoir, bay, etc.) and get you some water; you can't in [Hell]{.class_4725} (vs. 24). Hell isn't just separation from God; it's a place of [fire,]{.class_4725} a place of [torments.]{.class_4725} You will [burn;]{.class_4725} you will burn in the [flames.]{.class_4725} You will never get out until you face the judgment, and you won't get any comfort or respite from that brief interim. You go straight into the Lake of Fire, and the flames and torments continue. [That's]{.class_4725} Hell. All Billy and the Pope are doing is politicking to keep their audiences who don't want to believe in a [literal, burning Hell.]{.class_4725}

If you are lost, let me give you some good advice: take the Bread of Life (John 6:35) before you end up [burnt toast.]{.class_4725} That Bible says, ["our God is a consuming FIRE"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 12:28). He is purer than the sun. The sun has dark patches called "sunspots." The Bible says, ["God is light, and in him is NO DARKNESS AT ALL"]{.class_4723} (1 John 1:5). That sun is over 9,000 degrees Fahrenheit. How are you going to stand before a God who made an inferno like that? The answer is, [you aren't;]{.class_4725} not in your own strength anyway.

The unsaved will say, "Well, if ['God is love,']{.class_4723} how could He send anybody to Hell and burn them forever?" They forget (or [ignore,]{.class_4725} more likely) that God is [holy, righteous,]{.class_4725} and [just]{.class_4725} as well. Those attributes demand that He judge sin. A sinner suffers the ["consuming fire"]{.class_4723} of God's nature for his sins, and since sin is against an eternal Being, he will suffer that fire for as long as the One against whom he sinned is alive.

Sinner friend, there is only one place to experience God's love, and that is at Calvary (John 3:16; Rom. 5:8). You will either accept ["the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:39), or you will experience the ["consuming fire"]{.class_4723} of the wrath of a holy God against your sins forever. Hell is being on the wrong side of God's nature. The only way you are going to get on the side with the love, mercy, and kindness is to accept the perfect righteousness of God in Christ Jesus.

Note that the soul is a [bodily shape;]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_494} it is not a croquet ball or ping pong ball in the back of the skull like the Greek philosophers taught. The soul has [eyes]{.class_4725} (vs. 23), [fingers]{.class_4725} (vs. 24), and a [tongue]{.class_4725} (vs. 24). That soul is a body that's not a [physical]{.class_4725} body.

When Paul got ["caught up to the third heaven"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 12:2), he says twice there: ["whether in the body, or out of the body, I cannot tell"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 12:2--3). Why couldn't he tell? Well, he was standing up there in Glory hearing things with his ears that he couldn't utter with his tongue (2 Cor. 12:4), but his body was back on earth under a pile of rocks (Acts 14:19).

That opens a great truth that isn't taught in any Christian College, University, or Seminary in the world. Now we teach it here at PBI, but we're a [Bible Institute.]{.class_4725} If you went to any of those other "institutions of higher learning," they couldn't tell what a soul is to save their souls. If you don't believe me, just ask them what a soul looks like. They won't tell you because [they don't know.]{.class_4725}

Over in the book of Revelation, you read about the souls beneath the altar being given white robes to wear (Rev. 6:9--11). A soul can wear a white robe. That means that a soul isn't a croquet ball; it's a [body.]{.class_4725} It would be like Casper the Friendly Ghost. If you put that white robe over that soul, you would see the outline of a human body, but when you take it away, you wouldn't see anything because that soul is invisible.

Suppose I had a bad accident and had to have my right arm amputated two inches above the elbow. A few months later, I'm sitting in my living room and feel the index finger on my right hand itching. How can that be? The doctors say, "It's the nerve endings." I'm not sure I believe that. If it were the nerve endings, why wouldn't you feel the itch on the stub instead of twenty inches down where a hand should be that's no longer there? The answer is that even though the physical hand is gone, there is still a hand there you can't see. That's why you don't have to worry about an organ transplant or a blood transfusion like the JWs do. Changing the physical parts or fluids doesn't affect the spiritual body of the soul on the inside.

Paul calls your physical body a [tent]{.class_4725} and says, ["if our earthly house of this tabernacle were dissolved, we have a building []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_495}of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 5:1). That's not your glorified body; that's the heavenly body of the soul that awaits reunion with the resurrection body at the Rapture (Phil. 3:21).

Back to Luke 16: ["and seeth Abraham afar off, and Lazarus in his bosom. And he cried and said, Father Abraham"]{.class_4723} (vss. 23--24). So the rich man is a Jew, for he says ["Father Abraham."]{.class_4723} Now we have reached the part of the account that actually could be considered a [type.]{.class_4725} Back there in Genesis 22 and 24, Abraham is a type of God the Father (see our remarks on those chapters in that Commentary). John the Baptist said, ["God is able of these stones to raise up children unto Abraham"]{.class_4723} (3:8). Although those Jews were the physical seed of Abraham, that would not have been possible if God hadn't intervened with the miraculous conception and birth of Isaac (Rom. 4:19); so in John 8, right after those Jews say ["Abraham is our father"]{.class_4723} (John 8:39), they say, ["we have one Father, even God"]{.class_4723} (John 8:41).

In the account here in Luke 16, Abraham has to be a type of God the Father, for Abraham himself died and went to "Abraham's Bosom." How are you going to get every saved Jew who died from Isaac to Christ to lie on one man's chest? It would be like Mary trying to hear the prayers of 900 million Catholics.

Abraham himself wouldn't be interested in talking to anyone in Hell; he would be in the same state as Lazarus. When Saul called up Samuel in 1 Samuel 28:15--16, Samuel wasn't interested in talking to Saul at all. He was down there resting quietly until Saul ["disquieted"]{.class_4723} him. But even if you went to Hell, you wouldn't be able to get away from God because He's omnipresent. David said, ["if I make my bed in hell, behold, thou art there"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 139:8). Jonah prayed to God ["out of the belly of hell"]{.class_4723} (Jon. 2:2) and got his prayer answered. So there is little doubt that ["Father Abraham"]{.class_4723} here in Luke 16 is actually God the Father.

["Father Abraham, have mercy on me"]{.class_4723} (vs. 24). There are some terrible lessons to be learned by the request that follows here in verse 24. The first and foremost is that [there is no mercy in Hell.]{.class_4725} Once you take your last breath, the mercy of God is over for you, if you are lost. Whatever love God had for you is done, it's gone, it's []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_496}over with. God doesn't even have enough love or mercy on this fellow---one of His own chosen people---to put a [drop]{.class_4725} of water on his tongue. Who is that God? Why, He's the One who loved the poor sinner enough to die for him. "Jesus loves me, this I know, for the Bible tells me so\...." Not then, boy. It will be nothing but the unsheathed wrath of God without mercy.

Secondly, if this were the actual Abraham to whom the rich man is talking, a prayer to a dead saint accomplishes absolutely [nothing.]{.class_4725} Abraham doesn't grant the rich man's request, nor does he pass it on to anyone "higher up." The intercession of the saints is just more Catholic mythology borrowed from pagan mystery religions.

["But Abraham said, Son"]{.class_4723} (vs. 25). How's that for a verse proving you can lose your salvation? There's a "son" who died and went to Hell. Of course, that's not a son of God who has received Jesus Christ as Saviour (John 1:12). This rich man is one of those ["children of the kingdom"]{.class_4723} who wind up in ["outer darkness"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 8:12; also see our comments on 13:28--29 here in Luke).

["Son, remember that thou in thy lifetime receivedst thy good things, and likewise Lazarus evil things"]{.class_4723} (vs. 25). So? As I recall, Abraham himself was ["very rich"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 13:2) and enjoyed some ["good things"]{.class_4723} in this life himself. If having money is what sends you to Hell, why wasn't Abraham himself on the other side of that ["great gulf"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26)? But as I pointed out at the beginning of this account, this points to the time when the only Jews who have the money are the ones who take the Mark of the Beast in the Tribulation.

["But now he is comforted, and thou art tormented."]{.class_4723} There is no doubt that the man in Hell is [conscious.]{.class_4725} He feels the torments of the flames (vs. 24); he's seeing things and talking to people. But you never hear Lazarus say one word. Abraham says Lazarus ["is comforted."]{.class_4723} How is he being ["comforted"]{.class_4723}? Well, he's in Abraham's [bosom]{.class_4725} (vs. 23); that speaks of [rest.]{.class_4725} Saul ["disquieted"]{.class_4723} Samuel when he called him up (1 Sam. 28:15): [he disturbed him from his rest.]{.class_4725}

This brings up the doctrine of [soul sleep.]{.class_4725} The problem is that the cults who believe this doctrine pull it from a text like this one [before the Resurrection.]{.class_4725} Whatever the state of the saved dead in the Old []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_497}Testament, things changed at the resurrection of Jesus Christ. Captivity was led captive (Eph. 4:8), and the Old Testament saints were transferred to Heaven (Heb. 12:22--23). In that state, they are ["witnesses"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 12:1): they are [conscious.]{.class_4725} The dead Tribulation saints cry ["with a loud voice"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 6:10): they are [conscious.]{.class_4725} If departing and being with Christ is ["gain"]{.class_4723} and ["far better"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 1:21, 23), then there has to be consciousness to enjoy the blessing of being ["present with the Lord"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 5:8).

Now, what you have in verse 26 is both Hell and Abraham's Bosom located ["in the heart of the earth"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 12:40), and between them is an uncrossable expanse (["a great gulf fixed"]{.class_4723}). You can see the whole thing illustrated in Clarence Larkin's book [The Spirit World.]{.class_4725} Below Hell proper is a deeper part called the Bottomless Pit (["tartarus"]{.class_4725} in Greek) where the ["sons of God"]{.class_4723} from Genesis 6:2 are chained until the White Throne Judgment (2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6).

["I pray thee therefore, father, that thou wouldest send him to my father's house"]{.class_4723} (vs. 27). The fellow in Hell can't get his prayer answered about Lazarus coming over with water for his tongue, so he switches gears and asks for Lazarus to be sent back to earth to witness to his family. That rich fellow had five lost brothers headed for Hell (vs. 28). That must have been some family. Whoever the daddy of that rich man was, he must have been a piece of work. He had six boys, and he didn't raise a one of them right.

Now Christian, let the word of that rich man in Hell sink down into your soul. The fellow in Hell had a burden for souls that most Christians don't have. Don't you worry about the people in Hell; they're all Bible believers. They finally found out that everything God said is absolutely right, but [they found out too late.]{.class_4725}

If you are going to win your family to Christ, you have to do it NOW, not after you're dead. If you're reading this page, Christian, you have the opportunity to do what the rich man in Hell couldn't, even though he wanted to. You had better get busy while you have the chance.

The answer the rich man gets is very important: ["They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them"]{.class_4723} (vs. 28). Abraham says, "They have an Old Testament. Let them read it and []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_498}believe it." It's a testimony to the authority and power of the Scriptures.

Paul told Timothy, ["And that from a child thou hast known the holy scriptures, which are able to make thee wise unto salvation"]{.class_4723} (2 Tim. 3:15). The only ["holy scriptures"]{.class_4723} Timothy had growing up as a boy were Genesis--Malachi.

James said, ["receive with meekness the engrafted word, which is able to save your souls"]{.class_4723} (James 1:21).

Peter wrote, ["Being born again, not of corruptible seed, but of incorruptible, by the word of God, which liveth and abideth for ever"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 1:23).

Jesus Himself said, ["Search the scriptures; for in them ye think ye have eternal life: and they are they which testify of me"]{.class_4723} (John 5:39). All the modern translations have removed that command from the Bible (along with the command to study the Bible in 2 Tim. 2:15) by turning the imperative into a mere statement of fact: ["You search the scriptures."]{.class_4725}[ ]{.class_4737}That's because the revisers and translators of those corrupt translations don't want you searching the Scriptures to get eternal life.

Now do you know what that rich man says to Abraham when Abraham tells him his brothers need to listen to what ["Moses and the prophets"]{.class_4723} said? That rich fellow in Hell says, ["Nay, father Abraham."]{.class_4723} He says, "No." He knows his brothers won't read or listen to the Scriptures. Whoever that guy's daddy was, he raised a pack of infidels. But that's what you run into in a lot of Jewish homes and a lot of Christian homes these days. The families attend church or synagogue as an outward show of religion but never believe what God said in His Book.

["Nay, father Abraham; but if one went unto them from the dead, they will repent"]{.class_4723} (vs. 30). No, they wouldn't (vs. 31). You say, "How do [you]{.class_4725} know, Ruckman?" Because a man [did]{.class_4725} come back from the dead, and the lost world [still didn't believe]{.class_4725} (Acts 17:31--22). The chief priests knew that Jesus said He would rise again the third day, and they did their best to stop it (Matt. 27:63--66). When the Resurrection took place anyway, did they repent and believe on Christ? Of course not! They bribed the soldiers to lie and say the body was stolen (Matt. 28:11--15). ["Neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3156.xhtml_page_499} (vs. 31).

That's not all. Every born-again believer is a [resurrected dead man]{.class_4725} (Eph. 2:1--6). There is many a lost man, Jew and Gentile, who sees the ["new creature" "in Christ"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 5:17) and still turns down the Lord.

The fact of the matter is that ["faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the WORD OF GOD"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 10:17). If a man won't believe what God said, he won't believe no matter what miracle or sign you show him (see our comments on 11:28--30).

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml}

# Chapter 17 {.heading_78-0}

17:1 Then said he unto the disciples, It is impossible but that offences will come: but woe unto him, through whom they come!

2 It were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and he cast into the sea, than that he should offend one of these little ones.

Verse 1 follows on the heels of what Jesus said about the rich man in Hell. ["It is impossible but that offences will come."]{.class_4723} Someone is going to Hell; there's no getting around that. Jesus wasn't just talking to the wall when He said ["Depart from me, ye cursed, into everlasting fire"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 25:41).

["It is impossible but that offences will come."]{.class_4723} There's Calvin's "predestination." The ["offences"]{.class_4723} are unavoidable. Somebody was going to betray Christ; the prophecies were explicit (Zech. 11:12--13; Psa. 109:8, 41:9). But look at what else Christ said: ["but woe unto him, through whom they come."]{.class_4723}

In other words, someone has to go to Hell, but [it doesn't have to be you.]{.class_4725} Somebody had to betray Christ, but it didn't have to be Judas. Jesus gave him a chance to get out of it all the way up to the Garden of Gethsemane (John 18:4--6; Matt. 26:47--50). You have predestination and free will right in the same verse with no conflict between the two. You have a [choice]{.class_4725} whether to commit the offence or not.

Verse 2: ["It were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and he cast into the sea, than he should offend one of these little ones."]{.class_4723} This verse is the counterpart of Matthew 18:6. The ["little ones"]{.class_4723} are little children who believe in Christ, and we have commented on this under 9:47--49 (which see).

Jesus is saying that you would be better off tying a concrete block around your neck (the lighter millstones weighed no less than 35 pounds) and drowning yourself (the Mafia expression is "concrete overshoes") than offending a little child who trusts Christ. []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_501}That would certainly be true of preventing a little child from being saved or destroying a child's faith in the Book. It would also be true of young believers in Jesus Christ, no matter what their physical age (see 1 John 2:19). If you applied the verse literally, that would send nearly the entire American education system---both public and "Christian," from kindergarten to post-graduate school---to the bottom of "Davey Jones' locker." The purpose of modern education, including "higher Christian education," is to create the maximum amount of doubt possible in the mind of the believer so that he no longer believes the Book as the final authority.

3 Take heed to yourselves: If thy brother trespass against thee, rebuke him; and if he repent, forgive him.

4 And if he trespass against thee seven times in a day, and seven times in a day turn again to thee, saying, I repent; thou shalt forgive him.

You can certainly apply the passage to the New Testament Christian. Paul says, ["Them that sin rebuke before all, that others also may fear"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 5:20). Christians are to forgive each other ["even as God for Christ's sake hath forgiven you"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 4:32).

The pattern for rebuke and forgiveness in the local church is given by Jesus in Matthew 18:15--17. In the context of that passage, Jesus doesn't just require you to forgive the offending brother ["seven times in a day"]{.class_4723} (vs. 4), but ["seventy times seven"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 18:22). We have commented on the doctrinal application of that statement to Israel in our Commentary on Matthew (which see). Here in Luke, we want to concentrate on the practical application made by Christ. A Christian is to watch his heart attitude when he is offended by a brother. He is to be forgiving and not seeking vengeance. The goal is the restoration of the offending brother, not retribution.

["Brethren, if a man be overtaken in a fault, ye which are spiritual, restore such an one in the spirit of meekness; considering thyself, lest thou also be tempted"]{.class_4723} (Gal. 6:1).

5 And the apostles said unto the Lord, Increase our faith.

6 And the Lord said, If ye had faith as a grain of mustard seed, ye might say unto this sycamine tree, Be thou plucked up by the root, and be thou planted in the sea; and it should obey you.

After being told to forgive an offending brother ["seven times in a day,"]{.class_4723} the apostles ask the Lord to increase their faith (vs. 5). You have to admit, forgiving a brother who offends against you that constantly would grow tiresome, and you would have to have some extra faith to accomplish the task.

Now the Apostle Paul said, ["faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 10:17). So if you want to increase your faith, you have to expose yourself to more of the word of God.

Here, Jesus says, ["If ye had faith as a grain of mustard seed"]{.class_4723} (vs. 6). What's a ["mustard seed"]{.class_4723}? The mustard seed is ["the least of all seeds"]{.class_4723} that you would plant in a garden (Matt. 13:32). So according to the Lord Jesus Christ, you don't have to have a lot of faith to accomplish something big for God.

Every Christian has some amount of faith; after all, he was saved by grace through faith (Eph. 2:8). The Bible says that ["God hath dealt to every man the measure of faith"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 12:3). So a believer does have at least a little bit of faith.

[That being the case, look at what the Lord says: ]{.class_4728}["If ye had faith as a grain of mustard seed, ye might say unto ]{.class_4730-1}[this sycamine tree, Be thou plucked up by the root, and be thou planted in the sea; and it should obey you."]{.class_4723} Is that for you? The Charismatics say it is. Well, can they do it? Of course not; they are just pulling your leg.

Charismatics will quote you John 14:12 as if they are actually able to do ["greater works"]{.class_4723} than Jesus Christ and the apostles. Well, then, take them on down to the nearest body of water and let them walk across it. After all, didn't Jesus walk on water? Sure He did. Then why can't they if they can do ["greater works"]{.class_4723} than He?

Brethren, that Book is a death trap to anyone trying to use it to []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_503}prove something they want to do that isn't so. It will help you justify your devilment until you go off the cliff and die on the rocks below. You have to rightly divide it (2 Tim. 2:15) and get everything in its proper place in order to survive.

Jesus is speaking to [apostles]{.class_4725} in John 14:12 and here in verse 6. The apostles were given signs that no other Christians were given (2 Cor. 12:12 cf. Mark 16:17--18). A person could be healed by the shadow of an apostle passing over him (Acts 5:15); an apostle could touch an apron or handkerchief and have it heal a sick person or cast out a devil (Acts 19:12). Those were things that Jesus didn't do when He was here on earth. Those were the ["greater things"]{.class_4723} about which the Lord was talking. He wasn't talking about some paper prayer mat that you send back with a check to a Charismatic fakir trying to relieve you of your cash (\$\$\$!).

7 But which of you, having a servant plowing or feeding cattle, will say unto him by and by, when he is come from the field, Go and sit down to meat?

8 And will not rather say unto him, Make ready wherewith I may sup, and gird thyself, and serve me, till I have eaten and drunken; and afterward thou shalt eat and drink?

9 Doth he thank that servant because he did the things that were commanded him? I trow not.

10 So likewise ye, when ye shall have done all those things which are commanded you, say, We are unprofitable servants: we have done that which was our duty to do.

Now Jesus just got through telling those apostles that if they had the right amount of faith, they could speak and what they said would happen. Look at how verse 7 starts out, though: ["But."]{.class_4723} So there is a condition to the ["If"]{.class_4723} of verse 6. What is it? Being a ["servant," "plowing," "feeding," ]{.class_4723}working in ["the field"]{.class_4723} (vs. 7). It's a picture of the Church Age.

You serve the Master [first,]{.class_4725} and then ["afterward"]{.class_4723} (vs. 8), you get the goodies. So plucking up sycamine trees by the roots with a "word []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_504}of faith" (to quote the Charismatics) and having them cast into the ocean takes place in the [Millennium,]{.class_4725} not now. In the Millennium, you will have a body just like your Saviour's (Phil. 3:21) and be able to do what He did after His Resurrection.

["I trow not"]{.class_4723} (vs. 9) is one of those archaic expressions in a [King James]{.class_4725} Bible that is used as an excuse to remove half of Romans 8:1, three-quarters of 1 John 5:7, all of Mark 9:44, 46; and attack the Deity of Christ in 1 Timothy 3:16, the virgin birth in Luke 2:33, the blood atonement in Colossians 1:14, etc. It obviously means "I don't think so." But you don't have to get rid of the God-honored text to understand archaic words and expressions. All you have to do is define them in the margin and leave the text the way it stands. But you see, such a solution would do away with 233-plus corrupt versions of the Bible and hurt book sales (\$\$\$).

Verse 10 is the application of what Jesus has been saying in verses 6--9. The purpose of the Christian life isn't to be showing off how much faith you have; it's to be [working for God.]{.class_4725}

The question is asked in the book of Job: ["Can a man be profitable unto God\...?"]{.class_4723} (Job 22:2). In the strictest sense, the answer is [no:]{.class_4725} a man will never be able to pay back God more than what God has given him. But in two chapters, Jesus is going to give a parable about a master who gave his servants each a pound and expected to receive a return on his investment. The good servants were the ones who made money for their master by trading, no matter how much they made. The wicked servant was the one who did not make even a minimal effort to increase his master's money. Jesus never says the wicked servant didn't perform his normal duties as a servant; but the plowing, the feeding, and the normal everyday tasks didn't bring the master any profit.

The lesson for the Christian is that if he wants to be profitable to his Lord, he has to [bear fruit.]{.class_4725} Growth is not fruit-bearing. Every Christian should ["grow in grace"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 3:18), but growth that doesn't result in fruit is unprofitable. The Christian is to bear ["fruit," "more fruit," ]{.class_4723}and ["much fruit"]{.class_4723} (John 15:2--5); and any growth that doesn't result in fruit is purged by the Husbandman (John 15:1--2).

11 And it came to pass, as he went to Jerusalem, that he passed through the midst of Samaria and Galilee.

12 And as he entered into a certain village, there met him ten men that were lepers, which stood afar off:

13 And they lifted up their voices, and said, Jesus, Master, have mercy on us.

14 And when he saw them, he said unto them, Go shew yourselves unto the priests. And it came to pass, that, as they went, they were cleansed.

15 And one of them, when he saw that he was healed, turned back, and with a loud voice glorified God,

16 And fell down on his face at his feet, giving him thanks: and he was a Samaritan.

17 And Jesus answering said, Were there not ten cleansed? but where are the nine?

18 There are not found that returned to give glory to God, save this stranger.

19 And he said unto him, Arise, go thy way: thy faith hath made thee whole.

As we said back in our remarks under 13:31, Jesus has been headed toward Jerusalem, and the last several chapters (since 9:51) have given you incidents and teachings along the way. Not everything, though, is given in chronological order. In 13:31--35, Jesus is getting ready for His Triumphal Entry into Jerusalem. The passage here goes back and gives an incident that took place when Jesus was up at the border of Galilee and Samaria (see 9:52--56).

The Samaritans were half-breed Jews who served both God and idols (see 2 Kings 17). That's why the Jews didn't want to have anything to do with the Samaritans (John 4:9).

["And as he entered into a certain village, there met him ten men that were lepers, which stood afar off"]{.class_4723} (vs. 12). The Old Testament law for the leper was that he was to live outside the camp and wear a covering over his mouth and cry ["Unclean, unclean"]{.class_4723} if anyone came near so he wouldn't infect others (Lev. 13:45--46).

Notice the number: ["TEN men that were lepers."]{.class_4723} Ten is the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_506}number of the Gentiles (see our comments on 15:8), and the tenth leper in this story is a [Gentile]{.class_4725}---a Samaritan.

Now when the lepers ask Christ to heal them, Jesus doesn't touch them like He did that leper back in 5:12--13 (see our comments on that passage). Instead, he tells them to go show themselves to the priests. This was according to the Mosaic Law.

["This shall be the law of the leper in the day of his cleansing: He shall be brought unto the priest"]{.class_4723} (Lev. 14:2).

Notice that the priest didn't cleanse the leper. The leper was already cleansed. All the priest could do was inspect him and pronounce him clean according to the Law.

Here, the healing did not take place when Jesus spoke; it took place ["as they went"]{.class_4723} (vs. 14). The lepers had to have enough faith to believe that they would be clean when the priest inspected them, and they had to act on that faith by going to the priest. That's the faith-and-works system of the Old Testament.

Verses 15--18 are an insightful revelation of human nature. Human nature is unthankful for the blessings God has given (see Rom. 1:21). Here are nine Jews dying of an incurable disease. Suddenly God heals them when they do what a man tells them, and they forget to go back and thank the man? But that's human nature.

If you want to see it illustrated in a more mundane setting, give out candy to a class of beginner children (four-five year olds). Our teachers here at Bible Baptist have done that as an object lesson for this passage of Scripture and had one child out of a class of twelve to twenty say thank you. Of course, once one child says thank you, all the rest will follow, but you would be surprised how many [don't]{.class_4725} to begin with. That's human nature, whether the person is a Christian or not, and whether the person has been "raised right" or not.

The one man to return and give thanks was the most unlikely of all: he was a [Samaritan.]{.class_4725} The word ["stranger"]{.class_4723} in verse 18 is a word used of the [Gentiles]{.class_4725} (Judg. 19:12; Ruth 2:10; 2 Sam. 1:13; 1 Kings 8:41; Acts 2:10). The Gentiles are ["aliens from the commonwealth of Israel, and strangers from the covenants of promise"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 2:12). So this is a picture of salvation being extended to the Gentiles in the Church Age (see Eph. 2:19).

20 And when he was demanded of the Pharisees, when the kingdom of God should come, he answered them and said, The kingdom of God cometh not with observation:

21 Neither shall they say, Lo here! or, lo there! for, behold, the kingdom of God is within you.

The verses, like Romans 14:17, are a definitive passage on the [spiritual]{.class_4725} nature of the Kingdom of God. They are set in contrast with Matthew 11:12, which defines the Kingdom of Heaven as a [physical ]{.class_4725}kingdom. The Kingdom of Heaven can be taken by violence and force; that is, it can be taken by swords, spears, bombs, guns, tanks, etc. Not too long ago, Hollywood made a movie about the Crusades called "The Kingdom of Heaven." I've never seen the movie, and I don't recommend it; but according to Matthew 11:12, the title was an accurate description of Catholics and Mohammedans fighting over Jerusalem "for the glory of God" "in the name of Allah." When Jesus Christ returns to "bring in the kingdom," He does it by [armed force]{.class_4725} (Rev. 19).

The Kingdom of God, on the other hand, is an [invisible]{.class_4725} kingdom: ["The kingdom of God cometh not with observation"]{.class_4723} (vs. 20). It has nothing to do with [church buildings,]{.class_4725} basilicas, Ka'abas, mosques, minarets, temples, cathedrals, etc. The Kingdom of God has nothing to do with [money.]{.class_4725} The standard prayer in Southern Baptist churches for the offering is: "Bless these tithes and offerings to the ongoing of Thy kingdom." The Kingdom of God isn't spread by money. The spread of the Gospel has nothing to do with taking [territory; ]{.class_4725}it has to do with the [hearts of men]{.class_4725} (vs. 21).

The Kingdom of God is an [inward]{.class_4725} kingdom: ["the kingdom of God is within you"]{.class_4723} (vs. 21). The "house of God" in the New Testament is the body of the believer because the Holy Spirit indwells him (1 Cor. 6:19). ["The kingdom of his dear Son"]{.class_4723} (Col. 1:13) has to do with ["Christ in you, the hope of glory"]{.class_4723} (Col. 1:27). It's you in Christ and Christ in you.

And according to Romans 14:17, the Kingdom of God is a [spiritual kingdom ]{.class_4725}of[ ]{.class_4725}["righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost"]{.class_4723}; not a starchy cookie and a jug of hootch (the Mass) or "Ramadan" (an Islamic feast) or Jewish dietary laws or Hindu []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_508}vegetarianism, etc.

The problem was that when the Jews rejected their Messiah, they lost both kingdoms [temporarily.]{.class_4725} The physical kingdom (the Kingdom of Heaven) was postponed until the King returns in force to reign, and the spiritual kingdom was taken from the Jews and turned over to a spiritual nation born again by the word of God and the blood of the Lord Jesus Christ (Matt. 21:42--43 cf. 1 Pet. 2:5--9, 1:18--23). When the King returns, those Jews get that kingdom back (Rom. 11:26--27; Heb. 8:8--10). In the meantime, if you want to enter into the Kingdom of God, whether you are a Jew or Gentile (John 1:12--13), you have to have a New Birth---a [spiritual]{.class_4725} birth (John 3:5).

22 And he said unto the disciples, The days will come, when ye shall desire to see one of the days of the Son of man, and ye shall not see it.

23 And they shall say to you, See here; or, see there: go not after them, nor follow them.

24 For as the lightning, that lighteneth out of the one part under heaven, shineth unto the other part under heaven; so shall also the Son of man be in his day.

25 But first must he suffer many things, and be rejected of this generation.

26 And as it was in the days of Noe, so shall it be also in the days of the Son of man.

27 They did eat, they drank, they married wives, they were given in marriage, until the day that Noe entered into the ark, and the flood came, and destroyed them all.

28 Likewise also as it was in the days of Lot; they did eat, they drank, they bought, they sold, they planted, they builded;

29 But the same day that Lot went out of Sodom it rained fire and brimstone from heaven, and destroyed them all.

30 Even thus shall it be in the day when the Son of man is revealed.

Now everything from verse 22 to verse 37 is a sermon on the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_509}Second Advent. You will find a lot of the same material covered in the Olivet Discourse in Matthew 24, except the material here is given before Jesus enters Jerusalem at His Triumphal Entry.

["The days will come, when ye shall desire to see one of the days of the Son of man, and ye shall not see it"]{.class_4723} (vs. 20). Now what are ["the days of the Son of man"]{.class_4723}? Well, notice that there are four periods of time in the passage: 1) there are ["the days of the Son of man"]{.class_4723} (vs. 20), 2) there are ["the days of Noe"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26), 3) there are ["the days of Lot"]{.class_4723} (vs. 28), and 4) ["the day when the Son of man is revealed"]{.class_4723} (vs. 30). The first three are [plural ]{.class_4725}(["days"]{.class_4723}), and the last one is [singular ]{.class_4725}(["day"]{.class_4723}). Well, we know what ["the day when the Son of man is revealed"]{.class_4723} is: it's the Day of the Lord---the Second Advent. But what about those ["days"]{.class_4723}?

The problem you have here is the use of the term ["Son of man."]{.class_4723} That's a [Jewish]{.class_4725} term never used of Christ in the Pauline Epistles. Paul writes to Gentiles, so he says [Son of God,]{.class_4725} never Son of man. But what you have to keep in mind here in Luke 17:22 is that Jesus is talking to [Jewish]{.class_4725} disciples to whom the Body of Christ composed of Jews and Gentiles has not yet been revealed. Hence, the term ["Son of man"]{.class_4723} is used, even though you have some reference to the Church Age (vss. 23--26).

Notice, first of all, that ["the days of the Son of man"]{.class_4723} are likened to the days of Noah in verse 26. What were the days of Noah? They were the time heading up to the Flood (vs. 27); that would be everything from Genesis 4--6, granting that the genealogy of Cain in Genesis 4 overlaps the genealogy of Seth in Genesis 5. What was the Flood? It was a time of universal judgment on the earth. That's a picture of the Tribulation.

Do you know what took place in the time before the Flood? You have a [rapture]{.class_4725} (Gen. 5:24). That wasn't a rapture of a Jew who will return to the earth to die again (2 Kings 2 cf. Rev. 11), such as will happen with the saints of the post-Tribulation rapture. That was a rapture of a Gentile who doesn't die and [never will die:]{.class_4725} it's a picture of the Rapture of the Church (John 11:26). So ["the days of the Son of man"]{.class_4723} are the days preceding the Rapture and leading into the Tribulation (1 Tim. 4:1; 2 Tim. 3:1). There's an overlap there just as there was between the rapture of Enoch and the beginning of the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_510}Flood.

["The days of Lot,"]{.class_4723} however, are connected with ["the day when the Son of man is revealed,"]{.class_4723} so they typify the time just before the Second Advent. Lot is taken out of Sodom before the fire falls, so before Jesus Christ returns ["in flaming fire taking vengeance on them that know not God"]{.class_4723} (2 Thess. 1:8), there is a rapture at the end of the Tribulation of the Tribulation saints (see our remarks under Revelation 14 in that Commentary). For a full study, get our booklet [The Two Raptures.]{.class_4725}

Verse 22 is connected with Christ's statement in Matthew 24:5. It's a reference to false Christs showing up in the Church Age. The Jews have a whole "passel" of them starting with Simon bar Kokhba in A.D. 132 and running all the way to Rabbi Schneerson in Brooklyn, New York in 1991 (see chap. 7 of [Israel: A Deadly Piece of Dirt]{.class_4725}). Then there's the "Christian" ones like "Daddy Grace," "Father Divine," and "Bishop Johnson."

But such "flashes in the pan" aren't to be compared to the line of false Christs who all come from [one church.]{.class_4725} Ever since the Fifth Century (Gelasius I, A.D. 492--496), every Pope of Rome has claimed to be "the Vicar of Christ." The inscription on the papal crown is ["Vicarius Filii Dei"]{.class_4725}---the [Substitute Son of God.]{.class_4725} The Popes not only usurp the title of God the Son; they usurp the title of God the Father as well (see our comments in John 17:11 in that Commentary). Jesus Christ told you that when anyone comes along making such blasphemous claims, ["go not after them, nor follow them"]{.class_4723} (vs. 23).

Although verse 24 is technically about the Advent (note the phrase ["in his day"]{.class_4723}), it is still a wonderful picture of the Rapture. The experts who know say that before lightning strikes, a vacuum is created by negative charges going from the cloud to the ground which allows the positive charges to come up from the ground and meet them and cause the electric discharge we see as lightning. The air is superheated, causing it to expand so rapidly that it breaks the sound barrier, which is what we hear as thunder. They say all the steps to create a lightning strike take place before you have time to blink your eye.

Well, that's a perfect picture of the Rapture (1 Col. 15:52). The []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_511}Lord comes down at the Rapture, but He doesn't touch the earth. Instead, you are attracted up to Him and go up to meet Him. That's 1 Thessalonians 4:17. The Bible says He descends ["with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trump of God"]{.class_4723} (1 Thess. 4:16). When John is caught up to Heaven, he said he heard a voice talking to him like a trumpet and saying, ["Come up hither"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 4:1). When God speaks to His Son there in John 12:28, the people who heard it said, ["it THUNDERED"]{.class_4723} (John 12:29).

So when Jesus Christ returns for His saints at the Rapture, the saved people will see their Saviour and hear Him call them each by name (John 10:3) and say, ["Come up hither!"]{.class_4723} What the unsaved world will see and hear, though, is a flash of light and a roar like a cobalt bomb going off. All the world leaders will think the other side has "pushed the button."

Verse 25 is telling the Jewish disciples that before Messiah returns in power as He does in Isaiah 63, He has to die as the suffering Servant of Isaiah 53. That's what those Pharisees, scribes, and Sadducees never could get. They were looking for that conquering King, but they weren't expecting that suffering Saviour.

["As it was in the days of Noe"]{.class_4723} (vs. 26); ["as it was in the days of Lot"]{.class_4723} (vs. 28). Christ validates the historical authenticity of the Old Testament. Characters like Noah and Lot are not just fairy tales concocted to teach moral lessons; they were actual, historical figures. The Holy Spirit records their history as examples and warnings to you today (1 Cor. 10:6--11); in this instance, a [prophetic]{.class_4725} warning. So if you want to know where you are prophetically, you look at two places in your Bible: Genesis 4--6 (Noah) and Genesis 11--13 (Lot).

What takes place in Noah's days? You have visitors coming down from "upstairs." So since 1959, the government has been getting you ready to receive visitors claiming to be from the second heaven. You've got satellites orbiting the earth beaming television signals into your living room and telephone signals to your pocket (cell phones). You have men going up to the moon and building space stations orbiting the earth. You have probes launched to Venus, Mars, and Jupiter trying to find life on other planets; and you have giant radio telescopes trying to "make contact." They're trying to []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_512}locate the "gods" who helped you jump the gap between apes and men (see [Black is Beautiful]{.class_4725}).

In Noah's day, ["there were giants in the earth"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 6:4). All the giants who show up after the Flood are [Hamites:]{.class_4725} they're [Africans ]{.class_4725}(Deut. 2). So since 1964, the push has been toward African [rights,]{.class_4725} African [music,]{.class_4725} and African [morals.]{.class_4725} Right now, you have an African [religion]{.class_4725} fighting to take over the world (Islam).

In Noah's days, ["all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 6:12). That's ["ALL flesh,"]{.class_4723} not just human flesh. The animals were corrupted too (Lev. 18:23). So under Obama, the Democrats voted to lift the ban on [bestiality]{.class_4725} in the military, and the NEA is pushing to have it taught to children in the Federal schools (formerly public schools) all the way down to kindergarten. The scientists right now are trying to mix human DNA and animal DNA in the laboratory so there will be no genetic distinction between you and a beast. That way you can have "animal rights" (PETA) as well as "human rights" and "civil rights."

Do you recognize where you are yet? Is the "lay of the land" familiar? Well, if there's any doubt where you are, let's look at ["the days of Lot."]{.class_4723}

The first mention of Lot occurs in the same chapter where the first "United Nations" is created (Gen. 11:27 cf. Gen. 11:1--4). You have one universal language, like English is today. The action takes place in Shinar: that's Babylon. That's Bagdad in Iraq, today, where Bush fought his "War on Terror." You still have troops over there under Obama.

What else do you have in ["the days of Lot"]{.class_4723}? You have the Frisco-fruity-fairy-faggots showing up in Genesis 13:13. They are militant (Gen. 19:4--9), and if you give them free rein, they will take over your cities and your government.

Now here in the passage, the emphasis is placed on how unprepared the populations of Noah's day and Lot's day were for judgment. In Noah's day, everyone was carrying on as usual right up until the moment the rain started to fall (vs. 27). They were eating, drinking, and raising families as if God had never said He was sending a flood to wipe them all out. It was life as usual. It was the same situation in Lot's day: business as usual (vs. 28).

Note the words ["destroyed them all"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_513} in verses 27 and 29. That's God wiping out the populations of the whole earth and four cities. Who did that? The God who loved you enough to die for you\![ It's the same God.]{.class_4725}

Modern Christians are [Modernists]{.class_4725} in their view of God. They think the God of the Old Testament is [different]{.class_4725} than the God of the New Testament. They think the God of the New Testament has replaced that angry, vicious God of the Old Testament. Jesus is just so sweet, nice, kind, and loving that He would never do the things Jehovah did in the Old Testament. [He would burn you just as quick as look at you if it had to be done]{.class_4725} (2 Thess. 1:7--9).

The problem is that Americans have become spoiled. They have lived under God's blessings for so long that they have too high an estimation of themselves. Paul warned you: ["Be not wise in your own conceits"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 12:16, 11:25). They have forgotten with whom they are dealing: they are dealing with a God who thinks nothing of killing 300,000--300,000,000 people at a shot when He has to---and that includes the little babies and infants, the toddlers, the kindergartners, the handicapped, and the old folks. In the Tribulation, He doesn't hesitate to wipe out [five billion]{.class_4725} of them.

31 In that day, he which shall be upon the housetop, and his stuff in the house, let him not come down to take it away: and he that is in the field, let him likewise not return back.

32 Remember Lot's wife.

33 Whosoever shall seek to save his life shall lose it; and whosoever shall lose his life shall preserve it.

34 I tell you, in that night there shall be two men in one bed; the one shall be taken, and the other shall be left.

35 Two women shall be grinding together; the one shall be taken, and the other left.

36 Two men shall be in the field; the one shall be taken, and the other left.

37 And they answered and said unto him, Where, Lord? And he said unto them, Wheresoever the body is, thither will the eagles be gathered together.

["In that day"]{.class_4723} (vs. 31)---which day? []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_514}["The day when the Son of man is revealed"]{.class_4723} (vs. 30)---the Second Advent. Verse 31 is a repeat of the material given in the Olivet Discourse in Matthew 24:17--18. The warning is that when the Antichrist goes into the Temple to proclaim himself as God and set up his image (Matt. 24:15 cf. 2 Thess. 2:3--4; Rev. 13:14--15), those Jews are to flee to the mountains immediately and not take the time to get any of their stuff to take with them.

The picture Jesus gives for this warning is ["Lot's wife"]{.class_4723} (vs. 32). While Lot and his two unmarried daughters were entering the gates of Zoar, Mrs. Lot was back behind them in the plain, looking toward the fire falling on Sodom, and fretting over her house, the china, the furniture, her closet full of clothes and shoes, the entertainment center, the car, the boat, etc., when the fallout hit her and crystallized her to salt. She became clean for the first time in her life (see our remarks under 14:35). ["Remember Lot's wife"]{.class_4723}: she lost her life over [things]{.class_4725} that went up in smoke (2 Pet. 3:10--12).

Now why aren't those Jews to return to their houses when the judgments of the Great Tribulation start? Because God sends a plague that infects the [houses]{.class_4725} (see our comments on Zech. 5:5 in that Commentary). That's a plague like [leprosy]{.class_4725} that can get into the [houses]{.class_4725} (Lev. 14:33--45) and the [clothes]{.class_4725} (Lev. 13:47), and it is connected with taking the Mark of the Beast (Rev. 16:2). So the Lord is telling those Jews in the Tribulation that if they don't want to suffer the same fate as those who worship the Antichrist, they had better get out of town just as quickly as they can. They had better leave their stuff behind in their houses because it is probably already infected.

Verse 33:[ "Whosoever shall seek to save his life shall lose it; and whosoever shall lose his life shall preserve it."]{.class_4723} Now Jesus said something very similar to this back in 9:24, but there it had to do with [discipleship]{.class_4725} (which see). Here it has to do with [salvation in the Tribulation.]{.class_4725} The only way for a Jew to save his life in the Tribulation is to take the Mark of the Beast, and when he does that, the Lord plagues him with leprosy, and he goes to Hell (Rev. 14:9--11). Of course, if he doesn't take the Mark of the Beast, he has his head cut off, but in so doing, he preserves his life at Christ's []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_515}return at the Second Advent (Rev. 20:4).

Notice in verse 34 that the Second Advent takes place ["in that NIGHT"]{.class_4723} after you were told in verse 31 that it happens ["in that DAY."]{.class_4723} How can it be day and night at the same time? Obviously it is a reference to the fact that the earth is [round;]{.class_4725} so while it is day on one side, it is night on the other. The Bible always runs [centuries]{.class_4725} ahead of science (in this case, over 1,400 years).

Some of the brethren connect verse 34 to the sodomites ["in the days of Lot"]{.class_4723} (vs. 28). While it certainly could be understood in that sense, I don't know that I would stretch things that far. When I traveled around to evangelistic meetings with my song leader big Bob Perssons, we sometimes had to sleep in the same bed; I would be on a slant all night long. Well, we weren't queers; we just couldn't afford a motel room with more than one bed. That kind of thing probably still goes on, especially in poorer countries.

Verses 34--36 are a reference to a post-Tribulation rapture that takes place before the Second Advent (see the companion passage in Matt. 24:40--42). And the implication of the passage is that it will be a split rapture.

Now that's not your case as a Christian. When Christ returns for the Church, it is [before]{.class_4725} the Tribulation starts (Rev. 4:1), and no believer is left behind (1 Thess. 4:13--18). That's because the Church is the Body of Christ (Col. 1:18), and the Body of Christ is [one]{.class_4725} body (1 Cor. 12:13). Christ doesn't take His legs and hips to Heaven and leave His arms and torso down here on earth.

But in the Tribulation, the Body of Christ is no longer here; it's in Heaven (Rev. 5:9). You have two separate bodies here on earth: a body of saved Jews and a separate body of saved Gentiles (Rev. 7). The Holy Spirit doesn't put any believer---Jew or Gentile---into Christ's spiritual body during the Tribulation, so there is no eternal security (Eph. 1:13, 4:30). No believer during the Tribulation is saved by grace through faith in the shed blood of Jesus Christ alone; they're saved by trusting Christ and keeping the commandments (Rev. 12:17, 14:12). So the rapture of the Tribulation saints is not like yours.

Haven't you ever wondered where these Holiness and Pentecostal preachers get their doctrine of a split rapture? They []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3322.xhtml_page_516}never go to a Pauline epistle written to a New Testament local church; they go to a book written to [Jews,]{.class_4725} like Matthew 25:1--13 or Hebrews 9:27. In those passages, you have somebody who wasn't watching for his Lord to return, so he gets left behind. Well, those passages weren't written to you as a Christian. They were written to [Jews ]{.class_4725}(see our remarks under 12:35--37). The book of Hebrews wasn't written to any local, New Testament church; it was written to [Hebrews.]{.class_4725} You say, "Which Hebrews?" All of them---saved or lost. Why take something written to the nation of Israel and put it on a born-again child of God when you are clearly given the doctrine for the Christian in 1 Corinthians 15 and 1 Thessalonians 4? Do you see the mess into which you get when you don't rightly divide that Book?

Verse 37:[ "Wheresoever the body is, thither will the eagles be gathered together."]{.class_4723} A reference to the Battle of Armageddon (Rev. 19:17--18; Job 39:26--30; Prov. 30:17). One of the signs that the Second Advent is getting close is that vultures are now nesting in Israel after [fifteen centuries]{.class_4725} of being absent. The Israeli air force avoids their nesting areas during the mating season, so their numbers are growing. The good news for the Christian is that when you see a sign for the Second Advent being fulfilled, that means the Rapture is that much closer, because the Rapture takes place before the Advent. The signs may not be for you, but that doesn't mean you can't read them and take heart in them.

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml}

# Chapter 18 {.heading_78-0}

18:1 And he spake a parable unto them to this end, that men ought always to pray, and not to faint;

2 Saying, There was in a city a judge, which feared not God, neither regarded man:

3 And there was a widow in that city; and she came unto him, saying, Avenge me of mine adversary.

4 And he would not for a while: but afterward he said within himself, Though I fear not God, nor regard man;

5 Yet because this widow troubleth me, I will avenge her, lest by her continual coming she weary me.

6 And the Lord said, Hear what the unjust judge saith.

7 And shall not God avenge his own elect, which cry day and night unto him, though he bear long with them?

8 I tell you that he will avenge them speedily. Nevertheless when the Son of man cometh, shall he find faith on the earth?

Here is another parable to teach importunity in prayer (see 11:5--8 and our comments on the passage). The word ["faint"]{.class_4723} in verse 1 doesn't mean "to pass out" in the context; it means "to give up." The parable is meant to teach you to keep on praying about a thing and not give up just because you don't get an answer when you want.

This passage is often called the Parable of the Unjust Judge based on the judge of verse 2. This judge is quite a character in that he doesn't fear God or man. He's got guts, you have to give him that. The rule is that if a man fears God he won't fear man, and if he fears man he won't fear God. This judge doesn't fear either.

He is approached by a widow who wants a judgment against her adversary. The implication is that she is the violated party in the law suit, but that is never stated outright. In fact, the judge doesn't even hear her case. He puts her off and ignores her.

That doesn't stop the widow woman. She keeps coming to the judge demanding he avenge her of her adversary. She just didn't do it once or twice either. Jesus said she did it ["for a while"]{.class_4723} (vs. 4). []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_518}Day in and day out that widow showed up at that judge's doorstep and said, ["Avenge me of mine adversary"]{.class_4723} (vs. 3). In colloquial terms, she "bugs him to death."

Finally, in desperation, he gives in to her request, ["lest by her continual coming she weary me"]{.class_4723} (vs. 5). He gets tired of listening to her, so he gives her what she wants.

That's how the Syrophenician woman in Matthew 15 got her request answered: she wouldn't leave the Lord and the disciples alone until her daughter was delivered from the devil that vexed the girl (Matt. 15:22--28). Jesus ignored the woman, the disciples ignored the woman, and finally Jesus insulted her and called her a female dog.

That woman had more sense than the Pharisees and Sadducees. The Pharisees and Sadducees never were able to catch the Lord in His words, but that Canaanite woman did. When Jesus said it wasn't fit to give the children of the kingdom's bread to a Gentile dog like her, that woman didn't miss a beat. She said, "That's true, Lord. I'm just an old Gentile, female dog, but even dogs like me get a few crumbs that fall from the master's table." That woman had backed the Lord into a corner by professing to be [His]{.class_4725} dog (He was the Master), and it was His responsibility to take care of her. And when she said that, the Lord stepped out of His dispensation ["to the lost sheep of the house of Israel"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 10:5--6) and healed that Gentile woman's daughter.

So the lesson of the parable is to have enough faith (vs. 8) to keep on praying until you get an answer from God. God is not like that unjust judge. If He puts off an answer to your prayer, it's not because He doesn't love you or doesn't care what happens to you. He's working things out for your good (Rom. 8:28), and until He works everything out, He wants you to keep praying and keep trusting Him for the answer to prayer. That's the [practical]{.class_4725} lesson from the text. Now let's go back and pick up the [doctrine.]{.class_4725}

["And shall not God avenge his own elect"]{.class_4723} (vs. 7). Who's that? Well, it's certainly not any [Christian]{.class_4725} in the context. There are no ["Christians"]{.class_4723} in the Bible until Acts 11:26. He's talking to [Jews.]{.class_4725}

["For Jacob my servant's sake, and ISRAEL MINE ELECT, I have called thee by name"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 45:4).

["And I will bring forth a seed out of JACOB, and out of JUDAH an inheritor of my mountains: and MINE ELECT shall inherit it, and my servants shall dwell there"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 65:9).

So the ["elect"]{.class_4723} that cry out to God ["day and night"]{.class_4723} to be avenged of their enemies are [Jews,]{.class_4725} and they are Jews during a specific time period. When those Rabbis were in the Nazi concentration camps, they prayed all those imprecatory prayers of David in the Psalms, and God didn't answer a one of them. He let them die right and left in the gas chambers and the ovens. Those Jews had rejected their Messiah and asked for Caesar to be their King (John 19:15), so the Lord let Caesar rule over them---Adolf Hitler and all the concentration camp Commandants were [Roman]{.class_4725} Catholics. But there is coming a time when those Jews will cry out to God for vengeance, and the Lord will tell them to be patient because He [is]{.class_4725} going to avenge them.

["And when he had opened the fifth seal, I saw under the altar the souls of them that were slain for the word of God, and for the testimony which they held: And they cried with a loud voice, saying, How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood on them that dwell on the earth? And white robes were given unto every one of them: and it was said unto them, that they should rest yet for a little season, until their fellowservants also and their brethren, that should be killed as they were, should be fulfilled"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 6:9--11).

The doctrinal setting of Luke 18:1-7 is ["when the Son of man cometh"]{.class_4723} (vs. 8). There are Jews in the Tribulation who won't take the Mark of the Beast and are beheaded by the Antichrist. They are crying out to God for vengeance on their enemies, and they are told to rest a little while longer until some more of their fellow Jews are killed and join them. The Lord lets the Antichrist ["make war with the saints"]{.class_4723} for three-and-a-half years (Rev. 13:5--7), and then He comes back and takes vengeance on the Antichrist and his armies.

["If I whet my glittering sword, and mine hand take hold on judgment; I will render judgment to mine enemies, and will reward them that hate me. I will make mine arrows []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_520}drunk with blood, and my sword shall devour flesh; and that with the blood of the slain and of the captives, from the beginning of revenges upon the enemy. Rejoice, O ye nations, with his people: for he will avenge the blood of his servants, and will render vengeance to his adversaries, and will be merciful unto his land, and to his people"]{.class_4723} (Deut. 32:41--43).

Now that's the meaning of this parable in Luke 18, but it is scattered all through the Scriptures. That's the Lord's dirty trick on a bunch of lazy Christians. You won't get it unless you spend time in that Book [searching]{.class_4725} it (John 5:39) and [studying]{.class_4725} it (2 Tim. 2:15), which is why the "New Age," Satanic modern translations have removed those commands from the Bible.

["Nevertheless, when the Son of man cometh, shall he find faith on the earth?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 8). The way things are going, it sure doesn't seem like it, does it? The Body of Christ here on earth has lost faith in the Book all over the world right now without the Tribulation even taking place yet. During the Tribulation, faith gets so low that the Lord can't even get people to believe in a gospel that has been reduced just to believing on Him as the Creator (Rev. 14:6--7). They won't believe creationism instead of evolution; that's how bad it gets. They won't believe Genesis 1, much less Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, Romans, Galatians, etc.

9 And he spake this parable unto certain which trusted in themselves that they were righteous, and despised others:

10 Two men went up into the temple to pray; the one a Pharisee, and the other a publican.

11 The Pharisee stood and prayed thus with himself, God, I thank thee, that I am not as other men are, extortioners, unjust, adulterers, or even as this publican.

12 I fast twice in the week, I give tithes of all that I possess.

13 And the publican, standing afar off, would not lift up so much as his eyes unto heaven, but smote upon his breast, saying, God be merciful to me a sinner.

14 I tell you, this man went down to his house justified rather than the other: for every one that exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted.

Here is another parable, this time on self-righteousness. Notice that when a man is self-righteous, he is trusting in [himself,]{.class_4725} not in God: ["certain which TRUSTED IN THEMSELVES that they were righteous"]{.class_4723} (vs. 9).
