Paul said of his Jewish brethren, ["For they being ignorant of God's righteousness, and going about to establish their own righteousness, have not submitted themselves unto the righteousness of God"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 10:3).

["And despised others."]{.class_4723} Our expression is ["holier than thou,"]{.class_4723} which is taken from Isaiah 65:5. That was the problem of the Pharisees and the scribes which Jesus hit on in chapters 15 and 16. The Pharisees and scribes despised the publicans and sinners who were repenting at the teaching of Jesus and getting right. The Pharisees thought they were superior to the common people, especially the publicans and harlots, because of their self-righteousness.

So the contrast is made between a Pharisee and a publican who went to pray at the Temple. The Pharisees were the highest order of the Jewish religion; they were the ones who claimed to believe the Old Testament when the Sadducees didn't (Acts 23:8). Paul was a Pharisee of the Pharisees and could boast that he was ["blameless"]{.class_4723} when it came to ["the righteousness which is in the law"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 3:5--6). The publican was counted as a sinner because he was a collaborator with the Roman government and extorted money from his fellow Jews from whom he collected taxes.

Now both the Pharisee and the publican pray, but only one of them gets his prayer heard. Why is that? Because the Pharisee wasn't praying to God, even though that is to whom he addressed the prayer. Jesus said he ["prayed thus WITH HIMSELF"]{.class_4723} (vs. 11). He's bragging to God about how good he is, and his prayer isn't getting to the ceiling.

Listen, Job was better than any man you know (read Job 31), and []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_522}he needed to repent (see Job 42:6). The rich young ruler could honestly say he [never]{.class_4725} lied, [never]{.class_4725} stole, [never]{.class_4725} killed, [never ]{.class_4725}committed adultery, and [always]{.class_4725} honored his father and mother; and Jesus never contradicted him (vs. 20--21). Can you say that? Well, if that young ruler couldn't make it on his own righteousness, what makes you think you can make it on yours? In Acts 10, Cornelius ["feared God,"]{.class_4723} ["gave much alms,"]{.class_4723} and ["prayed to God always"]{.class_4723} (Acts 10:2); and in that state, he was [lost.]{.class_4725}

Jesus told those Jews that unless their righteousness exceeded ["the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees"]{.class_4723} that they wouldn't even make it into the Kingdom of Heaven (Matt. 5:20), and ["the scribes and Pharisees"]{.class_4723} were the best people they knew. In comparison to the righteousness of Jesus Christ, the righteousness of the Pharisees was ["dung"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 3:8)---you won't find [that]{.class_4725} updated in the modern versions.

In the Old Testament context of the Law, the problem was not what they did; it was their motivation for doing it. Jesus said, ["all their works they do for to be seen of men"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 23:5). Their problem was [pride]{.class_4725} (vs. 14).

["I fast twice in the week"]{.class_4723} (vs. 12). Sure they did, but they didn't fast to God (Zech. 7:5). They would look all sad and ["disfigure their faces"]{.class_4723} so that everyone would know they were fasting and be impressed with them (Matt. 6:16). Jesus said, ["They have their reward"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 6:16), but it wasn't from God.

["I give tithes of all that I possess."]{.class_4723} You aren't kidding they tithed of everything they possessed. They would count out the little herb seeds they planted in the garden (Matt. 23:23); that is how meticulous they were in their tithing. The problem was they omitted mercy, faith, and judgment; they should have observed those while they were counting out their mint, anise, and cummin (Matt. 23:23).

["The Pharisee stood and prayed"]{.class_4723} (vs. 11); ["And the publican standing afar off"]{.class_4723} (vs. 13). This brings up the posture of prayer. Notice that both the Pharisee and the publican stand as they pray. This position follows the pattern set down by Solomon when he dedicated the Temple.

["And Solomon stood before the altar of the LORD in the presence of all the congregation of Israel, and spread forth his hands toward heaven"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_523} (1 Kings 8:22).

Of course, by the time Solomon ends that prayer, he has gone from standing to kneeling (1 Kings 8:54). The picture is that of a man on his knees begging, and that reveals something about prayer. God is not so much impressed by your position as He is your heart attitude. That publican may be ["standing,"]{.class_4723} but he is ["standing AFAR OFF."]{.class_4723} He figures he's not good enough to be close to the altar in prayer like the Pharisee. He is not looking up to Heaven as he prays; he has his head hung in shame over his sin. And he's beating his breast, which is an Oriental gesture of sorrow: this publican is genuinely sorry for his sins. So while the Pharisee approaches God in his [pride,]{.class_4725} the publican approaches God in [humility.]{.class_4725}

Again, God is not so much impressed with the position you take in prayer as He is the condition of your heart. You take what the publican did: ["And the publican\...would not lift up so much as his eyes unto heaven."]{.class_4723} So today when we pray, we bow our heads and close our eyes. That's fine if you approach God with a humble heart, trying to eliminate the distractions around you so you can focus on talking to God; but if bowing your head and closing your eyes is just a ritual, it wouldn't mean anything if you lifted your head up, down, or sideways and looked around.

Mohammed took that bowing of the head and said if you lifted up your eyes to Heaven while you were praying that your eyeballs would be snatched out (["Holy" Hadith,]{.class_4725} Muslim, Vol. I, no. 383, chap. CLXXIII). That's the ranting of a demon-possessed madman who will kill you if you make fun of him. God didn't snatch Solomon's eyeballs out for praying like that. That's some legalist trying to put something on you that God never said so you will be in subjection to [him]{.class_4725} instead of the Book; that's exactly what the Pharisees did (Matt. 23:4).

["Saying, God be merciful to me a sinner."]{.class_4723} This is the source of the so-called "sinner's prayer." Since it is an Old Testament prayer before the Crucifixion, Christians add: "and save me for Jesus' sake." This brings up the question of whether it is necessary to pray to be saved.

There is a movement among Bible-believing Christians today that says if you pray to receive Christ, that is a [work,]{.class_4725} and you aren't []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_524}saved by works (Eph. 2:8--9; Tit. 3:5). The logical conclusion to such an argument is that if you prayed to get saved, that you are [lost]{.class_4725} and need to be saved (again!). It is the Baptist version of the Brownsville Revival retreaders. It's propagated by pastors who aren't seeing many people saved in their churches, so they turn on their congregations and try to reconvert them. In one of these churches over in Milton, the associate pastor (who was the son of the pastor) professed to have gotten saved[ seven times.]{.class_4725} That was six times past the first time when he [prayed]{.class_4725} to be saved. And you are going to trust a man with no more assurance or stability than that to lead you to Christ?

Now is prayer necessary for salvation? No. You can say a prayer and be no more saved than the Pharisee was when he prayed. The touchstone of the Gospel is not Acts 16:31 or Romans 10:13. It is John 1:12.

"But as many as RECEIVED HIM, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name."

Did you know you can believe on Jesus Christ and still die lost and go to Hell? Simon the sorcerer did; look up Acts 8:13 and READ IT! You can even call on the name of the Lord and be lost. Those exorcists in Acts 19:13--15 did. But you can't receive Jesus Christ and the free gift of God's righteousness through Jesus Christ (Rom. 5:15--18) and not be saved. You can believe without receiving, but you can't receive without believing.

The question is not can you be saved by praying, because the Scriptures say you can: ["For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 10:13). That calling involves the [mouth]{.class_4725} (Rom. 10:9--10). You are confessing Jesus Christ with your [mouth]{.class_4725} and believing on Him with your [heart.]{.class_4725} The prayer is expressing the [faith]{.class_4725} of the sinner in Jesus Christ to save him.

So the real question is what are you doing when you say the sinner's prayer? If you are repeating it as a ritual (like they do in Russia and the Ukraine due to the influence of the Russian Orthodox Church), that's not salvation. If you are relying on the prayer itself to save you, that's not salvation. But if the sinner prays to [receive Christ]{.class_4725} and [express his faith]{.class_4725} in what Jesus Christ did for him, that will []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_525}do it.

Here's a sinner who comes to Jesus Christ in sincerity and faith, and prays, "Dear God, I know I'm a sinner, and if you don't do something for me, I'm headed for Hell. I've been told that your Son the Lord Jesus Christ can save me, so I'm going to trust Him right now. I believe He died on the cross to take away my sins and rose again to take me to Heaven. Right now I receive Him the best way I know how. Please save me, Lord Jesus, and help me to know I'm saved." Do you think the Lord is going to tell that sinner, "No, I can't save you because you just prayed and prayer is a work"? Do you really think the Lord is going to turn down a sinner who comes to Him like that after He said, ["him that cometh to me I will in no wise cast out"]{.class_4723} (John 6:37)?

From a practical standpoint, it is good for a sinner to pray when he gets saved because it can reinforce the believer's assurance. The prayer gives him a tangible time to which he can point and say, "That's the time and place when I trusted Jesus Christ as my Saviour."

Back to Luke 18: ["I tell you, this man ]{.class_4723}\[the publican\][ went down to his house justified rather than the other ]{.class_4723}\[the Pharisee\]["]{.class_4723} (vs. 14). That publican was ["justified"]{.class_4723}: he was declared righteous before God even though he had done wrong. Now this is [Old Testament]{.class_4725} justification, not New Testament justification. New Testament justification comes by faith in the shed blood of Jesus Christ and His resurrection from the dead (Rom. 5:1, 9, 4:25). Still, there is one characteristic of this publican's justification that matches the New Testament believer's justification. This publican came to the Lord in [humble repentance.]{.class_4725} Anyone who thinks he is good enough to make it to Heaven without Jesus Christ winds up in Hell; he is exalting himself, and down he goes (vs. 14). It is the man, woman, or child who admits that he [can't]{.class_4725} make it on his own who gets to Heaven.

15 And they brought unto him also infants, that he would touch them: but when his disciples saw it, they rebuked them.

16 But Jesus called them unto him, and said, Suffer little []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_526}children to come unto me, and forbid them not: for of such is the kingdom of God.

17 Verily I say unto you, Whosoever shall not receive the kingdom of God as a little child shall in no wise enter therein.

Here is another place where Jesus uses little children as an example of faith (see our comments on 9:46--48). The ["touch"]{.class_4723} of verse 15 is a symbol of conferring a blessing through prayer (Matt. 19:13). After Christ's words in 9:48, you wouldn't think the disciples would rebuke the people for bringing their children to Jesus, but they do. So once more the Lord emphasizes the importance of little children. Jesus says ["for of such is the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (vs. 16) because when a sinner receives the New Birth, he enters the Kingdom of God as a newborn babe in Christ (see our remarks under 17:2).

The word ["suffer"]{.class_4723} in verse 16 obviously means "allow." Again, there is no need to change the text. A simple notation in the margin helps the reader understand the meaning of a word without destroying the readers' faith in what God said.

18 And a certain ruler asked him, saying, Good Master, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?

19 And Jesus said unto him, Why callest thou me good? none is good, save one, that is, God.

20 Thou knowest the commandments, Do not commit adultery, Do not kill, Do not steal, Do not bear false witness, Honour thy father and thy mother.

21 And he said, All these have I kept from my youth up.

22 Now when Jesus heard these things, he said unto him, Yet lackest thou one thing: sell all that thou hast, and distribute unto the poor, and thou shalt have treasure in heaven: and come, follow me.

23 And when he heard this, he was very sorrowful: for he was very rich.

This is the account of the rich young ruler, and you have to watch out here because this is salvation [before]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_527} the Resurrection. The question asked by the ruler is: ["what shall I do to INHERIT eternal life?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 18). Well, there are two kinds of inheritances for the born-again child of God in the Body of Christ. In 1 Corinthians 6:9--10 and Galatians 5:19--21, Paul talks about the Christian inheriting the Kingdom of God.

Bob Jones III equates that with inheriting eternal life, and [it's not.]{.class_4725} Bob Jones III says if you believe the [Authorized Version]{.class_4725} of the Holy Bible is God's perfect word (and [words]{.class_4725}) without proven error, and is the final authority in all matters of faith and practice, and can judge [any]{.class_4725} work by [any]{.class_4725} man including the Greek and Hebrew texts used by the faculty at Bob Jones University, then you are guilty of [idolatry and witchcraft]{.class_4725} (Gal. 5:20) and are [lost.]{.class_4725} There is nothing in 1 Corinthians 6 or Galatians 5 about inheriting [eternal life;]{.class_4725} those texts talk about inheriting the [Kingdom of God.]{.class_4725} In other words, when the King shows up with both the Kingdom of God and the Kingdom of Heaven, the way you live right now as a Christian in the spiritual Kingdom of God determines what your inheritance will be in the Millennium when the spiritual and physical kingdoms are right there together. A Christian can be guilty of any of the sins listed in those two passages and still have his ["spirit \...saved in the day of the Lord Jesus"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 5:5).

If you want the inheritance of eternal life, you have to go over to 1 Peter 1:4. That is the ["inheritance\...that fadeth not away, reserved in heaven for you."]{.class_4723} It isn't based on any works at all. It is based on the [New Birth]{.class_4725} accomplished through ["the resurrection of Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 1:3). The only thing you "do" to get that inheritance is to receive Jesus Christ as your personal Saviour (John 1:12--13).

["Good Master"]{.class_4723} (vs. 18): the rich young ruler is trying to "butter up" the Lord like Nicodemus did in John 3:2. Notice that the Lord doesn't answer with the common understanding of the words [good]{.class_4725} and [righteous]{.class_4725} as they are found in the Old Testament (see our comments on 1:6). Instead, He uses the doctrinal definition of [good]{.class_4725} as found in the Pauline Epistles (Rom. 3:10--12).

["Why callest thou me good?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 19). Jesus' question here matches Matthew's version of the account (Matt. 19:17) word for []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_528}word in Greek and English. But Westcott and Hort change the Matthew account (although they don't touch the verse here in Luke) to read: ["Why askest thou me concerning THE GOOD?"]{.class_4725} Nobody in Matthew 19 or Luke 18 is concerned with ["the good"]{.class_4725} or ["what is good"]{.class_4725} ([ASV, NASV]{.class_4725}). The rich young ruler knows perfectly well what is "good" (vs. 20). ["The good"]{.class_4725} is what Dean John Burgon called "a stale crumb of Greek philosophy." It is the ["Summum Bonum"]{.class_4725}---the bullshooting of pagan Greek philosophers over "the supreme good." No orthodox Jew would be interested in such nonsense, not when he had an Old Testament to read. Westcott and Hort picked up their corrupt reading in Matthew 19:17 from [Vaticanus,]{.class_4725} which bears the marks of [Origen's]{.class_4725} philosophical guesswork on the passage.

["Why callest thou me good? none is good, save one, that is, God."]{.class_4723} If Jesus Christ were not God, then He wasn't good; He was just one more lost sinner like Mohammed, Buddha, Joseph Smith, Ellen G. White, Mary Baker Eddy, the Popes, et al.

Now notice Jesus' answer to the rich young ruler's question in verse 18: ["what shall I do to inherit eternal life?"]{.class_4723} He doesn't tell the young man to trust Him or be born again or anything like that. It's [works]{.class_4725}---from start to finish: ["Do not commit adultery, Do not kill, Do not steal, Do not bear false witness, Honour thy father and thy mother"]{.class_4723} (vs. 20). [Works---]{.class_4725}there's no faith involved at all. Why is that? Because one day, those Jews [inherit eternal life from their mother and father.]{.class_4725}

Hebrews 1:14---["Are they not all ministering spirits, sent forth to minister for them who shall be HEIRS OF SALVATION?"]{.class_4723}

No one in this age "inherits salvation"; you receive it as a [free gift]{.class_4725} (Rom. 5:15--18). But someone at sometime does.

["For this is the covenant that I will make with the HOUSE OF ISRAEL after those days, saith the Lord; I will put my laws into their mind, and write them in their hearts: and I will be to them a God, and they shall be to me a people\...for all shall know me, from the least ]{.class_4723}\[why, there are those ["little children"]{.class_4723} and ["infants"]{.class_4723} from vss. 15--16 here in Luke 18. In the Millennium, the children know the Lord [from birth]{.class_4725}\][ to the greatest"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 8:10--11).

Those Jewish saints that go through the Tribulation and the Millennium and keep the commandments (Rev. 12:17, 14:12, 22:14) come up at the White Throne Judgment and are judged according to their works and are given a reward (Rev. 11:18). What's the reward? They get to come into the New Jerusalem and eat of the Tree of Life and live forever (Rev. 22:14). When they do, all their children (["of the INCREASE of his government and peace there shall be no end"]{.class_4723}---Isa. 9:7) inherit their eternal life from their parents.

[That's not you.]{.class_4725} You don't get your eternal life from the Tree of Life; you get it from a tree of [death]{.class_4725} (the cross). In this age, God has no grandchildren. As a Christian, you receive your rewards at the Judgment Seat of Christ, and they have nothing to do with your salvation. So although what you are reading here in Luke 18 may have some [practical]{.class_4725} application to some aspects of New Testament salvation, it has absolutely [nothing]{.class_4725} to do with you doctrinally. No Greek or Hebrew scholar in any major Christian institution in the world could find what I just showed you in the Scriptures; they were all just as blind as a bat backing up in a blizzard.

["And he said, All these have I kept from my youth up" ]{.class_4723}(vs. 21). Christ doesn't deny that what the young ruler says is so. In fact, in Mark 10:21, the Bible says, ["Jesus beholding him loved him."]{.class_4723} Jesus loved that young fellow because he was trying to do right.

["All these have I kept from my youth up."]{.class_4723} That's a good man. Could you [honestly]{.class_4725} say that? I couldn't. But even if you could, it wouldn't do you any good. This young fellow was better than the best person you know, and as you are about to see, he walks away [lost.]{.class_4725}

["Yet lackest thou one thing"]{.class_4723} (vs. 22). Now if you read Jesus' words in verse 20 very carefully, you noticed that He left out all the commandments that deal with the ["first and great commandment"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 22:37--38). He left out the first four commandments that deal with loving God with all your heart, soul, strength, and mind. But He left something else out as well: He left out the command against [covetousness.]{.class_4725} That's the commandment with which Jesus deals with the young man, because if you break the tenth commandment, you've broken the first two commandments about [idolatry]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_530} (see Col. 3:5). If you covet, you don't love the Lord like you should; if you loved the Lord like you should, you wouldn't covet.

Jesus tells the young man to sell everything he owns and give the money to the poor; that way he would have ["treasure in heaven"]{.class_4723} (vs. 22). Then he was to follow Jesus. That is what the apostles did (Matt. 19:27). Notice that the reward there in Matthew 19 is a Millennial reign with Jesus Christ (Matt. 19:28). The Christian is given a reign with his Lord by [suffering for Christ's sake]{.class_4725} (2 Tim. 2:12).

["And when he heard this, he was very sorrowful: for he was very rich"]{.class_4723} (vs. 23). His problem was [covetousness.]{.class_4725} He didn't have a problem with adultery, murder, stealing, lying, or disobeying his parents; but when it came to that dollar bill (shekel, denari, drachma, "Euro," yen, mark, etc.), he was a slave (16:13).

24 And when Jesus saw that he was very sorrowful, he said, How hardly shall they that have riches enter into the kingdom of God!

25 For it is easier for a camel to go through a needle's eye, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God.

26 And they that heard it said, Who then can be saved?

27 And he said, The things which are impossible with men are possible with God.

["How hardly ]{.class_4723}\[we would say "how hard it is"\][ shall they that have riches enter into the kingdom of God."]{.class_4723} He's talking about a spiritual kingdom, so we can make devotional application to New Testament salvation. A man enters the Kingdom of God in this age by the New Birth (John 3:5), and you get the New Birth when you receive Jesus Christ as Saviour (John 1:12--13). So the only sin that will damn you in this age is [rejecting God's Son.]{.class_4725}

Suppose you have a life-threatening illness like terminal cancer, and suppose I had a pill that could cure you if you swallowed it. I would say to you, "Just take this pill, and you'll get better." You say, "I think I'll take my chances with chemo and radiation therapy." I say, "Listen, I've seen this pill work a hundred times; no one who []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_531}takes this pill dies of cancer. Just open your mouth and swallow it." You say, "No thanks; I'll be fine," and you end up dying. [You died because you wouldn't take the cure offered to you.]{.class_4725}

But even though that is the case, if some particular sin keeps you from receiving Jesus Christ as your Saviour, then in a very real sense, that sin will damn you as well. I used to have a father-in-law like that. "Pop May" was an unsaved drunk who was witnessed to by his own family and by several preachers. Pop May's problem was that every time someone brought him to Calvary, he would see Jesus Christ on one hand and a bottle of liquor on the other, and he would choose the bottle every time. Someone told me that several months before he died, he finally gave up the liquor and got saved. I hope he did.

Here in verse 24, it is hard for a rich man to get saved because he loves that money too much. The more money a man has, the more he tends to trust in it because money gives you power. You can buy [people]{.class_4725} with money. The old expression is: "Every man has his price." Generally, that's true: you can bribe the majority of people. Of course, every once in a while you run across someone with [integrity]{.class_4725} that can't be bought.

There was once a Governor of the State of Louisiana named Nicols. Nicols was a veteran of the Civil War and had fought in three of its bloodiest battles: Richmond (KY), Manasseh, and Antietam. The war had left him with only one leg, one arm, and one eye. The gambling interest in the State had gotten a bill through the legislature approving a State lottery (like the one in Florida right now). Nicols was going to veto the bill, and he was paid a visit by a committee of well-dressed men who offered him a bribe if he would just sign the lottery into law. Nicols rose to his one good foot and leaned on his crutch with his one good arm and looked at those men with his one good eye and said, "Gentlemen, do you see this one eye? I lost the other at Richmond. Do you see this one leg? I had the other amputated at Manasseh. Do you see this one arm? The other one was shot off at Sharpsburg (Antietam). As poor as I am, you may crush me and jab me, leaving me to go through life legless, armless, and sightless to a pauper's grave. [But there isn't enough money on this earth to buy me."]{.class_4725} You won't find many left like Governor Nicols []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_532}anymore.

Verse 35:[ "For it is easier for a camel to go through a needle's eye, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God."]{.class_4723} Surely Christ didn't mean what He just said, at least not if you are a stuffed-shirt educated beyond your intelligence. Surely He was talking about a small gate in the wall of Jerusalem called "the needle's eye" through which a camel had to crawl with great difficulty at night when the main gates of the city were shut. Of course, [such a gate has never been found;]{.class_4725} it is the product of a depraved imagination that is unwilling to accept what God says at face value. But that doesn't stop the apostate professors; they go on, year after year, passing down the tale from campfire to campfire as if it were the God's honest truth.

Now look at how the disciples react to Christ's words in verse 25: ["And they that heard it said, Who then can be saved?"]{.class_4723} Matthew adds: ["they were exceedingly amazed"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 19:25). Why would they be ["exceedingly amazed"]{.class_4723} at something which must have occurred on a regular basis? Why would a camel supposedly crawling on its knees through a small gate prompt such a question as ["Who then can be saved?"]{.class_4723} Why, anybody who struggles with great effort like the camel would as it goes through "the needle's eye" gate, of course: in other words, WORKS.

But look at how the Lord answers their question in verse 27: ["The things which are IMPOSSIBLE WITH MEN are possible with God."]{.class_4723} Why, a camel crawling on its knees through a little gate wasn't ["impossible with men"]{.class_4723}; otherwise, no one would have come up with the idea to begin with. Jesus is obviously using hyperbole to make a point: it is impossible for any man to run a real camel through a real eye of a needle, but God can do it. Come on, folks, if God can create light, space, land, plants, stars, and animals simply by [speaking,]{.class_4725} do you really think He would have any problem shrinking a camel down so it can go through a needle's eye?

Now what's the problem with these apostates? They don't like the sarcastic Jesus of the Scriptures. If you don't believe Jesus was one of the most sarcastic men you've ever heard in your life, just go read Matthew 23. Those educated jackasses don't like a Jesus like that. They want a refined, respectable Jesus because they want to associate [themselves]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_533} with Him.

When James White wrote his book on [The King James Only Controversy,]{.class_4725} he said the [KJV]{.class_4725} was [wrong]{.class_4725} for calling Christians ["a peculiar people"]{.class_4723} in 1 Peter 2:9. Christians aren't ["peculiar"]{.class_4723}; they're "special." Isn't that "affirming"? I mean, there's no negative connotation there.

Do you know [why]{.class_4725} James White changed that word in 1 Peter 2:9? He tells you why he did it. He says that when he was a boy, there was a fellow in his church that was a "fanatic." That old man would go out and [preach on the street]{.class_4725} and wave signs around. People said he was "peculiar," and [James White didn't want to be associated with someone like that.]{.class_4725}

People like James White and Gary Hudson are so [stuck on themselves]{.class_4725} that they can't bear being the brunt of any ridicule from a lost world. So do you know what they do? They go over to 1 Corinthians 1:21 where God chose ["the foolishness of PREACHING to save them that believe"]{.class_4723} and change it to ["the foolishness of the MESSAGE PREACHED"]{.class_4725} (the [New King James Version]{.class_4725} here reads exactly like the [New American Standard Version,]{.class_4725} proving that the [NKJV]{.class_4725} isn't a revision of the [KJV]{.class_4725} at all; it's just one more [Alexandrian corruption]{.class_4725} of the truth). That's because they fancy themselves respectable and intellectual, and if they are preaching, how can that possibly be "foolish" (it can---see 1 Cor. 3:18, 4:10). The problem must be with [what God told them to preach ]{.class_4725}("the message") and [not with them.]{.class_4725} That's [blasphemy against the words of God]{.class_4725} in order to make themselves look good.

They do it over and over again. Paul wasn't ["rude in speech"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 11:6); he was just ["untrained"]{.class_4725} or ["unskillful."]{.class_4725} Peter and John weren't ["unlearned and ignorant"]{.class_4723} (Acts 4:13); they were ["uneducated and untrained."]{.class_4725} That doesn't sound as bad as ["unlearned and ignorant."]{.class_4723} Those highly educated Greek philosophers in Athens weren't ["too superstitious"]{.class_4723} (Acts 17:22); they were only ["very religious."]{.class_4725} If they were ["too superstitious,"]{.class_4723} that would be a damning commentary against men who go to Colleges and Universities to get their Doctor of PHILOSOPHY degrees.

What you have is a conspiracy: an [educational]{.class_4725} conspiracy. The []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_534}whole "educational community"---saved or lost---has gotten together to fashion a "Jesus" in their own image. It is the image of a refined, respectable, well-spoken, mild-mannered [milquetoast]{.class_4725} who would never raise his voice to anyone or call anyone names or be sarcastic to anyone or be caught dead preaching on the street. Their "Jesus" is always nice, sweet, kind, gentle, and "respectable." [It is a "Jesus" that exists nowhere in Scripture.]{.class_4725}

The "historical Jesus" (to borrow a Liberal nomenclature) was a fellow who reminded those who heard Him of such rough, crude individuals as [Elijah]{.class_4725} and [John the Baptist]{.class_4725} (see our comments on 9:19). He not only used sarcasm against His enemies (e.g., Matt. 23), but against His [disciples]{.class_4725} as well (see 11:13).

Now why were the disciples ["exceedingly amazed"]{.class_4723} at what Jesus said in verse 15, and why did they ask ["Who then can be saved?"]{.class_4723} The reason they were so astonished at Christ's statement was that, in the Old Testament, if a man lived right, God rewarded him with material prosperity (see Deut. 28:1--3). That's why Job's three friends all assumed he had committed some heinous sin: if Job had been living right, wouldn't he still be prosperous? So the disciples are astonished at the fact that it is a hard thing for a rich man to be saved because wasn't the fact that he was rich evidence that he was living right?

Well, not always. Job said in sarcasm, ["The tabernacles of robbers prosper"]{.class_4723} (Job 12:6). The Mafia made its billions off the [drug trade]{.class_4725}. Hugh Hefner and Larry Flynt made their money through [pornography]{.class_4725}. The Kennedy family made its money off [bootlegged liquor]{.class_4725} during Prohibition. So just because a person is rich is no sign he's living right. Nabal was a rich man, and he was a [fool]{.class_4725} (see 1 Sam. 25).

At the same time, God is able to save a man in spite of his riches. The Bible says, ["not many mighty, not many noble, are called"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 1:26). It says ["not MANY"]{.class_4723}; it doesn't say not ANY. There have been rich men throughout church history who have gotten saved. Joseph of Arimathea was a rich man, and he got right. J. C. Penney was rich, and he got saved. R. G. LeTourneau invented all those big earth-moving machines; he was so rich that he gave 90% of his income to the Lord and lived on 10%. He was a born-again []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_535}Christian. John Wanamaker was a saved Presbyterian who built the largest department store in the world at the time. So it is possible for God to save a rich man.

Brethren, the thing to keep in mind is that it is not money that damns a man; it is ["the LOVE of money"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 6:10). A fellow can be poor and be in just as bad a shape as a rich man if he loves money. The fact that the poor fellow doesn't have the money doesn't mean anything.

28 Then Peter said, Lo, we have left all, and followed thee.

29 And he said unto them, Verily I say unto you, There is no man that hath left house, or parents, or brethren, or wife, or children, for the kingdom of God's sake,

30 Who shall not receive manifold more in this present time, and in the world to come life everlasting.

Jesus just got through telling that rich young ruler to go sell everything he had and give it to the poor, and then come follow Him in order to "inherit eternal life." The young man walks away, and Jesus tells the disciples that it is a hard thing for a rich man to be saved. Now most of the disciples weren't rich, but they had given up everything to follow Christ. Peter, James, and John had left the family business behind. Matthew had walked away from a lucrative government job. So Peter asks the question that's on all the disciples' minds: ["Lo, we have left all, and followed thee"]{.class_4723} (vs. 28); and Matthew adds: ["what shall we have therefore?"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 19:27). In other words, "If you promised that rich young ruler eternal life and treasures in Heaven for giving up everything to follow you, what's in it for those of us who actually did what that fellow was afraid to do?"

Jesus doesn't chide Peter or rebuke him for the question. It is taken for granted throughout the Scriptures that if you serve God, you can expect to be rewarded for the service, whether it be here or in the future. Jesus answers Peter forthrightly.

Notice in verse 29 the conditions of discipleship as given back in 14:26 (which see). This is [before]{.class_4725} the cross, where discipleship was connected with salvation (note the ["life everlasting"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_536} in vs. 30). That is NOT your case. You receive everlasting life as a ["free gift"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 5:15--18) through faith in Christ (John 3:16). Here, everlasting life is a reward for leaving your family---WORKS. No "grace through faith," no blood atonement. But that's Old Testament salvation when Jesus Christ was here bodily on earth. He hadn't died yet; there was no one, effectual sacrifice for sin yet.

Now there's good stuff all through the Gospels ["for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness" ]{.class_4723}(2 Tim. 3:16); but that ["doctrine"]{.class_4723} is something else. Nine times out of ten, the doctrine of Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John is not aimed at you unless you are a [Jew under the Law at the time of Christ's earthly ministry.]{.class_4725} The trouble the "Lordship salvation" boys get into is trying to ram passages like this one into the Pauline Epistles written to [Gentile Christians in the Body of Christ.]{.class_4725}

31 Then he took unto him the twelve, and said unto them, Behold, we go up to Jerusalem, and all things that are written by the prophets concerning the Son of man shall be accomplished.

32 For he shall be delivered unto the Gentiles, and shall be mocked, and spitefully entreated, and spitted on:

33 And they shall scourge him, and put him to death: and the third day he shall rise again.

34 And they understood none of these things: and this saying was hid from them, neither knew they the things which were spoken.

Here is the greatest proof that Jesus was (and is) exactly who He claimed to be: [He prophesied]{.class_4725} about His death. Jesus told the disciples [when]{.class_4725} He was going to die, [where]{.class_4725} He was going to die, and then He gave [eight]{.class_4725} [details]{.class_4725} about His death. Can [you]{.class_4725} do that? Of course you can't; not unless you plan to commit suicide, and that doesn't always work out the way you plan.

Mohammed couldn't tell you [anything]{.class_4725} about his death in 114 Suras of the [Koran.]{.class_4725} He wasn't expecting to die when he did, which is evident by the fact that he made no plans to choose a successor or to have the [Koran]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_537} gathered into one authoritative text. Mohammed a prophet? Hah! I have kids eight years old in my vacation Bible school who can give you more prophecy in ten minutes than Mohammed could in 62 years.

Whoever "Allah" was, he must have been as dumb as his prophet for picking a fool like that. Imagine picking some epileptic to be your prophet and then not being able to give him any better prophecy than he would win a battle. Sheesh, what an insight: one chance out of two that he would win. Jehovah was able to give 48 prophecies about one man's life where the odds were one out of ten with 157 zeros after it that they would all take place as predicted. That's not a "chance"; that's an [impossibility]{.class_4725}---unless, that is, you are the one, true God who can tell ["the end from the beginning"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 46:9--10).

Now, verse 34 is the condition of [all]{.class_4725} the Old Testament prophets (1 Pet. 1:10--11). When Isaiah got through writing about the blood atonement in chapter 53 of his prophecy, he looked at that thing and wondered about what in the world it was talking. Well, then, when Abraham saw Christ's day ["and was glad"]{.class_4723} (John 8:56), he didn't see much, did he?

To try to use John 8:56 to prove salvation is the same in the Old Testament as it is in the New Testament is to strut one's ignorance of Christ's words and Abraham's actions. Abraham saw a [type]{.class_4725} of the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ when he offered up Isaac in Genesis 22 (Heb. 11:17--19), but he never saw Christ on the cross or the actual Resurrection. Moreover, there is no evidence in Scripture that he understood the type that he saw. Abraham ["was glad"]{.class_4723} when God provided the ram to be sacrificed in the place of Isaac; he didn't see the blood atonement of Jesus Christ and then not tell anybody else about it.

Here in verse 34, the disciples didn't understand about the death, burial, and resurrection even when the Lord told them point blank. Mark says they didn't understand what Christ meant when He said He would rise from the dead (Mark 9:9--10), and John says they still didn't know what it meant after He actually arose (John 20:9). And you think Abraham understood it without such direct revelation or the event itself? As the teenagers say, "Yeah, right!"

35 And it came to pass, that as he was come nigh unto Jericho, a certain blind man sat by the way side begging:

36 And hearing the multitude pass by, he asked what it meant.

37 And they told him, that Jesus of Nazareth passeth by.

38 And he cried, saying, Jesus, thou Son of David, have mercy on me.

39 And they which went before rebuked him, that he should hold his peace: but he cried so much the more, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me.

40 And Jesus stood, and commanded him to be brought unto him: and when he was come near, he asked him,

41 Saying, What wilt thou that I shall do unto thee? And he said, Lord, that I may receive my sight.

42 And Jesus said unto him, Receive thy sight: thy faith hath saved thee.

43 And immediately he received his sight, and followed him, glorifying God: and all the people, when they saw it, gave praise unto God.

This is the account of Jesus healing a blind man at Jericho. Although you aren't given the blind man's name here, you are in Mark: this is ["blind Bartimaeus, the son of Timaeus"]{.class_4723} (Mark 10:46). Matthew gives you a further detail not recorded by Mark or Luke: there are [two]{.class_4725} blind men that are healed (Matt. 20:30). Luke deals with one of the two, and Mark personalizes the account by giving you the one man's name.

Verse 35 is sometimes brought up as a "contradiction" in the Bible. Luke says Jesus was ["come nigh unto Jericho"]{.class_4723} (vs. 35), while Matthew and Mark say He was [leaving]{.class_4725} Jericho (Matt. 20:29; Mark 10:46). Of course, this would only be a contradiction to a hot-house scholar who has no experience with [real life.]{.class_4725} Here in Pensacola, you can enter, leave, and re-enter the city limits in some places within a three block radius. But the fact of the matter here is that there were [two]{.class_4725} Jerichos during the time of Christ: there was one destroyed by Joshua and rebuilt during the reign of Ahab (1 Kings 16:34), and then there was a second city about a mile further on the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_539}plain, which was built by the Herods. So Matthew is speaking of the Jewish city, while Mark and Luke speak of the Roman city. The "contradiction" only exists in the minds of Liberals and other infidels who reject the scientific and historical [facts]{.class_4725} of the Scriptures for the [superstitions]{.class_4725} of scholarship.

As with nearly all the accounts of Christ's miraculous healings in the Gospels, what you have here is a wonderful picture of salvation. The Bible says, ["the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ\...should shine unto them"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 4:4). John said Christ ["was the true Light, which lighteth every man that cometh into the world"]{.class_4723} (John 1:9), but ["the darkness comprehended it not"]{.class_4723} (John 1:5). Man in his natural sinful state is [blind]{.class_4725} spiritually.

The blind man who was healed in John 9 said, ["one thing I know, that, whereas I was blind, now I see"]{.class_4723} (John 9:28). So you sing about it in your hymn book: "I once was lost, but now I'm found; [was blind, but now I see"]{.class_4725} ("Amazing Grace"). So the healing of blind Bartimaeus is a type of salvation. He's a poor, blind sinner in the ["filthy rags"]{.class_4723} of his own self-righteousness, crying out to Jesus for mercy (see our comments on vs. 13).

["A certain blind man sat by the way side BEGGING ]{.class_4723}(vs. 35). It's a picture of an unsaved man begging things of the world. He has nothing of his own, so he begs the world to take care of him. It's ["give me."]{.class_4725} The prodigal son says, ["GIVE ME the portion of goods that falleth to me"]{.class_4723} (Luke 15:12). The rich man in Hell says, ["GIVE ME]{.class_4725} some water." When Judas sells out Jesus Christ, he asks the priests, ["What will ye GIVE ME, and I will deliver him unto you?"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 26:15). Give me, give me, give me---that's what a beggar does.

Unsaved people want something from the world. They think the world owes them a living, and it's behind three payments. But lost friend, whatever the world gives you, it is going to take back. The world is an "Indian giver." Every pleasure this world gives you---good or bad---it is going to take back some day; that includes your wife and family. Some day you are going to die and leave them, or they are going to die and leave you. The world gives you [nothing]{.class_4725} []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_540}permanently (1 John 2:17), so why do you fool with it?

Now Jesus Christ will give you some things that are permanent. He gives you [eternal life,]{.class_4725} and He won't take it back. Some day He will give you a [sinless life]{.class_4725} and a [perfect body,]{.class_4725} and those things last [forever.]{.class_4725}

Notice in verse 39 that Bartimaeus addresses Jesus by His Messianic title: ["Son of David"]{.class_4723} (see Matt. 22:42). That's because when the Messiah showed up, He was to be the Prophet like unto Moses (Deut. 18:18 cf. Acts 3:22--26), and the gift of healing was first given to Moses as a [sign to Israel]{.class_4725} (Exod. 4:4--8). That means that every Charismatic faith healer in this age is just as fake as a three dollar bill with Obama's face on it. The gift of healing was given by Christ to the apostles and their immediate converts as a sign to [Israel,]{.class_4725} not the Gentiles or the Church (Mark 16:17--18; 2 Cor. 12:12; 1 Cor. 1:22).

The use of the Messianic title by Bartimaeus makes him not only a type of the salvation of a sinner in general, but a type of the conversion of Israel as a nation at the Second Advent in particular. Right now, Israel is in [partial]{.class_4725} blindness (Rom. 11:25); that is, [individual]{.class_4725} Jews trust Christ and are saved (2 Cor. 3:14--16), but as a [people,]{.class_4725} they have rejected their Messiah and are under the wrath of God as a nation (1 Thess. 2:16). But when Jesus Christ comes back at the end of the Tribulation, the spiritual blindness is lifted. They see Him for who He is, and they repent and are converted (Zech. 12:10).

Verse 39: ["And they which went before rebuked him, that he should hold his peace."]{.class_4723} The crowd that is ahead of Jesus tells this blind man to [shut up.]{.class_4725} It's a picture of someone trying to keep a lost sinner from finding the Lord.

I just bet you that when that prodigal set out to go back to the father that some of his old buddies showed up.

"Man, you can't go back home to your old man like this. What about that Porsche you said you were going to get?"

"You take it; I can't afford to make the payments on it."

"But what about that blonde and brunette you were running around with?"

"You can have them; I don't want to fool with them anymore."

"Don't you know what your old man is going to say to you for []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_541}showing up like this?"

"Yeah, but I sure am hungry. I'll take whatever he lays on me as long as I get something to eat."

After I got saved, I drove up to Delaware to see my parents and beg their forgiveness for the way I had lived back in Topeka, Kansas. When I began to witness to them, my dad said, "Well, son, don't take it so hard. We can see a doctor in the morning. Johnny (that was my brother) has a good psychiatrist up in Dover." When that didn't work, they tried to get me interested in my old life there in Rehoboth Beach. That's the lost world; if it can't stop you from getting saved, it will try to stop you from living for the Lord.

["But he cried so much the more, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me."]{.class_4723} Old Bartimaeus wouldn't be shut up; he just yelled louder. He was determined to get Christ's attention just like that Syrophenician woman back in Matthew 15.

I preached a meeting one time in Panama City and had a congregation of teenagers from the local high schools. I remember there was one row there with four or five teenage girls, and their teacher (a woman) was seated on the outside next to the aisle. As I preached, those girls hung on my every word, and when it came time for the invitation, all of them turned to come down front just like they were marionettes on a string. When they did, that teacher stepped forward to block them, and those girls turned around and went back to their seats. I looked at that and thought to myself, "If that had been me, I would have pushed that sister down and stepped over her and gotten down to the altar and found Christ." If you don't think I wouldn't do it, you don't know me very well. When I got saved, I had to step over momma and daddy to follow the Lord. Do you think some mini-skirted slut, fresh out of College, would stop me?

["And Jesus stood, and commanded him to be brought unto him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 40). He got the Lord to stop and pay attention to him. That's what you need. When you really think about it, it's amazing that the Lord pays you any mind at all. What are you but a little speck among all the star clusters and galaxies. There are now [seven billion]{.class_4725} people on this earth; why should the Lord pay any attention to you? But he does.

Job said, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_542}["What is man, that thou shouldest magnify him? and that thou shouldest set thine heart upon him?"]{.class_4723} (Job 7:17).

David wrote, ["What is man, that thou art mindful of him? and the son of man, that thou visitest him?"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 8:4; see also Psa. 144:3).

You're just one ant in an ant hill. Why should God take the time out of running the universe to deal with you as an [individual?]{.class_4725} I don't know, but He does. I know He did with me. Before I trusted Christ, I was down at the Catholic church trying to get saved by works. I was putting the ashes on my forehead on Ash Wednesday, and taking the palm leaf home on Palm Sunday and putting it on the mantel, and crossing myself tic-tac-toe three in a row, and hailing Mary full of grapes, blessed be the fruit of the loom, and all that stuff. I went through all that as a 27-year-old young man, and I was just as lost as a goose in a horse race. I was looking for something, and I couldn't find it.

When I got off work at the radio station, I would take a hymnal that was kept there for Sunday morning broadcasts and go down to St. Michael's Catholic Church. I would kneel there and read out loud, "Pass me not, O gentle Saviour. Hear my humble cry. While on others thou art calling, do not pass [me]{.class_4725} by." And the Lord didn't pass me by. But why He didn't, I have no idea.

To be right frank with you, sometimes the Lord's attention gets irritating. Job said, ["What is man\...that thou shouldest visit him every morning, and try him every moment?"]{.class_4723} (Job 7:17--18). I mean, what am I? I'm nothing but a little pee-wee shrimp. Then why does the Lord pick on me? Why does the Lord require certain things of me? I mean, He says, "Do this, and don't that, and if you do that, I'll whip you." What's the deal?

He says, "Ruckman, ['Be ye holy; for I am holy']{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 1:16)." You're kidding, right? Me be holy? There's nothing holy about me. I can't even [think]{.class_4725} like a holy God thinks. I wouldn't kill anybody for stealing fruit off a tree. Would you? Well, [He would]{.class_4725} (Gen. 3). But you see, He's holy and we're [not;]{.class_4725} that's why we don't understand what the Lord does.

Thank God, though, He doesn't just pay attention to us with His []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_543}holiness and justice. He also looks on us with mercy and love. That is why He sent His Son to die for us. He knew we couldn't be holy on our own, so He offers His righteousness to us as a [free gift.]{.class_4725}

Jesus commands Bartimaeus to be brought to Him, and when he comes, He offers Bartimaeus a blank check: ["What wilt thou that I should do unto thee?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 41). It is kind of like the blank check God offered Solomon back in 1 Kings 3:5. Now I have never been offered anything like that by the Lord. Solomon was and he asked for wisdom. Bartimaeus was and he asked for his sight. If the Lord ever makes me an offer like that---"What do you want, Ruckman? Just name it and it's yours"---you had better buckle up and get ready to fly, boy. If I could get just one prayer request answered for sure---I mean, absolutely guaranteed---it wouldn't be for anybody to get healed or for money or even for anybody to get saved. If I could get one prayer request answered for absolute certain, it would be that the Lord come back [right now]{.class_4725} and get us out of this mess. There isn't one problem you have that the return of Jesus Christ wouldn't solve.

Now notice that Bartimaeus is healed merely by Christ speaking to him (vss. 42--43). Jesus didn't have to lay hands on the blind man like the apostles did with the gift of healing (Mark 16:18). The Lord Jesus Christ was not limited to one method of healing. He healed one blind man in Mark 8:23 by spitting in his eyes and then touching him; He healed another blind guy in John 9:6--7 by rubbing clay made out of His spit in the fellow's eyes and then having him go wash it off ["in the pool of Siloam."]{.class_4723}

Can you imagine those three men who had been healed of being blind trying to get together afterwards to form a church? One guy would say, "You're healed with spit." The other would say, "That's not how it's done; you have to have clay rubbed in your eyes." Bartimaeus would say, "Neither one of you know what you're talking about; all Jesus has to do is speak to you." Before that thing would be over, you would have the Spittites, the Clayites, the Touchites, the Speakites, the Washites, and God knows what. If they were anything like Baptists, you would have splits off of splits until all you had was splinters.

["Thy faith hath saved thee"]{.class_4723} (vs. 42). That's how you were saved (Rom. 5:1; Eph. 2:8--9). Of course, this is physical salvation, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3437.xhtml_page_544}but it certainly pictures spiritual salvation. The blind man placed his faith in Jesus Christ, and the Lord saved him from being blind. If you place your faith in Jesus Christ, He'll save you from spiritual blindness.

["And IMMEDIATELY he received his sight"]{.class_4723} (vs. 43). No "foul balls," no "short rounds," no "misfires." The healing wasn't progressive; it was immediate. The only time when the healing was not immediate, the Lord didn't send the blind man away with the healing incomplete; He finished the job right then and there (Mark 8:23--25). The Charismatic fakirs like to magnify the [one exception]{.class_4725} and make it the rule. [It wasn't the rule.]{.class_4725} The rule was immediate, complete healing on the spot.

["And followed him, glorifying God."]{.class_4723} Upon salvation, the new believer should follow his Saviour as his [Lord.]{.class_4725} There should be a [public]{.class_4725} witness to what the Lord has accomplished in the life of the new believer in Jesus Christ. And where a public witness is made, sooner or later, it will bring public praise to God.

["Let the redeemed of the LORD say so"]{.class_4723} (Psa. 107:2). That's a good admonition for [any]{.class_4725} of the Lord's ["redeemed"]{.class_4723} in [any]{.class_4725} dispensation. If you have been freed from the slavery of sin (Rom. 6:22) and have ["passed from death unto life"]{.class_4723} (John 5:24), then [speak up]{.class_4725} for your Saviour and [testify.]{.class_4725}

["And ye shall be witnesses unto me"]{.class_4723} (Acts 1:8).

["Any why call ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say?"]{.class_4723} (Luke 6:46).

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3612.xhtml}

# Chapter 19 {.heading_78-0}

19:1 And Jesus entered and passed through Jericho.

2 And, behold, there was a man named Zacchaeus, which was the chief among the publicans, and he was rich.

3 And he sought to see Jesus who he was; and could not for the press, because he was little of stature.

4 And he ran before, and climbed up into a sycomore tree to see him: for he was to pass that way.

5 And when Jesus came to the place, he looked up, and saw him, and said unto him, Zacchaeus, make haste, and come down; for to day I must abide at thy house.

6 And he made haste, and came down, and received him joyfully.

7 And when they saw it, they all murmured, saying, That he was gone to be guest with a man that is a sinner.

8 And Zacchaeus stood, and said unto the Lord; Behold, Lord, the half of my goods I give to the poor; and if I have taken any thing from any man by false accusation, I restore him fourfold.

9 And Jesus said unto him, This day is salvation come to this house, forsomuch as he also is a son of Abraham.

10 For the Son of man is come to seek and to save that which was lost.

Here is another wonderful picture of New Testament salvation. It is the account of the conversion of Zacchaeus, immortalized in the children's Sunday school song "Zacchaeus Was a Wee Little Man."

Zacchaeus belonged to that despised class of people known as the [publicans.]{.class_4725} Aside from the fact that they were collaborators with a foreign army of occupation, the Jews despised the publicans because they would collect more taxes than were actually due and then pocket the excess amount. The publicans made themselves rich off their fellow Jews by [legalized extortion ]{.class_4725}(see our remarks about Lucky Luciano under 3:11).

["And he sought to see Jesus who he was; and could not for the PRESS"]{.class_4723} (vs. 3). Now we understand what the word []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3612.xhtml_page_546}["press"]{.class_4723} means here: it's a large crowd of people pressed so tightly together that their bodies are bumping, jostling, and touching one another (see 8:43). But isn't the wording interesting here. Zacchaeus couldn't see Jesus ["for the PRESS."]{.class_4723} Do you know what the purpose of the press is---as in CBS, NBC, ABC, CNN, FOX, NPR, [Time, Life, Newsweek, USA Today, U.S. News and World Report,]{.class_4725} and the Gannett string of newspapers? It's to keep you preoccupied with [trivia]{.class_4725} and [lies]{.class_4725} so that you won't find Christ. I understand that that is not the "grammatico-historico" meaning of the text here, but it is an interesting way of looking at it, don't you think? If you want to see the anti-biblical, anti-American, anti-Christian, immoral, pro-Socialist, pro-Marxist, pro-Communist, pro-black, pro-women's lib, pro-queer stance of the press, get an audio study from the Bookstore entitled "The Fourth Estate."

Now Zacchaeus was a short fellow, and he couldn't see over the crowd. So he finds a sycamore tree and climbs up in it. As is the case with such plants as the lily of the valley and the rose of Sharon (see our comments on Song of Sol. 2:1 in that Commentary), the American version of the sycamore tree is not the same as the Palestinian version. The sycamore tree of America is like a maple tree, while the Palestinian sycamore is like a fig tree. According to the commentators and expositors, the sycamore tree here is different from the sycamine tree of 17:6. The sycamine tree is supposed to be a mulberry tree.

As Jesus passes under the tree, He looks up at Zacchaeus. He knew Zacchaeus was there all along just as He knew Nathanael was ["under the fig tree"]{.class_4723} in John 1:48. Jesus tells Zacchaeus, ["make haste, and come down; for to day I must abide at thy house"]{.class_4723} (vs. 5). That's a great picture of salvation.

Isaiah wrote, ["Therefore thus saith the Lord GOD, Behold, I lay in Zion for a foundation a stone, a tried stone, a precious corner stone, a sure foundation: he that believeth shall not make haste"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 28:16).

Both Paul and Peter quote the verse in regards to New Testament salvation (Rom. 10:11; 1 Pet. 2:6). The ["stone,"]{.class_4723}[ ]{.class_4737}of course, is the Lord Jesus Christ. The Lord is saying in Isaiah 28:16 that you can't []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3612.xhtml_page_547}come to Christ too fast: the quicker you get to Him, the better. When those Jews celebrated that first Passover there in Egypt, they were to ["eat it in haste"]{.class_4723} and be dressed to leave at a moment's notice (Exod. 12:11). The best time to get saved is before you're twelve years old, and no later than twenty. After that, your chances of trusting Christ become less and less because you become more set in your ways.

I don't know why people put off getting saved. Southerners are bad about that; they're natural procrastinators anyway. Down south of the border, the Mexicans say ["manjana"]{.class_4725}; that means ["tomorrow."]{.class_4725} Why do today what you can put off until tomorrow?

But I've noticed that Yankees can be as bad at putting off salvation as Southerners. I don't know why; they're fast about everything else. They drive fast, they talk fast, they eat fast, and they figure fast. But when it comes to responding to a Gospel invitation, they'll sit there through three, four, five, and six stanzas of an invitation hymn and still not get saved. I have finished meetings up North and come home and had someone from the church where I held the meeting call me up and say, "So-and-so got saved after you left." Well, why did they wait?

When I finally heard a Gospel invitation for the first time in my life, it didn't take me one minute to move---I mean, [literally.]{.class_4725} Hugh Pyle led me to the Lord in the record room of WEAR and invited me out to Brent Baptist Church to publicly profess Christ. I came out and sat on the fourth row and heard the first Gospel message I had ever heard in my life. Hugh Pyle preached on Aaron's rod budding, showing who the great High Priest was (Heb. 3:1---Hugh knew I was Roman Catholic at the time). When the invitation was given, the choir hadn't sung the first line of the first stanza of "Just As I Am," and I was out of my seat and down the aisle, and when I did, I got assurance of my salvation.

I don't know why I came to Christ so quickly when I did while person after person to whom I have preached didn't, but I have come to one conclusion about it. I must have had such a horrible life that I recognized something good when I saw it, and other people [don't.]{.class_4725} It's never good to lead a life of sin, but there is one advantage to it: you realize how bad you really are, and when a deal like []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3612.xhtml_page_548}salvation comes along, you jump at it because you know a good thing when you see it. Some of you must not think you're really bad enough that you need to be saved, I guess.

["Make haste, and COME DOWN."]{.class_4723} So we invite sinners to come down the aisle to get saved. They don't have to, of course. They can be saved right there in the pew. But it's a good thing to come down: it shows a humbling process is taking place. They want to be saved more than they fear being embarrassed; they're willing to suffer a little humiliation to find Christ. The Apostle Paul had to ["come down"]{.class_4723} before he got saved. The Lord knocked old Saul off his horse and blinded him for three days. That Pharisee there in Luke 18 came to God bragging about his own righteousness, and he went away lost. That publican came to God in humility and repentance, and he went away justified. If you come to God as a prince, you'll walk away a beggar, and if you come to God like a beggar, you'll go away a prince. Before that prodigal could get enough sense to go home to the father, he had to end up in a hog pen half-starved to death.

["For to day I must abide at thy house."]{.class_4723} The Lord is going to go have dinner with a sinner (Rev. 3:20). When you receive Christ, He comes to live inside your body in the person of the Holy Spirit (Col. 1:27; Rom. 8:9--10).

Verse 6---["And he made haste, and came down, and RECEIVED HIM JOYFULLY."]{.class_4723} There's salvation: ["But as many as RECEIVED HIM, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name"]{.class_4723} (John 1:12). (See also our comments on 18:13.) If you take Jesus Christ, He'll take you; that's all there is to it.

["And when they saw it, they all murmured"]{.class_4723} (vs. 7). The same crowd that rebuked blind Bartimaeus and tried to stop him from coming to Christ now gripe and complain about Christ saving Zacchaeus (see our comments on 18:39).

If there's any doubt that Zacchaeus is getting saved, look at verse 8. That's a [Jew]{.class_4725} giving away half of everything he owns and making fourfold restitution for what he's stolen. That's real repentance for a Jew, boy (there's a reason for all those jokes about Jews and money). Jesus says, ["This day is salvation come to this house."]{.class_4723}

Zacchaeus is following the Old Testament law of restitution for stealing in verse 8. If a Jew stole a sheep and killed it, he was to restore four live sheep for the one he had stolen (Exod. 22:1). That's the law David quoted when Nathan gave him the parable about the rich man stealing his poor neighbor's little ewe lamb to feed to a visiting guest (2 Sam. 12:6). So when the Lord judged David for stealing Uriah's wife, David had to pay fourfold: he lost the child by Bathsheba, Absalom killed David's firstborn son Amnon, Absalom himself was slain in battle by Joab, and Joab killed David's nephew Amasa (2 Sam. 17:25 cf. 1 Chron. 2:15--17).

["This day is salvation come to this house, forsomuch as he also is a son of Abraham"]{.class_4723} (vs. 9). This is the parables of the lost sheep, the lost coin, and the prodigal son carried out in real life. This is Matthew 10:6 in action. Here is one child of the Kingdom who is [not]{.class_4725} going to end up in ["outer darkness"]{.class_4723} (see Matt. 8:12). This is a wicked Jew who turns from his wickedness and does right and saves his soul just like God said in Ezekiel 18:27. Although he is not required by Christ to sell all that he has, Zacchaeus overcomes what the rich young ruler could not---[his own covetousness.]{.class_4725}

Verse 10 is a critical passage of Scripture, for it is the Lord Jesus Christ Himself telling you why He came to earth. He didn't come to bring peace on earth or to unite all mankind into one, big global family or to tell you how to work your way to Heaven or to manifest God's love or any of that Liberal, Socialistic rot. ["For the Son of man is come to seek and to save that which was lost."]{.class_4723}

The context, of course, is a lost [Jew]{.class_4725} (vs. 9), but you can apply the verse practically to [any]{.class_4725} lost person---Jew or Gentile (2 Cor. 4:3). Paul said it was ["a faithful saying, and worthy of all acceptation, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save SINNERS"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 1:15). Are you [lost?]{.class_4725} Are you a [sinner?]{.class_4725} Well, if you're not, Jesus Christ didn't come to save you.

He may have come as the Jewish Messiah to ["save HIS PEOPLE from their sins"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 1:21), but that salvation was graciously extended to [all]{.class_4725} men (Acts 13:46, 18:6, 28:25--28). John says, ["he was manifested to take away our sins"]{.class_4723} (1 John 3:5); and if you are a Calvinist and limit that statement to "the elect," or if you are a Hyperdispensationalist and limit it to Jews, then just go over to 1 John 2:2 where Jesus Christ []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3612.xhtml_page_550}["is the propitiation\...for the sins of the WHOLE WORLD."]{.class_4723} When the Bible says that ["the Son of man came\...to give his life a ransom for MANY"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 20:28; Mark 10:45), Paul tells you who the ["many"]{.class_4723} are. First Timothy 2:6---["Who gave himself a ransom for ALL, to be testified in due time."]{.class_4723}

11 And as they heard these things, he added and spake a parable, because he was nigh to Jerusalem, and because they thought that the kingdom of God should immediately appear.

12 He said therefore, A certain nobleman went into a far country to receive for himself a kingdom, and to return.

13 And he called his ten servants, and delivered them ten pounds, and said unto them, Occupy till I come.

14 But his citizens hated him, and sent a message after him, saying, We will not have this man to reign over us.

15 And it came to pass, that when he was returned, having received the kingdom, then he commanded these servants to be called unto him, to whom he had given the money, that he might know how much every man had gained by trading.

16 Then came the first, saying, Lord, thy pound hath gained ten pounds.

17 And he said unto him, Well, thou good servant: because thou hast been faithful in a very little, have thou authority over ten cities.

18 And the second came, saying, Lord, thy pound hath gained five pounds.

19 And he said likewise to him, Be thou also over five cities.

20 And another came, saying, Lord, behold, here is thy pound, which I have kept laid up in a napkin:

21 For I feared thee, because thou art an austere man: thou takest up that thou layedst not down, and reapest that thou didst not sow.

22 And he saith unto him, Out of thine own mouth will I []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3612.xhtml_page_551}judge thee, thou wicked servant. Thou knewest that I was an austere man, taking up that I laid not down, and reaping that I did not sow:

23 Wherefore then gavest not thou my money into the bank, that at my coming I might have required mine own with usury?

24 And he said unto them that stood by, Take from him the pound, and give it to him that hath ten pounds.

25 (And they said unto him, Lord, he hath ten pounds.)

26 For I say unto you, That unto every one which hath shall be given; and from him that hath not, even that he hath shall be taken away from him.

27 But those mine enemies, which would not that I should reign over them, bring hither, and slay them before me.

This is the Parable of the Pounds, and it is to be set in contrast with the Parable of the Talents in Matthew 25:14--30. While the two are [similar,]{.class_4725} they are [not the same.]{.class_4725} The Parable of the Talents deals with the [Kingdom of Heaven;]{.class_4725} the Parable of the Pounds deals with the [Kingdom of God.]{.class_4725} In the Parable of the Talents, there are [three]{.class_4725} servants; in the Parable of the Pounds, there are [ten]{.class_4725} servants, showing you that the Parable of the Pounds deals with [Gentiles.]{.class_4725} Talents are a [Jewish]{.class_4725} weight; pounds are a [Gentile]{.class_4725} weight. In the Parable of the Talents, each servant gets [different]{.class_4725} amounts to invest; in the Parable of the Pounds, each servants gets the [same]{.class_4725} amount. In the Parable of the Talents, the unprofitable servant [goes to Hell;]{.class_4725} in the Parable of the Pounds, [he does not.]{.class_4725} So these are two [different]{.class_4725} parables despite their similarities.

So here we go with the Parable of the Pounds. Verse 11---["he added and spake a parable\...because they thought that the kingdom of God should immediately appear."]{.class_4723} Now after telling them that ["The kingdom of God cometh not with observation"]{.class_4723} because it ["is within you"]{.class_4723} (17:20--21), Jesus is going to tell them that the Kingdom of God [will]{.class_4725} appear. The nobleman in the parable goes away ["into a far country"]{.class_4723} (vs. 12) and receives a kingdom and returns as King (vs. 15).

When Jesus says []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3612.xhtml_page_552}["The kingdom of God cometh not with observation,"]{.class_4723} that doesn't mean it will [never]{.class_4727} come ["with observation,"]{.class_4723} for Jesus Himself said that if you are born again you will [see]{.class_4727} the Kingdom of God (John 3:3). The words ["cometh"]{.class_4723} and ["is"]{.class_4723} are [present tense]{.class_4727} verbs, meaning what Jesus said applied to the people to whom He was immediately talking. That time runs all the way to the Second Advent when the King returns with both Kingdoms and men will finally be able to see the spiritual kingdom because there is a whole race of glorified and resurrected beings like the King walking around in plain sight. The Kingdom of God wasn't going to come with observation [right then]{.class_4727} because it was not going to ["IMMEDIATELY appear."]{.class_4723}

["A certain nobleman went into a far country to receive for himself a kingdom, and to return" ]{.class_4723}(vs. 12). The ["nobleman,"]{.class_4723} of course, is Jesus Christ. The ["far country"]{.class_4723} is not a reference this time to the sinful world like it was back in 15:13 (see our comments on the verse). This is a reference to Heaven like you find it in Proverbs 25:25. Right now, Christ is up in Heaven, seated at God the Father's right hand, until the Lord makes His enemies His footstool (Psa. 110:1).

In Psalm 110, you have the physical Kingdom of Heaven and the spiritual Kingdom of God right there in the same chapter. Psalm 110:2---["The Lord shall send the rod of thy strength out of Zion: rule thou in the midst of thine enemies."]{.class_4723} That's Jesus Christ returning at the Second Advent, stomping all over the armies of the Antichrist, and then sitting down on the throne of David at Jerusalem over a physical kingdom (Psa. 110:5--6). But look at Psalm 110:4. There's Jesus Christ as the High Priest over a spiritual kingdom.

So right now, the Lord Jesus Christ is up in Heaven waiting for the Father to give Him the word to go and get what's rightfully [His.]{.class_4725} The kingdom belongs to [Jesus Christ;]{.class_4725} it doesn't belong to the Pope, the Catholic Church, Mohammed, the "Islamic Nation," the UN, the EU, Obama, Bush, Clinton, or anyone else. It's [His]{.class_4725} kingdom (Luke 1:32--33; Col. 1:13; Rev. 11:15).

The ["TEN servants"]{.class_4723} and the ["TEN pounds"]{.class_4723} mark this parable as a [Church Age]{.class_4725} parable that takes place during the times of the [Gentiles]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3612.xhtml_page_553} (see our remarks on the number ten under 15:8 and 17:12). Notice that the servants are not given each ["according to his several ability"]{.class_4723} as in the Parable of the Talents (Matt. 25:15). Here, each servant is given an equal amount: one pound each. There is one thing with which every Christian has been entrusted; and it's not talent, ability, spiritual gifts, money, social status, freedom, location, intelligence, or opportunity. The one thing every Christian has in common is the Gospel of the Lord Jesus Christ. As a Christian, you have no more and no less of the Gospel than I do. Now what you [do]{.class_4725} with it is a different story, and that is what this parable is all about. But every Christian has been given the marching orders to be a witness for Jesus Christ (Acts 1:8) and preach the Gospel to every creature (Mark 16:15).

Now when it comes to these ["pounds,"]{.class_4723} the "original Greek" boys will tell you that the word shouldn't be ["pounds"]{.class_4723} at all; it should be ["minas."]{.class_4725} ["Minas"]{.class_4725} is just a [transliteration]{.class_4725} of the Greek word; it's [not]{.class_4725} a [translation.]{.class_4725} The goal of the new "Bibles" is not to give you a "clearer, more accurate translation in the language of the receptor"; it's to correct the [King James]{.class_4725} Bible as much as possible so that you [lose revelation.]{.class_4725} The trick to correcting the Bible is simple: when the [AV]{.class_4725} is clear, you make it "more accurate," and when it is accurate, you make it "clearer"---anything but believe the text as it stands.

What "Bibles" mess with the text here? Oh, the [NASV,]{.class_4725} the [NIV,]{.class_4725} and the [NEW KING JAMES VERSION.]{.class_4725} The [NKJV]{.class_4725} isn't a revision of the [King James]{.class_4725} at all. It's a "Trojan horse" made to smuggle the readings of the corrupt Alexandrian text into the God-honored text of 1611.

No English speaker uses the word [minas.]{.class_4725} The [KJV]{.class_4725} gives you an advanced revelation not found in the Greek. A [pound]{.class_4725} is an [English]{.class_4725} measurement of money. So when the Lord comes back, the Bible that's responsible for taking the Gospel around the world is an [English]{.class_4725} Bible. Under Queen Victoria, the sun never set on the British Empire, and she wouldn't trade with any country that didn't allow in that [King James]{.class_4725} Bible.

That was the Philadelphia church period that kept God's word, and because of that, God opened doors all around the world to []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3612.xhtml_page_554}missionaries (Rev. 3:8), so much so that missiologists call that time period the "Great Century" for missions. And when the British and Foreign Bible Society dumped that Book in 1904, God dumped the British Empire and took away every colonial holding it had. The doors started slamming shut all around the world. You can still go through them, but not with the same results and not without a higher cost.

Now here are the Christian's marching orders in verse 13: ["Occupy till I come."]{.class_4723} The reader familiar with the contemporary Marxist movement in America under Obama and the Democratic Party will recognize from where the "Occupy Wall Street" movement got its name. They don't get it from an [NIV]{.class_4725} or an [NASV]{.class_4725} or even a [NKJV;]{.class_4725} they get it from an old, black-backed, "archaic" [King James]{.class_4725} Bible from 1611. The lost world can't get around that Book; they will quote it whether they know it or not, and whether they like it or not.

You don't need a new translation to understand the word ["occupy"]{.class_4723} here. All you need is to [study]{.class_4725} (2 Tim. 2:15): Scripture with Scripture. Get you a concordance and look up the word and its variants. With these new computer programs on the Bible, it doesn't take more than three seconds to have all the references brought up so you can see them at a glance. Ezekiel 27:9, 16, 19, 22 and Hebrews 13:9 tell you exactly what the word means. It's to do business by buying, selling, and trading. The servants in the parable were to go out into the marketplace and "buy low and sell high" to make their master a profit.

The lesson for the Christian in the Church Age should be obvious: a born-again child of God should be about his Father's business (2:49). The Christian is to keep himself busy serving Jesus Christ until his Lord returns. He's not to be like the Millerites (the original Seventh-day Adventists) and sell everything he owns, put on a white sheet, and go up on a mountain somewhere and wait for Jesus Christ to come back. Nor is he to be like a bunch of worldly Charismatics.

Back in 1988 when Whisenaut predicted the Rapture, a lot of those Charismatics who followed what he said went out and "maxed out" their credit cards buying a bunch of junk because they thought the Lord was coming back and they wouldn't have to pay the bills they ran up. Boy were they surprised in 1989 when they got their []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ3612.xhtml_page_555}credit card statements!

More recently, in 2011, a fellow named Harold Camping, who owns a big network of Christian radio stations, predicted the Lord was going to return on May 21. He had a big build-up all over the media about it and bilked a lot of suckers out of their money.
