In other words, it was Christ's [custom]{.class_4725} and [habit]{.class_4725} to go out to the Mount of Olives to spend the night when He was at Jerusalem (see our comments on 21:37). ["The place"]{.class_4723} where ["he was wont"]{.class_4723} to go at the Mount of Olives was the Garden of Gethsemane (Mark 14:32); John says, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_625}["Jesus ofttimes resorted thither with his disciples"]{.class_4723} (John 18:2).

When they arrive, Jesus tells the disciples, ["Pray that ye enter not into temptation"]{.class_4723} (vs. 40). Jesus leaves eight of the disciples in that spot to pray. He takes Peter, James, and John with Him a little ways and leaves them there to watch while He goes yet further to pray alone (Mark 14:33--35). Luke says Jesus went ["about a stone's cast"]{.class_4723} (vs. 41): roughly about fifty feet.

Notice in verse 42 that He does not pray ["Our Father"]{.class_4723} as He instructed the Jewish disciples to do back in 11:2 (see our comments on the verse). This is not a [national]{.class_4725} prayer of a [national]{.class_4725} son (Exod. 4:22); it is the [personal]{.class_4725} prayer of God's ["only begotten Son."]{.class_4723} In Mark 14:36, He says, ["Abba, Father." "Abba"]{.class_4723} is a Jewish term of endearment for a father which is roughly equivalent to our word [daddy.]{.class_4725} When you address God the Father, that's how you address Him because you are an [adopted]{.class_4725} son of God in Christ (Rom. 8:15; Gal. 4:16). You don't address Him as ["Our Father."]{.class_4723}

Now what is going on in verse 42 is that Jesus is beginning to take the sins of the world on Himself. The ["cup" ]{.class_4723}has nothing to do with Satan trying to kill Christ in the Garden of Gethsemane like John R. Rice teaches. The ["cup"]{.class_4723} isn't death or dying at all; not even dying on the cross. Jesus faced the cross with joy (Heb. 12:2). The ["cup"]{.class_4723} is the wrath of God poured out on sin (Jer. 25:15; Psa. 75:8; Isa. 51:17, 22).

John R. Rice will drag you over to Hebrews 5:7 and place that verse on Christ's prayer here in Gethsemane. The argument is that since the Father ["was able to save him from death"]{.class_4723} and the verse says Jesus ["was heard,"]{.class_4723} that He must have been praying to be saved from a death other than the cross because that's why He came to earth. But look at John 18:11.

"The cup which my Father hath given me, SHALL I NOT DRINK IT?"

Christ [drank]{.class_4725} the cup; it [wasn't]{.class_4725} taken from Him. Then it couldn't possibly have been the Devil trying to kill Him in Gethsemane. The Devil couldn't have killed Christ anyway, even if he had tried all night (John 10:18). Christ's prayer in the garden was answered, and the answer was [NO.]{.class_4725} The cup would not be taken from Him; He would []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_626}have to drink it.

The ["cup"]{.class_4723} had nothing to do with physical death. Hebrews 5:7 never says God delivered Jesus from [dying;]{.class_4725} it says He ["was able to save him FROM DEATH."]{.class_4723} That's exactly what happened at the Resurrection (Acts 2:24; Psa. 18:5, 21:4, 49:15; 1 Cor. 15:55). Rice may have been a good tractarian evangelist, but a Bible teacher he was not.

What is happening in verses 42--44 here in Luke 22 is that the atonement is beginning. The first blood is shed in verse 44. Christ is being ["made\...sin for us, who knew no sin; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him"]{.class_4723} (2 Cor. 5:21); He is ["being made a curse for us"]{.class_4723} to redeem ["us from the curse of the law"]{.class_4723} (Gal. 3:13).

Recently someone wrote the Bookstore and implied that I taught that Jesus became a [sinner]{.class_4725} on the cross, like the heretical Charismatic Kenneth Copeland teaches. I have never even insinuated that at all. Second Corinthians 5:20 doesn't say Christ became a sinner; it says God ["made him to be SIN for us."]{.class_4723} Jesus Christ Himself ["knew no sin."]{.class_4723} In the atonement, God identified Jesus with sin and the Devil himself (John 3:14) to execute the full extent of His wrath on both, so much so that Jesus prayed, ["My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 27:46). That's the cry of a fellow in Hell. Jesus didn't become a sinner on the cross, but He was certainly ["reckoned among"]{.class_4723} them (vs. 37).

Now verses 42--44 should be set in contrast with the Fall of man in Genesis 3. In Genesis 3, you have the [first Adam;]{.class_4725} here you have the [last Adam. Sin]{.class_4725} comes on mankind [in a garden;]{.class_4725} the [atonement]{.class_4725} from sin begins [in a garden.]{.class_4725} The first blood shed on this earth was shed in a garden to atone for Adam and Eve's sin (Gen. 3:21); the first blood shed to atone for the sins of all mankind was shed in a garden (vs. 44). Part of the punishment for Adam's sin was that he would [sweat,]{.class_4725} so when the atonement comes, it first comes through [sweat.]{.class_4725}

Given these great doctrinal truths on the vicarious blood atonement of Jesus Christ for sinners, you can imagine how the apostate Bible correctors handle the passage. The grossly corrupt Greek text of the United Bible Societies ([Nestle-Aland]{.class_4725}), and all the perverted translations that follow it, put double brackets around []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_627}verses 43--44 to indicate that it "was not in the original Greek text."

[Not one of the apostates ever saw "the original Greek text."]{.class_4725} No such thing as "the original Greek text" exists on the face of this earth. What they are calling "the original Greek text" are manuscripts ([Vaticanus, Sinaiticus, P]{.class_4725}[75]{.class_4761}, etc.), written anywhere from 200--400 years after Luke wrote his Gospel, which are known for their [omissions]{.class_4725} and [errors]{.class_4725} (see App. 5 of [The Christian's Handbook of Biblical Scholarship]{.class_4725}). The "oldest and best" manuscripts here attack a major fundamental of the faith (the blood atonement), just as they do in Luke 2:33, 23:42; John 3:13; Colossians 1:14; 1 Timothy 3:16; 1 John 5:7; etc.

Now Luke only records twice that Jesus prayed, but Matthew and Mark make it clear that He prayed [three ]{.class_4725}times (Matt. 26:44; Mark 14:41). Between each prayer, Jesus returned to Peter, James, and John to find them sleeping. Luke says, ["he found them sleeping for sorrow"]{.class_4723} (vs. 45). The sorrow is not on their part; it's on [His.]{.class_4725} ["For sorrow"]{.class_4723} means "to His sorrow."

The Lord is saddened to find the disciples sleeping when they should be praying that they won't ["enter into temptation"]{.class_4723} (vss. 40, 46). The temptation, here, is to be offended because of the Lord and to desert Him at His arrest (Matt. 26:31). They should have been praying for a way out of that temptation (1 Cor. 10:13), but they were tired and couldn't stay awake (Matt. 26:43). Instead of boasting of how he would go with the Lord to prison and death (vs. 33), Peter should have been praying that he wouldn't deny Christ (vs. 34).

The last time Jesus comes to them and finds them asleep, He says, ["Sleep on now, and take your rest: behold, the hour is at hand, and the Son of man is betrayed into the hands of sinners"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 26:45). He doesn't wake them again until Judas shows up to betray Him (Matt. 26:46).

47 And while he yet spake, behold a multitude, and he that was called Judas, one of the twelve, went before them, and drew near unto Jesus to kiss him.

48 But Jesus said unto him, Judas, betrayest thou the Son of man with a kiss?

49 When they which were about him saw what would []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_628}follow, they said unto him, Lord, shall we smite with the sword?

50 And one of them smote the servant of the high priest, and cut off his right ear.

51 And Jesus answered and said, Suffer ye thus far. And he touched his ear, and healed him.

52 Then Jesus said unto the chief priests, and captains of the temple, and the elders, which were come to him, Be ye come out, as against a thief, with swords and staves?

53 When I was daily with you in the temple, ye stretched forth no hands against me: but this is your hour, and the power of darkness.

Now the council of the elders, the Sanhedrin, sends ["a multitude"]{.class_4723} to arrest Jesus (vs. 47). Matthew and Mark call it ["a great multitude"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 26:47; Mark 14:43). Judas is leading a large band of armed men. This is the Temple "SWAT team."

To make sure they get the right man in the dark, Judas gives them ["a token" ]{.class_4723}(Mark 14:44; called ["a sign"]{.class_4723} in Matt. 26:48): ["Whomsoever I shall kiss, that same is he: hold him fast"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 26:48; Mark 14:44).

This is the Mafia "kiss of death" that you see in movies like "The Godfather." It means you're marked for a hit. There are all kinds of kisses in the Bible. Paul talks about ["an holy kiss"]{.class_4723} between brethren (Rom. 16:16; 1 Cor. 16:20; 2 Cor. 13:12; 1 Thess. 5:26). That's a kiss of greeting where you give the person a "peck" on both cheeks. It is still practiced many places in Europe and South America. It is counterfeited in the Roman Catholic Church by "the kiss of peace." There's an [unholy]{.class_4725} kiss of illicit lust (Prov. 7:13). There's a kiss of [blessing]{.class_4725} (2 Sam. 19:39), a kiss of [deception]{.class_4725} (Gen. 27:26--27; 2 Sam. 15:5), a kiss of [obeisance]{.class_4725} (Exod. 18:7; Psa. 2:12), a [familiar]{.class_4725} kiss (Gen. 31:28; 1 Kings 19:20), the kiss of [love]{.class_4725} (Song of Sol. 1:2), and a kiss of [sorrow]{.class_4725} (Ruth 1:9, 14; 1 Sam. 20:41). There is a kiss of [idolatry]{.class_4725} (1 Kings 19:18; Hos. 13:2), which is connected with the Mark of the Beast (see chap. 4 of our work [The Mark of the Beast]{.class_4725}).

The scene in the garden is an armed mob with torches and lanterns approaching Jesus and the disciples (John 18:3). Jesus steps forward and says, ["Whom seek ye?"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_629} (John 18:4). A voice from the crowd growls, ["Jesus of Nazareth"]{.class_4723} (John 18:5). Jesus answers, ["I am he,"]{.class_4723} and when He does, that whole mob of grown men falls backwards to the ground (John 18:6).

You will notice in the [King James]{.class_4725} Bible that the word ["he"]{.class_4723} is in italics; that means it was added by the translators for clarity. In Hebrew (the language Jesus is speaking to the Jewish Temple guards), Jesus says ["I am."]{.class_4723} That's the name the Lord gave to Moses back there in Exodus 3:14. In Hebrew, that's known as the "tetragrammaton," the four Hebrew letters that comprise the name Jehovah. For a brief instant, Jesus unleashes the power of His divine name as a warning with whom they are dealing.

What happens next is a testimony to the depravity of man. Having just been knocked down by the power of God, Judas picks himself off the ground and goes through with the betrayal. He steps forward with a smile on his face as if to alleviate any fears his comrades might have as to what's going on. He grabs Jesus by the shoulders as friends in the Middle East would upon greeting one another. ["Master, master,"]{.class_4723} he says, and then he kisses Jesus on the cheek (Mark 14:45).

Jesus looks Judas straight in the eyes and says, ["Friend, wherefore art thou come?"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 26:50). Judas stands there with his mouth hanging open, looking into the face of Jesus, not knowing what to say. As if in answer to His own question, Jesus says, ["Judas, betrayest thou the Son of man with a kiss?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 48).

Jesus looks back to the crowd whom Judas led to take Him. Again He asks, ["Whom seek ye?"]{.class_4723} Again the answer comes back, ["Jesus of Nazareth"]{.class_4723} (John 18:7). Jesus says, ["I have told you that I am he"]{.class_4723} (John 18:8), and that time, Christ does not exercise His Deity. ["If therefore ye seek me, let these go their way"]{.class_4723} (John 18:8). In other words, "You want me, not my disciples. There's no need to take them too."

Luke says at that point, ["When THEY which were about him ]{.class_4723}\[referring to the disciples who were with Jesus\][ saw what would follow, THEY said unto him, Lord, shall we smite with the sword?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 49). The question is attributed to all the disciples, but there is little doubt as to whom the spokesman for the group is. It is [Simon Peter,]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_630} for he is the one who draws his sword and cuts off the ear of Malchus, ["the servant of the high priest"]{.class_4723} (John 18:10).

["Suffer ye thus far"]{.class_4723} (vs. 51); in other words, "Allow them to take me." He tells Peter, "Put up your sword, Peter; I have to drink the cup my Father gave me (John 18:11). Go ahead and let them take me."

Over in Matthew, Jesus adds, ["Thinkest thou that I cannot now pray to my Father, and he shall presently give me more than twelve legions of angels? But how then shall the scriptures be fulfilled, that thus it must be?"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 26:53--54). Now think about that for a moment. Jesus has just prayed to the Father to take the cup of wrath from Him, and He has been given the definite answer of [no.]{.class_4725} Yet even with that answer, the Father would have spared Him the humiliation of being taken as a common thief if He had asked. Jesus was more concerned with the [integrity of His word]{.class_4725} than He was His own dignity.

Of course, if God had sent ["more than twelve legions of angels"]{.class_4723} to rescue Christ from the soldiers, there wouldn't have been anyone alive on earth to carry out the Crucifixion. [One]{.class_4725} angel back in the Old Testament killed [185,000 men]{.class_4725} (2 Kings 19:35). A Roman legion had a complement of 6,000 men, so ["twelve legions of angels"]{.class_4723} would be [72,000 angels,]{.class_4725} and Jesus said the Father could send Him ["MORE than"]{.class_4723} that. Seventy-two thousand angels killing 185,000 men each would have wiped out the entire population of the planet at that time. Jeremiah wrote, ["It is of the LORD'S mercies that we are not consumed, because his compassions fail not"]{.class_4723} (Lam. 3:22).

Then the Bible says, ["he]{.class_4723} \[i.e., Jesus\][ touched his ear, and healed him ]{.class_4723}\[speaking of the high priest's servant whose ear Peter cut off\][."]{.class_4723} Understand, the Bible doesn't say that Jesus picked up the ear and put it back on the guy's head. It says He ["touched his ear."]{.class_4723} Only one of two things could have happened: either Jesus touched the place on Malchus' head where his ear had been, and a new one grew back; or else Jesus touched the cut-off ear on the ground, and it jumped up and reattached itself to the guy's head. You talk about a sight to see, boy; I bet you that was one. That would be quite a feat []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_631}for Benny Hinn, Richard Roberts, or Todd Bentley to try (see the Charismatic perversion of John 14:12).

Verse 51 is the last public miracle Christ performs, and He performs it on an [enemy.]{.class_4725} This is Jesus putting into practice in the most dramatic of ways what He taught the disciples back in 6:27. It is a foretaste of what is about to take place on the cross.

["For if, when we were ENEMIES, we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son, much more, being reconciled, we shall be saved by his life"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 5:9).

Having "defused" the situation, Jesus speaks to the leaders in the mob: ["Be ye come out, as against a thief ]{.class_4723}\[see our comments on 12:39\][\...When I was daily with you in the temple, ye stretched forth no hands against me"]{.class_4723} (vss. 52--53). Well, they tried in John 7:44--46, but the officers were so taken with Christ's [words]{.class_4725} that they couldn't go through with it. That was ["because his hour was not yet come"]{.class_4723} (John 7:30, 8:20). Here in Luke 22, the hour had finally come: ["but this is your hour, and the power of darkness"]{.class_4723} (vs. 53).

54 Then took they him, and led him, and brought him into the high priest's house. And Peter followed afar off.

55 And when they had kindled a fire in the midst of the hall, and were set down together, Peter sat down among them.

56 But a certain maid beheld him as he sat by the fire, and earnestly looked upon him, and said, This man was also with him.

57 And he denied him, saying, Woman, I know him not.

58 And after a little while another saw him, and said, Thou art also of them. And Peter said, Man, I am not.

59 And about the space of one hour after another confidently affirmed, saying, Of a truth this fellow also was with him: for he is a Galilaean.

60 And Peter said, Man, I know not what thou sayest. And immediately, while he yet spake, the cock crew.

61 And the Lord turned, and looked upon Peter. And Peter remembered the word of the Lord, how he had said []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_632}unto him, Before the cock crow, thou shalt deny me thrice.

62 And Peter went out, and wept bitterly.

With the end of Christ's words in verse 53, the soldiers grab Jesus and haul Him off to the house of the high priest. When they do, all the disciples bolt (Mark 14:50). This was a fulfillment of Zechariah 13:7 (see our remarks on the verse in that Commentary).

As we saw in 3:2, there were two high priests serving at the same time: Annas and his son-in-law Caiaphas. Jesus is taken to Annas first (John 18:13).

["And Peter followed afar off"]{.class_4723} (vs. 54). Now what you have in verses 54--62 is the account of Peter denying Christ. It is a great picture of a Christian getting out of fellowship with his Lord. Notice that it starts out with the believer following the Lord [at a distance.]{.class_4725} He doesn't want to get too close lest he suffer the reproach and persecution that comes with following Christ (1 Tim. 4:10; 2 Tim. 3:12).

Now Peter was not the only disciple to follow Jesus; John did too (John 18:15). Since John had some kind of connection to the high priest, he went in to see the trial of Jesus before Annas, and he had the servant girl who ["kept the door"]{.class_4723} bring Peter in too (John 18:15--17). While John was observing the proceedings, ["Peter sat without in the palace"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 26:69). He's in the hall of the high priest (a kind of open-air pavilion), but he's not with John observing the proceeding of Christ's trial. Instead, he's at the other end of the building with a group of servants and soldiers around a fire (vs. 55; John 18:18).

That was the next step in Peter's backsliding. He warmed himself by the Devil's fire. He kept company with unsaved men. These were the same men who had come to the garden to take Jesus (John 18:26), and now Peter is sitting with them and warming himself at their fire. That's a picture of a Christian getting back in fellowship with the world that hates his Saviour (John 15:18, 25).

Now Peter is about to deny his Lord, and the trouble is that he is mad at Jesus. Peter wanted to go with Jesus to prison and death (vs. 33), and the Lord wouldn't let him do it (John 18:8--9). He was ready to fight to the death to prove his love for Christ, and the Lord []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_633}stopped him. All of that made him look like a coward, and Peter was no coward. That was the trap the Devil set for Peter (see our remarks on vs. 31). Peter was brave when it came to physical combat, but he had a hard time sitting and taking a beating for Christ's sake.

["But a certain maid beheld him\...and said, This man was also with him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 56). Now look at that: Peter is about to deny the Lord to the girl who let him in the hall (John 18:19). (John had played a dirty trick on Peter. He sicked the door maid on him trying to prod him into joining him to observe the trial and give the Lord some moral support. What the girl said was not so much an accusation as an invitation.) Do you think he's scared of a girl? Of course not! I mean, he had been ready to single-handedly fight off the soldiers who came to take Jesus; he wasn't scared of this girl. He's just mad at the Lord.

Did you ever get mad at the Lord? Suppose you were God. Would you have handled some situations in your life the way He handled them? I can think of a couple of things in my own life that I sure wouldn't have worked out the way the Lord did. If I were God, I would have fixed the situation before it got bad, but [He didn't.]{.class_4725}

Now when you get in a situation like that where the Lord lets it get in a first-rate mess (from your point of view), what do you do? How do you react? Well, you can get upset with the Lord and gripe and complain about it, but the murmuring and the fussing don't do you any good. You can get an attitude about it and say to God, "Well if that's the way you feel about it, watch this\...." Then you backslide and go out and live like you want just to show Him who's the boss. That's really going to teach the Lord a lesson. Or you can accept what the Lord has allowed to happen and wait on Him. Peter hasn't accepted what happened in the garden yet.

The girl says, "You were with Jesus." Peter replies, ["Woman, I know him not"]{.class_4723} (vs. 57). That's the one who said, ["Thou art the Christ, the Son of the living God"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 16:16). Now he's saying, "I don't even know the man."

Luke says the next person to confront Peter was a [man]{.class_4725} (vs. 58); Matthew and Mark say it was a [woman]{.class_4725} (Matt. 26:71; Mark 14:69). John solves this apparent discrepancy by saying, ["THEY]{.class_4723} \[plural\][ said therefore unto him"]{.class_4723} (John 18:25).

About an hour later a fellow who has been staring intently at Peter ever since his second denial speaks up and says, "Sure you're one of His disciples. You're from Galilee; I can tell it by the way you speak" (vs. 59; Mark 14:70). When it came to Israel, Peter was a "Yankee": he had a northern accent from Galilee in the north of Israel.

That's true of just about any country. There's a difference between the way a Prussian in the north of Germany speaks as compared to a Bavarian in the south of Germany. Different sections of a country have different accents. Texans have a drawl. People from Minnesota and Wisconsin talk through their noses. New England Yankees won't pronounce the "r" at the end of a word: they say, "Go pawk the caw down by the baw." A lot of Southerners, especially in the country and in small towns, have a kind of lazy way of speaking that results in a hillbilly twang.

You can generally tell from where a person is by the way he speaks. It comes out even when you don't think it does. There is one of our workers at the Bookstore who doesn't have a particularly noticeable Southern accent, but when he visits his in-laws in northern Pennsylvania, he always gets, "Oh, I just love your Southern accent." Others can tell even when you don't think they can. That's what's going on with Peter here.

The account in Matthew has an interesting way of putting it: ["thy speech bewrayeth thee"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 26:73). That's an archaic way of saying that Peter's speech betrayed from where he was. Now it's talking about Peter being from Galilee, but you can make spiritual application to the speech of a backslidden Christian. Peter may have been hanging out with the unsaved crowd, but he was still talking like a "Christian." That Book will ruin you for life.

"Aren't you a follower of Jesus, Peter, because you sure talk like one?" And just to prove he's not one of Christ's disciples, Peter begins to cuss a blue streak (Matt. 26:74; Mark 14:71). The soldiers' eyes get wide, and they take notes to use in drill the next day. By the time Peter gets through, someone says, "Well, no disciple of Jesus would ever talk like that," and they leave him alone. Peter has successfully shot his testimony before an unsaved world.

Did you ever stop to think, what if that girl and those servants []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_635}and soldiers who heard Peter cuss that night had been at Pentecost in Acts 2 and heard Peter preach? Why, you know what they would have said; human nature hasn't changed any in 2,000 years. They would have said, "Look at that hypocrite! You call that a Christian? Why, I heard him use words that would make a sailor blush. Blah, blah, blah." That's a bunch of self-righteous people looking at the way you live, Christian, in order to find an excuse to live like the devil. Give them as little "ammunition" as possible.

Now there is an apparent contradiction between verses 34, 60 and Mark 14:30. Marks says, ["before the cock crow TWICE, thou shalt deny me thrice"]{.class_4723} (Mark 14:30), and then he gives you the two times the cock crows (Mark 14:68, 72). Both Luke and Matthew (Matt. 26:34) only cite one cock crowing. Now aside from the obvious explanation that Mark gives a more detailed account than the other two Gospel writers (see our remarks on Bartimaeus under 18:35) is the fact that ["cockcrowing"]{.class_4723} was a watch of the night (Mark 13:35). Mark is referring to the number of sounds one rooster made, while Luke and Matthew refer to the time of night. The ["cockcrowing"]{.class_4723} watch was from midnight to three in the morning, so before this watch started, Peter denied Jesus three times.

Now as Peter gets through cussing, Jesus' appearance before Annas has ended, and He is being led away to appear before Caiaphas (John 18:24). About the time that cock crows, Jesus passes by Peter and looks at him (vs. 61). He doesn't say anything; He doesn't have to. The Holy Spirit is already working Peter over. Peter looks into Jesus' eyes and hears Him say in his mind, ["Before the cock crow, thou shalt deny me thrice."]{.class_4723} That just tears Peter up. He runs out of that hall, sobbing and crying, and heads straight to Gethsemane to pray like he should have a few hours earlier. I guarantee you he didn't fall asleep in that prayer meeting. He was still sobbing and crying out to God when the sun came up.

63 And the men that held Jesus mocked him, and smote him.

64 And when they had blindfolded him, they struck him on the face, and asked him, saying, Prophesy, who is it that smote thee?

65 And many other things blasphemously spake they against him.

66 And as soon as it was day, the elders of the people and the chief priests and the scribes came together, and led him into their council, saying,

67 Art thou the Christ? tell us. And he said unto them, If I tell you, ye will not believe:

68 And if I also ask you, ye will not answer me, nor let me go.

69 Hereafter shall the Son of man sit on the right hand of the power of God.

70 Then said they all, Art thou then the Son of God? And he said unto them, Ye say that I am.

71 And they said, What need we any further witness? for we ourselves have heard of his own mouth.

Now Jesus is before Caiaphas. Of course, the whole trial is illegal. It is a "kangaroo court" made to railroad Christ into a conviction and execution with no "justice" involved at all. Both the Mosaic and Talmudic laws were violated.

Jesus was arrested without warrant or charge.

He was arrested at night.

He was tried by prejudiced jurors.

He was privately examined by Annas.

The two main magistrates were related to each other.

He was abused without condemnation (John 18:22--23).

He was allowed no defense and had no defense attorney from the Sanhedrin appointed to him.

He had false witnesses brought against Him (Exod. 21:1; Deut. 19:16--19).

He was forced to testify against Himself and condemned on that testimony (vss. 66--71).

He was condemned with no collaborating testimony (Deut. 17:6, 19:15 cf. Mark 14:55--59).

He was tried on the eve of a feast day and a special sabbath (cf. John 19:31).

He was tried in a capital case that took place in less than a day.

He was condemned with no examination into the legitimacy of His claims (vss. 67--68 cf. Prov. 18:13; John 7:51).

Now those things are illegal in anybody's court. But that's the kind of situation you are in today. The government has given itself the power to have you arrested and held with no warrant, no trial, and no due process. Your property and assets can be seized by the IRS, the DEA, and the BATF based on [accusation.]{.class_4725} For further reading on the subject, get volume five of [Twenty-two Years of the Bible Believer's Bulletin]{.class_4725} on "Politics and Conspiracies."

Verse 64 is a form of the game "blind man's bluff." The soldiers blindfold Christ and then move around Him, striking Him on the face and taunting Him to tell them who struck Him. The idea is: "If you're really the Christ and the Prophet like unto Moses (Matt. 26:68), then you ought to be able to tell us who hit you." Jesus could have given out the names, ranks, and serial numbers of every "dogface" in that room if He had wanted to, but He chose not to exercise His Deity. The Bible says, ["he humbled himself, and became obedient unto death"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 2:8), ["that he by the grace of God should taste death for every man"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 2:9).

["And many other things blasphemously spake they against him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 65). They're mocking and ridiculing His claims to be the Messiah and God. During His ministry, His enemies called Him ["a Samaritan"]{.class_4723} and said He had ["a devil"]{.class_4723} (John 8:48). They said He was ["mad"]{.class_4723} (John 10:20) and was ["born of fornication"]{.class_4723} (John 8:41). They're saying that about [God in the flesh.]{.class_4725}

Now up to verse 66, Jesus has been privately tried by the two high priests, Annas and Caiaphas. That was illegal according to Jewish law. No man was to be tried in private. Verse 66 says, ["And as soon as it was day,"]{.class_4723} Jesus was ["led\...into their council."]{.class_4723} The word [council]{.class_4725} appears 24 times in the New Testament (Matt. 5:22, 10:17, 12:14, 26:59; Mark 13:9, 14:55, 15:1; Luke 22:66; John 4:47; Acts 4:15, 5:21, 27, 34, 41, 6:12, 15, 20, 28, 24:20, 28:12), and in every instance except one (Matt. 5:22), it refers to a group of religious people organized to [oppose the truth.]{.class_4725} That is why the Holy Spirit is careful never to refer to the gathering of the apostles and elders in Acts 15 as a "council." When you read about the National [Council]{.class_4725} of Churches, the World [Council]{.class_4725} of Churches, and the ecumenical [council,]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_638} you aren't dealing with Bible-believing Christians at all; those are apostate Modernists and Liberals and lost Catholics coming together to get rid of the Gospel of the Lord Jesus Christ and the truth of the Scriptures. The council of the Jews here was known as the Sanhedrin, and according to Jewish law, it was illegal for them to meet and hold a trial before the morning sacrifice as they did in verse 66.

Verse 67: ["Art thou the Christ? tell us."]{.class_4723} In verse 70, they ask, ["Art thou then the Son of God?"]{.class_4723}; Mark says ["Son of the Blessed"]{.class_4723} (Mark 14:61). The question is put to Him directly after He refuses to answer His accusers (Matt. 26:62--63; Mark 14:60--61). Isaiah said, ["He was oppressed, and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth: he is brought as a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before her shearers is dumb, so he openeth not his mouth"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 53:7).

But now He is being put under oath. He is being called on to testify against Himself before a jury prejudiced against Him. He had already told them ["plainly"]{.class_4723} back in John 10 (see John 10:24--28). The problem was they weren't[ "of God"]{.class_4723}; that's why they didn't believe (John 8:46--47). They weren't any more prepared to believe Him here than they were back in John 8, 10.

Now He ends up answering them directly. He says, ["Ye say that I am"]{.class_4723} (vs. 70), which is His way of saying ["I am"]{.class_4723} (Mark 14:62); in other words, "You said it and you got it right." But before He does that, He prefaces it with: ["If I tell you, ye will not believe."]{.class_4723} He was right: they didn't. They weren't there to get the truth---legally or spiritually. They were there to find some accusation with which to charge Him.

How would you like to go to trial and not know what the charges were against you until the trial was over? In fact, the whole purpose of the trial was to figure out some charge against you that would stick. That was the case with Christ. No wonder He wasn't allowed a defense. How could you defend against what you don't know?

["And if I also ask you, ye will not answer me, nor let me go"]{.class_4723} (vs. 68). No cross-examination or acquittal allowed. Jesus knew He wasn't getting out of there without a guilty verdict and a death sentence.

Now what Jesus answers them with is a prophecy: []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_639}["Hereafter shall the Son of man sit on the right hand of the power of God"]{.class_4723} (vs. 69). In other words, if they kill Him, that's not going to stop Him from fulfilling Psalm 110:1 (see our comments on 20:42--43).

That's what got them so riled up at Stephen in Acts 7. Stephen didn't see Jesus sitting at the Father's right hand; he saw Him [standing]{.class_4725} getting ready to [come back]{.class_4725} (Acts 7:56). When Stephen told the council what he saw, they went berserk: ["Then they cried out with a loud voice, and stopped their ears]{.class_4723} \[they didn't want to hear it\][, and ran upon him with one accord, And cast him out of the city, and stoned him"]{.class_4723} (Acts 7:57--58).

Matthew and Mark add this to what Luke recorded in verse 69:[ "and coming in the clouds of heaven"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 26:64; Mark 14:62)---Second Advent. That's Revelation 14:14--20 (which see). John wrote, ["Behold, he cometh with clouds; and every eye shall see him, AND THEY ALSO WHICH PIERCED HIM]{.class_4723} \[see Zech. 12:10\][: and all kindreds of the earth shall wail because of him. Even so, Amen"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 1:7).

"Jesus loves me. This I know, for the Bible tells me so." Yeah, well, He's coming back to [squash you]{.class_4725} if you don't get saved (Isa. 63:1--6); the Bible tells you [that's so]{.class_4725} too!

["And they said, What need we any further witness? for we ourselves have heard of his own mouth"]{.class_4723} (vs. 71). They finally have their charge---[blasphemy]{.class_4725} (Matt. 26:65; Mark 14:63--64). And, of course, the sentence for blasphemy was death (Matt. 26:66; Mark 14:64 cf. Lev. 24:16).

Of course, what Jesus said was only blasphemy if He weren't ["the Christ, the Son of God"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 26:63). Over in John 10, when they said, ["If thou be the Christ, tell us plainly"]{.class_4723} (John 10:24), Jesus produced His [works]{.class_4725} as evidence that He was the Christ (John 10:25). He said down in John 10:37--38, ["If I do not the works of my Father, believe me not. But if I do, though ye believe not me, believe the works: that ye may know, and believe, that the Father is in me, and I in him."]{.class_4723} The Sanhedrin makes no examination of the witness that God the Father gave of His Son. In fact, in the case of the raising of Lazarus, they tried to [eliminate]{.class_4725} the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4052.xhtml_page_640}evidence (John 12:10).

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml}

# Chapter 23 {.heading_78-0}

23:1 And the whole multitude of them arose, and led him unto Pilate.

2 And they began to accuse him, saying, We found this fellow perverting the nation, and forbidding to give tribute to Caesar, saying that he himself is Christ a King.

3 And Pilate asked him, saying, Art thou the King of the Jews? And he answered him and said, Thou sayest it.

4 Then said Pilate to the chief priests and to the people, I find no fault in this man.

5 And they were the more fierce, saying, He stirreth up the people, teaching throughout all Jewry, beginning from Galilee to this place.

6 When Pilate heard of Galilee, he asked whether the man were a Galilaean.

7 And as soon as he knew that he belonged unto Herod's jurisdiction, he sent him to Herod, who himself also was at Jerusalem at that time.

Now the Sanhedrin hauls Jesus off to Pilate. When they get there, they won't go in to Pilate's judgment hall because they don't want to be defiled so they can eat the Passover (John 18:28). You talk about a bunch of hypocrites, boy! They're worried about being around a Gentile while they're lying about a righteous man in order to have him murdered. You mean lying and murder aren't enough to defile you?!

Notice that the charge brought against Christ has changed in verse 2. Back up in 22:71, it was blasphemy, but they had to switch the charge to get it by Pilate. When they first bring Jesus to Pilate, they try to get the Governor to pass a summary judgment without any clear charge.

["Pilate then went out unto them, and said, What accusation bring ye against this man? They answered and said unto him, If he were not a malefactor, we would not have delivered him up unto thee"]{.class_4723} (John 18:29--30).

Now Pilate's not stupid. He knows they're evading the question in an attempt to manipulate him. So he says, ["Take ye him, and judge him according to your law"]{.class_4723} (John 18:31). Jesus had already been judged by them, and they had [broken their law]{.class_4725} to do it (see our remarks on Christ's trial under 22:63).

The council's comeback to Pilate is: ["It is not lawful for us to put any man to death"]{.class_4723} (John 18:31). Of course, that didn't stop them from stoning Stephen in Acts 7, but Stephen wasn't popular with the people like Christ was (see our remarks under 19:47--48). They wanted the Romans to kill Jesus so it wouldn't look like they were guilty of His death (see Acts 5:28).

But while the Pharisees and Sadducees are busy carrying out their schemes against Christ, God is using them to fulfill Scripture. The penalty for blasphemy was [stoning]{.class_4725} (Lev. 24:16), but the Scriptures had prophesied that not a bone of Christ would be broken (Psa. 34:20 cf. Exod. 12:46). As Jesus Himself prophesied, He would have to suffer a [Gentile]{.class_4725} form of execution (18:32--33) in order to be ["made a curse for us"]{.class_4723} (Gal. 3:13).

Now the priests and scribes know that a blasphemy charge will get nowhere with Pilate. Oh, they eventually bring it up, and it shakes Pilate up pretty badly (John 18:7--8). But when it came to religious matters, the Romans, at that time, didn't care how or what their conquered subjects worshipped (see Acts 18:12--15). So the accusation against Jesus is changed to [tax evasion]{.class_4725} and [sedition.]{.class_4725}

["We found this fellow perverting the nation"]{.class_4723} (vs. 2)---[a lie.]{.class_4725} They found Him ["doing good, and healing all that were oppressed of the devil"]{.class_4723} (Acts 10:38). ["And forbidding to give tribute to Caesar"]{.class_4723}---[another lie]{.class_4725} (see our comments on 20:25). ["Saying that he himself is Christ a King"]{.class_4723}---[a half truth]{.class_4725} (which makes it a [whole lie]{.class_4725}). When Pilate asks Jesus, ["Art thou the king of the Jews?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 3), Christ doesn't deny it: ["Thou sayest it"]{.class_4723} (You said it). Having gotten confirmation of the final accusation, do you know what Pilate does? He goes out to the Jews and says, ["I find no fault in this man"]{.class_4723} (vs. 4).

Now why would he do that when Rome allowed no King but one it set up under its own control? Because over in John, Jesus tells Pilate what kind of King He was [right then.]{.class_4725}

["Jesus answered, My kingdom is not of this world: if my kingdom were of this world, then would my servants fight, that I should not be delivered to the Jews: but now is my kingdom not from hence"]{.class_4723} (John 18:36).

The literal, visible, physical kingdom had been postponed; the only kingdom right then (and presently in this age) was the spiritual Kingdom of God. To make sure that Christ's kingdom will [never]{.class_4725} be ["of this world,"]{.class_4723} corrupt perversions of the Scriptures, like the [ASV, NASV, RSV, NRSV,]{.class_4725} and [ESV,]{.class_4725} get rid of the word ["now"]{.class_4723} from the verse.

[Right then,]{.class_4725} though, Christ wasn't there to be a King over the political kingdom of Israel and overthrow Rome. He told Pilate, ["To this end was I born, and for this cause came I into the world, THAT I SHOULD BEAR WITNESS UNTO THE TRUTH"]{.class_4723} (John 18:37). Pilate wasn't concerned with the truth (John 18:38), so he went out to the Jews and told them: ["I find in him no fault at all"]{.class_4723} (John 18:38). Jesus had broken no Roman law; He should have been set free.

Now look at that thing. A man is brought to trial, and the judge finds Him [not guilty.]{.class_4725} Is He set free? Oh, no! He is tried a [second]{.class_4725} time on the exact same charge (vs. 14)---[double jeopardy.]{.class_4725} Having been declared innocent a second time, He's tried a [third ]{.class_4725}time---[triple jeopardy]{.class_4725}---and found innocent by the same judge three times in a row (vs. 22). How would you like to be tried for the same crime three times, and be found [innocent]{.class_4725} all three times, and then be sentenced to death? That's what happened to Jesus Christ.

You talk about a miscarriage of justice, the trial of Jesus Christ was it. Not only did the judge find Him innocent, so did the judge's wife (Matt. 27:19), the man who betrayed Him (Matt. 27:4), and the man who carried out the execution (vs. 47). He asked His enemies one time, ["Which of you convinceth me of sin?"]{.class_4723} (John 8:46), and [no one opened his mouth.]{.class_4727}

["And they were the more fierce"]{.class_4723} (vs. 5). They become angrier and more indignant; they are out for blood. ["He stirreth up the people."]{.class_4723} Well, not against Rome, no. He stirred up the publicans, harlots, and sinners to get right. He stirred up the Pharisees, scribes, and Sadducees against Him because He preached []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_644}against their hypocrisy. But He didn't stir up the people to loot, riot, or revolt.

The word ["Jewry"]{.class_4723} in verse 5 originally referred to the Israelites in the southern kingdom of Judah (Dan. 5:13), but notice that here, it includes the northern kingdom of Israel as well (["beginning from Galilee"]{.class_4723}). Peter wasn't from Judah, but he was a [Jew]{.class_4725} (Gal. 2:14). The idea propagated by the anti-Semites that Jews aren't the nation of Israel chosen by God in the Old Testament is a bald-faced[ lie.]{.class_4725}

Now Pilate is trying to get rid of Jesus Christ. He has already gotten in trouble with Rome over his handling of the Jews (see our comments on 13:1); the last thing he needs is problems with the Jewish leaders over this would-be King brought before him. So when he finds out that Jesus is from Galilee, Pilate jumps at the chance to pawn Jesus off on Herod, who is in Jerusalem for the Passover (vs. 7). He figures that Galilee is ["Herod's jurisdiction,"]{.class_4723} so he'll let Herod handle the problem.

8 And when Herod saw Jesus, he was exceeding glad: for he was desirous to see him of a long season, because he had heard many things of him; and he hoped to have seen some miracle done by him.

9 Then he questioned with him in many words; but he answered him nothing.

10 And the chief priests and scribes stood and vehemently accused him.

11 And Herod with his men of war set him at nought, and mocked him, and arrayed him in a gorgeous robe, and sent him again to Pilate.

12 And the same day Pilate and Herod were made friends together: for before they were at enmity between themselves.

Herod is excited to see Jesus. He has been wanting to see Him since 9:9 because he thought Jesus was ["John the Baptist\...risen from the dead"]{.class_4723} (Mark 6:14). Bad conscience: Herod had seared his conscience with a belly dancer. Before that, he listened to John preach and did a lot of things John told him to do (Mark 6:20). But []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_645}once he had John executed to fulfill a rash promise to a dancing girl made in the heat of lust, the Lord stopped dealing with him. Herod defiled and seared his conscience, and the Lord had nothing more to say to him. The first place God deals with a man is in his conscience (Rom. 2:15), and when a fellow kills his conscience, God feels no obligation to deal with the man.

Jesus Christ stands before Herod, and Herod asks, "Are you the Christ? Are you the Messiah?" No answer. "Are you John the Baptist risen from the dead?" Silence. "Show me a sign; do a miracle." Nothing. There is one person that the Lord won't help or answer, and that's a dishonest skeptic. ["Then he questioned with him in many words; but he answered him nothing"]{.class_4723} (vs. 9).

Now although ["the chief priests and scribes\...vehemently accused him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 10), Herod is not in the least interested in trying Jesus Christ. He already has a bad conscience about murdering John the Baptist, and he's not going to be responsible for the blood of this Prophet of Nazareth too. So he and his soldiers mock Jesus and send Him back to Pilate.

["A gorgeous robe"]{.class_4723} is placed on Christ. According to Mark 15:17 and John 19:2, this robe was the [purple]{.class_4725} robe of a King (see our remarks under 16:19). Later on at His scourging, the purple robe is replaced by the ["scarlet robe"]{.class_4723} of the Roman infantryman (Matt. 27:28). Like the Roman guard in Matthew 27:29 and Mark 15:18, Herod and his men are bowing to Jesus and hailing Him in mockery as ["King of the Jews."]{.class_4723}

The ["robe"]{.class_4723} of verse 11 is the famous robe of Roman Catholic fiction. It has been popularized in American "Christianity" by Lloyd Douglas' book (and later on the movie) [The Robe]{.class_4725} and by George Beverly Shea's song "His robe, His robe, His wonderful robe." One should note that the Holy Spirit is careful never to call Christ's regular clothing a "robe." That woman in 8:43 with the ["issue of blood"]{.class_4723} didn't touch Christ's "robe" at all; she ["touched the border of his GARMENT"]{.class_4723} (8:44). When Jesus goes to the cross, ["the robe"]{.class_4723} is removed and replaced by ["his own RAIMENT"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 27:31). The soldiers at the cross don't gamble for Christ's "robe"; they throw dice for His ["VESTURE"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 27:35; John 19:24), called a ["COAT"]{.class_4723} in John 19:23. ["The robe"]{.class_4723} put on Christ is []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_646}connected with a Roman Whore (Rev. 17:4) and is not the clothing of a Jewish Messiah. It is the hypocritical scribes and Pharisees who loved to go around ["in long robes"]{.class_4723} (20:46), not the Lord Jesus Christ. "His robe, His robe, His wonderful robe" is a demoniac addition to the Biblical narrative.

Verse 12 reveals a great truth. Here were two Roman bureaucrats who were at odds with each other until Jesus Christ came along. At last they could agree on something; their "common ground" was [getting rid of Jesus Christ.]{.class_4725} If the world can't get together on any other issue, it can get together on [that.]{.class_4725}

Jesus Christ did not only come to bring ["division"]{.class_4723} (12:51), but to join certain people together as well. Jesus Christ joins people together who reject [Him]{.class_4725} and [His Book.]{.class_4725} ["Pilate and Herod were made friends together"]{.class_4723} over their rejection of Jesus Christ. "Mary" and "Mohammed" get together to overthrow what God said about that Jew. And when Jesus Christ comes back, He gets all 191 (+/-) Gentile nations in the UN together (Joel 3:11), and He gets them together to [burn them ]{.class_4725}(Zeph. 3:8; Matt. 13:30, 40--42). So the lost world is marked by [joining]{.class_4725} (conformity---Rom. 12:2), while the disciple of Christ is marked by [separation]{.class_4725} (2 Cor. 6:14--18).

13 And Pilate, when he had called together the chief priests and the rulers and the people,

14 Said unto them, Ye have brought this man unto me, as one that perverteth the people: and, behold, I, having examined him before you, have found no fault in this man touching those things whereof ye accuse him:

15 No, nor yet Herod: for I sent you to him; and, lo, nothing worthy of death is done unto him.

16 I will therefore chastise him, and release him.

17 (For of necessity he must release one unto them at the feast.)

18 And they cried out all at once, saying, Away with this man, and release unto us Barabbas:

19 (Who for a certain sedition made in the city, and for murder, was cast into prison.)

20 Pilate therefore, willing to release Jesus, spake again []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_647}to them.

21 But they cried, saying, Crucify him, crucify him.

22 And he said unto them the third time, Why, what evil hath he done? I have found no cause of death in him: I will therefore chastise him, and let him go.

23 And they were instant with loud voices, requiring that he might be crucified. And the voices of them and of the chief priests prevailed.

24 And Pilate gave sentence that it should be as they required.

25 And he released unto them him that for sedition and murder was cast into prison, whom they had desired; but he delivered Jesus to their will.

So far Pilate has tried [carelessness]{.class_4725} to get rid of Jesus Christ: "You take Him and judge Him according to your law." "According to our law, He ought to die, and you as part of the army of occupation have reserved capital punishment for yourself." So Pilate got Jesus Christ back on his hands.

Then Pilate tried [cowardice:]{.class_4725}[ ]{.class_4737}"Send Him to Herod and let Herod judge Him. He's part of Herod's jurisdiction; He's not my problem." But that didn't work either. Herod sent Christ back to Pilate, and Pilate had Him on his hands again.

Finally, in desperation, Pilate tries [compromise]{.class_4725} to rid himself of Jesus Christ. Pilate tried Jesus Christ and found Him innocent (vs. 14); Herod tried Jesus Christ and found Him innocent (vs. 15). Solution: ["I will therefore chastise him, and release him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 16).

Now Pilate was trying to get Jesus off his hands (vs. 20). He knew that the Sadducees and Pharisees had delivered Christ to him out of envy (Matt. 27:18), and in addition to that, his wife had a bad dream about Christ and warned her husband not to have anything to do with condemning a just man (Matt. 27:19). John says in his Gospel that the Jews had a custom of releasing a prisoner at the Feast of Passover (John 18:39), so Pilate figured he would rig the release. He gave the people a choice between Jesus and a prisoner named Barabbas (Matt. 27:17).

Luke says Barabbas was being held []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_648 .class-1}["for a certain sedition\...and for murder"]{.class_4730-0} (vs. 19). Mark says Barabbas ["committed murder in the insurrection"]{.class_4730-0} (Mark 15:7), and John adds, ["Now Barabbas was a robber"]{.class_4730-0} (John 18: 40). Pilate figured that the people would look at Jesus and say to themselves, "Well, Jesus is odd; He's kind of strange; He's different. But He never has killed anybody; He's never really done anybody any harm. It's not like He's committed adultery with anyone's wife or stolen anything. Let Him go." Then they would look at Barabbas and say, "That guy's a devil. He's a thief and a murderer. He's a seditionist, and if we let him go, he'll get us in trouble with Rome. Kill him!"

But that's not how human nature works. They looked at Barabbas and thought, "Well, he had a rough childhood. I mean to grow up in the kind of neighborhood he did, and to be called that all of your life; no wonder he turned out the way he did. He was just acting out the aggression instilled in him by society. He was just angry and couldn't help himself. In fact, [he's kind of like me."]{.class_4725} Then they looked over at Jesus and thought, "Now there's that Prophet. I never did like that guy. He's always quoting Scripture and telling people they need to repent and calling them evil. He's too strait; He's too dogmatic. He thinks He's right and everyone else is wrong. KILL HIM!"[ That's]{.class_4725} how people think.

Pilate looks out at the mob and says, "Whom do you want me to let go?" And the priests and scribes stir up that crowd with chants of "Release Barabbas!" (Mark 15:11). "What do you want me to do with this Jesus who is called Christ?" asks Pilate. ["Crucify him, crucify him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 21). And Pilate had Him right back on his hands again.

Jesus Christ is like the bad penny: He keeps coming back. You don't get rid of Him by carelessness, cowardice, or compromise. Sooner or later you will have to deal with Him.

Verse 22: ["And he said unto them the third time, Why, what evil hath he done? I have found no cause of death in him: I will therefore chastise him, and let him go." "Chastise him"]{.class_4723}? For what? What law had He broken? But you see, Pilate is trying to pacify the priests and scribes and at the same time get out of having Christ put to death. He figures the crowd has whipped themselves into a blood lust, and if he gives them a little []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_649}blood, they'll be satisfied and calm down.

Pilate has Jesus scourged. Roman scourging wasn't like a Jewish beating. In a Jewish beating, a person lay flat on his belly and was beaten across the back with a long rod (Deut. 25:2). The beating was not to exceed forty stripes (Deut. 25:2), so to make sure the Law was not broken, the beating was stopped at 39 stripes (2 Cor. 11:24).

Roman scourging was much worse. The Romans didn't use rods; they used a cat-of-nine-tails. That's a whip with nine long pieces of leather, and all along each piece of leather are shards of glass, pottery, and bone. When that cat-of-nine-tails was brought down on the victim, those leather strips would wrap around the person, and those sharp objects would dig into the skin, tearing the flesh out when the whip was drawn back. And the Romans didn't limit themselves to 39 or forty stripes either. The whipping would continue until the soldier administering it was worn out. By the time he was through, the flesh was in shreds, and the muscle and bones underneath were exposed. Many condemned criminals died of the scourging and never made it to the cross.

As Jesus was being scourged, one of the soldiers got the bright idea to make a crown for this Jewish King. He went over to a thorn bush and cut some of the long thorny branches off and plaited them into a crown (Mark 15:17). Now why was that? Because when Jesus died, He didn't just die for the sins of man; He died to redeem creation (Matt. 19:28; Rom. 8:19--23; Isa. 11:6--9). Part of the curse placed on this earth because of Adam's sin was that it would bring forth thorns (Gen. 3:18), so when Jesus died, He took that curse on His head.

The wounds Christ bore at His crucifixion represent the price paid for our sins. That crown of thorns wounded Him in His head for our sins of [thought.]{.class_4725} He was wounded in His hands because our hands have touched things they shouldn't have touched; He was wounded in His feet because our feet have gone places they shouldn't have gone. The spear pierced His side into His heart because of our sins of [affection.]{.class_4725} He was wounded across the back because of the burden of sin which we all bear.

I know there are a lot of things wrong with that Roman Catholic movie on "The Passion of the Christ," but I think it did some good. I []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_650}think it woke a lot of folks up as to exactly what Jesus went through in His sufferings. It wasn't like the pictures you see from Roman Catholic painters of the Renaissance. It was a horrific, bloody mess.

You take that crown of thorns. Those weren't thorns like you see on a rose bush, where the thorns are about a quarter of an inch long at the most. Those thorn bushes in Palestine have thorns on them that are two to three inches long and as sharp as an ice pick. Those thorns were so long and sharp that the soldiers couldn't shove the crown on Jesus' head with their hands. They had to beat it down on His forehead with a reed (Mark 15:19).

Those Roman soldiers beat Christ; they spat on Him and mocked Him. Finally, Pilate had Jesus brought out to the people. Jesus Christ stood there, beaten to a bloody pulp, with that crown of thorns on His head and the purple robe draped over His shoulders. Isaiah, speaking prophetically for Jesus Christ, says, ["I gave my back to the smiters, and my cheeks to them that plucked off the hair: I hid not my face from shame and spitting"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 50:6); he says, ["his visage was so marred more than any man, and his form more than the sons of men"]{.class_4723} (Isa. 52:14).

Pilate's impressed, boy. Pilate points to that bloody mess and says, ["Behold the man!"]{.class_4723} (John 19:5), just like Jesus was the only man who ever lived. And the people look at Christ and yell, "Kill Him!" And Pilate has Him right back on His hands again.

"You take Him and crucify Him," Pilate said, "I don't want anything to do with it."

Those Jews yell, ["By our law he ought to die, because he made himself the Son of God"]{.class_4723} (John 19:7). And when Pilate hears that, He turns as white as a sheet. He has Christ brought back into that judgment hall and says, "Where are you from (John 19:9)?"

Now why did he ask that? He already knew Jesus was from Galilee; that's why he had Christ sent to Herod (vss. 6--7). That was a "Star Trek" question. For the first time in this trial, Pontius Pilate begins to wonder if his prisoner isn't from "upstairs," and it scares the fire out of him.

Jesus Christ doesn't say a word; He just stands there. Pilate screams, "You'd better answer me! I have the power to kill you or let you go (John 19:10)!" Calmly but with strength and authority in His voice that belies the broken state of His body, Jesus says, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_651}["Thou couldest have no power at all against me, except it were given thee from above: therefore he that delivered me unto thee hath the greater sin"]{.class_4723} (John 19:11). And that's the last thing Jesus said to Pilate. The last word Pontius Pilate heard from the lips of the Son of God was the word ["sin."]{.class_4723} The next time Pilate faces Christ, he faces Him as a sinner being judged for his [sin]{.class_4725} (Acts 17:31).

Once more Pilate goes out to try to reason with the Jews to release Jesus. "If you let Him go, you won't be Caesar's friend, because He says He's a King, and whoever makes himself a King is against Caesar (John 19:12)."

Pilate looks at that mob, and he knows there's nothing more to be done. The decision is his alone now, despite his best efforts to "pass the buck." He can either go with what he knows is right, or he can cave in to the Pharisees and Sadducees for political expediency.

I have often thought what would have happened if Pontius Pilate had done the right thing. Suppose he brought Jesus Christ out to that mob screaming for His blood and placed his robe on Christ's shoulders, and took his laurel wreath and placed it on Christ's head, and said, "There's your King! Crucify me!" Why, if he had done that, there would have been more books written about him than there have been about Napoleon or Abraham Lincoln, but he couldn't rise to the occasion. Pontius Pilate didn't have the courage of his convictions.

Luke says, ["Pilate gave sentence that it should be as THEY required"]{.class_4723} (vs. 24). What did they "require"? Why, ["they required"]{.class_4723} a [Roman dictator]{.class_4725} (John 19:15) and the [blood guilt of their Messiah]{.class_4725} (Matt. 27:25). Those priests and scribes rejected Jesus Christ and had Him crucified, and in His place, they got 2,000 years of persecution from [Rome.]{.class_4725} It was [papal Rome]{.class_4725} that put the yellow stars on the Jews ([Fourth Lateran Council]{.class_4725}), put them in ghettos ([Pope]{.class_4725} [Paul IV]{.class_4725}), declared them to be sub-human ([St. Peter the Venerable of Cluny]{.class_4725}), deprived them of citizenship ([Pope Paul V and Pope Clement VII]{.class_4725}), had them tortured and murdered ([Spanish Inquisition]{.class_4725}), and had them exiled so their countries would be ["Judenrein"]{.class_4725} ([Spain, 1492]{.class_4725}). Adolf Hitler, [a baptized, confirmed Roman Catholic]{.class_4725} who had a concordat with [Pope Pius XI,]{.class_4725} said, "As for the Jews, I am just carrying on with []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_652}[the same policy which the Catholic Church has adopted for fifteen hundred years,]{.class_4725} when it has regarded the Jews as dangerous and pushed them into ghettos, etc., because it knew what the Jews were like" (cited by J. S. Consay in [The Nazi Persecution of the Churches]{.class_4725}). [That's]{.class_4725} what the Jews ["required,"]{.class_4723} and they'll get it until Zechariah 12 is fulfilled. The biggest and best Roman dictator is yet to come (Rev. 17), and the holocaust he brings to the Jews will make Hitler's Holocaust look like a Sunday picnic in the park.

["And he released unto them him that for sedition and murder was cast into prison, whom they had desired"]{.class_4723} (vs. 25). That's Barabbas (vs. 18). The name means "son of the father" ([Bar]{.class_4725}---"son of," as in "Simon Barjona"---Matt. 16:17; and [abbas]{.class_4725}---"father," see Mark 14:36; Rom. 8:15). Barabbas was A "son of the father" who had THE Son of the Father, Jesus Christ, take his place on the cross. You see, Barabbas was supposed to be the third thief on the cross that day, but he was released, and Jesus Christ took his place (see vs. 33). I call Barabbas "the only man who understood the Crucifixion" because he understood 1 Peter 2:24 in a way no one else could: Jesus Christ [literally]{.class_4725} died in Barabbas' place.

["But he delivered Jesus to their will."]{.class_4723} Now that's what a preacher does every time he gives an invitation to sinners to receive Christ. Jesus Christ is turned over to the will of sinners: they either [will]{.class_4725} accept Him as their Saviour, or they [will]{.class_4725} reject Him. Jesus said, ["And ye WILL NOT come to me, that ye might have life"]{.class_4723} (John 5:40). The Holy Spirit says, ["WHOSOEVER WILL, let him take the water of life freely"]{.class_4723} (Rev. 22:17).

The problem is in the [will.]{.class_4725} When Abraham's servant went to get a wife for Isaac, Rebekah was asked: ["Wilt thou go with this man? And she said, I WILL go"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 24:58). So in a [traditional]{.class_4725} marriage ceremony (not these new Frisco-fairy-fruit "weddings"), the bride is asked: "Wilt thou have this man to be thy lawfully wedded husband?" And the reply is: "I will" or "I do." If she gets "cold feet," I guess the answer is "I won't." But she either [will]{.class_4725} or she [won't.]{.class_4725}

[Will]{.class_4725} you receive Jesus Christ as your personal Saviour? You either [will]{.class_4725} or you [won't.]{.class_4725} There is no middle ground.

"In the silent midnight watches,

Hark, thy bosom door:

Someone knocking, knocking;

Someone knocking ever more.

"Say not 'tis my pulse beating,

For 'tis thy heart of sin,

And thy Saviour knocking, crying,

Soul arise and let me in.

"Death comes on with wreckless footsteps

To palace, home, and hut.

Think you death will tarry knocking

Where the door is shut?

"Jesus standing, waiting,

But your sinful heart stands fast.

Grieved, alone the Saviour leaveth;

Death comes in at last.

"Then will you be standing,

Begging Christ to let thee in;

Beating at the gate of Heaven,

Covered with your sin.

"God forbid, you guilty sinner!

Heaven's not your lot.

Jesus waited long to know thee,

But now He knows thee not."

Do you see how things go? In this life, God has turned His Son over to your will. Pilate asked, ["What shall I do then with Jesus which is called Christ?"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 27:11). Any cottin' pickin' thing you want to---RIGHT NOW. You can accept Him; you can reject Him; you can ignore Him; you can kick Him out of your schools; you can give Him the brush off; you can cuss Him and use His name as a cuss word; you can laugh at Him and make jokes about Him. [You can do anything you want;]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_654} it won't make any difference. And then one day, one second after you die, He'll do with you whatever [He wants.]{.class_4725} You will stand before Him, and you will have no will left to exercise. It will be [His will,]{.class_4725} and you will have nothing to say about it whatsoever. You had better make your decisions [now]{.class_4725} with [that]{.class_4725} in mind.

26 And as they led him away, they laid hold upon one Simon, a Cyrenian, coming out of the country, and on him they laid the cross, that he might bear it after Jesus.

27 And there followed him a great company of people, and of women, which also bewailed and lamented him.

28 But Jesus turning unto them said, Daughters of Jerusalem, weep not for me, but weep for yourselves, and for your children.

29 For, behold, the days are coming, in the which they shall say, Blessed are the barren, and the wombs that never bare, and the paps which never gave suck.

30 Then shall they begin to say to the mountains, Fall on us; and to the hills, Cover us.

31 For if they do these things in a green tree, what shall be done in the dry?

Now Jesus starts out bearing His cross (John 19:17), but He has been beaten within an inch of His life. To make sure Jesus is able to make the fifteen-minute climb to Gordon's Calvary north of Jerusalem ([not]{.class_4725} the Catholic Calvary that is [not]{.class_4725} ["without the gate"]{.class_4723}---Heb. 13:12), the Romans take a man ["coming out of the country"]{.class_4723} into Jerusalem (vs. 26), and they force him to bear Christ's cross the rest of the way up the hill (Matt. 27:32). This is Simon the Cyrenian. Cyrene was a coastal town in the north African province of Cyrenaica. It is about where Tunisia is today; it is around where Obama's Benghazi fiasco took place.

At this point, the Roman Catholic Church adds more of its mythology to the Scriptural account of the Gospel (see 1 Cor. 15:3--4 and note the phrase ["according to the scriptures"]{.class_4723} which occurs [twice]{.class_4725}) with its "twelve stations of the cross." The Catholics have []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_655}Christ falling twice: once here in verse 26 and again in verse 27. In Mel Gibson's movie "The Passion of the Christ," Jesus falls down, and Simon the Cyrenian steps up and helps Him out. Then Jesus falls again, and "Veronica" comes up to wipe His face and catches the image of His face on her hanky.

Absolute poppycock! You will search the Gospel accounts in vain for any mention of Jesus Christ stumbling or falling on the way to Calvary. You won't find it because [it's not there.]{.class_4725} And the image of a red-brown-haired European-Irish Christ is contrary to the description the [Holy Spirit]{.class_4725} gave you in His Book (see Song of Sol. 5:10--13 and comments in that Commentary). You may take the "twelve stations of the cross" and put them in "File 13" along with "Good Friday," eating fish on Friday, Santa Claus, and the Easter Bunny.

Back to Luke 23: ["And there followed him a great company of people, and of women"]{.class_4755} (vs. 27). Notice that the ["women"]{.class_4755} are separated from the ["people."]{.class_4755} The ["people"]{.class_4755} are there to see a public execution (vs. 35); the ["women"]{.class_4755} are there to bewail and lament Him (vs. 27). These are the women who followed Him and ministered to Him throughout His public ministry (vs. 49 cf. 8:2--3 and our comments on that passage).

Verses 29--30 are a prophecy on the Second Advent (see Matt. 24:19; Rev. 6:15--17). Jesus Christ could prophesy more on a fifteen-minute walk to His death than Mohammed could in a lifetime of over sixty years. Mohammed should have stuck with his camel trading because he couldn't prophesy "a lick."

Note the contrast between the ["green tree"]{.class_4723} in verse 31 and ["the dry."]{.class_4723} The reference is obviously to Israel. One is reminded immediately of the fig tree Christ cursed in Mark 11:13. It had green leaves but no fruit, so Jesus cursed it, and it ["dried up"]{.class_4723} (Mark 11:20).

That was a picture of Israel. Israel was green and flourishing with her Messiah right there exercising the Mosaic sign gifts, but she rejected the Messiah and had Him crucified. He comes up at the Resurrection and gives the Jews a second chance in the book of Acts, and they blow it again. So God sends His wrath on them ["to the uttermost"]{.class_4723} in A.D. 70 (1 Thess. 2:16), and the nation dries up. []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_656}Those Jews remain that way until 1917 (the Balfour Declaration) when God starts dealing with them as a nation again, and in 1948, He makes ["the dry tree to flourish"]{.class_4723} (see Ezek. 17:24), and for the first time since A.D. 70, that nation "shoots forth" (see 21:29--30 and our comments on the passage). As we pointed out under 21:31--32, the generation that saw that happen will be the generation that sees Christ's return. Barring disease or an accidental or unforeseen death, I think any saved child of God under fourteen, who lives a normal lifespan (Psa. 90:10), will live to see the Lord come back.

32 And there were also two other, malefactors, led with him to be put to death.

33 And when they were come to the place, which is called Calvary, there they crucified him, and the malefactors, one on the right hand, and the other on the left.

34 Then said Jesus, Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do. And they parted his raiment, and cast lots.

35 And the people stood beholding. And the rulers also with them derided him, saying, He saved others; let him save himself, if he be Christ, the chosen of God.

36 And the soldiers also mocked him, coming to him, and offering him vinegar,

37 And saying, If thou be the king of the Jews, save thyself.

38 And a superscription also was written over him in letters of Greek, and Latin, and Hebrew, THIS IS THE KING OF THE JEWS.

The word [malefactor]{.class_4725} from verse 32 means "a worker of evil." A benefactor is a worker of [good]{.class_4725} (see our remarks under 22:25); a malefactor is a worker of [bad.]{.class_4725} These ["malefactors"]{.class_4723} are the ["two thieves"]{.class_4723} of Matthew 27:38 and Mark 15:27. Mark says this was a fulfillment of Isaiah 53:12 (Mark 15:28 cf. Luke 22:37).

The Hyperdispensationalist Ethelbert Bullinger "loses it" here just as he did with the Triumphal Entry and Peter's denial of Christ []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_657}(see our remarks under John 18:25--27 in that Commentary). He has [five]{.class_4725} men crucified on Calvary: Jesus, two thieves, and two malefactors. That's an example of [wrongly]{.class_4725} dividing the word.

["Calvary"]{.class_4723} (vs. 33) is the Latin word for the Hebrew ["Golgotha"]{.class_4723} (John 19:17), and it means "bare skull." It is one of 28 Latin words found in the New Testament, and you can find these listed in Appendix 75 of the [Ruckman Reference Bible.]{.class_4725}

Of course, Moslems don't believe Jesus died on the cross (Sura 23:50, note 1723; 5:75 note 724; 3:54), even though an eyewitness who was [there at the cross]{.class_4725} and [saw Him die]{.class_4725} said He did (John 19:26--27, 30--35). If you call Mohammed a [liar]{.class_4725} for teaching that, according to Sharia ([Koranic]{.class_4725}) law, Moslems are to [kill]{.class_4725} you. That's what Obama and the district court judges want enforced here in the United States (it is already being enforced in England, France, and the Netherlands). That is, you can mock Jesus Christ, use His name as a cuss word, picture Him in urine (funded by taxpayers), call the Bible a lie over government television (PBS), and that's freedom of speech protected by the Constitution. But if you call Mohammed and the [Koran]{.class_4725} a lie, draw political cartoons against Mohammed, produce a short video against Mohammed, or even publish the [facts about the Satanic nature of the Koran]{.class_4725} ([Satanic Verses]{.class_4725} by Salman Rushdie), you are to be [killed]{.class_4725} according to Sharia law.

That is where you are headed in the United States, and it has already started. An atheist dressed up in a costume mocking Mohammed during a Halloween parade and was attacked and beaten by a Moslem. When the Moslem was brought to court on assault charges, the judge (who himself was a Moslem) threw the case out on the grounds that the atheist [got what he deserved]{.class_4725} for mocking "the prophet." That Moslem judge then proceeded to tell that atheist that if he were in a Moslem country, he should be beheaded according to the [Koran.]{.class_4725} In other words, the only thing that prevented that Moslem judge from [acting on what he believed]{.class_4725} was the fact that the Moslems were not in complete charge here---YET. Obama is doing all he can to see to it that that changes.

["There they crucified him, and the malefactors, one on the right hand, and the other on the left"]{.class_4723} (vs. 33). One time a fellow was over in Rome and saw a stain glass window with Christ in []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_658}the center and two men standing on either side of Him. He asked a Cardinal who happened to be standing there, "What's that a picture of?" "It's a picture of Christ," said the Cardinal. "Well, who are the two men with Him?" asked the man. "The one is Cardinal Spellman, and the other is Pope John XXIII," replied the Cardinal. "Oh," said the fellow, "I always knew Jesus was crucified between two thieves, but I never knew their names."

Verse 34 is the first in what is known as the "Seven Sayings of the Cross." That is, while Jesus is on the cross, there are seven times where what He said was recorded by the Holy Ghost. Any preacher worth his salt has preached on these seven sayings, and they make great preaching. They are as follows:

I. The Word of [Forgiveness:]{.class_4725} ["Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34).

II\. The Word of [Comfort:]{.class_4725} ["Woman, behold thy son!\...Behold thy mother!"]{.class_4723} (John 19:26--27). That's Jesus making sure His earthly mother will be cared for after His death.

III\. The Word of [Salvation:]{.class_4725} ["Verily I say unto thee, To day shalt thou be with me in paradise"]{.class_4723} (vs. 43).

IV\. The Word of [Pain:]{.class_4725} ["I thirst"]{.class_4723} (John 19:28). Jesus is experiencing the torments of Hell on the cross (cf. 16:24).

V. The Word of [Desolation:]{.class_4725}[ "My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 27:46; Mark 15:34). That's not a break in the Trinity; that's the cry of Jesus as a [man.]{.class_4725} Since Jesus is bearing the sins of mankind (1 Pet. 2:24), God forsakes the man Jesus as He would a sinner in Hell. This is the wrath of God poured out on sin, and the human nature of Jesus feels its full force.

VI\. The Word of [Victory:]{.class_4725}[ "It is finished"]{.class_4723} (John 19:30). Jesus fulfilled what He came to do: the atonement was [complete.]{.class_4725} ["Once for all\...for ever"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 10:10--14).

VII\. The Word of [Resignation:]{.class_4725} ["Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit"]{.class_4723} (vs. 46). Having accomplished the redemption for our sins, Jesus lays down His life voluntarily, as He said He would (John 10:18).

Now, Jesus prays for His murderers in verse 34. That's what Stephen did in Acts 7:60. That's not the Moslem response (see our comments on 6:27--28). In Islam, a martyr is not someone who []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_659}suffers persecution and is killed for what he believes; he is one who dies trying to [kill you for what you believe.]{.class_4725} Jesus Christ sets an example in His death for all those who would follow Him in persecution and martyrdom. There was many a martyr during the Middle Ages and the Reformation who prayed for his tormentors and executioners. As William Tyndale was being strangled and burned at the stake, he prayed that God would open the King of England's eyes: that was Henry VIII, the one who was having him executed.

["Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do"]{.class_4723} (vs. 34). Well, they knew [part]{.class_4725} of what they were doing. They knew they were killing an innocent man; Judas admitted as much (Matt. 27:4). The centurion who carried out the execution declared Jesus to be ["a righteous man"]{.class_4723} (vs. 47), and the people who saw Jesus die ["smote their breasts"]{.class_4723} in an Oriental gesture of sorrow over what they had done back in verses 18--24.

But what they don't realize is that they are crucifying [God in the flesh.]{.class_4725} They didn't believe He was the Son of God and that God was His Father. If they had, they wouldn't have had Him crucified.

["But we speak the wisdom of God in a mystery]{.class_4723} \[what ["mystery"]{.class_4723} is that? It's ["the mystery of godliness"]{.class_4723} mentioned over in 1 Tim. 3:16---keep on reading)[, even the hidden wisdom, which God ordained before the world unto our glory: Which none of the princes of this world knew ]{.class_4723}\[Pilate, Herod, Caiaphas, Annas\][: for had they known it, they would not have crucified THE LORD OF GLORY"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 2:7--8).

["And they parted his raiment, and cast lots"]{.class_4723}---a fulfillment of Psalm 22:18.

["And the rulers also with them derided him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 35). Those are the chief priests, scribes, and members of the Sanhedrin mocking Him and ridiculing Him as He hangs on the cross dying. Another fulfillment of prophecy (see Psa. 22:7--8, 12--13).

["He saved others; let him save himself, if he be Christ, the chosen of God."]{.class_4723} Now thank God He didn't. It was the very fact that He was the ["Christ, the chosen of God"]{.class_4723} that ensured He wouldn't save Himself (Phil. 2:8--9). If He had, there would be no way of having your sins removed, and you would have to pay for them yourself. He couldn't save Himself and you at the same time, which is []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_660}why He submitted Himself to the will of the Father and drank the cup of God's wrath on sin (see our comments on 22:42).

["And the soldiers also mocked him\...saying, If thou be the king of the Jews, save thyself"]{.class_4723} (vss. 36--37). Now what looks like the Jewish leaders and the Roman soldiers mocking Christ turns out to be the Devil trying to get Him to quit. In Isaiah 50:8, Jesus challenges Satan to step in close and give it all he's got.
