The Bible says that when Jesus was on the cross, He ["spoiled principalities and powers"]{.class_4723} and ["made a shew of them openly, triumphing over them in it"]{.class_4723} (Col. 2:14--15). Jesus took on the Devil publicly and won by a knockout to become the all-time champion of the universe.

["Coming to him, and offering him vinegar"]{.class_4723} (vs. 36). Matthew tells you the ["vinegar"]{.class_4723} was ["mingled with gall"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 27:34), and Mark tells you exactly what the ["vinegar"]{.class_4723} and ["gall"]{.class_4723} were: ["wine mingled with myrrh"]{.class_4723} (Mark 15:23). Once again Jesus Christ fulfills prophecy. This offer of the vinegar with gall was prophesied by David a thousand years before it took place (Psa. 69:21). There's nothing like it in the [Koran]{.class_4725} because Allah, Mohammed, and "Gabriel" the 600-winged angel didn't know enough about future history to run a fortune-telling booth at a carnival. The Bible can predict 33 specific details of one man's death anywhere from 400-1,500 years before it happens and have every one of them come right through on the money.

The mixture of wine--vinegar and myrrh was used to deaden the pain so the condemned man could bear the torments of his execution. That is what Solomon recommended wine for (Prov. 31:6). He [didn't]{.class_4725} recommend it for social occasions, for communion, for a night cap, to relax after work, to calm your nerves, or to celebrate Christmas, New Year's, the Fourth of July, or Groundhog Day. Solomon recommends it as a [medicine,]{.class_4725} which is exactly what Paul recommended it for in the New Testament (1 Tim. 5:23).

Now Christ did not drink the wine (Matt. 27:34; Mark 15:23). He tasted it to see what it was (Matt. 27:34), but when He knew what it was, He turned it down (John 19:29--30 is a second offering that is just vinegar, and Christ receives that just before He dies). You see, He's dying as a righteous King (Luke 23:38), not as a guilty criminal. Solomon says, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_661}["it is not for kings to drink wine; nor for princes strong drink"]{.class_4723} (Prov. 31:4), so Christ doesn't drink it. Besides that, Jesus is taking the full punishment of sin for us on the cross (Isa. 53:3--6), so He doesn't take anything that would lessen the pain and suffering of sin's penalty.

Corrupt perversions of the Scripture, like the [NASV, NIV, RSV,]{.class_4725} etc. (Fundamentalists, Evangelicals, Liberals all produce the same trash when abandoning what God said), leave out the fact that the inscription above the cross was in three languages (vs. 38), once more destroying truth revealed by the Holy Spirit in His Book. Those three languages---Hebrew, Greek, and Latin---represent the three main races of people from Noah's three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth. Hebrew---that's Shem, the Oriental. Greek---that's the European; that's Japheth. Latin wasn't originally a European language; it came from [north Africa.]{.class_4725} So Latin represents Ham. In other words, the King of the Jews who was dying on the cross wasn't just for the nation of Israel; He is the ["king over all the earth"]{.class_4723} prophesied by Zechariah (Zech. 14:9). He is dying to save any person in the world, regardless of whether he is Caucasian, Negroid, or Mongoloid.

In a desperate attempt to get out of answering to Jesus Christ at the judgment day (Acts 17:31; Rom. 2:16), the infidels and skeptics will claim that the Bible contradicts itself as to exactly what was written on the inscription on the cross. But this is really grasping at straws. It doesn't take a genius to figure out that each of the Gospels gives [part]{.class_4725} of what is written, and to get the full inscription, Matthew 27:37; Mark 15:26; Luke 23:38; and John 19:19 should all be taken [together.]{.class_4725} The full inscription would read: "THIS IS JESUS OF NAZARETH THE KING OF THE JEWS."

39 And one of the malefactors which were hanged railed on him, saying, If thou be Christ, save thyself and us.

40 But the other answering rebuked him, saying, Dost not thou fear God, seeing thou art in the same condemnation?

41 And we indeed justly; for we receive the due reward of our deeds: but this man hath done nothing amiss.

42 And he said unto Jesus, Lord, remember me when thou comest into thy kingdom.

43 And Jesus said unto him, Verily I say unto thee, To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.

Now we come to the two thieves being crucified with Jesus. Luke says, ["ONE of the malefactors\...railed on him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 39; the word [rail,]{.class_4725} here, means "to scoff; to ridicule"); but Matthew makes it clear that when things started out, [both]{.class_4725} thieves were mocking Christ (Matt. 27:44). What changed the one thief's mind in verse 40 here in Luke 23? He read the inscription above Christ's head. The request of verse 42 isn't a prayer of salvation as you know it; it's a prayer that when the King comes into His kingdom to remember him.

You talk about faith, boy; that's it. Here's a fellow beaten to a bloody pulp, nailed to a cross, with all His bones out of joint (Psa. 22:14). Does that look like a King to you?

But the Holy Spirit came to that one thief as he was mocking Jesus and said, "Read that superscription again." The thief shut up and read the inscription above Christ's head. The Holy Spirit said to that old boy, "That's true; you had better believe it. If you don't, you're going to burn just like John the Baptist said (3:9)."

And that thief looked at that figure hanging next to him. He couldn't even recognize it as a man anymore; that's how badly Jesus Christ was beaten (Isa. 52:14). And when that thief looked at Jesus hanging there like that, he believed that bloody, mangled figure would indeed be God's chosen King one day, and he got right. That took real faith to believe that.

Verses 40--41 are one of the greatest sermons preached in the entire Bible, rivaling even Paul's addresses at Athens (Acts 17) and Ephesus (Acts 20). It's a short message preached by a dying thief to his buddy hanging across from him, but it is very effective.

["Dost not thou fear God\..."]{.class_4723} (vs. 40). Good question. You had better (Eccl. 12:13; 1 Pet. 2:17). ["\...seeing thou art in the same condemnation?"]{.class_4723} The other thief is dying on a cross just like his buddy. He should be preparing to meet God as Judge (Amos 4:12), not ridiculing an innocent man.

["And we indeed justly; for we receive the due reward of our deeds\..."]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_663} (vs. 41). He's owning up to his own guilt. That's the essence of repentance when it comes to salvation. It's not only admitting you are a sinner; it's admitting that you actually deserve to go to Hell for that sin. It's admitting God is right in His judgment, and you are guilty under that just condemnation. If you don't come to [that]{.class_4725} realization, I don't care how much sin you quit, you are still just a sinner headed for Hell, trying to justify yourself through your own self-righteousness.

["\...but this man hath done nothing amiss."]{.class_4723} Another testimony to the sinlessness of Jesus Christ (see our remarks under vs. 4). That thief tells his buddy, "Shut your mouth! Don't you know a good man when you see one?" That is some message, boy; I wish God Almighty would take the backbone out of that dying thief and put it in every preacher in this country.

["And he said unto Jesus, Lord, remember me when thou comest into thy kingdom"]{.class_4723} (vs. 42). And here the new "Bibles" snatch salvation out of the dying thief's mouth, just as they do to the blind man in John 9:35, the Ethiopian eunuch in Acts 8:37, and Saul of Taursus in Acts 9:6 (see our comments on those passages in those Commentaries). The modern Alexandrian corruptions change ["Lord"]{.class_4723} to ["Jesus."]{.class_4725} That's a heresy from the Second and Third Centuries called "Monarchianism." It taught that the man Jesus became "the Christ" when the Holy Spirit came on Him at His baptism, and He ceased to be Christ when God forsook Him on the cross (Matt. 27:46; Mark 15:34). The reading of the [AV]{.class_4725} shows you that even while Jesus was suffering the wrath of God on sin as a man, He did not cease to be the Son of God.

The problem with these apostates who promote these perverted translations is that they do not apply their changes doctrinally once they have made them. They teach the doctrine as it stands in the [AV.]{.class_4725} That leads their congregations to believe that "no fundamental doctrine is affected" by the new versions when they are [shot through with heresy.]{.class_4725}

Now as we said, this thief doesn't offer a prayer for salvation as we know it, but he still is a wonderful picture of salvation by grace through faith. There he is on the cross: there is absolutely [nothing]{.class_4725} he can do to earn salvation. And when Jesus answers the thief's request, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_664}He doesn't tell Him to "repent, believe, confess, and be buptized" (Campbellites) or talk in tongues and be slain in the Spirit (Charismatics) or "There's Mary right there at the foot of the cross; pray to her because you have to come to me through her" (Catholics). The thief comes to Jesus Christ in faith, and Jesus tells him, ["To day shalt thou be with me in paradise"]{.class_4723} (vs. 43).

We have already commented on ["paradise"]{.class_4723} in verse 43 in our remarks on 16:22 (which see).

44 And it was about the sixth hour, and there was a darkness over all the earth until the ninth hour.

45 And the sun was darkened, and the veil of the temple was rent in the midst.

46 And when Jesus had cried with a loud voice, he said, Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit: and having said thus, he gave up the ghost.

47 Now when the centurion saw what was done, he glorified God, saying, Certainly this was a righteous man.

48 And all the people that came together to that sight, beholding the things which were done, smote their breasts, and returned.

49 And all his acquaintance, and the women that followed him from Galilee, stood afar off, beholding these things.

The hours of verse 44 are measured from the beginning of the Jewish day at 6:00 a.m. Jesus has been on the cross since 9:00 a.m. (Mark 15:25). At noon (["the sixth hour"]{.class_4723}), God shuts down the light of the sun until three in the afternoon (["the ninth hour"]{.class_4723}), which was the time of the evening prayer at the Temple (Acts 3:1).

["The sun was darkened"]{.class_4723} (vs. 45) was not an [eclipse,]{.class_4725} as the [RSV]{.class_4725} blasphemously declares in its footnote on the verse. The Passover always takes place on a [full moon,]{.class_4725} and it is astronomically [impossible]{.class_4725} for a solar eclipse to occur then. Such corrupt readings as ["the sun failing"]{.class_4725} or ["the sun being obscured"]{.class_4725} come from the Nestle-Aland Greek text, following the lead of Westcott and Hort, changing the reading of the Received text ([eskotisthe]{.class_4725}) to [eklipontos]{.class_4725} (an eclipse of the sun) based on [Vaticanus]{.class_4725} and [Sinaiticus.]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_665} The Alexandrian reading is a scientifically [inaccurate]{.class_4725} attempt to explain away a supernatural phenomenon.

["And the veil of the temple was rent in the midst"]{.class_4723} (vs. 45). This rending of the veil of the Temple takes place the moment Christ dies (vs. 46; Matt. 27:50--51). According to Hebrews 10:20, the veil that separated the Holy Place from the Holy of Holies represents [Christ's flesh,]{.class_4725} and when Jesus dies, the way is made into the presence of God apart from priests, sacrifices, holy water, incense, temples, etc. (Heb. 10:19--20).

Now don't you know when that veil was rent, those Levitical priests were in a panic. It was the time of the evening prayer (see our remarks under vs. 44), and one of those priests was there at the Altar of Incense when the earth shook (Matt. 27:51) and God took that veil (which was [six inches thick,]{.class_4725} woven new every year!) and split it right down the middle from top to bottom. Suddenly that priest is standing there staring into the Holy of Holies where only the high priest was allowed to go once a year.

That priest stands there shaking from head to toe, expecting to be struck dead any minute, and then he realizes that nothing is happening. He runs out and tells his buddies what has happened, and gingerly they enter the Holy Place and look into the Holy of Holies themselves. Again, nothing happens; no one dies. "Don't tell anyone," they say to one another, "or else everyone will know they don't need us to get to God anymore." That's your Roman Catholic Church. It's nothing but a counterfeit of the Old Testament priesthood that was done away with at Calvary (Col. 2:14). It is made to create a [barrier]{.class_4725} between you and God that was [removed]{.class_4725} by Jesus Christ at His death (Eph. 2:13--16).

Notice the difference between ["spirit"]{.class_4723} and ["ghost"]{.class_4723} in verse 46. The Greek word [eskeneusen,]{.class_4725} meaning "to expire or die," has been translated with the archaic English idiom ["gave up the ghost."]{.class_4723} That is known as "dynamic equivalence," and all translations use it to one degree or another.

Now why use the word ["ghost"]{.class_4723} instead of [spirit?]{.class_4725}[ ]{.class_4737}Because in the Old Testament, when a person died, the expression was ["gave up the ghost\...and was gathered unto his people"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 25:8, 17, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_666}35:29, 49:33). That's a reference to the soul departing from the body and going to Paradise (Gen. 35:18). So the ["ghost"]{.class_4723} of a man is the spiritual body of the man (see our remarks on the soul under 16:23).

That's why the [King James]{.class_4725} Bible calls the Holy Spirit the ["Holy Ghost."]{.class_4723} The Holy Spirit is Jesus Christ living in your body (Col. 1:27 cf. Rom. 8:9--10) and placing you in Christ's spiritual body (1 Cor. 12:13), so He is Christ's "Ghost." Now I understand that you may not be able to make that distinction in other languages (like the [Valera]{.class_4725} Spanish Bible), but you can in English, and God has given me an [English]{.class_4725} Bible. So I don't change the word like the modern translations do, and that's why. In English, a ghost is the spiritual body of a dead man, and Christ [died.]{.class_4725} So [in English,]{.class_4725} it is perfectly all right to refer to the Holy Spirit as the Holy Ghost.

Now here in Luke 23:46, the [KJV]{.class_4725} translators couldn't use the word [soul]{.class_4725} because the Greek word [psuche]{.class_4725} isn't in any Greek manuscript. Christ's human spirit returned to God, but his soul went [down]{.class_4725} (Acts 2:27, 31). So the [KJV]{.class_4725} translators gave you the dynamic equivalent to draw the distinction here between what happened to Christ's soul and what happened to His spirit.

Verse 47 is not only another eyewitness testimony to the sinless nature of Jesus Christ (see our comments on vs. 4), it is the first Gentile convert under the New Testament (Heb. 9:16--17). The Lord Jesus got a Jewish thief saved before He died and a Roman centurion saved after He died.

I had an amazing thing happen one time up in Louisville, Kentucky. I was preaching for Don Mangus, and a bunch of young men from Fort Knox came to the Saturday night meeting. That night a tall boy from Arkansas got saved, and he really got a dose. After the service, we went and played hockey from about midnight to two o'clock in the morning, and that young soldier went with us. He was playing right in front of me as I was playing goalie. The whole time he was playing he kept turning around and saying, "This is great! I'm going to come back to that church again!" I tried to encourage him to stay with it, and we talked about the Lord a little bit in between plays.

Well, after the game was over, we went our separate ways, and he got a ride with some other GIs back to the base. Within thirty minutes that new convert was [dead]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_667}. A car had broken down on the road, and those GIs were out of their car trying to help those folks when a drunken teenager about seventeen years old came over the hill at ninety miles an hour.

That young fellow who had gotten saved pushed his two buddies out of the way and saved their lives, but he got hit and died.

Well, the firemen came out to clean up the mess, and after it was all done, one unsaved fireman was talking to his buddy who happened to go to Mangus' church and knew that that young man had gotten saved at the meeting that night. The unsaved fireman said, "Isn't that something. That young man was a good kid, wasn't he?"

His saved buddy replied, "Yeah, do you know where he was right before he died? He was at church."

That unsaved fireman said in consternation, "At church on Saturday night?! Well, why would God let someone like that die when there are young people like that seventeen-year-old out getting drunk?"

That gave the saved fireman a chance to witness to him, and he led him to Christ. But that's not all. A nurse got saved at the hospital where the body was taken, two workers in the mortuary got saved, and some of the GIs back at the base got saved. I think there were eight people in all who trusted Christ because of that young man's bravery and testimony. It was such an amazing story that it made the front page of the Louisville newspaper. That young man was leading souls to Christ after he was dead, and when he got to Heaven, I guarantee you he got a soulwinner's crown.

["And all the people that came together to that sight, beholding the things which were done, smote their breasts, and returned"]{.class_4723} (vs. 48). They realized they had done something wrong. Too bad; it was too late. Of course the Lord prayed that they would be forgiven back up in verse 34, so in the book of Acts they got a second chance. But as far as the blood guilt of the nation (Matt. 27:25), it was too late. Folks, you may get saved and have your sins forgiven and be headed to Glory, yet reap the consequences of sins you committed before you were saved.

We had a young man come through school here who had lost a []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_668}leg and had to walk around on crutches. Before he got saved, he got drunk and passed out on a train track. When he woke up, he only had one leg because while he was out, a train had come by and cut the other leg off. That fellow was saved and loved the Lord, but he had to suffer for that one night of drunkenness the rest of his life.

One time some young men were headed across the border into Tijuana, Mexico for a night of sin and devilment. There at the crossing was an older fellow in a wheelchair selling pencils. That crippled man caught one of those young men's eye and motioned him over to talk. He said, "Young man, do you consider yourself to be intelligent?"

That young fellow kind of puffed up and said, "Yeah, I go to College."

"Are you any good at math?" asked the man in the wheelchair.

"I'm pretty good," said the younger guy.

"Then I've got an equation for you," said the crippled man. "One night of sin over there," and he motioned to Tijuana across the border, "And twenty years in a wheelchair over here. Does that equation equal out to you?"

That shook the young man up pretty badly. One night of sin cost that older fellow his legs for twenty years. That's the same situation those Jews have been in for 2,000 years now.

We have already commented on the women who followed Christ (vs. 49) in our remarks on verse 27 (which see). Matthew 27:56 and Mark 15:40 give you the names of some of these women.

50 And, behold, there was a man named Joseph, a counsellor; and he was a good man, and a just:

51 (The same had not consented to the counsel and deed of them;) he was of Arimathaea, a city of the Jews: who also himself waited for the kingdom of God.

52 This man went unto Pilate, and begged the body of Jesus.

53 And he took it down, and wrapped it in linen, and laid it in a sepulchre that was hewn in stone, wherein never man before was laid.

54 And that day was the preparation, and the sabbath []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_669}drew on.

55 And the women also, which came with him from Galilee, followed after, and beheld the sepulchre, and how his body was laid.

56 And they returned, and prepared spices and ointments; and rested the sabbath day according to the commandment.

Now this is Joseph of Arimathea in verses 50--51. He is the fulfillment of Isaiah 53:9 (cf. Matt. 27:57). He is ["a counsellor"]{.class_4723} (vs. 50), which means he was part of the Sanhedrin (vs. 51). He was one of two men on the council who did not condemn Christ with the rest of the Sadducees and Pharisees. The other one was [Nicodemus]{.class_4725} (cf. John 7:50--51). They were both "secret disciples" who feared being excommunicated for following Christ (John 12:42, 19:38). But now they both step forward to claim the body of the Lord Jesus Christ for burial (John 19:38--39).

The word ["begged"]{.class_4723} in verse 52 defines the word ["craved"]{.class_4723} in Mark 15:43. Scripture with Scripture. When we say the Bible is a "self-interpreting Book," we mean the Bible is well able to define its own terms without the need for any church, school, or scholar.

We have already commented on how the body of Jesus was wrapped for burial under our remarks on 2:12 (which see). Whatever the so-called "Shroud of Turin" may be, there is one thing for certain that it is NOT: the burial shroud of Jesus of Nazareth.

The sepulchre of verse 53 is Joseph's own tomb (Matt. 27:60). Joseph is anxious to get Christ's body in the tomb before 6:00 p.m. before the Passover supper is eaten and the Feast of Unleavened Bread begins. His tomb was in a garden right there at Golgotha (John 19:41--42).

Now this brings up the time of Christ's burial. All the Catholics and all the Greek Orthodox and all the Protestants have Christ crucified on "Good Friday" based on ["the sabbath"]{.class_4723} of verse 54. [That's a gross error that insults the word of God and the Holy Spirit.]{.class_4725}

Matthew 12:40---["For as Jonas was three days and three nights in the whale's belly; so shall the Son of man be THREE DAYS AND THREE NIGHTS in the heart of the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_670}earth."]{.class_4723}

There is no way of getting ["three days and three nights"]{.class_4723} if He goes in the tomb Friday between 5:00 and 6:00 p.m. You say, "Well, they're counting partial days as whole days." Aw, your father's moustache!

Those ["three days and three nights"]{.class_4723} there in Matthew 12:40 are the Hebrew days like you find them in Genesis 1 starting at 6:00 p.m. for the evening and 6:00 a.m. for the morning. That's three whole nights running from 6:00 p.m. to 6 a.m. and three whole days running from 6:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m. It's not Friday night, Saturday, Saturday night, and Sunday morning.

To meet the demands of Matthew 12:40, Jesus Christ would have to go into the tomb before 6:00 Wednesday evening. The ["three nights"]{.class_4723} would be Wednesday 6:00 p.m.--Thursday 6:00 a.m., Thursday 6:00 p.m.--Friday 6:00 a.m., and Friday 6:00 p.m.--Saturday 6:00 a.m. The ["three days"]{.class_4723} would be Thursday 6:00 a.m.--Thursday 6:00 p.m., Friday 6:00 a.m.--Friday 6:00 p.m., and Saturday 6:00 a.m.--Saturday 6:00 p.m. The Jewish ["first day of the week"]{.class_4723} begins on Saturday 6:00 p.m. and runs to Sunday 6:00 p.m., which means that Jesus arose on Saturday 6:00 p.m., but the empty tomb was not discovered until 6:00 a.m. Sunday morning.

You say, "What about that ['sabbath']{.class_4723} in verse 54, Ruckman?" [That wasn't the regular weekly Sabbath.]{.class_4725}

John 19:31---["The Jews therefore, because it was the preparation, that the bodies should not remain upon the cross on the sabbath day, (FOR THAT SABBATH DAY WAS AN HIGH DAY,) besought Pilate that their legs might be broken, and that they might be taken away."]{.class_4723}

The preparation was the day before the Passover supper was eaten; it is the time when the Passover lamb was killed (Mark 14:12), which means that Christ died within mere moments of the Passover lamb being slain (1 Cor. 5:7). The next day began the Feast of Unleavened Bread, and the first day of that feast was treated as a sabbath in which no work was to be done (Exod. 12:15--16).

So Nicodemus and Joseph of Arimathea do a "rush job" of anointing the body for burial (John 19:39--40), and they roll the stone over the door of the tomb sealing the body of Jesus in (Mark 14:46). All this is marked by Mary Magdalene and []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4276.xhtml_page_671}["Mary the mother of James and Joses"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 27:56, 61 cf. Mark 14:47), who plan to come back after the special sabbath of unleavened bread, and the weekly Sabbath which followed on its heels, and finish the job of anointing the body (vs. 56). They don't get the chance, though. The only anointing for burial that Christ's body ever received was by Mary the sister of Martha and Lazarus six days earlier (John 12:1--7; Mark 14:8) and by Joseph and Nicodemus before the entombment. The next time these women see the body of Jesus, it isn't in any need of "embalming."

[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml}

# Chapter 24 {.heading_78-0}

24:1 Now upon the first day of the week, very early in the morning, they came unto the sepulchre, bringing the spices which they had prepared, and certain others with them.

2 And they found the stone rolled away from the sepulchre.

3 And they entered in, and found not the body of the Lord Jesus.

4 And it came to pass, as they were much perplexed thereabout, behold, two men stood by them in shining garments:

5 And as they were afraid, and bowed down their faces to the earth, they said unto them, Why seek ye the living among the dead?

6 He is not here, but is risen: remember how he spake unto you when he was yet in Galilee,

7 Saying, The Son of man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, and be crucified, and the third day rise again.

8 And they remembered his words,

This is the resurrection of the Lord Jesus Christ; it is the [foundation]{.class_4725} of Biblical Christianity. Christianity is the only "religion" whose founder [isn't dead.]{.class_4725} Mohammed is [dead;]{.class_4725} you can go over to Medina and see where he is buried. Lenin is [dead;]{.class_4725} the Communists put his desiccated corpse in a glass coffin and made the Russian people march by and pay homage to it. Buddha is dead; there's a city over in India where they parade his bones through the streets every year. Where's the body of Jesus Christ? ["He is not here, but is risen"]{.class_4723} (vs. 6).

The Resurrection is the proof that Jesus Christ is the virgin-born Son of God (Rom. 1:4). Paul writes a whole chapter on it in 1 Corinthians 15, and he says this:

["And if Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_673}in your sins. Then they also which are fallen asleep in Christ are perished. If in this life only we have hope in Christ, we are of all men most miserable"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 15:17--19).

If Jesus stayed in the grave, then there hasn't been anybody saved since Adam. The only reason the Old Testament saints were put in Paradise was because Jesus Christ was coming along someday to [get them out]{.class_4725} (1 Pet. 4:6 cf. Eph. 4:8--10). Paul says that if Jesus hadn't come up from the dead, then the dead ["are perished"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 15:18).

That's not all: you couldn't be saved either, because the atonement of Jesus Christ was worthless without the Resurrection. No atonement---no removal of sin (Heb. 10:4--12): ["ye are yet in your sins"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 15:17). The reason Paul said ["we are of all men most miserable"]{.class_4723} is because you trust Christ, you live right, you witness and bear reproach for Christ's sake, and [you still go to Hell if Jesus didn't rise from the dead.]{.class_4725}

So the resurrection of Jesus Christ is [absolutely essential]{.class_4725} to your salvation. Of course, there are all kinds of ways infidels have of getting around it. A Mohammedan will tell you right quick that it never happened, because in the [Koran,]{.class_4725} "Isa" never died on the cross (Sura 23:50 note 1723, 3:54, 5:75 note 724); that is, Islam teaches that Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, and [Jesus Himself]{.class_4725} were all [liars.]{.class_4725}

"We respect the prophet Jesus"---shut your mouth, you big liar! Don't tell me you honor my Prophet when you contradict what He said about Himself and what He came to do. You honor a spook who never existed.

The Liberals and Modernists will tell you that the Resurrection was a [hoax]{.class_4725} or a [mistake]{.class_4725} or a [hallucination]{.class_4725} or that Jesus [swooned]{.class_4725} on the cross and revived in the tomb or that the "resurrection" was only [spiritual.]{.class_4725} The "Hoax Theory" was the original lie put forth by the chief priests: they bribed the Roman soldiers guarding the tomb to say Jesus' disciples perpetrated a hoax by stealing the body and claiming Christ had risen. How could a ragtag band of street preachers with only two swords between them take on and overcome (without anybody being killed) a group of highly trained, well equipped Roman legionnaires? If this were an attempt to quickly extract the body before the Roman guard came to, why []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_674}would the thieves unwrap the body and then wind the grave clothes up again to make it look like a body had been in them (John 20:5)? And why take the time to neatly fold the napkin covering the head and set it to the side (John 20:7)? That was quite an "op" those disciples had going there, wouldn't you say?

The very fact that the Roman guard lived to tell the tale is evidence of its dubious nature. If a Roman soldier fell asleep on guard duty or failed to keep a prisoner left in his charge, he was [executed]{.class_4725} (see Acts 12:19). The "hoax" was not on the part of those witnessing to the resurrection of Jesus Christ; it was on the part of those trying to denounce it.

The idea that the women and the disciples were mistaken as to the correct tomb is also absurd. If the women had gotten the wrong tomb by mistake, what were the Roman soldiers doing guarding the wrong tomb (Matt. 28:15)? What would Peter and John be doing going to the wrong tomb (John 20:3--4), and what would Christ's grave clothes be doing in the wrong tomb? All the chief priests would have to do to squash the rumor of Jesus rising from the dead would be to go to the [right tomb]{.class_4725} and [produce the body!]{.class_4725} In an attempt to get out of believing [historical facts,]{.class_4725} infidels don't think through the ridiculous arguments they put forth to deny Jesus coming out of the grave.

It was an hallucination, was it? The disciples wanted to see their Lord so badly that they imagined the whole thing? Well, first of all, the disciples didn't even believe the women when they told them of the Resurrection (vs. 11). Thomas wouldn't believe unless he put his hand into the nail prints and spear thrust (John 20:25). Some illusion too: it had flesh and bones (vs. 39, vss. 41--43), and it walked around and talked with them for forty days (Acts 1:3). If the resurrected Christ were an hallucination, then it was one shared by 500 people all at the same time (1 Cor. 15:6).

The "Swoon Theory" is an "oldie but a goodie." Jesus only fainted on the cross, and then He revived in the "cool freshness of the tomb." Once again the stupid Modernists and Liberals don't think through such an absurd notion. Jesus had gone through a Roman scourging which often took the life of the victim before he could be crucified. He had suffered a tremendous loss of blood on the cross []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_675}that would result in traumatic shock to His system. You don't treat a shock victim by cooling the person down; you cover him with blankets to keep him warm. "The cool freshness of the tomb" wouldn't have revived a person in Jesus' state; it would have [killed him]{.class_4725} if He were not already dead. And the spices that Joseph and Nicodemus used to anoint the dead body of Jesus were [poisonous toxins.]{.class_4725} They were made to [embalm a dead body,]{.class_4725} not revive the living.

Moreover, Roman soldiers trained in executing criminals recognized a [dead]{.class_4725} man when they saw one. That's why they didn't break Christ's legs (John 19:33). Anyone familiar with how crucifixion affected the body would know that fainting wouldn't have left Christ alive. A crucifixion victim had to raise himself up just to get a breath; if his body hung down for too long, he would suffocate to death. Fainting would have resulted in Christ's death for sure. And if that weren't enough, a spear was thrust in Christ's side, piercing His pericardium and His heart. You reckon He could survive for three days with a wound like that?

Now think about it for a moment. Suppose Jesus actually had revived in the tomb. His body was in shreds from the beating. All His bones were dislocated (Psa. 22:14). His face was unrecognizable from being smacked around, having His beard snatched out, and having the crown of thorns beaten into His head. He had holes in His hands and feet, and a gaping wound in His side. Could somebody in that condition roll a massive stone away from the door of His sepulcher? In that condition, would He be able to convince His disciples that He was the Lord of Glory who had conquered death? Could He have even walked to Emmaus that day (vss. 13--15)? Are you kidding me?!

Then for the compromisers, there's the "Ghost Theory." Jesus didn't raise [bodily]{.class_4725} from the grave; the disciples saw the ghost or spirit of Jesus. Again, the resurrected Christ could be [touched;]{.class_4725} He could [eat;]{.class_4725} He had ["flesh and bones"]{.class_4723} (vs. 39). The very idea of resurrection is not that you walk around as a ghost, but that your body gets out of the grave and lives again.

The latest idea that is making a comeback is that the whole story of Jesus, including His resurrection, is a [myth]{.class_4725} like the stories of the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_676}pagan gods. Pagan mythology has all kinds of accounts of gods dying and coming back to life; the story of Jesus is no different.

Problem: there were no [eyewitnesses]{.class_4725} to the resurrections of Osiris or Apollo or Adonis or whomever (see our comments on 1:2). Paul told the Corinthians that they could go talk to above [500 witnesses]{.class_4725} who [saw]{.class_4725} the resurrected Christ (1 Cor. 15:6). The resurrection of Jesus Christ is a well documented, historical [fact;]{.class_4725} not a myth.

Listen, people, the Devil was [right there]{.class_4725} when Abraham offered up Isaac. He certainly knew what it meant even if you don't (Heb. 11:17--19). What better way of damning people for the next 4,000 years than for Satan to use such knowledge to counterfeit the Resurrection so that when the real thing showed up, all his children (John 8:44) would classify it right along with all the fake stuff?

So with that, let us get into Luke's account of the Resurrection. ["Now upon the first day of the week"]{.class_4723} (vs. 1). That is important because the Seventh-day Adventist teach that Sunday as a day of worship was instituted for the Roman Catholic Church by the Emperor Constantine, who remained a sun-worshipper till a week before he died (Constantine was not "baptized" a "Christian" until he was on his [death bed]{.class_4725}---see chap. 7 of [The History of the New Testament Church,]{.class_4725} Vol. I). Regardless, though, of what Constantine did, the early churches in the book of Acts [met]{.class_4725} on ["the first day of the week"]{.class_4723} (Acts 20:7), and they [took up the collection]{.class_4725} on ["the first day of the week"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 16:1--2). That is because[ Jesus arose]{.class_4725} on ["the first day of the week"]{.class_4723} and [the Holy Spirit came down on the first day of the week]{.class_4725} (Acts 2:1 cf. Lev. 23:15--16). Constantine was over 300 years too late to determine practice for New Testament local churches.

Now the women of 23:55--56 return to the tomb ["very early"]{.class_4723} on Sunday morning. Matthew, Mark, and John tell you that they arrive just before dawn (Matt. 28:1; Mark 16:2; John 20:1). So this is between 5:30 and 6:30 in the morning.

In Mark 16:3, they wonder who will roll the stone away from the door of the sepulcher for them. Mark 16:9 says the stone ["was very great."]{.class_4723} The implication is that the stone was so large and heavy that four or five women wouldn't be able to move it. Of course, when they []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_677}arrived, the stone was already rolled away (vs. 2).

The question is, who rolled it away? The disciples didn't, despite the lies spread about them by the chief priests (Matt. 28:13). The disciples were in hiding ["for fear of the Jews"]{.class_4723} (John 20:19). The soldiers didn't. They were there to make sure the tomb remained sealed (Matt. 27:66).

Matthew tells you that some time before dawn, ["the angel of the Lord descended from heaven, and came and rolled back the stone from the door, and sat upon it"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 28:2). An angel comes down and rolls that huge stone away all by himself. But he did not roll it away to let Jesus out; Jesus had already risen at 6:00 Saturday evening (see our comments on 23:54). Jesus didn't need an angel to let Him out of the tomb. He had already gone down to the center of the earth and come back (Eph. 4:9). What would a couple of feet of rock mean to Him? Jesus could pass through locked doors (John 20:19); no stone, no matter how big, could hold Him in that sepulcher.

Seeing the open tomb, Mary Magdalene goes to get Peter and John (John 20:1--2). The other women enter the sepulcher, and the angel who rolled the stone away appears to them (Matt. 28:5; Mark 16:5) and tells them to go tell the disciples that Jesus is risen (Matt. 28:7; Mark 16:7).

As the women exit the tomb, Peter and John arrive (John 20:3--4). John stays outside, but Peter goes in. Not seeing any angel, Peter leaves the tomb wondering about the empty grave clothes and what the women claim to have seen (vss. 11--12). Mary Magdalene arrives and encounters two angels in the tomb (John 20:12--13), and when she goes outside, she is the first to see the resurrected Christ (Mark 16:9; John 20:14--17).

Mary Magdalene leaves to go tell the disciples what she has seen (Mark 16:20; John 20:18). In the meantime, the other women return, and this time they all go into the sepulcher and see the grave clothes and the two angels (vss. 3--4). As they are returning to tell the disciples what they have seen, they also meet Jesus along the way (Matt. 28:8--9).

The four Gospel accounts are to be taken all together in regards to the order of events on the first Resurrection Sunday. Charges of []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_678}discrepancies and contradictions in the accounts of the Resurrection are nothing but the rantings of a lazy, unread infidel. The material I just gave you was in print back in [1845]{.class_4725} ([Bibliotheca Sacra]{.class_4725}) and again in 1927 by Halley in his [Bible Handbook.]{.class_4725}

So the women go into the tomb, and they find it empty. [Jesus Christ is gone.]{.class_4725} And while they're standing there wondering what's going on, ["two MEN stood by them in shining garments"]{.class_4723} (vs. 4). Those are [angels,]{.class_4725} but notice once again that they are called ["men."]{.class_4723} There's not a sexless angel in that Bible (see our remarks under 20:36).

The women react to those two angels just as Zacharias did when he saw Gabriel (1:12) and as the shepherds did when they saw ["the angel of the Lord"]{.class_4723} (2:9). No "warm glows," no "sense of security"; the reaction is [fear]{.class_4725} (vs. 5). If you see ["an angel of light"]{.class_4723} who makes you feel good and gives you the "warm fuzzies," that's NOT an angel of the Lord (2 Cor. 11:14).

["Why seek ye the living among the dead?"]{.class_4723} (vs. 5). Good question. The average American looks at the people in the bars, discos, and rock concerts, and he says to himself, "Man, that's living." No, that's [dying. ]{.class_4727}Those are sinners ["dead in trespasses and sins"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 2:1), in a desperate pursuit of the ["pleasures of this life"]{.class_4723} (Luke 8:14). That Bible says, ["she that liveth in pleasure is dead while she liveth"]{.class_4723} (1 Tim. 5:6). When you go to the beer joints, the pool halls, the dance halls, etc., you are looking at the [living dead.]{.class_4727} The Christian has been ["quickened"]{.class_4723} from that mess (Eph. 2:1): he's been [made]{.class_4727} [alive]{.class_4727}. In his daily walk, he is commanded to ["arise from the dead, and Christ shall give thee light"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 5:14). The [live believer]{.class_4727} is not to be hanging out with [dead sinners]{.class_4727} in pursuit of their [dead pleasures.]{.class_4727}[ ]{.class_4737}["Why seek ye the living among the dead?"]{.class_4723}

Verse 6:[ "He is not here, but is risen."]{.class_4723} That's the best news the world ever heard. The best news the world ever got came from a graveyard. ["He is not here, but is risen."]{.class_4723}

You walk among the tombstones of the "greats" of this world, and you read: "Here lies Albert Einstein"---[dead.]{.class_4729-0} "Here lies Pope John Paul II"---[dead.]{.class_4729-0} "Here lies Karl Marx"---stone cold dead on the market. "Here lies Mohammed\...Lao Tze\...Confucius\...Buddha [\
]{.class-1}\...Joseph Smith\...Mary\...Simon Peter"---[they're all dead.]{.class_4729-0}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_679 .class-1} But when you get to Jesus Christ's tombstone, the grave is open, the coffin's sitting there empty, and a sign has been taped over the tombstone that reads: ["He is not here, but is risen."]{.class_4730-0}

Do you know what that means? That means if you belong to Him, you're not staying down.

He said, ["because I live, ye shall live also"]{.class_4723} (John 14:19).

He said, ["I am the resurrection, and the life; he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live"]{.class_4723} (John 11:25).

The promise is: ["For as in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 15:22).

["But if the Spirit of him that raised up Jesus from the dead dwell in you, he that raised up Christ from the dead shall also quicken your mortal bodies by his Spirit that dwelleth in you"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:11).

Do you know how I know Islam is a crock? Because [Mohammed is dead.]{.class_4725} The founder of my religion [got out of the grave.]{.class_4725} And Mohammed did what? That's what I thought---[absolutely nothing.]{.class_4725} Like the bumper sticker says: "Mohammed is dead; [Jesus Christ is alive."]{.class_4725}

["He is not here, but is risen."]{.class_4723} I heard an old hillbilly preacher preaching on this text, and he had the angel saying, "He ain't here; He's risen." I like that; I bet that's in the "original Greek." It would be just like the Lord to send down an uneducated angel to speak plainly to a bunch of common folk.

Verses 7--8 are the fulfillment of the prophecy Christ made concerning Himself in 9:22, 44, and you can see our comments on those verses.

9 And returned from the sepulchre, and told all these things unto the eleven, and to all the rest.

10 It was Mary Magdalene, and Joanna, and Mary the mother of James, and other women that were with them, which told these things unto the apostles.

11 And their words seemed to them as idle tales, and they believed them not.

12 Then arose Peter, and ran unto the sepulchre; and stooping down, he beheld the linen clothes laid by themselves, and departed, wondering in himself at that which was come to pass.

Notice the words ["and to all the rest"]{.class_4723} in verse 9; they go along with the words ["and them that were with them"]{.class_4723} in verse 33. There were more people in the room where the disciples were gathered than just the eleven remaining apostles. The reason that is so important is because in John 20:23 when Jesus gives the power to remit and retain sins, He gives it to more than just Simon Peter and the other apostles. He gives it to every believer in the room. In the New Testament, there is no special priest class; every child of God is a priest (1 Pet. 2:5, 9). The Apostle Paul exercises the power even though he wasn't even in the room when the Lord gave the disciples the power (see 2 Cor. 2:10 and comments in that Commentary).

Verse 10 gives you three of the names of the women who were at the empty tomb. ["Joanna"]{.class_4723} was ["the wife of Chuza Herod's steward"]{.class_4723} back in 8:3. ["Mary the mother of James"]{.class_4723} is the mother of a disciple called ["James the less"]{.class_4723} and her other son was named ["Joses"]{.class_4723} (Mark 15:40); you can see our remarks on the "Jameses" under 6:15 for the possible identity of ["James the less."]{.class_4723} Aside from those two and Mary Magdalene, ["Salome,"]{.class_4723} the mother of James and John Zebedee, is also given among the women in Mark 16:1 (Mark 15:40 cf. Matt. 27:56).

Of course, there were ["other women"]{.class_4723} with them whose names are not given. Some commentators give an estimate of at least a dozen women who came to the sepulcher that first Resurrection day, and that number would not be unreasonable given the fact that Luke says, ["MANY others ]{.class_4723}\[["women"]{.class_4723} in 8:2\][\...ministered unto him of their substance"]{.class_4723} (8:3).

["And their words seemed to them as idle tales, and they believed not"]{.class_4723} (vs. 11). First Mary Magdalene comes running in saying that someone had taken the body of Jesus out of the tomb (John 20:2). Then the other women come in saying they had seen an angel. When Peter goes to the tomb, he sees no angel (vs. 12), so the natural assumption is made that the women are either lying or []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_681}hallucinating due to their grief. Even with what Jesus told them back in 9:22 and with the women's testimony, the disciples ["believed them not"]{.class_4723} because ["as yet they knew not the scripture, that he must rise again from the dead"]{.class_4723} (John 20:9).

Notice once again in verse 12 that the body of Jesus was not wrapped in a single "winding sheet" like the "Shroud of Turin." The ["linen clothes"]{.class_4723} are strips of linen cloth wound around the body as the Egyptians did to mummies. John 20:7 tells you that the head was wrapped in a separate piece of cloth, called a ["napkin"]{.class_4723} in the verse.

13 And, behold, two of them went that same day to a village called Emmaus, which was from Jerusalem about threescore furlongs.

14 And they talked together of all these things which had happened.

15 And it came to pass, that, while they communed together and reasoned, Jesus himself drew near, and went with them.

16 But their eyes were holden that they should not know him.

17 And he said unto them, What manner of communications are these that ye have one to another, as ye walk, and are sad?

18 And the one of them, whose name was Cleopas, answering said unto him, Art thou only a stranger in Jerusalem, and hast not known the things which are come to pass there in these days?

19 And he said unto them, What things? And they said unto him, Concerning Jesus of Nazareth, which was a prophet mighty in deed and word before God and all the people:

20 And how the chief priests and our rulers delivered him to be condemned to death, and have crucified him.

21 But we trusted that it had been he which should have redeemed Israel: and beside all this, to day is the third day since these things were done.

22 Yea, and certain women also of our company made []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_682}us astonished, which were early at the sepulchre;

23 And when they found not his body, they came, saying, that they had also seen a vision of angels, which said that he was alive.

24 And certain of them which were with us went to the sepulchre, and found it even so as the women had said: but him they saw not.

This is the account of Jesus appearing to the two men on the road to Emmaus. It takes place late in the day (vs. 29), so several hours have passed since the events of verses 1--12. Verse 12 ended with Peter wondering what was going on, but what you aren't told until you get to verse 34 is that sometime between verse 12 and verse 13, Jesus made a special appearance to Simon Peter.

The Apostle Paul wrote that Christ ["was seen of Cephas, then of the twelve"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 15:5). ["Cephas"]{.class_4723} was the Aramaic name Jesus gave to Simon back in John 1:42. Jesus appeared to Peter privately to deal with him about his denial back in 22:54--62. So in 1 Corinthians 15:5, there is a private restoration of Peter's discipleship, and later on in John 21:14--17, there is a public restoration of his discipleship.

Now, the village of Emmaus was ["about threescore furlongs"]{.class_4723} (vs. 13). A furlong is an eighth of a mile, so sixty furlongs works out to seven-and-a-half miles. Josephus mentions an Emmaus, but that one was only four miles from Jerusalem, so it would not be the one mentioned here.

There are two men headed back to Emmaus. This was not the Sabbath day, so they could have been returning from work or business. But regardless of the reason they are out and about, they are discussing the reports of Jesus' resurrection (vs. 14 cf. vss. 21--24). They are disciples of Jesus, for they ["trusted it had been he]{.class_4723} \[Jesus\][ which should have redeemed Israel"]{.class_4723} (vs. 21), and they refer to the other disciples as ["our company"]{.class_4723} (vss. 22). Only one is named (["Cleopas"]{.class_4723}---vs. 18), and this [may]{.class_4725} have been the husband of one of the Marys at the cross (John 19:25).

While they are walking and talking, Jesus comes alongside them (vs. 15), but they don't recognize Him because the Bible says, ["their eyes were holden that they should not know him"]{.class_4723}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_683} (vs. 16). In His resurrected state, the Lord Jesus could withhold His identity from those who saw Him. Mary Magdalene did not recognize Him until He spoke her name; she thought He was the gardener (John 20:15).

God can fix you sometimes so that you cannot see what's right there before your eyes. He said He gave those Jews who wouldn't believe on Him ["eyes that they should not see, and ears that they should not hear"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 11:8).

The guy who used to smuggle Bibles into Communist countries during the years of the Iron Curtain (Bro. Andrew) used to have a prayer he prayed when he came to a border crossing in Eastern Europe: "Lord, I read in the Bible where you made blind eyes to see. Would you please now make seeing eyes blind?" That's because the trunk and backseat of his Volkswagen were filled with Bibles, and the only way he could get them in was for God to blind those Communist border guards to their presence.

Notice how the word ["communications"]{.class_4723} is used in verse 17. It is used of the two men's [speech,]{.class_4725} which is the common use of the term. That is how the term is used in Matthew 5:37; Ephesians 4:29; and Colossians 3:8. But look at how the word is used in 1 Corinthians 15:33.

"Be not deceived: evil communications corrupt good manners."

There it is used of the [people]{.class_4725} with whom you [commune]{.class_4725} (see vs. 15 here in Luke 24): your [friends.]{.class_4725} In Philippians 4:14, though, the word ["communicate"]{.class_4723} is used of a [financial gift]{.class_4725} to help someone out. So English words in the [King James]{.class_4725} can be very flexible. You want to remember that the next time you hear some educated jackleg go on and on about the "richness" of a Greek word. The English of the [AV]{.class_4725} can be very "rich" itself, and it doesn't take a Greek scholar to dig "nuggets" out of it. Any fool with a sixth grade education can own the whole mine.

So Jesus walks up to these two fellows, and He begins talking to them. "Hey guys, what's up? What are you two talking about that's got you so down in the mouth?" (This is Ruckman's "Living Bible.")

"What planet are you from, man? Don't you know about all the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_684}things that have gone on in Jerusalem the past few days?"

"[What things?"]{.class_4723} He knew ["what things"]{.class_4723}; they happened to [Him.]{.class_4725} You talk about deceptive, boy; that's the Lord acting like He doesn't know what went on just to see how those two guys would react and what they would say. The Lord's messing with their minds.

A little later in verse 28 He pretends He's going to leave them just to get them to ask Him to stay. He did that to see what they would do, and what they did showed Him that they wanted Him to stay. [Jesus wanted to feel wanted by His disciples.]{.class_4725} The Lord is a character, I kid you not.

The explanation given by the two men in verses 19--20 is simple and straightforward enough and needs no further comment. The redemption of verse 21 is the [physical redemption]{.class_4725} of Israel from her enemies that we discussed back under 1:68--71, which see.

["And beside all this, to day is the third day since these things were done"]{.class_4723} (vs. 21). This is used to bolster the false teaching that Christ was crucified on "Good Friday." But this is no more a definitive statement on the chronology of the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ than what Mary said back in 2:48 was a definitive statement on the identity of Christ's father. These are two men in casual conversation referring back to the third full day since Jesus had been entombed (see the chronology laid out under our remarks on 23:54). They are obviously not counting Sunday since the day is not yet over. The last full day Jesus was in the grave was [Saturday,]{.class_4725} the third full day since Wednesday 6 p.m.

The rule of Biblical hermeneutics, which we have brought up time and time again, holds true here: [never]{.class_4725} interpret a clear statement by an unclear statement. The clear, definitive passage on the time Jesus was in the grave is Matthew 12:40, and you can't override [the words of Jesus Christ]{.class_4725} with the words of men He is about to call ["fools"]{.class_4723} in verse 25.

Verse 24 shows you that Luke didn't give you all the information on who went to the tomb in verse 12. Verse 12 only mentions Peter, but in verse 24, you find out that someone else was with him, for ["him THEY saw not." "They"]{.class_4723} were Peter and John (John 20:3--8). There is no contradiction in the Gospel accounts if they are taken together as complementary and supplementary to each other.

25 Then he said unto them, O fools, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken:

26 Ought not Christ to have suffered these things, and to enter into his glory?

27 And beginning at Moses and all the prophets, he expounded unto them in all the scriptures the things concerning himself.

28 And they drew nigh unto the village, whither they went: and he made as though he would have gone further.

29 But they constrained him, saying, Abide with us: for it is toward evening, and the day is far spent. And he went in to tarry with them.

30 And it came to pass, as he sat at meat with them, he took bread, and blessed it, and brake, and gave to them.

31 And their eyes were opened, and they knew him; and he vanished out of their sight.

32 And they said one to another, Did not our heart burn within us, while he talked with us by the way, and while he opened to us the scriptures?

33 And they rose up the same hour, and returned to Jerusalem, and found the eleven gathered together, and them that were with them,

34 Saying, The Lord is risen indeed, and hath appeared to Simon.

35 And they told what things were done in the way, and how he was known of them in breaking of bread.

["O fools]{.class_4723} (vs. 25). Uh oh, Lord, you shouldn't have said that. ["Whosoever shall say, Thou fool, shall be in danger of hell fire"]{.class_4723} (Matt. 5:22).

Jesus Christ wasn't worried about calling someone a fool anymore than Paul was (1 Cor. 15:36). Matthew 5:22 is part of the "Constitution of the Kingdom"; it is rules the Jews are to live by when the King is there in Jerusalem on His throne ruling over the whole earth (Matt. 5:34--35).

It is never a sin to identify a fool from the word of God. You are a [fool]{.class_4725} not to believe in God (Psa. 14:1). You are a [fool]{.class_4725} to turn down God's free gift of salvation through Jesus Christ. You are a [fool]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_686} to live for just this life or for the flesh (Luke 12:20). And you are a [fool]{.class_4725} not to believe everything God said in His Book (vs. 25).

["O fools, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken."]{.class_4723} In the eyes of the lost, hypocritical religious leaders, you are a [heretic]{.class_4725} if you believe ["all that the prophets have spoken"]{.class_4723} (Acts 24:14). But according to Jesus Christ, you are not only a [fool]{.class_4725} if you reject Moses and the prophets, you are headed for [Hell]{.class_4725} (Luke 16:31).

Verse 26 solves the problem of the "two Messiahs" in the Old Testament. It is ["the sufferings of Christ"]{.class_4723} [first,]{.class_4725} and [then]{.class_4725} ["the glory that should follow"]{.class_4723} (1 Pet. 1:11). Joseph had to suffer slavery and imprisonment before the Lord exalted him to Prime Minister of Egypt. David had to live in exile running from Saul before he could reign as King over Israel.

["And beginning at Moses and all the prophets, he expounded unto them in all the scriptures the things concerning himself" ]{.class_4723}(vs. 27). That's Jesus Christ Himself taking those two men through the Old Testament and showing them through types and prophecies ["the things concerning himself."]{.class_4723} He starts in Genesis and shows them how He was the lamb who had to die to cover Adam and Eve's sins (Gen. 3:21). He showed them how the Serpent had to bruise the heel of the Seed of the woman before that Seed could stomp the Serpent's head (Gen. 3:15). He showed them how His blood ["speaketh better things than that of Abel"]{.class_4723} (Heb. 12:24). He showed them how He was the ark of safety that they had to be in to escape the judgment of God. He showed them how He was the One who would bless all the families and nations of the earth (Gen. 12:3, 18:18 cf. Gal. 3:8). He showed them how He was to be ["King of righteousness"]{.class_4723} first and then ["King of peace"]{.class_4723} like Melchisedec (Heb. 7:2). He showed them His death and resurrection in type when Abraham offered up Isaac (Heb. 11:17--19).

That's all in Genesis, and I haven't even hit the greatest type of Christ in the entire Bible---Joseph. The Lord Jesus took those two disciples through the Passover lamb, the Tabernacle, the striking of the rock, the brazen serpent, Joshua, the judges, David, Solomon, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_687}Isaiah, Jeremiah, Zechariah, Malachi, and all the rest. What a Bible study! No wonder they didn't want Him to leave.

By the time they reach Emmaus, it is ["toward evening"]{.class_4723} (vs. 29) around suppertime. They urged Christ to stay with them. I say "urged," but the word ["constrained"]{.class_4723} is much more forceful than that. It means "to compel or [force;]{.class_4725} to urge with [irresistible power."]{.class_4725} The picture is Cleopas and his buddy taking Jesus by both arms and ushering Him into the house.

Now when Jesus takes the bread and blesses it and brakes it, the two disciples immediately recognize that this mysterious stranger is the Lord Jesus Christ. Of course, the scene would have been familiar to them if they had been at the feeding of the 5,000 (see 9:16). But the thing that opened their eyes (vs. 31) was those nail prints in His wrists when he gave the bread to them (see John 20:27 and our comments in that Commentary).

As soon as they recognize that their guest is the Lord Jesus Christ, ["he vanished out of their sight"]{.class_4723} (vs. 31). As we are about to see in verse 36, the resurrection body can pass through solid objects.

["And they said one to another, Did not our heart burn within us"]{.class_4723} (vs. 32). So your expression for indigestion is [heartburn.]{.class_4725} That's language from 1611 in common use 402 years later in 2013. That Book is evidently not as out of date as the scholars and Bible publishers would have you believe.

["Did not our heart burn within us, while he talked with us by the way, and while he opened to us the scriptures?" ]{.class_4723}One of the symbols of the word of God is [fire]{.class_4725} (Jer. 23:29). The Lord told Jeremiah, ["I will make my words in thy mouth fire"]{.class_4723} (Jer. 5:14). When Jeremiah vowed not to preach anymore, that word was like a fire burning in his heart so that he couldn't hold it in anymore (Jer. 20:9).

With Jesus gone, the two disciples leave Emmaus and travel the seven-and-a-half miles back to Jerusalem, undoubtedly in the dark by that hour. Again note that there were more in the room where the disciples were gathered than just the eleven apostles (vs. 33), and you can see our comments on verse 9 for the importance of that.

We have already commented on verse 34 before we got into []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_688}verse 13 (which see), and verse 35 is self-explanatory. The two disciples from Emmaus tell the others how they had seen the risen Christ.

36 And as they thus spake, Jesus himself stood in the midst of them, and saith unto them, Peace be unto you.

37 But they were terrified and affrighted, and supposed that they had seen a spirit.

38 And he said unto them, Why are ye troubled? and why do thoughts arise in your hearts?

39 Behold my hands and my feet, that it is I myself: handle me, and see; for a spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have.

40 And when he had thus spoken, he shewed them his hands and his feet.

41 And while they yet believed not for joy, and wondered, he said unto them, Have ye here any meat?

42 And they gave him a piece of a broiled fish, and of an honeycomb.

43 And he took it, and did eat before them.

Now just as Jesus vanished out of the house at Emmaus, He appears in the room at Jerusalem. John says, ["the doors were shut where the disciples assembled for fear of the Jews"]{.class_4723} (John 20:19). So the resurrection body can pass through solid objects, and it can become visible and invisible at will. That's the kind of body you will be given at the Rapture if you are saved (Phil. 3:21).

The resurrection body is not limited by time or space. Between the time Jesus talked with Mary Magdalene in John 20:17 and the time He appeared to the other women in Matthew 28:9, He had traveled up to the third heaven and back.

Look at it. In John 20:17, Jesus tells Mary, ["Touch me not; for I am not yet ascended to my Father."]{.class_4723} That's not a reference to the Ascension in Acts 1, because He said right in the same verse, ["I ascend ]{.class_4723}\[present tense; not future\][ unto my Father."]{.class_4723} A little under two hours later, He appears to the other women, and they []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_689}grab Him around the feet, and He doesn't say anything about stopping at all. He had ascended to the Father and returned. He's traveling so fast that it makes Einstein's speed of light look like riding a tricycle. That's the resurrection body.

Although the resurrection body is supernatural in the sense that it can pass through solid objects, become visible, and invisible at will, and is not limited by time and space, it is still physical in the sense that it is not a ghost or spirit. Jesus told the disciples, ["handle me, and see; for a spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have"]{.class_4723} (vs. 39).

That's what Thomas wanted to do. He wasn't there when Jesus told the other disciples to handle Him to see that He was real (Thomas was a typical Baptist and skipped the Sunday evening service). He said, ["Except I shall see in his hands the print of the nails, and put my finger into the print of the nails, and thrust my hand into his side, I will not believe"]{.class_4723} (John 20:25).

That display of skepticism earned him the nickname "doubting Thomas," but we shouldn't be too hard on him. He was only asking for what the other disciples had gotten to do (vss. 39--40). They handled Christ's body, and they still didn't believe (vs. 41). When Jesus Christ appeared to Thomas the next Sunday night and invited him to ["Reach hither thy finger, and behold my hands; and reach hither thy hand, and thrust it into my side: and be not faithless, but believing"]{.class_4723} (John 20:27), it didn't take Thomas two seconds to believe. He immediately made arguably one of the greatest professions of faith found anywhere in the New Testament: ["My Lord and my God"]{.class_4723} (John 20:28). That profession is so great that although the new "Bibles" have gotten rid of Christ's Deity in 1 Timothy 3:16; Luke 23:42; John 9:35; and Acts 9:6, 4:27, 30, they haven't been able to touch that one.

["Handle me, and see; for a spirit hath not FLESH AND BONES, as ye see me have."]{.class_4723} What happened to the blood? [It was shed out on the cross]{.class_4725} (John 19:34). So when you go up at the Rapture, you leave your blood behind because ["FLESH AND BLOOD cannot inherit the kingdom of God; neither doth corruption inherit incorruption"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 15:50). You leave your blood behind [because it's no good.]{.class_4725} There's []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_690}["no good thing"]{.class_4723} in your flesh either (Rom. 7:18), but your flesh is [changed]{.class_4725} (Phil. 3:21) from a natural, earthly, sinful body to a spiritual, heavenly body that is without sin (1 Cor. 15:42--49).

Do you want to see something interesting? Turn over to Genesis 2. When God makes a helpmeet for Adam, He makes her out of a [rib]{.class_4725} (Gen. 2:21--22), like the place where [the spear pierced Christ's side.]{.class_4725} When God presents the woman to Adam, Adam says, ["This is now BONE OF MY BONES, and FLESH OF MY FLESH" ]{.class_4723}(Gen. 2:24). What's missing? [The blood.]{.class_4725}

Do you know what that's a picture of? It's a picture of the Body of Christ (Eph. 5:29--32). The church was ["purchased with his own blood"]{.class_4723} (Acts 20:28). But in the spiritual union between ["Christ and the church," "we are members\...of his FLESH, and of his BONES"]{.class_4723} (Eph. 5:30--32), because that's how we end up (Rom. 8:29).

Back to Luke 24 and the resurrection body. Not only can the resurrection body become visible and invisible at will, pass through solid objects, not be limited by time and space, and is a physical, supernatural body; but it can also [eat without having to digest or eliminate food.]{.class_4725}

Having just seen and touched Christ's body, the disciples still can't believe what's happening. Of course, we can understand their dilemma. It would be like burying a loved one before prayer meeting on Wednesday night and having that person walk into the Sunday evening service the next weekend in perfect health. You'd be so happy to see him (or her) that you wouldn't know if it were real or if you were dreaming.

But for one last proof of His resurrection, Jesus takes ["a piece of broiled fish, and of an honeycomb"]{.class_4723} and eats it before the disciples (vss. 42--43). Now did He [need]{.class_4725} to eat to stay alive? Of course not! He had already died and come back to life. He had no blood to transport nutrients to the various parts of His body. There was no exchange for waste material to be eliminated. He ate for the [sheer enjoyment of eating.]{.class_4725}

If you are a glutton, don't miss Heaven. You can eat what you want, when you want, how much you want, and not have to worry []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_691}about indigestion, gas, or gaining any weight. You won't have to worry about fiber, vitamins, red meat, cholesterol, calories, or saturated fat ever again.

44 And he said unto them, These are the words which I spake unto you, while I was yet with you, that all things must be fulfilled, which were written in the law of Moses, and in the prophets, and in the psalms, concerning me.

45 Then opened he their understanding, that they might understand the scriptures,

Now these are two salient verses on the Scriptures themselves. They are definitive statements on what the Old Testament is and who interprets Scripture. The reason that is so important is because the Catholic Church professes that [it]{.class_4733} is able to tell you both of those things, but to do that, they had to override the words of the Lord Jesus Christ Himself.

There are two definitive verses in Luke on the canon of the Old Testament, and they are 11:51 and 24:44.

Luke 11:51 says, ["From the blood of Abel unto the blood of Zacharias, which perished between the altar and the temple."]{.class_4723} What's that? That's Genesis 4 and 2 Chronicles 24. The way a Hebrew Old Testament is arranged, it starts with Genesis and ends with 2 Chronicles.

Now the Hebrew Old Testament still contains all 39 books as found in your [King James]{.class_4725} Bible, but they are arranged differently. They are divided into the three groups that Jesus lists here in verse 44: ["the law of Moses,"]{.class_4723} called the [Torah]{.class_4725} in Hebrew; ["the prophets,"]{.class_4723} called the [Nabhim]{.class_4725} in Hebrew; ["the psalms"]{.class_4723} or [the writings,]{.class_4725} called the [Kethubhim]{.class_4725} in Hebrew.

The [Torah]{.class_4725} would be the first five books of the Old Testament: Genesis--Deuteronomy.

The [Nabhim]{.class_4725} includes Joshua, Judges, 1 & 2 Samuel, 1 & 2 Kings, Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and the twelve Minor Prophets, which are counted as one book because they are all on one scroll. That is why the count of books in the Hebrew Old Testament comes out to 22 instead of 39. Sometimes you will see the number 24 given []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_692}depending on whether Ruth is attached to Judges and Lamentations is attached to Jeremiah.

The [Kethubhim]{.class_4725} includes Psalms, Proverbs, Job, Song of Solomon, Ruth, Lamentations, Esther, Ecclesiastes, Daniel, Ezra, and Nehemiah (which are sometimes combined into one book), and 1 & 2 Chronicles.

As you can see, [not one Apocryphal book appears in the Hebrew canon.]{.class_4725} Neither Christ nor any apostle ever quoted any book of the Apocrypha, and the Apostle Paul would quote [heathen poetry]{.class_4725} before he would quote the Apocrypha (see Acts 17:28; Tit. 1:12). Every book of the Apocrypha (1 & 2 Esdras, Tobit, Judith, Wisdom of Solomon, Ecclesiasticus, Baruch, Song of the Three Children, Susanna, Bel and the Dragon, Prayer of Manasses, 1 & 2 Maccabees, and additions to Esther) were written [after]{.class_4725} the Holy Spirit closed the Old Testament canon with Malachi. No orthodox Jew of Christ's day accepted any of the books as canonical, and the fact that some of the books (see the list in App. 48 of the [Ruckman Reference Bible]{.class_4725}) were found in the Dead Sea Scrolls of the Qumran community just shows you that some apostate Jews, who rejected the Old Testament Temple worship and sacrificial system set up by God Almighty, copied down some other [junk]{.class_4725} along with the Holy Scriptures (like The War of the Children of Light and The Manual of Discipline). When the Jewish leaders met at the Council of Jamnia in A.D. 90 to make an official declaration on what books belonged in the Hebrew canon, they accepted the 39 books of a [King James]{.class_4725} Old Testament and rejected all fourteen books of the Apocrypha. Josephus excluded the apocryphal books and spoke of them as being "slipped in."

It was not until A.D. 382 (the Council of Rome) that the Catholic Church gave any official credence to the Apocrypha. Even then there was no official statement on the Apocrypha itself. The decree of the Council of Rome was that the [Septuagint]{.class_4725} was "Scripture," and since the only extant copies of the [Septuagint]{.class_4725} we have today ([Vaticanus]{.class_4725} and [Sinaiticus!]{.class_4725}) contain [both]{.class_4725} Old Testament and New Testament Apocrypha (The Epistle of Barnabas and The Shepherd of Hermas), the conclusion you are supposed to reach is that the Apocrypha was accepted as inspired.

However, up until the Council of Hippo in A.D. 393 and the []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_693}Council of Carthage in A.D. 397, no canonical list contained the Apocrypha as part of the Old Testament. The Council of Laodicea in A.D. 367 excluded the Apocrypha from the canon. According to Cyril of Jerusalem and Athanasius, the original [Septuagint]{.class_4725} did [not]{.class_4725} include the Apocrypha. Jerome would not even include it in his Latin [Vulgate;]{.class_4725} it was the [Pope]{.class_4725} who had it reinserted.

The fact of the matter is, it was [Augustine---after]{.class_4725} the death of Jerome---who pushed for the inclusion of the Apocrypha in the Old Testament, and Augustine was the leading force behind the Councils of Hippo and Carthage. Philip Schaff called Augustine [the first real Roman Catholic.]{.class_4725} The idea that the Roman Catholic Church is right on the Apocrypha because it was right on the 27 books of the New Testament at the Council of Carthage is [blasphemous nonsense]{.class_4725} given the fact that the Lord Jesus Christ gave you the definitive statements on what constituted an Old Testament and what didn't over 321 years before Augustine had the syrup taken out of his baby formula.

Back to Luke 24: ["Then opened HE their understanding, that they might understand the scriptures"]{.class_4723} (vs. 45). The Catholic Church claims for itself "supreme magisterium," meaning it is the only official, infallible interpreter of Scripture. According to the "first Pope" (Simon Peter, according to the Roman Catholic Church), that is a [bald-faced lie.]{.class_4725}

Simon Peter wrote, ["Knowing this first, that no prophecy of the scripture is of any private interpretation"]{.class_4723} (2 Pet. 1:20). There is no man, council, Bishop, Cardinal, Pope, or church that can tell you what the Scriptures mean. [Only God can tell you what the Scriptures mean.]{.class_4725}

Joseph said, ["Do not interpretations belong to GOD?"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 40:8); and ["It is NOT IN ME: GOD shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace"]{.class_4723} (Gen. 41:16).

Daniel told Nebuchadnezzar, ["the secret which the king hath demanded cannot the wise men, the astrologers, the magicians, the soothsayers, shew unto the king; BUT THERE IS A GOD IN HEAVEN THAT REVEALETH SECRETS, and maketh known to the king Nebuchadnezzar what shall be in the latter days"]{.class_4723} (Dan. 2:27--28).

Here in Luke 24:45, if Jesus Christ Himself hadn't opened the apostles' understanding of the Scriptures, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_694}[they couldn't have gotten anything.]{.class_4725} Those were the original apostles, man, and you think some fat wino over in Rome messing around with altar boys is going to fare any better?

In this day and age, it takes the Holy Spirit of God indwelling the heart of the believer and comparing Scripture with Scripture to lead and ["guide you into all truth"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 2:13; John 16:13). The [Author]{.class_4725} of the Scripture is its only infallible Interpreter (2 Pet. 1:21).

46 And said unto them, Thus it is written, and thus it behoved Christ to suffer, and to rise from the dead the third day:

47 And that repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name among all nations, beginning at Jerusalem.

48 And ye are witnesses of these things.

49 And, behold, I send the promise of my Father upon you: but tarry ye in the city of Jerusalem, until ye be endued with power from on high.

["Behoved"]{.class_4723} in verse 46 is the old spelling of [behooved.]{.class_4725} It means it was "necessary or fit for." In other words, it was necessary for Christ to die and rise from the dead based on what the Scriptures said. That's Psalm 22; Isaiah 53; Jonah; and Isaac, among others.

Verses 47--49 are Luke's version of what is known as "the Great Commission"; he gives another version of it in Acts 1:8. Hyperdispensationalists like to limit the Great Commission to just the Jewish apostles or the Tribulation based on the Matthew and Mark versions of it. But that won't work at all. The book of Acts is the account of how the apostles carried out the commission of Luke 24:47--49 and Acts 1:8. They start out in Jerusalem in Acts 2 and go to Judea and Samaria in Acts 8 and end up going to the Gentile nations with the Apostle Paul in Acts 13--28. Paul preached ["repentance toward God, and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_4723} to those Gentiles (Acts 20:21); he preached ["forgiveness of sins"]{.class_4723} through Jesus Christ (Acts 13:38).

The message of the Great Commission, as given here by Luke, is the same Gospel Paul preached. It is: ["Christ died ]{.class_4736}\[vs. 46\][]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_695 .class-1}[ for our sins ]{.class_4736}\[vs. 47\][ according to the scriptures ]{.class_4736}\[vss. 45--46\][, And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day ]{.class_4736}\[vs. 46\][ according to the scriptures"]{.class_4736} (1 Cor. 15:3--4).

["And that repentance and remission of sins should be preached IN HIS NAME"]{.class_4723} (vs. 47). Peter said, ["Neither is there salvation in any other: for there is none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved"]{.class_4723} (Acts 4:12). He told Cornelius, ["To him give all the prophets witness, that through his name whosoever believeth in him shall receive the remission of sins"]{.class_4723} (Acts 10:43).

Paul wrote, ["For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 10:13). What's ["the name of the Lord"]{.class_4723}? Oh, that's the ["name\...above every name"]{.class_4723} (Phil. 2:9) to which you'll bow now in humble repentance and faith, or you'll bow later in subjection at the judgment (Phil. 2:10); it's your choice.

["And ye are witnesses of these things"]{.class_4723} (vs. 48 cf. Acts 1:8). A witness testifies as to what he knows to be so: what he has seen and what he has heard.
