If you are saved, you should be testifying to a lost world about what you know (1 John 5:13). If you can't do anything else, the least you can do is tell others how you got saved.

Verse 49 is obviously a reference to the coming of the Holy Ghost at Pentecost. Pentecost in Acts 2 is a very dangerous place for a Christian to settle doctrinally. Three things take place in Acts 2 that never happen again. In Acts 2, the Holy Spirit came [on]{.class_4725} those believers as well as [in]{.class_4725} them. Every believer in Acts 2 received the Holy Spirit without most of them knowing it, because the Body of Christ was formed on earth but was not revealed till Paul showed up (Acts 1:5 cf. 1 Cor. 12:13). And thirdly, all the saved people in Acts 2 were in [one church;]{.class_4725} at that point in time (and it hasn't been that way since), the spiritual Body of Christ [was the local church.]{.class_4725} The Charismatics break their necks on the first point; the Hyperdispensationalists break their necks on the second point; and the Baptist Briders break their necks on the third point. And if that weren't enough, the Campbellites break their necks on Peter's message in Acts 2:38, which was the [only time]{.class_4725} receiving the Holy Spirit was connected with water baptism. Acts 2 is a bloody place to []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_696}get your doctrine.

50 And he led them out as far as to Bethany, and he lifted up his hands, and blessed them.

51 And it came to pass, while he blessed them, he was parted from them, and carried up into heaven.

52 And they worshipped him, and returned to Jerusalem with great joy:

53 And were continually in the temple, praising and blessing God. Amen.

Now the material here is what takes place in Acts 1; it is an abbreviated account of Christ's Ascension. Of course, Luke is the author of both the Gospel of Luke and the book of Acts, so he gives you a short account in his Gospel and the fuller account in Acts.

Since the passage deals with the glorification and present ministry of Jesus Christ, you can expect the new "Bibles" to mess with it. For [82 years]{.class_4725} (1897--1979), Nestle's Greek text omitted ["and carried up into heaven"]{.class_4723} from verse 51, ["worshipped him"]{.class_4723} from verse 52, and ["blessing God. Amen"]{.class_4723} from verse 53. This was done, basically, on the evidence of [one manuscript]{.class_4725}---manuscript [D]{.class_4725} (although [Sinaiticus]{.class_4725} also makes the omission from vs. 51)---which all the textual scholars and manuscript detectives attribute to the work of [Marcion the heretic]{.class_4725} (see [New Age Bible Versions]{.class_4725} by Riplinger, 1993, pg. 487).

What "Bible" followed Marcion in getting rid of Christ's ascension and the worship and blessing given to Christ and God? Why, the darling of Bob Jones University and Tennessee Temple University: the [New American Standard Version.]{.class_4725} The [NASV]{.class_4725} dropped the ascension of Christ completely from its text, even though Luke told you in Acts 1:1--2 that he put it in there. How's that for a "contradiction in the Bible"?

Well, in 1979, with its 26th edition, Nestle added [all three readings back into the text,]{.class_4725} meaning that the [AV]{.class_4725} was [right all along]{.class_4725} (see our remarks about Stewart Custer's "infallible Greek text" under 2:2). So when the Lockman Foundation put out its [NASV]{.class_4725} "Update," they had to "eat crow" and admit that the [NASV]{.class_4725} had been [in error]{.class_4725} since 1963. Brethren, never give up your Bible ([AV]{.class_4725}[]{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_697} 1611) to a bunch of scholars and professors too stupid to figure out they were wrong until late in the Twentieth Century.

["And they worshipped him"]{.class_4723} (vs. 52). The claim by Liberals and Modernists that Jesus Christ never claimed to be God is completely against the clear statements of Scripture. Not only did He claim Deity (e.g., John 10:30, 14:9), He never turned down worship offered to Him (Matt. 8:2, 9:18, 14:33, 15:25, 18:26, 28:9; John 9:38). When Thomas called Him ["My Lord and my God"]{.class_4723} (John 20:28), Jesus didn't say, "Watch out, Thomas. I'm not God; you got that wrong." He said, "Thomas you say that because of what you've seen, but there are some folks who will be blessed because they believe that without seeing" (John 20:29). Worship offered to Jesus Christ is [never misplaced.]{.class_4725}

["And returned to Jerusalem with great joy."]{.class_4723} That's how the book of Luke started out. The angel brought the shepherds ["good tidings of great joy"]{.class_4723} over the birth of the Saviour (2:10--11), and that ["great joy"]{.class_4723} found its fulfillment in the resurrection and ascension of that Saviour. He conquered sin and death, and He ascended to the right hand of His Father where He lives to make ["intercession for us"]{.class_4723} (Rom. 8:34). When I sin as a saint, I ["have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous"]{.class_4723} (1 John 2:1). The Devil can't condemn me, and nothing can separate me from God's love (Rom. 8:34--35). I couldn't go to Hell now if I tried (Rom. 8:37--39). WELL GLORY! If that doesn't make you shout, your "shouter" is broken.

Now, verse 53 should serve as a warning that what you are about to get into in the first few chapters of Luke's next book, Acts, is not going to be for you doctrinally as a New Testament believer in the Body of Christ. These are still [Jewish disciples]{.class_4725} operating under the only revelation they know: [the Old Testament Law.]{.class_4725} All they have is Genesis--Malachi. No New Testament Scriptures have been written yet; not even Matthew, Mark, Luke, or John.

["And were continually in the temple]{.class_4723} (vs. 53). That's not for you as a Christian. You don't need to go to any Jewish Temple in Jerusalem. ["Your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost"]{.class_4723} (1 Cor. 6:19). When Peter preaches at Pentecost, he's preaching to []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_698}Sabbath-observing, Temple-worshipping, pork-abstaining, bearded [Jews]{.class_4725} and [Jewish proselytes]{.class_4725} (Acts 2:10, 14, 20).

What's going on in Acts 1--7 is that God is giving those Jews a second chance to receive their Messiah through the preaching of the twelve apostles. So during the forty days before His ascension, when Jesus speaks to the disciples about ["the things pertaining to the kingdom of God"]{.class_4723} (Acts 1:3), He's giving them more of the same material He gave them in Luke; He's not giving them any revelation about the new testament or the Body of Christ.

Now the new testament is there, and it is in force because the testator has died (Heb. 9:16). The Body of Christ is there. It was formed at Calvary (Eph. 2:16), and the first members are placed in it in Acts 2 (Acts 1:5 cf. 1 Cor. 12:13). The problem is, nobody in Acts 1--7 knows anything about either one. That revelation isn't given until the Apostle Paul shows up (Eph. 3:2--6).

That's what the Hyperdispensationalists don't get. They see the difference between Peter's message in Acts 2 and Paul's message in Acts 13, but they jump to the conclusion that the Body wasn't there until Paul got the revelation. If I ask you what I have in my pocket, and I bring out my pocket knife, the pocket knife didn't suddenly "appear" the moment I brought it out. It was there all along. You just didn't know it was there until I showed it to you.

That's the same thing with the new testament and the Body of Christ. The Old Testament ordinances were blotted out at Calvary (Col. 2:14). Peter could have eaten catfish, shrimp, and clams there in John 21; but it wasn't shown to him until Acts 10. Once Christ died and rose again, there was no need for temples, sacrifices, incense, priests, holy water, dietary restrictions, etc.; but God makes a gradual transition from Old Testament Law and practice to New Testament grace and practice. The book of Acts is the historical record of that transition. That is why you don't take your doctrine from Acts: things are still switching over.

As a New Testament Christian in the Body of Christ, you take your doctrine from the revelation given for that group to the Apostle to the Gentiles who wrote to New Testament local churches and pastors. The rule of thumb is that if what you are reading matches what Paul wrote to Gentile Christians in Romans--Philemon, []{#aanhLZRRSx2riOtByQm3wQ4501.xhtml_page_699}then you can apply that part of the Scriptures to you doctrinally, and if it doesn't, it applies to someone else (Old Testament, Tribulation, or Millennium).

The command given in the Scriptures, that will save you from making a shipwreck doctrinally, is found in 2 Timothy 2:15. Naturally it has been changed in the apostate, corrupt modern translations to make sure you get your Bible all screwed up.

"Study to shew thyself approved unto God, a workman that needeth not to be ashamed, rightly dividing the word of truth."
